verses.kjv_text: And the LORD called unto Moses, and spake unto him out of the tabernacle of the congregation, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֵלָיו מֵאֹהֶל מוֹעֵד לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayikra el-Mosheh vayedabber Yahweh elav me'Ohel Mo'ed lemor.
verses.study_note: The opening word 'Vayikra' (And He called) gives the Hebrew name to the entire book. God initiates the conversation — grace precedes law. He speaks from the Tabernacle (Ohel Mo'ed — 'Tent of Meeting'), the place where heaven and earth intersect. Exodus ended with God's glory filling the Tabernacle so intensely that even Moses could not enter (Exod 40:34-35); now God calls Moses in. The shift from Sinai (Exodus) to the Tabernacle (Leviticus) marks a move from God speaking in terrifying thunder to God speaking in intimate instruction.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's desire is not distance but communion. He provides the way of approach. Leviticus is not a burden but an invitation — the holy God making access possible for sinful humanity.

verses.kjv_text: Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, If any man of you bring an offering unto the LORD, ye shall bring your offering of the cattle, even of the herd, and of the flock.
verses.original_text: דַּבֵּר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵהֶם אָדָם כִּי־יַקְרִיב מִכֶּם קָרְבָּן לַיהוָה מִן־הַבְּהֵמָה מִן־הַבָּקָר וּמִן־הַצֹּאן תַּקְרִיבוּ אֶת־קָרְבַּנְכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Dabber el-benei Yisra'el ve'amarta alehem adam ki-yakriv mikkem korban laYahweh min-habbehemah min-habbakar umin-hatzon takrivu et-korbankhem.
verses.study_note: The word 'adam' (man/person) rather than 'ish' (individual man) is significant — it echoes Genesis, linking sacrifice to the universal human condition since Adam. 'Korban' (offering) comes from 'karav' (to draw near) — the entire purpose of sacrifice is to bring the worshipper near to God. The offering must come from domesticated animals (behemah) — cattle (herd) or sheep/goats (flock) — animals that cost the owner something, not wild game.
verses.doctrinal_note: Sacrifice is the divinely appointed means of drawing near to God. The root 'karav' (to approach/draw near) reveals that offerings are not about appeasing an angry deity but about restoring relationship. God provides the way back to Himself.

verses.kjv_text: If his offering be a burnt sacrifice of the herd, let him offer a male without blemish: he shall offer it of his own voluntary will at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation before the LORD.
verses.original_text: אִם־עֹלָה קָרְבָּנוֹ מִן־הַבָּקָר זָכָר תָּמִים יַקְרִיבֶנּוּ אֶל־פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד יַקְרִיב אֹתוֹ לִרְצֹנוֹ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Im-olah korbano min-habbakar zakhar tamim yakrivenu el-petach Ohel Mo'ed yakriv oto lirtzono lifnei Yahweh.
verses.study_note: Four critical requirements: (1) 'Olah' — burnt offering, the offering that fully ascends. (2) 'Zakhar' — male, representing strength and firstfruits. (3) 'Tamim' — without blemish, physically perfect, foreshadowing Christ's sinlessness. (4) 'Lirtzono' — of his own voluntary will, or 'for his acceptance.' The Hebrew can mean both: the offering is voluntary AND it secures acceptance before God. This verse is the gospel in miniature: a perfect substitute, willingly offered, securing the worshipper's acceptance before a holy God.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christ fulfilled every requirement: He was the unblemished Lamb (1 Pet 1:19), offered voluntarily (John 10:18), at the appointed place (Golgotha, outside the camp — Heb 13:12), securing our acceptance before God (Eph 1:6).

verses.kjv_text: And he shall put his hand upon the head of the burnt offering; and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement for him.
verses.original_text: וְסָמַךְ יָדוֹ עַל רֹאשׁ הָעֹלָה וְנִרְצָה לוֹ לְכַפֵּר עָלָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamakh yado al rosh ha'olah venirtzah lo lekhapper alav.
verses.study_note: The laying on of hands (semikah) is the critical act of identification and transference. The worshipper presses his hand firmly on the animal's head, symbolically transferring his sin and guilt to the substitute. The result: 'nirtzah' (it shall be accepted) — the offering stands in the worshipper's place. 'Lekhapper' (to make atonement) literally means 'to cover' — the sin is covered by the substitute's death. This is the clearest Old Testament picture of substitutionary atonement: the innocent dies in place of the guilty.
verses.doctrinal_note: Imputation and substitution: the worshipper's guilt is transferred to the innocent victim, and the victim's acceptance before God is transferred to the worshipper. This is the pattern fulfilled in Christ — 'He hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin; that we might be made the righteousness of God in him' (2 Cor 5:21).

verses.kjv_text: And he shall kill the bullock before the LORD: and the priests, Aaron’s sons, shall bring the blood, and sprinkle the blood round about upon the altar that is by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation.
verses.original_text: וְשָׁחַט אֶת־בֶּן הַבָּקָר לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וְהִקְרִיבוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֲנִים אֶת־הַדָּם וְזָרְקוּ אֶת־הַדָּם עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב אֲשֶׁר־פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Veshahat et-ben habbakar lifnei Yahweh vehikrivu benei Aharon hakkohanim et-haddam vezareku et-haddam al-hammizbeach saviv asher-petach Ohel Mo'ed.
verses.study_note: The worshipper himself slaughters the animal — he must personally execute the death of his substitute. This was not done at a distance; the offerer felt the animal die under his hands. The priests then handle the blood, which represents the life (Lev 17:11). 'Zareku' (sprinkle/dash) — the blood is thrown against the sides of the altar, not merely dripped. Blood on the altar sanctifies it and makes atonement. The altar stands at the entrance — there is no way into God's presence except past the place of sacrifice.
verses.doctrinal_note: Without the shedding of blood there is no remission of sin (Hebrews 9:22). The worshipper's own hand slaying the victim makes personal the cost of sin — it is my sin that requires this death. The priestly mediation of the blood foreshadows Christ as both sacrifice and High Priest.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall flay the burnt offering, and cut it into his pieces.
verses.original_text: וְהִפְשִׁיט אֶת־הָעֹלָה וְנִתַּח אֹתָהּ לִנְתָחֶיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehifshit et-ha'olah venittach otah lintacheha.
verses.study_note: The animal is skinned and dissected into prescribed pieces. This is not butchery but sacred preparation. The flaying exposes the interior — every part is laid open before God. Nothing is hidden. The skin was given to the officiating priest as his portion (Lev 7:8). Typologically, Christ was stripped and exposed on the cross (Matt 27:35), every aspect of His life and character open to divine scrutiny and found perfect.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Aaron the priest shall put fire upon the altar, and lay the wood in order upon the fire:
verses.original_text: וְנָתְנוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֵן אֵשׁ עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וְעָרְכוּ עֵצִים עַל־הָאֵשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Venatenu benei Aharon hakkohen esh al-hammizbeach ve'arekhu etzim al-ha'esh.
verses.study_note: The priests prepare the altar fire and arrange the wood in order. Later, the fire on the altar would be divinely ignited (Lev 9:24) and was never to go out (Lev 6:12-13). 'Arekhu' (lay in order/arrange) — the same word used for setting the showbread in order (Exod 40:23) and for battle array. Worship requires careful, reverent preparation. The wood typifies the humanity of Christ — the fuel upon which the fire of divine judgment fell.

verses.kjv_text: And the priests, Aaron’s sons, shall lay the parts, the head, and the fat, in order upon the wood that is on the fire which is upon the altar:
verses.original_text: וְעָרְכוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֲנִים אֵת הַנְּתָחִים אֶת־הָרֹאשׁ וְאֶת־הַפָּדֶר עַל־הָעֵצִים אֲשֶׁר עַל־הָאֵשׁ אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'arekhu benei Aharon hakkohanim et hannethachim et-harosh ve'et-happader al-ha'etzim asher al-ha'esh asher al-hammizbeach.
verses.study_note: The pieces are arranged on the altar with the head and fat (peder — the suet/richest fat surrounding the kidneys). The head represents the thoughts and will; the fat represents the richest, choicest portion. Every part is specified — nothing is random. The meticulous ordering reflects God's attention to detail in worship. In the burnt offering, every part of the animal goes to God — unlike peace offerings where portions are shared.

verses.kjv_text: But his inwards and his legs shall he wash in water: and the priest shall burn all on the altar, to be a burnt sacrifice, an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְקִרְבּוֹ וּכְרָעָיו יִרְחַץ בַּמָּיִם וְהִקְטִיר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת־הַכֹּל הַמִּזְבֵּחָה עֹלָה אִשֵּׁה רֵיחַ נִיחוֹחַ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vekirbo ukhra'av yirchatz bammayim vehiktir hakkohen et-hakkol hammizbeachah olah ishsheh reach nichoach laYahweh.
verses.study_note: The innards (internal organs) and legs (the walk) are washed with water — purification of the hidden inner life and the outward conduct. Then the priest burns 'the whole' (hakkol) on the altar. 'Hiktir' (burn as incense) is distinct from 'saraph' (burn to destroy) — this is a fragrant, ascending offering, not destruction. The climactic phrase: 'reach nichoach laYahweh' — a sweet savour (literally 'a soothing/restful aroma') unto the LORD. This phrase appears over 40 times in the Pentateuch and is applied to Christ in Ephesians 5:2.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'sweet savour' reveals the Godward purpose of the burnt offering. It is not primarily about the offerer's sin (that is the sin offering) but about God's pleasure. Christ's total self-offering was a 'fragrant offering' to God (Eph 5:2) — the Father found complete satisfaction and delight in the Son's perfect obedience unto death.

verses.kjv_text: And if his offering be of the flocks, namely, of the sheep, or of the goats, for a burnt sacrifice; he shall bring it a male without blemish.
verses.original_text: וְאִם־מִן־הַצֹּאן קָרְבָּנוֹ מִן־הַכְּשָׂבִים אוֹ מִן־הָעִזִּים לְעֹלָה זָכָר תָּמִים יַקְרִיבֶנּוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-min-hatzon korbano min-hakkesavim o min-ha'izzim le'olah zakhar tamim yakrivenu.
verses.study_note: The second tier of burnt offering — from the flock (sheep or goats) rather than the herd. The same standards apply: male, without blemish. A sheep or goat was significantly less costly than a bull, accommodating those of lesser means. God does not grade the worshipper by the size of the offering but by the condition of the heart. The lamb points directly to Christ: 'Behold the Lamb of God' (John 1:29). The goat connects to the Day of Atonement scapegoat (Lev 16).

verses.kjv_text: And he shall kill it on the side of the altar northward before the LORD: and the priests, Aaron’s sons, shall sprinkle his blood round about upon the altar.
verses.original_text: וְשָׁחַט אֹתוֹ עַל יֶרֶךְ הַמִּזְבֵּחַ צָפֹנָה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וְזָרְקוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֲנִים אֶת־דָּמוֹ עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Veshahat oto al yerekh hammizbeach tzafonah lifnei Yahweh vezareku benei Aharon hakkohanim et-damo al-hammizbeach saviv.
verses.study_note: A new detail: the flock animal must be slain on the north side of the altar. The north (tzafonah) is significant: (1) In ancient Near Eastern cosmology, north was the direction of the divine mountain (cf. Isa 14:13, Ps 48:2). (2) The brazen altar's south side faced the entrance, so the north side provided a more private, sacred space. (3) Prophetically, Christ was crucified north of the altar of the Temple on Golgotha. The blood ritual is identical to the bull offering — God's acceptance does not depend on the animal's value.
verses.doctrinal_note: The northward slaughter is one of Scripture's subtle prophetic details. Jerusalem's topography places Golgotha to the north of the Temple mount. The Lamb of God was slain 'on the north side of the altar.'

verses.kjv_text: And he shall cut it into his pieces, with his head and his fat: and the priest shall lay them in order on the wood that is on the fire which is upon the altar:
verses.original_text: וְנִתַּח אֹתוֹ לִנְתָחָיו וְאֶת־רֹאשׁוֹ וְאֶת־פִּדְרוֹ וְעָרַךְ הַכֹּהֵן אֹתָם עַל־הָעֵצִים אֲשֶׁר עַל־הָאֵשׁ אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Venittach oto lintachav ve'et-rosho ve'et-pidro ve'arakh hakkohen otam al-ha'etzim asher al-ha'esh asher al-hammizbeach.
verses.study_note: The same dissection as the bull offering — cut into pieces, head and fat arranged on the wood. The repetition is not redundancy but emphasis: God's standards do not change based on the economic tier of the offering. Whether a wealthy man brings a bull or a poor man brings a sheep, the procedure, the care, and the divine acceptance are identical. Every piece laid in order speaks of Christ's life — examined in every detail and found entirely worthy.

verses.kjv_text: But he shall wash the inwards and the legs with water: and the priest shall bring it all, and burn it upon the altar: it is a burnt sacrifice, an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהַקֶּרֶב וְהַכְּרָעַיִם יִרְחַץ בַּמָּיִם וְהִקְרִיב הַכֹּהֵן אֶת־הַכֹּל וְהִקְטִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחָה עֹלָה הוּא אִשֵּׁה רֵיחַ נִיחוֹחַ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehakkerev vehakkera'ayim yirchatz bammayim vehikriv hakkohen et-hakkol vehiktir hammizbeachah olah hu ishsheh reach nichoach laYahweh.
verses.study_note: The identical conclusion as the bull offering: washing, complete burning, and the sweet savour ascending to the LORD. 'Olah hu' — 'it IS a burnt offering' — the emphatic pronoun 'hu' (it/he) declares its identity. Whether bull or sheep, the offering achieves the same result: fragrant acceptance before God. The repetition of 'reach nichoach laYahweh' underscores the central purpose — God's satisfaction in the offering.

verses.kjv_text: And if the burnt sacrifice for his offering to the LORD be of fowls, then he shall bring his offering of turtledoves, or of young pigeons.
verses.original_text: וְאִם מִן־הָעוֹף עֹלָה קָרְבָּנוֹ לַיהוָה וְהִקְרִיב מִן־הַתֹּרִים אוֹ מִן־בְּנֵי הַיּוֹנָה אֶת־קָרְבָּנוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im min-ha'of olah korbano laYahweh vehikriv min-hattorim o min-benei hayyonah et-korbano.
verses.study_note: The third and most affordable tier — birds. Turtledoves (torim) or young pigeons (benei yonah) were the offering of the poorest Israelites. This is what Mary and Joseph offered at Jesus' dedication (Luke 2:24), revealing their poverty. God ensures that no one is too poor to worship. The turtledove was known for its faithfulness and mourning cry; the pigeon for its innocence and gentleness — both qualities of Christ.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's grace accommodates every economic level. The poor man's pigeon and the rich man's bull produce the same 'sweet savour.' This principle carries into the New Testament: access to God through Christ is available to all, regardless of status (Gal 3:28, James 2:5).

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall bring it unto the altar, and wring off his head, and burn it on the altar; and the blood thereof shall be wrung out at the side of the altar:
verses.original_text: וְהִקְרִיבוֹ הַכֹּהֵן אֶל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וּמָלַק אֶת־רֹאשׁוֹ וְהִקְטִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחָה וְנִמְצָה דָמוֹ עַל קִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehikrivo hakkohen el-hammizbeach umalak et-rosho vehiktir hammizbeachah venimtzah damo al kir hammizbeach.
verses.study_note: For the bird offering, the priest performs the entire ritual — the worshipper does not slaughter it himself as with larger animals. 'Malak' (wring off/pinch) is a unique verb used only for the bird sacrifice — the priest severs the head with his thumbnail, not with a knife. The blood is drained ('nimtzah' — wrung/squeezed out) against the wall (kir) of the altar. Though the ritual is simpler and the animal smaller, blood is still required — there is no atonement without it.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall pluck away his crop with his feathers, and cast it beside the altar on the east part, by the place of the ashes:
verses.original_text: וְהֵסִיר אֶת־מֻרְאָתוֹ בְּנֹצָתָהּ וְהִשְׁלִיךְ אֹתָהּ אֵצֶל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ קֵדְמָה אֶל־מְקוֹם הַדָּשֶׁן׃
verses.transliteration: Vehesir et-mur'ato benotzatah vehishlik otah etzel hammizbeach kedmah el-mekom haddashen.
verses.study_note: The crop (mur'ah — the gullet/craw where food is stored) and its feathers/contents are removed and discarded on the east side of the altar — the ash heap. The crop represents what the bird has consumed — the unclean food of the earth. It is the one part rejected, cast aside. Everything else ascends to God. This small detail pictures the removal of defilement: Christ bore our sin but was Himself without sin. The ash heap (deshen) on the east side collected the residue of all burnt offerings — a place of cleansing (Lev 6:10-11).

verses.kjv_text: And he shall cleave it with the wings thereof, but shall not divide it asunder: and the priest shall burn it upon the altar, upon the wood that is upon the fire: it is a burnt sacrifice, an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְשִׁסַּע אֹתוֹ בִכְנָפָיו לֹא יַבְדִּיל וְהִקְטִיר אֹתוֹ הַכֹּהֵן הַמִּזְבֵּחָה עַל־הָעֵצִים אֲשֶׁר עַל־הָאֵשׁ עֹלָה הוּא אִשֵּׁה רֵיחַ נִיחוֹחַ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Veshissa oto vikhnafav lo yavdil vehiktir oto hakkohen hammizbeachah al-ha'etzim asher al-ha'esh olah hu ishsheh reach nichoach laYahweh.
verses.study_note: The bird is split open by its wings but not completely divided — it remains one piece. This preserves the unity of the offering. The wings, which once bore the bird aloft, now spread open on the altar. The chapter closes with the same sublime phrase: 'olah hu ishsheh reach nichoach laYahweh' — 'it is a burnt sacrifice, an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.' Whether a costly bull, a modest sheep, or a humble pigeon, the conclusion is identical. God evaluates the heart, not the wealth, of the worshipper.
verses.doctrinal_note: The threefold repetition of 'sweet savour unto the LORD' (vv.9, 13, 17) across all three tiers of offering teaches a foundational truth: God's acceptance is based on the nature of the offering (pointing to Christ), not the economic status of the offerer. The poorest Israelite with a pigeon found the same divine acceptance as the wealthiest with a bull. This is grace: access to God through Christ is not earned by human merit or measured by human resources but granted through the perfect Substitute.

meta.overview: The Law of the Burnt Offering — the first and most fundamental of the five Levitical offerings. God speaks from the newly erected Tabernacle, instructing Moses on how sinful humanity may approach Him through blood sacrifice. The burnt offering (olah — "that which ascends") is unique among the offerings because the entire animal is consumed on the altar, representing complete consecration and total surrender to God. Three grades of offering are prescribed — from the herd (bull), from the flock (sheep or goat), and from birds (turtledoves or pigeons) — ensuring that no Israelite, regardless of wealth, is excluded from worship. Every element of this offering typifies the Person and work of Christ: the voluntary approach, the unblemished victim, the laying on of hands (identification/imputation), the substitutionary death, the blood atonement, and the "sweet savour" ascending to God.
meta.theme: Complete Consecration & Substitutionary Atonement
meta.keyWordMeaning: Burnt offering — literally "that which goes up/ascends." The whole sacrifice ascends to God as smoke, picturing total dedication.
outline: God Speaks from the Tabernacle (vv.1-2): The LORD calls Moses and establishes the principle of voluntary offering
The Burnt Offering from the Herd (vv.3-9): A male bull without blemish — laying on of hands, slaughter, blood sprinkling, complete burning
The Burnt Offering from the Flock (vv.10-13): A male sheep or goat — same ritual with northward slaughter
The Burnt Offering of Birds (vv.14-17): Turtledoves or young pigeons — provision for the poor

verses.kjv_text: And when any will offer a meat offering unto the LORD, his offering shall be of fine flour; and he shall pour oil upon it, and put frankincense thereon:
verses.original_text: וְנֶפֶשׁ כִּי־תַקְרִיב קָרְבַּן מִנְחָה לַיהוָה סֹלֶת יִהְיֶה קָרְבָּנוֹ וְיָצַק עָלֶיהָ שֶׁמֶן וְנָתַן עָלֶיהָ לְבֹנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Venefesh ki-takriv korban minchah laYHVH solet yihyeh korbano veyatsak aleha shemen venatan aleha levonah.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with the Hebrew word 'nefesh' (soul/person), not the generic 'adam' — emphasizing the deeply personal nature of this offering. 'When any [soul] will offer' — the minchah is entirely voluntary, an outpouring of personal devotion rather than a commanded atonement. The three required elements establish the typological architecture: (1) 'Solet' — fine flour: the finest grade of ground wheat, sifted to perfect uniformity with no lumps or inconsistencies, typifying the sinless, balanced perfection of Christ's humanity — no roughness, no coarseness. (2) 'Shemen' (olive oil): universally in the Levitical system a type of the Holy Spirit — Christ was supremely 'anointed' (Messiah means 'Anointed One') with the Spirit without measure (John 3:34). (3) 'Levonah' (frankincense): a white aromatic resin burned to produce sweet smoke, typifying the fragrant prayers and intercession of Christ ascending before the Father (Ps 141:2, Rev 8:3). The offering begins with pouring oil and placing frankincense — the Spirit and prayer frame everything.
verses.doctrinal_note: The three elements of the raw flour offering form a trinitarian typology of Christ: the 'solet' (fine flour) pictures His perfect, Spirit-formed humanity (Luke 1:35); the 'shemen' (oil) pictures the Holy Spirit who conceived, anointed, and empowered Him (Luke 4:18); the 'levonah' (frankincense) pictures the sweet-smelling life of prayer and intercession that defines His eternal priesthood (Heb 7:25). The minchah prefigures the Incarnation — deity taking on perfect, unblemished humanity.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall bring it to Aaron’s sons the priests: and he shall take thereout his handful of the flour thereof, and of the oil thereof, with all the frankincense thereof; and the priest shall burn the memorial of it upon the altar, to be an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD:
verses.original_text: וֶהֱבִיאָהּ אֶל־בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֲנִים וְקָמַץ מִשָּׁם מְלֹא קֻמְצוֹ מִסָּלְתָּהּ וּמִשַּׁמְנָהּ עַל כָּל־לְבֹנָתָהּ וְהִקְטִיר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת־אַזְכָּרָתָהּ הַמִּזְבֵּחָה אִשֵּׁה רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehevia'ah el-benei Aharon hakkohanim vekamatz mishsham melo kumtzo missoletah umishsmanah al kol-levontatah vehiktir hakkohen et-azkaratah hamizbeachah ishheh reiyach nichoach laYHVH.
verses.study_note: The offerer brings the offering to the priest, who takes 'a handful' (kumtzo — the cupped palm) of the flour and oil, along with ALL of the frankincense. This 'handful' is called the 'azkarah' — the memorial/representative portion. The entire frankincense is burned, but only a handful of the flour is offered on the altar — meaning the full fragrance of intercession ascends entirely, while only a representative portion of the grain is consumed. The rest belongs to the priests (v.3). 'Ishheh reiyach nichoach' — an offering made by fire, a sweet-smelling savour — this three-word formula ('fire-offering, sweet, restful') occurs throughout Leviticus and pictures God's delight in what is offered. The word 'nichoach' (restful, soothing) suggests that the offering brings God's 'rest' — His satisfaction. Christ's offering was the ultimate 'sweet savour' that fully satisfied the Father (Eph 5:2).
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'azkarah' (memorial portion) burned on the altar represents the whole offering — a part standing for the whole. This principle underlies the entire sacrificial system and finds its fulfillment in Christ: He is the One whose life, offered once, represents and covers all. The burning of all the frankincense (with only part of the flour) shows that intercession and prayer are given entirely to God, while provision and sustenance are shared with His servants — a principle of priestly ministry.

verses.kjv_text: And the remnant of the meat offering shall be Aaron’s and his sons’: it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire.
verses.original_text: וְהַנּוֹתֶרֶת מִן־הַמִּנְחָה לְאַהֲרֹן וּלְבָנָיו קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים מֵאִשֵּׁי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehannoteret min-haminchah leAharon ulvanav kodesh kodashim me'ishhei YHVH.
verses.study_note: What remains after the 'azkarah' (memorial handful) is burned belongs entirely to Aaron and his sons — the priests. They eat it in the court of the tabernacle, without leaven, as their portion of the fire-offering. Critically, the remainder is designated 'kodesh kodashim' — most holy, or holy of holies. This is the highest holiness category in Leviticus, shared with the sin and guilt offerings (Lev 6:17, 7:1). The grain offering's remnant is not a lesser portion but 'most holy' — the priests are fed from the altar, sustained by what has been consecrated to God. This becomes a principle for NT ministry: 'they which minister about holy things live of the things of the temple' (1 Cor 9:13).
verses.doctrinal_note: The priest eating from 'most holy' offerings is a profound type of the believer feeding on Christ. Just as the priest was sustained by what was offered on the altar, believers are sustained by Christ's sacrifice — feeding on Him in the Word and in the Lord's Supper (John 6:51-58). The remnant being called 'most holy' teaches that there is nothing secondary about the provision Christ makes for His people; what remains after His perfect offering is holy nourishment for the household of God.

verses.kjv_text: And if thou bring an oblation of a meat offering baken in the oven, it shall be unleavened cakes of fine flour mingled with oil, or unleavened wafers anointed with oil.
verses.original_text: וְכִי תַקְרִיב קָרְבַּן מִנְחָה מַאֲפֵה תַנּוּר סֹלֶת חַלּוֹת מַצֹּת בְּלוּלֹת בַּשֶּׁמֶן וּרְקִיקֵי מַצּוֹת מְשֻׁחִים בַּשֶּׁמֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi takriv korban minchah ma'afeh tannur solet challot matzot belulot bashshemen urekikei matzot meshuchim bashshemen.
verses.study_note: The second form of the Minchah: oven-baked (ma'afeh tannur). Two varieties are permitted — (1) 'challot matzot' (thick unleavened cakes) 'belulot' (mingled/kneaded through) with oil — the oil is incorporated throughout the dough, permeating every part, typifying the Holy Spirit's thoroughgoing presence through Christ's entire person and nature; (2) 'rekikei matzot' (thin wafers) 'meshuchim' (anointed/smeared) with oil on the surface — oil applied externally, typifying the Spirit's visible anointing of Christ at His baptism (Luke 3:22). Both forms are 'matzot' — unleavened — establishing the absolute requirement of sinlessness. The oven is a hidden, intense heat — some interpreters see in this the hidden sufferings of Christ's soul (Ps 22:14, Isa 53:10) in contrast to the external sufferings visible on the cross.
verses.doctrinal_note: The two forms of the oven offering present two aspects of Christ's relationship to the Holy Spirit: (1) The oil mingled throughout the cake pictures the Spirit as the agent of Christ's conception and the principle of His holy nature — He was 'full of the Holy Ghost' from the womb (Luke 1:15 parallel; Luke 4:1). (2) The oil anointed on the surface pictures the Spirit's empowerment at His baptism for public ministry (Acts 10:38). Both are true simultaneously — Christ was Spirit-filled in His nature and Spirit-anointed in His mission.

verses.kjv_text: And if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in a pan, it shall be of fine flour unleavened, mingled with oil.
verses.original_text: וְאִם־מִנְחָה עַל־הַמַּחֲבַת קָרְבָּנֶךָ סֹלֶת בְּלוּלָה בַשֶּׁמֶן מַצָּה תִהְיֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-minchah al-hammachavat korbonekha solet belulah vashshemen matzah tihyeh.
verses.study_note: The third form: the griddle (machavat) offering. The 'machavat' is a flat, open iron griddle or plate — the heat is direct, surface-level, and visible, unlike the enclosed oven of v.4. The offering on the griddle could be watched and tended throughout the cooking — nothing is hidden. The requirements remain identical: fine flour (solet), oil mixed in (belulah), unleavened (matzah). The variety of cooking methods across the Minchah's forms is theologically significant: God accepts the labor of different circumstances, different conditions of life. Whether one's devotion is shaped in the hidden suffering of the oven or the open trial of the griddle, the substance — Christ — remains unchanged.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt part it in pieces, and pour oil thereon: it is a meat offering.
verses.original_text: פָּתֹות אֹתָהּ פִּתִּים וְיָצַקְתָּ עָלֶיהָ שָׁמֶן מִנְחָה הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Patot otah pittim veyatsakhta aleha shamen minchah hi.
verses.study_note: The griddle cake must be broken (patot) into pieces before being offered. 'Pittim' — fragments, broken pieces. The breaking is not optional but commanded. This detail is remarkable: the offering must be broken before it can be presented. Oil is then poured over the broken pieces — the Spirit applied to brokenness. The typological force is unmistakable: Christ was broken for His people — 'the LORD hath laid on him the iniquity of us all' (Isa 53:6). The breaking does not diminish the offering; it prepares it for acceptance. Christ's body, broken and poured out, is what the Father receives as the full Minchah.

verses.kjv_text: And if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in the fryingpan, it shall be made of fine flour with oil.
verses.original_text: וְאִם מִנְחַת מַרְחֶשֶׁת קָרְבָּנֶךָ סֹלֶת בַּשֶּׁמֶן תֵּעָשֶׂה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im minchat marcheshet korbonekha solet bashshemen te'aseh.
verses.study_note: The fourth cooking method: the 'marcheshet' — a deep pan or pot, likely a covered vessel with fat or oil (distinct from the flat griddle). The cooking in deep oil pictures immersion in the Spirit — not just surface anointing but complete envelopment. Whatever the cooking vessel — oven, griddle, or deep pot — the essential ingredients remain non-negotiable: 'solet' (fine flour) and 'shemen' (oil). This consistency across all four cooking methods is itself a statement: the varied circumstances of a worshiper's life do not alter the nature of the offering — Christ remains perfect and Spirit-filled regardless of the trials through which He passes.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt bring the meat offering that is made of these things unto the LORD: and when it is presented unto the priest, he shall bring it unto the altar.
verses.original_text: וְהֵבֵאתָ אֶת־הַמִּנְחָה אֲשֶׁר יֵעָשֶׂה מֵאֵלֶּה לַיהוָה וְהִקְרִיבָהּ אֶל־הַכֹּהֵן וְהִגִּישָׁהּ אֶל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Veheveita et-haminchah asher ye'aseh me'elleh laYHVH vehikrivo'ah el-hakkohen vehiggisha'ah el-hammizbeach.
verses.study_note: The movement of the offering is described in three stages: (1) the offerer brings it 'to the LORD' — the ultimate recipient, (2) presents it to the priest — the mediator, (3) the priest brings it to the altar — the place of transformation. This three-stage movement is fundamental to the sacrificial system and reflects the NT pattern: the believer brings his offering (himself, his worship, his sacrifice) to God through Christ the High Priest, who presents it at the heavenly altar. 'He shall bring it' — the priest acts as the indispensable mediator; without the priest, the offering cannot reach the altar.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall take from the meat offering a memorial thereof, and shall burn it upon the altar: it is an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהֵרִים הַכֹּהֵן מִן־הַמִּנְחָה אֶת־אַזְכָּרָתָהּ וְהִקְטִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחָה אִשֵּׁה רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Veherim hakkohen min-haminchah et-azkaratah vehiktir hammizbeachah ishheh reiyach nichoach laYHVH.
verses.study_note: The priest 'lifts' (herim — same root as the terumah/heave offering) the azkarah from the offering — a gesture of elevation before God. The burning (hiktir — Hiphil of 'qatar,' to cause smoke to rise) of the memorial portion produces the 'reiyach nichoach' — the sweet, soothing savour. The Hebrew picture is deeply evocative: the rising smoke of the grain offering carried the fragrance of devoted human labor upward to the nostrils of God, who 'smelled' it as a restful, pleasing aroma. This language, applied anthropomorphically, expresses God's genuine satisfaction and delight in the offering. The same formula ('sweet savour') is used of every major sacrifice — God is not indifferent to devoted worship.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'sweet savour' (reiyach nichoach) terminology applied to Christ's offering in Ephesians 5:2 is the direct NT fulfillment of this Levitical formula. Paul writes that Christ 'hath given himself for us an offering and a sacrifice to God for a sweet-smelling savour' — explicitly using the language of Lev 2:9. The Father's delight in Christ's offering is the ground of our acceptance: we are received 'in the Beloved' (Eph 1:6), accepted because He was accepted.

verses.kjv_text: And that which is left of the meat offering shall be Aaron’s and his sons’: it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire.
verses.original_text: וְהַנּוֹתֶרֶת מִן־הַמִּנְחָה לְאַהֲרֹן וּלְבָנָיו קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים מֵאִשֵּׁי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehannoteret min-haminchah leAharon ulvanav kodesh kodashim me'ishhei YHVH.
verses.study_note: This verse repeats v.3 verbatim — a literary device of emphasis (inclusion) that brackets the four cooking methods within the same priestly principle: the remainder is 'most holy.' Whether the offering was raw, oven-baked, griddle-cooked, or deep-fried, the portion remaining after the altar's portion belongs to the priests and carries the highest holiness designation. The repetition teaches that the priestly prerogative is not dependent on the form of the offering — in every variation, the priests are nourished from what is most holy. This priestly feeding on most-holy offerings is a type of the believer's feeding on Christ through Scripture, sacrament, and prayer.

verses.kjv_text: No meat offering, which ye shall bring unto the LORD, shall be made with leaven: for ye shall burn no leaven, nor any honey, in any offering of the LORD made by fire.
verses.original_text: כָּל־הַמִּנְחָה אֲשֶׁר תַּקְרִיבוּ לַיהוָה לֹא תֵעָשֶׂה חָמֵץ כִּי כָל־שְׂאֹר וְכָל־דְּבַשׁ לֹא־תַקְטִירוּ מִמֶּנּוּ אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Kol-haminchah asher takrivu laYHVH lo te'aseh chametz ki kol-se'or vekhol-devash lo-taktiru mimmennu ishheh laYHVH.
verses.study_note: The great prohibition: 'NO leaven... NO honey.' Two substances are permanently excluded from all fire-offerings. (1) 'Chametz' (leaven/yeast) — the Bible consistently uses leaven as a symbol of corruption, permeation of evil, and the malice and wickedness of sin (1 Cor 5:8). Leaven works invisibly, puffs up, and spreads through a lump. Paul in 1 Corinthians 5:7 connects the removal of leaven to Christ as the Passover Lamb. No sin, no corruption, not even the appearance of evil, can approach the altar. (2) 'Devash' (honey) — natural sweetness. Some interpret the honey prohibition as guarding against the sweetening or flattering of worship — making religion merely pleasant, attractive, or palatable. Others note that honey ferments and can produce leaven. Both leaven and honey can produce fermentation — the common denominator is corruption. Christ, the true Minchah, was absolutely without sin (Heb 4:15, 2 Cor 5:21) — no leaven in His nature, no corruption in His service.
verses.doctrinal_note: The dual prohibition — no leaven, no honey — establishes the holiness criterion for all worship: (1) No moral corruption (leaven) — worship must proceed from sincere, undefiled hearts (1 Cor 5:8). (2) No artificial sweetness or worldly attractiveness (honey) — worship must not be shaped by what is merely pleasant to human taste but by what is true and holy. The positive counterpart is salt (v.13) — which preserves without corrupting. This verse is the foundation for the New Testament principle that God is to be worshiped 'in spirit and in truth' (John 4:24), not in the ferment of sin or the flattery of worldly appeal.

verses.kjv_text: As for the oblation of the firstfruits, ye shall offer them unto the LORD: but they shall not be burnt on the altar for a sweet savour.
verses.original_text: קָרְבַּן רֵאשִׁית תַּקְרִיבוּ אֹתָם לַיהוָה וְאֶל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ לֹא־יַעֲלוּ לְרֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Korban reshit takrivu otam laYHVH ve'el-hammizbeach lo-ya'alu lereiyach nichoach.
verses.study_note: A nuanced distinction: leaven and honey may be offered as 'firstfruits' (reshit — firstlings, the beginning of the harvest) to the LORD but not burned on the altar as a 'sweet savour.' The firstfruits offering (see also Lev 23:17, where leavened loaves are waved at Pentecost) may include honey and leaven — they represent the imperfect, fallen human worshiper who still brings his best to God. These are received as gifts of acknowledgment and gratitude, not as atonement or as fire-offerings. The altar cannot accept corruption; the LORD's table can accept the worshiper where he is. This principle protects the holiness of the altar while not excluding imperfect worshipers from giving.

verses.kjv_text: And every oblation of thy meat offering shalt thou season with salt; neither shalt thou suffer the salt of the covenant of thy God to be lacking from thy meat offering: with all thine offerings thou shalt offer salt.
verses.original_text: וְכָל־קָרְבַּן מִנְחָתְךָ בַּמֶּלַח תִּמְלָח וְלֹא תַשְׁבִּית מֶלַח בְּרִית אֱלֹהֶיךָ מֵעַל מִנְחָתֶךָ עַל כָּל־קָרְבָּנְךָ תַּקְרִיב מֶלַח׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol-korban minchatekha bammelach timlach velo tashbit melach berit Elohekha me'al minchatekha al kol-korbonekha takriv melach.
verses.study_note: The positive, permanent, universal requirement: SALT on every offering. While leaven and honey are always excluded, salt is always included — in every form, without exception. Three aspects of salt illuminate this command: (1) Salt preserves — it arrests decay and corruption, preserving from the rot that leaven produces. (2) Salt purifies — it cleanses and disinfects. (3) Salt flavors — it enhances without corrupting. The text names it specifically 'melach berit' — the SALT OF THE COVENANT. Salt in the ancient Near East was the standard substance used to ratify covenants — 'covenant of salt' appears in Numbers 18:19 (Aaron's perpetual portion) and 2 Chronicles 13:5 (David's eternal throne). A covenant sealed with salt was binding, permanent, and incorruptible. Every offering seasoned with salt is therefore an offering that declares covenant fidelity — worship is a covenantal act before a covenant God.
verses.doctrinal_note: Salt of the covenant (melach berit) is one of the most theologically rich phrases in the sacrificial law. Jesus echoes it in Mark 9:49-50 ('have salt in yourselves, and have peace') and Matthew 5:13 ('Ye are the salt of the earth'). The believer's life is to be a perpetual 'salted offering' — preserved from corruption, offering covenant fidelity, and bringing the flavor of Christ's character to the world. The exclusion of leaven and inclusion of salt together define the character of true worship: no sin, but covenantal faithfulness. No corruption, but preserving grace.

verses.kjv_text: And if thou offer a meat offering of thy firstfruits unto the LORD, thou shalt offer for the meat offering of thy firstfruits green ears of corn dried by the fire, even corn beaten out of full ears.
verses.original_text: וְאִם תַּקְרִיב מִנְחַת בִּכּוּרִים לַיהוָה אָבִיב קָלוּי בָּאֵשׁ גֶּרֶשׂ כַּרְמֶל תַּקְרִיב אֵת מִנְחַת בִּכּוּרֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im takriv minchat bikkurim laYHVH aviv kalui ba'esh geres karmel takriv et minchat bikkurekha.
verses.study_note: The fifth and final form of the Minchah: the first-fruits grain offering (minchat bikkurim). This offering is presented when the grain is at its youngest and most tender — 'aviv' (fresh, young ears of grain, the same word for the spring month 'Abib/Aviv' when Passover falls). The grain is 'kalui ba'esh' — roasted by fire — and 'geres karmel' — crushed from full ears. Three stages of preparation: (1) young grain (not yet fully formed), (2) fire-roasted (passed through fire), (3) crushed from the full ear. The first-fruits offering acknowledges that the first and best belongs to God — the anticipation of full harvest, not yet complete but already consecrated. Typologically, this points to the resurrection: Christ is 'the firstfruits of them that slept' (1 Cor 15:20) — the first, young, fire-tested grain whose offering guarantees the full harvest of resurrection to come.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christ as the First-fruits: Paul explicitly identifies the risen Christ as the 'firstfruits' in 1 Corinthians 15:20-23. The 'bikkurim' offering of Leviticus 2:14 is enacted liturgically in Leviticus 23:10-14 (the wave sheaf at first-fruits, the day after the Sabbath following Passover — the precise day of Christ's resurrection). The 'fire-roasted' grain that has passed through fire before being offered as first-fruits typifies Christ passing through the 'fire' of God's judgment and rising as the consecrated first-fruits of the new creation.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt put oil upon it, and lay frankincense thereon: it is a meat offering.
verses.original_text: וְנָתַתָּ עָלֶיהָ שֶׁמֶן וְשַׂמְתָּ עָלֶיהָ לְבֹנָה מִנְחָה הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Venatatah aleha shemen vesamta aleha levonah minchah hi.
verses.study_note: Even the first-fruits offering requires the full complement: oil (shemen) and frankincense (levonah). The same elements required in v.1 for the raw flour offering are required here. The five forms of the Minchah are varied in their preparation — raw, oven-baked, griddle-cooked, deep-fried, fire-roasted — but all share the same essential elements: fine flour (or young grain), oil, frankincense, and salt (v.13), and all exclude leaven and honey. This consistency teaches that the essence of the offering — the spiritual substance typified by these elements — is invariable regardless of the circumstances of offering. Christ is always Spirit-anointed and prayerful, in every circumstance of His life and ministry.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall burn the memorial of it, part of the beaten corn thereof, and part of the oil thereof, with all the frankincense thereof: it is an offering made by fire unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהִקְטִיר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת־אַזְכָּרָתָהּ מִגִּרְשָׂהּ וּמִשַּׁמְנָהּ עַל כָּל־לְבֹנָתָהּ אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiktir hakkohen et-azkaratah miggirsah umishsmanah al kol-levontatah ishheh laYHVH.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with the same formula it opened with: the priest burns the 'azkarah' (memorial portion) — part of the crushed grain, part of the oil, ALL of the frankincense — as a fire-offering to the LORD. The closing echo of the opening (vv.2,9,16) creates a literary inclusio framing the entire chapter. Once again, all the frankincense is given to God, only a part of the grain and oil. The pattern is consistent: the full fragrance of intercession and prayer ascends entirely to God; the nourishment of the grain is shared between the altar and the priests. The chapter that began with a 'soul' voluntarily bringing an offering closes with the priestly mediation complete — the fire has consumed the memorial, the sweet savour has ascended, and God is satisfied.
verses.doctrinal_note: The minchah in its totality presents the comprehensive portrait of Christ's perfect humanity in devoted service to God: (1) Fine flour — perfection of character, no coarseness or inconsistency; (2) Oil throughout — conceived of the Spirit, anointed without measure; (3) Frankincense — a life of fragrant prayer and intercession; (4) Salt of the covenant — absolute fidelity to the Father's covenant; (5) No leaven — no sin, no corruption; (6) No honey — no worldly enticement or flattery; (7) Fire — tested, proven, wholly consumed for God. What was burned rose as a 'sweet savour' — the Father's full delight in the Son's perfect humanity and consecrated life. This is the ground of the believer's acceptance: presented to the Father 'in the Beloved' (Eph 1:6), we are accepted because Christ, the true Minchah, was wholly accepted.

meta.overview: Leviticus 2 prescribes the Minchah — the grain/meal offering — the only bloodless offering in the Levitical system. Unlike the burnt offering (ch.1) which pictures total consecration through atoning death, the Minchah is an offering of labor and life: fine flour, olive oil, frankincense, and salt. The offerant presents the work of his hands — ground grain, kneaded dough, baked bread — as an act of homage and dependence. Five forms are described: raw flour (vv.1-3), oven-baked (v.4), griddle-cooked (vv.5-6), deep-fried (vv.7-10), and first-fruits (vv.14-16). Two substances are permanently forbidden: leaven and honey (v.11). Two are permanently required: oil and salt (vv.1,13). The chapter is richly typological — every element points to the sinless humanity, Spirit-anointed ministry, and covenant fidelity of the Lord Jesus Christ, the true Minchah whom the Father accepted as a 'sweet savour.'
meta.theme: The Grain Offering — Human Devotion, Divine Perfection & the Typology of Christ's Humanity
meta.keyWordMeaning: Grain/Meal/Tribute offering — a gift of homage presented to a superior. From a root meaning 'to give' or 'to present.' The offering that represents consecrated human labor brought before a holy God.
outline: The Raw Flour Offering (vv.1-3): Fine flour, oil, frankincense — the handful burned; the remainder for the priests
Oven-Baked Offering (v.4): Unleavened cakes or wafers mingled/anointed with oil
Griddle-Baked Offering (vv.5-6): Unleavened fine flour mixed with oil, broken in pieces
Deep-Fried Offering (vv.7-10): Fine flour with oil; a memorial burned on the altar
The Eternal Prohibition — No Leaven, No Honey (vv.11-12): First-fruits may be offered but not burned
The Eternal Requirement — Salt of the Covenant (v.13): Salt never to be absent from any oblation
The First-Fruits Grain Offering (vv.14-16): Green roasted ears, oil, and frankincense presented to the LORD

verses.kjv_text: And if his oblation be a sacrifice of peace offering, if he offer it of the herd; whether it be a male or female, he shall offer it without blemish before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְאִם־זֶבַח שְׁלָמִים קָרְבָּנוֹ אִם מִן־הַבָּקָר הוּא מַקְרִיב אִם־זָכָר אִם־נְקֵבָה תָּמִים יַקְרִיבֶנּוּ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-zevach shelamim korbano im min-habbakar hu makriv im-zakhar im-nekevah tamim yakrivennu lifnei YHVH.
verses.study_note: The Shelamim (peace offering) is introduced. 'Zevach shelamim' — a slaughter-offering of peace/completion. Unlike the burnt offering (ch.1) which required a male, the peace offering uniquely permits 'a male or female' — the widest accessibility of any animal sacrifice. This openness reflects the all-encompassing nature of the peace it celebrates: God's shalom extends to all. The one non-negotiable requirement retained from the burnt offering is 'tamim' — without blemish, perfect, whole. No peace can be founded on anything less than perfection. The peace offering is a 'zevach' — a slaughter-sacrifice, implying a meal — while the burnt offering was an 'olah' (going up). From the outset, the peace offering signals a different purpose: not ascending in total consecration but providing the basis for a fellowship meal.
verses.doctrinal_note: The permission of male or female in the peace offering reflects the New Covenant truth that 'there is neither male nor female: for ye are all one in Christ Jesus' (Gal 3:28). The peace Christ has made is not for a restricted category but for all who come. Yet the 'tamim' (without blemish) remains absolute — the peace of God can only rest on the perfect, unblemished sacrifice of Christ. He is 'our peace' (Eph 2:14) precisely because He was tamim — wholly without defect.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall lay his hand upon the head of his offering, and kill it at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation: and Aaron’s sons the priests shall sprinkle the blood upon the altar round about.
verses.original_text: וְסָמַךְ יָדוֹ עַל־רֹאשׁ קָרְבָּנוֹ וּשְׁחָטוֹ פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְזָרְקוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֲנִים אֶת־הַדָּם עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamakh yado al-rosh korbano ushechato petach ohel mo'ed vezarku benei Aharon hakkohanim et-haddam al-hammizbeach saviv.
verses.study_note: Two defining acts open the peace offering ritual: (1) 'Samakh yado' — laying of the hand upon the animal's head. This act of transference is central to every major sacrifice in Leviticus (1:4, 3:2, 4:4, 16:21). The weight of the entire hand pressed down ('samakh' means to lean upon with full weight, not merely to touch) upon the substitute's head identifies offerer with offering and transfers moral accountability. The animal bears what the offerer brings. (2) 'Shechato petach ohel mo'ed' — the slaughter at the door of the tabernacle. The killing happens at the threshold — the precise boundary between the holy and the profane, between God's dwelling and human life. Peace is found precisely at this threshold. The priests then 'zarku' (cast, dashed) the blood 'saviv' (all around) the altar — not sprinkled drop by drop but hurled in full, covering all sides. The blood of the peace offering is applied to the whole altar.
verses.doctrinal_note: The laying of the hand (samakh) upon the substitute is the earliest enacted picture of imputation in Scripture — the theological act of one person's guilt or identity being placed upon another. The NT fulfillment is explicit: God 'hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin' (2 Cor 5:21). The offerer's hand on the animal's head and the knife in his own hand make him simultaneously the one who condemns and the one who is pardoned. This double role — judge and beneficiary — is the moral logic of substitutionary atonement.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall offer of the sacrifice of the peace offering an offering made by fire unto the LORD; the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards,
verses.original_text: וְהִקְרִיב מִזֶּבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה אֶת־הַחֵלֶב הַמְכַסֶּה אֶת־הַקֶּרֶב וְאֵת כָּל־הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַקֶּרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vehikriv mizzevach hashelamim ishheh laYHVH et-hachelev hamkhassah et-hakerev ve'et kol-hachelev asher al-hakerev.
verses.study_note: The division of the peace offering begins. God's portion — the 'ishheh' (fire-offering) — is now specified: the fat (chelev). Two categories are listed here: (1) 'hachelev hamkhassah et-hakerev' — the fat covering/enveloping the inner organs (the caul, the great omentum, a large sheet of fat that covers the abdomen), and (2) 'kol-hachelev asher al-hakerev' — all the fat upon the inner organs (the retroperitoneal fat). 'Chelev' — the designated fat — is a specific term for the rich, hard, internal fat deposits that surround the organs of digestion and reproduction, the seat of the animal's vitality and richness. This fat is reserved exclusively for God throughout Leviticus and is the category explicitly prohibited for human consumption in v.17. God claims the richest, most nourishing portion as His own — the 'best' belongs to Him.
verses.doctrinal_note: The fat (chelev) given entirely to God represents several theological truths: (1) The richest, most life-sustaining portion belongs to God — He is owed the best and the first. (2) The inner organs — seat of the animal's vitality — typify the inner life, the 'hidden man of the heart' (1 Pet 3:4). In Christ's offering, His inner life — His deepest motivations, His soul's devotion — was wholly given to the Father (John 4:34, Ps 40:8). (3) Chelev is also the word for the 'cream' or 'best' of a substance (Gen 45:18 — 'fat of the land'). God receives the cream of the sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: And the two kidneys, and the fat that is on them, which is by the flanks, and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת שְׁתֵּי הַכְּלָיֹת וְאֶת־הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עֲלֵיהֶן אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַכְּסָלִים וְאֶת־הַיֹּתֶרֶת עַל־הַכָּבֵד עַל־הַכְּלָיוֹת יְסִירֶנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et shetei hakkelayot ve'et-hachelev asher aleihen asher al-hakkeselim ve'et-hayyoteret al-hakkaved al-hakkelayot yeserenah.
verses.study_note: Three specific organs are identified as God's portion: (1) 'Shetei hakkelayot' — the two kidneys. In Hebrew thought and throughout the OT, the kidneys (kelayot) are the seat of the deepest emotions, secret motivations, and moral examination — equivalent in function to the modern understanding of the conscience or moral core (Ps 7:9, Jer 11:20, 17:10, Rev 2:23: 'I am he which searcheth the reins and hearts'). That the kidneys are burned on God's altar is profoundly significant: God receives the organ of deepest moral self-knowledge. (2) The fat upon them on the flanks. (3) 'Hayyoteret al-hakkaved' — the appendage of/above the liver (the caudate lobe, or the lobe that projects from the liver). The liver was the organ used in ancient pagan divination (Ezek 21:21) — giving this part to God instead of to pagan soothsaying is a radical repudiation of the nations' practices.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron’s sons shall burn it on the altar upon the burnt sacrifice, which is upon the wood that is on the fire: it is an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהִקְטִירוּ אֹתוֹ בְנֵי־אַהֲרֹן הַמִּזְבֵּחָה עַל־הָעֹלָה אֲשֶׁר עַל־הָעֵצִים אֲשֶׁר עַל־הָאֵשׁ אִשֵּׁה רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiktiru oto venei-Aharon hammizbeachah al-ha'olah asher al-ha'etsim asher al-ha'esh ishheh reiyach nichoach laYHVH.
verses.study_note: A critical liturgical detail: the peace offering's fat portions are burned 'upon the burnt offering' (al-ha'olah) — on top of the already-burning burnt sacrifice, on the wood, on the fire. The order is theologically mandatory. The peace offering is not presented independently but upon the foundation of the burnt offering. This establishes a permanent liturgical principle: fellowship (shelamim) is only possible upon the basis of total consecration and atonement (olah). Peace with God is never independent of His claims having been fully met. The sweet savour that arises — reiyach nichoach — is the combined fragrance of full consecration (olah) and fellowship (shelamim) ascending together. This is the grammar of reconciliation: atonement first, then fellowship.
verses.doctrinal_note: The peace offering placed 'upon the burnt offering' establishes the inviolable theological sequence in salvation: (1) the burnt offering — total propitiation, God's righteous claims fully met in Christ — must precede (2) the peace offering — fellowship, communion, the believer eating with God. There is no shortcut to peace. Cheap peace — that which bypasses the altar — is what the false prophets cried: 'Peace, peace; when there is no peace' (Jer 6:14). True shalom is founded on Calvary's altar, where the olah and shelamim of Christ were presented together.

verses.kjv_text: And if his offering for a sacrifice of peace offering unto the LORD be of the flock; male or female, he shall offer it without blemish.
verses.original_text: וְאִם מִן־הַצֹּאן קָרְבָּנוֹ לְזֶבַח שְׁלָמִים לַיהוָה זָכָר אוֹ נְקֵבָה תָּמִים יַקְרִיבֶנּוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im min-hattzon korbano lezevach shelamim laYHVH zakhar o nekevah tamim yakrivennu.
verses.study_note: The second category: the peace offering from the flock (tzon — the collective term for sheep and goats). The same dual permission — male or female — and the same absolute requirement — tamim (without blemish). The flock offering was more accessible to the common Israelite who could not afford an ox from the herd. The peace offering's openness to different economic levels — ox, lamb, goat — reflects the broad scope of the fellowship it establishes. God's table is not for the wealthy alone. The only non-negotiable is perfection of the offering — but the size and cost of the animal varies. The poorest Israelite can still bring a perfect goat to God's fellowship table.

verses.kjv_text: If he offer a lamb for his offering, then shall he offer it before the LORD.
verses.original_text: אִם־כֶּבֶשׂ הוּא מַקְרִיב אֶת־קָרְבָּנוֹ וְהִקְרִיב אֹתוֹ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Im-kheves hu makriv et-korbano vehikriv oto lifnei YHVH.
verses.study_note: The lamb ('keves' — the domestic sheep) is introduced as the first specific animal from the flock. Throughout Scripture the lamb carries the preeminent sacrificial significance: the Passover lamb (Exod 12), the daily morning and evening lamb (Exod 29:38-42), the Suffering Servant as 'a lamb to the slaughter' (Isa 53:7), and ultimately John the Baptist's identification of Jesus as 'the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world' (John 1:29). The lamb brought 'before the LORD' (lifnei YHVH) — into the divine presence — is the picture of the sinner's substitute being presented at the throne of God. The simplest statement of the peace offering: a lamb, brought before the LORD.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall lay his hand upon the head of his offering, and kill it before the tabernacle of the congregation: and Aaron’s sons shall sprinkle the blood thereof round about upon the altar.
verses.original_text: וְסָמַךְ אֶת־יָדוֹ עַל־רֹאשׁ קָרְבָּנוֹ וְשָׁחַט אֹתוֹ לִפְנֵי אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְזָרְקוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן אֶת־דָּמוֹ עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamakh et-yado al-rosh korbano veshachat oto lifnei ohel mo'ed vezarku benei Aharon et-damo al-hammizbeach saviv.
verses.study_note: The identical ritual sequence as v.2: hand-laying, slaughter, blood dashed around the altar. The repetition is deliberate — whatever the animal, the ritual logic is the same. Identification-substitution-blood application: these three acts are the irreducible minimum of biblical sacrifice. The repetition across all three animal types (ox, lamb, goat) teaches that the ritual form is not incidental but essential — the liturgical precision reflects the precision required in the antitype. Christ's atonement was not improvisational but carefully ordained: He was crucified in the 'determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God' (Acts 2:23).

verses.kjv_text: And he shall offer of the sacrifice of the peace offering an offering made by fire unto the LORD; the fat thereof, and the whole rump, it shall he take off hard by the backbone; and the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards,
verses.original_text: וְהִקְרִיב מִזֶּבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה חֶלְבּוֹ הָאַלְיָה תְמִימָה לְעֻמַּת הֶעָצֶה יְסִירֶנָּה וְאֶת־הַחֵלֶב הַמְכַסֶּה אֶת־הַקֶּרֶב וְאֵת כָּל־הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַקֶּרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vehikriv mizzevach hashelamim ishheh laYHVH chelbo ha'alyah temimah le'ummat he'atzeh yeserenah ve'et-hachelev hamkhassah et-hakerev ve'et kol-hachelev asher al-hakerev.
verses.study_note: For the lamb specifically, a unique addition: 'ha'alyah temimah' — the whole fat tail. The 'alyah' is the broad, fatty tail of the Oriental fat-tailed sheep (Awassi), which was — and remains — one of the prized delicacies of the ancient Near East. This fatty tail could weigh 8-15 pounds and was considered among the richest portions of the animal. Its explicit, wholeness-specific inclusion ('temimah' — whole, entire, perfect, the same word as 'without blemish') alongside the internal fat deposits signals that for the lamb, an additional sign of the richest and best is given to God. The word 'temimah' here connects the wholeness of the fat tail to the wholeness required of the animal — the same root, applied to the portion given to God. God receives what is whole and perfect.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'alyah' (fat tail) offered whole and entire deepens the typological portrait: in Christ's offering, nothing of the richest and best was withheld. The Father 'spared not his own Son, but delivered him up for us all' (Rom 8:32). The entire 'fat' — all the richness of Christ's sinless humanity, the fullness of His inner devotion, His boundless love — was placed on the altar. Nothing held back. 'Temimah' — whole.

verses.kjv_text: And the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them, which is by the flanks, and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת שְׁתֵּי הַכְּלָיֹת וְאֶת־הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עֲלֵיהֶן אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַכְּסָלִים וְאֶת־הַיֹּתֶרֶת עַל־הַכָּבֵד עַל־הַכְּלָיוֹת יְסִירֶנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et shetei hakkelayot ve'et-hachelev asher aleihen asher al-hakkeselim ve'et-hayyoteret al-hakkaved al-hakkelayot yeserenah.
verses.study_note: The identical specification as the ox offering (v.4): two kidneys, fat upon the flanks, and the lobe of the liver. The word-for-word repetition across ox and lamb is deliberate — the organs of deepest interior life (kidneys) and vital force (liver) are given to God regardless of which animal is offered. The consistency across species underlines the universal principle: God always receives the innermost, hidden depths of what is offered to Him. No sacrifice before the LORD is merely surface-level. God searches and receives what lies deepest.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall burn it upon the altar: it is the food of the offering made by fire unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהִקְטִירוֹ הַכֹּהֵן הַמִּזְבֵּחָה לֶחֶם אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiktiro hakkohen hammizbeachah lechem ishheh laYHVH.
verses.study_note: A phrase that appears here for the first time in the peace offering: 'lechem ishheh laYHVH' — the food/bread of the fire-offering of the LORD. The word 'lechem' (bread, food, sustenance) applied to the altar portions is theologically charged. The altar is depicted as the LORD's table; the fat portions are His food. This anthropomorphic language is deliberate — God is presented as the host of a meal, receiving His portion while His guests (offerer and priest) also receive theirs. The peace offering is, at its heart, a shared meal — a covenant banquet in which God, priest, and offerer eat together around the altar. 'Lechem' later becomes the term used for the showbread ('lechem hapanim' — bread of the Presence), and ultimately points to the one who said 'I am the bread of life' (John 6:35).
verses.doctrinal_note: The concept of the altar as God's table, with the fat as His 'lechem' (food), establishes the theological background for the Lord's Supper. In the peace offering's covenant meal, God and His people eat together — a fellowship that was broken by sin and restored by sacrifice. The NT fulfillment is the Eucharist: 'The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the communion of the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, is it not the communion of the body of Christ?' (1 Cor 10:16). Christ is simultaneously the sacrifice and the bread — the one offered and the one in whom we feast.

verses.kjv_text: And if his offering be a goat, then he shall offer it before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְאִם־עֵז קָרְבָּנוֹ וְהִקְרִיב אֹתוֹ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-ez korbano vehikriv oto lifnei YHVH.
verses.study_note: The third and final animal for the peace offering: the goat (ez). The goat represents the most accessible tier of the peace offering — less costly than an ox or even a young lamb, the goat was within reach of ordinary Israelite households. That God accepts the peace offering of a goat with the identical ceremony and the identical divine declaration ('sweet savour,' v.16) as the peace offering of an ox underscores that the value of the offering is not in the monetary worth of the animal but in its tamim (perfection) and the heart behind it. The goat — later used in the Day of Atonement (Lev 16) as the scapegoat — is here the vehicle of the most joyful offering: peace and fellowship. The same animal that will bear Israel's sins away from the camp will also be the means of their fellowship with God.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall lay his hand upon the head of it, and kill it before the tabernacle of the congregation: and the sons of Aaron shall sprinkle the blood thereof upon the altar round about.
verses.original_text: וְסָמַךְ אֶת־יָדוֹ עַל־רֹאשׁוֹ וְשָׁחַט אֹתוֹ לִפְנֵי אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְזָרְקוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן אֶת־דָּמוֹ עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamakh et-yado al-rosho veshachat oto lifnei ohel mo'ed vezarku benei Aharon et-damo al-hammizbeach saviv.
verses.study_note: The third repetition of the identical ritual sequence: hand-laying, slaughter before the tabernacle, blood dashed all around the altar. The triple repetition across all three animal types (vv.2, 8, 13) is the literary and liturgical heartbeat of Leviticus 3 — whatever the animal, whatever the worshiper's economic means, the blood must be applied. No peace is possible without the blood. The blood is not supplementary to the peace offering — it is its essential foundation. Before the covenant meal can be enjoyed, before the fellowship portions can be distributed, before anyone eats — the blood is applied to the altar. The order of Levitical worship is always: blood first, then bread.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall offer thereof his offering, even an offering made by fire unto the LORD; the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards,
verses.original_text: וְהִקְרִיב מִמֶּנּוּ קָרְבָּנוֹ אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה אֶת־הַחֵלֶב הַמְכַסֶּה אֶת־הַקֶּרֶב וְאֵת כָּל־הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַקֶּרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vehikriv mimmennu korbano ishheh laYHVH et-hachelev hamkhassah et-hakerev ve'et kol-hachelev asher al-hakerev.
verses.study_note: The fat portions of the goat are identical to those of the ox and the lamb: the covering fat of the inner organs and all fat upon the inner organs. The triple repetition of the same fat portions across all three animals (vv.3-4, 9-10, 14-15) is not liturgical laziness but intentional theological drumbeat: the principle holds constant regardless of the animal. God always receives the fat. Human fellowship around God's table is always predicated on the richest portion having first been given to Him. The consistent designation of the fat as God's portion, repeated three times with three animals, has the force of an unconditional covenant statute — which is exactly how v.17 formalizes it.

verses.kjv_text: And the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them, which is by the flanks, and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת שְׁתֵּי הַכְּלָיֹת וְאֶת־הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עֲלֵיהֶן אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַכְּסָלִים וְאֶת־הַיֹּתֶרֶת עַל־הַכָּבֵד עַל־הַכְּלָיוֹת יְסִירֶנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et shetei hakkelayot ve'et-hachelev asher aleihen asher al-hakkeselim ve'et-hayyoteret al-hakkaved al-hakkelayot yeserenah.
verses.study_note: The third verbatim repetition of the kidneys and liver-lobe specification. This exact phrase appears identically in vv.4, 10, and 15 — a triple anchoring of the principle. In Hebrew rhetoric, triple repetition indicates absolute, immovable certainty. What is stated three times in the same words is beyond dispute. The 'kidneys and liver' given to God three times over declares: the organ of conscience (kidneys) and the organ of life-force (liver) belong to the LORD in every sacrificial context — regardless of species, regardless of the worshiper's station. God searches the inward parts (Ps 139:13, Jer 11:20) and receives them at the altar.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall burn them upon the altar: it is the food of the offering made by fire for a sweet savour: all the fat is the LORD’s.
verses.original_text: וְהִקְטִירָם הַכֹּהֵן הַמִּזְבֵּחָה לֶחֶם אִשֶּׁה לְרֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ כָּל־חֵלֶב לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiktram hakkohen hammizbeachah lechem ishheh lereiyach nichoach kol-chelev laYHVH.
verses.study_note: The climactic statement of the peace offering legislation: 'kol-chelev laYHVH' — ALL the fat is the LORD's. This short, absolute declaration is the theological summit of chapter 3. 'Kol' — all, every bit — 'chelev' — the fat, the rich, the best — 'laYHVH' — belongs to the LORD. No exceptions, no portions retained, no sharing of this category. The burnt offering belonged entirely to God; the sin offering would have specific priestly portions; but in every sacrifice, every piece of fat — without exception — belongs to the LORD alone. This absolute claim precedes the formal prohibition in v.17 and provides its theological grounding: the prohibition against eating fat is not arbitrary health legislation but a theological declaration of divine ownership.
verses.doctrinal_note: The declaration 'all the fat is the LORD's' has a New Covenant parallel in Paul's exhortation: 'Ye are not your own... therefore glorify God in your body, and in your spirit, which are God's' (1 Cor 6:19-20). The fat — the richness, the best, the vitality — belongs to God. The believer is to offer the 'fat' of their life, not the leftovers. The peace offering enjoyed by priest and worshiper is made possible precisely because the fat has first been given away — to the LORD. The joy of the fellowship table is possible only because the best has been placed on the altar.

verses.kjv_text: It shall be a perpetual statute for your generations throughout all your dwellings, that ye eat neither fat nor blood.
verses.original_text: חֻקַּת עוֹלָם לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם בְּכֹל מֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם כָּל־חֵלֶב וְכָל־דָּם לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Chukkat olam ledoroteikhem bekhol moshevoteikhem kol-chelev vekhol-dam lo tokheilu.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with one of the most emphatic formulas in the Pentateuch: 'chukkat olam' — a perpetual statute, an everlasting ordinance. The double prohibition encompasses the two elements that God has claimed as His own throughout Leviticus 3: (1) 'Kol-chelev' — no fat to be eaten. The fat belongs to God (v.16); to eat it is to rob God of what is His. The Israelite eating fat was transgressing the divine ownership declared at every altar. (2) 'Vekhol-dam' — no blood to be eaten. The blood prohibition is even more fundamental — given first in Noah's covenant (Gen 9:4) and grounded theologically in Leviticus 17:11: 'the life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it to you upon the altar to make atonement for your souls.' Blood is life; life belongs to God; to eat blood is to consume what atones. The two prohibitions together guard the two divine claims that frame all sacrificial worship: God receives the fat (the richest of the flesh) and the blood (the life of the flesh). 'In all your dwellings' — not merely in the sanctuary but in every home, in every meal, in every generation. The altar's theology governs the kitchen table.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'perpetual statute' (chukkat olam) closing Leviticus 3 is one of the most important legislative phrases in the Torah — used of Sabbath, Passover, Day of Atonement, and the priestly garments. Its application here to the fat and blood prohibitions elevates these two dietary laws to the level of covenantal theology, not merely ritual hygiene. The NT does not explicitly repeal either prohibition (the Jerusalem Council of Acts 15:29 retains the blood prohibition for Jewish-Gentile fellowship), though the principle behind them — that blood atones and that the best belongs to God — is fulfilled in Christ. The blood of Christ is not merely symbolic but the actual, precious, sin-atoning blood that the Levitical system pointed toward (1 Pet 1:18-19, Heb 9:12). The 'chukkat olam' finds its ultimate permanence not in a dietary code but in the eternal covenant: 'This is my blood of the new testament' (Matt 26:28).

meta.overview: Leviticus 3 prescribes the Shelamim — the peace offering, or fellowship offering — the most joyful and communal of all the Levitical sacrifices. Unlike the burnt offering (ch.1), which is entirely consumed on the altar, or the grain offering (ch.2), which is shared between altar and priests, the peace offering uniquely creates a three-way fellowship meal: the fat portions and kidneys are burned on the altar for God; the breast and right shoulder are given to the priests; and the remainder of the flesh is eaten by the offerer and his household before the LORD. Three animals are permitted: an ox from the herd (vv.1-5), a lamb from the flock (vv.6-11), and a goat from the flock (vv.12-16). Each prescription follows the same liturgical pattern — laying of hands, slaughter, blood manipulation at the altar, separation and burning of the fat and inner organs. The chapter closes with an eternal statute (v.17): no fat and no blood shall be eaten, ever. The Shelamim is the offering of peace already established — it presupposes the sin offering and burnt offering and celebrates the resulting fellowship. Typologically, it points to Christ as our Peace (Eph 2:14), the one through whom the three-way communion of God, priest, and people is perfectly and permanently realized at the table of the New Covenant.
meta.theme: The Peace Offering — Fellowship, Communion & the Restoration of the Three-Way Covenant Meal
meta.keyWordMeaning: Peace offerings / Fellowship offerings — from the root 'shalom' (peace, wholeness, completeness). The plural form suggests multiple dimensions of peace: peace with God, peace within oneself, peace with one's neighbor. The offering that celebrates and enacts covenant fellowship.
outline: The Peace Offering from the Herd — An Ox (vv.1-5): Laying of hands, slaughter, blood at the altar, fat portions burned as sweet savour
The Peace Offering from the Flock — A Lamb (vv.6-11): The fat tail ('alyah') specifically included among the portions
The Peace Offering from the Flock — A Goat (vv.12-16): The identical pattern; the goat's fat covers the kidneys and liver
The Perpetual Statute (v.17): No fat and no blood shall be eaten — an everlasting covenant ordinance for all generations

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHVH el-Moshe lemor.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with a formula used over 90 times in Leviticus: 'Vaydabber YHVH el-Moshe lemor' — 'And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying.' This repeated divine address is not a literary formality but a theological statement: the entire sacrificial system is not of human invention or priestly development but of divine revelation. God initiates; Moses receives. Every detail of Levitical law — the type of animal, the blood ritual, the burning instructions — is revealed from heaven. This framing is critical for understanding the typological weight of the sacrifices: if God designed every element, every element carries divine intentionality pointing toward the ultimate fulfillment in Christ. The book of Hebrews is built on this premise: the Levitical system is the 'shadow' (skia) of the good things to come (Heb 10:1), designed by the same God who sent His Son.
verses.doctrinal_note: The divine origin of the sacrificial law is the foundation of its typological authority. If human priests invented these rites, they may point nowhere beyond themselves. But if God revealed them — as this formula insists — then they are intentional foreshadowings, designed by the Author of redemption to preview and prefigure the work of Christ. Paul's argument in Galatians, and the entire argument of Hebrews, rest on this premise: the law was given 'till the seed should come' (Gal 3:19), and its every detail was a 'schoolmaster' (paidagogos) pointing to Christ (Gal 3:24).

verses.kjv_text: Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, If a soul shall sin through ignorance against any of the commandments of the LORD concerning things which ought not to be done, and shall do against any of them:
verses.original_text: דַּבֵּר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר נֶפֶשׁ כִּי־תֶחֱטָא בִשְׁגָגָה מִכֹּל מִצְוֹת יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר לֹא תֵעָשֶׂינָה וְעָשָׂה מֵאַחַת מֵהֵנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Dabber el-benei Yisra'el lemor nefesh ki-techeta bishgagah mikkol mitzvot YHVH asher lo te'aseynah ve'asah me'achat mehennah.
verses.study_note: The governing condition for the entire chapter: 'bishgagah' — through ignorance, inadvertently, in error. The word comes from 'shagag' (to err, to go astray unintentionally, to wander). This is sin committed without deliberate intent — a violation of God's commandments that occurs through oversight, momentary weakness, insufficient knowledge, or inadvertent transgression. The critical distinction is established against Numbers 15:30: sins committed 'beyad ramah' (with a high hand — presumptuously, defiantly, in full knowledge) are a different category altogether, cut off from the congregation with no atoning sacrifice. Leviticus 4 does not excuse unintentional sin — it still requires blood. But it provides a path of restoration. The phrase 'nefesh ki-techeta' (when a soul sins) — using 'nefesh' (soul) rather than 'ish' (man) — personalizes the law: every soul, every individual person before God, is accountable and provided for.
verses.doctrinal_note: The distinction between intentional and unintentional sin in the sacrificial law illuminates the NT distinction between sins that can be forgiven and the 'sin unto death' (1 John 5:16-17). More profoundly, it teaches that even ignorance of God's law does not exempt from its consequences — sin is sin whether or not the sinner was fully aware. Paul writes: 'I was alive without the law once: but when the commandment came, sin revived, and I died' (Rom 7:9). Ignorance does not constitute innocence before a holy God. This is why the atonement of Christ covers sins committed 'in the times of this ignorance' (Acts 17:30, Rom 3:25) — His blood reached backward and forward across all generations of sinners, intentional and inadvertent alike.

verses.kjv_text: If the priest that is anointed do sin according to the sin of the people; then let him bring for his sin, which he hath sinned, a young bullock without blemish unto the LORD for a sin offering.
verses.original_text: אִם הַכֹּהֵן הַמָּשִׁיחַ יֶחֱטָא לְאַשְׁמַת הָעָם וְהִקְרִיב עַל חַטָּאתוֹ אֲשֶׁר חָטָא פַּר בֶּן־בָּקָר תָּמִים לַיהוָה לְחַטָּאת׃
verses.transliteration: Im hakkohen hammashiach yecheta le'ashmat ha'am vehikriv al chattato asher chata par ben-bakar tamim laYHVH lechattat.
verses.study_note: The highest category of offender is addressed first: 'hakkohen hammashiach' — the anointed priest. Significantly, the priest's sin is described as bringing guilt 'le'ashmat ha'am' — to/upon the guilt of the people. The anointed high priest is not a private individual — his sin affects the entire congregation, because he is their representative before God.
