This is the burden of priestly office: one person's moral failure has communal consequences. The higher the office, the farther the ripple of sin. The remedy is proportionally weighty: a 'par ben-bakar' — a young bull from the herd (the most expensive animal) — 'tamim' (without blemish). The word used for the anointed priest — 'hammashiach' — is the exact Hebrew word for 'Messiah/Anointed One.' The anointed priest who must offer for his own sin is the shadow; the Messiah who has no sin of His own and offers for others is the reality.
verses.doctrinal_note: The anointed priest's sin affecting 'the guilt of the people' is the negative counterpart of the high-priestly typology. The earthly high priest could contaminate the whole congregation by his personal sin (hence the enormous atonement machinery of Leviticus 16). Christ, the true Anointed Priest (Mashiach/Messiah), is our High Priest 'holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners' (Heb 7:26) — He carries no personal sin to contaminate us. The earthly anointed priest offers a bull for himself AND for the people (Lev 16:6,11); Christ 'needeth not daily, as those high priests, to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins, and then for the people's: for this he did once, when he offered up himself' (Heb 7:27).

verses.kjv_text: And he shall bring the bullock unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation before the LORD; and shall lay his hand upon the bullock’s head, and kill the bullock before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהֵבִיא אֶת־הַפָּר אֶל־פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וְסָמַךְ אֶת־יָדוֹ עַל־רֹאשׁ הַפָּר וְשָׁחַט אֶת־הַפָּר לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehevi et-happar el-petach ohel mo'ed lifnei YHVH vesamakh et-yado al-rosh happar veshachat et-happar lifnei YHVH.
verses.study_note: The sin offering ritual opens with the same foundational acts as the peace offering (ch.3): hand-laying and slaughter at the tabernacle door. But in the sin offering the significance of 'samakh' (laying with full weight) is intensified — this is not merely identification with an animal but the transference of guilt. The priest's own sin, in all its weight and gravity, is pressed upon the animal's head. Then the priest himself — the sinner — performs the slaughter ('veshachat et-happar lifnei YHVH' — and shall kill the bull before the LORD). The priest is simultaneously penitent and executioner: he lays his guilt on the substitute and then kills it. This is the most vivid picture in the OT of personal, costly engagement with the price of sin. The offerer does not hire someone else to feel the weight; he presses it down with his own hand and draws the knife himself.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest that is anointed shall take of the bullock’s blood, and bring it to the tabernacle of the congregation:
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח הַכֹּהֵן הַמָּשִׁיחַ מִדַּם הַפָּר וְהֵבִיא אֹתוֹ אֶל־אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Velakach hakkohen hammashiach middam happar vehevi oto el-ohel mo'ed.
verses.study_note: Now the anointed priest's unique role begins. After the slaughter, the priest himself takes the blood — 'middam happar' (of the blood of the bull) — and carries it INTO the tabernacle. For the common person's sin (vv.27-35) and a ruler's sin (vv.22-26), the blood stays at the outer altar. But for the anointed priest's sin and the congregation's sin (the two highest categories), the blood must penetrate deeper — into the Holy Place itself. This escalation of blood-application depth is the governing structural principle of Leviticus 4: the gravity of the sin offering is measured by how far the blood must travel into the sanctuary. The high priest carries his own blood deeper than anyone else — a principle fulfilled by Christ who 'entered in once into the holy place, having obtained eternal redemption' (Heb 9:12), carrying His own blood all the way to the heavenly mercy seat.
verses.doctrinal_note: The blood traveling deeper into the sanctuary for higher offenders establishes the typological principle that Christ's atonement penetrated to the deepest possible place — not the Holy Place of a tent but the heaven of heavens itself. 'Christ is not entered into the holy places made with hands, which are the figures of the true; but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God for us' (Heb 9:24). The anointed priest carrying blood into the Holy Place is the figure; Christ carrying His own precious blood into the very presence of the Father is the eternal reality.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall dip his finger in the blood, and sprinkle of the blood seven times before the LORD, before the vail of the sanctuary.
verses.original_text: וְטָבַל הַכֹּהֵן אֶת־אֶצְבָּעוֹ בַּדָּם וְהִזָּה מִן־הַדָּם שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים לִפְנֵי יְהוָה אֶת־פְּנֵי פָּרֹכֶת הַקֹּדֶשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Vetaval hakkohen et-etzba'o baddam vehizzah min-haddam sheva pe'amim lifnei YHVH et-penei farokhet hakkodesh.
verses.study_note: Two precise, prescribed acts: (1) 'Taval' — dip the finger in the blood. The finger (etzba) is the instrument of God's own power ('the finger of God' in Exod 8:19, 31:18) — here the priest's finger, as God's representative, applies the atoning blood. (2) 'Hizzah sheva pe'amim' — sprinkle seven times before the veil (parokhet). 'Sheva' (seven) throughout Scripture is the number of completeness, divine fullness, and covenantal perfection. The blood is not sprinkled once or twice but seven times — a complete, full, thorough application before the most holy barrier in Israel's worship. The 'parokhet' (veil) was the great curtain separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place, from the Ark and the mercy seat. To sprinkle blood before this veil is to bring the atonement as close to the divine Presence as the Levitical system allows. At the cross, this veil was 'rent in twain from the top to the bottom' (Matt 27:51) — the barrier the sin offering blood could only approach has been permanently removed.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sevenfold sprinkling before the veil is the most proximate application of blood in the regular Levitical calendar (the Day of Atonement goes beyond — Lev 16:14-15). The veil was the barrier between humanity and God's immediate Presence. The blood sprinkled seven times before it declares: atonement is complete (seven = fullness), and it is presented at the very boundary of God's holiness. The rending of this veil at Christ's death (Matt 27:51) is the antitype: the blood of the sin offering has been fully applied, the barrier is abolished, and the way into the Holiest is opened for all who believe (Heb 10:19-20).

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall put some of the blood upon the horns of the altar of sweet incense before the LORD, which is in the tabernacle of the congregation; and shall pour all the blood of the bullock at the bottom of the altar of the burnt offering, which is at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation.
verses.original_text: וְנָתַן הַכֹּהֵן מִן־הַדָּם עַל־קַרְנוֹת מִזְבַּח קְטֹרֶת הַסַּמִּים לִפְנֵי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר בְּאֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְאֵת כָּל־דַּם הַפָּר יִשְׁפֹּךְ אֶל־יְסוֹד מִזְבַּח הָעֹלָה אֲשֶׁר פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Venatan hakkohen min-haddam al-karnot mizbach kettoret hassammim lifnei YHVH asher be'ohel mo'ed ve'et kol-dam happar yishpokh el-yesod mizbach ha'olah asher petach ohel mo'ed.
verses.study_note: Two further blood applications: (1) Blood put on the 'karnot' (horns) of the incense altar — the golden altar inside the Holy Place where incense was burned daily, the altar closest to the veil and the Ark. The 'horns' of the altar are its four projecting upper corners, the points of maximum strength and power. Blood on the horns of the incense altar is blood applied at the intersection of prayer and holiness — the incense altar is where Israel's prayers rose to God (Ps 141:2, Rev 8:3-4). The sin offering's blood applied here declares that the path of prayer has been opened through atonement. (2) All remaining blood is 'poured out' (yishpokh) at the 'yesod' — the base/foundation — of the burnt offering altar. The Hebrew 'yesod' means foundation, base, ground. The blood is poured not sprinkled — the entire remainder is emptied out at the very foundation of the altar. Blood at the foundation: atonement is the ground upon which all worship stands.
verses.doctrinal_note: The two blood applications — on the incense altar's horns (prayer) and at the burnt offering altar's base (atonement) — together declare that both communion (prayer/incense) and consecration (the olah/burnt offering) rest entirely on the foundation of blood atonement. There is no prayer that reaches God and no consecration He accepts apart from the blood of the chattat. In NT terms: Christ's high-priestly intercession ('ever liveth to make intercession' — Heb 7:25) and our total consecration to God (Rom 12:1) are both founded on and enabled by His atoning blood.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall take off from it all the fat of the bullock for the sin offering; the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards,
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־כָּל־חֵלֶב פַּר הַחַטָּאת יָרִים מִמֶּנּוּ אֶת־הַחֵלֶב הַמְכַסֶּה עַל־הַקֶּרֶב וְאֵת כָּל־הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַקֶּרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-kol-chelev par hachattat yarim mimmennu et-hachelev hamkhassah al-hakerev ve'et kol-hachelev asher al-hakerev.
verses.study_note: The sin offering shares the fat-removal specification with the peace offering (Lev 3:3): the covering fat of the inner organs and all fat upon the inner organs. The identical fat portions given to God in the peace offering are given to Him in the sin offering as well. The principle established in Lev 3:16 — 'all the fat is the LORD's' — applies across all offerings. Even in the offering for sin, even in the darkest liturgical moment when guilt is being confessed and blood is being shed, God still receives the richest portions. Sin does not change the nature of what belongs to God. The fat is His in celebration (peace offering) and in confession (sin offering) alike.

verses.kjv_text: And the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them, which is by the flanks, and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away,
verses.original_text: וְאֵת שְׁתֵּי הַכְּלָיֹת וְאֶת־הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עֲלֵיהֶן אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַכְּסָלִים וְאֶת־הַיֹּתֶרֶת עַל־הַכָּבֵד עַל־הַכְּלָיוֹת יְסִירֶנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et shetei hakkelayot ve'et-hachelev asher aleihen asher al-hakkeselim ve'et-hayyoteret al-hakkaved al-hakkelayot yeserenah.
verses.study_note: The two kidneys and liver-lobe — verbatim from Leviticus 3:4, 10, 15 — are removed and given to God. As noted in Leviticus 3, the kidneys are the seat of deepest conscience and moral life in Hebrew thought (Ps 7:9, Jer 17:10). In the sin offering, the organ of conscience is placed upon the altar. This is profoundly appropriate: the sin offering is precisely the offering of conscience — the recognition that one's innermost moral life has fallen short of God's standard. The inner life that sinned is symbolically represented at the altar. The God who searches the kidneys (Rev 2:23) receives them in the sin offering — the acknowledgment that nothing is hidden from Him.

verses.kjv_text: As it was taken off from the bullock of the sacrifice of peace offerings: and the priest shall burn them upon the altar of the burnt offering.
verses.original_text: כַּאֲשֶׁר יוּרַם מִשּׁוֹר זֶבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים וְהִקְטִירָם הַכֹּהֵן עַל מִזְבַּח הָעֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ka'asher yuram mishshor zevach hashelamim vehiktram hakkohen al mizbach ha'olah.
verses.study_note: The fat and organs of the sin offering's bull are treated exactly as those of the peace offering's bull (Lev 3:3-5): burned on the altar of the burnt offering. The explicit cross-reference to the peace offering ('as it was taken off from the bullock of the sacrifice of peace offerings') is deliberate: the Levitical system is internally unified. The same portions given to God in fellowship are given to God in atonement. The parallel declares that atonement and fellowship share the same anatomy — they are two expressions of the same fundamental truth that the best belongs to God, in joy and in penitence alike.

verses.kjv_text: And the skin of the bullock, and all his flesh, with his head, and with his legs, and his inwards, and his dung,
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־עוֹר הַפָּר וְאֶת־כָּל־בְּשָׂרוֹ עַל־רֹאשׁוֹ וְעַל־כְּרָעָיו וְקִרְבּוֹ וּפִרְשׁוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-or happar ve'et-kol-besaro al-rosho ve'al-kera'av vekirbo ufarsho.
verses.study_note: What is not burned on the altar — everything except the fat and organs — is removed: the skin (or), all the flesh (kol-besaro), the head, the legs, the inwards (inner organs), and even the dung/offal (pirsho). Everything the fire-offering does not consume is to be removed and dealt with separately. The completeness of the list — skin to dung — is striking and important. Nothing of the carcass remains at the sanctuary. This thoroughness anticipates v.12: all this material must be carried outside the camp to be burned. The very completeness of the list prepares the reader for the profoundly significant act of v.12.
verses.doctrinal_note: The removal of everything — including the dung — outside the camp carries a startling typological implication. Hebrews 13:11-12 makes this the explicit basis of its appeal: 'For the bodies of those beasts, whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin, are burned without the camp. Wherefore Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate.' Christ became 'a curse for us' (Gal 3:13), bearing not only the penalty of sin but its shame and defilement — carried 'outside the camp' where the dung was burned. The sin offering's complete disposal of the carcass pictures Christ bearing the totality of sin's defilement.

verses.kjv_text: Even the whole bullock shall he carry forth without the camp unto a clean place, where the ashes are poured out, and burn him on the wood with fire: where the ashes are poured out shall he be burnt.
verses.original_text: וְהוֹצִיא אֶת־כָּל־הַפָּר אֶל־מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה אֶל־מָקוֹם טָהוֹר אֶל־שֶׁפֶךְ הַדֶּשֶׁן וְשָׂרַף אֹתוֹ עַל־עֵצִים בָּאֵשׁ עַל־שֶׁפֶךְ הַדֶּשֶׁן יִשָּׂרֵף׃
verses.transliteration: Vehotzi et-kol-happar el-michuts lamachaneh el-makom tahor el-shefekh hadddeshen vesaraf oto al-etsim ba'esh al-shefekh haddeshen yissaref.
verses.study_note: 'El-michuts lamachaneh' — outside the camp. This is one of the most theologically charged geographical designations in the entire Levitical system. The camp of Israel was the domain of the covenant community, the place of God's dwelling, the holy sphere. To be sent 'outside the camp' was to be removed from the covenant community, associated with defilement and exclusion (Lev 13:46 — the leper; Num 5:3; 15:35-36 — the Sabbath-breaker executed). The burned carcass is taken to a 'makom tahor' (a clean place) — the ash-heap — and burned entirely with fire on wood. The complete burning outside the camp of the sin offering whose blood was brought into the sanctuary creates the most precise Levitical type of the crucifixion: Christ's blood was brought into the heavenly sanctuary (Heb 9:12) while His body suffered the full shame of crucifixion 'outside the gate' of Jerusalem — outside the camp.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is the specific OT text that Hebrews 13:11-13 applies to Christ: 'Let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp, bearing his reproach.' The Christian's call to 'go outside the camp' is a call to bear the same exclusion, shame, and identification with the rejected One that the sin offering's disposal pictures. Jerusalem 'outside the gate' was outside the camp of the holy city — the place of shame, cursing, and death. Christ went there willingly, bearing both the blood (atoning) and the dung (degradation) of the sin offering. This is the full depth of 2 Cor 5:21: He became sin, not merely a sin-bearer.

verses.kjv_text: And if the whole congregation of Israel sin through ignorance, and the thing be hid from the eyes of the assembly, and they have done somewhat against any of the commandments of the LORD concerning things which should not be done, and are guilty;
verses.original_text: וְאִם כָּל־עֲדַת יִשְׂרָאֵל יִשְׁגּוּ וְנֶעְלַם דָּבָר מֵעֵינֵי הַקָּהָל וְעָשׂוּ אַחַת מִכָּל־מִצְוֹת יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר לֹא־תֵעָשֶׂינָה וְאָשֵׁמוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im kol-adat Yisra'el yishgu vene'elam davar me'einei hakkuhal ve'asu achat mikkol-mitzvot YHVH asher lo-te'aseynah ve'ashemu.
verses.study_note: The second category: 'kol-adat Yisra'el' — the entire congregation of Israel. The phrase 'vene'elam davar me'einei hakkuhal' — 'the thing is hidden from the eyes of the assembly' — describes corporate, communal blindness to sin. The congregation has sinned as a body, and the sin has not been recognized. 'Ve'ashemu' — they bear guilt. Corporate guilt is as real as individual guilt: a society, a community, a nation can be collectively guilty before God even when no individual has acted with full personal knowledge. This is the foundation for national days of repentance, for corporate confession, and for the principle that the sins of fathers visit children (Exod 20:5). The congregation's sin offering requires the same costly sacrifice as the anointed priest's — a young bull — recognizing that collective sin is not lesser sin.

verses.kjv_text: When the sin, which they have sinned against it, is known, then the congregation shall offer a young bullock for the sin, and bring him before the tabernacle of the congregation.
verses.original_text: וְנוֹדְעָה הַחַטָּאת אֲשֶׁר חָטְאוּ עָלֶיהָ וְהִקְרִיבוּ הַקָּהָל פַּר בֶּן־בָּקָר לְחַטָּאת וְהֵבִיאוּ אֹתוֹ לִפְנֵי אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Venoda'ah hachatta't asher chat'u aleha vehikrivu hakkuhal par ben-bakar lechattat vehevi'u oto lifnei ohel mo'ed.
verses.study_note: 'Venoda'ah' — when it becomes known. The sin that was hidden (v.13) must be acknowledged before atonement can be made. Discovery of hidden sin is not a disaster but a mercy — the exposure of what was concealed is the necessary first step toward restoration. The congregation brings the same offering as the anointed priest: a young bull (par ben-bakar). The equal weight of offering for priest and congregation reinforces the theological truth that corporate sin is no less serious than individual priestly sin. The community as a whole bears the burden of its collective failures before God.

verses.kjv_text: And the elders of the congregation shall lay their hands upon the head of the bullock before the LORD: and the bullock shall be killed before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְסָמְכוּ זִקְנֵי הָעֵדָה אֶת־יְדֵיהֶם עַל־רֹאשׁ הַפָּר לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וְשָׁחַט אֶת־הַפָּר לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamkhu ziknei ha'edah et-yedeihem al-rosh happar lifnei YHVH veshachat et-happar lifnei YHVH.
verses.study_note: For the congregational sin offering, it is the 'ziknei ha'edah' — the elders of the congregation — who perform the hand-laying (samakh). The elders act as the representative heads of the community, laying hands collectively ('yedeihem' — their hands, plural) on the bull's head. This plural hand-laying is unique in the sacrificial system — elsewhere one person lays one hand. The corporate nature of the sin requires a corporate act of identification and transference. The elders, as the community's appointed representatives, identify the entire congregation with the substitute and transfer the communal guilt.
verses.doctrinal_note: The elders laying plural hands on the sacrifice pictures the NT principle of representative solidarity in both sin and atonement. 'In Adam all die' (1 Cor 15:22) — corporate guilt through the representative head. 'In Christ shall all be made alive' — corporate justification through the new representative head. Paul's argument in Romans 5:12-21 is the explicit NT unfolding of this Levitical logic: one man's sin brought condemnation on all; one Man's righteousness brings justification on all who are identified with Him by faith. The multiple hands of the elders on the single substitute prefigure the entire humanity of the elect identified with Christ's one atoning sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest that is anointed shall bring of the bullock’s blood to the tabernacle of the congregation:
verses.original_text: וְהֵבִיא הַכֹּהֵן הַמָּשִׁיחַ מִדַּם הַפָּר אֶל־אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Vehevi hakkohen hammashiach middam happar el-ohel mo'ed.
verses.study_note: Even for the congregation's sin, it is the anointed priest who carries the blood into the tabernacle — not the elders who laid hands. The act of identification and transference (hand-laying) is communal; the act of atonement (blood-presentation) is priestly. This division of function is theologically precise: the community owns the need and participates in identifying with the sacrifice, but the mediation of that sacrifice's blood before God is the unique prerogative of the priest. No one enters the holy places on their own behalf; a mediator is necessary. 'There is one God, and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus' (1 Tim 2:5).

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall dip his finger in some of the blood, and sprinkle it seven times before the LORD, even before the vail.
verses.original_text: וְטָבַל הַכֹּהֵן אֶצְבָּעוֹ מִן־הַדָּם וְהִזָּה שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים לִפְנֵי יְהוָה אֶת פְּנֵי הַפָּרֹכֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Vetaval hakkohen etzba'o min-haddam vehizzah sheva pe'amim lifnei YHVH et penei happarokhet.
verses.study_note: Verbatim repetition of v.6 — the identical blood rite for the congregation as for the anointed priest: dip the finger, sprinkle seven times before the veil. The equality of the ritual for priest and congregation is significant: the corporate sin of the entire nation requires the same depth of atonement as the personal sin of the highest individual. No corporate sin is cheaper to atone than a personal sin. The full sevenfold blood application before the veil is needed in both cases. This equality before the veil testifies to the equality of all before the holiness of God — there is no preferential treatment at the boundary of divine Presence.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall put some of the blood upon the horns of the altar which is before the LORD, that is in the tabernacle of the congregation, and shall pour out all the blood at the bottom of the altar of the burnt offering, which is at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation.
verses.original_text: וּמִן־הַדָּם יִתֵּן עַל־קַרְנֹת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ אֲשֶׁר לִפְנֵי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר בְּאֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְאֵת כָּל־הַדָּם יִשְׁפֹּךְ אֶל־יְסוֹד מִזְבַּח הָעֹלָה אֲשֶׁר פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Umin-haddam yitten al-karnot hammizbeach asher lifnei YHVH asher be'ohel mo'ed ve'et kol-haddam yishpokh el-yesod mizbach ha'olah asher petach ohel mo'ed.
verses.study_note: Again verbatim with v.7 for the anointed priest: blood on the horns of the incense altar inside the Holy Place, and all remaining blood poured at the base of the outer burnt offering altar. The word-for-word repetition across the priest's offering (v.7) and the congregation's offering (v.18) is not editorial carelessness but structural theology: the sin of the leader and the sin of the community require identical atonement pathways. The same blood must travel the same distance, reach the same altar horns, be poured at the same foundation. God makes no distinction in the process of atonement based on the identity of the sinner — what matters is that the blood reaches the right places in the right way.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall take all his fat from him, and burn it upon the altar.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת כָּל־חֶלְבּוֹ יָרִים מִמֶּנּוּ וְהִקְטִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et kol-chelbo yarim mimmennu vehiktir hammizbeachah.
verses.study_note: All the fat is removed and burned on the altar — as in vv.8-10 for the anointed priest's offering, and as in the peace offering of Leviticus 3. The brevity here (compared to the fuller specification in vv.8-10) is because the pattern has been established; the reader understands. The fat portions are consistently God's portion across all sacrifices. The economy of the text reflects the economy of the principle: by this point in Leviticus, 'he shall burn the fat' requires no elaboration. The principle is settled.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall do with the bullock as he did with the bullock for a sin offering, so shall he do with this: and the priest shall make an atonement for them, and it shall be forgiven them.
verses.original_text: וְעָשָׂה לַפָּר כַּאֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה לְפַר הַחַטָּאת כֵּן יַעֲשֶׂה־לּוֹ וְכִפֶּר עֲלֵהֶם הַכֹּהֵן וְנִסְלַח לָהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asah lappar ka'asher asah lefar hachattat ken ya'aseh-lo vekhipper aleihem hakkohen venislach lahem.
verses.study_note: The first 'vekhipper... venislach' formula in Leviticus 4 — and it rings like a bell: 'And the priest shall make atonement for them, AND IT SHALL BE FORGIVEN THEM.' Two words that together constitute the most merciful declaration in the Levitical law: 'kipper' (to make atonement, to cover) and 'salach' (to forgive, to pardon). The word 'salach' is used exclusively of divine forgiveness — only God 'salach.' No human being, king, or prophet 'salach' sins. When the priest makes atonement (kipper — he performs the ritual), God grants forgiveness (salach — He acts sovereignly). The division of roles is absolute: the priest does his part precisely; God does His part freely. The result is certain: 'venislach lahem' — it SHALL be forgiven them. Not 'may be,' not 'could be' — shall be. The atonement guarantees the forgiveness.
verses.doctrinal_note: The formula 'venislach lahem' (it shall be forgiven them) establishes the certainty and completeness of Levitical atonement — and therefore the certainty and completeness of Christ's atonement which the Levitical system prefigures. When Christ accomplished the antitype of every chattat in His one offering, the forgiveness pronounced is equally certain and complete: 'their sins and iniquities will I remember no more' (Heb 10:17). The distinction between 'kipper' (the ritual act of the priest) and 'salach' (the sovereign act of God) maps onto the NT distinction between the historical act of Christ on the cross and the ongoing application of that forgiveness by the Father — two sides of the one transaction of redemption.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall carry forth the bullock without the camp, and burn him as he burned the first bullock: it is a sin offering for the congregation.
verses.original_text: וְהוֹצִיא אֶת־הַפָּר אֶל־מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה וְשָׂרַף אֹתוֹ כַּאֲשֶׁר שָׂרַף אֵת הַפָּר הָרִאשׁוֹן חַטַּאת הַקָּהָל הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vehotzi et-happar el-michuts lamachaneh vesaraf oto ka'asher saraf et-happar harishon chattat hakkuhal hu.
verses.study_note: The congregation's bull is also carried 'outside the camp' (el-michuts lamachaneh) and burned, exactly as the anointed priest's bull (v.12). The disposal 'outside the camp' is explicitly confirmed for the congregational offering, reinforcing the principle established in v.12: the sin offering, whose blood enters the sanctuary, must have its body burned outside the holy precincts. The two movements — blood in, carcass out — are the two directions of the sin offering's typological force: atonement penetrates the holiest place while the full weight of sin and its shame is borne outside.

verses.kjv_text: When a ruler hath sinned, and done somewhat through ignorance against any of the commandments of the LORD his God concerning things which should not be done, and is guilty;
verses.original_text: אֲשֶׁר נָשִׂיא יֶחֱטָא וְעָשָׂה אַחַת מִכָּל־מִצְוֹת יְהוָה אֱלֹהָיו אֲשֶׁר לֹא תֵעָשֶׂינָה בִשְׁגָגָה וְאָשֵׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Asher nasi yecheta ve'asah achat mikkol-mitzvot YHVH Elohav asher lo te'aseynah bishgagah ve'ashem.
verses.study_note: The third category: 'nasi' — a ruler, prince, or tribal chieftain. The word 'nasi' designates a leader within the community — below the high priest but above the common person. The text shifts significantly here in its formula: instead of 'If (im)... then' (used for priest and congregation), it uses the relative 'asher' — 'when/in that' a ruler sins. The grammatical shift suggests this case is treated as a regular occurrence, not a hypothetical. Rulers sin; this is expected. The offering required drops from a bull (priest/congregation) to a 'se'ir izzim' (male goat — v.23): the offering diminishes with the office. But notice that the law for the ruler still names God as 'YHVH Elohav' — 'the LORD his God.' The ruler is under the same divine authority as the people he leads, accountable to the same God.

verses.kjv_text: Or if his sin, wherein he hath sinned, come to his knowledge; he shall bring his offering, a kid of the goats, a male without blemish:
verses.original_text: אוֹ הוֹדַע אֵלָיו חַטָּאתוֹ אֲשֶׁר חָטָא בָּהּ וְהֵבִיא אֶת־קָרְבָּנוֹ שְׂעִיר עִזִּים זָכָר תָּמִים׃
verses.transliteration: O hoda' elav chattato asher chata bah vehevi et-korbano se'ir izzim zakhar tamim.
verses.study_note: When the ruler's sin 'comes to his knowledge' (hoda' — becomes known to him), he brings his offering: 'se'ir izzim zakhar' — a male kid of the goats. The offering is a step down in scale from the bull required of the priest or congregation — a male goat rather than a young bull. This calibration of offering cost to social position establishes a theological principle: the greater the privilege, the greater the responsibility and the greater the offering required. Noblesse oblige runs through the Levitical system. But note: the ruler still brings a male animal and still requires the blood rite — even the reduced offering has no place for compromise on the essentials.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall lay his hand upon the head of the goat, and kill it in the place where they kill the burnt offering before the LORD: it is a sin offering.
verses.original_text: וְסָמַךְ יָדוֹ עַל־רֹאשׁ הַשָּׂעִיר וְשָׁחַט אֹתוֹ בִּמְקוֹם אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁחַט אֶת־הָעֹלָה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה חַטָּאת הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamakh yado al-rosh hassa'ir veshachat oto bimkom asher yishchat et-ha'olah lifnei YHVH chattat hu.
verses.study_note: The same foundational ritual acts: hand-laying and slaughter. A new detail appears: the location — 'bimkom asher yishchat et-ha'olah' — in the place where they slaughter the burnt offering. The sin offering of the ruler is slaughtered in the same location as the burnt offering. This spatial connection is significant: the place of total consecration (the burnt offering) is the same place where atonement for specific sin is dealt with. The geography of the altar yard is theologically unified — at the same spot where Israel's total devotion is expressed, sin is also confronted and covered.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall take of the blood of the sin offering with his finger, and put it upon the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and shall pour out his blood at the bottom of the altar of burnt offering.
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח הַכֹּהֵן מִדַּם הַחַטָּאת בְּאֶצְבָּעוֹ וְנָתַן עַל־קַרְנֹת מִזְבַּח הָעֹלָה וְאֶת־דָּמוֹ יִשְׁפֹּךְ אֶל־יְסוֹד מִזְבַּח הָעֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Velakach hakkohen middam hachattat be'etzba'o venatan al-karnot mizbach ha'olah ve'et-damo yishpokh el-yesod mizbach ha'olah.
verses.study_note: The blood application for the ruler's sin offering differs critically from the priest's and congregation's: the blood goes on the horns of the BURNT OFFERING altar (the outer altar in the courtyard), NOT on the incense altar inside the Holy Place. The blood does not enter the sanctuary at all. This is the first and most decisive structural difference between the high-status and low-status sin offerings in Leviticus 4: the priest/congregation require blood penetrating into the Holy Place; the ruler/common person require blood only at the outer altar. The ruler's blood is applied to the same altar that received the pouring of the higher offerings — the yesod (foundation base). The horns of the outer altar still provide refuge and the blood still atones — but the depth of penetration is less.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall burn all his fat upon the altar, as the fat of the sacrifice of peace offerings: and the priest shall make an atonement for him as concerning his sin, and it shall be forgiven him.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־כָּל־חֶלְבּוֹ יַקְטִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחָה כְּחֵלֶב זֶבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן מֵחַטָּאתוֹ וְנִסְלַח לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-kol-chelbo yaktir hammizbeachah kechelev zevach hashelamim vekhipper alav hakkohen mechattatо venislach lo.
verses.study_note: The fat is burned as in the peace offering, the atonement is made, and the same divine guarantee rings out: 'venislach lo' — it SHALL be forgiven him. Individual. Singular. This single person's sin, fully covered, fully forgiven. The same two-word formula used for the congregation (v.20) is now applied personally: the communal 'lahem' (for them) becomes the individual 'lo' (for him). The Levitical system does not only address collective guilt — it addresses the single soul. Every category of sinner in Leviticus 4 — priest, congregation, ruler, common person — receives the same divine guarantee: venislach. It shall be forgiven.
verses.doctrinal_note: The individual 'venislach lo' is the basis of David's confidence in Psalm 32:1: 'Blessed is he whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered.' Nathan's word to David after the Bathsheba affair — 'The LORD also hath put away thy sin' (2 Sam 12:13) — is the verbal equivalent of 'venislach.' God's forgiveness is both communal (lahem — for a people) and individual (lo — for a person). The New Covenant fulfillment is equally both: 'This is my blood of the new testament, which is shed for many' (Matt 26:28) — and 'who loved ME and gave himself for ME' (Gal 2:20, Paul's intensely personal appropriation).

verses.kjv_text: And if any one of the common people sin through ignorance, while he doeth somewhat against any of the commandments of the LORD concerning things which ought not to be done, and be guilty;
verses.original_text: וְאִם־נֶפֶשׁ אַחַת תֶּחֱטָא בִשְׁגָגָה מֵעַם הָאָרֶץ בַּעֲשֹׂתָהּ אַחַת מִמִּצְוֹת יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר לֹא תֵעָשֶׂינָה וְאָשֵׁמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-nefesh achat techeta bishgagah me'am ha'aretz ba'asotah achat mimitzvot YHVH asher lo te'aseynah ve'ashemah.
verses.study_note: The fourth and final category: 'am ha'aretz' — the common people of the land. The broadest, most inclusive category — every ordinary Israelite who has no special office or title. 'Nefesh achat' — one soul. The Levitical system descends through four tiers — anointed priest, whole congregation, ruler, and now the single common person — and treats each one with the same theological seriousness. The humblest member of the covenant community who sins inadvertently is not beneath God's attention or beyond His provision for atonement. The single soul matters as much as the whole congregation. This democratic reach of the sacrificial system reflects the democratic reach of the gospel: 'Whosoever will may come' (Rev 22:17).

verses.kjv_text: Or if his sin, which he hath sinned, come to his knowledge: then he shall bring his offering, a kid of the goats, a female without blemish, for his sin which he hath sinned.
verses.original_text: אוֹ הוֹדַע אֵלָיו חַטָּאתוֹ אֲשֶׁר חָטָא וְהֵבִיא קָרְבָּנוֹ שְׂעִירַת עִזִּים תְּמִימָה נְקֵבָה עַל חַטָּאתוֹ אֲשֶׁר חָטָא׃
verses.transliteration: O hoda' elav chattato asher chata vehevi korbano se'irat izzim temimah nekevah al chattato asher chata.
verses.study_note: The common person's offering: 'se'irat izzim nekevah' — a female kid of the goats. The step down from the ruler's male goat to the common person's female goat is the final calibration in the chapter's descending scale: bull (priest/congregation) → male goat (ruler) → female goat (common person). A female animal is permitted (as in the peace offering) for the common person. The offering is still 'temimah' — without blemish. The scale of cost diminishes; the standard of perfection does not. Even the smallest, most accessible sin offering must be without defect. Compromise on the standard of the offering would be compromise on the nature of the Substitute. The female goat offered by the poorest Israelite is as thoroughly 'without blemish' as the bull offered by the high priest.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall lay his hand upon the head of the sin offering, and slay the sin offering in the place of the burnt offering.
verses.original_text: וְסָמַךְ אֶת־יָדוֹ עַל רֹאשׁ הַחַטָּאת וְשָׁחַט אֶת־הַחַטָּאת בִּמְקוֹם הָעֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamakh et-yado al rosh hachattat veshachat et-hachattat bimkom ha'olah.
verses.study_note: The common person performs the identical foundational acts as the priest (v.4) and ruler (v.24): hand-laying on the head of the sin offering, slaughter in the place of the burnt offering. There is no special exemption for the common person from the requirement of personal identification with the substitute and personal engagement with the cost. The humblest worshiper presses his hand down with full weight — samakh — and draws the knife. The transaction of substitution is equally personal, equally serious, and equally transformative regardless of social rank. Every sinner must personally own the guilt, personally lay it on the substitute, and personally witness the cost.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall take of the blood thereof with his finger, and put it upon the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and shall pour out all the blood thereof at the bottom of the altar.
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח הַכֹּהֵן מִדָּמָהּ בְּאֶצְבָּעוֹ וְנָתַן עַל־קַרְנֹת מִזְבַּח הָעֹלָה וְאֶת־כָּל־דָּמָהּ יִשְׁפֹּךְ אֶל־יְסוֹד הַמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Velakach hakkohen middamah be'etzba'o venatan al-karnot mizbach ha'olah ve'et-kol-damah yishpokh el-yesod hammizbeach.
verses.study_note: The blood rite for the common person's sin offering is identical to the ruler's (v.25): finger dipped in blood, applied to the horns of the outer altar, remainder poured at the base. Not a drop of this blood enters the sanctuary. For priest and congregation, the blood traveled deepest — into the Holy Place. For the ruler and common person, the blood is applied at the outer altar only. This structural difference across Leviticus 4 is the scaffolding for the entire argument of Hebrews 9: the earthly system had priests who entered the Holy Place once a year, and even then only with blood — but Christ entered the 'greater and more perfect tabernacle' with His own blood, 'once for all' (Heb 9:11-12), penetrating all the way to the heavenly throne.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall take away all the fat thereof, as the fat is taken away from off the sacrifice of peace offerings; and the priest shall burn it upon the altar for a sweet savour unto the LORD; and the priest shall make an atonement for him, and it shall be forgiven him.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־כָּל־חֶלְבָּהּ יָסִיר כַּאֲשֶׁר הוּסַר חֵלֶב מֵעַל זֶבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים וְהִקְטִיר הַכֹּהֵן הַמִּזְבֵּחָה לְרֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ לַיהוָה וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן וְנִסְלַח לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-kol-chelba yasiyr ka'asher husar chelev me'al zevach hashelamim vehiktir hakkohen hammizbeachah lereiyach nichoach laYHVH vekhipper alav hakkohen venislach lo.
verses.study_note: A remarkable feature of this verse: the sin offering's fat is burned and described as 'reiyach nichoach' — a sweet savour to the LORD. This phrase has been used of the burnt offering (Lev 1:9,13,17) and the peace offering (Lev 3:5,16), but now it appears in the sin offering as well. The sin offering's fat, burned on the altar after the blood has been applied, also produces a sweet savour. God is satisfied — not just by the peace offering's fellowship or the burnt offering's consecration, but by the sin offering's atonement. The atoning work satisfies God. Then the double guarantee closes: 'vekhipper... venislach lo.' Atonement made. Forgiveness sealed. The single common soul, the humblest member of Israel, receives the same divine 'venislach lo' as the high priest. The forgiveness formula is identical for all four categories. Rank makes no difference in God's willingness to forgive.

verses.kjv_text: And if he bring a lamb for a sin offering, he shall bring it a female without blemish.
verses.original_text: וְאִם כֶּבֶשׂ יָבִיא קָרְבָּנוֹ לְחַטָּאת נְקֵבָה תְמִימָה יְבִיאֶנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im kheves yavi korbano lechattat nekevah temimah yevi'enah.
verses.study_note: An alternative animal is permitted for the common person: 'keves' — a female lamb. The same animal as the daily tamid offering, the Passover lamb, and the prominent sacrificial lamb throughout the OT, now appears as an option for the sin offering of the humblest worshiper. The provision of an alternative (female goat or female lamb) reflects divine pastoral concern: God provides a range of options that can fit different circumstances without lowering the standard of the offering. Both alternatives are 'nekevah temimah' — a perfect female. The lamb for the common person's sin offering is the same species as the Lamb of God — and perhaps this is not incidental.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall lay his hand upon the head of the sin offering, and slay it for a sin offering in the place where they kill the burnt offering.
verses.original_text: וְסָמַךְ אֶת־יָדוֹ עַל רֹאשׁ הַחַטָּאת וְשָׁחַט אֹתָהּ לְחַטָּאת בִּמְקוֹם אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁחַט אֶת־הָעֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamakh et-yado al rosh hachattat veshachat otah lechattat bimkom asher yishchat et-ha'olah.
verses.study_note: The fourth repetition of the hand-laying and slaughter at the place of the burnt offering — now for the lamb alternative. The identical ritual for the goat and the lamb confirms that the liturgical form is not dependent on the specific animal but on the principles: identification, substitution, blood. Every sin offering, regardless of species, passes through the same foundational acts. The repetition of samakh (hand-laying) four times across Leviticus 4 (vv.4, 15, 24, 29, 33) is Leviticus's way of establishing beyond all ambiguity that the act of identification and transference is universal and required — there are no shortcuts to the altar.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall take of the blood of the sin offering with his finger, and put it upon the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and shall pour out all the blood thereof at the bottom of the altar:
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח הַכֹּהֵן מִדַּם הַחַטָּאת בְּאֶצְבָּעוֹ וְנָתַן עַל־קַרְנֹת מִזְבַּח הָעֹלָה וְאֶת־כָּל־דָּמָהּ יִשְׁפֹּךְ אֶל־יְסוֹד הַמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Velakach hakkohen middam hachattat be'etzba'o venatan al-karnot mizbach ha'olah ve'et-kol-damah yishpokh el-yesod hammizbeach.
verses.study_note: The blood rite for the lamb sin offering is identical to the goat (v.30) and the ruler (v.25): finger, blood on the horns of the outer altar, remainder poured at the base. The fourth time this identical blood formula appears for the lower-status sin offerings (vv.25, 30, 34). The repetition within repetition creates a literary and liturgical certainty: for the common person offering either a goat or a lamb, the blood rite is always the same. The outer altar's horns receive the blood; the foundation receives the rest. The outer altar is the place of public, accessible atonement — where every ordinary Israelite can see the blood and know that the sacrifice has been made.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall take away all the fat thereof, as the fat of the lamb is taken away from the sacrifice of the peace offerings; and the priest shall burn them upon the altar, according to the offerings made by fire unto the LORD: and the priest shall make an atonement for his sin that he hath committed, and it shall be forgiven him.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־כָּל־חֶלְבָּהּ יָסִיר כַּאֲשֶׁר יוּסַר חֵלֶב הַכֶּבֶשׂ מִזֶּבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים וְהִקְטִיר הַכֹּהֵן אֹתָם הַמִּזְבֵּחָה עַל אִשֵּׁי יְהוָה וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן עַל חַטָּאתוֹ אֲשֶׁר חָטָא וְנִסְלַח לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-kol-chelba yasiyr ka'asher yusar chelev hakkeves mizzevach hashelamim vehiktir hakkohen otam hammizbeachah al ishhei YHVH vekhipper alav hakkohen al chattato asher chata venislach lo.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes as it must: 'vekhipper... venislach lo' — atonement made, forgiveness sealed. The final 'venislach lo' is the fourth such declaration in Leviticus 4 (vv.20, 26, 31, 35), one for each category of sinner. The fourfold repetition of this guarantee across the entire chapter creates an unassailable theological rhythm: it shall be forgiven — for the priest, for the congregation, for the ruler, for the single common soul. No category of sinner is excluded from the scope of 'venislach.' The fat is removed and burned as in the peace offering — the sin offering, having accomplished its atoning work, shares the final altar act with the fellowship offering. Atonement and fellowship are inseparable in Leviticus: the sin dealt with (chattat) opens the way for the fellowship enjoyed (shelamim). Leviticus 4 is the foundation on which chapters 1-3 rest.
verses.doctrinal_note: The fourfold 'venislach' of Leviticus 4 is the Levitical basis for the NT certainty of forgiveness. John writes: 'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins, and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness' (1 John 1:9) — 'faithful and just' because the chattat has been offered, the blood has been applied, and the divine guarantee has been given. The author of Hebrews applies this certainty to Christ's single, permanent offering: 'By one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified' (Heb 10:14). The four-fold 'venislach' of Leviticus 4 — repeated for priest, congregation, ruler, and common person — finds its eternal fulfillment in the once-for-all declaration of Romans 8:1: 'There is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus.'

meta.overview: Leviticus 4 introduces the Chattat — the sin offering — the most complex and theologically profound of the Levitical sacrifices. Unlike the voluntary offerings of chapters 1-3 (burnt, grain, and peace), the sin offering is obligatory: it is required whenever sin has been committed. The chapter addresses a specific category of sin: 'bishgagah' — sins committed in ignorance, inadvertently, or unintentionally. The deliberate, high-handed sin ('beyad ramah' — with a raised hand, Num 15:30-31) has no atonement within the Levitical system; it falls under direct divine judgment. But unintentional sin is not treated as inconsequential — it still requires blood atonement. Four categories of offender are addressed in descending social order, each with its own animal and its own blood ritual: (1) The anointed priest (vv.1-12) — a young bull, blood applied to the veil and the incense altar inside the Holy Place; (2) The whole congregation (vv.13-21) — a young bull, identical blood rite; (3) A ruler/prince (vv.22-26) — a male goat, blood on the horns of the burnt offering altar; (4) A common person (vv.27-35) — a female goat or female lamb, blood on the horns of the burnt offering altar. The blood application — its location, its method — varies with the seriousness of the offender's position. The higher the office, the further into the sanctuary the blood must penetrate. Typologically, Leviticus 4 is the most direct prefiguration of Christ's atoning work as the sin offering: He 'was made sin for us' (2 Cor 5:21) and offered His blood not on the outer altar but at the heavenly mercy seat itself (Heb 9:11-12).
meta.theme: The Sin Offering — Atonement for Inadvertent Sin, Substitutionary Blood, and the Gradation of Priestly Mediation
meta.keyWordMeaning: Sin offering / Sin — from the root 'chata' (to miss the mark, to sin). The word serves double duty: it names both the sin itself and the sacrifice that covers it, teaching that sin and its remedy are inseparably linked. The chattat is the offering where the sin is laid upon the substitute and dealt with at the altar.
outline: The Sin of the Anointed Priest (vv.1-12): A young bull; blood brought into the Holy Place, applied to the veil and incense altar; carcass burned outside the camp
The Sin of the Whole Congregation (vv.13-21): A young bull; identical to the priest's offering; the elders lay hands; burned outside the camp
The Sin of a Ruler (vv.22-26): A male goat; blood on the horns of the burnt offering altar only; fat burned on the altar
The Sin of a Common Person (vv.27-35): A female goat or female lamb; blood on the horns of the burnt offering altar; fat burned; full forgiveness pronounced

verses.kjv_text: And if a soul sin, and hear the voice of swearing, and is a witness, whether he hath seen or known of it; if he do not utter it, then he shall bear his iniquity.
verses.original_text: וְנֶפֶשׁ כִּי־תֶחֱטָא וְשָׁמְעָה קוֹל אָלָה וְהוּא עֵד אוֹ רָאָה אוֹ יָדָע אִם־לוֹא יַגִּיד וְנָשָׂא עֲוֺנוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Venefesh ki-techeta veshame'ah kol alah vehu ed o ra'ah o yada im-lo yaggid venasa avono.
verses.study_note: The first of four specific sins requiring the sin offering: the sin of suppressed testimony. 'Kol alah' — the voice of a public oath or adjuration — describes the formal, judicial call to testify under oath. An Israelite who witnessed a matter and was under oath to testify but withheld his testimony was guilty before God, not merely before the court. 'Venasa avono' — he shall bear his iniquity. The guilty silence is not merely a social failure but a theological one: truth-telling is a covenant obligation, and the suppression of truth when God's name has been invoked makes the silent witness a bearer of iniquity. This first case establishes a principle that runs through the entire chapter: sin is not limited to dramatic actions but encompasses omissions, silence, and the failure to fulfill covenant obligations. Jesus' warning about 'every idle word' (Matt 12:36) and His model as the faithful witness (Rev 1:5 — 'the faithful witness') stand against this background.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sin of suppressed testimony (bearing false witness by silence) is addressed first because it involves speech — the very medium through which covenants are made and through which truth is upheld before God. The believer as 'witness' to the truth of the gospel (Acts 1:8) carries the same category of obligation: to suppress testimony about Christ is a sin of omission with weight before God. James writes: 'Therefore to him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin' (Jas 4:17). The sin of silence has its own guilt before heaven.

verses.kjv_text: Or if a soul touch any unclean thing, whether it be a carcase of an unclean beast, or a carcase of unclean cattle, or the carcase of unclean creeping things, and if it be hidden from him; he also shall be unclean, and guilty.
verses.original_text: אוֹ נֶפֶשׁ אֲשֶׁר תִּגַּע בְּכָל־דָּבָר טָמֵא אוֹ בְנִבְלַת חַיָּה טְמֵאָה אוֹ בְּנִבְלַת בְּהֵמָה טְמֵאָה אוֹ בְנִבְלַת שֶׁרֶץ טָמֵא וְנֶעְלַם מִמֶּנּוּ וְהוּא טָמֵא וְאָשֵׁם׃
verses.transliteration: O nefesh asher tigga bekhol-davar tameh o benivlat chayyah temeh o benivlat behemah temeh o venivlat sherets tameh vene'elam mimmennu vehu tameh ve'ashem.
verses.study_note: The second case: inadvertent contact with ritual uncleanness through touching the carcass (nevlah) of an unclean animal — wild beast, livestock, or creeping thing. The critical phrase is 'vene'elam mimmennu' — it was hidden from him, he was unaware. The contact was unintentional; the uncleanness is real regardless. 'Vehu tameh ve'ashem' — he is unclean AND guilty. Two states coexist: ritual impurity (tameh) and moral accountability (ashem). This distinction between defilement and guilt is important: the uncleanness is a ritual/ceremonial state; the guilt is a moral/covenantal accountability. Both must be addressed. The Levitical system insists that objective defilement and objective guilt exist independently of subjective awareness. Modern sensibility wants to say 'if I didn't know, I'm not guilty' — Leviticus says: you are defiled, and you bear guilt, and the offering must be brought.

verses.kjv_text: Or if he touch the uncleanness of man, whatsoever uncleanness it be that a man shall be defiled withal, and it be hid from him; when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty.
verses.original_text: אוֹ כִי יִגַּע בְּטֻמְאַת אָדָם לְכֹל טֻמְאָתוֹ אֲשֶׁר יִטְמָא בָּהּ וְנֶעְלַם מִמֶּנּוּ וְהוּא יָדַע וְאָשֵׁם׃
verses.transliteration: O ki yigga betumat adam lekhol tumatо asher yitma bah vene'elam mimmennu vehu yada ve'ashem.
verses.study_note: The third case: contact with human uncleanness (tumat adam). The Levitical laws governing human uncleanness are extensive — covering bodily discharges, skin diseases, childbirth, and contact with human corpses (Lev 12-15, Num 19). The phrase 'lekhol tumato' (whatever his uncleanness) indicates the category is broad, not restricted to one type. Again the formula 'vene'elam mimmennu' (hidden from him) — unintentional contact. But the guilt-moment is defined differently here: 'vehu yada' — when he knows (comes to awareness) — at that point 've'ashem' — then he bears guilt. The guilt is anchored to the moment of awareness. This is a fine theological distinction: knowledge activates moral accountability even when the initial act was unconscious. The moment of recognition is the moment of responsibility.

verses.kjv_text: Or if a soul swear, pronouncing with his lips to do evil, or to do good, whatsoever it be that a man shall pronounce with an oath, and it be hid from him; when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty in one of these.
verses.original_text: אוֹ נֶפֶשׁ כִּי תִשָּׁבַע לְבַטֵּא בִשְׂפָתַיִם לְהָרַע אוֹ לְהֵיטִיב לְכֹל אֲשֶׁר יְבַטֵּא הָאָדָם בִּשְׁבֻעָה וְנֶעְלַם מִמֶּנּוּ וְהוּא יָדַע וְאָשֵׁם לְאַחַת מֵאֵלֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: O nefesh ki tishshava levatteh bisefatayim lehareh o leheitiv lekhol asher yevatteh ha'adam bishvu'ah vene'elam mimmennu vehu yada ve'ashem le'achat me'elleh.
verses.study_note: The fourth case: the rash oath — 'levatteh bisefatayim' — speaking rashly with the lips. Whether the oath was to do something evil or something good is remarkably inclusive: even an oath sworn with good intent, if forgotten or overlooked ('vene'elam mimmennu'), creates guilt when remembered. The human tongue, even in well-meaning religious declarations, can generate binding obligations that, if neglected, constitute sin. Jephthah's vow (Judges 11) is the paradigm of the tragic rash oath. The NT warns: 'But let your communication be, Yea, yea; Nay, nay: for whatsoever is more than these cometh of evil' (Matt 5:37). James echoes: 'swear not, neither by heaven, neither by the earth, neither by any other oath' (Jas 5:12). The four cases together — testimony withheld, unclean contact (animal), unclean contact (human), rash oath — are not exhaustive but representative: sins of omission, sins of inadvertent contact with the defiling world, and sins of the careless tongue.
verses.doctrinal_note: The four cases of Leviticus 5:1-4 reveal that the Levitical system's reach of atonement covers the full range of human moral failure: the court (testimony), the body (defilement), the social world (human uncleanness), and the tongue (oaths). Together they anticipate James 2:10: 'Whosoever shall keep the whole law, and yet offend in one point, he is guilty of all.' The comprehensiveness of the sin offering's reach — covering all four categories — is a shadow of Christ's comprehensive atonement: 'the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from all sin' (1 John 1:7). Not from most sin, not from certain categories — from all sin.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall be, when he shall be guilty in one of these things, that he shall confess that he hath sinned in that thing:
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה כִי־יֶאְשַׁם לְאַחַת מֵאֵלֶּה וְהִתְוַדָּה אֲשֶׁר חָטָא עָלֶיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah ki-ye'sham le'achat me'elleh vehitvaddah asher chata aleha.
verses.study_note: Between the identification of sin (vv.1-4) and the offering of sacrifice (v.6) comes a verse that stands alone and structurally vital: confession. 'Vehitvaddah' — he shall confess. This is the first explicit requirement of verbal confession (vidui) in the sacrificial system. The Hitpael form of 'yadah' — to acknowledge, to confess, to give thanks — is the same verb that underlies 'todah' (the thanksgiving offering) and the act of praise. Confession and praise share a root: to confess sin is to acknowledge God's righteousness; to praise is to acknowledge God's goodness. Both are acts of truthful acknowledgment. Verse 5 inserts confession as the mandatory bridge between recognition of guilt (vv.1-4) and the ritual of atonement (v.6). The offering without the confession is empty ritual; the confession without the offering remains an unprovided-for guilt. Both are required. This principle is the Levitical foundation for 1 John 1:9: 'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins.'
verses.doctrinal_note: The requirement of confession (vidui) before the sin offering is the Levitical basis for the NT insistence that saving faith includes verbal acknowledgment: 'with the mouth confession is made unto salvation' (Rom 10:10). The confession is not meritorious — it does not earn forgiveness — but it is the necessary posture of the soul that brings the offering. A person who refuses to name their sin has not brought their soul to the altar. The full vidui formula developed in later Judaism ('I have sinned, I have done wrong, I have transgressed') is the elaboration of this single word: 'vehitvaddah' — he shall confess that he sinned in that thing. Specific, personal, honest speech before God.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall bring his trespass offering unto the LORD for his sin which he hath sinned, a female from the flock, a lamb or a kid of the goats, for a sin offering; and the priest shall make an atonement for him concerning his sin.
verses.original_text: וְהֵבִיא אֶת־אֲשָׁמוֹ לַיהוָה עַל חַטָּאתוֹ אֲשֶׁר חָטָא נְקֵבָה מִן־הַצֹּאן כִּשְׂבָּה אוֹ שְׂעִירַת עִזִּים לְחַטָּאת וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן מֵחַטָּאתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehevi et-ashamo laYHVH al chattato asher chata nekevah min-hattzon kishbah o se'irat izzim lechattat vekhipper alav hakkohen mechatato.
verses.study_note: After the confession, the offering. The standard level is 'nekevah min-hattzon' — a female from the flock, a female lamb (kishbah — the feminine of keves) or a female kid. This is equivalent to the common person's offering in Leviticus 4:27-31. The word 'asham' appears here not for the first time as a technical term for the guilt offering (that will come in vv.14-19) but in its broader sense of the guilt-bearing offering — what the person owes for their guilt. Then the formula: 'vekhipper alav hakkohen' — and the priest shall make atonement for him. The same kipper that sounded through all four categories of Leviticus 4. Here it applies to the common case of the everyday Israelite guilty of one of the four specific sins named in vv.1-4. No sin is too ordinary to require atonement; no atonement is too ordinary to be accepted by God.
verses.doctrinal_note: The female lamb or goat for the common person's sin confirms the principle established in Leviticus 4: accessibility of atonement without compromise of its standard. The female is less costly; it is still 'tamim' by implication (without blemish). Christ's atonement is both fully accessible ('whosoever will') and fully perfect ('a lamb without blemish and without spot' — 1 Pet 1:19). The democratic reach of the sin offering and the perfection of its standard are simultaneously maintained in Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And if he be not able to bring a lamb, then he shall bring for his trespass, which he hath committed, two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, unto the LORD; one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering.
verses.original_text: וְאִם לֹא תַגִּיעַ יָדוֹ דֵּי שֶׂה וְהֵבִיא אֶת־אֲשָׁמוֹ אֲשֶׁר חָטָא שְׁתֵּי תֹרִים אוֹ שְׁנֵי בְנֵי־יוֹנָה לַיהוָה אֶחָד לְחַטָּאת וְהָאֶחָד לְעֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im lo taggia yado dei seh vehevi et-ashamo asher chata shetei torim o shenei venei-yonah laYHVH echad lechattat veha'echad le'olah.
verses.study_note: The first tier-down in the sliding scale: 'im lo taggia yado dei seh' — if his hand does not reach (afford) a lamb. The phrase 'taggia yado' (his hand reaches) is an idiomatic expression for financial capacity — what one can 'reach' or 'attain to.' For the person who cannot afford a lamb, two turtledoves (torim) or two young pigeons (benei yonah) are accepted. Notice that two birds are required, and they serve two distinct functions: one for a sin offering (chattat), one for a burnt offering (olah). This is the only place in Leviticus where the sin offering of the poor automatically includes a burnt offering as well. The poor person who comes for atonement does not only receive forgiveness of the specific sin — he also receives the full benefit of the burnt offering's total consecration. The bird sin offering covers the specific sin; the bird burnt offering dedicates the whole person to God. The poorer the worshiper, the more the grace encompasses.
verses.doctrinal_note: The provision of two birds — one for sin offering, one for burnt offering — for the person who cannot afford a lamb reveals the generosity of divine grace. The poor worshiper receives both specific atonement (chattat) and comprehensive consecration (olah) in a single visit. This double provision for the poor anticipates the NT principle: 'God is no respecter of persons' (Acts 10:34) and 'hath not God chosen the poor of this world rich in faith' (Jas 2:5). Luke 2:24 records that Mary and Joseph offered 'a pair of turtledoves, or two young pigeons' at Christ's presentation — indicating their economic status. The Son of God entered the world in a family that offered the bird sacrifice of the poor. He who was rich became poor, that through His poverty we might be made rich (2 Cor 8:9).

verses.kjv_text: And he shall bring them unto the priest, who shall offer that which is for the sin offering first, and wring off his head from his neck, but shall not divide it asunder:
verses.original_text: וְהֵבִיא אֹתָם אֶל־הַכֹּהֵן וְהִקְרִיב אֶת־אֲשֶׁר לַחַטָּאת רִאשׁוֹנָה וּמָלַק אֶת־רֹאשׁוֹ מִמּוּל עָרְפּוֹ וְלֹא יַבְדִּיל׃
verses.transliteration: Vehevi otam el-hakkohen vehikriv et-asher lachattat rishonah umalak et-rosho mimmul orpo velo yavdil.
verses.study_note: The sin offering bird must be brought 'rishonah' — first. The order of precedence: sin is dealt with before consecration. The chattat precedes the olah. This is the Levitical insistence that there is no acceptable consecration (burnt offering) until sin has been addressed (sin offering). The method of slaughter for the bird sin offering is distinctive: 'malak' — the priest wrings off ('pinches off') the head from 'mul orpo' (at/near the back of the neck) but 'velo yavdil' — does not completely sever it. The head remains attached. This is different from the bird burnt offering (Lev 1:15-16 where the head is also wrung off). The partial severance — wring but not divide — may signify the partial nature of what is killed: the sin is dealt with, but the life continues under God's hand. Some Talmudic interpreters see the partial cut as suggesting that the sin is 'suppressed' but not entirely cut off — requiring ongoing watchfulness.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall sprinkle of the blood of the sin offering upon the side of the altar; and the rest of the blood shall be wrung out at the bottom of the altar: it is a sin offering.
verses.original_text: וְהִזָּה מִדַּם הַחַטָּאת עַל־קִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וְהַנִּשְׁאָר בַּדָּם יִמָּצֵא אֶל־יְסוֹד הַמִּזְבֵּחַ חַטָּאת הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vehizzah middam hachattat al-kir hammizbeach vehannisha'ar baddam yimmatzeh el-yesod hammizbeach chattat hu.
verses.study_note: The bird sin offering's blood rite differs in method from the animal sin offerings: the blood is 'sprinkled' (hizzah) on the 'kir' (side/wall) of the altar rather than applied with a finger to the horns. The remainder is 'wrung out' (yimmatzeh — squeezed out) at the base (yesod) of the altar. Two consistent elements persist from all the sin offering blood rites: the blood contacts the altar itself, and the remainder is emptied at the altar's foundation. These two elements — altar contact and foundation-pouring — are the non-negotiable minimum. The blood must reach the altar, and the life (represented by the blood) must be poured out at the very base of worship. For the poorest person in Israel, the smallest acceptable offering still satisfies both requirements.
verses.doctrinal_note: The blood of the smallest bird sin offering poured at the foundation of the altar is equal in covenantal weight to the bull's blood poured by the high priest. The altar does not measure the volume of blood but the validity of the rite and the faith behind it. This pictures the NT truth that Christ's atonement is not a quantitative transaction — it is not that a greater volume of suffering covers more sin. 'By one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified' (Heb 10:14). The bird's blood at the foundation and the bull's blood at the foundation both declare: the foundation of all worship is blood, and the blood has been provided.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall offer the second for a burnt offering, according to the manner: and the priest shall make an atonement for him for his sin which he hath sinned, and it shall be forgiven him.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הַשֵּׁנִי יַעֲשֶׂה עֹלָה כַּמִּשְׁפָּט וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן מֵחַטָּאתוֹ אֲשֶׁר חָטָא וְנִסְלַח לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-hashsheni ya'aseh olah kammishpat vekhipper alav hakkohen mechatato asher chata venislach lo.
verses.study_note: The second bird is offered as a burnt offering 'kammishpat' — according to the ordinance, the standard procedure for the bird burnt offering (Lev 1:14-17). The sin offering precedes; now the burnt offering follows — consecration after atonement. Then the closing formula returns: 'vekhipper... venislach lo' — atonement made, it shall be forgiven him. The same guarantee given to the anointed priest (4:20), the congregation (4:26 parallel), the ruler (4:26), and the common person (4:31, 35) now applies to the poorest worshiper who brings only two birds. The 'venislach lo' is utterly indiscriminate across economic classes. God's forgiveness pronouncement does not scale with the cost of the offering. Two turtledoves receive exactly the same divine 'venislach' as a young bull. The atoning value is not in the animal's price but in God's provision and the worshiper's faith.

verses.kjv_text: But if he be not able to bring two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, then he that sinned shall bring for his offering the tenth part of an ephah of fine flour for a sin offering; he shall put no oil upon it, neither shall he put any frankincense thereon: for it is a sin offering.
verses.original_text: וְאִם לֹא תַשִּׂיג יָדוֹ לִשְׁתֵּי תֹרִים אוֹ לִשְׁנֵי בְנֵי יוֹנָה וְהֵבִיא אֶת־קָרְבָּנוֹ אֲשֶׁר חָטָא עֲשִׂירִת הָאֵפָה סֹלֶת לְחַטָּאת לֹא יָשִׂים עָלֶיהָ שֶׁמֶן וְלֹא יִתֵּן עָלֶיהָ לְבֹנָה כִּי חַטָּאת הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im lo tassig yado lishetei torim o lishenei venei yonah vehevi et-korbano asher chata asirit ha'efah solet lechattat lo yasim aleha shemen velo yitten aleha levonah ki chattat hi.
verses.study_note: The second tier-down, and the most remarkable provision in the entire Levitical system: if even two birds are beyond financial reach, the worshiper may bring 'asirit ha'efah solet' — one tenth of an ephah of fine flour. Roughly 2 liters of grain. The flour sin offering immediately raises a paradox: the grain offering of Leviticus 2 required oil and frankincense — but this flour sin offering explicitly prohibits both. 'Lo yasim aleha shemen velo yitten aleha levonah' — he shall put no oil on it and no frankincense on it. Why? Because this is a 'chattat' — a sin offering. Oil (the Spirit) and frankincense (prayer/intercession) belong to the presentation of consecrated life (the minchah of ch.2). The sin offering of flour is stripped of these beautiful elements because it addresses the brokenness of sin, not the beauty of devotion. The flour chattat is sin at its starkest: no fragrance, no anointing — just the bare acknowledgment of need brought to the altar.
verses.doctrinal_note: The flour sin offering without oil or frankincense is one of the most theologically precise details in Leviticus. The oil (the Spirit) and the frankincense (intercession/prayer) are omitted because the chattat strips away all enhancement and presents the soul in its bare, unadorned sinfulness. Christ, the true Sin Offering, voluntarily laid aside the comforts of divine glory (Phil 2:7) and was made 'a curse for us' (Gal 3:13) — stripped of earthly glory, hung naked on a cross, bearing the bare weight of human sin. The absence of oil and frankincense in this lowest sin offering mirrors the desolation of the cross where the soul of Christ was 'poured out unto death' (Isa 53:12). This is why He cried 'My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?' — the fullest expression of a sin offering without the felt comfort of the Spirit's presence.

verses.kjv_text: Then shall he bring it to the priest, and the priest shall take his handful of it, even a memorial thereof, and burn it on the altar, according to the offerings made by fire unto the LORD: it is a sin offering.
verses.original_text: וֶהֱבִיאָהּ אֶל־הַכֹּהֵן וְקָמַץ הַכֹּהֵן מִמֶּנָּה מְלֹא קֻמְצוֹ אֶת־אַזְכָּרָתָהּ וְהִקְטִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחָה עַל אִשֵּׁי יְהוָה חַטָּאת הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Vehevia'ah el-hakkohen vekamatz hakkohen mimennah melo kumtzo et-azkaratah vehiktir hammizbeachah al ishhei YHVH chattat hi.
verses.study_note: The flour sin offering is processed as a grain offering: the priest takes a handful (kumtzo) as the 'azkarah' (memorial portion) and burns it on the altar. The same 'azkarah' language used in Leviticus 2 for the minchah is used here — the flour chattat is handled like a grain offering in its altar ritual. But the fundamental designation is repeated: 'chattat hi' — it is a sin offering. No matter how much it resembles the grain offering in outward form, it is essentially and categorically a sin offering. The form of an offering does not determine its nature; the purpose and context do. A handful of flour burned on God's altar, brought by the most destitute Israelite, carries the full weight of the sin offering and receives the full benefit of priestly atonement and divine forgiveness.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall make an atonement for him as touching his sin that he hath sinned in one of these, and it shall be forgiven him: and the remnant shall be the priest’s, as a meat offering.
verses.original_text: וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן עַל חַטָּאתוֹ אֲשֶׁר חָטָא מֵאַחַת מֵאֵלֶּה וְנִסְלַח לוֹ וְהָיְתָה לַכֹּהֵן כַּמִּנְחָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhipper alav hakkohen al chattato asher chata me'achat me'elleh venislach lo vehayetah lakhkohen kammminchah.
verses.study_note: The formula closes: 'vekhipper... venislach lo.' Atonement, forgiveness. The poorest Israelite who brings a tenth of an ephah of flour for one of the four specific sins of vv.1-4 receives the identical divine guarantee — 'venislach lo' — as the high priest who brought a young bull. The sliding scale of the offering adjusts to human poverty; the divine promise of forgiveness is not adjusted at all. Then: 'vehayetah lakhkohen kammminchah' — the remainder belongs to the priest as a grain offering. The flour sin offering's remainder is treated as a minchah for the priests' sustenance — exactly as Leviticus 2:3,10. Even in its most impoverished form, the sin offering provides for those who minister at God's altar. Grace covers the worshiper; the provision feeds the minister.
verses.doctrinal_note: The full 'venislach lo' applied to the flour offering is the Levitical foundation for Jesus' teaching in Luke 21:3-4 about the widow's two mites: 'she of her penury hath cast in all the living that she had.' God's acceptance is not calibrated by the world's standard of value but by the worshiper's faith and the completeness of their giving. The flour chattat — the very bottom of the economic scale — receives the full 'venislach.' In Christ, the poorest sinner receives the fullest forgiveness. 'Ho, every one that thirsteth, come ye to the waters; and he that hath no money; come ye' (Isa 55:1).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHVH el-Moshe lemor.
verses.study_note: A new divine address formula marks the transition to a new section — the Asham proper. The formulaic 'vaydabber YHVH el-Moshe lemor' divides Leviticus into discrete revealed units. Its appearance here signals that what follows — the guilt offering — is a distinct revelation, a new category of sacrifice with its own theology, not merely an extension of the sin offering. God initiates this new disclosure to Moses; the Asham is not a priestly development or a practical adaptation but a divinely revealed institution. The repetition of the divine speech formula after the sliding-scale sin offering (v.13) creates a deliberate structural break: the sliding scale addresses the chattat; what follows addresses the Asham.
verses.doctrinal_note: The double divine address formula within Leviticus 5 (here at v.14, and also at v.1 via Lev 4:1) reflects the literary structure of priestly law: each major new category of sacrifice receives its own divine introduction. The Asham is sufficiently distinct from the Chattat to require its own revelation. This distinction is important theologically: atonement covers the defilement of sin (chattat); the guilt offering addresses the damage caused by sin and the debt incurred (asham). Both dimensions of sin — its polluting effect and its liability-creating effect — are covered by divine provision.

verses.kjv_text: If a soul commit a trespass, and sin through ignorance, in the holy things of the LORD; then he shall bring for his trespass unto the LORD a ram without blemish out of the flocks, with thy estimation by shekels of silver, after the shekel of the sanctuary, for a trespass offering:
verses.original_text: נֶפֶשׁ כִּי תִמְעֹל מַעַל וְחָטְאָה בִשְׁגָגָה מִקָּדְשֵׁי יְהוָה וְהֵבִיא אֶת־אֲשָׁמוֹ לַיהוָה אַיִל תָּמִים מִן־הַצֹּאן בְּעֶרְכְּךָ כֶּסֶף־שְׁקָלִים בְּשֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ לְאָשָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Nefesh ki tim'ol ma'al vechate'ah bishgagah mikkodshei YHVH vehevi et-ashamo laYHVH ayil tamim min-hattzon be'erkkha kesef-shekalim beshekel hakkodesh le'asham.
verses.study_note: The Asham's first case: 'tim'ol ma'al' — to commit a trespass, to act treacherously, to misappropriate (ma'al). This verb 'ma'al' is stronger than ordinary sin — it carries the sense of a breach of trust, an embezzlement, a faithless violation of something held in trust. Specifically: 'mikkodshei YHVH' — from the holy things of the LORD. This covers inadvertent misuse of things consecrated to God: eating of sacred food by mistake, failing to pay a tithe, using dedicated property, or any unauthorized appropriation of what belongs exclusively to the sanctuary. Even when 'bishgagah' — in ignorance — this creates an Asham, a guilt-debt. The required offering is now specified for the first time in the Levitical system: 'ayil tamim' — a ram without blemish. Not a lamb or goat (as in the sin offering) but a ram — a more costly and specific animal. Moreover, the assessment of monetary value ('be'erkkha kesef-shekalim' — with your estimation in silver shekels by the shekel of the sanctuary) must accompany the ram. The Asham requires both a sacrifice AND a monetary valuation of the damage done.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Asham (guilt offering) introducing a ram of specific monetary value plus restitution reveals a dimension of sin beyond defilement — the dimension of debt and damage. Where the Chattat covers the sinner's guilty state, the Asham addresses the creditor's (God's) legitimate claim for what was taken. This two-dimensionality of atonement is fulfilled in Christ: He not only covers our guilty state (chattat) but satisfies God's righteous claim, paying the full debt of the law (asham). Isaiah 53:10 says 'when thou shalt make his soul an asham for sin' — the Suffering Servant's death is specifically identified as an asham, a guilt offering — paying the full liability incurred by human sin against God's holy things.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall make amends for the harm that he hath done in the holy thing, and shall add the fifth part thereto, and give it unto the priest: and the priest shall make an atonement for him with the ram of the trespass offering, and it shall be forgiven him.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת אֲשֶׁר חָטָא מִן־הַקֹּדֶשׁ יְשַׁלֵּם וְאֶת־חֲמִישִׁתוֹ יוֹסֵף עָלָיו וְנָתַן אֹתוֹ לַכֹּהֵן וְהַכֹּהֵן יְכַפֶּר עָלָיו בְּאֵיל הָאָשָׁם וְנִסְלַח לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et asher chata min-hakkodesh yeshalleim ve'et-chamishito yosef alav venatan oto lakhkohen vehakkohen yekhapper alav be'eil ha'asham venislach lo.
verses.study_note: The Asham's mandatory restitution component: 'yeshalleim' — he shall make full restitution (from 'shalam,' to be complete, to pay back in full — the same root as 'shalom'). Full restitution of what was taken from the holy things, plus 'chamishito' — one fifth added on top. The 20% surcharge (one fifth on top of the principal = 25% of the restored total) is a penalty/reparation over and above simple repayment. The payment is made 'to the priest' — God's representative. Then and only then does the ram complete the atonement: 'vehakkohen yekhapper alav be'eil ha'asham venislach lo.' The sequence is clear: restitution to the human/priestly representative → sacrifice on the altar → divine forgiveness. Restitution without the ram would leave the blood-debt unsatisfied. The ram without restitution would leave the material debt unpaid. Both are required. Grace does not annul the debt — it covers it through the sacrifice while requiring the restoration.
verses.doctrinal_note: The restitution-plus-fifth-part requirement of the Asham introduces the concept of 'making things right' as a component of genuine repentance and forgiveness. This is the Levitical foundation for Jesus' instruction in Matthew 5:23-24: 'if thou bring thy gift to the altar, and there rememberest that thy brother hath ought against thee; leave there thy gift before the altar... first be reconciled to thy brother, and then come and offer thy gift.' Zacchaeus's spontaneous declaration — 'I restore him fourfold' (Luke 19:8) — is recognized by Jesus as a sign of genuine salvation. The Asham's restitution principle is not salvation by works but the evidence that the offering has been genuinely appropriated. Christ's atonement as the ultimate Asham (Isa 53:10) pays the full principal plus the infinite surcharge of God's righteousness — He restores 'that which I took not away' (Ps 69:4).
