verses.kjv_text: And if a soul sin, and commit any of these things which are forbidden to be done by the commandments of the LORD; though he wist it not, yet is he guilty, and shall bear his iniquity.
verses.original_text: וְאִם נֶפֶשׁ כִּי תֶחֱטָא וְעָשְׂתָה אַחַת מִכָּל מִצְוֹת יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר לֹא תֵעָשֶׂינָה וְלֹא יָדַע וְאָשֵׁם וְנָשָׂא עֲוֺנוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im nefesh ki techeta ve'asetah achat mikkol mitzvot YHVH asher lo te'aseynah velo yada ve'ashem venasa avono.
verses.study_note: The second Asham case: violation of God's commandments (mitzvot YHVH) without full awareness. 'Velo yada' — he did not know. The critical theological statement arrives: 've'ashem venasa avono' — he is guilty AND he bears his iniquity. Without any qualification, without any mitigation: he is guilty. The ignorance does not remove the guilt. This verse is one of the most important in all of Leviticus for understanding the objective nature of guilt before God. Subjective awareness does not determine objective moral accountability. Paul's argument in Romans 2:12-16 engages this principle: those without the law are judged by natural law; those with the law are judged by revealed law. In both cases, guilt is objective. This is why Paul says 'by the law is the knowledge of sin' (Rom 3:20) — the law does not create sin, it reveals what was already there.
verses.doctrinal_note: The principle of objective guilt regardless of subjective awareness ('velo yada ve'ashem') is the Levitical basis for the universal human condition described in Romans 3:19-23. All humanity is guilty before God — not merely those who had the law explicitly, but those whose conscience testifies against them (Rom 2:15). The guilt offering (asham) for unknown commandment violations is the sacrificial acknowledgment that sin has occurred even when the sinner was unaware. Christ's atoning work as the Asham (Isa 53:10) covers not only the sins we know about but the vast, dark territory of sins committed in ignorance — 'cleanse thou me from secret faults' (Ps 19:12). The blood of Jesus covers what we cannot see about ourselves.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall bring a ram without blemish out of the flock, with thy estimation, for a trespass offering, unto the priest: and the priest shall make an atonement for him concerning his ignorance wherein he erred and wist it not, and it shall be forgiven him.
verses.original_text: וְהֵבִיא אַיִל תָּמִים מִן־הַצֹּאן בְּעֶרְכְּךָ לְאָשָׁם אֶל־הַכֹּהֵן וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן עַל שִׁגְגָתוֹ אֲשֶׁר שָׁגַג וְלֹא יָדַע וְנִסְלַח לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehevi ayil tamim min-hattzon be'erkkha le'asham el-hakkohen vekhipper alav hakkohen al shiggatо asher shagag velo yada venislach lo.
verses.study_note: Again the ram without blemish (ayil tamim) with monetary assessment — the identical Asham animal as v.15. Now the atonement formula is applied to the sin of ignorance: 'vekhipper alav hakkohen al shiggatо asher shagag velo yada' — the priest shall make atonement for him concerning his ignorance in which he erred and did not know. The priest atones for what the sinner did not even know he did. This is a profound statement about the reach of priestly intercession: effective beyond the boundaries of the sinner's own awareness. The priest covers what the penitent cannot name. Then: 'venislach lo' — it shall be forgiven him. The forgiveness is as certain for the unknown sin as for the known. Christ our High Priest atones for sins we have not yet confessed, sins we do not yet recognize, the 'secret faults' (Ps 19:12) and 'unknown sins' (Lev 4:2) of our entire lives.

verses.kjv_text: It is a trespass offering: he hath certainly trespassed against the LORD.
verses.original_text: אָשָׁם הוּא אָשֹׁם אָשַׁם לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Asham hu ashom asham laYHVH.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with one of the most emphatic short declarations in the Levitical law: 'Asham hu ashom asham laYHVH' — It is a guilt offering: he has certainly committed a trespass against the LORD. The emphatic infinitive absolute construction ('ashom asham' — literally 'guiltying he is guilty') is the strongest grammatical form of emphasis in Hebrew, equivalent to the English double or triple affirmation: 'he has CERTAINLY, TRULY, UNDENIABLY trespassed.' The trespass is real, objective, and directed specifically against the LORD — not merely a social failure or a human mistake but an offense against the holy God. The chapter that opened with the requirement to confess specific sin (v.5) and legislated a sliding scale all the way down to a handful of flour (v.11) closes with this absolute: however insignificant the trespass may seem, however inadvertent, however small — it is a real asham against the LORD Himself. No sin is trivial before God. And no sin is beyond the reach of the asham's provision.
verses.doctrinal_note: The emphatic closing declaration 'ashom asham laYHVH' — he has certainly committed a trespass against the LORD — is the theological foundation of all sacrificial law: every sin, without exception, is primarily and ultimately an offense against God. David grasps this in Psalm 51:4: 'Against thee, thee only, have I sinned' — even his sin against Uriah and Bathsheba is ultimately and supremely a sin against the LORD. This is why only God can forgive ('salach' — Lev 4:20,26,31,35; 5:10,13,16,18) and only God could provide the ultimate Asham. Isaiah 53:10 — 'when thou shalt make his soul an asham' — is God the Father making the soul of God the Son the guilt offering for the trespass committed against God by humanity. The Offended becomes the Offerer; the Creditor pays the debt. This is the gospel compressed into one verse of Leviticus.

meta.overview: Leviticus 5 occupies a unique transitional position in the sacrificial law. It opens by specifying four concrete cases that require the sin offering of chapter 4 — a public oath-violation (v.1), contact with ritual uncleanness (vv.2-3), and a rash verbal oath (v.4) — and then does something unprecedented in the Levitical system: it introduces a sliding scale of sacrifice (vv.6-13) that adjusts the required offering to the economic capacity of the worshiper. A female lamb or goat is the standard; if that is beyond reach, two turtledoves or pigeons; if even that is impossible, a tenth of an ephah of fine flour. God does not exclude the destitute from atonement. The second half of the chapter (vv.14-19) introduces an entirely new offering category — the Asham (guilt/trespass offering). Where the Chattat (sin offering of ch.4) addresses the state of sinfulness and its defilement, the Asham addresses the specific damage caused by sin — the violation of rights, the breach of trust, the tangible harm done to God or neighbor. The Asham requires both a sacrifice AND a restitution payment of the principal plus one fifth. Two cases are given: inadvertent misappropriation of holy things belonging to the LORD (vv.14-16) and violation of God's commandments without full awareness (vv.17-19). Together, these two offerings — the graduated Chattat and the initiatory Asham — reveal the comprehensive reach of divine grace: no sinner is too poor to be forgiven, no category of sin is too subtle to be covered.
meta.theme: The Graduated Sin Offering, the Requirement of Confession & the Introduction of the Guilt Offering
meta.keyWordMeaning: Guilt offering / Guilt — from the root 'asham' (to be guilty, to incur a debt, to trespass). The word covers both the objective state of guilt and the sacrifice that addresses it. Unlike the chattat (which addresses the nature of sin and its defilement), the asham addresses the damage, the debt, and the violation of rights that sin creates. It requires not only sacrifice but restitution.
outline: Four Specific Cases of Sin (vv.1-4): Suppression of testimony; contact with unclean animals; contact with human uncleanness; a rash oath
The Requirement of Confession (v.5): Sin acknowledged before the offering is brought
The Sliding Scale of Sacrifice (vv.6-13): Female lamb/goat → two birds → fine flour — God provides a way for every economic level
Introduction of the Asham — Guilt Offering (vv.14-16): Inadvertent misappropriation of holy things; a ram, restitution, and one-fifth added
The Asham for Unknown Commandment Violations (vv.17-19): Sin against God's commandments without full awareness; guilt is objective regardless of knowledge

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: The formulaic divine-speech introduction marks a new legislative section within the Mosaic revelation at Sinai. This exact phrase — 'And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — appears over ninety times in Leviticus alone, functioning as a formal authorization stamp on every piece of legislation that follows. In the Hebrew, the divine name יְהוָה (YHWH) frames the communication, emphasizing that these are not Mosaic inventions but direct divine disclosure. The trespass offering laws of verses 2-7 represent a completion and expansion of the guilt-offering section begun in chapter 5, here moving beyond inadvertent cultic violations to deliberate interpersonal fraud.

verses.kjv_text: If a soul sin, and commit a trespass against the LORD, and lie unto his neighbour in that which was delivered him to keep, or in fellowship, or in a thing taken away by violence, or hath deceived his neighbour;
verses.original_text: נֶפֶשׁ כִּי תֶחֱטָא וּמָעֲלָה מַעַל בַּיהוָה וְכִחֵשׁ בַּעֲמִיתוֹ בְּפִקָּדוֹן אוֹ בִתְשׂוּמֶת יָד אוֹ בְגָזֵל אוֹ עָשַׁק אֶת־עֲמִיתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Nefesh ki techeta uma'alah ma'al baYHWH vekhikhesh ba'amito befikkad on o vitsomet yad o vegazel o ashak et-'amito.
verses.study_note: The verse opens a crucial theological connection: sins against a neighbor are simultaneously defined as 'trespass against the LORD' (ma'al baYHWH). The word מַעַל (ma'al, H4603) denotes a treacherous act, a breach of covenant — originally used of unfaithful violation of a sacred trust. By framing interpersonal fraud as ma'al against YHWH, the text reflects the covenantal structure in which all ethical obligations are grounded in the divine-human relationship. Four categories of offense are listed: (1) פִּקָּדוֹן (piqqadon) — a deposit entrusted to one's care; (2) תְּשׂוּמֶת יָד (tessomet yad) — a pledge put into the hand, a business partnership; (3) גָּזֵל (gazel) — violent seizure, robbery; (4) עֹשֶׁק (oshek) — oppression, unjust withholding of wages or property. These span the full range of social-commercial dishonesty known to the ancient Near East.
verses.doctrinal_note: The theological principle here — that sin against a neighbor constitutes trespass against YHWH — is foundational to biblical ethics. It anticipates Christ's summary of the law: love of God and love of neighbor are inseparable (Matt 22:37-40). The NT reinforces this in 1 John 4:20: one cannot claim love for God while sinning against the brother. James 5:4 invokes the same principle, declaring that defrauded wages 'cry out' before the Lord of Sabaoth. Levitical ethics is never merely social — it is always covenantal.

verses.kjv_text: Or have found that which was lost, and lieth concerning it, and sweareth falsely; in any of all these that a man doeth, sinning therein:
verses.original_text: אוֹ מָצָא אֲבֵדָה וְכִחֶשׁ בָּהּ וְנִשְׁבַּע עַל־שָׁקֶר עַל־אַחַת מִכֹּל אֲשֶׁר־יַעֲשֶׂה הָאָדָם לַחֲטֹא בָהֵנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: O matza aveidah vekhikhesh bah venishba al-shaqer al-achat mikkol asher-ya'aseh ha'adam lachato vahenah.
verses.study_note: This verse adds a fifth category to the previous four: the sin of finding lost property, denying it, and reinforcing the lie with a false oath. The false oath is particularly grave because it invokes the name of YHWH in the service of deception, violating the third commandment (Exod 20:7). The phrase 'in any of all these that a man doeth' functions as a comprehensive legal catch-all, ensuring no variant of these social sins escapes the restitutive requirement. The word אֲבֵדָה (aveidah — lost thing) appears in Deuteronomy 22:1-3 in the obligation to restore found property to its owner, showing the legal and ethical coherence of Torah legislation across books.

verses.kjv_text: Then it shall be, because he hath sinned, and is guilty, that he shall restore that which he took violently away, or the thing which he hath deceitfully gotten, or that which was delivered him to keep, or the lost thing which he found,
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה כִּי־יֶחֱטָא וְאָשֵׁם וְהֵשִׁיב אֶת־הַגְּזֵלָה אֲשֶׁר גָּזָל אוֹ אֶת־הָעֹשֶׁק אֲשֶׁר עָשָׁק אוֹ אֶת־הַפִּקָּדוֹן אֲשֶׁר הֻפְקַד אִתּוֹ אוֹ אֶת־הָאֲבֵדָה אֲשֶׁר מָצָא׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah ki-yecheta ve'ashem veheshiv et-hagg'zelah asher gazal o et-ha'oshek asher ashak o et-happiqqadon asher hufkad itto o et-ha'aveidah asher matza.
verses.study_note: Verse 4 establishes the foundational Levitical principle: guilt (asham) requires restitution (heshiv) before sacrifice can be offered. The verb וְהֵשִׁיב (veheshiv, H7725 — to return, restore) appears first, before any mention of the offering, deliberately sequencing justice before ritual. This order is theologically crucial — it demonstrates that sacrifice was never intended to substitute for moral accountability but to accompany it after wrongdoing is rectified. Each of the four fraud categories from verse 2 is recapped, linking the obligation directly to each specific offense. The repetition is pedagogically intentional: there is no general path around restitution, only the specific undoing of each specific wrong.
verses.doctrinal_note: The restitution-before-sacrifice sequence in verse 4 directly anticipates Christ's teaching in Matthew 5:23-24: 'If thou bring thy gift to the altar, and there rememberest that thy brother hath ought against thee; leave there thy gift before the altar, and go thy way; first be reconciled to thy brother.' Jesus is not innovating; he is applying the Levitical principle that the holiness of worship requires prior restoration of broken human relationships. This is the Levitical foundation for what the Reformers called the 'civil use of the law' — its function in ordering human society justly.

verses.kjv_text: Or all that about which he hath sworn falsely; he shall even restore it in the principal, and shall add the fifth part more thereto, and give it unto him to whom it appertaineth, in the day of his trespass offering.
verses.original_text: אוֹ מִכֹּל אֲשֶׁר־יִשָּׁבַע עָלָיו לַשֶּׁקֶר וְשִׁלַּם אֹתוֹ בְּרֹאשׁוֹ וַחֲמִשִׁתָיו יֹסֵף עָלָיו לַאֲשֶׁר הוּא לוֹ יִתְּנֶנּוּ בְּיוֹם אַשְׁמָתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: O mikkol asher-yishshava alav lashsheqer veshillam oto be'rosho vachamishitav yosef alav la'asher hu lo yittenenu beyom ashemato.
verses.study_note: The twenty-percent surcharge (chamishit — a fifth) levied on top of the principal serves both a penal and a restorative function. It acknowledges that theft or fraud causes more harm than the mere monetary value of what was taken — there is loss of use, emotional damage, and breach of trust. The phrase 'in the day of his trespass offering' (beyom ashemato) is precise: the restitution must be made on the very same day the sacrifice is offered, not before or after. There is an urgency and simultaneity required that prevents any separation between the acts of justice and worship. Zacchaeus in Luke 19 embodies this principle, making fourfold restitution — exceeding even the Levitical minimum — as the fruit of genuine repentance.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall bring his trespass offering unto the LORD, a ram without blemish out of the flock, with thy estimation, for a trespass offering, unto the priest:
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־אֲשָׁמוֹ יָבִיא לַיהוָה אַיִל תָּמִים מִן־הַצֹּאן בְּעֶרְכְּךָ לְאָשָׁם אֶל־הַכֹּהֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-ashamo yavi laYHWH ayil tamim min-hattzon be'erkekha le'asham el-hakkohen.
verses.study_note: The guilt offering requires a ram (ayil) — a mature male sheep — unlike the burnt offering which could range from a bull to a turtledove based on financial capacity. The ram's fixed status reflects the gravity of these particular offenses: fraud and false oaths are not minor infractions but substantial violations of covenant. The qualification תָּמִים (tamim — without blemish) appears across all major offerings, signaling that only perfect animals adequately represent the perfect moral standard required. The phrase בְּעֶרְכְּךָ (be'erkekha — with thy estimation/valuation) introduces the role of a priest-assessed monetary equivalent, allowing for accountability and standardization in the offering.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall make an atonement for him before the LORD: and it shall be forgiven him for any thing of all that he hath done in trespassing therein.
verses.original_text: וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וְנִסְלַח לוֹ עַל אַחַת מִכֹּל אֲשֶׁר־יַעֲשֶׂה לְאַשְׁמָה בָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhipper alav hakkohen lifnei YHWH venislach lo al achat mikkol asher-ya'aseh le'ashmah vah.
verses.study_note: This verse concludes the trespass offering section with the definitive outcome: atonement (kipper) made by the priest before YHWH, followed by divine forgiveness (venislach — 'and it shall be forgiven him'). The structure is architecturally significant: restitution (vv.4-5) → offering (v.6) → priestly atonement (v.7a) → divine forgiveness (v.7b). No step can be skipped. The Niphal form of סָלַח (salach — to forgive) is notable: it is used exclusively of God's forgiveness in the Old Testament. No human being is ever the subject of salach — only YHWH forgives in this way. The comprehensive phrase 'for any thing of all that he hath done' signals the completeness of the divine pardon when the full process is followed.
verses.doctrinal_note: The verb כִּפֶּר (kipper — to make atonement) is the theological heartbeat of Leviticus. It derives from the root kafar, meaning to cover, to ransom, to wipe away. The Piel (intensive) form used here indicates vigorous, deliberate covering of guilt. The entire sacrificial system of Leviticus is a shadow (Heb 10:1) pointing to the one priestly act of atonement that alone fully satisfies: Christ presenting his own blood before the Father (Heb 9:24-26). The sequence here — restitution, sacrifice, priestly intercession, divine forgiveness — maps onto the gospel: genuine repentance, substitutionary atonement, Christ's heavenly intercession, and justification by grace.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: A second divine speech formula marks the beginning of an entirely new section — the priestly instructions (torot) for handling the sacrifices. The shift from verse 1 to verse 8 is more than sectional; it is a change of audience. Verses 1-7 addressed the worshiper (the one who had sinned). From verse 9 onward, Moses is commanded to speak to 'Aaron and his sons' — the priestly class. This transition represents a second layer of Levitical legislation: one governing what the offerer must do, another governing what the priest must do with what the offerer brings. Both layers are equally divine in origin and authority.

verses.kjv_text: Command Aaron and his sons, saying, This is the law of the burnt offering: It is the burnt offering, because of the burning upon the altar all night unto the morning, and the fire of the altar shall be burning in it.
verses.original_text: צַו אֶת־אַהֲרֹן וְאֶת־בָּנָיו לֵאמֹר זֹאת תּוֹרַת הָעֹלָה הִוא הָעֹלָה עַל מוֹקְדָה עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ כָּל־הַלַּיְלָה עַד־הַבֹּקֶר וְאֵשׁ הַמִּזְבֵּחַ תּוּקַד בּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Tzav et-Aharon ve'et-banav lemor zot torat ha'olah hi ha'olah al mokedah al-hammizbeach kol-halaylah ad-habboker ve'esh hammizbeach tukkad bo.
verses.study_note: The command opens with צַו (tzav — command, H6680), the imperative form of the root that names the entire book Leviticus in the Hebrew tradition ('Vayikra' means 'And He called,' while this section is characterized by the stronger verb of command). The phrase זֹאת תּוֹרַת הָעֹלָה (zot torat ha'olah — 'this is the law/instruction of the burnt offering') introduces the Torah-formula used four times in chapters 6-7. The burnt offering (olah — that which goes up) must burn all night until morning (kol-halaylah ad-habboker), demonstrating that the worshiper's consecration to God does not end at sunset but persists through the darkness. The altar fire is specified as 'of the altar' — divinely lit (Lev 9:24) — and must be maintained.
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase תּוֹרַת הָעֹלָה (torat ha'olah — the law of the burnt offering) marks the first of five Torah-formulas in chapters 6-7. This structural device is deliberate: the same term Torah used for the entire Mosaic revelation (Deut 4:44) is applied here to each specific offering instruction, signaling that these priestly regulations carry the full weight of divine revelation. The overnight burning of the burnt offering (olah — going up entirely to God) typologically points to Christ's total self-offering — not partial, not reserved, but wholly consecrated. The writer of Hebrews sees this fulfillment in Christ who 'offered himself without spot to God' (Heb 9:14).

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall put on his linen garment, and his linen breeches shall he put upon his flesh, and take up the ashes which the fire hath consumed with the burnt offering on the altar, and he shall put them beside the altar.
verses.original_text: וְלָבַשׁ הַכֹּהֵן מִדּוֹ בַד וּמִכְנְסֵי בַד יִלְבַּשׁ עַל־בְּשָׂרוֹ וְהֵרִים אֶת־הַדֶּשֶׁן אֲשֶׁר תֹּאכַל הָאֵשׁ אֶת־הָעֹלָה עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וְשָׂמוֹ אֶצֶל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Velavash hakkohen middo vad umikhnsei vad yilbash al-besaro veheirim et-haddeshen asher tokhel ha'esh et-ha'olah al-hammizbeach vesammo etzel hammizbeach.
verses.study_note: The ash-removal ritual is among the most humble priestly duties, yet it receives careful legislative attention. The priest must don his full linen vestments (מִדּוֹ בַד — middo vad, his linen tunic) and linen breeches even for this early-morning task, emphasizing that no duty before YHWH is casual or beneath dignity. The ashes (דֶּשֶׁן — deshen) represent the fully consumed sacrifice — what the fire has reduced of the offering. Placing them 'beside the altar' rather than discarding them shows reverence: the residue of consecration is not refuse. Linen is consistently specified for priestly garments (Exod 28:39-43) because it does not cause sweating — a symbol of the holy service not being done in fleshly effort.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall put off his garments, and put on other garments, and carry forth the ashes without the camp unto a clean place.
verses.original_text: וּפָשַׁט אֶת־בְּגָדָיו וְלָבַשׁ בְּגָדִים אֲחֵרִים וְהוֹצִיא אֶת־הַדֶּשֶׁן אֶל־מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה אֶל־מָקוֹם טָהוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Ufashat et-begadav velavash begadim acherim vehotzi et-haddeshen el-michuts lamachaneh el-maqom tahor.
verses.study_note: The garment change required for ash transport illustrates the graded holiness system of the Levitical camp. The most holy vestments worn at the altar were not to be taken outside the camp's sacred perimeter, lest they transmit holiness into ordinary space or be defiled by contact with the common world. The priest therefore changes into 'other garments' — presumably ordinary priestly wear — before carrying the ashes to the clean place outside. The detail מָקוֹם טָהוֹר (maqom tahor — 'a clean place') indicates that even the ash dump must be ritually clean, not merely any convenient location. This same 'outside the camp' geography will become profoundly significant in Hebrews 13:11-13, applied to Christ's suffering.

verses.kjv_text: And the fire upon the altar shall be burning in it; it shall not be put out: and the priest shall burn wood on it every morning, and lay the burnt offering in order upon it; and he shall burn thereon the fat of the peace offerings.
verses.original_text: וְהָאֵשׁ עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ תּוּקַד בּוֹ לֹא תִכְבֶּה וּבִעֵר עָלֶיהָ הַכֹּהֵן עֵצִים בַּבֹּקֶר בַּבֹּקֶר וְעָרַךְ עָלֶיהָ הָעֹלָה וְהִקְטִיר עָלֶיהָ חֶלְבֵי הַשְּׁלָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Veha'esh al-hammizbeach tukkad bo lo tikhbeh uvi'er aleha hakkohen etzim babboker babboker ve'arakh aleha ha'olah vehiqtir aleha chelvei hashsh'lamim.
verses.study_note: The repeated command לֹא תִכְבֶּה (lo tikhbeh — 'it shall not be put out') echoes in both this verse and verse 13, the double repetition underlining its absolute priority. The priestly duty each morning (babboker babboker — 'morning by morning,' the distributive repetition emphasizing regularity) to add wood ensures the fire is actively maintained, not merely allowed to survive. The fat (chelev) of the peace offerings is specifically added to the perpetual fire — the richest portion of the animal, reserved for God (Lev 3:16-17), burns on the same flame that consumed yesterday's burnt offering. This creates a visual theology: the fire of total consecration (burnt offering) and the fire of fellowship and gratitude (peace offering) share one eternal altar flame.
verses.doctrinal_note: The perpetual altar fire (esh tamid) is one of the most powerful symbols in the Levitical system. Kindled miraculously by YHWH at the inauguration of the tabernacle worship (Lev 9:24), it was to be humanly sustained but never artificially restarted if extinguished — any other fire was 'strange fire' (Lev 10:1). Theologically, this fire typifies the Holy Spirit, who descended at Pentecost as tongues of fire (Acts 2:3) and whose presence is the continuous, unquenchable source of divine life in the believer. Paul's injunction 'Quench not the Spirit' (1 Thess 5:19) is a direct New Covenant echo of the Levitical command 'it shall not be put out.'

verses.kjv_text: The fire shall ever be burning upon the altar; it shall never go out.
verses.original_text: אֵשׁ תָּמִיד תּוּקַד עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ לֹא תִכְבֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Esh tamid tuqqad al-hammizbeach lo tikhbeh.
verses.study_note: This terse, majestic sentence stands as the single most iconic command in the burnt offering legislation. In only six Hebrew words, it encapsulates the theology of perpetual worship: 'A fire continually shall be burning upon the altar; it shall not go out.' The word תָּמִיד (tamid, H8548) — 'continual, perpetual, regular' — is the same word used for the daily (tamid) offerings of the tabernacle (Exod 29:38-42) and the Menorah that burns תָּמִיד (Exod 27:20). It communicates unbroken, uninterrupted continuity. The altar of the outer court burns without ceasing — day and night, Sabbath and festival, war and peace — a ceaseless testimony that YHWH is never without a worshiper, never without an offering burning before him.
verses.doctrinal_note: אֵשׁ תָּמִיד (esh tamid — perpetual fire) is one of the deepest typological images in the Old Testament. The fire represents the consuming holiness of God (Heb 12:29) that must be continually met by sacrifice. In Christ, this finds its ultimate fulfillment: his intercession at the right hand of the Father is described as perpetual and unceasing — 'he ever liveth to make intercession' (Heb 7:25). The Levitical fire never went out because the need for atonement never ceases; the priestly intercession of Christ never ceases because the church's need for advocacy never ceases. John's vision in Revelation 4:5 of 'seven lamps of fire burning before the throne' is the eschatological counterpart of this Levitical esh tamid.

verses.kjv_text: And this is the law of the meat offering: the sons of Aaron shall offer it before the LORD, before the altar.
verses.original_text: וְזֹאת תּוֹרַת הַמִּנְחָה הַקְרֵב אֹתָהּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן לִפְנֵי יְהוָה אֶל־פְּנֵי הַמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Vezot torat hamminchah haqrev otah benei Aharon lifnei YHWH el-penei hammizbeach.
verses.study_note: The second Torah-formula introduces the grain offering legislation from the priestly perspective. The KJV renders מִנְחָה (minchah) as 'meat offering' following the older English usage in which 'meat' meant food generally, not specifically flesh. The minchah was a bloodless offering of fine flour, oil, and frankincense (Lev 2) — the grain or produce offering representing the fruit of human agricultural labor brought before YHWH. The priests are now addressed as the handlers of what the worshiper brings. לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (lifnei YHWH — before the LORD) and אֶל־פְּנֵי הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (el-penei hammizbeach — before the face of the altar) together emphasize the double presence: before the divine Person and before the sacred object.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall take of it his handful, of the flour of the meat offering, and of the oil thereof, and all the frankincense which is upon the meat offering, and shall burn it upon the altar for a sweet savour, even the memorial of it, unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהֵרִים מִמֶּנּוּ בְּקֻמְצוֹ מִסֹּלֶת הַמִּנְחָה וּמִשַּׁמְנָהּ וְאֵת כָּל־הַלְּבֹנָה אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַמִּנְחָה וְהִקְטִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחָה רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ אַזְכָּרָתָהּ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Veheirim mimmennu bequmtzo missolet hamminchah umishamnah ve'et kol-hallevonah asher al-hamminchah vehiqtir hammizbeachah rei'ach nichoach azkaratah laYHWH.
verses.study_note: The priestly act of taking the handful (qometz — cupped handful) is precise and deliberate: a measured portion representing the whole is elevated (herim) and burned. The all the frankincense (kol-hallevonah) is burned entirely — frankincense belongs wholly to God and cannot be retained by the priest. The term אַזְכָּרָה (azkarah — memorial portion, H234) designates the burned portion as a 'reminder' before YHWH, recalling the worshiper's devotion in God's presence. Rabbinic tradition understood the azkarah as activating God's remembrance (zikaron) of the offerer — not informing an omniscient God but enacting the covenantal dynamic in which the worshiper formally presents himself before YHWH.

verses.kjv_text: And the remainder thereof shall Aaron and his sons eat: with unleavened bread shall it be eaten in the holy place; in the court of the tabernacle of the congregation they shall eat it.
verses.original_text: וְהַנּוֹתֶרֶת מִמֶּנָּה יֹאכְלוּ אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו מַצּוֹת תֵּאָכֵל בַּחֲצַר אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד יֹאכְלוּהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehannoterot mimmenah yokhlu Aharon uvanav matzot te'akhel bachatsar ohel mo'ed yokhluha.
verses.study_note: The remainder (notiret — that which is left over after the memorial portion is burned) becomes the priestly portion for Aaron and his sons. This priestly eating is not optional or incidental — it is theologically integral. The priest who eats the grain offering participates in bearing the worshiper's offering before God; eating is the sacramental act of priestly identification with the sacrifice. The requirement that it be unleavened (matzot) reflects the universal Levitical principle that leaven symbolizes corruption and pride (1 Cor 5:8), and that all that remains for human consumption after divine service must be in its pure, uncorrupted form. The location — the court of the tabernacle of meeting — maintains the sacred geography of consumption.
verses.doctrinal_note: The priestly eating of the grain offering remainder establishes a pattern that runs through all the 'most holy' offerings: the priest who ministers the offering participates in its sanctifying effect. This anticipates the NT theology of the Lord's Supper, where the church's priestly eating (1 Cor 10:16-18) signifies participation in the one offering of Christ. Paul explicitly invokes the Levitical parallel: 'Are not they which eat of the sacrifices partakers of the altar?' (1 Cor 10:18). The unleavened bread requirement finds its NT counterpart in Paul's exhortation to 'keep the feast... with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth' (1 Cor 5:8).

verses.kjv_text: It shall not be baken with leaven. I have given it unto them for their portion of my offerings made by fire; it is most holy, as is the sin offering, and as the trespass offering.
verses.original_text: לֹא תֵאָפֶה חָמֵץ חֶלְקָם נָתַתִּי אֹתָהּ מֵאִשַּׁי קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הִוא כַּחַטָּאת וְכָאָשָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Lo te'afeh chametz chelqam natatti otah me'ishhai qodesh qadashim hi kachatta't vekha'asham.
verses.study_note: The prohibition against leaven is stated absolutely and without exception. The ownership declaration 'I have given it' (natatti) is emphatic — God himself assigns the priestly portion, making it a divine gift rather than a human wage. The classification קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים (qodesh qadashim — most holy, the superlative of holiness) places the grain offering in the highest category alongside the sin offering and guilt offering. This ranking is theologically significant: though bloodless, the grain offering shares the sanctity of the blood-offerings when handled by the priest. The comparison to the sin offering (chatta't) and trespass offering (asham) signals that even the offerings representing gratitude and consecration carry the same weight of divine holiness as those representing atonement.
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים (qodesh qadashim) — the superlative of holiness — appears in Leviticus to classify the inner sanctuary (Most Holy Place, Exod 26:33), the ark (Exod 30:10), and the key offerings. It represents the highest category of the holy, that which admits no degree of mixture with the common. In the NT, the fulfillment of 'most holy' is Christ himself — the one in whom the divine and the human unite without corruption, who offers himself as the ultimate qodesh qadashim (Heb 9:3,11-14). The leaven-free requirement anticipates Paul's exhortation about 'the sincere milk of the Word' — the pure, unfermented gospel without admixture of human tradition.

verses.kjv_text: All the males among the children of Aaron shall eat of it. It shall be a statute for ever in your generations concerning the offerings of the LORD made by fire: every one that toucheth them shall be holy.
verses.original_text: כָּל־זָכָר בִּבְנֵי אַהֲרֹן יֹאכְלֶנָּה חָק־עוֹלָם לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם מֵאִשֵּׁי יְהוָה כֹּל אֲשֶׁר־יִגַּע בָּהֶם יִקְדָּשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Kol-zakhar bivenei Aharon yokhalenah chuq-olam ledoroteikhem me'ishhei YHWH kol asher-yigga' bahem yiqqadash.
verses.study_note: The restriction to males (kol-zakhar) for eating most holy offerings reflects the patrilineal priestly structure of Israel's worship system. The declaration חָק־עוֹלָם (chuq-olam — 'a statute forever') signals permanent, unconditional legislation valid across all generations. The final clause — 'every one that toucheth them shall be holy' (kol asher-yigga' bahem yiqqadash) — introduces the principle of transmitted or contagious holiness. Physical contact with the most holy offerings imparts holiness to the person or object that makes contact. This is not mere ritual contamination in the negative sense but the positive transmission of the sanctity belonging to that which has been wholly devoted to YHWH.
verses.doctrinal_note: The transmitted holiness principle ('every one that toucheth them shall be holy') is one of the most distinctive features of Levitical theology and has generated extensive discussion. It stands in apparent tension with the principle that uncleanness is contagious (Haggai 2:12-13), yet both operate simultaneously in the Levitical system. Holiness transmits from the sacred object outward; uncleanness transmits from the defiled person outward. This double dynamic reaches its NT fulfillment in Christ: his touch heals and sanctifies the unclean (Mark 1:41-42; Luke 8:43-48), while his own holiness is never diminished by contact with sinners. He is the ultimate 'most holy' who transmits sanctification to all who touch him in faith (Mark 5:56).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: The third divine speech formula in chapter 6 introduces the most distinctive section of grain offering legislation: the daily offering of the high priest himself. Each new speech formula in Leviticus signals not merely a new topic but a new act of divine disclosure — God has more to say, and each section is given as a fresh word. The structure of the chapter (divine speech → Aaron and sons → divine speech → particular priestly regulation → divine speech → sin offering) mirrors the concentric holiness structure of the tabernacle itself, moving progressively inward to more restricted and more solemn legislation.

verses.kjv_text: This is the offering of Aaron and of his sons, which they shall offer unto the LORD in the day when he is anointed; the tenth part of an ephah of fine flour for a meat offering perpetual, half of it in the morning, and half thereof at night.
verses.original_text: זֶה קָרְבַּן אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו אֲשֶׁר יַקְרִיבוּ לַיהוָה בְּיוֹם הִמָּשַׁח אֹתוֹ עֲשִׂירִת הָאֵפָה סֹלֶת מִנְחָה תָּמִיד מַחֲצִיתָהּ בַּבֹּקֶר וּמַחֲצִיתָהּ בָּעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Zeh qorban Aharon uvanav asher yaqrivu laYHWH beyom himmashach oto asirit ha'eifah solet minchah tamid machatzitah babboker umachatzitah ba'arev.
verses.study_note: This verse introduces the unique grain offering of Aaron and his successors, initiated on the day of their anointing and maintained daily thereafter. The measure — a tenth of an ephah (approximately two liters) of fine flour — is the same as the daily omer of manna (Exod 16:36), the individual's daily portion from God. The offering is described as תָּמִיד (tamid — perpetual, continual), paralleling the perpetual fire of verse 13. Divided equally between morning and evening, it frames the entire day with priestly self-offering. The phrase בְּיוֹם הִמָּשַׁח אֹתוֹ (beyom himmashach oto — 'on the day when he is anointed') connects the offering directly to the anointing, inaugurating the priest's ministry with this act of self-dedication.
verses.doctrinal_note: The anointed priest's daily grain offering (qorban ha-mashiach — literally 'the offering of the anointed') is uniquely offered wholly to God without any portion being retained by the priest or his sons (v.23). This stands in deliberate contrast to the regular grain offering where the priests eat the remainder. The theological principle: the priest who offers for others cannot keep what belongs to God on his own behalf. He is a mediator, not a beneficiary. This typology reaches its fullest expression in Christ, the Anointed One (Mashiach), who 'pleased not himself' (Rom 15:3) but offered himself entirely for others — the High Priest whose offering was wholly consumed in the service of our redemption, retaining nothing for himself (Phil 2:6-8).

verses.kjv_text: In a pan it shall be made with oil; and when it is baken, thou shalt bring it in: and the baken pieces of the meat offering shalt thou offer for a sweet savour unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: עַל־מַחֲבַת בַּשֶּׁמֶן תֵּעָשֶׂה מֻרְבֶּכֶת תְּבִיאֶנָּה תֻּפִינֵי מִנְחַת פִּתִּים תַּקְרִיב רֵיחַ־נִיחֹחַ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Al-machavat bashshemen te'aseh murbekhet tevi'ennah tuffinei minchat pittim taqriv rei'ach-nichoach laYHWH.
verses.study_note: The detailed preparation instructions — a griddle pan (machavat), mixed thoroughly with oil (murbekhet — thoroughly mixed or moistened), brought in baked, and presented as broken pieces (pittim) — reflect careful liturgical precision. The word מֻרְבֶּכֶת (murbekhet, H7246) indicates a well-saturated, mixed dough, ensuring the oil is thoroughly incorporated. The offering is then broken into pieces (pittim — morsels) before being offered. The fragmentation into pieces mirrors what happens with the grain offering in chapter 2, ensuring even burning and complete consumption. The sweet savor (rei'ach nichoach) formula confirms divine acceptance.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest of his sons that is anointed in his stead shall offer it: it is a statute for ever unto the LORD; it shall be wholly burnt.
verses.original_text: וְהַכֹּהֵן הַמָּשִׁיחַ תַּחְתָּיו מִבָּנָיו יַעֲשֶׂה אֹתָהּ חָק־עוֹלָם לַיהוָה כָּלִיל תִּקְטָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vehakkohen hammashiach tachtav mibanav ya'aseh otah chuq-olam laYHWH kalil tiqtar.
verses.study_note: The anointed successor priest (hamashiach tachtav — 'the anointed one in his stead') inherits the obligation of the daily offering. The office is perpetual; the individual occupant changes but the offering continues uninterrupted across generations. The word כָּלִיל (kalil — wholly, entirely) is the definitive classification: this offering is entirely consumed, no portion reserved. Kalil appears in Deuteronomy 13:16 and 33:10 for wholly burned offerings and in 1 Samuel 7:9 for a whole burnt offering. The contrast with verse 16 is deliberate: the regular grain offering has a priestly remainder; the anointed priest's offering has none.
verses.doctrinal_note: The term הַמָּשִׁיחַ (hamashiach — the anointed one) here refers to Aaron's high-priestly successors, but the very vocabulary anticipates the ultimate fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the Mashiach par excellence. The daily offering wholly burned without remainder typifies Christ's total self-giving in the incarnation and atonement. Philippians 2:6-8 describes this as kenosis — the emptying of prerogatives, the complete offering of self without reservation. Unlike every Aaronic high priest who ate portions of the offerings that belonged to him, Christ the Great High Priest offered himself wholly, 'who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God' (Heb 9:14). This is the chuq-olam (eternal statute) fulfilled.

verses.kjv_text: For every meat offering for the priest shall be wholly burnt: it shall not be eaten.
verses.original_text: וְכָל־מִנְחַת כֹּהֵן כָּלִיל תִּהְיֶה לֹא תֵאָכֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol-minchat kohen kalil tihyeh lo te'akhel.
verses.study_note: This brief ruling is a sweeping principle that extends beyond just the high priestly daily offering to all grain offerings brought by any priest. The contrast with lay grain offerings (where priests eat the remainder) is absolute: a priest cannot eat of his own offering. The logic is rooted in the mechanics of priestly mediation — the priest stands in the gap between the offerer and YHWH, presenting the offering on behalf of another. If the priest himself is the offerer, there is no mediatorial dynamic; the offering belongs entirely to God. This principle will be extended in the NT: Christ, the Great High Priest, did not offer for himself because he had no sin (Heb 7:26-27), and his self-offering was wholly given — kalil, entirely — for the people.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: The fourth divine speech formula in chapter 6 opens the final and most sobering section: the law of the sin offering for the priests. After regulating the burnt offering (vv.8-13), the grain offering (vv.14-18), and the anointed priest's special offering (vv.19-23), the text now turns to the most theologically complex offering — the chatta't (sin offering). The progression from the offering of total consecration (olah) through communal thanksgiving (minchah) to the handling of sin (chatta't) mirrors the comprehensive scope of the priestly vocation: mediating every dimension of the human-divine relationship.

verses.kjv_text: Speak unto Aaron and to his sons, saying, This is the law of the sin offering: In the place where the burnt offering is killed shall the sin offering be killed before the LORD: it is most holy.
verses.original_text: דַּבֵּר אֶל־אַהֲרֹן וְאֶל־בָּנָיו לֵאמֹר זֹאת תּוֹרַת הַחַטָּאת בִּמְקוֹם אֲשֶׁר תִּשָּׁחֵט הָעֹלָה תִּשָּׁחֵט הַחַטָּאת לִפְנֵי יְהוָה קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Dabber el-Aharon ve'el-banav lemor zot torat hachatta't bimeqom asher tishshachet ha'olah tishshachet hachatta't lifnei YHWH qodesh qadashim hi.
verses.study_note: The third Torah-formula — זֹאת תּוֹרַת הַחַטָּאת (zot torat hachatta't — 'this is the law of the sin offering') — introduces the priestly regulations for the chatta't. The most striking initial requirement is the geographical: the sin offering must be slaughtered in the same location as the burnt offering — the north side of the altar (cf. Lev 1:11). This spatial alignment is theologically deliberate: the place of total consecration (burnt offering) is the same place where atonement for sin is made. There is no separate geography for grace and holiness; they meet at the same altar, in the same place, before the same God. The sin offering, like the burnt offering and grain offering, is classified as 'most holy' — the severest category demanding strictest purity protocols.
verses.doctrinal_note: The spatial coincidence of the burnt offering and sin offering at the same location is a profound liturgical statement: consecration and atonement are not competing but complementary. The worshiper who consecrates himself wholly to God (olah) and the worshiper who seeks forgiveness for sin (chatta't) both come to the same place, before the same LORD. In Christ, this merging is complete: he is simultaneously the wholly consecrated Son (the perfect olah — Matt 3:17) and the sin-bearing substitute (the ultimate chatta't — 2 Cor 5:21). The same cross at Calvary is both the place of his total self-offering to the Father and the place of our atonement. One location, one altar, one sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: The priest that offereth it for sin shall eat it: in the holy place shall it be eaten, in the court of the tabernacle of the congregation.
verses.original_text: הַכֹּהֵן אֲשֶׁר יְחַטְּאֶנָּה יֹאכְלֶנָּה בְּמָקוֹם קָדֹשׁ תֵּאָכֵל בַּחֲצַר אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Hakkohen asher yechatte'ennah yokhlenah bemaqom qadosh te'akhel bachatsar ohel mo'ed.
verses.study_note: The priest who officiates at the sin offering has both the privilege and the responsibility of eating it. The verb יְחַטְּאֶנָּה (yechatte'ennah — 'who makes atonement with it') is a Piel (intensive) form from chata, showing the vigorous, intentional nature of the priestly atoning act. The priestly eating of the sin offering is a mediatorial act — by consuming the offering, the priest symbolically bears the sin of the offerer, incorporating it and dealing with it within the sacred precincts. Numbers 18:10 confirms this is the highest category of holy food. Deuteronomy 18:1-3 establishes the priestly portions as a covenant entitlement. Eating within the sacred court maintains the sacrifice within the holy zone.

verses.kjv_text: Whatsoever shall touch the flesh thereof shall be holy: and when there is sprinkled of the blood thereof upon any garment, thou shalt wash that whereon it was sprinkled in the holy place.
verses.original_text: כֹּל אֲשֶׁר־יִגַּע בִּבְשָׂרָהּ יִקְדָּשׁ וַאֲשֶׁר יִזֶּה מִדָּמָהּ עַל־הַבֶּגֶד אֲשֶׁר יִזֶּה עָלָיו תְּכַבֵּס בְּמָקוֹם קָדֹשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Kol asher-yigga' bivsarah yiqqadash va'asher yizzeh midamah al-habbeged asher yizzeh alav tekhabbes bemaqom qadosh.
verses.study_note: The sin offering's transmitted holiness (v.18 principle now applied specifically to the sin offering) is so potent that any person touching its flesh, and any garment receiving a blood spatter, must be handled within the sacred precincts. The blood of the sin offering is particularly dangerous to mishandle because it carries the concentrated holiness of the atoning act — the sins it has borne make it both most holy and uniquely charged. The garment must be washed בְּמָקוֹם קָדֹשׁ (in the holy place), not in any common water source. The seriousness with which blood is treated reflects the theology of Leviticus 17:11 — the life is in the blood, and atoning blood belongs to the sphere of the holy.
verses.doctrinal_note: The careful treatment of the sin offering's blood illustrates the gravity of atonement. Atoning blood is not waste; it is the most sacred substance in the Levitical world because it has borne the weight of human sin in the act of propitiation. This explains why the New Testament so frequently invokes 'the blood of Christ' as the specific agent of salvation (Heb 9:12-14; 1 Pet 1:18-19; Rev 1:5). The blood is not a metaphor for death generically but the specific atoning substance that carries the sins of the people. The washing requirement also typologically anticipates the NT call to be 'washed... in the blood of the Lamb' (Rev 7:14), maintaining the connection between the atoning blood and the purification it effects.

verses.kjv_text: But the earthen vessel wherein it is sodden shall be broken: and if it be sodden in a brasen pot, it shall be both scoured, and rinsed in water.
verses.original_text: וּכְלִי חֶרֶשׂ אֲשֶׁר תְּבֻשַּׁל בּוֹ יִשָּׁבֵר וְאִם בִּכְלִי נְחֹשֶׁת בֻּשָּׁלָה וּמֹרַק וְשֻׁטַּף בַּמָּיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Ukhli cheres asher tevushshal bo yishshaver ve'im bikhli nechoshet bushshalah umoraq veshuuttaf bammayim.
verses.study_note: The distinction between earthenware (cheres) and bronze (nechoshet) vessels reflects practical holiness law. Earthenware is porous and absorbs the residue of whatever is cooked in it; if the sin offering's most-holy juices have permeated its clay walls, no washing can fully extract what the porous material has absorbed. The vessel must be broken and destroyed, not merely cleaned, preventing any future contamination of ordinary food from residual holiness. Bronze, being non-porous, can be thoroughly scoured (moraq) and rinsed (shutaf) — a two-stage cleaning process. This material theology anticipates the doctrine of sanctification: some corruption requires radical removal (breaking), while other defilement can be cleansed without destruction.

verses.kjv_text: All the males among the priests shall eat thereof: it is most holy.
verses.original_text: כָּל־זָכָר בַּכֹּהֲנִים יֹאכַל אֹתָהּ קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Kol-zakhar bakkohanim yokhal otah qodesh qadashim hi.
verses.study_note: The eating restriction to male priests mirrors the grain offering rule of verse 18, confirming the consistent treatment of most-holy offerings. The sin offering is the highest category of the atoning offerings, and its consumption is restricted to those consecrated for that purpose. The threefold declaration across verses 25, 29, and its thematic echo throughout — 'it is most holy' — forms a literary refrain that drums the sanctity of the sin offering into priestly consciousness. Every priest who handles the chatta't must do so knowing it is not ordinary food or common work but the most holy transaction of the covenant — the bearing of human sin by the atoning sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: And no sin offering, whereof any of the blood is brought into the tabernacle of the congregation to reconcile withal in the holy place, shall be eaten: it shall be burnt in the fire.
verses.original_text: וְכָל־חַטָּאת אֲשֶׁר יוּבָא מִדָּמָהּ אֶל־אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד לְכַפֵּר בַּקֹּדֶשׁ לֹא תֵאָכֵל בָּאֵשׁ תִּשָּׂרֵף׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol-chatta't asher yuva midamah el-ohel mo'ed lekhapper baqqodesh lo te'akhel ba'esh tishsharef.
verses.study_note: The final verse introduces the crucial distinction that governs the sin offering's treatment: when the blood of a sin offering is brought inside the tabernacle (into the holy place or the most holy place) for atonement, the entire carcass must be burned outside the camp, not eaten by the priests. This applies specifically to the sin offerings for the whole congregation and for the anointed priest (Lev 4:3-21), whose blood is sprinkled before the veil and on the incense altar inside the sanctuary. The blood's entry into the holy place disqualifies the flesh from priestly consumption — once atonement is made in the innermost place, the offering has been wholly given over to God. The burning outside the camp creates an important spatial theology: the most solemn atonement ends outside the sacred precincts, where the carcass is consumed.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is the Levitical foundation for one of the most striking christological arguments in the entire New Testament. Hebrews 13:11-13 explicitly applies this law to Christ: 'For the bodies of those beasts, whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin, are burned without the camp. Wherefore Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate. Let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp, bearing his reproach.' Christ is the sin offering whose blood was carried into the heavenly sanctuary (Heb 9:12) and whose body was 'burned' — suffered, died — outside Jerusalem's gate. The call to go 'outside the camp' is a call to identify with the sin-bearing Christ in his reproach, following the sin offering to where it was wholly consumed in bearing the weight of human guilt.

meta.overview: Leviticus 6 stands at a critical juncture in the Mosaic legislation, pivoting from the worshiper-oriented sacrificial laws of chapters 1-5 to the priest-oriented instructions for handling the offerings. The chapter divides into two major movements. Verses 1-7 complete the guilt/trespass offering legislation (begun in 5:14), now addressing sins of fraud, theft, extortion, false oaths, and unjust withholding — offenses that combine trespass against YHWH with concrete injury to a neighbor. Critically, full restitution plus a twenty-percent penalty must precede the bringing of the ram — demonstrating that biblical atonement is never divorced from restorative justice. From verse 8 onward, God addresses not the congregation but Aaron and his sons, delivering the תּוֹרוֹת (torot — 'instructions') that govern priestly handling of each sacrifice: the perpetual-fire command for the burnt offering (vv.8-13), the grain offering regulation including the high priest's own daily offering (vv.14-23), and the sin offering laws (vv.24-30). The theological centerpiece of the chapter is the command of verses 12-13 — 'The fire shall ever be burning upon the altar; it shall never go out' — which captures the unceasing nature of Israel's consecration to YHWH and typologically points to the eternal, inexhaustible sufficiency of Christ's atoning work (Heb 7:25). Throughout, the phrase קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים (qodesh qadashim — 'most holy') marks the burnt, grain, sin, and guilt offerings as belonging to the highest category of sacred objects, demanding absolute priestly purity in their handling. The anointed priest's daily grain offering (vv.19-23), offered wholly to God and never eaten, stands in deliberate contrast to the regular grain offering portions consumed by the priests — typologically anticipating Christ, who unlike Aaron offered himself entirely for others, retaining nothing. The chapter closes (vv.24-30) with the pivotal distinction: sin offerings whose blood enters the sanctuary are wholly burned, not eaten, a principle that the writer of Hebrews applies directly to the sufferings of Christ outside the gate (Heb 13:11-13).
meta.theme: The Priestly Torah: Perpetual Fire, Most Holy Offerings, and Atonement Through Restitution
meta.keyWordMeaning: Instruction/law-of — the bound (construct) form of Torah, appearing four times in this chapter to introduce each major section: 'the law of the burnt offering' (v.9), 'the law of the grain offering' (v.14), and 'the law of the sin offering' (v.25). Torah derives from the root יָרָה (yarah, H3384), meaning 'to shoot, to point the way, to instruct,' carrying the sense of divine directional guidance rather than merely legal obligation. These torot are not burdensome regulations but priestly wisdom, divinely ordered pathways for maintaining the holy fire of covenant relationship.
outline: The Trespass Offering Completed: Fraud, False Oath, and Restitution (vv.1-7): Restitution plus a fifth before the ram is brought
The Law of the Burnt Offering: The Perpetual Fire (vv.8-13): Priestly ash removal and the eternal altar flame
The Law of the Grain Offering: Most Holy Portions for the Priests (vv.14-18): The handful burned, the remainder eaten unleavened
The Anointed Priest's Daily Grain Offering (vv.19-23): Wholly burned — the offering that must not be eaten
The Law of the Sin Offering: Holiness, Vessels, and the Sanctuary (vv.24-30): Transmitted holiness and the wholly-burned offering whose blood enters the sanctuary

verses.kjv_text: Likewise this is the law of the trespass offering: it is most holy.
verses.original_text: וְזֹאת תּוֹרַת הָאָשָׁם קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vezot torat ha'asham qodesh qadashim hu.
verses.study_note: The fourth Torah-formula of chapters 6-7 opens the guilt offering legislation. The word וְזֹאת (vezot — 'and this/likewise this') links the chapter to what preceded, continuing the priestly instruction seamlessly from chapter 6. The guilt offering (asham, H817) is immediately classified as קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים (qodesh qadashim — 'most holy'), the superlative holiness category. This classification places it alongside the sin offering and grain offering in the highest tier of sacred objects, demanding the strictest handling protocols. The guilt offering addressed specific violations involving sacred property and social trust (Lev 5:14-6:7), and its most-holy status signals that these interpersonal offenses have a vertical dimension — they are ultimately trespasses against the holiness of YHWH himself.
verses.doctrinal_note: The guilt offering (asham) is one of the most theologically nuanced offerings in the Levitical system. Isaiah 53:10 uses the precise term asham to describe Christ's self-offering: 'When thou shalt make his soul an offering for sin (asham).' This is the only place in the prophetic literature where a specific Levitical offering type is applied directly to the Messiah's death. The guilt offering specifically addressed violations of holy property and broken trust — precisely what human sin constitutes before God: trespass against his holiness and breach of covenant. Christ's death as an asham thus carries the specific nuance of making full restitution for humanity's breach of covenant with God.

verses.kjv_text: In the place where they kill the burnt offering shall they kill the trespass offering: and the blood thereof shall he sprinkle round about upon the altar.
verses.original_text: בִּמְקוֹם אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁחֲטוּ אֶת־הָעֹלָה יִשְׁחֲטוּ אֶת־הָאָשָׁם וְאֶת־דָּמוֹ יִזְרֹק עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Bimeqom asher yishchatu et-ha'olah yishchatu et-ha'asham ve'et-damo yizroq al-hammizbeach saviv.
verses.study_note: The guilt offering is slaughtered in the same northern location as the burnt offering (Lev 1:11) and the sin offering for the layperson (Lev 4:29). This geographical consistency creates a visual theology: the most solemn acts of atonement and consecration share common ground. The blood treatment of the asham differs from the sin offering: rather than being applied to the horns of the altar (Lev 4:25), the asham blood is זָרַק (zaraq — sprinkled/dashed) round about the altar, the same treatment as the burnt offering (Lev 1:5). This links the guilt offering's atonement to the pattern of total consecration, indicating that guilt is dealt with in the same relational register as full dedication to God.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall offer of it all the fat thereof; the rump, and the fat that covereth the inwards,
verses.original_text: וְאֶת כָּל־חֶלְבּוֹ יַקְרִיב מִמֶּנּוּ אֵת הָאַלְיָה וְאֶת הַחֵלֶב הַמְכַסֶּה אֶת הַקֶּרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et kol-chelbo yaqriv mimmennu et ha'alyah ve'et hachelev hamekhasseh et haqqerev.
verses.study_note: The fat portions offered from the guilt offering are identical to those of the peace offering (Lev 3:9-10): the broad tail (alyah, the fatty appendage of the eastern broad-tailed sheep), and the fat covering the viscera (chelev hamekhasseh et haqqerev). The fat (chelev) is the richest, most energy-dense portion of the animal and is consistently reserved for YHWH across all animal offerings (Lev 3:16-17). This recurring allocation of the choicest part to God reflects the theology of firstfruits: what is best belongs to the Creator. The fat's burning on the altar produces the rich, fragrant smoke that ascends as the 'sweet savor' indicating divine acceptance.

verses.kjv_text: And the two kidneys, and the fat that is on them, which is by the flanks, and the caul that is above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away:
verses.original_text: וְאֵת שְׁתֵּי הַכְּלָיֹת וְאֶת הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עֲלֵיהֶן אֲשֶׁר עַל הַכְּסָלִים וְאֶת הַיֹּתֶרֶת עַל הַכָּבֵד עַל הַכְּלָיֹת יְסִירֶנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et shtei hakkelajot ve'et hachelev asher aleihen asher al-hakkesalim ve'et hayoterot al-hakkabed al-hakkelajot yesirenah.
verses.study_note: The kidneys and the lobe (or caudate lobe) of the liver are among the most metabolically active organs in the body, dense with blood vessels and fatty tissue. In the ancient Near East, the kidneys were considered the seat of the deepest inner emotions and will (Ps 7:9; 16:7; 26:2), which may explain their prominent place in the divine portion. The caul (yoteret) of the liver — translated variously as the lobe or appendage — refers to a specific fatty protrusion of the liver. By reserving these 'inner organs' for God, the legislation enacts a theology: the hidden, deep interior of the creature is offered to the One who searches all hearts (Ps 139:23).

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall burn them upon the altar for an offering made by fire unto the LORD: it is a trespass offering.
verses.original_text: וְהִקְטִיר אֹתָם הַכֹּהֵן הַמִּזְבֵּחָה אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה אָשָׁם הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiqtir otam hakkohen hammizbeachah ishsheh laYHWH asham hu.
verses.study_note: The priestly burning of the fat portions upon the altar is the culminating act of the guilt offering's divine portion. The verb וְהִקְטִיר (vehiqtir — 'and he shall burn') from the root qatar (H6999) specifically denotes the burning of offerings to produce fragrant smoke, distinguishing it from ordinary burning (saraf). The smoke ascending from the altar communicates the priestly offering before YHWH in the vertical dimension, while the blood application to the altar sides (v.2) addresses the horizontal dimension of atonement. The final declaration אָשָׁם הוּא (asham hu — 'it is a guilt offering') is a formal legal certification: this act constitutes a valid guilt offering before YHWH.
verses.doctrinal_note: The burning of the fat on the altar as the divine portion of the guilt offering is the Levitical embodiment of Romans 4:25 — Christ was 'delivered for our offences.' The asham's fat ascending to God as the richest portion typologically represents Christ's perfect obedience (his 'choicest' self) being presented to the Father at the cross. The ascending smoke — the rei'ach nichoach, the pleasing aroma — speaks of a Father well-pleased with the Son's self-offering (Matt 3:17; Eph 5:2). The formal legal certification 'it is a guilt offering' anticipates Paul's declaration: God made Christ 'to be sin for us' (2 Cor 5:21) — a legal divine verdict of substitution.

verses.kjv_text: Every male among the priests shall eat thereof: it shall be eaten in the holy place: it is most holy.
verses.original_text: כָּל־זָכָר בַּכֹּהֲנִים יֹאכְלֶנּוּ בְּמָקוֹם קָדֹשׁ יֵאָכֵל קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Kol-zakhar bakkohanim yokhlennu bemaqom qadosh ye'akhel qodesh qadashim hu.
verses.study_note: The priestly eating of the guilt offering follows the same rules as the sin offering (Lev 6:26,29): restricted to male priests, consumed in the holy place (the tabernacle court), and declared 'most holy.' This consistent pattern across the two atoning offerings (sin and guilt) reinforces their shared theological function: both deal with the problem of guilt before YHWH and require the same strict handling. The priest who eats of the most holy offering participates in the mediatorial act — he who officiates at the atonement also bears the offering's sanctity by ingesting it within the sacred boundary. This communal eating within the holy place is a priestly sacramental act, distinct from the ordinary food consumed in the camp.

verses.kjv_text: As the sin offering is, so is the trespass offering: there is one law for them: the priest that maketh atonement therewith shall have it.
verses.original_text: כַּחַטָּאת כָּאָשָׁם תּוֹרָה אַחַת לָהֶם הַכֹּהֵן אֲשֶׁר יְכַפֶּר בּוֹ לוֹ יִהְיֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Kachatta't ka'asham torah achat lahem hakkohen asher yekapper bo lo yihyeh.
verses.study_note: The declarative principle — 'one law for them' (torah achat lahem) — is among the most explicit statements of legal equivalence in the Torah. The sin offering (chatta't) and guilt offering (asham) are here formally equated in their priestly handling, their most-holy status, their eating restrictions, and their priestly portions. The phrase הַכֹּהֵן אֲשֶׁר יְכַפֶּר (hakkohen asher yekapper — 'the priest who makes atonement') identifies the specific priest who officiated as the one who receives the priestly portion — a direct personal reward for the mediatorial act. This links faithful priestly service to divinely ordained compensation, establishing the covenant of priestly sustenance as inseparable from the act of atonement itself.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest that offereth any man’s burnt offering, even the priest shall have to himself the skin of the burnt offering which he hath offered.
verses.original_text: וְהַכֹּהֵן הַמַּקְרִיב אֶת עֹלַת אִישׁ עוֹר הָעֹלָה אֲשֶׁר הִקְרִיב לַכֹּהֵן לוֹ יִהְיֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehakkohen hammakriv et olat ish or ha'olah asher hiqriv lakkohen lo yihyeh.
verses.study_note: The hide (or) of the burnt offering is assigned to the officiating priest as his compensation. This is the only portion of the burnt offering that does not ascend to God in fire; the hide, being the outer covering of the animal, is removed before the sacrifice and becomes the priestly perquisite. The economic logic is clear: priests had no land inheritance in Canaan (Num 18:20; Deut 18:1-2) and depended entirely on the offerings for material provision. Hides in the ancient Near East were enormously valuable — used for clothing, tents, scrolls, water containers, and trade goods. The assignment of hides to the individual officiating priest, rather than to the priestly order collectively, rewarded personal diligence in service.

verses.kjv_text: And all the meat offering that is baken in the oven, and all that is dressed in the fryingpan, and in the pan, shall be the priest’s that offereth it.
verses.original_text: וְכָל מִנְחָה אֲשֶׁר תֵּאָפֶה בַּתַּנּוּר וְכָל נַעֲשָׂה בַמַּרְחֶשֶׁת וְעַל מַחֲבַת לַכֹּהֵן הַמַּקְרִיב אֹתָהּ לוֹ תִהְיֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol minchah asher te'afeh battannur vekhol na'asah bammarkheshet ve'al machavat lakkohen hammakriv otah lo tihyeh.
verses.study_note: Three methods of grain offering preparation are enumerated: oven-baked (tannur), pan-cooked (markheshet — a deep pan with a lid), and griddle-cooked (machavat — flat griddle, mentioned in Lev 6:21). All three types of the grain offering go entirely to the officiating priest when prepared as cooked offerings. This contrasts with the uncooked grain offering, whose remainder (after the azkarah is burned) is shared among the priests collectively (v.10). The principle here rewards the priest who does the specific work of offering with the specific benefit of that offering — a consistent pattern across chapters 6-7 that honors individual priestly labor.

verses.kjv_text: And every meat offering, mingled with oil, and dry, shall all the sons of Aaron have, one as much as another.
verses.original_text: וְכָל מִנְחָה בְלוּלָה בַשֶּׁמֶן וַחֲרֵבָה לְכָל בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן תִּהְיֶה אִישׁ כְּאָחִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol minchah velulah bashshemen vachaRevah lekhol benei Aharon tihyeh ish ke'achiv.
verses.study_note: The uncooked grain offerings — those mixed with oil (belulah bashshemen) and those dry (charevah, perhaps the uncooked flour offering) — are divided equally among all the sons of Aaron: 'each as his brother' (ish ke'achiv). This stands in contrast to verses 8-9 where cooked offerings go to the individual officiant. Uncooked offerings require no individual labor of preparation and so belong to the priestly community collectively. The equal distribution principle (ish ke'achiv — man as his brother) establishes a community of equity within the Aaronic priesthood: differential reward for individual labor, but equal portion for collective entitlement.

verses.kjv_text: And this is the law of the sacrifice of peace offerings, which he shall offer unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְזֹאת תּוֹרַת זֶבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים אֲשֶׁר יַקְרִיב לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vezot torat zevach hashsh'lamim asher yaqriv laYHWH.
verses.study_note: The fifth and final Torah-formula of chapters 6-7 introduces the peace offering, the most extensive and theologically rich of all five offerings. The full designation זֶבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים (zevach hashsh'lamim) combines two terms: zevach (a slaughtered sacrifice, involving blood) and shelamim (peace/completeness offerings, plural). The shelamim is the only offering that directly involves the lay worshiper eating before YHWH — creating the unique dynamic of a meal shared between the offerer, the priests, and God himself. This makes it the most explicitly covenantal of the offerings: a table fellowship that enacts the reconciled relationship between YHWH and Israel.
verses.doctrinal_note: The peace offering (shelamim) stands as the most direct OT typological precursor to the NT Lord's Supper. Both involve a slaughtered sacrifice, the eating of the sacrifice in community, and the sharing of a meal in the context of the covenant. Paul's eucharistic theology in 1 Corinthians 10:16-18 explicitly draws on the Levitical peace offering: 'Are not they which eat of the sacrifices partakers of the altar?' The Lord's Supper is the fulfilment of the shelamim — the New Covenant peace meal in which Christ is both the sacrifice and the host, the offering and the one in whose presence the feast is kept. Shalom (peace) achieved by his blood (Col 1:20) is the foundation of the eternal feast.

verses.kjv_text: If he offer it for a thanksgiving, then he shall offer with the sacrifice of thanksgiving unleavened cakes mingled with oil, and unleavened wafers anointed with oil, and cakes mingled with oil, of fine flour, fried.
verses.original_text: אִם עַל תּוֹדָה יַקְרִיבֶנּוּ וְהִקְרִיב עַל זֶבַח הַתּוֹדָה חַלּוֹת מַצּוֹת בְּלוּלֹת בַּשֶּׁמֶן וּרְקִיקֵי מַצּוֹת מְשֻׁחִים בַּשֶּׁמֶן וְסֹלֶת מֻרְבֶּכֶת חַלֹּת בְּלוּלֹת בַּשָּׁמֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Im al todah yaqrivennu vehiqriv al zevach hatodah challot matzot belulot bashshemen ureqiqei matzot meshuchim bashshemen vesolet murbekhet challot belulot bashshamen.
verses.study_note: The thanksgiving offering (todah) is the first and most solemn sub-type of the peace offering. It is offered in response to a specific divine act of deliverance or salvation — when God has answered prayer, rescued from danger, or shown extraordinary mercy. Psalm 107 provides the paradigmatic framework for the todah: four types of distress (desert wandering, imprisonment, illness, sea storm) each resolved by divine rescue, each answered with the call 'Let them offer the sacrifices of thanksgiving (todah)' (Ps 107:22). The elaborate bread accompaniment — three types of unleavened bread plus leavened loaves (v.13) — exceeds that of any other offering, reflecting the lavish gratitude of the delivered worshiper.
verses.doctrinal_note: The thanksgiving offering (todah) occupies a uniquely exalted place in later Hebrew theology and early Christian interpretation. Psalm 50:23 declares 'Whoso offereth praise (todah) glorifieth me,' and rabbinic tradition taught that when all other sacrifices cease in the Messianic age, the todah would remain forever. Hebrews 13:15 directly applies todah theology to the NT: 'By him therefore let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually, that is, the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name.' The 'sacrifice of praise' (thusia aineseos) in the LXX of Psalm 107:22 renders todah. Christ's resurrection is the ultimate divine act of deliverance that calls forth the eternal todah of his redeemed people.

verses.kjv_text: Besides the cakes, he shall offer for his offering leavened bread with the sacrifice of thanksgiving of his peace offerings.
verses.original_text: עַל חַלֹּת לֶחֶם חָמֵץ יַקְרִיב קָרְבָּנוֹ עַל זֶבַח תּוֹדַת שְׁלָמָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Al challot lechem chametz yaqriv qorbano al zevach todat shh'lamav.
verses.study_note: The leavened bread (lechem chametz) accompanying the thanksgiving offering is the single exception in all Levitical legislation where leavened bread is presented as part of an offering. Critically, the leavened bread is not burned on the altar — it is brought near (yaqriv) as part of the offering complex and then eaten by the worshipers and priests (v.15). Its inclusion alongside the three types of unleavened bread reflects a theological completeness: leavened bread represents ordinary human life, the bread of everyday existence, brought into the presence of God. The todah encompasses all of life — the holy (unleavened) and the ordinary (leavened) — in one comprehensive act of grateful thanksgiving.

verses.kjv_text: And of it he shall offer one out of the whole oblation for an heave offering unto the LORD, and it shall be the priest’s that sprinkleth the blood of the peace offerings.
verses.original_text: וְהִקְרִיב מִמֶּנּוּ אֶחָד מִכָּל קָרְבָּן תְּרוּמָה לַיהוָה לַכֹּהֵן הַזֹּרֵק אֶת דַּם הַשְּׁלָמִים לוֹ יִהְיֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiqriv mimmennu echad mikkol qorban terumah laYHWH lakkohen hazzoreq et dam hashsh'lamim lo yihyeh.
verses.study_note: From the four types of bread accompanying the thanksgiving offering, one cake from each type is designated as a תְּרוּמָה (terumah — heave offering, a portion lifted up) for YHWH and given to the priest who sprinkled the blood of the peace offering. The heave offering (terumah, H8641) involved a vertical lifting gesture, presenting the portion upward before God before it passed to the priest. Again, the individual priest who performed the blood rite receives the specific bread portion — reward directly tied to priestly service. The blood-sprinkling priest receives the bread — an elegant literary pairing of the two central ritual acts of the peace offering.

verses.kjv_text: And the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offerings for thanksgiving shall be eaten the same day that it is offered; he shall not leave any of it until the morning.
verses.original_text: וּבְשַׂר זֶבַח תּוֹדַת שְׁלָמָיו בְּיוֹם קָרְבָּנוֹ יֵאָכֵל לֹא יַנִּיחַ מִמֶּנּוּ עַד בֹּקֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Uvasar zevach todat sh'lamav beyom qorbano ye'akhel lo yanniach mimmennu ad boqer.
verses.study_note: The same-day consumption requirement for the thanksgiving offering is the most stringent time limit in the peace offering sub-types. The flesh must be eaten on the very day of the offering; nothing may remain until morning. This urgency reflects the character of the todah itself: genuine thanksgiving cannot be deferred, stored up, or rationed. The offering and the eating are simultaneous acts — the gratitude is expressed fully now. The prohibition against leaving any until morning mirrors the manna prohibition (Exod 16:19-20), where hoarding overnight was a failure of trust in God's daily provision. In both cases, the divine intent is an immediacy of response that cannot be postponed.

verses.kjv_text: But if the sacrifice of his offering be a vow, or a voluntary offering, it shall be eaten the same day that he offereth his sacrifice: and on the morrow also the remainder of it shall be eaten:
verses.original_text: וְאִם נֶדֶר אוֹ נְדָבָה זֶבַח קָרְבָּנוֹ בְּיוֹם הַקְרִיבוֹ אֶת זִבְחוֹ יֵאָכֵל וּמִמָּחֳרָת וְהַנּוֹתָר מִמֶּנּוּ יֵאָכֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im neder o nedavah zevach qorbano beyom haqrivo et zivcho ye'akhel umimmacharat vehannatar mimmennu ye'akhel.
verses.study_note: The vow offering (neder) and freewill offering (nedavah) are granted a more generous consumption window than the thanksgiving offering: the day of offering plus the following day. The distinction reflects the different motivational contexts. The todah responds to a specific, immediate act of divine deliverance and its gratitude is urgent. The neder arises from a deliberate vow made during a time of crisis (Gen 28:20-22; Num 30) and its fulfillment is more measured. The nedavah is entirely spontaneous, arising from the impulse of generous devotion rather than either gratitude or obligation. Both receive a two-day window because neither carries the same urgency of immediacy as the thank offering.

verses.kjv_text: But the remainder of the flesh of the sacrifice on the third day shall be burnt with fire.
verses.original_text: וְהַנּוֹתָר מִבְּשַׂר הַזָּבַח בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי בָּאֵשׁ יִשָּׂרֵף׃
verses.transliteration: Vehannatar mibbasar hazzavach bayyom hashsh'lishi ba'esh yissaref.
verses.study_note: Any flesh remaining on the third day must be burned with fire — it has passed beyond its lawful consumption window and is no longer acceptable. The third-day prohibition may relate to the concern about meat spoilage in the ancient Near Eastern climate, but the legislation frames the issue theologically rather than hygienically. The offering has served its sacred purpose within the appointed time; what remains beyond that boundary is no longer holy food but refuse. Burning it with fire (rather than eating or discarding) ensures that flesh that had been presented before YHWH is not treated as ordinary waste but is disposed of in the sacred manner.

verses.kjv_text: And if any of the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offerings be eaten at all on the third day, it shall not be accepted, neither shall it be imputed unto him that offereth it: it shall be an abomination, and the soul that eateth of it shall bear his iniquity.
verses.original_text: וְאִם הֵאָכֹל יֵאָכֵל מִבְּשַׂר זֶבַח שְׁלָמָיו בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי לֹא יֵרָצֶה הַמַּקְרִיב אֹתוֹ לֹא יֵחָשֵׁב לוֹ פִּגּוּל יִהְיֶה וְהַנֶּפֶשׁ הָאֹכֶלֶת מִמֶּנּוּ עֲוֹנָהּ תִּשָּׂא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im he'akhol ye'akhel mibbesar zevach sh'lamav bayyom hashsh'lishi lo yeiratzeh hammakriv oto lo yechashev lo piggul yihyeh vehannefesh ha'okhelet mimmennu avonah tissa.
verses.study_note: Eating the peace offering on the third day transforms a holy act into an abomination. The technical term פִּגּוּל (piggul, H6292) denotes something ritually offensive, an offering rendered invalid by improper intent or timing. The consequences are threefold: (1) לֹא יֵרָצֶה (lo yeiratzeh — 'it shall not be accepted'), the sacrifice is retroactively disqualified; (2) לֹא יֵחָשֵׁב לוֹ (lo yechashev lo — 'it shall not be reckoned/imputed to him'), no atonement benefit accrues; (3) עֲוֹנָהּ תִּשָּׂא (avonah tissa — 'he shall bear his iniquity'), the offender personally carries the guilt. The language of 'imputation' (chashav) is particularly theologically significant, appearing in Genesis 15:6 and throughout Paul's justification theology.
