verses.doctrinal_note: The term לֹא יֵחָשֵׁב לוֹ (lo yechashev lo — 'it shall not be reckoned/imputed to him') introduces the vocabulary of imputation into the Levitical system. The root חָשַׁב (chashav, H2803) is the same word used in Genesis 15:6 ('and he counted it to him for righteousness') and in Romans 4:3-8 where Paul quotes David's words about the man to whom God 'imputeth righteousness without works.' The peace offering eaten at the wrong time is not merely rejected; it fails to produce the divine reckoning that a valid offering would generate. Conversely, valid sacrifice produces positive divine reckoning. This is the Levitical foundation for the NT doctrine of imputed righteousness — the valid sacrifice of Christ is divinely reckoned (chashav) to the believer's account.

verses.kjv_text: And the flesh that toucheth any unclean thing shall not be eaten; it shall be burnt with fire: and as for the flesh, all that be clean shall eat thereof.
verses.original_text: וְהַבָּשָׂר אֲשֶׁר יִגַּע בְּכָל טָמֵא לֹא יֵאָכֵל בָּאֵשׁ יִשָּׂרֵף וְהַבָּשָׂר כָּל טָהוֹר יֹאכַל בָּשָׂר׃
verses.transliteration: Vehabbas ar asher yigga bekhal tame lo ye'akhel ba'esh yissaref vehabbas ar kol tahor yokhal basar.
verses.study_note: Ritual uncleanness (tumah) can transmit to the flesh of the peace offering through contact, rendering it unfit for consumption. The peace offering's flesh, unlike the most-holy offerings consumed only by priests in the sacred precincts, is eaten by lay Israelites who carry the full range of possible ritual states. The protective ruling here prevents a clean worshiper from unknowingly eating defiled meat by requiring that any contaminated flesh be burned. Conversely, anyone who is ritually clean (tahor) may freely eat the peace offering's flesh — the offering is open and inclusive to all who approach in the proper state. This purity-before-participation principle governs all covenant meal contexts.

verses.kjv_text: But the soul that eateth of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings, that pertain unto the LORD, having his uncleanness upon him, even that soul shall be cut off from his people.
verses.original_text: וְהַנֶּפֶשׁ אֲשֶׁר תֹּאכַל בָּשָׂר מִזֶּבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים אֲשֶׁר לַיהוָה וְטֻמְאָתוֹ עָלָיו וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מֵעַמֶּיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehannefesh asher tokhal basar mizzevach hashsh'lamim asher laYHWH vetumato alav venikretah hannefesh hahiv me'ammeha.
verses.study_note: Eating the peace offering while in a state of uncleanness (tumah) carries the penalty of כָּרֵת (karet — 'cutting off,' v.20 uses the Niphal nikretah). Karet is the most severe non-capital punishment in the Torah, meaning exclusion from the covenant community — whether by divine action, communal excision, or premature death is debated, but the effect is separation from Israel and from YHWH's covenant blessings. The peace offering is 'that which belongs to YHWH' (asher laYHWH), and to approach the covenant table in an unclean state is a direct desecration of divine holiness. This severity reflects the sacredness of covenant fellowship: approaching God's table is a privilege requiring preparation.
verses.doctrinal_note: The karet penalty for eating the peace offering while unclean has a direct NT counterpart in 1 Corinthians 11:27-30, where Paul warns that eating the Lord's Supper 'unworthily' results in eating and drinking 'damnation to himself, not discerning the Lord's body.' Paul notes that some in Corinth are 'weak and sickly' and some have 'fallen asleep' (died) as a result of this profanation — a New Covenant equivalent of karet. The peace offering (shelamim) and the Lord's Supper are the closest typological pair in Scripture: both are covenant meals, both demand ritual/moral preparation, and both carry severe consequences for presumptuous approach.

verses.kjv_text: Moreover the soul that shall touch any unclean thing, as the uncleanness of man, or any unclean beast, or any abominable unclean thing, and eat of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings, which pertain unto the LORD, even that soul shall be cut off from his people.
verses.original_text: וְנֶפֶשׁ כִּי תִגַּע בְּכָל טָמֵא בְּטֻמְאַת אָדָם אוֹ בִּבְהֵמָה טְמֵאָה אוֹ בְּכָל שֶׁקֶץ טָמֵא וְאָכַל מִבְּשַׂר זֶבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים אֲשֶׁר לַיהוָה וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מֵעַמֶּיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Venefesh ki tigga bekhal tame betumaat adam o bivhemah teme'ah o bekhol sheqetz tame ve'akhal mibbasar zevach hashsh'lamim asher laYHWH venikretah hannefesh hahiv me'ammeha.
verses.study_note: Verse 21 extends the defilement categories from verse 20: human uncleanness (tumaat adam — bodily emissions, skin disease, corpse contact), unclean animals (behemah teme'ah — any of the forbidden animals of Lev 11), and abominable unclean things (sheqetz tame — swarming creatures, creeping things). The exhaustive list closes every loophole. The same karet penalty applies regardless of which specific defilement category was contracted. The comprehensive nature of the prohibition reflects the comprehensive holiness required of all who share in the covenant meal of peace. The peace offering's table is not exclusive, but it is holy — open to all who prepare themselves, closed to those who presume upon it.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: A fresh divine speech formula introduces the absolute prohibitions of fat and blood — the two most theologically significant dietary restrictions in the entire Levitical system. These are not merely dietary regulations but statements about divine sovereignty over life itself. The fat (chelev) belongs to God as the richest portion of the animal; the blood (dam) belongs to God as the carrier of the animal's life (Lev 17:11). Both prohibitions carry the karet penalty — the same as eating the peace offering while unclean — indicating that these dietary violations rank among the most serious covenant infractions. The speech formula establishes divine authority for what follows: these are not human opinions but divine declarations.

verses.kjv_text: Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, Ye shall eat no manner of fat, of ox, or of sheep, or of goat.
verses.original_text: דַּבֵּר אֶל בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר כָּל חֵלֶב שׁוֹר וְכֶשֶׂב וָעֵז לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Dabber el benei Yisra'el lemor kol chelev shor vakesev va'ez lo tokhelu.
verses.study_note: The fat prohibition is addressed to 'the children of Israel' — the entire covenant community, not just the priests. The three specified animals (ox/bull, sheep, goat) are the three primary sacrificial animals of the Levitical system. The prohibition of their chelev (hard internal fat) covers precisely those animals most commonly brought to the altar. This creates a consistent theology: what is offered to God on the altar (the fat portions) is not to be consumed by humans. The altar portions and the human portions are permanently distinguished. Eating the fat would be a form of consuming what belongs to God — an act of appropriating the divine prerogative.
verses.doctrinal_note: The absolute prohibition of fat (chelev) reflects one of the deepest principles of Levitical theology: that God claims the best. The fat was the energy-richest, most flavorful portion of the animal — the equivalent of what we would consider the most desirable part. Its reservation for God alone enacts the firstfruits principle: what is finest belongs to the Creator. This anticipates NT stewardship theology: 'Whether therefore ye eat or drink, or whatsoever ye do, do all to the glory of God' (1 Cor 10:31). The fat prohibition is not about dietary health but about divine acknowledgment: the richest of every category of life belongs to the One who gives life.

verses.kjv_text: And the fat of the beast that dieth of itself, and the fat of that which is torn with beasts, may be used in any other use: but ye shall in no wise eat of it.
verses.original_text: וְחֵלֶב נְבֵלָה וְחֵלֶב טְרֵפָה יֵעָשֶׂה לְכָל מְלָאכָה וְאָכֹל לֹא תֹאכְלֻהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vechelev neveIah vechelev terefah ye'aseh lekhol melakhah ve'akhol lo tokhaluhhu.
verses.study_note: A practical distinction: the fat of animals that died naturally (nevelah — carrion) or were killed by predators (terefah — torn) may be used for non-food purposes (lampfuel, leather-working, waterproofing) but must not be eaten. These animals, not having been ritually slaughtered, cannot be offered on the altar — but their fat, while equally prohibited as food, can serve utilitarian purposes. This practical concession prevents waste while maintaining the absolute dietary prohibition. The terefah category is significant: it introduces terminology that became central in later Jewish dietary law (kashrut), where 'treif' (forbidden food) derives from this root.

verses.kjv_text: For whosoever eateth the fat of the beast, of which men offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD, even the soul that eateth it shall be cut off from his people.
verses.original_text: כִּי כָּל אֹכֵל חֵלֶב מִן הַבְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר יַקְרִיב מִמֶּנָּה אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הָאֹכֶלֶת מֵעַמֶּיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki kol okhel chelev min-habb'hemah asher yaqriv mimmenah ishsheh laYHWH venikretah hannefesh ha'okhelet me'ammeha.
verses.study_note: The karet penalty for eating the fat of sacrificial animals is stated explicitly, closing the fat prohibition section with its severest consequence. The specific animals are those from which fire-offerings are brought (asher yaqriv mimmenah ishsheh laYHWH — 'from which one brings a fire-offering to YHWH') — i.e., the sacrificial three (ox, sheep, goat). Eating their fat is not merely a dietary infraction but a form of theft from the altar: it appropriates for human appetite what has been declared divine property. Karet — being cut off from the covenant people — is the proportionate consequence for consuming what belongs to God himself.

verses.kjv_text: Moreover ye shall eat no manner of blood, whether it be of fowl or of beast, in any of your dwellings.
verses.original_text: וְכָל דָּם לֹא תֹאכְלוּ בְּכֹל מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם לָעוֹף וְלַבְּהֵמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol dam lo tokhelu bekhol moshvoteikhem la'of velabbehemah.
verses.study_note: The blood prohibition is far more expansive than the fat prohibition: it covers every species (fowl and beast) in every location ('in all your dwellings' — not just at the sanctuary). The fat prohibition was focused on sacrificial animals; the blood prohibition extends universally. The scope reflects the theological basis: blood is the carrier of life (Lev 17:11), and all life belongs to YHWH regardless of species or location. The phrase בְּכֹל מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם (bekhol moshvoteikhem — 'in all your dwellings') is the same phrase used for the Sabbath and other perpetual ordinances (Lev 23:3), indicating the blood prohibition is a constant of daily life, not merely a sanctuary regulation.
verses.doctrinal_note: The blood prohibition is grounded in the most explicit theological rationale in Leviticus: 'the life of the flesh is in the blood: and I have given it to you upon the altar to make an atonement for your souls' (Lev 17:11). Blood is the medium of atonement because it is the vehicle of life — and life is God's alone to give and to receive back. This theological foundation reaches its NT climax in the blood of Christ: 'the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish' (1 Pet 1:19) is the atoning blood that the animal sacrifices pointed toward. Hebrews 9:22 declares that 'without the shedding of blood there is no remission,' grounding NT atonement in the same theology as the Levitical blood prohibition.

verses.kjv_text: Whatsoever soul it be that eateth any manner of blood, even that soul shall be cut off from his people.
verses.original_text: כָּל נֶפֶשׁ אֲשֶׁר תֹּאכַל כָּל דָּם וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מֵעַמֶּיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Kol nefesh asher tokhal kol dam venikretah hannefesh hahiv me'ammeha.
verses.study_note: The blood prohibition closes with the same karet penalty as the fat prohibition (v.25). The phrasing is deliberate and symmetrical: both prohibitions use 'whosoever' (kol nefesh — 'every soul'), both specify 'shall be cut off from his people' (venikretah... me'ammeha), and both address violations that appropriate for human use what belongs exclusively to God. The parallelism of fat and blood penalties creates a theological pairing: fat (the best of the body's substance) and blood (the life of the body) are the two irreducibly divine portions of every creature. To consume either is to usurp God's exclusive prerogative over the very best and the very life of his creation.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: Another divine speech formula marks the transition to the specific allocation of the peace offering's priestly portions — the wave breast and heave shoulder. The regularity of these speech formulas throughout Leviticus 6-7 creates a liturgical rhythm, each section receiving its own divine mandate. The legislation that follows (vv.29-36) establishes the permanent covenant of priestly sustenance from the peace offerings — extending beyond the most holy priestly portions to the portions eaten by the priests and their families in any clean place (Num 18:11). This transition from prohibition (vv.22-27) to provision (vv.29-36) mirrors the broader Levitical pattern: God defines what is his, then defines what he gives to his servants.

verses.kjv_text: Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, He that offereth the sacrifice of his peace offerings unto the LORD shall bring his oblation unto the LORD of the sacrifice of his peace offerings.
verses.original_text: דַּבֵּר אֶל בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר הַמַּקְרִיב אֶת זֶבַח שְׁלָמָיו לַיהוָה יָבִיא אֶת קָרְבָּנוֹ לַיהוָה מִזֶּבַח שְׁלָמָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Dabber el benei Yisra'el lemor hammakriv et zevach sh'lamav laYHWH yavi et qorbano laYHWH mizzevach sh'lamav.
verses.study_note: The worshiper who brings the peace offering takes an active role in the presentation before YHWH. The repetition of 'the sacrifice of his peace offerings' and 'his oblation' (qorbano) emphasizes personal ownership and personal responsibility: this is his offering, his act of devotion, and he himself must bring it. The worshiper is not merely delivering the animal to a priest as a transaction; he is personally participating in the act of offering. This active worshiper role is characteristic of the peace offering, the only sacrifice in which the offerer eats — he is participant, not merely patron. The personal engagement of the worshiper in bringing his own portion before YHWH is integral to the offering's covenant meaning.

verses.kjv_text: His own hands shall bring the offerings of the LORD made by fire, the fat with the breast, it shall he bring, that the breast may be waved for a wave offering before the LORD.
verses.original_text: יָדָיו תְּבִיאֶינָה אֵת אִשֵּׁי יְהוָה אֶת הַחֵלֶב עַל הַחָזֶה יְבִיאֶנּוּ אֵת הַחָזֶה לְהָנִיף אֹתוֹ תְּנוּפָה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Yadav tevieinah et ishhei YHWH et hachelev al hechazeh yevi'ennu et hechazeh lehaniph oto tenufah lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: The worshiper's own hands (yadav — 'his hands' — the subject is emphatic) must present the fat together with the breast before YHWH. The wave offering gesture (תְּנוּפָה, tenufah, H8573) involves a horizontal swinging or presenting motion before YHWH — moving the offering toward the altar and then back, signifying its presentation before God and its return as a gift to the priest. The fat upon the breast is held together: the divine portion (fat, to be burned) and the priestly portion (breast, to be eaten after waving) are presented simultaneously. The gesture is both liturgical and theological: the offerer's own hands physically enact the covenant exchange between God and his worshiper.
verses.doctrinal_note: The wave offering gesture (tenufah) has been interpreted as a horizontal motion (forward and back) before the altar, signifying that the worshiper presents the offering to YHWH and YHWH returns a portion to the priest. Some have seen in this gesture a typological anticipation of the crucifixion: Christ's arms outstretched horizontally upon the cross, simultaneously presenting himself to the Father and drawing all people to himself (John 12:32). Whether or not this specific typology was intended, the theological dynamic is exact: the cross is the ultimate wave offering — Christ presented wholly to God, and the result (resurrection, salvation, the gift of the Spirit) returned to God's servants. The offerer's hands doing the waving anticipates the 'reasonable service' (logike latreia) of Romans 12:1.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall burn the fat upon the altar: but the breast shall be Aaron’s and his sons’.
verses.original_text: וְהִקְטִיר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַחֵלֶב הַמִּזְבֵּחָה וְהָיָה הַחָזֶה לְאַהֲרֹן וּלְבָנָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiqtir hakkohen et hachelev hammizbeachah vehayah hechazeh le'Aharon ulevana.
verses.study_note: The division of the peace offering's priestly portion is now made explicit: the priest burns the fat on the altar (God's portion), while the breast (which had been waved) becomes the possession of Aaron and all his sons — the entire priestly family. Unlike the portions that go to the individual officiating priest (the hide in v.8, the bread terumah in v.14), the wave breast is communal priestly property. This distinction ensures both individual incentive (personal portions) and community provision (shared portions). The waved breast is thus a priestly family meal — the entire Aaronic household shares in the covenant fellowship of the peace offering.

verses.kjv_text: And the right shoulder shall ye give unto the priest for an heave offering of the sacrifices of your peace offerings.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת שׁוֹק הַיָּמִין תִּתְּנוּ תְרוּמָה לַכֹּהֵן מִזִּבְחֵי שַׁלְמֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et shoq hayyamin tittenu terumah lakkohen mizzivchei shalmeikhhem.
verses.study_note: The right shoulder (shoq hayyamin) is the heave offering (terumah) given to the individual officiating priest, distinct from the wave breast (tenufah) given to Aaron's sons collectively. The right side was consistently the side of honor and strength in the ancient Near East (Gen 48:14; Exod 29:20; Ps 110:1). Giving the right shoulder — the strongest, most prized joint of the animal — to the officiating priest personally honors his specific service. The term shoq (H7785 — thigh/shoulder) refers to the upper foreleg or shoulder of the animal, a large and meaty portion. Together, the breast (waved, communal) and the right shoulder (heaved, individual) constitute the priestly 'share' of the peace offering.

verses.kjv_text: He among the sons of Aaron, that offereth the blood of the peace offerings, and the fat, shall have the right shoulder for his part.
verses.original_text: הַמַּקְרִיב אֶת דַּם הַשְּׁלָמִים וְאֶת הַחֵלֶב מִבְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן לוֹ תִהְיֶה שׁוֹק הַיָּמִין לְמָנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Hammakriv et dam hashsh'lamim ve'et hachelev mibenei Aharon lo tihyeh shoq hayyamin lemanah.
verses.study_note: The right shoulder goes specifically to the son of Aaron who officiated at the peace offering — specifically the one who performed the blood rite and fat-burning (the two most critical priestly acts of the sacrifice). The term לְמָנָה (lemanah, H4490 — 'as his portion/share') formalizes the entitlement as a divinely allocated covenant portion. The individual officiating priest thus receives: the right shoulder (v.33, personal), the bread terumah (v.14, personal), and — for burnt offerings — the hide (v.8, personal). The entire priestly family receives: the wave breast (v.31, communal). This layered system of personal and communal portions rewards individual labor while maintaining community solidarity.

verses.kjv_text: For the wave breast and the heave shoulder have I taken of the children of Israel from off the sacrifices of their peace offerings, and have given them unto Aaron the priest and unto his sons by a statute for ever from among the children of Israel.
verses.original_text: כִּי אֶת חֲזֵה הַתְּנוּפָה וְאֵת שׁוֹק הַתְּרוּמָה לָקַחְתִּי מֵאֵת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מִזִּבְחֵי שַׁלְמֵיהֶם וָאֶתֵּן אֹתָם לְאַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֵן וּלְבָנָיו לְחָק עוֹלָם מֵאֵת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Ki et chazeh hattenufah ve'et shoq hatterumah laqachti me'et benei Yisra'el mizzivchei shalmeihem va'etten otam le'Aharon hakkohen ulevana lechuq olam me'et benei Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: This verse states the theological foundation of the priestly portions with remarkable clarity: God himself took (laqachti — 'I took') the wave breast and heave shoulder from Israel and gave them to Aaron and his sons. The priestly portions are not Israel's gift to the priests; they are God's gift, redistributed from his own share to his servants. The construction 'I took... and I gave' (laqachti... va'etten) frames the priestly stipend as divine action: YHWH receives the offering from Israel and immediately designates a portion for his ministers. The chuq olam ('eternal statute') formula places this covenant of priestly sustenance among the most permanent of Levitical institutions.
verses.doctrinal_note: The pattern of divine reception and redistribution — God 'taking' from Israel and 'giving' to the priests — is the theological architecture of grace. In the NT, this becomes explicit: God 'takes' the sacrifice of Christ and 'gives' the benefits to the church. Ephesians 4:8-11 quotes Psalm 68:18 ('he... gave gifts unto men') and applies it to Christ's ascension: having offered himself (the wave offering), Christ receives from the Father and redistributes gifts to his people — apostles, prophets, teachers. The same divine economy: one takes up (consecration), God receives, God gives to his ministers. Priestly sustenance is thus a type of the gifts of the ascended Christ.

verses.kjv_text: This is the portion of the anointing of Aaron, and of the anointing of his sons, out of the offerings of the LORD made by fire, in the day when he presented them to minister unto the LORD in the priest’s office;
verses.original_text: זֹאת מִשְׁחַת אַהֲרֹן וּמִשְׁחַת בָּנָיו מֵאִשֵּׁי יְהוָה בְּיוֹם הִקְרִיב אֹתָם לְכַהֵן לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Zot mishchat Aharon umishchat banav me'ishhei YHWH beyom hiqriv otam lekahhen laYHWH.
verses.study_note: The term מִשְׁחַת (mishchat, H4888 — 'the anointing portion') is derived from mashach (to anoint), connecting the priestly portions directly to the act of anointing that inaugurated priestly service. The priestly stipend is thus the 'anointing share' — what their anointed status entitles them to receive from the fire-offerings of YHWH. This creates a direct linkage between consecration (anointing) and compensation (the mishat portion): those who have been set apart by divine anointing for the service of YHWH are supported by the offerings of that service. The phrase 'in the day when he presented them to minister' connects the priestly portions to the day of ordination (Lev 8-9) when the entire system was inaugurated.

verses.kjv_text: Which the LORD commanded to be given them of the children of Israel, in the day that he anointed them, by a statute for ever throughout their generations.
verses.original_text: אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה לָתֵת לָהֶם בְּיוֹם מָשְׁחוֹ אֹתָם מֵאֵת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל חָק עוֹלָם לְדֹרֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Asher tzivvah YHWH latet lahem beyom mashcho otam me'et benei Yisra'el chuq olam ledorotam.
verses.study_note: The covenant of priestly sustenance receives its double stamp of permanence: YHWH himself commanded (tzivvah) it on the day of anointing (beyom mashcho), and it is declared a chuq olam (eternal statute) through all generations (ledorotam). Both the divine command and the temporal scope are maximally emphatic. 'The day that he anointed them' refers to the priestly ordination ceremony of Leviticus 8, connecting the ongoing daily portions to the founding moment of priestly consecration. The eternal statute formula (chuq olam ledorotam) appears elsewhere for the Sabbath (Exod 31:16), the Passover (Exod 12:14), and the priesthood itself (Exod 40:15) — placing the priestly covenant of sustenance in the same category as the most foundational ordinances of Israel.

verses.kjv_text: This is the law of the burnt offering, of the meat offering, and of the sin offering, and of the trespass offering, and of the consecrations, and of the sacrifice of the peace offerings;
verses.original_text: זֹאת הַתּוֹרָה לָעֹלָה לַמִּנְחָה וְלַחַטָּאת וְלָאָשָׁם וְלַמִּלּוּאִים וּלְזֶבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Zot hattorah la'olah lamminchan velachatta't vela'asham velammillu'im ulezevach hashsh'lamim.
verses.study_note: Verse 37 is the formal literary colophon that closes the entire first offering block (Lev 1-7). The phrase זֹאת הַתּוֹרָה (zot hattorah — 'this is the law/instruction') functions as a concluding seal, retrospectively gathering all five major offerings under one comprehensive declaration. Six items are named: (1) the burnt offering (olah, ch.1), (2) the grain offering (minchah, ch.2), (3) the sin offering (chatta't, ch.4), (4) the guilt offering (asham, ch.5-6), (5) the consecrations (millu'im — ordination offerings, anticipating ch.8), and (6) the peace offerings (shelamim, ch.3 and 7). The inclusion of the millu'im (ordination/filling offerings) is prospective, pointing forward to the upcoming ordination of Aaron and his sons in Leviticus 8-9.
verses.doctrinal_note: The comprehensive summary in verse 37 is more than a literary device — it is a theological declaration that the entire sacrificial system of Leviticus 1-7 constitutes one unified Torah, one coherent divine instruction, one complete system of approach to YHWH. The writer of Hebrews understands this completeness and announces that the entire system has been fulfilled and superseded in a single event: 'By the which will we are sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all' (Heb 10:10). The six categories of Leviticus summarized here are answered in Christ: he is the olah (total consecration), the minchah (perfect grain of human life), the chatta't (sin bearer, 2 Cor 5:21), the asham (guilt offering, Isa 53:10), the millu'im (the ordaining High Priest, Heb 5:5-6), and the shelamim (our peace, Eph 2:14).

verses.kjv_text: Which the LORD commanded Moses in mount Sinai, in the day that he commanded the children of Israel to offer their oblations unto the LORD, in the wilderness of Sinai.
verses.original_text: אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת מֹשֶׁה בְּהַר סִינָי בְּיוֹם צַוֹּתוֹ אֶת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לְהַקְרִיב אֶת קָרְבְּנֵיהֶם לַיהוָה בְּמִדְבַּר סִינָי׃
verses.transliteration: Asher tzivvah YHWH et Mosheh behar Sinai beyom tzavvoto et benei Yisra'el lehaqriv et qorboneihem laYHWH bemidbar Sinai.
verses.study_note: The final verse of chapter 7 — and of the entire first offering block — grounds the entire Levitical sacrificial system in its historical origin: Mount Sinai. The geographical and historical anchoring (behar Sinai — 'on Mount Sinai'; bemidbar Sinai — 'in the wilderness of Sinai') is deliberate. These are not timeless abstract religious regulations but historically revealed divine legislation, given at a specific place and time to a specific person (Moses) for a specific people (the children of Israel). The double Sinai reference (mountain and wilderness) echoes the canonical weight of the Mosaic revelation throughout Deuteronomy and the Prophets. The entire sacrificial system thus carries the full authority of the Sinai covenant.
verses.doctrinal_note: The closing declaration — 'which the LORD commanded Moses in Mount Sinai' — is the canonical seal that the apostle Paul acknowledges when he calls the entire Mosaic law 'holy, and just, and good' (Rom 7:12). The sacrificial Torah of Leviticus 1-7, rooted in Sinai, is not superseded by Christ as though it were flawed, but fulfilled in him as the one to whom all its types and shadows pointed. Hebrews 8:5 cites Moses' Sinai instructions about the tabernacle ('See... that thou make all things according to the pattern showed to thee in the mount') as the foundation for demonstrating that the earthly is the shadow of the heavenly — the Sinai-given system is a faithful copy of the heavenly reality realized in Christ. This is how the Torah of Leviticus is simultaneously temporary (its sacrifices cease) and eternally valid (its theology is fulfilled in Christ).

meta.overview: Leviticus 7 completes the great legislative block of chapters 1-7 known as the Torah of the Offerings (torot hakorbanot). Chapter 7 operates on two levels simultaneously: it provides the concluding priestly instructions for the guilt offering (asham, vv.1-10) and the peace offering (shelamim, vv.11-36), while also issuing sweeping prohibitions that apply to all Israel (the absolute bans on eating fat and blood, vv.22-27). The chapter closes with a formal literary summary (vv.37-38) that retrospectively names all five offerings treated in chapters 1-7, functioning as the colophon — the closing seal — of the entire first legislative unit of Leviticus. The guilt offering section (vv.1-10) completes the priestly perspective on the asham, specifying the priestly portions (the hide, the grain offerings, the flesh) that accrue to the officiating priests. The bulk of the chapter (vv.11-36) is devoted to the peace offering (shelamim), the most communal and joyful of the five offerings. Three sub-types are distinguished: the thanksgiving offering (todah, v.12), the vow offering (neder, v.16), and the freewill offering (nedavah, v.16) — each with slightly different time constraints for consumption. The peace offering uniquely involves the worshiper eating in fellowship before YHWH, making it the closest OT analog to the NT concept of eucharistic communion. The absolute prohibition of fat (chelev, vv.22-25) and blood (dam, vv.26-27) carries the death penalty for violation, signaling that these substances belong exclusively to God: the fat as the choicest portion of the animal, the blood as the carrier of life itself (Lev 17:11). The wave breast (chazeh hatenuphah, v.30) and heave shoulder (shoq haterumah, v.34) assigned to Aaron and his sons establish the permanent covenant of priestly sustenance, described as 'a statute for ever' in verse 36. The christological resonances of chapter 7 are deep: the peace offering's todah (thanksgiving) points to Christ as the ground of all true praise (Heb 13:15); the blood prohibition grounds the NT theology of propitiation (Rom 3:25; Heb 9:22); and the wave offering's gesture — horizontal motion before YHWH — typologically anticipates the outstretched arms of Christ on the cross, the ultimate wave offering presented before the Father.
meta.theme: The Completion of the Offering Laws: Guilt, Peace, Fat, Blood, and Priestly Portions
meta.keyWordMeaning: Peace offerings — from shalom (wholeness, completeness, wellbeing). The plural form shelamim encompasses all that shalom means: completeness of relationship, restored fellowship, comprehensive wellbeing between God and worshiper. The peace offering is the only sacrifice where the worshiper himself eats before God, making it the sacramental meal of the covenant — the fellowship table of the Levitical system. Its three sub-types (todah, neder, nedavah) cover the full range of Israel's joyful response to divine grace: gratitude for deliverance, fulfilment of solemn vows, and spontaneous voluntary devotion.
outline: The Law of the Guilt Offering: Priestly Portions and Fat (vv.1-7): Most holy, slaughtered in the north, handled like the sin offering
Priestly Portions from Burnt and Grain Offerings (vv.8-10): The hide, baked grain, and mixed grain allocations
The Law of the Peace Offering Introduced (vv.11-15): The thanksgiving sub-type and its unleavened and leavened breads
Vow and Freewill Peace Offerings (vv.16-21): Extended consumption window and the deadly consequence of ritual impurity
The Absolute Prohibition of Fat (vv.22-25): Chelev belongs to YHWH alone — violation is karet (cutting off)
The Absolute Prohibition of Blood (vv.26-27): Dam is the seat of life — karet for any who consume it
The Wave Breast and Heave Shoulder: Aaron's Perpetual Portion (vv.28-36): The gesture of presentation and the eternal priestly covenant of sustenance
The Summary Colophon of All Five Offerings (vv.37-38): The formal close of the first great legislative unit of Leviticus

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: The divine speech formula that opens chapter 8 initiates one of the most theologically charged sequences in the entire Pentateuch: the ordination of Aaron and his sons. This command was first given in Exodus 29, before the tabernacle was even constructed. Now, with the tabernacle standing (Exod 40) and the sacrificial Torah given (Lev 1-7), the moment of priestly inauguration has arrived. The command structure — God speaks to Moses who then acts — establishes the pattern for the entire ordination: it is entirely God's initiative, Moses's execution, and Aaron's reception. No element of the priesthood originates with the priests themselves; they are entirely passive recipients of a divinely ordered consecration.

verses.kjv_text: Take Aaron and his sons with him, and the garments, and the anointing oil, and a bullock for the sin offering, and two rams, and a basket of unleavened bread;
verses.original_text: קַח אֶת אַהֲרֹן וְאֶת בָּנָיו אִתּוֹ וְאֵת הַבְּגָדִים וְאֵת שֶׁמֶן הַמִּשְׁחָה וְאֵת פַּר הַחַטָּאת וְאֵת שְׁנֵי הָאֵילִים וְאֵת סַל הַמַּצּוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Qach et-Aharon ve'et-banav itto ve'et-habbegedim ve'et shemen hammishchah ve'et par hachatta't ve'et shnei ha'eilim ve'et sal hammatzot.
verses.study_note: The divine inventory for the ordination assembles every element needed: (1) Aaron and his sons — the men to be consecrated; (2) the garments (begadim) — the sacred vestments already prepared in Exodus 28; (3) the anointing oil (shemen hammishchah) — the specially compounded oil of Exodus 30:22-33; (4) a bull for the sin offering (par hachatta't); (5) two rams — one for the burnt offering, one for the ordination (millu'im); and (6) a basket of unleavened bread (sal hammatzot). The combination of garments, oil, blood, and bread creates a comprehensive consecration that addresses the priest's identity, power, guilt, dedication, and sustenance simultaneously. Nothing is omitted; the ordination is total.

verses.kjv_text: And gather thou all the congregation together unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת כָּל הָעֵדָה הַקְהֵל אֶל פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et kol-ha'edah haqhel el-petach ohel mo'ed.
verses.study_note: The entire congregation (kol-ha'edah) is summoned to witness the ordination — not merely the Levites or the elders, but all Israel. This public, communal character of the priestly consecration is theologically deliberate: the priesthood is not a private arrangement between God and Aaron but a covenantal institution established before the whole community. The location — פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (petach ohel mo'ed — the door/entrance of the tabernacle of meeting) — is the liminal space between the common camp and the sacred precinct, a threshold of holiness. The congregation gathers at the boundary, witnessing the consecration of those who will cross it on their behalf.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses did as the LORD commanded him; and the assembly was gathered together unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ מֹשֶׁה כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֹתוֹ וַתִּקָּהֵל הָעֵדָה אֶל פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaas Mosheh ka'asher tzivvah YHWH oto vattiqqahel ha'edah el-petach ohel mo'ed.
verses.study_note: The compliance formula — 'Moses did as the LORD commanded him' (ka'asher tzivvah YHWH oto) — is the same refrain that concluded each stage of the tabernacle construction in Exodus 39-40 (appearing seven times there). Its appearance here begins a new series: the ordination ceremony is executed with the same perfect obedience as the tabernacle was built. Moses's unqualified obedience is a recurring theme in Leviticus 8 (vv.4, 9, 13, 17, 21, 29, 36 — seven occurrences), structurally mirroring the seven-fold obedience of Exodus 40 and the seven-day ordination period. Seven-fold obedience for seven days: the priestly consecration is a work of complete, creation-level fidelity.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto the congregation, This is the thing which the LORD commanded to be done.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל הָעֵדָה זֶה הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה לַעֲשׂוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Mosheh el-ha'edah zeh haddavar asher tzivvah YHWH la'asot.
verses.study_note: Moses addresses the assembled congregation before beginning the ordination: 'This is the thing which the LORD commanded to be done.' The declaration זֶה הַדָּבָר (zeh haddavar — 'this is the word/thing') functions as a divine warranty — everything about to occur proceeds from YHWH's specific command. Moses positions himself not as innovator or religious entrepreneur but as executor of a prior divine order. This rhetorical move is crucial: it prevents the congregation from attributing the priesthood to Moses's authority or Aaron's self-appointment. The priesthood is God's institution, established by God's word, executed by God's servant.
verses.doctrinal_note: The principle Moses articulates here — 'This is what the LORD commanded' — is the fundamental hermeneutic of all true worship. Every element of the Levitical system, and by typological extension every element of NT worship, is authorized by divine command, not human preference or religious tradition. Hebrews 8:5 grounds this in the Sinai principle: Moses was told 'See that thou make all things according to the pattern shown thee in the mount.' True priesthood — and in the NT, the entire church's ministry — is a matter of divine command, not human ordination alone. Christ himself was not glorified by self-appointment: 'So also Christ glorified not himself to be made an high priest' (Heb 5:5).

verses.kjv_text: And Moses brought Aaron and his sons, and washed them with water.
verses.original_text: וַיַּקְרֵב מֹשֶׁה אֶת אַהֲרֹן וְאֶת בָּנָיו וַיִּרְחַץ אֹתָם בַּמָּיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqrev Mosheh et-Aharon ve'et-banav vayyirchatz otam bammayim.
verses.study_note: The first act of the ordination is washing: Moses brings Aaron and his sons near (vayyaqrev — the same root as qorban, offering; he 'draws them near' as one draws near an offering) and washes them with water. This washing (rachatz) at the laver (kiyor) of the tabernacle court is the foundational purification upon which all subsequent consecration rests. No robe can cover an unwashed priest; no oil can anoint what has not first been cleansed. The order is theologically irreversible: cleansing precedes clothing, which precedes anointing. Aaron and his sons are entirely passive — Moses washes them. They do not wash themselves.
verses.doctrinal_note: The washing of Aaron and his sons at ordination is one of the richest baptismal typologies in the OT. It is externally applied (Moses washes them), water-based, precedes the bestowal of the Spirit (the anointing oil), and is prerequisite to priestly service. The NT counterpart is unmistakable: Christian baptism is an external washing applied in Christ's name (Acts 22:16; Titus 3:5), which precedes the Spirit's anointing (Acts 10:44-48) and constitutes entry into the royal priesthood (1 Pet 2:9). Hebrews 10:22 unites both dimensions: 'having our hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience, and our bodies washed with pure water.' The Levitical washing is the shadow; baptism into Christ is the substance.

verses.kjv_text: And he put upon him the coat, and girded him with the girdle, and clothed him with the robe, and put the ephod upon him, and he girded him with the curious girdle of the ephod, and bound it unto him therewith.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתֵּן עָלָיו אֶת הַכְּתֹנֶת וַיַּחְגֹּר אֹתוֹ בָּאַבְנֵט וַיִּלְבַּשׁ אֹתוֹ אֶת הַמְּעִיל וַיִּתֵּן עָלָיו אֶת הָאֵפֹד וַיַּחְגֹּר אֹתוֹ בְּחֵשֶׁב הָאֵפֹד וַיֶּאְפֹּד לוֹ בּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitten alav et-hakktonet vayyachgor oto ba'avnet vayyilbash oto et-hamme'il vayyitten alav et-ha'efod vayyachgor oto becheshev ha'efod vayyefod lo vo.
verses.study_note: Moses dresses Aaron in five items in this verse: (1) the coat/tunic (ktonet) — the basic linen undergarment; (2) the girdle/sash (avnet) — a woven belt securing the tunic; (3) the robe (me'il) — the blue woven outer robe with its pomegranates and golden bells at the hem (Exod 28:31-35); (4) the ephod — the sacred breastplate-bearing garment of gold, blue, purple, and scarlet; and (5) the ephod's curious girdle (cheshev ha'efod) — the intricately woven waistband of the ephod. Each garment is applied by Moses's hands to Aaron's body. Aaron is entirely passive, receiving the dignity of high-priestly identity as an external gift. The robe's bells would announce the high priest's movements in the sanctuary so he would not die (Exod 28:35).

verses.kjv_text: And he put the breastplate upon him: also he put in the breastplate the Urim and the Thummim.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשֶׂם עָלָיו אֶת הַחֹשֶׁן וַיִּתֵּן אֶל הַחֹשֶׁן אֶת הָאוּרִים וְאֶת הַתֻּמִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasem alav et-hachoshen vayyitten el-hachoshen et-ha'Urim ve'et-haTummim.
verses.study_note: The breastplate (choshen) is the most elaborate and theologically rich of the high priest's garments, set with twelve precious stones bearing the names of the twelve tribes and carried over the heart (Exod 28:15-30). Moses places it upon Aaron's chest and then inserts the Urim and Thummim into its pouch. The Urim (אוּרִים — lights) and Thummim (תֻּמִּים — perfections) were the divinely ordained means of seeking God's will in specific situations (Num 27:21; 1 Sam 28:6). Their exact nature remains uncertain — they may have been lots or sacred objects — but their function was unambiguous: through them the high priest could discern YHWH's answer to binary questions on behalf of the nation. The high priest thus bore Israel's names on his heart and carried Israel's access to divine guidance on his person.
verses.doctrinal_note: The breastplate with its twelve tribal stones and its Urim and Thummim is the most explicit OT image of high-priestly intercession. The high priest carried Israel's names into the sanctuary on his chest — close to his heart — just as Jesus Christ 'ever liveth to make intercession' for his people (Heb 7:25). The Urim and Thummim (light and perfection) typologically anticipate Christ as the fullness of divine revelation (John 1:9; Col 2:3): in him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, and through him access to the Father's will is perfectly and completely granted. We no longer need lots or sacred objects — we have direct access through the Spirit (1 Cor 2:10-12). The breastplate over Aaron's heart is the type; Christ's intercession is the antitype.

verses.kjv_text: And he put the mitre upon his head; also upon the mitre, even upon his forefront, did he put the golden plate, the holy crown; as the LORD commanded Moses.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשֶׂם אֶת הַמִּצְנֶפֶת עַל רֹאשׁוֹ וַיָּשֶׂם עַל הַמִּצְנֶפֶת אֶל מוּל פָּנָיו אֵת צִיץ הַזָּהָב נֵזֶר הַקֹּדֶשׁ כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasem et-hammitzniefet al rosho vayyasem al-hammitzniefet el-mul panav et-tzitz hazzahav nezer haqqodesh ka'asher tzivvah YHWH et-Mosheh.
verses.study_note: The robing sequence culminates with the head: the mitre (mitzniefet — the white linen turban, H4701) is placed on Aaron's head, and upon the front of the mitre is fastened the golden plate (tzitz hazzahav) engraved with the words קֹדֶשׁ לַיהוָה (qodesh laYHWH — 'Holiness to the LORD,' Exod 28:36). This golden plate is called נֵזֶר הַקֹּדֶשׁ (nezer haqqodesh — the holy crown/consecration), using the term nezer (H5145) which also describes the Nazirite's consecration (Num 6:7) and the royal crown (2 Sam 1:10). The inscription on the high priest's forehead declares his identity: he is YHWH's holy one, dedicated entirely to God. Moses's compliance formula closes the robing section: 'as the LORD commanded Moses.'
verses.doctrinal_note: The golden plate bearing 'Holiness to the LORD' (qodesh laYHWH) upon the high priest's forehead is one of the most profound identity markers in all of Scripture. It declares what the high priest is, not merely what he does: he is YHWH's holy one, consecrated to bear the iniquity of the holy things (Exod 28:38). The typological fulfillment in Christ is exact: he is the Holy One of God (Mark 1:24; Acts 3:14), whose entire being — not merely his acts — is consecrated to the Father. The nezer (crown/consecration) also links the high priest to the king (2 Sam 1:10; Ps 89:39), anticipating Christ's fusion of the priestly and royal offices — the King-Priest of Psalm 110 and Hebrews 7.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses took the anointing oil, and anointed the tabernacle and all that was therein, and sanctified them.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה אֶת שֶׁמֶן הַמִּשְׁחָה וַיִּמְשַׁח אֶת הַמִּשְׁכָּן וְאֶת כָּל אֲשֶׁר בּוֹ וַיְקַדֵּשׁ אֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Mosheh et-shemen hammishchah vayyimshach et-hammishkan ve'et kol-asher bo vayyeqaddesh otam.
verses.study_note: Before anointing Aaron, Moses anoints the tabernacle itself and everything in it. This order is theologically significant: the sanctuary is consecrated first, then the high priest who will serve within it. The anointing (mashach) of the tabernacle and its furnishings with the specially compounded oil (Exod 30:22-33) transmits holiness to every object — the furniture, vessels, and altar become 'most holy' by this anointing (Exod 30:29). The verb וַיְקַדֵּשׁ (vayyeqaddesh — 'and he sanctified/made holy them') indicates that anointing with the sacred oil is the act by which sanctification is formally conferred. The sanctuary must be holy before the holy priest enters it.
verses.doctrinal_note: The anointing of the tabernacle and its furnishings with the sacred oil establishes a typological pattern: the Spirit's anointing sanctifies not only persons but also spaces and objects associated with divine presence. In the NT, this extends to the church as the dwelling place of God in the Spirit (Eph 2:21-22): the building of living stones is consecrated by the same Spirit who anoints individual believers. The verb מָשַׁח (mashach — to anoint) in this context gives the theological root from which Messiah/Christ (the Anointed One) derives. Every anointing in the OT — tabernacle, altar, Aaron — points toward the one supreme anointing: the descent of the Spirit upon Jesus at his baptism (Matt 3:16-17), the ultimate fulfillment of all Levitical consecrations.

verses.kjv_text: And he sprinkled thereof upon the altar seven times, and anointed the altar and all his vessels, both the laver and his foot, to sanctify them.
verses.original_text: וַיַּז מִמֶּנּוּ עַל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים וַיִּמְשַׁח אֶת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וְאֶת כָּל כֵּלָיו וְאֶת הַכִּיֹּר וְאֶת כַּנּוֹ לְקַדְּשָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaz mimmennu al-hammizbeach sheva pe'amim vayyimshach et-hammizbeach ve'et kol-kelav ve'et-hakkiyyor ve'et-kanno leqaddisham.
verses.study_note: The sprinkling of anointing oil seven times upon the altar (sheva pe'amim — 'seven times') is a distinct act from the anointing of the altar. The sevenfold sprinkling invokes the completeness of divine consecration — seven is the number of completeness in the Levitical system (cf. the sprinkling of blood seven times before the veil in Lev 4:6,17; the sevenfold sprinkling of the red heifer in Num 19:4). The bronze altar of burnt offering, all its vessels, the bronze laver (kiyyor) and its base (kanno) are all anointed — the altar of sacrifice and the laver of cleansing are equally consecrated, establishing their sacred status before a single offering is made. The altar cannot receive what has not been prepared; the preparation begins with consecration.

verses.kjv_text: And he poured of the anointing oil upon Aaron’s head, and anointed him, to sanctify him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּצֹק מִשֶּׁמֶן הַמִּשְׁחָה עַל רֹאשׁ אַהֲרֹן וַיִּמְשַׁח אֹתוֹ לְקַדְּשׁוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitzoq mishshemen hammishchah al-rosh Aharon vayyimshach oto leqaddesho.
verses.study_note: The anointing of Aaron's head is described with the verb וַיִּצֹק (vayyitzoq — 'and he poured', H3332), a different and more lavish word than the sprinkling (vayaz) used for the altar. Moses pours (tzaq — to pour out liberally) the anointing oil upon Aaron's head. Psalm 133:2 captures this moment poetically: 'It is like the precious ointment upon the head, that ran down upon the beard, even Aaron's beard: that went down to the skirts of his garments.' The oil runs freely, copiously, over the head, through the beard, down the robes — a visual theology of consecration flowing from the head downward, encompassing the entire person. Aaron is drenched in the symbol of God's Spirit.
verses.doctrinal_note: The pouring of anointing oil upon Aaron's head is the supreme OT type of the Spirit's anointing. Psalm 133 connects the oil flowing down from Aaron's head to the dew of Hermon — both images of life-giving abundance from above. The NT fulfillment is explicit: at the baptism of Jesus, the Spirit descended upon him (Matt 3:16-17; Luke 4:18, citing Isa 61:1: 'The Spirit of the LORD is upon me; because he hath anointed me'). Christ is the supreme Anointed One — the Mashiach — upon whom the Spirit rested without measure (John 3:34). From this head-anointing, as from Aaron's, the consecration flows to his body: 'the unction from the Holy One' that John describes (1 John 2:20,27) flows from the Head to all who are in him.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses brought Aaron’s sons, and put coats upon them, and girded them with girdles, and put bonnets upon them; as the LORD commanded Moses.
verses.original_text: וַיַּקְרֵב מֹשֶׁה אֶת בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן וַיַּלְבִּשֵׁם כֻּתֳּנֹת וַיַּחְגֹּר אֹתָם אַבְנֵט וַיַּחֲבֹשׁ לָהֶם מִגְבָּעוֹת כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqrev Mosheh et-benei Aharon vayyalbishem kuttanot vayyachgor otam avnet vayyachavosh lahem migba'ot ka'asher tzivvah YHWH et-Mosheh.
verses.study_note: After Aaron's elaborate seven-garment robing, his sons receive a simpler four-garment set: tunics (kuttanot — linen coats), sashes (avnet), and caps (migba'ot — bonnets or caps, distinct from the high priest's mitre/turban). The sons are not anointed with oil poured on their heads at this stage — that distinction belongs to Aaron alone. Their consecration comes through the blood application (vv.23-24) and the ordination ceremony, not through the head-pouring. The compliance formula once again closes the section: 'as the LORD commanded Moses.' This is the second of seven such formulae in chapter 8, maintaining the rhythm of perfect obedience throughout the ordination narrative.

verses.kjv_text: And he brought the bullock for the sin offering: and Aaron and his sons laid their hands upon the head of the bullock for the sin offering.
verses.original_text: וַיַּגֵּשׁ אֶת פַּר הַחַטָּאת וַיִּסְמֹךְ אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו אֶת יְדֵיהֶם עַל רֹאשׁ פַּר הַחַטָּאת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyagesh et-par hachatta't vayyismoch Aharon uvanav et-yedeihem al-rosh par hachatta't.
verses.study_note: The first of the three ordination sacrifices is the sin offering bull (par hachatta't). Before any priestly service can begin, the priests themselves must be atoned for. Aaron and all his sons lay their hands (vayyismoch — the Qal of samach, to press firmly, H5564) upon the head of the bull. The hand-laying (semikhat yadayim) is the central act of identification: the offerer transfers his guilt to the animal by this physical act of pressing, establishing the substitutionary relationship between the sinner and the sin offering. That Aaron himself — the man about to be ordained as high priest — must first bring a sin offering demolishes any notion of Aaronic sinlessness or priestly self-sufficiency. Every Aaronic priest enters his office as a sinner who has been atoned for.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sin offering that Aaron himself must bring is one of the sharpest typological contrasts between the Aaronic and Melchizedekan priesthoods in Hebrews. Hebrews 7:27 states explicitly that the Aaronic high priests 'offered up sacrifice, first for their own sins, and then for the people's.' Aaron cannot approach God without first being atoned for himself. Christ, by contrast, is 'holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners' (Heb 7:26): he needed no sin offering for himself because he had no sin. This is the decisive superiority of Christ's priesthood — he is simultaneously the sinless Priest who needs no atonement and the perfect Sacrifice who provides it. Where Aaron must be atoned before he can atone, Christ atones without any prior need of atonement himself.

verses.kjv_text: And he slew it; and Moses took the blood, and put it upon the horns of the altar round about with his finger, and purified the altar, and poured the blood at the bottom of the altar, and sanctified it, to make reconciliation upon it.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁחַט וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה אֶת הַדָּם וַיִּתֵּן עַל קַרְנוֹת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב בְּאֶצְבָּעוֹ וַיְחַטֵּא אֶת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וְאֶת הַדָּם יָצַק אֶל יְסוֹד הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וַיְקַדְּשֵׁהוּ לְכַפֵּר עָלָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishchat vayyiqqach Mosheh et-haddam vayyitten al-qarnot hammizbeach saviv be'etzba'o vaychatte' et-hammizbeach ve'et-haddam yatzaq el-yesod hammizbeach vayyeqaddishehu lekhapper alav.
verses.study_note: Moses himself slaughters the sin-offering bull and applies the blood. The blood is placed on the four horns of the altar (qarnot hammizbeach saviv — 'round about') with his finger (be'etzba'o), and the remaining blood is poured at the base of the altar (yesod — the foundation). The verb וַיְחַטֵּא (vaychatte — 'and he purified/de-sinned,' the Piel of chata) means literally to unsin or to remove sin from the altar. The altar itself — though it was just anointed with holy oil — requires blood purification as well. The multistage purification (oil anointing + blood application) of the altar mirrors the multistage consecration of the priests (water washing + oil anointing + blood application), revealing the comprehensiveness of Levitical holiness.

verses.kjv_text: And he took all the fat that was upon the inwards, and the caul above the liver, and the two kidneys, and their fat, and Moses burned it upon the altar.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח אֶת כָּל הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עַל הַקֶּרֶב וְאֵת יֹתֶרֶת הַכָּבֵד וְאֶת שְׁתֵּי הַכְּלָיֹת וְאֶת חֶלְבְּהֶן וַיַּקְטֵר מֹשֶׁה הַמִּזְבֵּחָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach et-kol-hachelev asher al-haqerev ve'et yoteret hakkaved ve'et shtei hakkelajot ve'et chelbehen vayyaqter Mosheh hammizbeachah.
verses.study_note: The divine portions of the sin offering bull — the fat covering the viscera, the lobe of the liver, the two kidneys with their fat — are burned by Moses upon the altar. This is identical to the standard sin offering procedure of Leviticus 4:8-10, applied here to the priestly ordination offering. The verb וַיַּקְטֵר (vayyaqter — Hiphil of qatar, to cause to go up in smoke) specifies the cultic burning that produces the ascending sweet-smelling smoke before YHWH. Moses acts as the priestly officiant over this entire ordination ceremony — he who is not a priest performs all the priestly functions at the inauguration of the priesthood, a unique and unrepeatable role.

verses.kjv_text: But the bullock, and his hide, his flesh, and his dung, he burnt with fire without the camp; as the LORD commanded Moses.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת הַפָּר וְאֶת עֹרוֹ וְאֶת בְּשָׂרוֹ וְאֶת פִּרְשׁוֹ שָׂרַף בָּאֵשׁ מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-happar ve'et-oro ve'et-besaro ve'et-pirsho saraf ba'esh michuts lamachaneh ka'asher tzivvah YHWH et-Mosheh.
verses.study_note: The bulk of the sin offering bull — its hide, flesh, and dung — is burned with fire outside the camp (michuts lamachaneh). This is the standard disposal procedure for the most solemn sin offerings whose blood is brought inside the sanctuary (Lev 4:11-12, 21; 6:30). The 'outside the camp' burning is theologically loaded: the sin-bearing sacrifice ends up outside the sacred precincts, in the place of waste and uncleanness, bearing the full weight of priestly sin away from the holy community. This outside-the-camp geography will become one of the NT's most powerful christological locations (Heb 13:12-13). The compliance formula closes the section once more.
verses.doctrinal_note: The burning of the ordination sin offering bull outside the camp is the Levitical precursor of the crucifixion's geography. Hebrews 13:12-13 makes the connection explicit: 'Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate. Let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp, bearing his reproach.' Jesus died outside Jerusalem — outside the camp of Israel — as the sin offering whose blood was brought into the heavenly sanctuary (Heb 9:12) while his body was 'burned' (suffered) in the place of rejection and uncleanness. The Levitical outside-the-camp burning is not a footnote but the precise geographical type of Calvary.

verses.kjv_text: And he brought the ram for the burnt offering: and Aaron and his sons laid their hands upon the head of the ram.
verses.original_text: וַיַּקְרֵב אֵת אֵיל הָעֹלָה וַיִּסְמְכוּ אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו אֶת יְדֵיהֶם עַל רֹאשׁ הָאָיִל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqrev et-eil ha'olah vayyismekhu Aharon uvanav et-yedeihem al-rosh ha'ayil.
verses.study_note: The second ordination sacrifice is the burnt offering ram. After the sin offering has dealt with the negative dimension of priestly consecration (the removal of sin and guilt), the burnt offering addresses the positive dimension (total consecration and dedication to YHWH). The same hand-laying rite (semikhat yadayim) is performed — Aaron and his sons press their hands upon the ram's head, identifying themselves with the offering. For the burnt offering, hand-laying signifies not guilt-transfer but personal identification: 'this is my total consecration being expressed through this animal.' The worshiper's dedication is enacted bodily in the pressing of hands before the animal is slaughtered.

verses.kjv_text: And he killed it; and Moses sprinkled the blood upon the altar round about.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁחַט וַיִּזְרֹק מֹשֶׁה אֶת הַדָּם עַל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishchat vayyizroq Mosheh et-haddam al-hammizbeach saviv.
verses.study_note: The slaughter of the burnt offering ram and the dashing of its blood round about (saviv) the altar follows the standard burnt offering procedure of Leviticus 1:5,11. Moses continues to function as the priestly officiant. The blood dashing (zaraq — not the delicate finger-application of the sin offering's horn blood, but a robust tossing of blood against the altar sides) publicly consecrates the altar with the blood of total dedication. The altar that was just purified by sin-offering blood is now consecrated by burnt-offering blood — the two dimensions of consecration (cleansing and dedication) together prepare the altar for its priestly ministry.

verses.kjv_text: And he cut the ram into pieces; and Moses burnt the head, and the pieces, and the fat.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת הָאַיִל נִתַּח לִנְתָחָיו וַיַּקְטֵר מֹשֶׁה אֶת הָרֹאשׁ וְאֶת הַנְּתָחִים וְאֶת הַפָּדֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-ha'ayil nitach linetachav vayyaqter Mosheh et-harosh ve'et-hannetachim ve'et-hapader.
verses.study_note: The burnt offering ram is cut into its pieces (netachim) following the procedure of Leviticus 1:6,8,12. The head, the pieces, and the suet fat (pader — H6309, the thick fat layer) are burned. Unlike the sin offering, where only specific fat portions burned, the entire burnt offering is consumed — head, pieces, fat, everything — in fire on the altar. Nothing is retained, nothing set aside, no priestly portion from this sacrifice: total consumption expresses total consecration. For the priests to be ordained, they must first declare through a burnt offering that their entire selves — symbolized by the fully consumed ram — belong entirely to YHWH.

verses.kjv_text: And he washed the inwards and the legs in water; and Moses burnt the whole ram upon the altar: it was a burnt sacrifice for a sweet savour, and an offering made by fire unto the LORD; as the LORD commanded Moses.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת הַקֶּרֶב וְאֶת הַכְּרָעַיִם רָחַץ בַּמָּיִם וַיַּקְטֵר מֹשֶׁה אֶת כָּל הָאַיִל הַמִּזְבֵּחָה עֹלָה הוּא לְרֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-haqerev ve'et-hakkera'ayim rachatz bammayim vayyaqter Mosheh et-kol-ha'ayil hammizbeachah olah hu lereiach nichoach ishsheh laYHWH ka'asher tzivvah YHWH et-Mosheh.
verses.study_note: The washing of the inner organs and legs before burning is the standard procedure of Leviticus 1:9,13, ensuring that the internal contents do not defile the offering. Then the whole ram (kol-ha'ayil — every part) is burned on the altar. The declaration עֹלָה הוּא לְרֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה ('it is a burnt offering, a pleasing aroma, a fire-offering to the LORD') formally certifies the validity and divine acceptance of the sacrifice. The compliance formula (the fourth in chapter 8) seals the burnt offering section. Two sacrifices down, one to go — and the most distinctive of the three is last.

verses.kjv_text: And he brought the other ram, the ram of consecration: and Aaron and his sons laid their hands upon the head of the ram.
verses.original_text: וַיַּקְרֵב אֶת הָאַיִל הַשֵּׁנִי אֵיל הַמִּלֻּאִים וַיִּסְמְכוּ אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו אֶת יְדֵיהֶם עַל רֹאשׁ הָאָיִל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqrev et-ha'ayil hashsheni eil hammillu'im vayyismekhu Aharon uvanav et-yedeihem al-rosh ha'ayil.
verses.study_note: The third and final ordination sacrifice is introduced with its distinctive name: אֵיל הַמִּלֻּאִים (eil hammillu'im — 'the ram of consecration/ordination'), the ram whose blood will be used in the most distinctive rite of the entire ceremony. The millu'im (filling) offering is unique to the ordination — it does not appear in any other sacrificial context in the OT. The hand-laying rite is performed a third time. By this point in the ceremony, the priests have identified themselves three times with their sacrifices: first with sin (sin offering), then with total dedication (burnt offering), and now with ordination itself (millu'im). The threefold identification comprehensively encompasses all dimensions of priestly consecration.
verses.doctrinal_note: The three ordination sacrifices form a theological trilogy that maps perfectly onto the three dimensions of Christ's own consecration: (1) the sin offering — Christ as the one who bears sin (2 Cor 5:21; Isa 53:10); (2) the burnt offering — Christ as the one wholly dedicated to the Father (Heb 10:7; John 4:34); (3) the millu'im — Christ as the one uniquely installed in the eternal priestly office (Heb 5:5-6; Ps 110:4). No other person in human history undergoes this consecration; Aaron's ordination is a one-time shadow of Christ's eternal, unchangeable installation as Great High Priest by divine oath: 'The LORD hath sworn, and will not repent, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchizedek' (Ps 110:4).

verses.kjv_text: And he slew it; and Moses took of the blood of it, and put it upon the tip of Aaron’s right ear, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁחַט וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה מִדָּמוֹ וַיִּתֵּן עַל תְּנוּךְ אֹזֶן אַהֲרֹן הַיְמָנִית וְעַל בֹּהֶן יָדוֹ הַיְמָנִית וְעַל בֹּהֶן רַגְלוֹ הַיְמָנִית׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishchat vayyiqqach Mosheh midamo vayyitten al-tenukh ozen Aharon hayemanit ve'al-bohen yado hayemanit ve'al-bohen raglo hayemanit.
verses.study_note: The most distinctive rite of the entire ordination ceremony: blood is applied to three specific points on Aaron's body: (1) תְּנוּךְ אֹזֶן הַיְמָנִית (tenukh ozen hayemanit — the tip of the right ear); (2) בֹּהֶן יָד הַיְמָנִית (bohen yad hayemanit — the thumb of the right hand); (3) בֹּהֶן רֶגֶל הַיְמָנִית (bohen regel hayemanit — the big toe of the right foot). The threefold application covers the whole person from head to foot: the ear (hearing and obedience), the hand (work and action), the foot (walk and movement). All three are right-sided — the side of honor, strength, and preeminence. Every faculty of the high priest — what he hears, what he does, where he goes — is consecrated by atoning blood.
verses.doctrinal_note: The application of blood to the ear, thumb, and toe of the priest is the most bodily specific act of consecration in all of Scripture. Its theological meaning is comprehensive: priestly consecration is not merely spiritual or ceremonial but bodily, covering every faculty of human action. This rite was also applied to cleansed lepers (Lev 14:14-18), creating a profound link between the ordination of priests and the purification of the unclean: both require blood on the same three right-sided points. The NT counterpart is the consecration of the believer as a priest (1 Pet 2:9): the blood of Christ applied by faith consecrates the whole person — what they hear (Rom 10:17), what they do (Col 3:23-24), and where they walk (Eph 2:10). Hearing, doing, and walking in holiness are the three dimensions of the Spirit-filled life.

verses.kjv_text: And he brought Aaron’s sons, and Moses put of the blood upon the tip of their right ear, and upon the thumbs of their right hands, and upon the great toes of their right feet: and Moses sprinkled the blood upon the altar round about.
verses.original_text: וַיַּקְרֵב אֵת בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן וַיִּתֵּן מֹשֶׁה מִן הַדָּם עַל תְּנוּךְ אָזְנָם הַיְמָנִית וְעַל בֹּהֶן יָדָם הַיְמָנִית וְעַל בֹּהֶן רַגְלָם הַיְמָנִית וַיִּזְרֹק מֹשֶׁה אֶת הַדָּם עַל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqrev et-benei Aharon vayyitten Mosheh min-haddam al-tenukh oznam hayemanit ve'al-bohen yadam hayemanit ve'al-bohen raglam hayemanit vayyizroq Mosheh et-haddam al-hammizbeach saviv.
verses.study_note: The same threefold blood application is given to Aaron's sons: ear, right thumb, right big toe. Then the remaining blood of the ordination ram is dashed (zaraq) round about the altar. The blood of the millu'im ram has now been applied in two directions: personally (to the priests' ears, thumbs, and toes) and publicly (to the altar). The altar and the priests share the same ordination blood — they are bound together by the same sacrifice. The priest and the altar upon which he ministers are consecrated by the same blood, creating an inseparable union between the minister and the ministry.

verses.kjv_text: And he took the fat, and the rump, and all the fat that was upon the inwards, and the caul above the liver, and the two kidneys, and their fat, and the right shoulder:
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח אֶת הַחֵלֶב וְאֶת הָאַלְיָה וְאֶת כָּל הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עַל הַקֶּרֶב וְאֵת יֹתֶרֶת הַכָּבֵד וְאֶת שְׁתֵּי הַכְּלָיֹת וְאֶת חֶלְבְּהֶן וְאֵת שׁוֹק הַיָּמִין׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach et-hachelev ve'et-ha'alyah ve'et kol-hachelev asher al-haqerev ve'et yoteret hakkaved ve'et shtei hakkelajot ve'et chelbehem ve'et shoq hayyamin.
verses.study_note: The fat portions of the ordination ram parallel those of the peace offering (Lev 3:9-10; 7:3-4): the broad tail (alyah), the fat covering the viscera, the lobe of the liver, the two kidneys with their fat. But the ordination ram adds one unique element: the right shoulder (shoq hayyamin). In the peace offering legislation (Lev 7:32-34), the right shoulder was designated as the heave offering for the officiating priest. Here, in the ordination, the right shoulder is burned on the altar along with the fat — it goes to God rather than to the priest. This reversal is deliberate: at ordination, the priests give the best (the right shoulder) to God rather than receiving it. The servant gives before he receives.

verses.kjv_text: And out of the basket of unleavened bread, that was before the LORD, he took one unleavened cake, and a cake of oiled bread, and one wafer, and put them on the fat, and upon the right shoulder:
verses.original_text: וּמִסַּל הַמַּצּוֹת אֲשֶׁר לִפְנֵי יְהוָה לָקַח חַלַּת מַצָּה אַחַת וְחַלַּת לֶחֶם שֶׁמֶן אַחַת וְרָקִיק אֶחָד וַיָּשֶׂם עַל הַחֲלָבִים וְעַל שׁוֹק הַיָּמִין׃
verses.transliteration: Umissal hammatzot asher lifnei YHWH laqach challat matzah achat vehallat lechem shemen achat veragiq echad vayyasem al-hachalavim ve'al-shoq hayyamin.
verses.study_note: From the unleavened bread basket, Moses takes one of each type: one unleavened cake (challat matzah), one oil-bread cake (challat lechem shemen), and one wafer (raqiq). These three bread items are placed upon the fat portions and the right shoulder, forming the complete wave offering. The bread is placed on top of the meat — grain offering and animal offering combined into a single presentation before YHWH. This combination of bread and meat in the ordination offering creates a complete representation of the priest's ministry: he offers both the animal sacrifices and the grain offerings, and the ordination ceremony incorporates elements of both into one comprehensive act of consecration.

verses.kjv_text: And he put all upon Aaron’s hands, and upon his sons’ hands, and waved them for a wave offering before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשֶׂם אֶת הַכֹּל עַל כַּפֵּי אַהֲרֹן וְעַל כַּפֵּי בָנָיו וַיָּנֶף אֹתָם תְּנוּפָה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasem et-hakkol al-kappei Aharon ve'al-kappei vanav vayyannef otam tenufah lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: The defining moment of the millu'im: Moses places the entire wave offering (fat, shoulder, and bread) upon the palms (kappei — the open cupped hands) of Aaron and his sons, and they wave it before YHWH. This is the act of 'filling the hands' from which millu'im derives its name. The newly ordained priests hold in their own hands the first offering they will present — a foretaste and earnest of all future priestly ministry. The waving (tenufah) before YHWH presents the offering in the divine presence before it is burned. The hands that have just been consecrated by blood now hold what they will spend their priestly lives offering: the fat of sacrifice and the bread of dedication.
verses.doctrinal_note: The placing of the ordination offerings upon the hands of Aaron and his sons — 'filling the hand' — is the act that constitutes ordination to the priesthood. The newly ordained priest's hands are first filled with an offering for God before they are ever used for any other priestly function. This principle — that the minister's hands must be filled with an offering before he can serve — is the Levitical foundation of the NT principle that those who serve at the altar live of the altar (1 Cor 9:13-14). More deeply, it typifies Christ, whose hands were first filled with the offering of himself — presented to the Father on the cross — before being filled with gifts to give to his church (Eph 4:8-11; Ps 68:18). The millu'im is the OT picture of Christ's priestly installation.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses took them from off their hands, and burnt them on the altar upon the burnt offering: they were consecrations for a sweet savour: it is an offering made by fire unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה אֹתָם מֵעַל כַּפֵּיהֶם וַיַּקְטֵר הַמִּזְבֵּחָה עַל הָעֹלָה מִלֻּאִים הֵם לְרֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ אִשֶּׁה הוּא לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Mosheh otam me'al kappeihem vayyaqter hammizbeachah al-ha'olah millu'im hem lereiach nichoach ishsheh hu laYHWH.
verses.study_note: Moses takes the wave offering from the priests' hands and burns it upon the altar — on top of (al) the burnt offering that is already burning there. The ordination offerings are burned upon the foundation of total consecration (the burnt offering). The declaration מִלֻּאִים הֵם (millu'im hem — 'they are ordination/filling offerings') formally names the offering type: these are the millu'im. The sweet savor formula certifies divine acceptance. The ordination offerings ascending in smoke before YHWH formally consecrates the priesthood in the divine presence: God receives the millu'im and with them, as it were, receives Aaron and his sons into the priestly office.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses took the breast, and waved it for a wave offering before the LORD: for of the ram of consecration it was Moses’ part; as the LORD commanded Moses.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה אֶת הֶחָזֶה וַיְנִיפֵהוּ תְנוּפָה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה מֵאֵיל הַמִּלֻּאִים לְמֹשֶׁה הָיָה לְמָנָה כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Mosheh et-hechazeh vayenifeh tenufah lifnei YHWH me'eil hammillu'im leMosheh hayah lemanah ka'asher tzivvah YHWH et-Mosheh.
verses.study_note: Moses receives the wave breast of the ordination ram as his personal portion (lemanah — 'as his allotted share'). In the peace offering legislation (Lev 7:31), the wave breast belongs to Aaron and his sons; here, since Aaron is the one being ordained rather than officiating, the breast passes to Moses who serves as the ordination officiant. This is the only recorded instance of Moses receiving a priestly portion — his role as the ordaining officiant entitles him to the portion normally belonging to the officiating priest. The compliance formula (the fifth of seven) seals the ordination offering section. Moses has served as priest, prophet, and king-representative throughout this ceremony — a tripartite role pointing toward the one who perfectly unites all three offices.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses took of the anointing oil, and of the blood which was upon the altar, and sprinkled it upon Aaron, and upon his garments, and upon his sons, and upon his sons’ garments with him; and sanctified Aaron, and his garments, and his sons, and his sons’ garments with him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה מִשֶּׁמֶן הַמִּשְׁחָה וּמִן הַדָּם אֲשֶׁר עַל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וַיַּז עַל אַהֲרֹן עַל בְּגָדָיו וְעַל בָּנָיו וְעַל בִּגְדֵי בָנָיו אִתּוֹ וַיְקַדֵּשׁ אֶת אַהֲרֹן אֶת בְּגָדָיו וְאֶת בָּנָיו וְאֶת בִּגְדֵי בָנָיו אִתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Mosheh mishshemen hammishchah umin-haddam asher al-hammizbeach vayyaz al-Aharon al-begadav ve'al-banav ve'al-bigdei vanav itto vayyeqaddesh et-Aharon et-begadav ve'et-banav ve'et-bigdei vanav itto.
verses.study_note: The climactic act of the ordination ceremony combines anointing oil with altar blood in a single sprinkling upon Aaron, his sons, and all their garments. This is the only place in Scripture where the anointing oil and sacrificial blood are mixed and applied together. The oil (Spirit/consecration) and the blood (atonement) are inseparable in the act of priestly consecration: one cannot be consecrated by the Spirit without first being atoned for by blood. The sprinkling covers not just the persons of Aaron and his sons but their garments as well — the priestly vestments themselves are consecrated, not merely the men who wear them. The declaration comes four times in this verse: 'sanctified Aaron... his garments... his sons... his sons' garments.'
verses.doctrinal_note: The combined sprinkling of oil and blood upon Aaron and his sons is perhaps the most concentrated image of the double grace of the gospel in the entire OT: atonement (blood) and sanctification (oil/Spirit) applied together in one act. These two are inseparable in NT soteriology as well — justification (the blood of Christ applied for forgiveness) and sanctification (the Spirit of Christ applied for holiness) are given together, not sequentially or optionally. John's Gospel grounds both in the crucifixion: when the soldier pierced Jesus's side, 'forthwith came there out blood and water' (John 19:34) — atonement and cleansing in one. The ordination sprinkling is the Levitical precedent; the cross is the fulfillment.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto Aaron and to his sons, Boil the flesh at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation: and there eat it with the bread that is in the basket of consecrations, as I commanded, saying, Aaron and his sons shall eat it.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל אַהֲרֹן וְאֶל בָּנָיו בַּשְּׁלוּ אֶת הַבָּשָׂר פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְשָׁם תֹּאכְלוּ אֹתוֹ וְאֶת הַלֶּחֶם אֲשֶׁר בְּסַל הַמִּלֻּאִים כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוֵּיתִי לֵאמֹר אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו יֹאכְלֻהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Mosheh el-Aharon ve'el-banav bashsh'lu et-habbasar petach ohel mo'ed vesham tokhlu oto ve'et-hallechem asher besal hammillu'im ka'asher tzivveiti lemor Aharon uvanav yokhluhu.
verses.study_note: Moses instructs Aaron and his sons to cook and eat the remaining flesh of the ordination ram at the door of the tabernacle, along with the bread from the ordination basket. The ordination meal is the priestly counterpart of the peace offering meal — a covenant feast at the threshold of the sacred precinct. The newly ordained priests eat their first priestly meal at the very spot where they will spend the next seven days. The location (door of the tabernacle) and the elements (meat and bread from the ordination offerings) create a Eucharistic prototype: the ordained minister eats the sacrifice through which he was consecrated, at the threshold between the holy and the common.

verses.kjv_text: And that which remaineth of the flesh and of the bread shall ye burn with fire.
verses.original_text: וְהַנּוֹתָר בַּבָּשָׂר וּבַלֶּחֶם בָּאֵשׁ תִּשְׂרֹפוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehannotar babbas ar uvalllechem ba'esh tisrofu.
verses.study_note: Any flesh or bread remaining from the ordination meal must be burned with fire — not left over, not taken home, not distributed. The ordination offerings, being in the most holy category of priestly eating, follow the same rule as the thanksgiving peace offering (Lev 7:15): they must be consumed in their appointed time. What cannot be eaten within the ordained window is consecrated by fire rather than discarded. The burning of the remainder ensures that sacred food does not pass into ordinary or defiled use. The ordination ceremony generates no leftovers that circulate beyond its sacred boundaries.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall not go out of the door of the tabernacle of the congregation in seven days, until the days of your consecration be at an end: for seven days shall he consecrate you.
verses.original_text: וּמִפֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד לֹא תֵצְאוּ שִׁבְעַת יָמִים עַד יוֹם מְלֹאת יְמֵי מִלֻּאֵיכֶם כִּי שִׁבְעַת יָמִים יְמַלֵּא אֶת יֶדְכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Umippetach ohel mo'ed lo tetze'u shivat yamim ad yom melot yemei milla'ukhem ki shivat yamim yemallex et-yedekhem.
