verses.study_note: The seven-day ordination period is not merely ceremonial duration but theological necessity. Aaron and his sons must remain at the tabernacle door for seven full days — they may not exit. The period is called מִלֻּאֵיכֶם (milla'ukhem — 'your filling/consecration'), playing on the millu'im root: the seven days 'fill' their hands, completing the installation into the priestly office. Seven days echoes the seven days of creation (Gen 1-2:3), the seven days of consecration for the tabernacle (Exod 29:35-37), and the seven-day purification periods throughout Leviticus. The ordination is a new-creation event: YHWH creating a new order of sacred mediators in seven days.
verses.doctrinal_note: The seven-day threshold-keeping of Aaron and his sons is one of Scripture's most powerful images of consecration as a process of separation and waiting. The newly ordained priests inhabit a liminal space — they are no longer ordinary Israelites but not yet functioning priests. They stand at the threshold for seven days, consecrated but not yet commissioned, set apart but not yet sent. This pattern recurs in the NT: after his resurrection, Christ spent forty days with his disciples in a liminal period of preparation before the Spirit came at Pentecost (Acts 1:3-5). The disciples were told to 'tarry... until ye be endued with power from on high' (Luke 24:49) — a NT echo of the seven-day threshold-keeping, waiting for the anointing that would commission them for ministry.

verses.kjv_text: As he hath done this day, so the LORD hath commanded to do, to make an atonement for you.
verses.original_text: כַּאֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה בַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה צִוָּה יְהוָה לַעֲשׂוֹת לְכַפֵּר עֲלֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ka'asher asah bayyom hazzeh tzivvah YHWH la'asot lekhapper aleikhem.
verses.study_note: The ultimate purpose of the entire elaborate ordination ceremony is stated plainly: לְכַפֵּר עֲלֵיכֶם (lekhapper aleikhem — 'to make atonement for you'). All the washing, robing, anointing, blood application, fat-burning, wave offerings, ordination meals, and seven-day threshold-keeping converge on this single purpose: atonement. The priests are not ordained primarily to perform rituals or maintain an institution but to stand in the gap between a holy God and a sinful people, making atonement. Kipper (H3722 — to make atonement) is the theological heartbeat of the Levitical system, and here it is explicitly named as the purpose of the entire priestly office.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore shall ye abide at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation day and night seven days, and keep the charge of the LORD, that ye die not: for so I am commanded.
verses.original_text: וּפֶתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד תֵּשְׁבוּ יוֹמָם וָלַיְלָה שִׁבְעַת יָמִים וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת מִשְׁמֶרֶת יְהוָה וְלֹא תָמוּתוּ כִּי כֵן צֻוֵּיתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Ufetach ohel mo'ed teshvu yomam valaylah shivat yamim ushmartem et-mishmertet YHWH velo tamutu ki khen tzuvveiti.
verses.study_note: The ordination requirements intensify: the priests must remain at the tabernacle door day and night (yomam valaylah — 'by day and by night') for seven full days. The stakes are stated with startling directness: 'that ye die not' (velo tamutu). Approaching the holy improperly, serving before being fully consecrated, failing to complete the ordination period — any of these could result in death. The holiness of YHWH is not abstract or sentimental; it is a consuming reality that demands the most complete and meticulous preparation from those who draw near. The phrase וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת מִשְׁמֶרֶת יְהוָה ('and keep the charge/watch of the LORD') establishes the priestly vocation as fundamentally watchful — they are guardians of the sacred boundary.
verses.doctrinal_note: The threshold-keeping of the ordination period — day and night, seven days, at the door — establishes the Levitical principle that proximity to divine holiness demands the most sustained and complete preparation. The warning 'that ye die not' is not hyperbolic; Nadab and Abihu would prove its reality immediately after the ordination (Lev 10:1-2). In the NT, this principle of prepared approach is not abolished but fulfilled: the way into the Most Holy Place is now open through the blood of Christ (Heb 10:19-22), but the call to 'draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith' still demands preparation — a heart sprinkled from an evil conscience and a body washed with pure water. The way is open; the approach is still serious.

verses.kjv_text: So Aaron and his sons did all things which the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו אֵת כָּל הַדְּבָרִים אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה בְּיַד מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaas Aharon uvanav et kol-haddvarim asher tzivvah YHWH beyad Mosheh.
verses.study_note: The final verse of chapter 8 is the chapter's capstone compliance declaration, but now with an important shift of subject: it is no longer only 'Moses did as the LORD commanded' but 'Aaron and his sons did all the things which the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses.' The newly ordained priests themselves are now active agents of obedience. The phrase בְּיַד מֹשֶׁה (beyad Mosheh — 'by the hand of Moses') establishes the mediatorial chain: God commanded → Moses transmitted → Aaron and his sons obeyed. The divine will flows through the appointed mediator to the consecrated servants. The chapter closes with the seventh and final compliance formula — seven perfect acts of obedience sealing the ordination as fully executed.
verses.doctrinal_note: The closing declaration — 'Aaron and his sons did all the things which the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses' — is the Levitical foundation of apostolic succession rightly understood: not a mechanical transmission of office but a chain of divinely commanded obedience through appointed mediators. God commands (YHWH); Moses mediates (beyad Mosheh); Aaron and his sons obey (vayyaas). In the NT, this chain becomes: the Father commands; the Son mediates (John 14:24; Heb 1:1-2); the church obeys by the Spirit (Acts 1:8; John 14:26). The ordination of Aaron is the type; the commissioning of the church as a royal priesthood (1 Pet 2:9) is the antitype — a people made priests not by human lineage but by the blood and Spirit of the Great High Priest.

meta.overview: Leviticus 8 is one of the most architecturally significant chapters in the entire Pentateuch — the moment when everything prepared in Exodus (the tabernacle, the priestly garments, the anointing oil, the altar) is finally activated through the ordination of Aaron and his sons. After seven chapters of sacrificial legislation addressing what offerings the priests would handle, chapter 8 answers the prior question: how do these men become priests? The answer is stunning in its comprehensiveness: washing, robing, anointing, blood, fat, bread, waving — a multi-layered ceremonial inauguration that takes seven days and involves every major element of the Levitical system. The chapter divides into five movements: (1) the gathering and the command (vv.1-5), establishing Moses as the officiant of the priestly ordination; (2) the clothing of Aaron in the full high-priestly regalia (vv.6-9) and the anointing of the tabernacle and Aaron (vv.10-12); (3) the clothing of Aaron's sons and the offering of the sin offering ram (vv.13-17); (4) the burnt offering ram (vv.18-21); and (5) the ordination ram (millu'im, vv.22-36), the most distinctive element of the entire ceremony, in which blood is applied to the right ear, right thumb, and right big toe of Aaron and his sons — consecrating their hearing, their work, and their walk to YHWH. The chapter concludes with the seven-day ordination period, during which Aaron and his sons remain at the tabernacle entrance, neither leaving nor entering the sanctuary proper, sanctifying the transition between ordinary and sacred life. The theological heart of the chapter is the principle of Moses as the inaugural officiant: no man could consecrate himself to the priesthood. The priests required an external agent — Moses, acting in God's name — to wash, robe, and anoint them. This principle of externally applied consecration is foundational to the NT doctrine of justification: no sinner can self-justify; righteousness must be applied from outside the self. As Moses robed and anointed Aaron, so Christ clothes his people in his own righteousness (Gal 3:27; Isa 61:10) and anoints them with the Spirit (2 Cor 1:21-22). The seven-day structure of the ordination mirrors the seven days of creation, signaling that the establishment of the priesthood is a new-creation event — God creating the sacred mediatorial institution through which Israel would approach him.
meta.theme: The Consecration of the Priesthood: Washing, Robing, Anointing, and Blood
meta.keyWordMeaning: Ordination/consecration offerings — from male (to fill). The millu'im were the 'filling' offerings of the ordination ceremony, and the ordination itself was called 'filling the hand' (mille' yad), a technical term for installing a priest into office. The imagery is of the hand being filled with the priestly portions — taking hold of what the office entails. The millu'im thus represent both the ceremony (the act of filling/installing) and the offerings associated with it. In a deeper sense, the millu'im point to the completeness (shleimut) of consecration: every part of the priest — ear, thumb, toe, garments, body — is addressed in the ordination ceremony, leaving nothing unconsecrated.
outline: The Divine Command and Gathering of the Congregation (vv.1-5): Moses as officiant at the door of the tabernacle
Washing and Robing Aaron in the High-Priestly Garments (vv.6-9): Coat, girdle, robe, ephod, breastplate, Urim and Thummim, mitre, holy crown
Anointing the Tabernacle, Altar, and Aaron (vv.10-12): The oil poured upon Aaron's head — the messianic anointing
Robing Aaron's Sons and the Sin Offering Ram (vv.13-17): The congregation's atonement and the burning outside the camp
The Burnt Offering Ram (vv.18-21): Total consecration as the foundation of priestly service
The Ordination Ram (Millu'im) and the Blood on the Ear, Thumb, and Toe (vv.22-29): The most distinctive rite of priestly consecration
The Seven-Day Ordination at the Tabernacle Door (vv.30-36): Do not go out — abide in the holy place until the ordination is complete

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass on the eighth day, that Moses called Aaron and his sons, and the elders of Israel;
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי קָרָא מֹשֶׁה לְאַהֲרֹן וּלְבָנָיו וּלְזִקְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi bayyom hash-shemini qara Moshe le-Aharon ul-vanav ul-ziqnei Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: The phrase 'on the eighth day' (bayyom hash-shemini) is laden with theological significance. The seven days of priestly consecration (Lev 8) correspond to the completion of a full week; the eighth day inaugurates a new order — just as circumcision on the eighth day signals covenant entrance and resurrection occurs on the first-and-eighth day. Moses 'called' (qara) Aaron and his sons together with the elders of Israel, indicating this event is both priestly and communal: the priesthood does not exist for itself but for the people. The elders represent the whole congregation as witnesses to this inaugurating moment. The number eight in Hebrew thought often signals new beginning, transition, and eschatological hope.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto Aaron, Take thee a young calf for a sin offering, and a ram for a burnt offering, without blemish, and offer them before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־אַהֲרֹן קַח־לְךָ עֵגֶל בֶּן־בָּקָר לְחַטָּאת וְאַיִל לְעֹלָה תְּמִימִם וְהַקְרֵב לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer el-Aharon qach-lecha egel ben-baqar le-chatta'at ve-ayil le-olah temimim ve-haqrev lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: Aaron is commanded to bring two offerings for himself before ministering for the people: a calf for the sin offering (chatta'at) and a ram for the burnt offering (olah). The sequence is deliberate — atonement precedes consecration and worship. The calf (egel) is particularly striking since it was a young bull, the premier sin offering for the priesthood (Lev 4:3). It may also carry an implicit allusion to the golden calf incident (Exod 32), reminding Aaron and his sons that the priesthood stands under the same judgment it mediates. The word temimim (without blemish) appears 50+ times in the sacrificial legislation — physical perfection typifying the moral perfection required in the antitype. Aaron must sacrifice for himself before sacrificing for Israel, a point the author of Hebrews seizes to argue Christ's superiority.
verses.doctrinal_note: The requirement that Aaron offer a sin offering for himself before ministering for others establishes a foundational typological contrast with Christ. Hebrews 7:26-28 explicitly argues: 'Such a high priest became us, who is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners... who needeth not daily, as those high priests, to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins, and then for the people's.' Aaron's personal sin offering reveals the insufficiency of the Aaronic system and points forward to the one High Priest who required no personal atonement (2 Cor 5:21; 1 Pet 2:22).

verses.kjv_text: And unto the children of Israel thou shalt speak, saying, Take ye a kid of the goats for a sin offering; and a calf and a lamb, both of the first year, without blemish, for a burnt offering;
verses.original_text: וְאֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל תְּדַבֵּר לֵאמֹר קְחוּ שְׂעִיר־עִזִּים לְחַטָּאת וְעֵגֶל וָכֶבֶשׂ בְּנֵי־שָׁנָה תְּמִימִם לְעֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-el-benei Yisra'el tedabber lemor qechu se'ir-izzim le-chatta'at ve-egel va-kheves benei-shanah temimim le-olah.
verses.study_note: The offerings prescribed for the congregation differ from Aaron's personal offerings, signaling the distinction between priestly and lay worship. The people bring a 'kid of the goats' (se'ir izzim) for their sin offering — the standard communal sin offering — while a calf and a lamb of the first year constitute their burnt offering. 'Both of the first year' (benei shanah) specifies age requirements that were standard for the olah: the animal must be in the prime of its life, unblemished, a firstfruits offering. The differentiation between priestly offerings (bull) and communal offerings (goat) reflects the graduated responsibility within the covenant community. Those who hold greater responsibility before God — the priests — must bring costlier atonement.

verses.kjv_text: Also a bullock and a ram for peace offerings, to sacrifice before the LORD; and a meat offering mingled with oil: for to day the LORD will appear unto you.
verses.original_text: וְשׁוֹר וָאַיִל לִשְׁלָמִים לִזְבֹּחַ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וּמִנְחָה בְּלוּלָה בַשָּׁמֶן כִּי הַיּוֹם יְהוָה נִרְאֶה אֲלֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-shor va-ayil lishelamim lizbboach lifnei YHWH u-minchah belulah va-shamen ki hayyom YHWH nir'eh aleikhem.
verses.study_note: To the sin and burnt offerings are added peace offerings (shelamim) — a bull and a ram — and a grain offering (minchah) mingled with oil. The peace offering is the climactic fellowship offering: after atonement (sin offering) and consecration (burnt offering) comes communion (peace offering). The grain offering mixed with oil represents the fruit of the land offered back to the Creator. The stunning announcement concludes: 'for today the LORD will appear unto you' (ki hayyom YHWH nir'eh aleikhem). This is the explicit promise of theophany — God has appointed this day for self-revelation. The verb nir'eh (to appear, to be seen) is the niphal of ra'ah, 'to see,' used for divine appearances throughout the patriarchal narratives. God here acts as the host who promises to show Himself at His own appointed time.

verses.kjv_text: And they brought that which Moses commanded before the tabernacle of the congregation: and all the congregation drew near and stood before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְחוּ אֵת אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה מֹשֶׁה אֶל פְּנֵי אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וַיִּקְרְבוּ כָּל הָעֵדָה וַיַּעַמְדוּ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqechu et asher tzivvah Moshe el penei ohel mo'ed vayyiqrevu kol ha-edah vayyya'amdu lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: The congregation's immediate obedience is noted — 'they brought that which Moses commanded.' Israel brings the prescribed offerings to the door of the tabernacle (ohel mo'ed, tent of meeting), the designated place where heaven and earth intersect, where God meets with His people. 'All the congregation drew near' (vayyiqrevu kol ha-edah) — the whole nation assembles as one body. They 'stood before the LORD' (vayyya'amdu lifnei YHWH) — the posture of attendants before a king. This scene anticipates the great assembly of the redeemed who will stand before the throne of God (Rev 7:9-10). The tabernacle as 'tent of meeting' (mo'ed, appointed time/place) signals that God has established a fixed appointment for encounter with His people.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said, This is the thing which the LORD commanded that ye should do: and the glory of the LORD shall appear unto you.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה זֶה הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה תַּעֲשׂוּ וְיֵרָא אֲלֵיכֶם כְּבוֹד יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Moshe zeh ha-davar asher tzivvah YHWH ta'asu ve-yera aleikhem kevod YHWH.
verses.study_note: Moses announces the theological equation governing all Israel's worship: obedience to the divine command results in the visible appearance of the divine glory. 'This is the thing which the LORD commanded' (zeh ha-davar asher tzivvah YHWH) — Moses invokes the divine authority behind every prescribed ritual. The conditional logic is clear: 'do this, and the glory of the LORD will appear.' The glory (kevod YHWH) is not earned by obedience but is promised by covenant — obedience is the condition of access, not the cause of grace. This verse establishes that the purpose of the entire sacrificial system is theophany: encounter with the living God. Worship is not an end in itself but the appointed means by which God draws near to His people.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse crystallizes the covenantal logic of worship in the Old Testament: command → obedience → divine appearing. The glory of the LORD (kevod YHWH) that appears here anticipates the entire trajectory of divine self-manifestation: tabernacle glory (Exod 40:34), Solomonic temple glory (1 Kgs 8:10-11), the glory departing in judgment (Ezek 10:18-19), the incarnate glory (John 1:14 — 'we beheld his glory'), Pentecostal glory (Acts 2), and the eschatological glory filling the new creation (Rev 21:23). Every instance of kevod YHWH appearing is a partial fulfillment of the promise made here and a pointer to its final, unmediated consummation in Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto Aaron, Go unto the altar, and offer thy sin offering, and thy burnt offering, and make an atonement for thyself, and for the people: and offer the offering of the people, and make an atonement for them; as the LORD commanded.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־אַהֲרֹן קְרַב אֶל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וַעֲשֵׂה אֶת חַטָּאתְךָ וְאֶת עֹלָתֶךָ וְכַפֵּר בַּעַדְךָ וּבְעַד הָעָם וַעֲשֵׂה אֶת קָרְבַּן הָעָם וְכַפֵּר בַּעֲדָם כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Moshe el-Aharon qerav el ha-mizbeach va'aseh et chatta'atekha ve-et olatekha ve-khapper ba'adekha u-ve'ad ha-am va'aseh et qorban ha-am ve-khapper ba'adam ka-asher tzivvah YHWH.
verses.study_note: Moses gives Aaron his final instruction before stepping back: 'Draw near to the altar' (qerav el ha-mizbeach). The verb qerav (to draw near, to approach) is the root of qorban (offering/sacrifice), reminding the reader that approaching God in worship is itself the fundamental act of sacrifice. Aaron must first 'make atonement for yourself' (ve-khapper ba'adekha) before making atonement for the people. The verb kipper (to atone, to cover) appears twice in this verse — first for Aaron, then for the congregation — establishing the irreversible sequence. The chapter closes with 'as the LORD commanded' (ka-asher tzivvah YHWH), the refrain of priestly obedience that echoes the sevenfold refrain of tabernacle construction in Exodus 39-40.
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase 'make atonement for thyself, and for the people' establishes the decisive limitation of the Aaronic priesthood that the New Testament uses to demonstrate Christ's superiority. Hebrews 7:27 quotes this principle explicitly: the Levitical high priests offer sacrifices 'first for their own sins, and then for the people's.' Christ, by contrast, 'offered up himself' (Heb 7:27) as the one who had no sin of His own to atone (Heb 4:15). The double kipper (atonement) of Leviticus 9:7 — priest then people — collapses into a single, unrepeatable act in Christ's sacrifice. The pattern of Lev 9:7 also prefigures the Day of Atonement order (Lev 16:6,11,15,17), when the high priest first sprinkles blood for himself, then for all Israel.

verses.kjv_text: Aaron therefore went unto the altar, and slew the calf of the sin offering, which was for himself.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרַב אַהֲרֹן אֶל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וַיִּשְׁחַט אֶת עֵגֶל הַחַטָּאת אֲשֶׁר לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqrav Aharon el ha-mizbeach vayyishchat et egel ha-chatta'at asher lo.
verses.study_note: Aaron moves from instruction to action — 'went unto the altar' echoes Moses' command of verse 7 (qerav el ha-mizbeach). The first act of Aaron's high priestly ministry is to kill his own sin offering: the calf for his personal atonement. 'Which was for himself' (asher lo) — the Hebrew could be rendered 'which was his own,' emphasizing the personal, not merely official, nature of Aaron's need for atonement. The high priest is not exempt from the guilt he mediates; he is subject to the same human condition before God. This is a moment of extraordinary humility: the man standing between Israel and God must himself be covered by the blood before he can effectively intercede.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Aaron brought the blood unto him: and he dipped his finger in the blood, and put it upon the horns of the altar, and poured out the blood at the bottom of the altar:
verses.original_text: וַיַּקְרִבוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן אֶת הַדָּם אֵלָיו וַיִּטְבֹּל אֶצְבָּעוֹ בַּדָּם וַיִּתֵּן עַל קַרְנוֹת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וְאֶת הַדָּם יָצַק אֶל יְסוֹד הַמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqrivu benei Aharon et ha-dam elav vayyittbol etzba'o ba-dam vayyitten al qarnot ha-mizbeach ve-et ha-dam yatzaq el yesod ha-mizbeach.
verses.study_note: Aaron's sons assist by bringing the blood, showing the priestly team at work. The blood ritual is precise: Aaron dips his finger (etzba'o) in the blood and applies it to the four horns (qarnot) of the altar — the projections at each corner of the bronze altar that symbolize power and refuge (cf. 1 Kgs 1:50). The remaining blood is 'poured out at the bottom of the altar' (yatzaq el yesod ha-mizbeach), at the foundation. The two applications — horns and base — indicate that atonement transforms both the highest and lowest points of the altar, the place of meeting between heaven and earth. The blood applied to the horns of the altar also evokes Psalm 118:27 and the ultimate sacrifice to which it points.

verses.kjv_text: But the fat, and the kidneys, and the caul above the liver of the sin offering, he burnt upon the altar; as the LORD commanded Moses.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת הַחֵלֶב וְאֶת הַכְּלָיֹת וְאֵת הַיֹּתֶרֶת מִן הַכָּבֵד מִן הַחַטָּאת הִקְטִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחָה כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-et ha-chelev ve-et ha-kelayot ve-et ha-yoteret min ha-kaved min ha-chatta'at hiqtir ha-mizbeachah ka-asher tzivvah YHWH et-Moshe.
verses.study_note: The specific portions burned on the altar — the fat (chelev), kidneys (kelayot), and the caul (yoteret) above the liver — are prescribed in Leviticus 3:3-5 and 4:8-10 for all sin and peace offerings. The fat was considered the richest, finest portion; offering it to God expressed that He deserves the best. The kidneys were associated in Hebrew thought with the deepest seat of emotion and conscience (they are often paired with the heart in wisdom literature). Burning these on the altar is described by the verb hiqtir, from qatar, to produce smoke — the smoke rising to God as a 'sweet savor.' The phrase 'as the LORD commanded Moses' closes the verse, establishing Aaron's meticulous obedience to the Mosaic instruction.

verses.kjv_text: And the flesh and the hide he burnt with fire without the camp.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת הַבָּשָׂר וְאֶת הָעוֹר שָׂרַף בָּאֵשׁ מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-et ha-basar ve-et ha-or saraf ba-esh mi-chutz la-machaneh.
verses.study_note: The flesh and hide of Aaron's personal sin offering are burned outside the camp (mi-chutz la-machaneh), following the prescribed procedure for priestly sin offerings (Lev 4:11-12,21). This disposal is significant: no priestly consumption of the sin offering brought for one's own atonement. The high priest cannot eat what atones for himself — the offering is entirely consumed, nothing returned to the offerer. This 'outside the camp' burning carries profound typological weight: Hebrews 13:11-13 explicitly applies it to Christ, who 'suffered without the gate' (exō tēs pūlēs) — outside Jerusalem, bearing the shame and rejection of those cast outside the covenant community. The camp represents God's holy presence; to be outside was to be under judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And he slew the burnt offering; and Aaron’s sons presented unto him the blood, which he sprinkled round about upon the altar.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁחַט אֶת הָעֹלָה וַיַּמְצִיאוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן אֵלָיו אֶת הַדָּם וַיִּזְרְקֵהוּ עַל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishchat et ha-olah vayyamtzi'u benei Aharon elav et ha-dam vayyizreqehu al ha-mizbeach saviv.
verses.study_note: Following the sin offering, Aaron now sacrifices his personal burnt offering (olah). The blood application for the burnt offering differs from the sin offering: rather than applying it to the horns with a finger, the priest sprinkles (yizreq) the blood 'round about upon the altar' (al ha-mizbeach saviv) — a thorough, encircling application. This sprinkling (zaraq) is characteristic of the burnt and peace offerings, while the dipping-and-applying (natan) is characteristic of the sin offering. Together they constitute two complementary atonement acts: the sin offering covers specific transgression; the burnt offering expresses total consecration. The sons of Aaron serve as assistants, bringing the blood to their father — the high priest acts, but not alone.

verses.kjv_text: And they presented the burnt offering unto him, with the pieces thereof, and the head: and he burnt them upon the altar.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת הָעֹלָה הִמְצִיאוּ אֵלָיו לִנְתָחֶיהָ וְאֶת הָרֹאשׁ וַיַּקְטֵר עַל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-et ha-olah himtzi'u elav li-netacheha ve-et ha-rosh vayyaqter al ha-mizbeach.
verses.study_note: The burnt offering animal is cut into 'pieces' (netachim) and the head (rosh) presented separately — following the butchering procedure of Leviticus 1:6-9. The precise dissection and arrangement on the altar reflects that nothing is careless or random in God's worship: even the mechanics of sacrifice are ordered and prescribed. The 'head' being offered separately may reflect a concern that nothing is omitted, that the whole animal — from the thinking and directing faculty (head) to the extremities — is consecrated to God. Aaron then burns them on the altar (vayyaqter), the smoke rising as an ascending whole-gift to the LORD. This total-surrender theme of the olah runs throughout Scripture as the model of complete devotion.

verses.kjv_text: And he did wash the inwards and the legs, and burnt them upon the burnt offering on the altar.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרְחַץ אֶת הַקֶּרֶב וְאֶת הַכְּרָעַיִם וַיַּקְטֵר עַל הָעֹלָה הַמִּזְבֵּחָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyirchatz et ha-qerev ve-et ha-kera'ayim vayyaqter al ha-olah ha-mizbeachah.
verses.study_note: The inward parts (qerev) and legs (kera'ayim) are washed before being burned — the prescribed procedure of Leviticus 1:9,13. The washing removes external defilement from the internal organs and feet, symbolically purifying the earthly parts of the animal before they are offered as a holy gift. Nothing unclean may ascend to God; even the practical butchering details of sacrifice carry the theological principle that 'without holiness no man shall see the Lord' (Heb 12:14). The thoroughness of Aaron's obedience — washing inwards and legs before burning — exemplifies the principle that priestly worship admits no shortcut, no approximation, no 'near enough.' Exact obedience is the grammar of worship.

verses.kjv_text: And he brought the people’s offering, and took the goat, which was the sin offering for the people, and slew it, and offered it for sin, as the first.
verses.original_text: וַיַּקְרֵב אֵת קָרְבַּן הָעָם וַיִּקַּח אֶת שְׂעִיר הַחַטָּאת אֲשֶׁר לָעָם וַיִּשְׁחָטֵהוּ וַיְחַטְּאֵהוּ כָּרִאשׁוֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqrev et qorban ha-am vayyiqqach et se'ir ha-chatta'at asher la-am vayyishchattehu vaychatte'ehu ka-rishon.
verses.study_note: Having completed his own offerings, Aaron now turns to the people's qorban (offering). He takes the goat (se'ir) of the sin offering for the people and sacrifices it, performing the sin offering 'as the first' (ka-rishon) — in the same manner as his own sin offering. The repetition of the verb chitte' (to perform a sin offering, to de-sin) in vaychatte'ehu is striking: the action removes sin, cleanses the defiled. The transition from priestly to communal offerings marks the essential flow of Levitical mediation: the priest first atoned for himself, then he could effectively mediate atonement for others. Every element of Aaron's priestly succession points forward to the one who mediates perfectly for His people.
verses.doctrinal_note: The two-stage progression — priest first, then people — mirrors the Day of Atonement logic and anticipates Hebrews' central argument about the inadequacy of repeated Levitical sacrifice. The phrase 'for the people' (la-am) signals that Aaron's ministry is fundamentally mediatorial: he exists before God not for himself but for the nation. This is the fundamental structure of priesthood that reaches its fullest expression in Christ, who 'ever liveth to make intercession' for His people (Heb 7:25). Where Aaron's mediation required annual repetition and personal sin offering, Christ's single offering permanently perfects those who are sanctified (Heb 10:14).

verses.kjv_text: And he brought the burnt offering, and offered it according to the manner.
verses.original_text: וַיַּקְרֵב אֶת הָעֹלָה וַיַּעֲשֶׂהָ כַּמִּשְׁפָּט׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqrev et ha-olah vayyya'aseha ka-mishpat.
verses.study_note: Aaron brings the people's burnt offering and performs it 'according to the manner' (ka-mishpat). The word mishpat (H4941) is a legal-judicial term meaning ordinance, right procedure, the established norm of justice and order. By applying it to the burnt offering, the text signals that proper worship is not improvised but governed by divine ordinance — it has a specific 'manner' established by God and regulated by law. This brevity of description ('according to the manner') compared to the detailed instructions earlier reflects that Aaron has now demonstrated his mastery of priestly procedure: the detailed instructions of chapters 1-8 are enacted here with full faithfulness. The mishpat of worship is now embedded in Aaron's priestly practice.

verses.kjv_text: And he brought the meat offering, and took an handful thereof, and burnt it upon the altar, beside the burnt sacrifice of the morning.
verses.original_text: וַיַּקְרֵב אֶת הַמִּנְחָה וַיְמַלֵּא כַפּוֹ מִמֶּנָּה וַיַּקְטֵר עַל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ מִלְּבַד עֹלַת הַבֹּקֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqrev et ha-minchah vaymalle kappo mimmenah vayyaqter al ha-mizbeach mil-levad olat ha-boqer.
verses.study_note: The grain offering (minchah) is brought and Aaron takes 'a handful' (kappoh) from it — the prescribed 'memorial portion' (azkarah) of the grain offering (Lev 2:2,9). The handful that Aaron burns on the altar represents the whole; the part stands for the total gift. The phrase 'beside the burnt sacrifice of the morning' (mil-levad olat ha-boqer) reveals that this inauguration ceremony takes place alongside the regular daily offering, not instead of it. The tamid (daily continual offering) continues even on this extraordinary day — ordinary faithfulness and extraordinary ceremony coexist. This detail teaches that the rhythms of daily worship are not suspended by great occasions but undergird them.

verses.kjv_text: He slew also the bullock and the ram for a sacrifice of peace offerings, which was for the people: and Aaron’s sons presented unto him the blood, which he sprinkled upon the altar round about,
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁחַט אֶת הַשּׁוֹר וְאֶת הָאַיִל זֶבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים אֲשֶׁר לָעָם וַיַּמְצִיאוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן אֶת הַדָּם אֵלָיו וַיִּזְרְקֵהוּ עַל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishchat et ha-shor ve-et ha-ayil zevach hash-shelamim asher la-am vayyamtzi'u benei Aharon et ha-dam elav vayyizreqehu al ha-mizbeach saviv.
verses.study_note: The climactic offering of the sequence — the peace offerings (shelamim) — now begins with the slaughter of the bull and ram for the people. The shelamim is uniquely communal: unlike the burnt offering (entirely consumed) or the sin offering (given to priest or burned), the peace offering's meat is shared between God (fat burned on the altar), priest (breast and right thigh, Lev 7:31-33), and worshiper (remaining meat). It is the feast-offering, the covenant meal, the table shared between heaven and earth. The blood is sprinkled 'round about' (saviv) in the same manner as the burnt offering blood — comprehensive application, leaving no part of the altar untouched by the blood of reconciliation.

verses.kjv_text: And the fat of the bullock and of the ram, the rump, and that which covereth the inwards, and the kidneys, and the caul above the liver:
verses.original_text: וְאֶת הַחֲלָבִים מִן הַשּׁוֹר וּמִן הָאַיִל הָאַלְיָה וְהַמְכַסֶּה וְהַכְּלָיֹת וְיֹתֶרֶת הַכָּבֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-et ha-chalavim min ha-shor u-min ha-ayil ha-alyah ve-ha-mekhassah ve-ha-kelayot ve-yoteret ha-kaved.
verses.study_note: This verse catalogs the specific fat portions of the peace offerings devoted to God: the fat (chalavim), the 'rump' or fat tail (alyah — uniquely mentioned here, a feature of the broad-tailed sheep of Canaan), the covering fat (mekhassah) over the inward organs, the kidneys, and the lobe of the liver. These are the same portions prescribed in Leviticus 3:9-11 for the peace offering. The meticulous enumeration serves a liturgical-legal function: nothing is ambiguous about what belongs to God. The alyah (fat tail) is mentioned only here and in Leviticus 3:9; 7:3; Exodus 29:22 — specific to the Canaanite sheep breed, showing the laws are calibrated to the actual livestock of the land Israel is entering.

verses.kjv_text: And they put the fat upon the breasts, and he burnt the fat upon the altar:
verses.original_text: וַיָּשִׂימוּ אֶת הַחֲלָבִים עַל הֶחָזוֹת וַיַּקְטֵר הַחֵלֶב הַמִּזְבֵּחָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasimu et ha-chalavim al he-chazot vayyaqter ha-chelev ha-mizbeachah.
verses.study_note: The fat portions are placed upon the breasts (chazot) for the waving ceremony before being burned. In the peace offering, the breast was the 'wave breast' (chazeh hat-tenufah, Lev 7:30-31) — elevated and waved before the LORD as an act of presentation, then given to the officiating priest. The fat was placed on top of the breast during this ceremony before being removed to be burned. This staging — fat on breast, wave before God, fat removed to altar — reflects the choreography of priestly worship where every movement has meaning: the offering is first 'waved' before God as a presentation, then the fat goes to God by fire while the breast goes to the priest as his covenant portion.

verses.kjv_text: And the breasts and the right shoulder Aaron waved for a wave offering before the LORD; as Moses commanded.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת הֶחָזוֹת וְאֵת שׁוֹק הַיָּמִין הֵנִיף אַהֲרֹן תְּנוּפָה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-et he-chazot ve-et shoq ha-yamin henif Aharon tenufah lifnei YHWH ka-asher tzivvah Moshe.
verses.study_note: Aaron performs the waving (tenufah) of the breast and right shoulder (shoq ha-yamin) before the LORD — the ceremony of presenting these portions to God symbolically before they are given to the priests as their covenant portion. The wave offering (tenufah) involves a lateral motion before the altar, presenting the food to God who then 'gives it back' to His priests as a covenant gift. 'The right shoulder' (shoq ha-yamin) is the 'heave shoulder' (terumah), the elevated portion devoted to God. Together the wave breast and heave shoulder represent the peace offering's distribution: fat to God, breast to ordinary priests, right shoulder to the officiating priest. The chapter closes this section with 'as Moses commanded,' the refrain of perfect priestly obedience.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron lifted up his hand toward the people, and blessed them, and came down from offering of the sin offering, and the burnt offering, and peace offerings.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשָּׂא אַהֲרֹן אֶת יָדָו אֶל הָעָם וַיְבָרְכֵם וַיֵּרֶד מֵעֲשֹׂת הַחַטָּאת וְהָעֹלָה וְהַשְּׁלָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyissa Aharon et yadav el ha-am vayvarekhm vayyered me'asot ha-chatta'at ve-ha-olah ve-hash-shelamim.
verses.study_note: Having completed the sacrificial sequence, Aaron 'lifted up his hand' (vayyissa et yadav) toward the people and blessed them. This is the priestly blessing — the formal act by which the priest pronounces God's covenant blessing over the congregation after sacrifice. The Aaronic blessing formula is prescribed in Numbers 6:24-26 ('The LORD bless thee and keep thee...'), though here the specific words are not given. The gesture 'lifted up his hand' is the classic posture of benediction. Aaron then 'came down' from the altar platform — the sacrificial ministry is complete; now the pastoral-blessing ministry begins. The three offerings named in reverse order (sin, burnt, peace) provide a liturgical summary of the entire ceremony.
verses.doctrinal_note: The priestly blessing at the conclusion of sacrifice establishes the liturgical pattern fulfilled in Christ's high priestly ministry. Hebrews 7:1-3 connects the Aaronic blessing to Melchizedek who 'blessed' Abraham, and ultimately to Christ who 'ever liveth to make intercession' (Heb 7:25). Significantly, the risen Christ's last act before His ascension echoes this Levitical pattern: 'And he led them out as far as to Bethany, and he lifted up his hands, and blessed them. And it came to pass, while he blessed them, he was parted from them' (Luke 24:50-51). The ascension is the fulfillment of Aaron lifting his hands from the altar — the Great High Priest blessing His people as He enters the heavenly sanctuary.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses and Aaron went into the tabernacle of the congregation, and came out, and blessed the people: and the glory of the LORD appeared unto all the people.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן אֶל אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וַיֵּצְאוּ וַיְבָרֲכוּ אֶת הָעָם וַיֵּרָא כְבוֹד יְהוָה אֶל כָּל הָעָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo Moshe ve-Aharon el ohel mo'ed vayyetze'u vayvarechu et ha-am vayyera kevod YHWH el kol ha-am.
verses.study_note: The climactic verse of the chapter. Moses and Aaron enter the tabernacle together — an act without explicit parallel elsewhere, suggesting a joint petitionary act or final consecration ceremony before the divine presence within. They emerge together, bless the people — the dual blessing of prophet and priest over the whole congregation — and then it happens: 'the glory of the LORD appeared unto all the people' (vayyera kevod YHWH el kol ha-am). The verb vayyera is niphal of ra'ah, the self-disclosure of God predicted in verses 4 and 6, now fulfilled. This is the moment the entire book has been building toward: God taking up visible residence among His redeemed people, accepting their worship, and revealing His glory. Kol ha-am — 'all the people' — witnesses this theophany together.
verses.doctrinal_note: The appearing of the kevod YHWH to all Israel establishes the definitive pattern of divine self-disclosure in the covenant community. This event is the fulfillment of the Exodus promise — God would dwell among His people (Exod 29:45-46). The glory-trajectory from this moment runs: tabernacle (Lev 9:23) → Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11) → Ezekiel's vision of departure and return (Ezek 10:18; 43:4) → incarnation, where the eternal Word 'tabernacled among us and we beheld his glory' (John 1:14) → Pentecost, where the Spirit-glory fills the new covenant community (Acts 2) → consummation, where the New Jerusalem has no need of sun or moon 'for the glory of God did lighten it' (Rev 21:23). Every moment of kevod YHWH appearing is a partial, anticipatory fulfillment pointing to the unmediated, face-to-face glory of the new creation (Rev 22:4).

verses.kjv_text: And there came a fire out from before the LORD, and consumed upon the altar the burnt offering and the fat: which when all the people saw, they shouted, and fell on their faces.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּצֵא אֵשׁ מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה וַתֹּאכַל עַל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ אֶת הָעֹלָה וְאֶת הַחֲלָבִים וַיַּרְא כָּל הָעָם וַיָּרֹנּוּ וַיִּפְּלוּ עַל פְּנֵיהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vattetze esh mi-lifnei YHWH vattokhal al ha-mizbeach et ha-olah ve-et ha-chalavim vayyar kol ha-am vayyaronnu vayyippelu al peneihem.
verses.study_note: The final verse is the chapter's thunderclap. Fire 'came out from before the LORD' (vattetze esh mi-lifnei YHWH) — divine fire of miraculous origin, not human kindling — and consumed the burnt offering and the fat portions on the altar. This is God's own sign of acceptance: He has received the sacrifice. The identical divine-fire sign appears at Gideon's offering (Judg 6:21), Elijah's sacrifice on Carmel (1 Kgs 18:38), David's altar at Araunah's threshing floor (1 Chr 21:26), and Solomon's temple dedication (2 Chr 7:1). In each case, fire from the LORD signals the divine acceptance of both sacrifice and worshiper. The people's response is twofold: they 'shouted' (vayyaronnu — a cry of exultant joy, a rinnah) and 'fell on their faces' (vayyippelu al peneihem) — spontaneous reverence before the theophany. Joy and awe together form the complete response to divine self-disclosure.
verses.doctrinal_note: The divine fire consuming Aaron's first offering is the definitive Old Testament sign of sacrificial acceptance, and its typological trajectory reaches its fulfillment at the cross and Pentecost. At Calvary, the fire of divine judgment — the wrath of God against sin — fell upon Christ, the true burnt offering, consuming Him as the perfect sacrifice (Isa 53:10 — 'it pleased the LORD to bruise him'). At Pentecost, fire from heaven appeared again, not as consuming judgment but as tongues of flame resting on the disciples (Acts 2:3) — the same divine fire now descending as acceptance of Christ's sacrifice and inauguration of the new covenant priesthood (1 Pet 2:9). The shouting (rinnah) of Israel before the fire anticipates the shout of the redeemed (Rev 19:1-6) who celebrate the final, complete sacrifice of the Lamb. The prostration (al peneihem) anticipates every knee bowing before Christ (Phil 2:10-11).

meta.overview:
Leviticus 9 records one of the most theologically momentous days in all of Old Testament history — the eighth day following the seven-day consecration of Aaron and his sons (Leviticus 8). On this inaugural day, Aaron formally assumes his office as High Priest of Israel and offers sacrifice for the first time in his own name and in the name of the congregation. The chapter divides naturally into two movements: first, Moses instructs Aaron on the precise offerings required (vv.1-6); second, Aaron executes those offerings in exact obedience (vv.7-21), culminating in the blessing of the people and the stunning theophany in which the glory of the LORD appears to all Israel and divine fire consumes the burnt offering on the altar (vv.22-24).

The theological weight of this chapter is staggering. For the first time since the giving of the Torah at Sinai, God visibly manifests His presence and glory in the midst of His redeemed people — not in cloud and fire on the mountain in terror, but in approval and acceptance over the altar of sacrifice. The text carefully distinguishes between the sin offerings Aaron brings for himself (v.2) and those brought for the people (v.3), a distinction the book of Hebrews makes central to its argument about the Aaronic priesthood's inadequacy and Christ's superiority (Heb. 7:26-28). Aaron, unlike Christ, must first atone for his own sin before ministering for others.

The chapter's climax — fire from the LORD consuming the burnt offering — is one of Scripture's recurring signs of divine acceptance (cf. Gen 15:17; 1 Kgs 18:38; 1 Chr 21:26; 2 Chr 7:1). When the people see it, they shout and fall on their faces. This prostration before the glory of God is the proper response of redeemed humanity to the presence of holiness. The entire chapter moves from instruction to obedience to theophany — a pattern that defines Israel's covenantal life: hear, obey, behold.

Christologically, Leviticus 9 is a shadow of the greater inauguration: Christ's own high priestly ministry established not through blood of bulls and goats but through His own blood (Heb. 9:11-14). The eighth day itself carries new-creation resonance — circumcision on the eighth day, the resurrection on the first day which is the eighth, the new creation inaugurated in Christ. Aaron's ministry begins on the eighth day; Christ's eternal priesthood is inaugurated by resurrection. The glory that falls on Aaron's altar as fire anticipates the glory that fills the new covenant community at Pentecost and the glory that will fill the new creation at the consummation.
meta.theme: The Inauguration of Priestly Ministry and the Appearing Glory of the LORD
meta.keyWordMeaning: The Glory of the LORD — from kavod (H3519), meaning weight, heaviness, honor, splendor. The visible, tangible manifestation of God's presence and majesty. In Leviticus 9 it appears over the altar as the fire of divine acceptance, vindicating Aaron's ministry and validating the entire sacrificial system. This glory-appearance trajectory runs from the tabernacle (Lev 9:23) to the temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11) to the incarnation (John 1:14) to the new Jerusalem (Rev 21:23).
outline: The Eighth Day Command: Offerings Prescribed (vv.1-4): Moses instructs Aaron on the sin, burnt, peace, and grain offerings
The Gathering of Israel and the Promise of Glory (vv.5-6): The congregation assembles and Moses announces the LORD will appear
Aaron's Offerings for Himself and His Sons (vv.7-14): The sin offering and burnt offering for the priesthood
Aaron's Offerings for the People (vv.15-21): The sin offering, burnt offering, grain offering, and peace offerings for Israel
The Double Blessing and the Theophany (vv.22-24): Aaron blesses the people, the glory appears, and fire consumes the offering
The People's Response: Shouting and Prostration (v.24b): Israel's worship before the manifest glory of God

verses.kjv_text: And Nadab and Abihu, the sons of Aaron, took either of them his censer, and put fire therein, and put incense thereon, and offered strange fire before the LORD, which he commanded them not.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְחוּ בְנֵי־אַהֲרֹן נָדָב וַאֲבִיהוּא אִישׁ מַחְתָּתוֹ וַיִּתְּנוּ בָהֵן אֵשׁ וַיָּשִׂימוּ עָלֶיהָ קְטֹרֶת וַיַּקְרִיבוּ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה אֵשׁ זָרָה אֲשֶׁר לֹא צִוָּה אֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikchu venei-Aharon Nadav va'Avihu ish machtato vayyittenu vahen esh vayyasimu aleha ketoret vayyakrivu lifnei YHWH esh zarah asher lo tzivvah otam.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with stunning abruptness — two of Aaron's four sons, who had been among the seventy elders privileged to see God on Sinai (Exod 24:1,9-11) and who had just been ordained as priests (Lev 8-9), commit a fatal act of liturgical presumption. The phrase "esh zarah" (strange/unauthorized fire) is the crux of the chapter. Debate persists over the exact nature of their sin: using fire from outside the altar of burnt offering, offering at the wrong time, using incense of the wrong formula, or acting while drunk (implied by v.9). The most probable interpretation is that they used fire not taken from the sacred altar as commanded (Lev 16:12; cf. Lev 1:7). The text does not say the fire was physically different — only that it was unauthorized. The defining clause "which he commanded them not" (asher lo tzivvah otam) is the theological center. The offense was not what they did per se but that they did what God had not authorized. Note that each took "his censer" (machtato — possessive, singular initiative), suggesting individual, uncoordinated action rather than prescribed liturgical order.
verses.doctrinal_note: The principle "which he commanded them not" (asher lo tzivvah otam) is one of the foundational phrases in Levitical theology and a touchstone of the regulative principle of worship: in the sanctuary, only what God explicitly commands is permitted. This stands in explicit contrast to a permissive principle (all that God has not forbidden is allowed). The New Testament applies this principle in John 4:24 ("worship in spirit and in truth"), Colossians 2:23 (will-worship, self-imposed religion), and Hebrews 12:28-29. Nadab and Abihu represent the universal human temptation to approach God on one's own terms rather than His.

verses.kjv_text: And there went out fire from the LORD, and devoured them, and they died before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּצֵא אֵשׁ מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה וַתֹּאכַל אֹתָם וַיָּמֻתוּ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vattetze esh millifnei YHWH vattokal otam vayyamutu lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: Divine judgment is swift and total. The same fire that had just consumed the burnt offering in vindication of Israel's worship (Lev 9:24) now consumes the priests who transgressed its prescribed use. This is fire "from the LORD" (millifnei YHWH — proceeding from before the LORD), the divine fire that had just inaugurated the tabernacle services. The verb "devoured" (vattokal, from akal — to eat, consume) is the same verb used for the consuming of the offering in Lev 9:24. The literary parallel is deliberate: the same holy fire that accepted proper sacrifice now judges improper worship. "They died before the LORD" (lifnei YHWH) — the phrase "before the LORD" is repeated twice in this single verse, emphasizing that this was not an accident or natural calamity but a divine judicial act in the very presence of God. The detail in v.5 that their bodies were carried out "in their coats" suggests the fire consumed the men while leaving their priestly garments intact — a targeted judicial act, not uncontrolled fire.
verses.doctrinal_note: The immediate divine judgment of Nadab and Abihu is one of several "boundary events" in Scripture where God acts dramatically to establish the seriousness of His holiness at inaugural moments: the plague at Israel's first idolatry (Exod 32:28), Uzzah at the beginning of David's ark-return (2 Sam 6:6-7), Ananias and Sapphira at the founding of the church (Acts 5:1-11), and the Corinthian deaths at the Lord's Supper (1 Cor 11:27-30). These are not arbitrary severity but divine instruction at critical thresholds. Hebrews 12:29 anchors the theology: "our God is a consuming fire" — the same phrase used of the burning bush and of Sinai, now applied to the New Covenant community.

verses.kjv_text: Then Moses said unto Aaron, This is it that the LORD spake, saying, I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me, and before all the people I will be glorified. And Aaron held his peace.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־אַהֲרֹן הוּא אֲשֶׁר־דִּבֶּר יְהוָה לֵאמֹר בִּקְרֹבַי אֶקָּדֵשׁ וְעַל־פְּנֵי כָל־הָעָם אֶכָּבֵד וַיִּדֹּם אַהֲרֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Mosheh el-Aharon hu asher-dibber YHWH lemor bikrovai ekkadesh ve'al-penei khol-ha'am ekkaved vayyiddom Aharon.
verses.study_note: Moses does not comfort Aaron with human sympathy but with theology. He quotes a divine principle — "I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me" — which may refer to an earlier unrecorded word or summarize the entire Levitical system (cf. Exod 19:22; 29:43). "Bikrovai ekkadesh" — "in those who draw near to me I will be hallowed." The priests who approach (karov — to draw near, the root of korban, offering) are the vehicles through which God's holiness is declared publicly. If they approach carelessly, God's holiness is still declared — through judgment. The nation witnesses both: the glory of Lev 9:24 and the judgment of 10:2. The closing phrase stands among the most profound in all of Torah: "vayyiddom Aharon" — "and Aaron was silent." The verb dom/damam means not merely quiet but stunned, submissive silence — the silence of one who has received a theological answer too weighty for reply. Aaron, who has just lost two sons, receives not consolation but instruction in divine holiness — and he submits.
verses.doctrinal_note: Aaron's silence (vayyiddom) is one of the most moving moments in all of Scripture and one of the highest models of faith under divine discipline in the Old Testament. Having just lost two sons, Aaron is not offered comfort but theology — and he submits to it without protest. This silence speaks of Psalm 39:9: "I was dumb, I opened not my mouth; because thou didst it." Aaron's silence anticipates Isaiah 53:7 — the Suffering Servant who "opened not his mouth" — and ultimately Christ Himself, who submitted without complaint to the Father's will in Gethsemane and on the cross (Matt 26:42; 1 Pet 2:23). The proper response to God's judicial acts is neither rage nor despair but awe and submission.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan, the sons of Uzziel the uncle of Aaron, and said unto them, Come near, carry your brethren from before the sanctuary out of the camp.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא מֹשֶׁה אֶל־מִישָׁאֵל וְאֶל־אֶלְצָפָן בְּנֵי עֻזִּיאֵל דֹּד אַהֲרֹן וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם קִרְבוּ שְׂאוּ אֶת־אֲחֵיכֶם מֵאֵת פְּנֵי־הַקֹּדֶשׁ אֶל־מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra Mosheh el-Mishael ve'el-Eltzafan benei Uzziel dod Aharon vayyomer alehem kirvu se'u et-acheikhem me'et penei-hakkodesh el-michutz lamachaneh.
verses.study_note: Moses acts with both pastoral wisdom and liturgical precision. Aaron and his two surviving sons cannot touch the dead or leave the tabernacle (vv.6-7) — they are in mid-service and their priestly ordination is not yet complete. So Moses summons Mishael and Elzaphan, cousins of Aaron and the dead priests (sons of Uzziel, who was son of Kohath and thus brother of Amram — Exod 6:18-22). As Kohathites not currently officiating at the altar, they are appropriate substitutes. "From before the sanctuary" (me'et penei-hakkodesh — literally "from before the face of the holy place") confirms the bodies lay in the court before the tabernacle entrance. They must be removed "out of the camp" — the dead, particularly the ritually contaminated dead, cannot remain within the sacred boundaries of the camp (Num 5:2; 19:11-13). The word "brethren" (acheikhem) is poignant — Moses asks cousins to carry cousins to burial.

verses.kjv_text: So they went near, and carried them in their coats out of the camp; as Moses had said.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרְבוּ וַיִּשָּׂאֻם בְּכֻתֳּנֹתָם אֶל־מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikrevu vayyissa'um bekuttonotam el-michutz lamachaneh ka'asher dibber Mosheh.
verses.study_note: The detail "in their coats" (bekuttonotam) carries significant theological weight. The priestly tunics (kuttonet) were one of the four garments prescribed for all priests (Exod 28:40). The fact that the garments remained while the men were consumed suggests the divine fire was a targeted judicial act — not uncontrolled burning. This remarkable detail echoes Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego emerging from the furnace with their garments unsinged (Dan 3:27), showing that divine fire operates with precise intentionality. The garments permitted handling of the corpses without direct skin contact, which would have been defiling. "As Moses had said" (ka'asher dibber Mosheh) — the compliance formula that appears throughout Leviticus and echoes the "as the LORD commanded" refrain of Exodus 40. Obedience is immediate, exact, and without recorded complaint.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto Aaron, and unto Eleazar and unto Ithamar, his sons, Uncover not your heads, neither rend your clothes; lest ye die, and lest wrath come upon all the people: but let your brethren, the whole house of Israel, bewail the burning which the LORD hath kindled.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־אַהֲרֹן וּלְאֶלְעָזָר וּלְאִיתָמָר בָּנָיו רָאשֵׁיכֶם אַל־תִּפְרָעוּ וּבִגְדֵיכֶם לֹא־תִפְרֹמוּ וְלֹא תָמֻתוּ וְעַל כָּל־הָעֵדָה יִקְצֹף וַאֲחֵיכֶם כָּל־בֵּית יִשְׂרָאֵל יִבְכּוּ אֶת־הַשְּׂרֵפָה אֲשֶׁר שָׂרַף יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Mosheh el-Aharon ule'Elazar ule'Itamar banav rasheikhem al-tifra'u uvigdeikhem lo-tifromu velo tamutu ve'al kol-ha'edah yiktzof va'acheikhem kol-beit Yisra'el yivku et-hasserefah asher saraf YHWH.
verses.study_note: Moses now addresses Aaron and his two surviving sons with an extraordinary demand. In ancient Israel, mourning was physically expressed by letting hair hang loose/disheveled ("uncover not your heads" — literally "do not let your hair go unbound") and tearing garments — both universal grief gestures across the ancient Near East (Gen 37:29; Job 1:20; 2 Sam 1:11). Moses prohibits both under penalty of death. The reasoning is twofold: (1) They remain in active priestly service — the tabernacle inauguration is not complete — and mourning rites render one ritually unfit for service; (2) Uncontrolled priestly grief could unleash divine wrath upon the entire congregation. The congregation of Israel may mourn, but the priests must maintain liturgical composure. This represents an extraordinary spiritual demand: Aaron must suppress the outward expression of grief for his own sons because his priestly office requires ongoing mediation on behalf of Israel. The cost of mediation is the subordination of personal loss to covenantal responsibility.
verses.doctrinal_note: The prohibition of priestly mourning points typologically toward Christ as High Priest. Aaron could not outwardly grieve as an ordinary mourner because his office required him to continue standing before God on behalf of Israel. In the New Testament, Christ as our great High Priest "ever liveth to make intercession for us" (Heb 7:25) — His priestly mediation is uninterrupted, not suspended by personal circumstance. The priestly office, by definition, is oriented outward toward others rather than inward toward self-expression. First Thessalonians 4:13 applies this principle to all believers: we do not "sorrow as others which have no hope" — not because grief is forbidden but because it is transformed by covenant reality.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall not go out from the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, lest ye die: for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you. And they did according to the word of Moses.
verses.original_text: וּמִפֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד לֹא תֵצְאוּ פֶּן־תָּמֻתוּ כִּי־שֶׁמֶן מִשְׁחַת יְהוָה עֲלֵיכֶם וַיַּעֲשׂוּ כִּדְבַר מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Umippetach ohel mo'ed lo tetze'u pen-tamutu ki-shemen mishchat YHWH aleikhem vayyaasu kidvar Mosheh.
verses.study_note: The priests must remain at their post — the tabernacle door — because "the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you." The anointing (mishchah) consecrated them permanently to a calling that transcends personal circumstance. This unique formula (Exod 30:22-33) was applied only to the tabernacle furniture and the priests — its application separated them from ordinary human life. To leave the sanctuary in a time of death and mourning would defile the anointing and invite death. The logic is relentless: the same fire that killed Nadab and Abihu for improper approach could kill Aaron's remaining sons for improper departure. The anointing creates both privilege and unbreakable obligation. "And they did according to the word of Moses" — Aaron and his sons obey without protest, extending the submissive silence of v.3 into action.
verses.doctrinal_note: The anointing oil (shemen mishchat) as the ground of the priests' obligation connects typologically to Christ, the ultimate Anointed One (Mashiach — Messiah). The oil's unique formula and its application to consecrated persons establishes that the anointing creates an indissoluble bond of service. Acts 10:38 describes how "God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Ghost and with power." In the New Testament, believers as "a royal priesthood" (1 Pet 2:9) share in this anointing through the Holy Spirit (1 John 2:20,27), receiving both the privilege of access and the obligation of set-apart living. The anointing that consecrates also constrains.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Aaron, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־אַהֲרֹן לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Aharon lemor.
verses.study_note: This is the only place in all of Leviticus where God speaks directly to Aaron alone, bypassing Moses as intermediary. Virtually all other Levitical legislation is given through Moses (note the refrain "the LORD spake unto Moses"). The singular directness of divine address to Aaron at this precise moment — immediately after his sons' deaths and the extraordinary demands placed upon him — is a profound act of divine pastoral care. God does not leave the grieving high priest to receive instruction only second-hand. The directness also amplifies the personal weight of the command that follows (vv.9-11): this is not a general Levitical regulation but a personal, direct word from God to the high priest himself, given in the immediate aftermath of tragedy.

verses.kjv_text: Do not drink wine nor strong drink, thou, nor thy sons with thee, when ye go into the tabernacle of the congregation, lest ye die: it shall be a statute for ever throughout your generations:
verses.original_text: יַיִן וְשֵׁכָר אַל־תֵּשְׁתְּ אַתָּה וּבָנֶיךָ אִתָּךְ בְּבֹאֲכֶם אֶל־אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְלֹא תָמֻתוּ חֻקַּת עוֹלָם לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Yayin veshekar al-tesht attah uvanekha ittekha bevo'akhem el-ohel mo'ed velo tamutu chukkat olam ledorotekhem.
verses.study_note: The placement of the wine prohibition immediately after the deaths of Nadab and Abihu is contextually decisive. Rabbinic tradition (Leviticus Rabbah; Tanchuma) and many scholars have concluded that the juxtaposition implies — if not explicitly states — that the brothers were intoxicated when they offered the strange fire. The text does not state this directly, but the etiological logic of placing this law precisely here would be remarkably unusual without a causal connection. "Yayin" (wine) and "shekar" (strong drink — typically barley-based fermented beverage) are the two standard biblical categories for intoxicating beverages; together they form a merism covering all intoxicants. "Lest ye die" (velo tamutu) directly echoes the fate of Nadab and Abihu in v.2 and the repeated warnings of vv.6-7 — the implicit argument is "lest what happened to them happen to you." "A statute forever throughout your generations" (chukkat olam ledorotekhem) — this is a permanent ordinance, not a crisis measure. Ezekiel 44:21 confirms its perpetuation into the later priestly tradition.
verses.doctrinal_note: The prohibition of intoxication for priests on duty is the clearest biblical statement that holy worship and ministry require full cognitive clarity, spiritual discernment, and unimpaired submission to divine instruction. Ephesians 5:18 establishes the New Covenant parallel with deliberate contrast: "be not drunk with wine... but be filled with the Spirit." The substitution of the Spirit's filling for wine's impairment is precisely the contrast Paul intends — do not seek ecstasy or freedom through wine but through the Spirit. First Timothy 3:3 and Titus 1:7 extend sobriety as a qualification for all church overseers, reflecting the principle that those who lead God's people into His presence must be most fully subject to His Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: And that ye may put difference between holy and unholy, and between unclean and clean;
verses.original_text: וּלֲהַבְדִּיל בֵּין הַקֹּדֶשׁ וּבֵין הַחֹל וּבֵין הַטָּמֵא וּבֵין הַטָּהוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Ulehavdil bein hakkodesh uvein hachol uvein hattame uvein hattahor.
verses.study_note: This verse provides the purpose statement behind the wine prohibition: the priest must retain the capacity to distinguish (havdil) between the holy (kodesh) and the common/profane (chol), between the unclean (tame) and the clean (tahor). The entire Levitical system rested upon these four distinctions, and the entire book of Leviticus is in many ways an extended taxonomy of these categories. The priest's role was fundamentally pedagogical as well as liturgical — he was to embody, maintain, and teach these distinctions (v.11). If priestly discernment was impaired — by wine, carelessness, or familiarity — the entire system of holiness collapsed at its center. Nadab and Abihu's fatal act may itself represent a failure of distinction: they could not tell the difference between authorized and unauthorized fire, between what God commanded and what seemed good to them.
verses.doctrinal_note: The priestly task of havdil (distinction/separation) is rooted in the theology of creation. God's own creative activity in Genesis 1 was a series of havdalot (separations): light from darkness (v.4), waters above from below (v.7), sea from land (v.9), holy from common. Priestly work is thus a participation in God's own ordering of creation against chaos — a calling to maintain the structures by which the world reflects divine order. In Christ, these distinctions are not abolished but fulfilled and elevated (Matt 5:17-18). The Spirit's gift of discernment (1 Cor 12:10) and the mature believer's capacity to "discern both good and evil" (Heb 5:14) are the New Covenant parallel to the Levitical priest's task of havdil.

verses.kjv_text: And that ye may teach the children of Israel all the statutes which the LORD hath spoken unto them by the hand of Moses.
verses.original_text: וּלְהוֹרֹת אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֵת כָּל־הַחֻקִּים אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה אֲלֵיהֶם בְּיַד מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Ulhorot et-benei Yisra'el et kol-hachukkim asher dibber YHWH alehem beyad Mosheh.
verses.study_note: The purpose of the wine prohibition culminates here: the priest must be able to teach. "Horot" (to teach, instruct) is from the verb yarah, the root of Torah — priestly teaching is itself Torah, the "shooting" of divine instruction toward its target. The priest's role is not merely liturgical (performing rituals) but pedagogical (transmitting divine knowledge). An intoxicated priest cannot instruct. Deuteronomy 33:10 and Malachi 2:7 both define the Levitical calling as teaching: "they shall teach Jacob thy judgments" and "the priest's lips should keep knowledge." Nehemiah 8:7-9 shows the Levites explaining the Law to the assembled people. "By the hand of Moses" (beyad Mosheh) — Moses is the mediatorial instrument through whom the divine statutes are transmitted. This mediatorial formula anticipates the ultimate Mediator (1 Tim 2:5) who not only transmits but embodies divine instruction.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses spake unto Aaron, and unto Eleazar and unto Ithamar, his sons that were left, Take the meat offering that remaineth of the offerings of the LORD made by fire, and eat it without leaven beside the altar: for it is most holy:
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־אַהֲרֹן וְאֶל אֶלְעָזָר וְאֶל־אִיתָמָר בָּנָיו הַנּוֹתָרִים קְחוּ אֶת־הַמִּנְחָה הַנּוֹתֶרֶת מֵאִשֵּׁי יְהוָה וְאִכְלוּהָ מַצּוֹת אֵצֶל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ כִּי קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber Mosheh el-Aharon ve'el Elazar ve'el-Itamar banav hannoterim kechu et-hamminchah hannoterit me'ishei YHWH ve'ikluha matzot etzel hamizbe'ach ki kodesh kodashim hi.
verses.study_note: Moses now returns to practical liturgical instruction, addressing Aaron and "his sons that were left" (hannoterim — the remaining ones). This quietly poignant phrase acknowledges the deaths of Nadab and Abihu while pressing forward: the worship of God must continue even in grief. The meal offering (minchah) that formed part of the day's ceremony must still be consumed by the priests. This is one of the chapter's more remarkable theological statements: despite the catastrophe, the sacred liturgy proceeds. The minchah must be eaten "without leaven" (matzot — unleavened) because leaven symbolized corruption and was excluded from all offerings burned to the LORD (Lev 2:11; cf. 1 Cor 5:7-8). It must be consumed "beside the altar" (etzel hamizbeach) — within the court of the tabernacle, not taken outside. Its designation as "most holy" (kodesh kodashim — literally "holy of holies") restricts it to the male priests in the holy court.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall eat it in the holy place, because it is thy due, and thy sons’ due, of the sacrifices of the LORD made by fire: for so I am commanded.
verses.original_text: וַאֲכַלְתֶּם אֹתָהּ בְּמָקוֹם קָדוֹשׁ כִּי חָקְךָ וְחָק־בָּנֶיךָ הִוא מֵאִשֵּׁי יְהוָה כִּי כֵן צֻוֵּיתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Va'akhaltem otah bemmakom kadosh ki chakka vekhak-banekha hi me'ishei YHWH ki khen tzuvveiti.
verses.study_note: Moses grounds the obligation to eat the offering in the concept of the priestly due (chok — prescribed portion, statute, divine entitlement): this is not optional piety but an ordained responsibility tied to the office. Eating the priestly portion was itself a covenantal act — the priest sustained by the altar was thereby bound to the altar. The closing phrase is theologically remarkable: "for so I am commanded" (ki khen tzuvveiti). Moses does not speak from his own authority but declares his own subordination to divine command. The man who wrote the Torah here models the appropriate posture of all who handle sacred things: I do this not because I originated it but because I was commanded. The pastor, teacher, or priest is always under authority before he is in authority.

verses.kjv_text: And the wave breast and heave shoulder shall ye eat in a clean place; thou, and thy sons, and thy daughters with thee: for they be thy due, and thy sons’ due, which are given out of the sacrifices of peace offerings of the children of Israel.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת חֲזֵה הַתְּנוּפָה וְאֵת שׁוֹק הַתְּרוּמָה תֹּאכְלוּ בְּמָקוֹם טָהוֹר אַתָּה וּבָנֶיךָ וּבְנֹתֶיךָ אִתָּךְ כִּי חָקְךָ וְחָק בָּנֶיךָ נִתְּנוּ מִזִּבְחֵי שַׁלְמֵי בְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et chazeh hattenufah ve'et shok hattenumah tokheluv bemmakom tahor attah uvanekha uvnotekha ittekha ki chakka vechak vanekha nittenu mizzivchei shalmei venei Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: This verse distinguishes between two tiers of priestly sanctity in consuming offerings. The "most holy" portions (vv.12-13) could only be eaten by the male priests in the sacred court. The "holy" portions of this verse — the wave breast and heave shoulder from the peace offerings — may be eaten by the entire priestly family, including daughters, in any "clean place" (makom tahor), not restricted to the court. The "wave breast" (chazeh hattenufah) was waved before the LORD in a presentation ritual before being granted to the priests (Lev 7:30-34). The "heave shoulder" (shok hattenumah) was the right thigh elevated before God. These portions from the peace offerings (shelamin — from shalom, completeness/peace) constituted the regular food provision for the priestly family — God's covenant to sustain those who sustained the nation's covenant worship (Num 18:11-19).

verses.kjv_text: The heave shoulder and the wave breast shall they bring with the offerings made by fire of the fat, to wave it for a wave offering before the LORD; and it shall be thine, and thy sons’ with thee, by a statute for ever; as the LORD hath commanded.
verses.original_text: שׁוֹק הַתְּרוּמָה וַחֲזֵה הַתְּנוּפָה עַל אִשֵּׁי הַחֲלָבִים יָבִיאוּ לְהָנִיף תְּנוּפָה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וְהָיָה לְךָ וּלְבָנֶיךָ אִתְּךָ לְחָק עוֹלָם כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Shok hattenumah vachazeh hattenufah al ishei hachalavim yavi'u lehaniph ttenufah lifnei YHWH vehayah lekha ulivvanekha ittekha lechok olam ka'asher tzivvah YHWH.
verses.study_note: This verse describes the ritual sequence: the priestly portions are brought alongside the fat portions destined to be burned on the altar, and the combined presentation is waved before the LORD. The fat (chelev) is then burned as God's portion; the breast and shoulder return to the priests as God's gift to them. The "waving" (tenufah — horizontal motion) and "heaving" (tenumah — vertical motion) represented the offering being given to God and then returned — a enacted theology of gift and grace. Everything belongs to God first; the priestly portion is what God graciously gives back from what is entirely His. "A statute forever as the LORD commanded" (lechok olam ka'asher tzivvah YHWH) — the closing formula establishing permanent covenantal obligation for priestly sustenance.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses diligently sought the goat of the sin offering, and, behold, it was burnt: and he was angry with Eleazar and Ithamar, the sons of Aaron which were left alive, saying,
verses.original_text: וְאֵת שְׂעִיר הַחַטָּאת דָּרֹשׁ דָּרַשׁ מֹשֶׁה וְהִנֵּה שֹׂרָף וַיִּקְצֹף עַל אֶלְעָזָר וְעַל אִיתָמָר בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן הַנּוֹתָרִים לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et se'ir hachatta't darosh darash Mosheh vehinneh soraf vayyiktzof al Elazar ve'al Itamar benei Aharon hannoterim lemor.
verses.study_note: The narrative shifts to a second confrontation. Moses "diligently sought" — the infinitive absolute construction (darosh darash — seeking he sought) is an emphatic form indicating urgent, thorough investigation. Moses discovers that the sin offering goat from the day's ceremony had been burned entirely rather than eaten by the priests in the holy place as the law required (Lev 6:26; cf. vv.17-18). His anger is appropriate and judicial. The irony of this scene is profound: on the very day that two priests were killed for liturgical presumption, two other priests violated a different liturgical regulation — and their deviation comes to light. The chapter thus holds two different failures in tension: unauthorized addition (Nadab and Abihu) and unauthorized omission (Eleazar and Ithamar). Both represent failures of exact obedience, though as we will see, the motivations and contexts are strikingly different.

verses.kjv_text: Wherefore have ye not eaten the sin offering in the holy place, seeing it is most holy, and God hath given it you to bear the iniquity of the congregation, to make atonement for them before the LORD?
verses.original_text: מַדּוּעַ לֹא אֲכַלְתֶּם אֶת הַחַטָּאת בִּמְקוֹם הַקֹּדֶשׁ כִּי קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הִוא וְאֹתָהּ נָתַן לָכֶם לָשֵׂאת אֶת עֲוֹן הָעֵדָה לְכַפֵּר עֲלֵיהֶם לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Madduah lo akhaltem et-hachatta't bimmakom hakkodesh ki kodesh kodashim hi ve'otah natan lakhem lase'et et avon ha'edah lekhapper alehem lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: Moses's rebuke reveals the profound theology behind the priestly eating of the sin offering. This is one of the most theologically dense verses in Leviticus. The priest eating the sin offering was not merely receiving his appointed portion but was performing a sacrificial act: he "bears the iniquity of the congregation" (lase'et et avon ha'edah) through the act of eating. The sin-laden offering, absorbed into the priest's body, represented the transference and bearing of the people's guilt. The eating was an essential component of the atonement mechanism, not a peripheral benefit. The verb nasa (lase'et — to bear, carry, lift) carries enormous theological weight throughout the Old Testament — it is the word for bearing guilt, bearing sin, forgiving sin. By burning the goat instead of eating it, the priests interrupted this atonement process for the congregation on the very day the tabernacle was inaugurated.
verses.doctrinal_note: Verse 17 contains one of the most profound Christological anticipations in Leviticus. The priest literally "bears the iniquity" (lase'et avon) through eating the sin offering — an act of vicarious absorption. Isaiah 53:4 uses the identical construction: "he hath borne (nasa) our griefs"; 53:12 — "he bare (nasa) the sin of many." The Levitical priest eating the sin offering is a type of Christ, our great High Priest, who not only officiated at the sacrifice but was Himself the sacrifice, and who bears our iniquities into His own person. Second Corinthians 5:21 states this explicitly: "he hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin." The priest's eating pointed forward across centuries to the ultimate Bearer of sin.

verses.kjv_text: Behold, the blood of it was not brought in within the holy place: ye should indeed have eaten it in the holy place, as I commanded.
verses.original_text: הֵן לֹא הוּבָא אֶת דָּמָהּ אֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ פְּנִימָה אָכוֹל תֹּאכְלוּ אֹתָהּ בַּקֹּדֶשׁ כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוֵּיתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Hen lo huva et-damah el-hakkodesh penimah akhol tokhelu otah bakkodesh ka'asher tzivveiti.
verses.study_note: Moses clarifies the specific legal principle at stake. Leviticus 6:30 specified that when the blood of a sin offering was brought "into the tent of meeting" (the inner sanctuary) — applied to the altar of incense or used to sprinkle before the veil — the offering's flesh could NOT be eaten; it had to be burned completely. But in this case, the blood had NOT been brought inside the tent — it was applied only to the outer altar of burnt offering. Therefore, according to Lev 6:26, the flesh absolutely should have been eaten. Moses employs the infinitive absolute for emphasis: "akhol tokhelu otah" — "eating you shall eat it" — the most emphatic possible form of obligation. This technical distinction — outer court blood (eat it) vs. inner sanctuary blood (burn it) — is a critical Levitical principle later developed in Hebrews 13:10-13 for understanding Christ's offering outside the gate.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron said unto Moses, Behold, this day have they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering before the LORD; and such things have befallen me: and if I had eaten the sin offering to day, should it have been accepted in the sight of the LORD?
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר אַהֲרֹן אֶל מֹשֶׁה הֵן הַיּוֹם הִקְרִיבוּ אֶת חַטָּאתָם וְאֶת עֹלָתָם לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וַתִּקְרֶאנָה אֹתִי כָּאֵלֶּה וְאָכַלְתִּי חַטָּאת הַיּוֹם הַיִּיטַב בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber Aharon el-Mosheh hen hayyom hikkrivu et-chattotam ve'et ollatam lifnei YHWH vattikrenah oti ka'elleh ve'akhalti chattat hayyom hayyitav be'einei YHWH.
