verses.study_note: Aaron's response is among the most extraordinary speeches in the entire Torah. Having been struck to silence in v.3, Aaron now speaks — and his words constitute a profound theological argument. He reasons: "such things have befallen me" (vattikrenah oti ka'elleh — literally "these things met me, encountered me") — his sons were destroyed by divine fire on this very day. Aaron's question is penetrating: on a day when he himself was the object of divine judicial action, when his sons were struck down in the sanctuary, could his eating of the sin offering that "bears the iniquity" of the congregation have been a genuine, spiritually true act — or would it have been a hollow, mechanical performance from a shattered man? Aaron perceives that the priestly eating of the sin offering was not a mechanical rite but a spiritually charged act requiring genuine inner participation. He is asking Moses: would God have truly accepted a traumatized, grief-destroyed priest's bearing of the congregation's iniquity as valid atonement?
verses.doctrinal_note: Aaron's defense reveals one of the most profound tensions in biblical theology: the relationship between formal compliance with law and genuine spiritual engagement in worship. The sin offering's consumption was not a mechanical act but a spiritually laden one — the priest bore the congregation's iniquity through it. Aaron's grief-destroyed inner state raised a legitimate theological question about the spiritual validity of the act. This anticipates Romans 12:1 ("a living sacrifice... which is your reasonable service") — worship is not merely external performance but an inner reality. It also anticipates the New Testament's treatment of the Lord's Supper: Paul condemns eating "unworthily" (1 Cor 11:27-29) because the inner spiritual condition determines whether the external act is valid or a further offense. Christ, our High Priest, bore the sins of the world while perfectly whole in His person — the antitype succeeds where the type could only approximate.

verses.kjv_text: And when Moses heard that, he was content.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁמַע מֹשֶׁה וַיִּיטַב בְּעֵינָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishma Mosheh vayyitav be'einav.
verses.study_note: The chapter ends with a single, beautifully terse sentence: "Moses heard and it was good in his eyes." Moses accepts Aaron's argument in full. This is one of the most humanly moving moments in Leviticus — Moses, who had been justifiably angry (v.16), listens to Aaron's theological reasoning and accepts it as valid. The Hebrew is exquisitely economical: no further explanation, no debate, no compromise formula — simply "it was good in his eyes." The same root (yatav) that Aaron used in his question ("would it have been accepted/good?") now appears in Moses's response ("it was good in his eyes") — a deliberate literary echo forming a bracket around vv.17-20: Aaron asked whether it would have been yatav, and Moses answers that Aaron's reasoning is yatav. This resolution demonstrates that Moses understood not only the letter but the spirit of the Torah he mediated. It also shows that the Torah's application requires wisdom, not merely mechanical compliance.
verses.doctrinal_note: The resolution of Leviticus 10 in Moses's gracious acceptance of Aaron's reasoning is one of Scripture's most profound statements about the nature of law and its application. The same Torah that insists on precise compliance (vv.1-2) makes room for genuine spiritual assessment when circumstances are extraordinary (vv.19-20). This is not antinomianism or relativism but principled theological discernment — exactly what v.10 identified as the priest's defining task. Second Corinthians 3:6 captures the principle: "the letter killeth but the spirit giveth life." Moses here anticipates Christ's own hermeneutic: "the sabbath was made for man, not man for the sabbath" (Mark 2:27). Law serves life; it does not crush it. The chapter that opened with divine fire against unauthorized worship closes with a shepherd's wisdom accepting a subordinate's genuine theological reasoning — a powerful pairing that captures the full complexity of life lived before a holy God.

meta.overview: Leviticus 10 is one of the most dramatic and theologically charged chapters in the entire Pentateuch. It opens with sudden, divine judgment: Nadab and Abihu, two of Aaron's four sons freshly ordained as priests, offer "strange fire" before the LORD — fire that was unauthorized, not prescribed by divine command — and are immediately consumed by fire from God's presence. The event occurs on the very day the tabernacle worship was inaugurated (Lev 9), when God's glory had just filled the sanctuary and His fire had consumed the offering. Within hours of this theophanic glory, priestly presumption meets divine holiness with fatal consequence. Moses's response to the stricken Aaron (v.3) is not pastoral comfort but theological instruction: "I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me." Aaron's silence before this word is one of the most profound moments of submission in Scripture. The chapter then moves through several pastoral and liturgical aftershocks: the removal of the bodies, the prohibition of priestly mourning, and — crucially — a direct divine command to Aaron forbidding wine and strong drink before entering the sanctuary (vv.8-11), widely understood as implying that intoxication may have contributed to the brothers' fatal act. The second half of the chapter details proper procedures for consuming the offerings, culminating in a confrontation between Moses and Aaron's surviving sons over the sin offering goat. Aaron's remarkable theological defense of his sons (v.19) and Moses's acceptance of it (v.20) demonstrate that obedience to Torah requires both exact precision and genuine spiritual discernment. Leviticus 10 establishes foundational principles that reverberate throughout both Testaments: the regulative principle of worship (only what God commands is acceptable), the consuming holiness of God (Heb 12:29), the priestly calling to discern between holy and common (v.10), and the typological significance of priestly bearing of iniquity (v.17 → Isa 53:4,12). This chapter demands that every reader answer the question: on whose terms do we approach God?
meta.theme: The Holiness of God and the Danger of Unauthorized Worship
meta.keyWordMeaning: Strange/foreign/unauthorized fire — the defining phrase of the chapter. Zarah (H2114) means foreign, strange, excluded, unauthorized — it describes that which does not belong, whether foreign gods, illicit sexuality, or, here, worship performed outside God's prescribed parameters. The fire was "strange" not necessarily in its physical nature but in its unauthorized origin and unsanctioned use. The phrase embodies the principle that acceptability before God is defined by divine prescription, not human sincerity or creativity.
outline: The Death of Nadab and Abihu (vv.1-2): Strange fire and divine judgment
Moses Instructs Aaron: God Must Be Sanctified (v.3): Aaron's Silence
Removal of the Bodies and Prohibition of Mourning (vv.4-7): Priestly Duty Above Personal Grief
The Wine Prohibition and Its Purpose (vv.8-11): Discernment Required for Holy Service
Instructions for Consuming the Offerings (vv.12-15): The Continuing Worship
The Sin Offering Dispute (vv.16-20): Moses's Anger and Aaron's Defense

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses and to Aaron, saying unto them,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל־אַהֲרֹן לֵאמֹר אֲלֵהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh ve'el-Aharon lemor alehem.
verses.study_note: This verse opens with a distinctive double address — God speaks to both Moses and Aaron together, a formula that appears only here and in chapters 13–15 in Leviticus. The inclusion of Aaron signals that the dietary laws have priestly dimensions even as they govern the entire covenant community. The phrase 'saying unto them' (lemor alehem) indicates these instructions are to be communicated directly to all Israel, not merely retained within the priesthood. This dual address marks the transition from laws governing the sanctuary to laws governing the household of every Israelite.
verses.doctrinal_note: The joint address to Moses and Aaron reflects the complementary roles of prophetic mediation and priestly application. Moses receives the word; Aaron administers its priestly implications. This pattern anticipates the NT distinction between the proclamation of the Word and its pastoral application in the life of the congregation. Every household in Israel is now drawn into the orbit of holiness previously associated primarily with the tabernacle.

verses.kjv_text: Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, These are the beasts which ye shall eat among all the beasts that are on the earth.
verses.original_text: דַּבְּרוּ אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר זֹאת הַחַיָּה אֲשֶׁר תֹּאכְלוּ מִכָּל־הַבְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר עַל־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Dabberu el-benei Yisra'el lemor zot hachayah asher tochelu mikkol-habehemah asher al-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The laws are addressed to 'the children of Israel' — the entire covenant community, not merely the priesthood. Two distinct terms are used for animals: chayah (living creatures, wild animals) and behemah (domesticated livestock). The distinction anticipates the criteria that follow. The phrase 'on the earth' (al-ha'aretz) echoes the creational language of Genesis 1, where God created living creatures 'according to their kinds' upon the earth — the dietary laws are thus read against a creational backdrop, not merely a pragmatic one.
verses.doctrinal_note: The specification of which beasts Israel may eat implies a theology of creational stewardship. Not all of God's creation is available for human consumption — boundaries are built into the covenant that reflect the order and holiness of God himself. The dietary laws encode the principle that the redeemed community does not simply live as the nations live; their most basic biological acts are consecrated to God.

verses.kjv_text: Whatsoever parteth the hoof, and is clovenfooted, and cheweth the cud, among the beasts, that shall ye eat.
verses.original_text: כֹּל מַפְרֶסֶת פַּרְסָה וְשֹׁסַעַת שֶׁסַע פְּרָסֹת מַעֲלַת גֵּרָה בַּבְּהֵמָה אֹתָהּ תֹּאכֵלוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Kol mafresset parsah veshosa'at shesa' perasot ma'alat gerah babehemah otah tochelu.
verses.study_note: Two simultaneous criteria define a clean land animal: split hooves (mafresset parsah) and cud-chewing (ma'alat gerah). Both conditions must be present. The split hoof criterion is stated with impressive precision using two Hebrew words for the same idea — parsah (hoof) and shesa' (cleft) — emphasizing the completeness of the division. Cud-chewing (ma'alah gerah) involves the regurgitation and re-chewing of partially digested food. Historically, early Jewish interpreters saw symbolic meaning: the split hoof represents discrimination in one's walk, and cud-chewing represents meditation on God's Word — applying it first to oneself, then to others.
verses.doctrinal_note: The dual requirement of split hoof and cud-chewing became a powerful metaphor in Jewish and early Christian interpretation. Barnabas (c. 100 AD) and Origen saw the cud-chewing as representing meditation on Scripture and the split hoof as discernment in daily conduct. While allegorical reading must not override the literal meaning, the structure of the criteria suggests that holiness involves both a discerning mind (meditation) and a distinctive walk (conduct) — themes deeply embedded in NT discipleship (Psalm 1:2; Joshua 1:8; Philippians 4:8).

verses.kjv_text: Nevertheless these shall ye not eat of them that chew the cud, or of them that divide the hoof: as the camel, because he cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof; he is unclean unto you.
verses.original_text: אַךְ אֶת־זֶה לֹא תֹאכְלוּ מִמַּעֲלֵי הַגֵּרָה וּמִמַּפְרִיסֵי הַפַּרְסָה אֶת־הַגָּמָל כִּי־מַעֲלֵה גֵרָה הוּא וּפַרְסָה אֵינֶנּוּ מַפְרִיס טָמֵא הוּא לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Akh et-zeh lo tochelu mimma'alei hagerah umimmapreisei haparsah et-hagamal ki-ma'aleh gerah hu ufarsah einennu mafris tame hu lakhem.
verses.study_note: The particle akh (nevertheless/however) signals the critical exceptions. The camel was the primary long-distance transport animal of the ancient Near East, economically significant to the Israelites and their neighbors. It is a true ruminant (chews cud) but has padded feet rather than split hooves. Its uncleanness despite one qualifying criterion illustrates the absolute nature of both requirements. The Deuteronomy 14 parallel confirms this list exactly. The camel's designation as tame (unclean) would have been countercultural in a camel-dependent economy.

verses.kjv_text: And the coney, because he cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof; he is unclean unto you.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הַשָּׁפָן כִּי־מַעֲלֵה גֵרָה הוּא וּפַרְסָה לֹא יַפְרִיס טָמֵא הוּא לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-hashafan ki-ma'aleh gerah hu ufarsah lo yafris tame hu lakhem.
verses.study_note: The shaphan (coney/rock hyrax) appears to chew its cud due to the constant movement of its jaws — a digestive behavior called caecotrophy or cecal fermentation — but it does not have split hooves. The rock hyrax (Procavia capensis) is a small mammal native to Israel, often seen in rocky terrain (Psalm 104:18; Proverbs 30:26). This creature has claws, not hooves at all. Its exclusion despite the apparent cud-chewing behavior demonstrates the precision of the criteria.

verses.kjv_text: And the hare, because he cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof; he is unclean unto you.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הָאַרְנֶבֶת כִּי־מַעֲלַת גֵּרָה הִוא וּפַרְסָה לֹא הִפְרִיסָה טְמֵאָה הִוא לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-ha'arnevet ki-ma'alat gerah hi ufarsah lo hifrissah teme'ah hi lakhem.
verses.study_note: The arnevet (hare/rabbit) similarly moves its jaw continuously in a way resembling cud-chewing and practices caecotrophy (re-ingestion of certain droppings) but does not have split hooves. The grammatical gender shifts here — teme'ah (feminine form of unclean) matches the feminine noun arnevet. This precision in Hebrew grammar demonstrates the careful composition of the text. The hare was abundant in Canaan and would have been a tempting food source, making its prohibition practically significant.

verses.kjv_text: And the swine, though he divide the hoof, and be clovenfooted, yet he cheweth not the cud; he is unclean to you.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הַחֲזִיר כִּי־מַפְרִיס פַּרְסָה הוּא וְשֹׁסַע שֶׁסַע פַּרְסָה וְהוּא גֵּרָה לֹא־יִגָּר טָמֵא הוּא לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-hachaziyr ki-mafris parsah hu veshosa' shesa' parsah vehu gerah lo-yiggar tame hu lakhem.
verses.study_note: The pig is singled out with particular emphasis: it possesses the visible criterion (the split hoof is described with two Hebrew terms for emphasis — mafris parsah and shosa' shesa') but lacks the internal criterion of cud-chewing. This emphasis may reflect the pig's unique status as a symbol of defilement in the biblical world. The pig became, in later Second Temple and Rabbinic thought, the paradigmatic unclean animal — associated with Antiochus Epiphanes' desecration of the Temple altar (1 Macc 1:47) and with Gentile ways. Isaiah 65:4 and 66:17 associate pig-eating with apostasy.
verses.doctrinal_note: The pig's prohibition carries extraordinary theological freight in the biblical canon. It has visible qualification but internal disqualification — a possible type of false religion that appears outwardly acceptable but lacks the inward reality. The Maccan crisis of 167 BC turned pig-sacrifice into the central act of Jewish apostasy, making this prohibition inseparable from covenant faithfulness. Jesus' sending of demons into a herd of swine (Mark 5:11-13) in Gentile territory may carry symbolic significance — unclean spirits into unclean animals. Peter's vision in Acts 10 that declared all animals clean (including the pig) was the dramatic theological reversal that opened the door to Gentile mission.

verses.kjv_text: Of their flesh shall ye not eat, and their carcase shall ye not touch; they are unclean to you.
verses.original_text: מִבְּשָׂרָם לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ וּבְנִבְלָתָם לֹא תִגָּעוּ טְמֵאִים הֵם לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Mibbsaram lo tochelu uvenivellatam lo tigga'u teme'im hem lakhem.
verses.study_note: The prohibition extends beyond eating to touching the carcass of an unclean animal. The word nivlah (carcass) refers to the body of an animal that has died of itself or been killed without proper slaughter. Contact with nivlah causes defilement until evening (cf. v.24). This double prohibition — no eating, no touching — creates a total separation from the unclean. The word teme'im is plural, indicating all unclean animals named in the preceding verses are covered. This rule would have been tested daily in agricultural and pastoral life.
verses.doctrinal_note: The extension of prohibition from eating to touching illustrates how holiness penetrates every dimension of covenant life. The defilement transmitted by a carcass without any volitional act by the person — merely by contact — teaches that impurity is objective, not merely intentional. This contrasts with later Protestant emphasis on the moral will alone. The NT equivalent is the principle that Christians are to be in the world without being contaminated by it (John 17:15-16; 2 Corinthians 6:17), and that the body as the temple of the Spirit must be kept holy (1 Corinthians 6:19-20).

verses.kjv_text: These shall ye eat of all that are in the waters: whatsoever hath fins and scales in the waters, in the seas, and in the rivers, them shall ye eat.
verses.original_text: אֶת־זֶה תֹּאכְלוּ מִכֹּל אֲשֶׁר בַּמָּיִם כֹּל אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ סְנַפִּיר וְקַשְׂקֶשֶׂת בַּמַּיִם בַּיַּמִּים וּבַנְּחָלִים אֹתָם תֹּאכֵלוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Et-zeh tochelu mikkol asher bamayim kol asher-lo snappir veqasqeset bamayim bayyammim uvannehalim otam tochelu.
verses.study_note: The transition to aquatic creatures introduces two parallel criteria: fins (snappir) and scales (qasqeset). Both must be present. The text carefully covers all water environments: the seas (yammim) and the rivers (nehalim), ensuring comprehensive scope. Fish with both fins and scales are universally identifiable in the ancient Near East — salmon, tuna, carp, and most commercially significant fish qualify. The criterion elegantly excludes shellfish, eels, sharks, and most bottom-feeders. The criteria may reflect aquatic equivalents of proper order: scales as a covering (like proper clothing = order) and fins as purposeful locomotion (as opposed to serpentine movement).
verses.doctrinal_note: The aquatic dietary laws follow the same binary structure as the land animal laws — two required criteria. This structural consistency reveals the underlying principle: God's holiness categories have a coherent logic that runs through creation. The restriction to fish with fins and scales may carry typological significance: in the NT, the fish becomes a symbol of the gospel mission (Matthew 4:19; John 21:6-11), and the great catch of fish in John 21 contains all types without distinction — perhaps anticipating the eschatological ingathering beyond the dietary boundaries.

verses.kjv_text: And all that have not fins and scales in the seas, and in the rivers, of all that move in the waters, and of any living thing which is in the waters, they shall be an abomination unto you:
verses.original_text: וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר אֵין־לוֹ סְנַפִּיר וְקַשְׂקֶשֶׂת בַּיַּמִּים וּבַנְּחָלִים מִכֹּל שֶׁרֶץ הַמַּיִם וּמִכֹּל נֶפֶשׁ הַחַיָּה אֲשֶׁר בַּמָּיִם שֶׁקֶץ הֵם לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol asher ein-lo snappir veqasqeset bayyammim uvannehalim mikkol sheretz hamayim umikkol nefesh hachayah asher bamayim sheqetz hem lakhem.
verses.study_note: The word sheqetz (abomination) appears here for the first time in this chapter — a term stronger than tame (unclean). Sheqetz describes something utterly detestable, to be avoided with revulsion. The category covers all swarming aquatic creatures (sheretz hamayim) and any living soul (nefesh chayah) in the water that lacks fins and scales — including shellfish, crustaceans, eels, sharks, rays, and all sea mammals. The elevation from 'unclean' to 'abomination' marks these creatures as carrying a heightened level of covenant danger.
verses.doctrinal_note: The term sheqetz (abomination) represents a heightened category of defilement beyond mere tame (unclean). The gradation of defilement language in Leviticus 11 is theologically significant: some things are simply prohibited (tahor/tame binary), while others are designated as sheqetz — an object of visceral aversion. This mirrors the NT distinction between things that are merely unwise and things that are deeply corrupting to the soul. The principle that not all violations carry equal weight is built into the structure of the dietary laws.

verses.kjv_text: They shall be even an abomination unto you; ye shall not eat of their flesh, but ye shall have their carcases in abomination.
verses.original_text: וְשֶׁקֶץ יִהְיוּ לָכֶם מִבְּשָׂרָם לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ וְאֶת־נִבְלָתָם תְּשַׁקֵּצוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vesheqetz yihyu lakhem mibbsaram lo tochelu ve'et-nivellatam teshaqetzu.
verses.study_note: The verse repeats sheqetz twice for emphasis and introduces the verb form teshaqetzu — you shall regard them as abomination. The dietary laws are not merely passive prohibitions but call for an active, cultivated attitude of revulsion. The Israelite is to internalize the law until the prohibited animal produces genuine aversion. This is consistent with the broader Levitical principle that holiness is not merely behavioral compliance but a transformation of desire and affection. The repetition of sheqetz/teshaqetzu frames this as a matter of deep covenantal formation.

verses.kjv_text: Whatsoever hath no fins nor scales in the waters, that shall be an abomination unto you.
verses.original_text: כֹּל אֲשֶׁר אֵין־לוֹ סְנַפִּיר וְקַשְׂקֶשֶׂת בַּמַּיִם שֶׁקֶץ הוּא לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Kol asher ein-lo snappir veqasqeset bamayim sheqetz hu lakhem.
verses.study_note: This verse functions as a summarizing inclusion (an inclusio) for the aquatic section begun in verse 9, closing with the same two criteria (fins and scales) and the same verdict (sheqetz). The brevity and clarity of the closing summary demonstrate the literary structure: positive permission (v.9), extended prohibition (vv.10-11), summarizing closure (v.12). The structural elegance reflects the careful compositional design of Leviticus. The inclusio technique is a common Hebrew literary device that brackets a unit of material.

verses.kjv_text: And these are they which ye shall have in abomination among the fowls; they shall not be eaten, they are an abomination: the eagle, and the ossifrage, and the ospray,
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־אֵלֶּה תְּשַׁקְּצוּ מִן־הָעוֹף לֹא יֵאָכְלוּ שֶׁקֶץ הֵם אֶת־הַנֶּשֶׁר וְאֶת־הַפֶּרֶס וְאֶת־הָעָזְנִיָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-elleh teshaqetzu min-ha'of lo ye'achelu sheqetz hem et-hannesher ve'et-haperes ve'et-ha'azniiyyah.
verses.study_note: The birds section introduces a different legislative approach — no general criteria are given (unlike the two-criteria system for land and water animals). Instead, a list of twenty prohibited species is enumerated. The positive rule is implied: all birds not on the prohibited list are permitted. The nesher (eagle or griffon vulture) leads the list — the most majestic and dominant of predatory birds. The peres is likely the bearded vulture (Gypaetus barbatus) and the ozniyyah possibly the short-toed eagle or osprey. The lack of explicit criteria may be because the forbidden birds share observable characteristics: all are birds of prey or carrion-feeders.
verses.doctrinal_note: The prohibited birds are overwhelmingly raptors and carrion-feeders — birds associated with death, predation, and scavenging. The clean/unclean avian distinction may reflect a principle of separation from the realm of death and violent predation. This connects to the broader Levitical theme that Israel is to be a community of life, not death. The eagle (nesher), though used positively as a symbol of God's care (Exod 19:4; Deut 32:11), is nevertheless prohibited for consumption — a reminder that symbolic dignity and dietary status are distinct categories.

verses.kjv_text: And the vulture, and the kite after his kind;
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הַדָּאָה וְאֶת־הָאַיָּה לְמִינָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-hada'ah ve'et-ha'ayyah leminah.
verses.study_note: The da'ah (black kite or red kite, Milvus migrans/milvus) and the ayyah (kite or falcon species) are both birds of prey. The phrase leminah (after its kind) echoes Genesis 1 and indicates that the entire species group is included in the prohibition — not merely the specific named bird but all closely related species. This phrase appears several times in the bird prohibition list, providing a comprehensive exclusion of related raptor families. The kite was common in ancient Israel and its prohibition would have been recognizable in daily life.

verses.kjv_text: Every raven after his kind;
verses.original_text: אֵת כָּל־עֹרֵב לְמִינוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Et kol-orev lemino.
verses.study_note: The orev (raven) family (Corvus spp.) is prohibited in its entirety — all varieties of ravens and crows. The raven appears memorably in the biblical narrative as the first bird released from Noah's ark (Gen 8:7) and as the bird that fed Elijah (1 Kings 17:4-6). Its prohibition for consumption despite these positive roles demonstrates again that symbolic dignity and dietary status are separate. Ravens are omnivores and scavengers, associating them with the death-realm that the dietary laws consistently exclude from Israel's table.

verses.kjv_text: And the owl, and the night hawk, and the cuckow, and the hawk after his kind,
verses.original_text: וְאֵת בַּת הַיַּעֲנָה וְאֶת־הַתַּחְמָס וְאֶת־הַשָּׁחַף וְאֶת־הַנֵּץ לְמִינֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et bat haya'anah ve'et-hattachmas ve'et-hashshachaf ve'et-hannetz lemineihu.
verses.study_note: The bat haya'anah (literally 'daughter of the desert/ostrich' — likely the great owl or ostrich), the tachmas (possibly the nighthawk or swift), the shachaf (cuckoo or sea gull), and the netz (hawk, all species) continue the prohibited list. The identification of several of these birds is uncertain in modern ornithology due to the ambiguity of ancient Hebrew bird names. The netz (hawk) again receives the 'after its kind' formula, excluding all hawk species. These birds combine predatory behavior, night activity (associated with death and chaos), and scavenging.

verses.kjv_text: And the little owl, and the cormorant, and the great owl,
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הַכּוֹס וְאֶת־הַשָּׁלָךְ וְאֶת־הַיַּנְשׁוּף׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-hakkos ve'et-hashshalakh ve'et-hayanshuph.
verses.study_note: The kos (little owl, Athene noctua — common in Palestine), the shalakh (pelican or cormorant, a water bird that dives for fish), and the yanshuph (great owl or barn owl) continue the enumeration. The presence of the pelican/cormorant alongside owls shows that the prohibition covers diverse avian types: nocturnal hunters, deep-water fishing birds, and carrion birds all appear in the same list. The owl (kos) is associated in the Psalms with desolation and loneliness (Psalm 102:6), reinforcing its association with the wilderness and death.

verses.kjv_text: And the swan, and the pelican, and the gier eagle,
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הַתִּנְשֶׁמֶת וְאֶת־הַקָּאָת וְאֶת־הָרָחָם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-hatinshemet ve'et-haqqaat ve'et-haracham.
verses.study_note: The tinshemet (possibly the white owl or swan — the same word appears in verse 30 for a lizard, illustrating the ambiguity of some identifications), the qa'at (pelican or owl), and the racham (possibly the Egyptian vulture or carrion crow) continue the prohibited list. The Egyptian vulture (Neophron percnopterus) was actually a sacred bird in Egyptian religion, associated with the goddess Nekhbet. Its prohibition reinforces the separation of Israel from Egyptian religious culture and its associated animals.

verses.kjv_text: And the stork, the heron after her kind, and the lapwing, and the bat.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת הַחֲסִידָה הָאֲנָפָה לְמִינָהּ וְאֶת־הַדּוּכִיפַת וְאֶת־הָעֲטַלֵּף׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et hachasidah ha'anafah leminah ve'et-haddukhifat ve'et-ha'atalef.
verses.study_note: The chasidah (stork) is particularly striking — its Hebrew name derives from chesed (lovingkindness), as the stork was observed caring faithfully for its young. Jeremiah 8:7 praises the stork for knowing 'her appointed times.' Yet this virtuous bird is prohibited for consumption. The anafah (heron, all species), the dukhifat (hoopoe — Upupa epops), and the atalef (bat) complete the avian list. The inclusion of the bat here (a mammal, not a bird in modern taxonomy) reflects the ancient classification system: any flying creature is categorized as 'bird' (of). Bats are cave-dwellers associated with darkness and the underworld across ancient Near Eastern cultures.

verses.kjv_text: All fowls that creep, going upon all four, shall be an abomination unto you.
verses.original_text: כֹּל שֶׁרֶץ הָעוֹף הַהֹלֵךְ עַל־אַרְבַּע שֶׁקֶץ הוּא לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Kol sheretz ha'of haholekh al-arba' sheqetz hu lakhem.
verses.study_note: This verse opens the insect/flying creature section. 'Fowls that creep, going upon all four' is a fascinating phrase — it describes insects that fly but also walk on legs. The 'four' here likely means 'more than two' in the ancient idiom, referring to six-legged insects. These flying-creeping creatures occupy a mixed category — they are neither pure flyers nor pure walkers — which in the Levitical taxonomy of order makes them anomalous and therefore suspect. Things that blur categorical boundaries tend to be classified as unclean in Levitical thinking.
verses.doctrinal_note: The prohibition of boundary-blurring creatures reflects the Levitical theology of order. Created categories (flying, walking, swimming) are to be kept distinct, and creatures that cross boundaries disrupt the created order. This principle has a direct typological dimension: in the NT, spiritual impurity is often associated with the mixing of sacred and profane (2 Corinthians 6:14-16; Revelation 3:16). The holy life is characterized by clarity of category, not confusion of realms — a principle the incarnation itself upholds (the eternal Word became fully human without confusion or mixing of natures).

verses.kjv_text: Yet these may ye eat of every flying creeping thing that goeth upon all four, which have legs above their feet, to leap withal upon the earth;
verses.original_text: אַךְ אֶת־זֶה תֹּאכְלוּ מִכֹּל שֶׁרֶץ הָעוֹף הַהֹלֵךְ עַל־אַרְבַּע אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ כְרָעַיִם מִמַּעַל לְרַגְלָיו לְנַתֵּר בָּהֵן עַל־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Akh et-zeh tochelu mikkol sheretz ha'of haholekh al-arba' asher-lo khra'ayim mimma'al leraglav lenatter bahen al-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The particle akh (nevertheless) introduces a remarkable exception to the flying insect prohibition. Insects with jointed legs for leaping (khra'ayim — literally 'thighs above their feet') are permitted. This refers to the Orthoptera order: locusts, crickets, and grasshoppers. These insects use explosive hind legs for jumping rather than mere crawling. Their locomotion is specialized and purposeful rather than the swarming crawl of unclean insects. The leaping mechanism (nattar) may symbolically represent a kind of purposeful upward movement rather than earthly swarming.
verses.doctrinal_note: This exception is among the most practically significant in the chapter: locusts, crickets, and grasshoppers were genuine food sources in the ancient Near East, high in protein, and the exception graciously permits their consumption. John the Baptist's diet of 'locusts and wild honey' (Matthew 3:4; Mark 1:6) was entirely consistent with Levitical law — he was not living in ascetic defiance of Torah but in faithful observance of it while dwelling in the wilderness. This detail authenticates the Baptist's Jewish identity and his law-observant life as the forerunner of Christ.

verses.kjv_text: Even these of them ye may eat; the locust after his kind, and the bald locust after his kind, and the beetle after his kind, and the grasshopper after his kind.
verses.original_text: אֶת־אֵלֶּה מֵהֶם תֹּאכֵלוּ אֶת־הָאַרְבֶּה לְמִינוֹ וְאֶת־הַסָּלְעָם לְמִינֵהוּ וְאֶת־הַחַרְגֹּל לְמִינֵהוּ וְאֶת־הֶחָגָב לְמִינֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Et-elleh mehem tochelu et-ha'arbeh lemino ve'et-hassa'lam lemineihu ve'et-hachargol lemineihu ve'et-hechagav lemineihu.
verses.study_note: Four specific genera of permitted jumping insects are named, each with the 'after its kind' expansion that permits all species within the genus. The arbeh (locust, Schistocerca gregaria — the migratory locust), the sal'am (bald locust or cricket), the chargol (cricket or katydid), and the chagav (grasshopper) are all Orthoptera. These insects represent the 'clean exception' within an otherwise prohibited insect category — the one pocket of permission within a sea of prohibition. The arbeh is the plague locust of Exodus 10, yet here it appears as a permitted food — an unexpected redemption of the plague instrument.

verses.kjv_text: But all other flying creeping things, which have four feet, shall be an abomination unto you.
verses.original_text: וְכֹל שֶׁרֶץ הָעוֹף אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ אַרְבַּע רַגְלָיִם שֶׁקֶץ הוּא לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol sheretz ha'of asher-lo arba' raglayyim sheqetz hu lakhem.
verses.study_note: This verse closes the flying insect section with a summary prohibition, functioning as an inclusio with verse 20. All flying insects that walk on four legs (i.e., do not have the specialized leaping legs of verse 21) are abominations. This covers beetles, flies, wasps, bees (interestingly), ants, and most other insects. The honey produced by bees, however, was entirely permitted (Leviticus 2:11 prohibits honey in grain offerings but not its consumption). This demonstrates that the product of an unclean creature can be clean — a principle that opens interesting avenues in both practical halakha and typological reflection.

verses.kjv_text: And for these ye shall be unclean: whosoever toucheth the carcase of them shall be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וּלְאֵלֶּה תִּטַּמָּאוּ כָּל־הַנֹּגֵעַ בְּנִבְלָתָם יִטְמָא עַד־הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Ule'elleh titamma'u kol-hanogea' benivellatam yitma' ad-ha'arev.
verses.study_note: The chapter now shifts from dietary prohibition to ritual defilement through contact. The key phrase is ad-ha'arev (until the evening) — the standard duration of minor defilement. This is not permanent impurity but a time-limited state that is resolved at sundown. The daily rhythm of Israel was structured around these cycles of cleanness, defilement, and restoration — every day potentially involved encounters with the dead or unclean that required the evening's resolution. This teaches a theology of regular, ongoing purification rather than a single once-for-all cleansing (though that too awaits in the sacrificial system).
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'until evening' purification cycle encodes an important theological rhythm: defilement is real, immediate, and disqualifying, but it is also temporary and resolvable. This prevents two errors simultaneously — it takes impurity seriously (unlike cheap grace that minimizes sin) while refusing to make it permanent (unlike legalism that has no mechanism for restoration). The NT equivalent is the daily cleansing through confession described in 1 John 1:9 — the believer who sins does not lose salvation but requires daily cleansing to maintain fellowship.

verses.kjv_text: And whosoever beareth ought of the carcase of them shall wash his clothes, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְכָל־הַנֹּשֵׂא מִנִּבְלָתָם יְכַבֵּס בְּגָדָיו וְטָמֵא עַד־הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol-hannose' minnivellatam yekhabbes begadav vetame' ad-ha'arev.
verses.study_note: Carrying a carcass (as opposed to merely touching it) requires an additional act of purification — washing the clothes (yekhabbes begadav). The increasing contact (touch → carry) corresponds to increasing defilement requirements. This graduated response to defilement reflects the careful calibration of the purity system. The washing of garments symbolizes the externalization of purification — the defilement that has been transferred to the person is visibly and materially removed. This 'washing until evening' pattern appears throughout Leviticus and Numbers in various purity contexts.

verses.kjv_text: The carcases of every beast which divideth the hoof, and is not clovenfooted, nor cheweth the cud, are unclean unto you: every one that toucheth them shall be unclean.
verses.original_text: לְכָל־הַבְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר הִוא מַפְרֶסֶת פַּרְסָה וְשֶׁסַע אֵינֶנָּה שֹׁסַעַת וְגֵרָה אֵינֶנָּה מַעֲלָה טְמֵאִים הֵם לָכֶם כָּל־הַנֹּגֵעַ בָּהֶם יִטְמָא׃
verses.transliteration: Lekhol-habehemah asher hi mafresset parsah veshesaw einennah shosa'at vegeirah einennah ma'alah teme'im hem lakhem kol-hanogea' bahem yitma'.
verses.study_note: This verse returns to land animals to clarify the defilement rules for those with only one clean characteristic (partial compliance with the criteria). Even animals that have one clean characteristic but not both — like the camel, hyrax, or hare mentioned earlier — are unclean, and touching their carcasses defiles. The verse demonstrates that partial holiness is not holiness: the binary is absolute. There is no partial clean category — an animal is either fully tahor (clean) or fully tame (unclean). Ambiguous cases fall on the unclean side, not the clean side.

verses.kjv_text: And whatsoever goeth upon his paws, among all manner of beasts that go on all four, those are unclean unto you: whoso toucheth their carcase shall be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְכֹל הוֹלֵךְ עַל־כַּפָּיו בְּכָל־הַחַיָּה הַהֹלֶכֶת עַל־אַרְבַּע טְמֵאִים הֵם לָכֶם כָּל־הַנֹּגֵעַ בְּנִבְלָתָם יִטְמָא עַד־הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol holekh al-kappav bekhol-hachayah haholekhet al-arba' teme'im hem lakhem kol-hanogea' benivellatam yitma' ad-ha'arev.
verses.study_note: Animals that walk on paws (kappayim — palms of the hands/paws) rather than hooves are declared unclean. This category covers carnivorous mammals: lions, bears, dogs, cats, wolves, foxes, and all feline and canine species. The paw (kaf) is the grasping, predatory extremity — associated with hunters and killers. These are the apex predators of the animal world, and their designation as unclean separates Israel from the predatory-scavenger complex that dominates the wild food chain. Touching their carcasses defiles until evening.

verses.kjv_text: And he that beareth the carcase of them shall wash his clothes, and be unclean until the even: they are unclean unto you.
verses.original_text: וְהַנֹּשֵׂא אֶת־נִבְלָתָם יְכַבֵּס בְּגָדָיו וְטָמֵא עַד־הָעָרֶב טְמֵאִים הֵם לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehannosbe et-nivellatam yekhabbes begadav vetame' ad-ha'arev teme'im hem lakhem.
verses.study_note: This verse parallels verse 25, applying the carry/wash clothes protocol to the paw-walking animals introduced in verse 27. The repetition of the laundering requirement for carcass-carriers (as opposed to mere touching) establishes a consistent principle across the animal categories: the same graduated defilement response applies regardless of which category of unclean animal is involved. The final declaration 'they are unclean unto you' (teme'im hem lakhem) closes the paw-walker section with the covenant-specific framing — this is law for Israel in particular.

verses.kjv_text: These also shall be unclean unto you among the creeping things that creep upon the earth; the weasel, and the mouse, and the tortoise after his kind,
verses.original_text: וְזֶה לָכֶם הַטָּמֵא בַּשֶּׁרֶץ הַשֹּׁרֵץ עַל־הָאָרֶץ הַחֹלֶד וְהָעַכְבָּר וְהַצָּב לְמִינֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vezeh lakhem hattame' bashsheretz hashore'tz al-ha'aretz hacheled veha'akhbar vehatzav lemineihu.
verses.study_note: The chapter now turns to ground-swarming creatures (sheretz hashorets) — small reptiles and rodents that move along the earth in a creeping, swarming manner. Eight specific creatures are named in verses 29-30. The choled (weasel), the akhbar (mouse or rat), and the tzav (monitor lizard or tortoise, after its kind) open the list. These animals live in the ground, burrow in the soil, and eat from the earth — associated with the dust and underworld realms in ancient Near Eastern thought. Their exclusion from the clean category reflects their association with death and the subterranean.
verses.doctrinal_note: The ground-swarming creatures recall the curse of the serpent in Genesis 3:14 — cursed to go on its belly and eat dust. Creatures that live in the dust, burrow in the ground, and slink along the earth are associated in the biblical imagination with the realm of death and the consequences of the fall. Their dietary prohibition encodes a symbolic distancing from the serpent's domain. The NT equivalent is the call to set one's mind on things above, not on earthly things (Colossians 3:2) — a dietary law spiritualized into a posture of the soul.

verses.kjv_text: And the ferret, and the chameleon, and the lizard, and the snail, and the mole.
verses.original_text: וְהָאֲנָקָה וְהַכֹּחַ וְהַלְּטָאָה וְהַחֹמֶט וְהַתִּנְשָׁמֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Veha'anaqah vehakkoach vehaleta'ah vehachomet vehatinshamet.
verses.study_note: Five more swarming ground creatures complete the prohibited list: the anaqah (ferret, gecko, or hedgehog), the koach (monitor lizard — the Nile monitor, Varanus niloticus, was common in Egypt and Canaan), the leta'ah (lizard), the chomet (snail or sand lizard), and the tinshemet (chameleon or mole — notably the same word used for a bird in verse 18, revealing the semantic flexibility of some Hebrew animal terms). The monitor lizard (koach) was actually worshipped in some Egyptian cults, making its prohibition culturally significant as a rejection of Egyptian religious associations.

verses.kjv_text: These are unclean to you among all that creep: whosoever doth touch them, when they be dead, shall be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה הַטְּמֵאִים לָכֶם בְּכָל־הַשֶּׁרֶץ כָּל־הַנֹּגֵעַ בָּהֶם בְּמֹתָם יִטְמָא עַד־הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Elleh hatteme'im lakhem bekhol-hashsheretz kol-hanogea' bahem bemotam yitma' ad-ha'arev.
verses.study_note: This closing summary verse for the swarming creatures establishes the standard contact-defilement rule for this category: touching any of the eight named swarming creatures when dead (bemotam — in their death) defiles until evening. The phrase 'in their death' is significant — these creatures do not defile when living (which would make farming, travel, and daily life impossible) but when dead. The defilement-causing condition is contact with death itself, not merely with the species. This reinforces the central association: unclean animals are associated with the realm of death.

verses.kjv_text: And upon whatsoever any of them, when they are dead, doth fall, it shall be unclean; whether it be any vessel of wood, or raiment, or skin, or sack, whatsoever vessel it be, wherein any work is done, it must be put into water, and it shall be unclean until the even; so it shall be cleansed.
verses.original_text: וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר־יִפֹּל עָלָיו מֵהֶם בְּמֹתָם יִטְמָא מִכָּל כְּלִי עֵץ אוֹ בֶגֶד אוֹ עוֹר אוֹ שָׂק כָּל כְּלִי אֲשֶׁר יֵעָשֶׂה מְלָאכָה בָּהֶם בַּמַּיִם יוּבָא וְטָמֵא עַד־הָעָרֶב וְטָהֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol asher-yippol alav mehem bemotam yitma' mikkol keli etz o beged o or o saq kol keli asher ye'aseh melakhah bahem bamayim yuva' vetame' ad-ha'arev vetaher.
verses.study_note: Defilement extends beyond persons to objects that a carcass falls upon: wooden vessels, clothing (beged), leather goods (or), and sacks (saq) — all items used for work (melakhah). These must be immersed in water (bamayim yuva' — brought into water, the earliest reference to immersion/mikveh purification) and remain unclean until evening, after which they are clean (vetaher). The material progression (wood, cloth, leather, sack) covers the main categories of everyday objects. The word melakhah (work/craftsmanship) is notably the same word used for God's creative work in Genesis 2:2-3, suggesting that tools of human labor participate in the purity system.
verses.doctrinal_note: The purification of inanimate objects through immersion (yuva' bamayim) is the earliest clear reference in Torah to the practice of ritual immersion — the mikveh. This practice became central to Jewish purity life and directly informs Christian baptism. The structure is the same: immersion in water + passage of time = purification. The NT's teaching on baptism draws on this Levitical substrate: immersion in water as the outward enactment of an inward cleansing. Peter connects the Noah flood-waters to baptism (1 Peter 3:20-21) in a similar typological move from the washing of the Old Testament to the washing of the New.

verses.kjv_text: And every earthen vessel, whereinto any of them falleth, whatsoever is in it shall be unclean; and ye shall break it.
verses.original_text: וְכָל כְּלִי חֶרֶשׂ אֲשֶׁר יִפֹּל מֵהֶם אֶל־תּוֹכוֹ כֹּל אֲשֶׁר בְּתוֹכוֹ יִטְמָא וְאֹתוֹ תִשְׁבֹּרוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol keli cheres asher yippol mehem el-tokho kol asher betokho yitma' ve'oto tishboru.
verses.study_note: Earthenware vessels (keli cheres — pottery) receive unique treatment: if a swarming creature falls into a clay pot, everything inside is defiled and the pot itself must be broken (tishboru). Pottery cannot be purified by immersion (unlike wood, cloth, and leather) because its porous surface permanently absorbs the defilement. This practical distinction reflects accurate knowledge of material properties: pottery is porous and cannot be thoroughly cleansed. The shattering of the vessel is an act of radical purification. This principle later appears in Leviticus 6:28 (the clay pot that boils the sin offering must be broken) and connects to the imagery of the broken vessel in Jeremiah 18-19.

verses.kjv_text: Of all meat which may be eaten, that on which such water cometh shall be unclean: and all drink that may be drunk in every such vessel shall be unclean.
verses.original_text: מִכָּל הָאֹכֶל אֲשֶׁר יֵאָכֵל אֲשֶׁר יָבֹא עָלָיו מַיִם יִטְמָא וְכָל מַשְׁקֶה אֲשֶׁר יִשָּׁתֶה בְּכָל כְּלִי יִטְמָא׃
verses.transliteration: Mikkol ha'okhel asher ye'akhel asher yavo alav mayim yitma' vekhol mashqeh asher yishshateh bekhol keli yitma'.
verses.study_note: Water that has been contaminated by a defiled vessel contaminates food and drink. The chain of defilement extends from creature → vessel → water → food/drink. This 'defilement by sequence' principle demonstrates the thoroughness of the purity system: it tracks the transmission of impurity through material connections. The phrase 'water that may come upon it' (asher yavo alav mayim) refers to water from a defiled vessel being poured onto food — the water carries the defilement with it. This sequential defilement contrasts with cleansing water: pure water cleanses, defiled water further defiles.

verses.kjv_text: And every thing whereupon any part of their carcase falleth shall be unclean; whether it be oven, or ranges for pots, they shall be broken down: for they are unclean, and shall be unclean unto you.
verses.original_text: וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר יִפֹּל מִנִּבְלָתָם עָלָיו יִטְמָא תַּנּוּר וְכִירַיִם יֻתָּץ טְמֵאִים הֵם וּטְמֵאִים יִהְיוּ לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol asher yippol minnivellatam alav yitma' tannur vekhirayim yuttatz teme'im hem uteme'im yihyu lakhem.
verses.study_note: The oven (tannur) and cooking ranges (kirayim) — the central appliances of the ancient kitchen — are included in the category of objects that must be smashed if a carcass falls upon them. Like pottery, clay ovens and clay cooking ranges are porous and cannot be purified by immersion. Their destruction is commanded. The fact that the domestic hearth itself falls under the purity laws illustrates how thoroughly the dietary regulations penetrated every corner of Israelite household life. The kitchen, the most intimate domestic space, was not exempt from covenant holiness.

verses.kjv_text: Nevertheless a fountain or pit, wherein there is plenty of water, shall be clean: but that which toucheth their carcase shall be unclean.
verses.original_text: אַךְ מַעְיָן וּבוֹר מִקְוֵה מַיִם יִהְיֶה טָהוֹר וְנֹגֵעַ בְּנִבְלָתָם יִטְמָא׃
verses.transliteration: Akh ma'yan ubor miqveh mayim yihyeh tahor venogea' benivellatam yitma'.
verses.study_note: This verse contains a remarkable and critically important word: miqveh (מִקְוֵה) — a collection of water. This is the origin of the term mikveh used for ritual immersion baths in Jewish practice. The principle stated here is fundamental: a natural spring (ma'yan) or cistern that is a 'collection of waters' (miqveh mayim) remains clean despite a carcass falling into it. Living or collected water has inherent purifying power that is not overcome by contact defilement. The carcass defiles the person who touches it, but the water body itself remains clean. This distinction between standing water and flowing/collected water became the basis for the entire mikveh halakha.
verses.doctrinal_note: The term miqveh mayim (collection of waters) used here gives its name to the ritual immersion pool (mikveh) that became central to Jewish purification practice and the direct antecedent of Christian baptism. The principle that pure water purifies while defiled water defiles runs through both Testaments. Jesus spoke of 'living water' (John 4:10-14; 7:37-38) drawing on the ma'yan (spring, flowing water) imagery. The distinction between a spring (ma'yan) and a cistern (bor) in this verse anticipates Jeremiah 2:13: Israel has forsaken God, 'the fountain of living waters,' and hewn out for themselves broken cisterns.

verses.kjv_text: And if any part of their carcase fall upon any sowing seed which is to be sown, it shall be clean.
verses.original_text: וְכִי יִפֹּל מִנִּבְלָתָם עַל כָּל זֶרַע זֵרוּעַ אֲשֶׁר יִזָּרֵעַ טָהוֹר הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vechi yippol minnivellatam al kol zera' zerua' asher yizzare'a tahor hu.
verses.study_note: Dry seed designated for planting is not defiled by carcass contact — the seed remains clean. This is a critical agricultural concession: in ancient farming, grain was stored, handled, and inevitably came into contact with all manner of unclean things. If dry seed became permanently defiled, the entire agricultural economy of Israel would be compromised. The law's wisdom is evident: it draws the line at the point where life is about to begin. Dry seed is inert — it has not yet begun its process of germination. Once wetted (verse 38), it enters a new category.

verses.kjv_text: But if any water be put upon the seed, and any part of their carcase fall thereon, it shall be unclean unto you.
verses.original_text: וְכִי יֻתַּן מַיִם עַל זֶרַע וְנָפַל מִנִּבְלָתָם עָלָיו טָמֵא הוּא לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vechi yuttan mayim al zera' venafal minnivellatam alav tame' hu lakhem.
verses.study_note: Once water is applied to the seed (initiating the germination process), it becomes susceptible to defilement. The contrast with verse 37 is precise: dry = clean; wet = susceptible. Water activates the seed's biological processes and simultaneously activates its susceptibility to purity law. This Rabbinic principle (called hechsher — 'preparation' for defilement) became an entire tractate of Mishnaic law (Machshirin). The principle that water is the medium of both life and defilement-susceptibility reflects the dual nature of water in the biblical imagination: it gives life (Genesis 2:10) and conveys both purity and impurity.

verses.kjv_text: And if any beast, of which ye may eat, die; he that toucheth the carcase thereof shall be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְכִי יָמוּת מִן הַבְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר הִיא לָכֶם לְאָכְלָה הַנֹּגֵעַ בְּנִבְלָתָהּ יִטְמָא עַד־הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vechi yamut min habehemah asher hi lakhem le'okhlah hanogea' benivellatah yitma' ad-ha'arev.
verses.study_note: Even clean animals that die of natural causes (as opposed to proper slaughter) defile those who touch their carcasses. This is a remarkable extension: the cleanness or uncleanness of an animal is not its only relevant quality for purity purposes. How the animal died matters. Properly slaughtered clean animals are food; naturally dead clean animals are defiling. The principle of proper, sanctioned slaughter (shechitah) is embedded in this law: death in its proper form (sacrifice or slaughter) is acceptable; uncontrolled death (from disease, accident, or old age) renders even a clean creature a source of defilement.

verses.kjv_text: And he that eateth of the carcase of it shall wash his clothes, and be unclean until the even: he also that beareth the carcase of it shall wash his clothes, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְהָאֹכֵל מִנִּבְלָתָהּ יְכַבֵּס בְּגָדָיו וְטָמֵא עַד־הָעָרֶב וְהַנֹּשֵׂא אֶת נִבְלָתָהּ יְכַבֵּס בְּגָדָיו וְטָמֵא עַד־הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Veha'okhel minnivellatah yekhabbes begadav vetame' ad-ha'arev vehannosbe et nivellatah yekhabbes begadav vetame' ad-ha'arev.
verses.study_note: Both eating the carcass of a naturally dead clean animal and carrying it require laundering clothes and waiting until evening. The graduated defilement system is consistent: eating (greater contact and internalization) requires laundering clothes, just as carrying does. Both actions — taking the carcass into the body or transporting it — represent significant engagement with the realm of uncontrolled death. The laundering requirement for eating is particularly striking: the defilement goes deeper than the body and extends to the garments. The body as a whole, including its clothing, is brought under the law of holiness.

verses.kjv_text: And every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth shall be an abomination; it shall not be eaten.
verses.original_text: וְכָל הַשֶּׁרֶץ הַשֹּׁרֵץ עַל הָאָרֶץ שֶׁקֶץ הוּא לֹא יֵאָכֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol hashsheretz hashore'tz al ha'aretz sheqetz hu lo ye'akhel.
verses.study_note: This verse launches the climactic prohibition of the chapter: all swarming things (sheretz) that creep on the earth are abominations. The comprehensive 'all' (kol) combined with the cognate absolute 'the swarming thing that swarms' (hashsheretz hashore'tz) creates the most emphatic possible declaration. The creeping earth-dwellers — slugs, worms, snakes, lizards, beetles, all creatures that live in or on the ground in a swarming manner — are covered by this absolute prohibition. The earth-swarming category inverts creation order: these creatures embody the lowest stratum of existence, closest to the dust-realm.

verses.kjv_text: Whatsoever goeth upon the belly, and whatsoever goeth upon all four, or whatsoever hath more feet among all creeping things that creep upon the earth, them ye shall not eat; for they are an abomination.
verses.original_text: כֹּל הוֹלֵךְ עַל גָּחוֹן וְכֹל הוֹלֵךְ עַל אַרְבַּע עַד כָּל מַרְבֶּה רַגְלַיִם לְכָל הַשֶּׁרֶץ הַשֹּׁרֵץ עַל הָאָרֶץ לֹא תֹאכְלוּם כִּי שֶׁקֶץ הֵם׃
verses.transliteration: Kol holekh al gachon vekhol holekh al arba' ad kol marbbeh raglayyim lekhol hashsheretz hashore'tz al ha'aretz lo tokhlum ki sheqetz hem.
verses.study_note: This verse provides a comprehensive taxonomy of prohibited locomotion: going on the belly (gachon — the serpentine crawl), going on four legs, and going on many feet (the multi-legged arthropods: centipedes, millipedes, scorpions). The word gachon (belly) appears only here in all of Torah in this form — it unmistakably echoes Genesis 3:14 where the serpent was cursed to go on its belly. The dietary prohibition against belly-crawlers is a direct enactment of the curse of the fall: what God cursed becomes food forbidden for his redeemed people.
verses.doctrinal_note: The word gachon (belly/abdomen) in verse 42 directly echoes the divine curse on the serpent in Genesis 3:14 ('on thy belly shalt thou go'). By prohibiting the consumption of all belly-crawlers, Leviticus 11 draws a dietary boundary around the symbol of the fall itself. The serpent, cursed to the lowest stratum of creation, defines the floor of the clean/unclean taxonomy. This typological connection is profound: the dietary laws encode the cosmic conflict between the seed of the woman and the seed of the serpent (Genesis 3:15) into the daily eating habits of the redeemed community. What the enemy eats, the redeemed do not eat.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall not make yourselves abominable with any creeping thing that creepeth, neither shall ye make yourselves unclean with them, that ye should be defiled thereby.
verses.original_text: אַל תְּשַׁקְּצוּ אֶת נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם בְּכָל הַשֶּׁרֶץ הַשֹּׁרֵץ וְלֹא תִטַּמְּאוּ בָּהֶם וְנִטְמֵתֶם בָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Al teshaqetzu et nafshotekhem bekhol hashsheretz hashore'tz velo titamme'u bahem venitmetem bam.
verses.study_note: This verse introduces a crucial theological concept: the self (nefesh — soul/self) can be made abominable. The soul is the seat of covenant identity, and eating prohibited creatures defiles the nefesh itself. Three verbs describe the progressive interior damage: al teshaqetzu (do not make yourselves an abomination), velo titamme'u (do not make yourselves unclean), venitmetem (lest you become defiled). The movement is from act → state → identity: eating defiles, defilement corrupts, corruption becomes a state of being. The dietary law is ultimately about protecting the integrity of the nefesh.
verses.doctrinal_note: The nefesh (soul/self) language in this verse reveals that dietary obedience is not external behavioral compliance but a matter of the inner person's covenant status. To eat prohibited food is to 'abominize one's own soul' (teshaqetzu et nafshotekhem). This is the most interior formulation of dietary law in the entire chapter — it reaches beneath outward behavior to the question of what happens to the soul. The NT parallel is Paul's teaching that the body is the temple of the Holy Spirit and that what one does with the body affects the spirit (1 Corinthians 6:18-20). Holiness is holistic — it encompasses body, soul, and spirit.

verses.kjv_text: For I am the LORD your God: ye shall therefore sanctify yourselves, and ye shall be holy; for I am holy: neither shall ye defile yourselves with any manner of creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth.
verses.original_text: כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם וְהִתְקַדִּשְׁתֶּם וִהְיִיתֶם קְדֹשִׁים כִּי קָדוֹשׁ אָנִי וְלֹא תְטַמְּאוּ אֶת נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם בְּכָל הַשֶּׁרֶץ הַרֹמֵשׂ עַל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki ani YHWH Eloheikhem vehitqaddishshetem vihyitem qedoshim ki qadosh ani velo tetamme'u et nafshotekhem bekhol hashsheretz harome's al ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: This verse is the theological heart of the entire dietary law — and one of the most significant holiness statements in all of Scripture. The grounding formula 'I am the LORD your God' (ki ani YHWH Eloheikhem) anchors the law in covenant identity, not merely divine authority. The imitative principle follows: 'be holy, for I am holy' (vihyitem qedoshim ki qadosh ani). Holiness is not an arbitrary standard imposed from outside but a participation in the nature of God himself. The Hithpael reflexive vehitqaddishshetem (sanctify yourselves) reveals that holiness involves active, ongoing personal engagement — not a passive state but a continuous striving.
verses.doctrinal_note: The declaration 'I am the LORD your God... be holy, for I am holy' (ki qadosh ani) is the theological summit of Leviticus 11 and one of the most cited verses in the NT. Peter quotes it directly in 1 Peter 1:15-16, applying it to Christians as the new covenant people of God: 'as he which hath called you is holy, so be ye holy in all manner of conversation.' The passage demonstrates the canonical continuity of the holiness imperative: the demand that flows from God's nature does not change with the covenant. What changes in the NT is the ground of holiness — no longer dietary separation but the sanctifying work of the Spirit. The imitation of God (imitatio Dei) rooted in the divine character (ki qadosh ani) is the foundation of all biblical ethics across both Testaments.

verses.kjv_text: For I am the LORD that bringeth you up out of the land of Egypt, to be your God: ye shall therefore be holy, for I am holy.
verses.original_text: כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה הַמַּעֲלֶה אֶתְכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לִהְיֹת לָכֶם לֵאלֹהִים וִהְיִיתֶם קְדֹשִׁים כִּי קָדוֹשׁ אָנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Ki ani YHWH hamma'aleh etkhem me'eretz Mitzrayim lihyot lakhem lElohim vihyitem qedoshim ki qadosh ani.
verses.study_note: The dietary laws are grounded not only in God's holy nature (v.44) but in his redemptive act — the Exodus from Egypt. The participle hamma'aleh (the one who brought you up) uses a different verb than the standard 'brought you out': alah means to bring up/ascend, suggesting elevation — God did not merely extract Israel from Egypt but lifted them to a higher existence. The covenant formula 'to be your God' (lihyot lakhem lElohim) is the relational climax of the Exodus (Exod 6:7): the purpose of redemption is communion with God. Holiness flows from redemption — because God rescued Israel, Israel must be holy.
verses.doctrinal_note: Verse 45 establishes the redemptive-historical grounding of all ethical law: 'I am the LORD who brought you up out of Egypt.' This is the Exodus foundation of ethics — obedience is the response of the redeemed, not the means of redemption. This structure — indicative (God acted) followed by imperative (therefore be holy) — is the canonical pattern of biblical ethics from Exodus 20:1-2 through Romans 12:1 ('by the mercies of God... present your bodies'). The NT equivalent is 'we love him because he first loved us' (1 John 4:19) and 'Christ died for us... therefore walk in love' (Ephesians 5:1-2). Holiness is always the fruit of grace, never its root.

verses.kjv_text: This is the law of the beasts, and of the fowl, and of every living creature that moveth in the waters, and of every creature that creepeth upon the earth:
verses.original_text: זֹאת תּוֹרַת הַבְּהֵמָה וְהָעוֹף וְכֹל נֶפֶשׁ הַחַיָּה הָרֹמֶשֶׂת בַּמָּיִם וּלְכָל נֶפֶשׁ הַשֹּׁרֶצֶת עַל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Zot torat habehemah veha'of vekhol nefesh hachayah haromeset bamayim ulekhol nefesh hashore'tzet al ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: This verse opens the formal legal colophon (closing summary formula) of the chapter. 'This is the law' (zot torat) is the standard Levitical closing formula — a literary marker that signals the end of a legal unit and its official canonical status. The word torah here means 'instruction, teaching, law' — its fundamental meaning before it became the title of the Pentateuch. The comprehensive enumeration that follows — beasts, birds, aquatic creatures, earth-creepers — mirrors the creation sequence of Genesis 1, confirming that the dietary laws operate within a creational framework. The law covers every major ecological domain.

verses.kjv_text: To make a difference between the unclean and the clean, and between the beast that may be eaten and the beast that may not be eaten.
verses.original_text: לְהַבְדִּיל בֵּין הַטָּמֵא וּבֵין הַטָּהוֹר וּבֵין הַחַיָּה הַנֶּאֱכֶלֶת וּבֵין הַחַיָּה אֲשֶׁר לֹא תֵאָכֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Lehavdil bein hattame' uvein hattahor uvein hachayah hanne'ekhellet uvein hachayah asher lo te'akhel.
verses.study_note: The final verse names the purpose of the entire dietary code: lehavdil — to distinguish, to separate, to divide. This is the same verb used in Genesis 1 for God's creative acts of separation (light from darkness, waters above from waters below, day from night). The root bdl (to separate) is the foundation of the concept of havdalah — the Jewish ceremony of separation that marks the end of Sabbath. The dietary laws are thus an extension of creation theology: just as God separated the elements of creation to bring order out of chaos, Israel's dietary practice enacts daily separations that maintain covenantal order in the midst of a disordered world.
verses.doctrinal_note: The closing word lehavdil (to distinguish, to separate) is the theological key to the entire chapter and to Levitical holiness as a whole. The root b-d-l (to separate) carries the weight of creation, covenant, and sanctification simultaneously: God separated at creation (Genesis 1); God separated Israel from the nations (Leviticus 20:24-26); Israel separates clean from unclean in daily life. This triple separation — creational, covenantal, personal — forms the structural theology of holiness in Leviticus. The NT transforms the outward separation principle into the inward sanctification of the Spirit (2 Corinthians 6:17 cites Leviticus's separation language directly): 'Come out from among them, and be ye separate, saith the Lord.' The dietary laws are fulfilled not by being abolished but by being internalized — the Spirit produces the separation of holy character that the dietary laws enacted in physical form.

meta.overview:
Leviticus 11 marks a profound transition in the book: from the altar to the table, from priestly regulations to the daily life of every Israelite household. Having established the sacrificial system (chapters 1–7) and ordained the priesthood (chapters 8–10), the text now extends the holiness principle into the most intimate rhythms of domestic life — what God's people may and may not eat. The chapter is addressed jointly to Moses and Aaron, signaling that these laws belong to both the priestly and communal spheres of Israel's existence.

The structure is logical and comprehensive. Land animals are evaluated by two criteria: split hooves and cud-chewing (vv.1–8). Water creatures require both fins and scales (vv.9–12). A list of prohibited birds follows (vv.13–19), then flying insects with a remarkable exception for leaping insects like locusts (vv.20–23). The second half addresses defilement by carcass contact — even touching a dead unclean animal renders one ceremonially impure until evening (vv.24–28). A specific list of swarming ground creatures (vv.29–38) and rules for clean animals that die naturally (vv.39–40) precede the climactic prohibition against all swarming things (vv.41–43).

The theological climax arrives in verses 44–45, where the dietary laws are grounded not in hygiene or pragmatism but in the holy character of God and the redemptive act of the Exodus: 'I am the LORD your God: ye shall therefore sanctify yourselves, and ye shall be holy; for I am holy... I am the LORD that bringeth you up out of the land of Egypt, to be your God: ye shall therefore be holy, for I am holy.' This passage is directly cited in 1 Peter 1:15–16, demonstrating its canonical authority across both Testaments.

The Hebrew binary of tahor (clean, טָהוֹר) and tame (unclean, טָמֵא) structures the entire chapter. These terms carry far more than hygienic meaning — they describe covenant status before a holy God. The dietary laws functioned as a daily enacted parable: every meal was a reminder that Israel was a separated, holy people. The clean/unclean distinction inscribed into Israel's diet the fundamental principle of havdalah (separation) — the same principle God demonstrated in creation when He divided light from darkness, waters from waters, and sea from land.

Typologically, the chapter anticipates the New Testament's treatment of clean and unclean. Jesus declared all foods clean (Mark 7:19), Peter's vision in Acts 10 used the dietary categories to signal the opening of the gospel to the Gentiles, and Paul in Colossians 2:16–17 identifies these regulations as 'a shadow of things to come; but the body is of Christ.' The holiness they encoded is now fulfilled not by dietary separation but by the sanctifying work of the Holy Spirit in the life of the believer.
meta.theme: Holiness in Daily Life: The Clean and Unclean Distinction as Covenant Identity
meta.keyWordMeaning: Clean, pure, unmixed — the state of ritual and moral purity that characterizes what is acceptable before a holy God. Derived from a root meaning to be bright or shining. Tahor appears throughout Leviticus 11 as the positive covenant category: animals that may be eaten, touched, or brought into the camp. Its antonym tame (unclean, H2931) describes what defiles. Together they form the binary that structures Israel's daily life as a holy people. In the NT, katharos (G2513) carries the same semantic weight, appearing in Christ's declaration in Mark 7:19 and in the vision of Acts 10.
outline: Land Animals: The Two-Criteria Law (vv.1-8): Split hoof and cud-chewing as markers of cleanness
Aquatic Creatures: Fins and Scales (vv.9-12): The only permitted water animals
Prohibited Birds Enumerated (vv.13-19): Twenty unclean species listed by name
Flying Insects: Leaping Exception (vv.20-23): Winged creeping things with jointed legs permitted
Defilement by Carcass Contact (vv.24-28): Touching the dead renders one unclean until evening
Swarming Ground Creatures (vv.29-38): Eight specific unclean reptiles and rodents
Clean Animals That Die Naturally (vv.39-40): Even permitted animals defile when found dead
The Great Prohibition: All Swarming Things (vv.41-43): The climactic ban on all creeping things
Theological Foundation: Be Holy for I Am Holy (vv.44-47): The Exodus-grounded rationale for all dietary law

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: The standard Levitical formula of divine revelation: "the LORD spoke to Moses saying." This formula (vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor) appears over eighty times in Leviticus alone, underscoring the book's fundamental character as direct divine legislation rather than human religious development. Each occurrence frames what follows as authoritative divine speech, not priestly custom or cultural convention. The chapter that follows — governing the most intimate human experience of childbirth — receives the same formula as the laws of sacrifice and the dietary code. Nothing in life is outside the scope of divine instruction; God's word addresses the holiest, most universal, and most bodily moments of human existence. This framing also establishes the essential context for interpretation: these regulations originate not in ancient Near Eastern taboo or patriarchal social custom but in divine revelation to the covenant mediator.

verses.kjv_text: Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, If a woman have conceived seed, and born a man child: then she shall be unclean seven days; according to the days of the separation for her infirmity shall she be unclean.
verses.original_text: דַּבֵּר אֶל בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר אִשָּׁה כִּי תַזְרִיעַ וְיָלְדָה זָכָר וְטָמְאָה שִׁבְעַת יָמִים כִּימֵי נִדַּת דְּוֹתָהּ תִּטְמָא׃
verses.transliteration: Dabber el-benei Yisra'el lemor ishah ki tazria veyyaldah zakhar vetam'ah shivat yamim kimei niddat devotah tittamma.
verses.study_note: The chapter's central law is introduced. "If a woman have conceived seed" (ishah ki tazria) — the verb zara (tazria) is the same root as "seed" (zera), used in Genesis 1:11-12 for plant seed and in Genesis 3:15 for the seed of the woman. The act of conceiving is cast in seed language, connecting the chapter to the creation mandate and the proto-gospel. After bearing a son, the woman is "unclean seven days" — the same period as her monthly separation (niddah). This is ritual uncleanness (tum'ah), not moral impurity or sin. Tum'ah is the state resulting from contact with elemental forces associated with the boundaries of life: blood, semen, childbirth, death, skin disease. The seven-day period mirrors the niddah (menstrual separation, Lev 15:19-24) — "according to the days of the separation for her infirmity" (kimei niddat devotah). The word devotah (her infirmity/her affliction) comes from davah, to be faint, weak, ill. Menstruation and childbirth are grouped under the same ritual category because both involve the discharge of blood, which in Levitical theology belongs uniquely to God (Lev 17:11) as the vehicle of atonement and life.
verses.doctrinal_note: The ritual uncleanness associated with childbirth is not a statement that birth is evil, sinful, or that woman is spiritually inferior. Childbirth fulfills God's creation mandate (Gen 1:28) and is declared "good" within creation. The Levitical category of tum'ah (ritual uncleanness) is distinct from chatta't (moral sin) — it is an objective ritual state, not a moral judgment. Rather, the Levitical system recognizes that contact with blood and bodily discharge — the very substances that mark the boundaries between life and death — requires a process of ritual re-integration into normal communal and cultic life. The theology of tum'ah ultimately points to the need for restoration to God's presence, foreshadowing the perfect cleansing achieved by Christ's own blood (Heb 9:13-14; 1 John 1:7). The woman who gives life needs atonement — not because birth is sin but because she has been at the boundary of the deepest mysteries of existence, and re-entry into the holy community requires a sacred process.

verses.kjv_text: And in the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised.
verses.original_text: וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי יִמּוֹל בְּשַׂר עָרְלָתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Uvayom hashemini yimmol besar orlato.
verses.study_note: The single verse on circumcision in this chapter is deceptively brief but theologically vast. Circumcision of the male child must occur on the eighth day — the same ordinance established in Genesis 17:12 with Abraham. The timing is significant: it falls after the seven days of the mother's uncleanness and on the first day beyond that cycle. Eight in biblical numerology consistently signals new beginning, new covenant, new creation — the eighth day is the first day of a new week, beyond the seven-day creational cycle. Circumcision on the eighth day thus embodies entrance into the covenant on the threshold of new creation. The word "foreskin" (orlah) appears throughout Scripture as a symbol of uncoverance, obstruction, and unreceptiveness (Lev 26:41, "uncircumcised heart"; Jer 6:10, "uncircumcised ear"). The removal of the foreskin enacts physically the covenant principle of removing the obstacle between the person and God. Note that the circumcision takes place during the mother's ongoing purification period — the male infant enters the covenant before the purification process for his birth is complete, pointing to the priority of divine grace over human ritual.
verses.doctrinal_note: Circumcision on the eighth day is one of Scripture's richest typological institutions. In Colossians 2:11-12, Paul explicitly identifies Christian circumcision as "the circumcision of Christ" — not made with hands but consisting of the putting off of the whole "body of the flesh" in union with Christ's death and burial (baptism). Romans 4:11 declares circumcision a "seal of the righteousness of the faith" Abraham had while still uncircumcised — establishing the principle that the sign follows and confirms the inward reality, it does not produce it. Philippians 3:3 identifies the true circumcision as those who "worship God in the spirit... and have no confidence in the flesh." The eighth day' s newness-symbolism is fulfilled in Christ's resurrection on the first day of the new week (Matt 28:1; John 20:1) — the ultimate eighth-day new creation event that renders physical circumcision fulfilled and transcended (Gal 5:6; 6:15).

verses.kjv_text: And she shall then continue in the blood of her purifying three and thirty days; she shall touch no hallowed thing, nor come into the sanctuary, until the days of her purifying be fulfilled.
verses.original_text: וּשְׁלֹשִׁים יוֹם וּשְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים תֵּשֵׁב בִּדְמֵי טָהֳרָה בְּכָל קֹדֶשׁ לֹא תִגַּע וְאֶל הַמִּקְדָּשׁ לֹא תָבֹא עַד מְלֹאת יְמֵי טָהֳרָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ushleoshim yom usheloshet yamim teshev bidmei taharah bekhal kodesh lo tigga ve'el hamikdash lo tavo ad melo't yemei taharah.
verses.study_note: The thirty-three days of purification following the initial seven-day uncleanness period bring the total to forty days for a male birth. During this extended period, the woman is described as being "in the blood of her purifying" (bidmei taharah) — a remarkable phrase. She is not still unclean in the full sense; she is in a transitional state moving toward taharah (purity/cleanness). Nevertheless, she cannot touch any "hallowed thing" (kodesh — anything consecrated to God) nor enter the sanctuary (mikdash). This intermediate state — neither fully unclean nor yet fully clean — reflects the Levitical understanding that restoration to full covenant communion requires both time and a concluding sacrificial act. The forty-day period carries typological resonance: Moses spent forty days on Sinai (Exod 24:18), Jesus was tempted forty days in the wilderness (Matt 4:2), and the ascension occurred forty days after the resurrection (Acts 1:3). Forty marks a sustained period of transition leading to a new beginning.

verses.kjv_text: But if she bear a maid child, then she shall be unclean two weeks, as in her separation: and she shall continue in the blood of her purifying threescore and six days.
verses.original_text: וְאִם נְקֵבָה תֵלֵד וְטָמְאָה שְׁבֻעַיִם כְּנִדָּתָהּ וְשִׁשִּׁים יוֹם וְשֵׁשֶׁת יָמִים תֵּשֵׁב עַל דְּמֵי טָהֳרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im neqevah teled vetam'ah shevuayim keniddatah veshishim yom vesheshet yamim teshev al demei taharah.
verses.study_note: The birth of a daughter doubles both periods: fourteen days of uncleanness (two weeks) followed by sixty-six days of purification, totaling eighty days. This asymmetry between male (forty days total) and female (eighty days total) births has generated extensive theological reflection. The text gives no explicit rationale, inviting interpretation. The most coherent explanations are: (1) The female child, at birth, already carries within her the biological capacity for future blood-discharge (menstruation, future childbirth), extending the ritual dynamic associated with blood; (2) The doubled period may reflect the greater biological involvement of the mother's body in the development of a daughter during gestation. What must be firmly rejected is the reading that daughters are spiritually inferior, more defiling, or less valued — Levitical tum'ah is ritual, not moral or ontological. The same God who doubled the purification period also declared woman made in the image of God (Gen 1:27), included daughters in the covenant community (Deut 12:12; Num 27:1-11), and fulfilled His covenant through the seed of a woman (Gen 3:15; Gal 4:4).
verses.doctrinal_note: The distinction between male and female birth periods in Leviticus 12 is one of several passages that have been misused to argue for female spiritual inferiority.
