The New Testament decisively closes this argument. Galatians 3:28 declares: "there is neither male nor female: for ye are all one in Christ Jesus." The purification laws of Leviticus applied equally to both parents' children, not as assessments of worth but as ritual processes. Furthermore, the entire law that Leviticus 12 represents was fulfilled by Mary's presentation offering (Luke 2:22-24), at which point the infant Christ was himself declared "a light to lighten the Gentiles" and "the glory of thy people Israel" (Luke 2:32) — the birth of this particular daughter's descendant rendered all birth-purification rituals fulfilled and complete (Heb 9:10-12).

verses.kjv_text: And when the days of her purifying are fulfilled, for a son, or for a daughter, she shall bring a lamb of the first year for a burnt offering, and a young pigeon, or a turtledove, for a sin offering, unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, unto the priest:
verses.original_text: וּבִמְלֹאת יְמֵי טָהֳרָהּ לְבֵן אוֹ לְבַת תָּבִיא כֶּבֶשׂ בֶּן שְׁנָתוֹ לְעֹלָה וּבֶן יוֹנָה אוֹ תֹר לְחַטָּאת אֶל פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד אֶל הַכֹּהֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Uvimlo't yemei taharah leven o levat tavi cheves ben shenato le'olah uven yonah o tor lechatta't el petach ohel mo'ed el hakkohen.
verses.study_note: At the completion of the purification period — whether for a son or daughter, both treated identically at this point — the woman comes to the tabernacle door with two offerings. First, "a lamb of the first year for a burnt offering" (cheves ben shenato le'olah) — the same high-quality offering used in the daily tamid sacrifice. The burnt offering (olah — ascending offering, wholly consumed) symbolizes total dedication and consecration to God. Second, "a young pigeon or a turtledove for a sin offering" (ben yonah o tor lechatta't) — the sin offering (chatta't) addresses the realm of ritual impurity and separation that the purification period has navigated. The combination of burnt offering and sin offering at the conclusion of the purification mirrors the pattern of Leviticus 1 and 4 — complete consecration and complete cleansing together restore full access to God's presence. The woman approaches "the door of the tabernacle of meeting" — she comes to the threshold, not presuming entry but standing at the boundary where human approach meets divine presence, the prescribed point of access.
verses.doctrinal_note: The two offerings required at the conclusion of childbirth purification — a burnt offering and a sin offering — together represent the twofold movement of restored relationship with God: total consecration (olah) and purification from impurity (chatta't). This twofold dynamic reaches its ultimate expression in Christ, who on the cross offered Himself as both the perfect burnt offering of total consecration to the Father (John 10:17-18; Heb 9:14) and the ultimate sin offering who became sin for us (2 Cor 5:21; Heb 13:11-12). The woman's post-partum offering of both these sacrifices simultaneously is a remarkable double typology. Furthermore, this verse stands in direct relationship with Luke 2:22-24: Mary brought these very offerings after the birth of Jesus. The One who fulfilled every offering was himself the occasion for His mother's offering.

verses.kjv_text: Who shall offer it before the LORD, and make an atonement for her; and she shall be cleansed from the issue of her blood. This is the law for her that hath born a male or a female.
verses.original_text: וְהִקְרִיבוֹ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וְכִפֶּר עָלֶיהָ וְטָהֲרָה מִמְּקֹר דָּמֶיהָ זֹאת תּוֹרַת הַיֹּלֶדֶת לַזָּכָר אוֹ לַנְּקֵבָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehikrivo lifnei YHWH vekhipper aleha vetaharah mimmkor damehah zo't torat hayyoledet lazzakhar o lanneqevah.
verses.study_note: The priest presents the offerings "before the LORD" — the active agent of atonement is the priest, not the woman. She brings; he presents. This priestly mediation is essential to the Levitical system: no one approaches God directly or independently. The result of the priest's atonement-act (kipper — to cover, cleanse, make atonement) is that the woman "shall be cleansed" (vetaharah — niphal passive: she shall be made clean). The source of her impurity is described with anatomical precision: "the issue of her blood" (mimmkor damehah) — from makor (spring, source, fountain), the flowing source of blood. Taharah is total: once the priest has made atonement, she is fully restored. "This is the law for her that hath born a male or a female" — the chapter's summary formula (zo't torat) applies equally to both births, confirming that the legal obligations of vv.2-6 apply in both cases. The closing summary formula "zo't torat" appears frequently in Leviticus (Lev 6:9,14,25; 7:1,11; 11:46; 13:59; 14:2,32,54,57) as the formal conclusion-marker of a legal pericope.
verses.doctrinal_note: The atonement made by the priest for the post-partum woman is a remarkably comprehensive provision. Even at the occasion of birth — the fulfillment of God's creation mandate — atonement is required. This is one of the strongest Levitical pointers to the doctrine of original sin as articulated in Romans 5:12: "by one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin; and so death passed upon all men." Birth itself, the continuation of human life, occurs under the conditions of a fallen creation, and therefore requires atonement and restoration to God's presence. Psalm 51:5 understands this: "Behold, I was shapen in iniquity; and in sin did my mother conceive me." The atonement Christ made on the cross was not merely for individual acts of transgression but for the entire human condition — the creatureliness of fallen humanity from birth. Romans 8:3 states it precisely: "God sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh, and for sin, condemned sin in the flesh."

verses.kjv_text: And if she be not able to bring a lamb, then she shall bring two turtles, or two young pigeons; the one for the burnt offering, and the other for a sin offering: and the priest shall make an atonement for her, and she shall be clean.
verses.original_text: וְאִם לֹא תִמְצָא יָדָהּ דֵּי שֶׂה וְלָקְחָה שְׁתֵּי תֹרִים אוֹ שְׁנֵי בְנֵי יוֹנָה אֶחָד לְעֹלָה וְאֶחָד לְחַטָּאת וְכִפֶּר עָלֶיהָ הַכֹּהֵן וְטָהֵרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im lo timtza yadah dei seh velakchah shetei torim o shenei venei yonah echad le'olah ve'echad lechatta't vekhipper aleha hakkohen vetaherah.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with one of the most gracious provisions in all of Torah: the offering for the poor. "If she be not able to bring a lamb" — the idiom is "if her hand does not find enough for a lamb" (im lo timtza yadah dei seh) — literally "if her hand does not reach to the extent of a sheep." Yadah (her hand) as the measure of financial reach is a beautiful idiom for personal economic means throughout Leviticus (cf. Lev 5:7,11; 14:21,31; 25:28). For the woman who cannot afford the full lamb, two turtledoves or two young pigeons are fully sufficient — one for the burnt offering, one for the sin offering. The atonement is identical, the cleansing complete, the priest's ministry the same. God makes no distinction between the rich woman's lamb and the poor woman's pigeon in terms of the efficacy of atonement. This verse is the verse that Luke 2:24 directly fulfills: Mary and Joseph brought "a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons" — the offering of the poor. The family of the Messiah was too poor for the full lamb. And yet within thirty-three years, that same child would be the Lamb — the one sacrifice that rendered all these offerings obsolete.
verses.doctrinal_note: Luke 2:22-24 records the most extraordinary fulfillment of Leviticus 12:8 in all of Scripture. Mary brought the offering of the poor — two turtledoves — because she and Joseph could not afford a lamb. The profound irony is that she presented as her sin offering a pair of birds in place of a lamb, while the true Lamb of God (John 1:29) lay in her arms. This poverty fulfills Isaiah 53:2-3 — the Servant had no outward majesty or wealth. More deeply still: the One who would become the world's ultimate sin offering and burnt offering (Eph 5:2; Heb 9:26) was present at His own mother's offering, fully submitted to the law He had given and was born to fulfill (Gal 4:4-5). Second Corinthians 8:9 captures the theology of divine poverty: "though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor, that ye through his poverty might be rich." The offering of two small birds at the temple was the economic signature of the incarnation's cost.

meta.overview: Leviticus 12 is a brief but theologically rich chapter of eight verses governing the ritual purification of a woman following childbirth. Situated between the laws of clean and unclean animals (ch.11) and the laws of skin diseases (chs.13-14), it forms part of the Levitical theology of holiness touching the most intimate boundaries of human bodily life. The chapter decrees that a woman who bears a son is ritually unclean for seven days, followed by thirty-three days of purification (forty total); if she bears a daughter, she is unclean for fourteen days and continues in purification for sixty-six days (eighty total). At the completion of her days she must bring a burnt offering and a sin offering for atonement before the LORD, with a provision for the poor to substitute two turtledoves or pigeons if unable to afford a lamb. The theological questions this chapter raises are profound and have been discussed throughout Jewish and Christian interpretation. Why is childbirth — the fulfillment of God's creation mandate "be fruitful and multiply" (Gen 1:28) — associated with ritual uncleanness? The impurity is not moral uncleanness (sin) but ritual uncleanness, connected to the discharge of blood, which in Levitical theology represents the seat of life belonging exclusively to God (Lev 17:11). The act of giving life — involving blood and bodily discharge — brings the woman into contact with the most elemental substances that Levitical law designated as requiring purification. The longer purification period for a female child has generated extensive discussion; the most theologically coherent explanation is that the daughter herself, at birth, already carries the biological potential for future blood-discharge associated with childbearing, extending the ritual dynamic. Christologically, Leviticus 12 is anchored in the New Testament by a single, luminous historical event: Luke 2:22-24 records that Mary and Joseph came to the temple "when the days of her purification according to the law of Moses were accomplished" and offered "a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons" — the offering of the poor (v.8). In the most striking irony in all of Scripture, the One who would become the ultimate sin offering (2 Cor 5:21) was presented at the temple while His mother offered a sin offering for His birth. The Son of God submitted to the whole apparatus of the law — including the purification law of Leviticus 12 — to fulfill it from within (Matt 5:17; Gal 4:4-5).
meta.theme: Ritual Purity, the Blood of Life, and the Grace of Restoration
meta.keyWordMeaning: Purification/cleansing — from taher (H2891), to be clean, pure, undefiled. Taharah is the completed state of ritual cleanness restored after a period of impurity. It is not the cancellation of guilt (that is kapporah/atonement) but the restoration of full communion with the covenant community and access to the sanctuary. The theological arc of the chapter is from tum'ah (impurity) through time and sacrifice to taharah — a pattern embedded in Israel's liturgical life at every level.
outline: A Son Born: Seven Days of Uncleanness and Circumcision on the Eighth Day (vv.1-3)
Thirty-Three Days of Purification for a Son (v.4)
A Daughter Born: Fourteen Days of Uncleanness and Sixty-Six Days of Purification (v.5)
The Purification Offerings at the Tabernacle: Burnt Offering and Sin Offering (v.6)
Atonement and the Restoration of Cleanness (v.7)
Provision for the Poor: Two Turtledoves or Pigeons (v.8)

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל אַהֲרֹן לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayedabber YHWH el Moshe ve-el Aharon lemor.
verses.study_note: The double address — to both Moses and Aaron together — is notable and deliberate. Of the first twelve chapters of Leviticus, only chapters 11 and 13-14 are addressed to both men jointly. Moses receives the law as mediator and lawgiver; Aaron receives it as the priest who must implement the diagnostic procedures. The conjunction 'and to Aaron' (ve-el Aharon) signals that what follows requires priestly expertise and judicial authority to execute. The divine origin of the diagnostic laws is thus established from the outset: these are not folk remedies or cultural taboos but divine legislation governing the boundary between holy and profane, clean and unclean.

verses.kjv_text: When a man shall have in the skin of his flesh a rising, a scab, or bright spot, and it be in the skin of his flesh like the plague of leprosy; then he shall be brought unto Aaron the priest, or unto one of his sons the priests:
verses.original_text: אָדָם כִּי יִהְיֶה בְעוֹר בְּשָׂרוֹ שְׂאֵת אוֹ סַפַּחַת אוֹ בַהֶרֶת וְהָיָה בְעוֹר בְּשָׂרוֹ לְנֶגַע צָרָעַת וְהוּבָא אֶל אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֵן אוֹ אֶל אַחַד מִבָּנָיו הַכֹּהֲנִים׃
verses.transliteration: Adam ki yihyeh be-or besaro se'et o sappachat o baheret ve-hayah be-or besaro le-nega tzara'at ve-huva el Aharon ha-kohen o el echad mi-banav ha-kohanim.
verses.study_note: Three distinct surface conditions trigger the examination procedure: se'et (a swelling or raised area), sappachat (a scab or eruption), and baheret (a bright or shining spot). These three terms likely represent a spectrum of the visual presentations of tzara'at, from raised to flat to discolored. The phrase 'in the skin of his flesh' (be-or besaro) is repeated twice in the verse, emphasizing that the condition is externally visible — the priest diagnoses by observation, not invasive examination. The afflicted person is 'brought' (ve-huva, a passive hiphil) to the priest — the initiative is not entirely the patient's; the community has a stake in diagnosing uncleanness. Any of Aaron's sons may perform the examination, establishing that priestly diagnostic authority is collegial and inheritable.
verses.doctrinal_note: The three terms for surface conditions — se'et, sappachat, baheret — function in Levitical law as the visible vocabulary of uncleanness. Theologically, the skin's surface is the body's boundary, and what appears on that boundary determines whether the person may remain within the covenant community. This spatial logic — inside/outside, clean/unclean, within the camp/without — is the physical grammar of holiness theology. Sin is understood in Leviticus not merely as moral failure but as a defilement that corrupts the boundary between the holy and the common. The priest's diagnostic role anticipates Christ who sees beyond surface appearances into the deeper condition of the human heart (John 2:25), and whose declaration "be thou clean" (Matt 8:3) is not diagnostic but creative.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall look on the plague in the skin of the flesh: and when the hair in the plague is turned white, and the plague in sight be deeper than the skin of his flesh, it is a plague of leprosy: and the priest shall look on him, and pronounce him unclean.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַנֶּגַע בְּעוֹר הַבָּשָׂר וְשֵׂעָר בַּנֶּגַע הָפַךְ לָבָן וּמַרְאֵה הַנֶּגַע עָמֹק מֵעוֹר בְּשָׂרוֹ נֶגַע צָרַעַת הוּא וְרָאָהוּ הַכֹּהֵן וְטִמֵּא אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen et ha-nega be-or ha-basar ve-se'ar ba-nega hafakh lavan u-mar'eh ha-nega amoq me-or besaro nega tzara'at hu ve-ra'ahu ha-kohen ve-timme' oto.
verses.study_note: The priest's diagnostic criteria for definitive tzara'at are twofold: the hair in the affected area has 'turned white' (hafakh lavan) and the plague appears 'deeper than the skin' (amoq me-or besaro). The whitening of hair signals structural change within the skin; the apparent depth signals that the condition has penetrated below the surface layer. Both criteria together constitute the diagnosis. The verb ra'ah (to see, to look) is used twice — 'the priest shall look' and 'he shall look on him' — emphasizing the visual, observational nature of priestly judgment. The decisive act is the pronouncement: the priest 'shall pronounce him unclean' (ve-timme' oto, piel of tame) — the priest's word creates the legal-social reality of the person's status.
verses.doctrinal_note: The priest's declarative function — 'pronounce him unclean' (ve-timme' oto) — is one of the most theologically significant speech-acts in the Old Testament legal system. The priest does not merely observe a condition; his spoken verdict establishes a social and covenant reality. This declarative authority is the Levitical anticipation of the priestly power to pronounce absolution or judgment. Jesus both assumes and transcends this authority: where the Levitical priest's word declares a pre-existing condition, Christ's word creates a new condition. When He says "be thou clean" (katharistheti, Matt 8:3), He does not discover cleanness but generates it — the Great Priest whose diagnosis is also the cure. The Reformers' doctrine of forensic justification resonates with this priestly-declarative structure: God declares the sinner righteous (Rom 4:5; 5:1) — a verdict that is simultaneously effective.

verses.kjv_text: If the bright spot be white in the skin of his flesh, and in sight be not deeper than the skin, and the hair thereof be not turned white; then the priest shall shut up him that hath the plague seven days:
verses.original_text: וְאִם בַּהֶרֶת לְבָנָה הִוא בְּעוֹר בְּשָׂרוֹ וְעָמֹק אֵין מַרְאֶהָ מִן הַעוֹר וּשְׂעָרָה לֹא הָפַךְ לָבָן וְהִסְגִּיר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַנֶּגַע שִׁבְעַת יָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im baheret levana hi be-or besaro ve-amoq ein mar'eha min ha-or u-se'arah lo hafakh lavan ve-hisgir ha-kohen et ha-nega shiv'at yamim.
verses.study_note: When the diagnostic criteria of verse 3 are not met — when the spot is white but does not appear deeper than the skin and the hair has not whitened — the priest does not immediately pronounce clean or unclean but institutes a quarantine. 'Shall shut him up' (ve-hisgir) — the piel of sagar, to close, to lock away — is the technical term for the isolation period. The seven-day quarantine serves as a waiting period for the condition to declare itself more clearly. Seven days mirrors the week of creation and the seven-day periods throughout Levitical law (consecration: seven days; Feast of Unleavened Bread: seven days). The uncertain case receives the gift of time for discernment rather than a hasty verdict, reflecting the equity and deliberateness of divine judicial procedure.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall look on him the seventh day: and, behold, if the plague in his sight be at a stay, and the plague spread not in the skin; then the priest shall shut him up seven days more:
verses.original_text: וְרָאָהוּ הַכֹּהֵן בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי וְהִנֵּה הַנֶּגַע עָמַד בְּעֵינָיו לֹא פָשָׂה הַנֶּגַע בָּעוֹר וְהִסְגִּירוֹ הַכֹּהֵן שִׁבְעַת יָמִים שֵׁנִית׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ahu ha-kohen bayyom hash-shevi'i ve-hinneh ha-nega amad be-einav lo fasah ha-nega ba-or ve-hisgiro ha-kohen shiv'at yamim shenit.
verses.study_note: On the seventh day the priest re-examines the case. The key diagnostic term is 'at a stay' (amad be-einav) — literally 'stood in his eyes,' meaning the condition has not changed or spread. The complementary criterion is that the plague has 'not spread' (lo fasah) in the skin. The verb pasah (to spread, to flow) is the decisive spreading indicator throughout the chapter: a spreading condition is active, growing tzara'at; a stable condition may resolve. If stable after seven days, the priest orders a second quarantine of seven more days — the two-stage process reflects pastoral patience. The law does not rush to condemn what may heal naturally.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall look on him again the seventh day: and, behold, if the plague be somewhat dark, and the plague spread not in the skin, the priest shall pronounce him clean: it is but a scab: and he shall wash his clothes, and be clean.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן אֹתוֹ בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי שֵׁנִית וְהִנֵּה כֵּהָה הַנֶּגַע וְלֹא פָשָׂה הַנֶּגַע בָּעוֹר וְטִהֲרוֹ הַכֹּהֵן מִסְפַּחַת הִוא וְכִבֶּס בְּגָדָיו וְטָהֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen oto bayyom hash-shevi'i shenit ve-hinneh kehah ha-nega ve-lo fasah ha-nega ba-or ve-tiharo ha-kohen mispachat hi ve-kibbes begadav ve-taher.
verses.study_note: After the second seven-day period, if the condition has 'become dark' (kehah) and has not spread, the priest pronounces him clean. The darkening (kahah, to be dim or faded) of the spot indicates it is healing, losing the abnormal brightness (baheret) that first triggered examination. The declaration 'pronounce him clean' (ve-tiharo, piel of taher) is the opposite of the condemnation of verse 3 — the same priestly authority that declares unclean also declares clean. The condition is identified as merely a 'scab' (mispachat) — a superficial condition, not tzara'at. The requirement to wash garments (kibbes begadav) before re-entering the community is a minor purification rite, acknowledging that even the quarantine period carried some measure of ritual uncertainty.

verses.kjv_text: But if the scab spread much abroad in the skin, after that he hath been seen of the priest for his cleansing, he shall be seen of the priest again:
verses.original_text: וְאִם פָּשֹׂה תִפְשֶׂה הַמִּסְפַּחַת בָּעוֹר אַחֲרֵי הֵרָאֹתוֹ אֶל הַכֹּהֵן לְטָהֳרָתוֹ וְנִרְאָה שֵׁנִית אֶל הַכֹּהֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im pasoh tifseh ha-mispachat ba-or acharei hera'oto el ha-kohen le-toharato ve-nir'ah shenit el ha-kohen.
verses.study_note: The chapter now addresses the case of apparent improvement followed by deterioration. If a condition that was initially judged as merely a scab (mispachat) subsequently spreads after the priest's examination, the person must return for a second examination. 'Spread much abroad' translates the emphatic infinitive absolute construction (pasoh tifseh) — the doubling of the verb root indicates intensive or certain spreading. The legal provision that post-examination deterioration requires re-examination demonstrates the law's responsiveness to changing conditions: the priest's verdict is not irrevocable when circumstances change. This revisability reflects a pastoral attentiveness to the ongoing reality of the person's condition.

verses.kjv_text: And if the priest see that, behold, the scab spreadeth in the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a leprosy.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה פָּשְׂתָה הַמִּסְפַּחַת בָּעוֹר וְטִמְּאוֹ הַכֹּהֵן צָרַעַת הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen ve-hinneh pasetah ha-mispachat ba-or ve-timme'o ha-kohen tzara'at hi.
verses.study_note: When re-examined and spreading is confirmed, the decisive verdict follows: 'the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a leprosy' (tzara'at hi). The demonstrative 'it is' (hi) is the formula of definitive classification — the condition has been conclusively identified as tzara'at. The brevity of this verdict after the extended case-law procedure underscores how thorough the process has been before judgment is rendered. The priest does not pronounce unclean hastily or on insufficient evidence; the diagnostic rigor of vv.1-8 demonstrates that the declaration is grounded in careful observation. False condemnation and false acquittal are equally avoided.

verses.kjv_text: When the plague of leprosy is in a man, then he shall be brought unto the priest;
verses.original_text: נֶגַע צָרַעַת כִּי תִהְיֶה בְּאָדָם וְהוּבָא אֶל הַכֹּהֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Nega tzara'at ki tihyeh be-adam ve-huva el ha-kohen.
verses.study_note: Verse 9 opens a new case-section dealing with the 'old' (yashan) or chronic form of tzara'at. The framing is general: whenever the plague of tzara'at is present in a person, he is to be brought to the priest. The repeated use of the passive 'he shall be brought' (ve-huva) reflects the community's corporate responsibility in the diagnosis process — it is not left to individual self-reporting but is a communal concern. The priest serves the entire covenant community as the boundary-guardian between clean and unclean; that boundary is everyone's interest to maintain. This communal responsibility anticipates the New Testament principle of corporate accountability for sin's spread within the church body (1 Cor 5:6-7).

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall see him: and, behold, if the rising be white in the skin, and it have turned the hair white, and there be quick raw flesh in the rising;
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה שְׂאֵת לְבָנָה בָּעוֹר וְהִיא הָפְכָה שֵׂעָר לָבָן וּמִחְיַת בָּשָׂר חַי בַּשְׂאֵת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen ve-hinneh se'et levana ba-or ve-hi hafekha se'ar lavan u-michyat basar chai ba-se'et.
verses.study_note: This verse adds a third diagnostic criterion not present in the earlier case-law: 'quick raw flesh' (michyat basar chai) — literally 'living flesh,' raw and exposed flesh visible within the swelling. The presence of raw, living flesh within the lesion indicates that the skin surface has broken down, exposing the living tissue beneath. This is a definitive indicator of active, deep tzara'at. The term 'chai' (living, alive) attached to the raw flesh is ironic: what appears most vitally alive — raw, fresh flesh — is actually the most sure sign of severe disease. Life exposed at the wrong place and in the wrong way signals deep impurity.

verses.kjv_text: It is an old leprosy in the skin of his flesh, and the priest shall pronounce him unclean, and shall not shut him up: for he is unclean.
verses.original_text: צָרַעַת נוֹשֶׁנֶת הִוא בְּעוֹר בְּשָׂרוֹ וְטִמְּאוֹ הַכֹּהֵן לֹא יַסְגִּרֶנּוּ כִּי טָמֵא הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Tzara'at noshenhet hi be-or besaro ve-timme'o ha-kohen lo yasgirenu ki tame hu.
verses.study_note: The phrase 'old leprosy' (tzara'at noshenhet) uses the verb yashan (to be old, established, chronic) — this is not a fresh eruption but a long-standing, chronic condition. For this case, no quarantine is needed: the evidence is so conclusive that immediate pronouncement of uncleanness follows. The redundancy 'for he is already unclean' (ki tame hu) explains the procedural abbreviation — quarantine serves to allow ambiguous cases to clarify, but here clarity already exists. The priest does not shut him up (lo yasgirenu) because there is nothing to wait and see. This provision saves the genuinely afflicted person from a superfluous quarantine while maintaining the rigor of the diagnostic system.

verses.kjv_text: And if a leprosy break out abroad in the skin, and the leprosy cover all the skin of him that hath the plague from his head even to his foot, wheresoever the priest looketh;
verses.original_text: וְאִם פָּרוֹחַ תִּפְרַח הַצָּרַעַת בָּעוֹר וְכִסְּתָה הַצָּרַעַת אֵת כָּל עוֹר הַנֶּגַע מֵרֹאשׁוֹ וְעַד רַגְלָיו לְכָל מַרְאֵה עֵינֵי הַכֹּהֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im paroch tifrach ha-tzara'at ba-or ve-khisetah ha-tzara'at et kol or ha-nega me-rosho ve-ad raglav le-khol mar'eh einei ha-kohen.
verses.study_note: Verses 12-13 introduce the most counterintuitive ruling in the entire chapter: a person covered from head to foot with tzara'at may be pronounced clean. The eruption (paroch tifrach — blooming it blooms, another emphatic infinitive absolute) that covers the entire body is paradoxically not condemned. This 'blooming' (parach) of tzara'at from head to foot is described with remarkable vividness — the condition is total, complete, covering every observable surface. The diagnostic gaze of the priest — 'wheresoever the priest looketh' (le-khol mar'eh einei ha-kohen) — scans the entire body and finds only the uniform white coverage.

verses.kjv_text: Then the priest shall consider: and, behold, if the leprosy have covered all his flesh, he shall pronounce him clean that hath the plague: it is all turned white: he is clean.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה כִסְּתָה הַצָּרַעַת אֶת כָּל בְּשָׂרוֹ וְטִהַר אֶת הַנֶּגַע כֻּלּוֹ הָפַךְ לָבָן טָהוֹר הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen ve-hinneh kisetah ha-tzara'at et kol besaro ve-tihar et ha-nega kullo hafakh lavan tahor hu.
verses.study_note: The stunning verdict: total tzara'at coverage = clean. 'It is all turned white: he is clean' (kullo hafakh lavan tahor hu). Commentators have long debated this ruling. The most theologically coherent explanation is that the condition of the raw flesh (basar chai, v.10) is the true sign of active, dangerous tzara'at — when the condition is total and uniform white coverage with no raw flesh visible, it has moved into a different category. The uniform whiteness with no living raw flesh means the disease is no longer actively spreading and penetrating. This is the law's most paradoxical case: total apparent disease = cleanness; partial apparent disease = uncleanness.
verses.doctrinal_note: The ruling of verse 13 — that total white coverage produces a clean verdict — carries extraordinary typological weight when read through the lens of Romans 3. Paul's diagnosis of universal human sinfulness ('all have sinned,' Rom 3:23; 'there is none righteous,' Rom 3:10) is the spiritual analog: the universality of sin does not make it less serious, but it does establish the precondition for universal grace. Just as the person uniformly white from head to foot receives a paradoxical clean verdict, so the person who fully acknowledges total sinfulness is the one positioned to receive total grace. The Calvinist doctrine of total depravity is not a counsel of despair but the precondition for total grace — the one who sees the disease covering all his flesh is the one who can cry out for complete healing. Compare: 'It is all turned white: he is clean' with 'where sin abounded, grace did much more abound' (Rom 5:20).

verses.kjv_text: But when raw flesh appeareth in him, he shall be unclean.
verses.original_text: וּבְיוֹם הֵרָאוֹת בּוֹ בָּשָׂר חַי יִטְמָא׃
verses.transliteration: U-ve-yom hera'ot bo basar chai yittama.
verses.study_note: The counterpart to verse 13 now states the critical exception: as soon as raw living flesh (basar chai) becomes visible, the person becomes unclean. The 'raw flesh' criterion is the pivot of the entire case-law for chronic tzara'at: without it, total white coverage is clean; with it, the person is unclean. The day (yom) on which raw flesh appears marks the transition from clean to unclean status — a temporal precision reflecting the immediate effect of the visible criterion. The raw flesh is the sign that the disease is actively penetrating and breaking down the body's integrity. The boundary between clean and unclean turns on the presence or absence of this one visible indicator.
verses.doctrinal_note: The raw flesh (basar chai) as the indicator of active uncleanness provides a theological key for understanding the nature of sin as defilement. Raw flesh exposed where it should not be represents the disruption of proper boundaries — what should be interior becomes exterior, the covering is broken, the hidden is exposed in the wrong way. This is sin's fundamental structure: the violation of divinely ordained boundaries. The one who becomes unclean the day raw flesh appears is an image of the soul in whom sin becomes actively expressed and visible — moving from latent condition to manifest defilement. The priest's role in detecting this is a type of the Spirit's convicting work (John 16:8) — identifying and declaring the active presence of sin.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall see the raw flesh, and pronounce him to be unclean: for the raw flesh is unclean: it is a leprosy.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַבָּשָׂר הַחַי וְטִמְּאוֹ הַבָּשָׂר הַחַי טָמֵא הוּא צָרַעַת הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen et ha-basar ha-chai ve-timme'o ha-basar ha-chai tame hu tzara'at hi.
verses.study_note: The threefold repetition of 'raw flesh' (basar chai — three times in vv.15-16) creates a rhetorical emphasis underscoring the importance of this single criterion. The explanation is given for the verdict: 'for the raw flesh is unclean: it is a leprosy' — the reasoning (ki tame hu, tzara'at hi) is appended to justify the pronouncement. The Levitical law is not arbitrary but reasoned; the priest can explain why he renders the verdict he does. The chain of logic is: raw flesh is seen → raw flesh is (by definition) unclean → this is tzara'at → he is unclean. The diagnostic syllogism is explicit and transparent.

verses.kjv_text: Or if the raw flesh turn again, and be changed unto white, he shall come unto the priest;
verses.original_text: אוֹ כִי יָשׁוּב הַבָּשָׂר הַחַי וְנֶהְפַּךְ לְלָבָן וּבָא אֶל הַכֹּהֵן׃
verses.transliteration: O ki yashuv ha-basar ha-chai ve-nehpakh le-lavan u-va el ha-kohen.
verses.study_note: The law makes provision for the reverse case: if the raw flesh that previously condemned the person as unclean subsequently heals and turns white, the person must return to the priest for re-examination. The verb yashuv (to return, to turn back) carries its full range of meaning — the healing of the flesh is itself a 'return,' a turning back from the exposed and vulnerable condition. The whitening (nehpakh le-lavan) reverses the condition that caused uncleanness. This provision establishes that the clean/unclean verdict is not permanent or irreversible — conditions change, and the law tracks those changes. The priest must be consulted at each transition.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall see him: and, behold, if the plague be turned into white; then the priest shall pronounce him clean that hath the plague: he is clean.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָהוּ הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה נֶהְפַּךְ הַנֶּגַע לְלָבָן וְטִהַר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַנֶּגַע טָהוֹר הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ahu ha-kohen ve-hinneh nehpakh ha-nega le-lavan ve-tihar ha-kohen et ha-nega tahor hu.
verses.study_note: The priest's examination of the healed condition yields the clean verdict: the plague has 'turned to white' and the priest pronounces clean. 'He is clean' (tahor hu) — the reversal of the unclean verdict of verse 15. The law here models spiritual restoration: the condition that produced uncleanness has been transformed; the diagnosis has changed; the verdict must change with it. This revisability of the priestly verdict in response to genuine change is a pastoral provision that neither permanently condemns the recoverable nor prematurely acquits the still-diseased. Every change in condition merits fresh examination and fresh verdict.

verses.kjv_text: The flesh also, in which, even in the skin thereof, was a boil, and is healed,
verses.original_text: וּבָשָׂר כִּי יִהְיֶה בוֹ בְעוֹרוֹ שְׁחִין וְנִרְפָּא׃
verses.transliteration: U-vasar ki yihyeh vo be-oro shechin ve-nirpa.
verses.study_note: A new case-section begins: tzara'at arising at the site of a healed boil (shechin). The boil (shechin) is a pustular skin infection, the same word used for the sixth plague upon Egypt (Exod 9:10-11) and the affliction of Job (Job 2:7). The fact that this section opens with the boil's healing (ve-nirpa) before any discussion of new eruption establishes the timeline: the boil has healed and the skin is recovering, but something new appears at the former site. This case-law tracks the specific vulnerability of skin that has already been compromised by a prior wound — healed tissue may still be a site of subsequent eruptions requiring priestly examination.

verses.kjv_text: And in the place of the boil there be a white rising, or a bright spot, white, and somewhat reddish, and it be shewed to the priest;
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה בִּמְקוֹם הַשְּׁחִין שְׂאֵת לְבָנָה אוֹ בַהֶרֶת לְבָנָה אֲדַמְדָּמֶת וְנִרְאָה אֶל הַכֹּהֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-hayah bi-mqom hash-shechin se'et levana o baheret levana adamdamet ve-nir'ah el ha-kohen.
verses.study_note: At the site of the healed boil, one of two new conditions may appear: a white swelling (se'et levana) or a white and reddish bright spot (baheret levana adamdamet). The addition of reddish coloration (adamdamet — from adom, red) to the white is a new diagnostic feature appearing here and in the burn-site case (v.24). The combination of white and red may represent an inflamed or partially healed eruption. The person must show himself to the priest (ve-nir'ah el ha-kohen) — the same niphal of ra'ah used for divine appearances throughout the narrative, now applied to the priestly examination. The priest's gaze parallels the divine gaze: seeing and discerning the reality beneath the surface.

verses.kjv_text: And if, when the priest seeth it, behold, it be in sight lower than the skin, and the hair thereof be turned white; the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a plague of leprosy broken out of the boil.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה מַרְאֶהָ שָׁפָל מִן הָעוֹר וּשְׂעָרָהּ הָפַךְ לָבָן וְטִמְּאוֹ הַכֹּהֵן נֶגַע צָרַעַת הִוא בַּשְּׁחִין פָּרָחָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen ve-hinneh mar'eha shafal min ha-or u-se'arah hafakh lavan ve-timme'o ha-kohen nega tzara'at hi ba-shechin parachah.
verses.study_note: The diagnostic criteria for the post-boil eruption to be condemned as tzara'at are the same as the original standard of verse 3: the appearance is lower (shafal) than the skin surface and the hair has turned white. Where verse 3 used amoq (deep) for the appearance below skin level, here shafal (low, lowly, humble) is used — a word that in other contexts describes humility before God (Isa 57:15). The vertical dimension — whether the condition rises, sits level with, or sinks below the skin — is consistently the most definitive spatial indicator. The verdict: 'it is a plague of leprosy broken out of the boil' (nega tzara'at hi ba-shechin parachah) — the boil has become the site of genuine tzara'at.

verses.kjv_text: But if the priest look on it, and, behold, there be no white hairs therein, and if it be not lower than the skin, but be somewhat dark; then the priest shall shut him up seven days:
verses.original_text: וְאִם יִרְאֶנָּה הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה אֵין בָּהּ שֵׂעָר לָבָן וּשְׁפָלָה אֵינֶנָּה מִן הָעוֹר וְהִיא כֵהָה וְהִסְגִּירוֹ הַכֹּהֵן שִׁבְעַת יָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im yir'ennah ha-kohen ve-hinneh ein bah se'ar lavan u-shefal'ah einenah min ha-or ve-hi kehah ve-hisgiro ha-kohen shiv'at yamim.
verses.study_note: The ambiguous post-boil case: no white hair, not lower than the skin, and the spot is somewhat dark (kehah). This mirrors the quarantine case of verse 4: when the full diagnostic criteria for tzara'at are not met, the seven-day quarantine is ordered. The three negatives ('no white hair,' 'not lower,' and the fading quality of 'dark') all point away from tzara'at, yet the eruption's presence at a compromised site (the former boil) warrants caution. The priest's judgment is neither reckless toward cleanness nor reckless toward condemnation — the seven days allow the ambiguous case to resolve.

verses.kjv_text: And if it spread much abroad in the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a plague.
verses.original_text: וְאִם פָּשֹׂה תִפְשֶׂה בָּעוֹר וְטִמֵּא הַכֹּהֵן אֹתוֹ נֶגַע הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im pasoh tifseh ba-or ve-timme' ha-kohen oto nega hi.
verses.study_note: After the seven-day quarantine, if the ambiguous spot spreads, the verdict of uncleanness follows. The emphatic 'spreading it shall spread' (pasoh tifseh) again signals the definitive, undeniable character of the growth. The verdict here is abbreviated: 'it is a plague' (nega hi) rather than 'it is a plague of tzara'at' (nega tzara'at hi) — perhaps indicating a category of spreading eruption that does not require the full tzara'at designation but still requires the unclean verdict. The critical diagnostic indicator throughout the chapter is always growth and spread: what is stable may be clean; what spreads is dangerous.

verses.kjv_text: But if the bright spot stay in his place, and spread not, it is a burning boil; and the priest shall pronounce him clean.
verses.original_text: וְאִם תַּחְתֶּיהָ תַּעֲמֹד הַבַּהֶרֶת לֹא פָשָׂתָה צָרֶבֶת הַשְּׁחִין הִוא וְטִהֲרוֹ הַכֹּהֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im tachteha ta'amod ha-baheret lo fasatah tzarevet hash-shechin hi ve-tiharo ha-kohen.
verses.study_note: The clean verdict for the non-spreading post-boil spot: if it remains stationary (ta'amod, stands in its place), it is merely the 'scar of the boil' (tzarevet hash-shechin) — a remnant mark of the healed wound, not active tzara'at. The noun tzarevet (scar, cicatrix) appears only here and in verse 28 in the Hebrew Bible. A scar is the body's own record of healing — it is not disease but its memorial. The law distinguishes between living disease (active, spreading) and its healed residue. The clean verdict restores the person who has a post-wound scar but no active disease — the record of past illness does not permanently condemn.

verses.kjv_text: Or if there be any flesh, in the skin whereof there is a hot burning, and the quick flesh that burneth have a white bright spot, somewhat reddish, or white;
verses.original_text: אוֹ בָשָׂר כִּי יִהְיֶה בְעוֹרוֹ מִכְוַת אֵשׁ וְהָיְתָה מִחְיַת הַמִּכְוָה בַּהֶרֶת לְבָנָה אֲדַמְדָּמֶת אוֹ לְבָנָה׃
verses.transliteration: O vasar ki yihyeh be-oro mikhvat esh ve-hayetah michyat ha-mikhvah baheret levana adamdamet o levana.
verses.study_note: A parallel case-section begins: tzara'at arising at the site of a burn wound (mikhvat esh — a burning of fire). The burn creates the same vulnerable skin surface as the boil — a site of prior trauma where new eruptions must be examined. The diagnostic presentation is identical to the boil case: a white and reddish bright spot or simply white. This structural parallelism between boil and burn cases suggests the law's systematic comprehensiveness: every type of prior skin trauma that creates a vulnerable area is addressed. The body's history of wounds becomes sites requiring ongoing priestly vigilance.

verses.kjv_text: Then the priest shall look upon it: and, behold, if the hair in the bright spot be turned white, and it be in sight deeper than the skin; it is a leprosy broken out of the burning: wherefore the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is the plague of leprosy.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה אֹתָהּ הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה נֶהְפַּךְ שֵׂעָר לָבָן בַּבַּהֶרֶת וּמַרְאֶהָ עָמֹק מִן הָעוֹר צָרַעַת הִוא בַּמִּכְוָה פָּרָחָה וְטִמֵּא אֹתוֹ הַכֹּהֵן נֶגַע צָרַעַת הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah otah ha-kohen ve-hinneh nehpakh se'ar lavan ba-baheret u-mar'eha amoq min ha-or tzara'at hi ba-mikhvah parachah ve-timme' oto ha-kohen nega tzara'at hi.
verses.study_note: The diagnostic criteria for tzara'at at the burn site are identical to the original standard: white hair + apparent depth below skin surface = tzara'at. The verdict formula is doubled for emphasis: 'it is a leprosy' (tzara'at hi) followed by 'it is the plague of leprosy' (nega tzara'at hi) — the repetition creates a rhetorical solemnity around the pronouncement. The parallelism between the boil section (vv.18-23) and the burn section (vv.24-28) is now apparent: the same diagnostic standards apply regardless of the predisposing trauma. The law is equitable and consistent — wound history does not change the diagnostic criteria, only the clinical context.

verses.kjv_text: But if the priest look on it, and, behold, there be no white hair in the bright spot, and it be no lower than the other skin, but be somewhat dark; then the priest shall shut him up seven days:
verses.original_text: וְאִם יִרְאֶנָּה הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה אֵין בַּבַּהֶרֶת שֵׂעָר לָבָן וּשְׁפָלָה אֵינֶנָּה מִן הָעוֹר וְהִיא כֵהָה וְהִסְגִּירוֹ הַכֹּהֵן שִׁבְעַת יָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im yir'ennah ha-kohen ve-hinneh ein ba-baheret se'ar lavan u-shefal'ah einenah min ha-or ve-hi kehah ve-hisgiro ha-kohen shiv'at yamim.
verses.study_note: The ambiguous burn-site case mirrors the ambiguous boil-site case of verse 21, using nearly identical language. No white hair, not deeper than the skin, somewhat dark — the borderline presentation receives the seven-day quarantine. This repetition across the two parallel sections reinforces the equitable consistency of the priestly diagnostic system. The law is not merely a series of individual rulings but a coherent system built on repeatable principles: ambiguity → quarantine → re-examination → definitive verdict. The parallelism itself is a feature of the law's literary architecture, demonstrating that the divine legislation is orderly, systematic, and fair.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall look upon him the seventh day: and if it be spread much abroad in the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is the plague of leprosy.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָהוּ הַכֹּהֵן בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי אִם פָּשֹׂה תִפְשֶׂה בָּעוֹר וְטִמֵּא הַכֹּהֵן אֹתוֹ נֶגַע צָרַעַת הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ahu ha-kohen bayyom hash-shevi'i im pasoh tifseh ba-or ve-timme' ha-kohen oto nega tzara'at hi.
verses.study_note: On the seventh day, if the ambiguous burn-site spot has spread, the unclean verdict is rendered: 'it is the plague of leprosy' (nega tzara'at hi). The parallelism with verse 22 (the spreading boil-site case) is complete. The chapter's diagnostic system at this point has demonstrated a fully consistent legal framework: ambiguous cases are quarantined, spreading cases are condemned, stable cases require further examination or clean verdicts. The repeatability and predictability of these criteria is itself a form of divine equity — everyone is subject to the same standards, examined by the same procedures, and receives verdicts based on the same observable criteria.

verses.kjv_text: And if the bright spot stay in his place, and spread not in the skin, but it be somewhat dark; it is a rising of the burning, and the priest shall pronounce him clean: for it is an inflammation of the burning.
verses.original_text: וְאִם תַּחְתֶּיהָ תַּעֲמֹד הַבַּהֶרֶת לֹא פָשְׂתָה בָעוֹר וְהִוא כֵהָה שְׂאֵת הַמִּכְוָה הִוא וְטִהֲרוֹ הַכֹּהֵן כִּי צָרֶבֶת הַמִּכְוָה הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im tachteha ta'amod ha-baheret lo faseta ba-or ve-hi kehah se'et ha-mikhvah hi ve-tiharo ha-kohen ki tzarevet ha-mikhvah hi.
verses.study_note: The clean verdict for the non-spreading burn-site spot mirrors verse 23 exactly: stability + fading = clean. The spot is identified as merely the 'rising of the burning' (se'et ha-mikhvah) — the inflammatory residue of the burn wound healing — and then more specifically as the 'scar of the burning' (tzarevet ha-mikhvah). The diagnostic and pastoral logic is identical to the boil case: a stable, fading mark is the wound's scar, not active tzara'at. The clean verdict restores the person to full community participation. The chapter's repeated clean verdicts remind the reader that the system is not designed primarily to exclude but to accurately identify and, wherever possible, to restore.

verses.kjv_text: If a man or woman have a plague upon the head or the beard;
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אוֹ אִשָּׁה כִּי יִהְיֶה בוֹ נֶגַע בְּרֹאשׁ אוֹ בְזָקָן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish o ishah ki yihyeh vo nega be-rosh o ve-zaqan.
verses.study_note: A new case-section addresses tzara'at specifically on the head (rosh) or beard/chin (zaqan). Notably this section explicitly includes both men and women (ish o ishah) — tzara'at is an equal-opportunity defilement, and the diagnostic law applies regardless of sex. The head and beard are specialized areas requiring their own sub-set of diagnostic criteria (scall/netaq conditions, vv.30-37) distinct from the general skin eruption cases. The beard (zaqan) in Israelite culture was a symbol of dignity and honor (Ps 133:2); its affliction would be both physically and socially significant.

verses.kjv_text: Then the priest shall see the plague: and, behold, if it be in sight deeper than the skin; and there be in it a yellow thin hair; then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a dry scall, even a leprosy upon the head or beard.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַנֶּגַע וְהִנֵּה מַרְאֵהוּ עָמֹק מִן הָעוֹר וּבוֹ שֵׂעָר צָהֹב דַּק וְטִמֵּא אֹתוֹ הַכֹּהֵן נֶתֶק הוּא צָרַעַת הָרֹאשׁ אוֹ הַזָּקָן הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen et ha-nega ve-hinneh mar'ehu amoq min ha-or u-vo se'ar tzahov daq ve-timme' oto ha-kohen neteq hu tzara'at ha-rosh o ha-zaqan hu.
verses.study_note: For head and beard conditions, the diagnostic criteria shift: instead of white hair, it is 'yellow thin hair' (se'ar tzahov daq) that signals tzara'at. The yellow (tzahov) and thin (daq) quality of hair at the lesion site indicates the specialized condition called netaq — the scall, a scalp/beard condition distinct from the general skin tzara'at but classified under the same umbrella. The yellowness of hair may indicate the absence of normal pigmentation and structural integrity. This condition is named 'neteq' (H5424), from nataqa, to tear or pull away — the hair or skin is 'torn' from its normal state. The diagnostic principle (apparent depth + affected hair) is consistent with the general tzara'at criteria.

verses.kjv_text: And if the priest look on the plague of the scall, and, behold, it be not in sight deeper than the skin, and that there is no black hair in it; then the priest shall shut up him that hath the plague of the scall seven days:
verses.original_text: וְכִי יִרְאֶה הַכֹּהֵן אֶת נֶגַע הַנֶּתֶק וְהִנֵּה אֵין מַרְאֵהוּ עָמֹק מִן הָעוֹר וְשֵׂעָר שָׁחֹר אֵין בּוֹ וְהִסְגִּיר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת נֶגַע הַנֶּתֶק שִׁבְעַת יָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-khi yir'eh ha-kohen et nega ha-neteq ve-hinneh ein mar'ehu amoq min ha-or ve-se'ar shachor ein bo ve-hisgir ha-kohen et nega ha-neteq shiv'at yamim.
verses.study_note: The ambiguous scall case introduces a unique criterion: the presence or absence of 'black hair' (se'ar shachor). For the scalp and beard, black hair is the indicator of normal, healthy hair — its absence (alongside no apparent depth) constitutes the borderline case requiring quarantine. The contrast between black (normal) and yellow (diseased) hair in the neteq cases is the specialized analog to the white/normal hair contrast in general tzara'at. The seven-day quarantine is applied again — the same pastoral patience governing all ambiguous cases. The diagnostic system's adaptability to the specific region of the body (scalp/beard) demonstrates its comprehensiveness.

verses.kjv_text: And in the seventh day the priest shall look on the plague: and, behold, if the scall spread not, and there be in it no yellow hair, and the scall be not in sight deeper than the skin;
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַנֶּגַע בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי וְהִנֵּה לֹא פָשָׂה הַנֶּתֶק וְלֹא הָיָה בוֹ שֵׂעָר צָהֹב וּמַרְאֵה הַנֶּתֶק אֵין עָמֹק מִן הָעוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen et ha-nega bayyom hash-shevi'i ve-hinneh lo fasah ha-neteq ve-lo hayah vo se'ar tzahov u-mar'eh ha-neteq ein amoq min ha-or.
verses.study_note: On the seventh day, if the scall has not spread (lo fasah), has no yellow hair (lo hayah vo se'ar tzahov), and is not apparently deeper than the skin (ein amoq min ha-or) — all three criteria pointing away from active neteq-tzara'at — the case remains ambiguous enough to require further action. Three negatives build the case for potential cleanness, but the absence of the condemned indicators is not yet sufficient for the clean verdict; the procedure requires a shaving and second quarantine (vv.33-34). The thoroughness of the neteq procedure reflects the difficulty of diagnosing scalp conditions where hair itself complicates visual examination.

verses.kjv_text: He shall be shaven, but the scall shall he not shave; and the priest shall shut up him that hath the scall seven days more:
verses.original_text: וְהִתְגַּלָּח וְאֶת הַנֶּתֶק לֹא יְגַלֵּחַ וְהִסְגִּיר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַנֶּתֶק שִׁבְעַת יָמִים שֵׁנִית׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-hitgallach ve-et ha-neteq lo yegallach ve-hisgir ha-kohen et ha-neteq shiv'at yamim shenit.
verses.study_note: The unique procedure for the ambiguous scall case: the person is to shave the hair around the affected area but must not shave the scall itself (et ha-neteq lo yegallach). Then a second seven-day quarantine follows. The shaving of surrounding hair makes the scall area more clearly visible for re-examination, removing the confounding factor of surrounding hair. But the scall itself is left unshaved because shaving it could remove the diagnostic indicators (yellow hair) needed for re-examination. This procedural nuance — shave around but not over the lesion — reflects a sophisticated understanding of the examination's requirements. The diagnosis must be preserved in its natural state for the most accurate re-evaluation.

verses.kjv_text: And in the seventh day the priest shall look on the scall: and, behold, if the scall be not spread in the skin, nor be in sight deeper than the skin; then the priest shall pronounce him clean: and he shall wash his clothes, and be clean.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַנֶּתֶק בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי וְהִנֵּה לֹא פָשָׂה הַנֶּתֶק בָּעוֹר וְאֵין מַרְאֵהוּ עָמֹק מִן הָעוֹר וְטִהַר אֹתוֹ הַכֹּהֵן וְכִבֶּס בְּגָדָיו וְטָהֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen et ha-neteq bayyom hash-shevi'i ve-hinneh lo fasah ha-neteq ba-or ve-ein mar'ehu amoq min ha-or ve-tihar oto ha-kohen ve-kibbes begadav ve-taher.
verses.study_note: After the second quarantine, if the scall has not spread and does not appear deeper than the skin, the clean verdict is given. The procedure concludes with the same requirement as verse 6: the person washes his garments (kibbes begadav) and is clean. The washing of garments at the conclusion of an examination period acknowledges that the quarantine itself carried some degree of ritual uncertainty that requires a minor purification act at the boundary of re-entry. The double quarantine procedure for scall cases (vv.31-34) mirrors the general double quarantine of verses 4-6, demonstrating the chapter's structural consistency across its various case-sections.

verses.kjv_text: But if the scall spread much in the skin after his cleansing;
verses.original_text: וְאִם פָּשֹׂה יִפְשֶׂה הַנֶּתֶק בָּעוֹר אַחֲרֵי טָהֳרָתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im pasoh yifseh ha-neteq ba-or acharei toharato.
verses.study_note: The post-clean-verdict relapse case: if the scall spreads after the person has received the clean verdict, he must return. The phrase 'after his cleansing' (acharei toharato) is legally significant — the clean verdict has already been formally given, yet the subsequent deterioration requires re-examination. No verdict is irrevocably final when the underlying condition changes. This legal provision prevents the abuse of a formally obtained clean verdict as a permanent shield against further examination. The law tracks reality: if the condition was genuinely clean when examined and then deteriorates, the deterioration creates a new situation requiring fresh priestly discernment.

verses.kjv_text: Then the priest shall look on him: and, behold, if the scall be spread in the skin, the priest shall not seek for yellow hair; he is unclean.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָהוּ הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה פָּשָׂה הַנֶּתֶק בָּעוֹר לֹא יְבַקֵּר הַכֹּהֵן לַשֵּׂעָר הַצָּהֹב טָמֵא הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ahu ha-kohen ve-hinneh fasah ha-neteq ba-or lo yevaqer ha-kohen la-se'ar ha-tzahov tame hu.
verses.study_note: When the priest examines the relapsing scall and finds it has spread, he does not need to search for the yellow-hair criterion — the spreading is itself sufficient for the unclean verdict: 'he is unclean' (tame hu). The verb 'shall not seek' (lo yevaqer, from baqar, to investigate, to seek diligently) indicates that the spreading condition makes further diagnostic investigation unnecessary. This is the one case in the neteq section where the yellow-hair criterion is set aside because spreading has already established uncleanness beyond reasonable doubt. The law is economical: where clear evidence exists, it does not require superfluous additional criteria.

verses.kjv_text: But if the scall be in his sight at a stay, and that there is black hair grown up therein; the scall is healed, he is clean: and the priest shall pronounce him clean.
verses.original_text: וְאִם בְּעֵינָיו עָמַד הַנֶּתֶק וְשֵׂעָר שָׁחֹר צָמַח בּוֹ נִרְפָּא הַנֶּתֶק טָהוֹר הוּא וְטִהֲרוֹ הַכֹּהֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im be-einav amad ha-neteq ve-se'ar shachor tzamach bo nirpa ha-neteq tahor hu ve-tiharo ha-kohen.
verses.study_note: The clean verdict for the relapsing-but-now-healing scall: the condition is stable (amad — stands still, has not spread) AND black hair has grown in it (se'ar shachor tzamach bo). The regrowth of black hair is the definitive sign of healing — normal pigmented hair returning to the affected area signals the restoration of healthy skin. The verb tzamach (to sprout, to grow, to spring up) is used elsewhere for plant growth and metaphorically for the 'sprouting' of the Messiah (Isa 4:2; Jer 23:5; Zech 3:8; 6:12 — 'the Branch'). The declaration is threefold: 'the scall is healed' (nirpa ha-neteq), 'he is clean' (tahor hu), and 'the priest shall pronounce him clean' (ve-tiharo ha-kohen) — an unusually emphatic triple clean-verdict.

verses.kjv_text: If a man also or a woman have in the skin of their flesh bright spots, even white bright spots;
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אוֹ אִשָּׁה כִּי יִהְיֶה בְעוֹר בְּשָׂרָם בֶּהָרֹת בֶּהָרֹת לְבָנֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish o ishah ki yihyeh be-or besaram beharot beharot levanot.
verses.study_note: Another case: bright spots (beharot — plural of baheret) appearing in men or women. The doubled beharot (bright spots, bright spots) creates a distributive plural — multiple spots scattered on the skin. The explicit 'man or woman' again establishes gender-equal application of the diagnostic law. This case is immediately distinguished from tzara'at by the following verse's clean verdict, but the person must still be examined. The mere presence of white spots requires priestly consultation — the community cannot self-diagnose or ignore visible changes to the skin surface.

verses.kjv_text: Then the priest shall look: and, behold, if the bright spots in the skin of their flesh be darkish white; it is a freckled spot that groweth in the skin; he is clean.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה בְעוֹר בְּשָׂרָם בֶּהָרֹת כֵּהוֹת לְבָנֹת בֹּהַק הוּא פָּרַח בָּעוֹר טָהוֹר הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen ve-hinneh be-or besaram beharot kehot levanot bohaq hu parach ba-or tahor hu.
verses.study_note: The priest examines the scattered white spots and finds them to be 'darkish white' (kehot levanot — faded white, dull white) rather than the brilliant baheret associated with tzara'at. The condition is identified as bohaq — a benign skin condition, possibly vitiligo or simple hypopigmentation — translated 'freckled spot' in the KJV. 'He is clean' — the bohaq condition, despite being a visible skin anomaly, does not fall within the tzara'at category. This is a pastoral provision for conditions that are cosmetically notable but not ritually defiling. Not every visible skin change is dangerous; not every anomaly is tzara'at. The priest's expertise distinguishes the dangerous from the benign.

verses.kjv_text: And the man whose hair is fallen off his head, he is bald; yet is he clean.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ כִּי יִמָּרֵט רֹאשׁוֹ קֵרֵחַ הוּא טָהוֹר הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish ki yimmaret rosho qereach hu tahor hu.
verses.study_note: The simple baldness case: a man whose hair has fallen out from the top of his head (qereach — bald on the crown) is declared clean. Natural male-pattern baldness is not tzara'at. The deliberate inclusion of this provision prevents the misapplication of the tzara'at laws to natural hair loss. The doubled 'he... he' (hu tahor hu) creates mild rhetorical emphasis, as if anticipating an anxious question: yes, ordinary baldness is clean. Qorach, the rebel of Numbers 16, shares this root for baldness — a coincidence that may carry ironic resonance for later readers.

verses.kjv_text: And he that hath his hair fallen off from the part of his head toward his face, he is forehead bald: yet is he clean.
verses.original_text: וְאִם מִפְּאַת פָּנָיו יִמָּרֵט רֹאשׁוֹ גִּבֵּחַ הוּא טָהוֹר הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im mi-pe'at panav yimmaret rosho gibbeach hu tahor hu.
verses.study_note: Frontal baldness (gibbeach — from the forehead area, 'from the part of his face') also receives the clean verdict: 'he is clean.' The law distinguishes between two types of natural baldness — crown baldness (qereach, v.40) and frontal baldness (gibbeach) — and declares both clean. The careful distinction between the two types of natural hair loss demonstrates the precision of the diagnostic system, but also its pastoral intent: all forms of natural hair loss are explicitly covered and declared clean, protecting individuals from false tzara'at diagnosis. The law anticipates and forecloses misapplication.

verses.kjv_text: And if there be in the bald head, or bald forehead, a white reddish sore; it is a leprosy sprung up in his bald head, or his bald forehead.
verses.original_text: וְכִי יִהְיֶה בַקָּרַחַת אוֹ בַגַּבַּחַת נֶגַע לָבָן אֲדַמְדָּם צָרַעַת פֹּרַחַת הִוא בְּקָרַחְתּוֹ אוֹ בְגַבַּחְתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-khi yihyeh va-qarrachat o va-gabbachat nega lavan adamdam tzara'at porachat hi be-qarachto o ve-gabbachto.
verses.study_note: The clean verdict for natural baldness is immediately qualified: if a white-reddish eruption (nega lavan adamdam) appears on the bald area, that is a different matter — 'it is a leprosy sprung up in his bald head' (tzara'at porachat hi be-qarachto). The 'blooming' (porachat) of tzara'at on a bald scalp is the specific condition that transforms a clean baldness into an unclean condition. The bald head and bald forehead, while themselves clean, are not immune from tzara'at eruptions. Natural baldness removes the hair-color criteria (white/yellow hair) but the white-reddish lesion in those areas provides its own diagnostic criterion.

verses.kjv_text: Then the priest shall look upon it: and, behold, if the rising of the sore be white reddish in his bald head, or in his bald forehead, as the leprosy appeareth in the skin of the flesh;
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה אֹתוֹ הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה שְׂאֵת הַנֶּגַע לְבָנָה אֲדַמְדֶּמֶת בְּקָרַחְתּוֹ אוֹ בְגַבַּחְתּוֹ כְּמַרְאֵה צָרַעַת עוֹר בָּשָׂר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah oto ha-kohen ve-hinneh se'et ha-nega levana adamdemet be-qarachto o ve-gabbachto ke-mar'eh tzara'at or basar.
verses.study_note: The diagnostic standard for baldness-area tzara'at is explicitly compared to the skin tzara'at criteria: 'as the leprosy appeareth in the skin of the flesh' (ke-mar'eh tzara'at or basar). The visual profile — the raised, white-reddish swelling — mirrors what tzara'at looks like on normal skin, confirming continuity of the diagnostic category despite the different anatomical location. This cross-reference to the standard skin criteria anchors the baldness-case laws within the same diagnostic framework as the chapter's earlier sections, maintaining the law's unified character across its multiple sub-cases.

verses.kjv_text: He is a leprous man, he is unclean: the priest shall pronounce him utterly unclean; his plague is in his head.
verses.original_text: צָרוּעַ הוּא טָמֵא הוּא טַמֵּא יְטַמְּאֶנּוּ הַכֹּהֵן בְּרֹאשׁוֹ נִגְעוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Tzaru'a hu tame hu tamme yetamme'ennu ha-kohen be-rosho nig'o.
verses.study_note: The verdict for baldness-area tzara'at is the most emphatic in the chapter: the triple declaration 'he is a leper, he is unclean, the priest shall pronounce him utterly unclean' (tzaru'a hu, tame hu, tamme yetamme'ennu) — three statements stacking upon one another. The piel intensive tamme yetamme'ennu is the grammatical superlative of unclean-pronouncement: 'pronouncing he shall pronounce him unclean' — the doubling of the verb root to express absolute certainty and finality. The specification 'his plague is in his head' (be-rosho nig'o) locates the defilement at the person's highest point — the head, the seat of thought and identity. Tzara'at at the head carries a special gravity.

verses.kjv_text: And the leper in whom the plague is, his clothes shall be rent, and his head bare, and he shall put a covering upon his upper lip, and shall cry, Unclean, unclean.
verses.original_text: וְהַצָּרוּעַ אֲשֶׁר בּוֹ הַנֶּגַע בְּגָדָיו יִהְיוּ פְרֻמִים וְרֹאשׁוֹ יִהְיֶה פָרוּעַ וְעַל שָׂפָם יַעְטֶה וְטָמֵא טָמֵא יִקְרָא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ha-tzaru'a asher bo ha-nega begadav yihyu ferumim ve-rosho yihyeh faru'a ve-al safam ya'ateh ve-tame tame yiqra.
verses.study_note: The most poignant verse in the chapter describes the required behavior of the declared leper. Four requirements: (1) torn garments (begadav ferumim) — the mourning posture associated with grief and loss (Gen 37:29; Job 1:20; Ezra 9:3); (2) uncovered head (rosho faru'a) — disheveled hair, the antithesis of priestly dignity (Lev 10:6; 21:10); (3) covered upper lip (al safam ya'ateh) — a covering over the beard/mouth area, visually marking the unclean person and preventing contamination of breath; (4) the cry 'Unclean! Unclean!' (tame tame yiqra) — a public warning shout, the most socially devastating requirement. The doubled 'unclean, unclean' mirrors the doubled verdict formula — the person becomes the verbal proclamation of his own condition, warning all who approach.
verses.doctrinal_note: The leper's required self-declaration — 'Unclean! Unclean!' — is one of the most theologically rich images in Leviticus. It anticipates two converging New Testament realities. First, it prefigures the leper's approach to Jesus: by approaching at all, the leper of Matthew 8 and Mark 1 has violated the requirement of verse 46 (staying outside the camp) and verse 45 (crying 'unclean' as a warning). His violation of the protocol to approach Jesus is itself a desperate act of faith — he has nowhere else to go. Second, it foreshadows Isaiah's response to the divine theophany: 'Woe is me! for I am undone; because I am a man of unclean lips' (Isa 6:5). The prophet's self-declaration of uncleanness ('unclean lips') before the Holy One echoes the leper's doubled 'tame tame' — awareness of one's own defilement is the precondition for the divine touch that makes clean (Isa 6:7; Matt 8:3).

verses.kjv_text: All the days wherein the plague shall be in him he shall be defiled; he is unclean: he shall dwell alone; without the camp shall his habitation be.
verses.original_text: כָּל יְמֵי אֲשֶׁר הַנֶּגַע בּוֹ יִטְמָא טָמֵא הוּא בָּדָד יֵשֵׁב מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה מוֹשָׁבוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Kol yemei asher ha-nega vo yittama tame hu badad yeshev mi-chutz la-machaneh moshavo.
verses.study_note: The leper's social fate: isolation for the full duration of the condition. 'He shall dwell alone' (badad yeshev) — badad means alone, solitary, isolated. 'Outside the camp shall his habitation be' (mi-chutz la-machaneh moshavo) — excluded from the covenant community, from the tabernacle, from family, from all social life. The phrase 'all the days wherein the plague shall be in him' (kol yemei asher ha-nega vo) indicates that this isolation is indefinite — not a fixed quarantine period but a continuous state until declared clean. This is the most complete expression of what ritual uncleanness means: separation from the holy community that gathered around the divine presence. The leper outside the camp is the image of humanity separated from God by sin — alone, defiled, and without access to the divine dwelling.
verses.doctrinal_note: The leper dwelling 'outside the camp' (mi-chutz la-machaneh) is one of the most theologically freighted spatial metaphors in the Old Testament. It anticipates three converging lines of fulfillment. First, the sin offering's flesh burned outside the camp (Lev 9:11) — Christ bears the defilement of the leper's place. Second, Hebrews 13:11-13 explicitly connects the 'outside the camp' of the sin offering to Christ: 'Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate.' Christ enters the leper's exile to end it. Third, Moses' own prefigurative act: when Miriam was stricken with tzara'at, Moses prayed and she was healed but shut outside the camp seven days (Num 12:14-15) — foreshadowing Israel's own patterns of purification. The leper outside the camp cries 'unclean'; Christ on the cross bears that cry on behalf of all the unclean, so that they may enter the camp of the new creation.

verses.kjv_text: The garment also that the plague of leprosy is in, whether it be a woollen garment, or a linen garment;
verses.original_text: וְהַבֶּגֶד כִּי יִהְיֶה בוֹ נֶגַע צָרָעַת בְּבֶגֶד צֶמֶר אוֹ בְּבֶגֶד פִּשְׁתִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ha-beged ki yihyeh vo nega tzara'at be-veged tzemer o be-veged pishtim.
verses.study_note: The chapter now extends the tzara'at laws from human skin to garments (vv.47-59). The application of the same term (nega tzara'at) to fabric is theologically significant: the principle of defilement is not limited to the human body but can inhabit objects in the human environment. Two primary fabric types are mentioned: woollen (tzemer) and linen (pishtim) garments. These are the two primary fabrics of the tabernacle itself (Exod 35:6; 39:28) — the extension of tzara'at law to garments connects the household and the sanctuary under the same purity principles. The comprehensive scope of tzara'at — from skin to fabric — reflects the pervasive, boundary-crossing nature of defilement.

verses.kjv_text: Whether it be in the warp, or woof; of linen, or of woollen; whether in a skin, or in any thing made of skin;
verses.original_text: אוֹ בִשְׁתִי אוֹ בְעֵרֶב לַפִּשְׁתִּים וְלַצָּמֶר אוֹ בְעוֹר אוֹ בְכָל מְלֶאכֶת עוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: O ve-sheti o ve-erev la-pishtim ve-la-tzamer o ve-or o ve-khol melekhet or.
verses.study_note: The scope of fabric examination is expanded: not just whole garments but the individual components of woven cloth — the warp (sheti, the longitudinal threads) and the woof (erev, the transverse threads) — and also leather goods (or) and anything made of leather (kol melekhet or). This specificity ensures that the tzara'at examination covers not just finished products but raw materials and components. The inclusion of warp and woof suggests that even partially woven cloth must be examined. The leather provision extends the law beyond textile to all animal-skin products — bags, sandals, belts, scrolls — the ubiquitous leather goods of the ancient world.

verses.kjv_text: And if the plague be greenish or reddish in the garment, or in the skin, either in the warp, or in the woof, or in any thing of skin; it is a plague of leprosy, and shall be shewed unto the priest:
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה הַנֶּגַע יְרַקְרַק אוֹ אֲדַמְדָּם בַּבֶּגֶד אוֹ בָעוֹר אוֹ בַשְּׁתִי אוֹ בָעֵרֶב אוֹ בְכָל כְּלִי עוֹר נֶגַע צָרַעַת הוּא וְהָרְאָה אֶת הַכֹּהֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-hayah ha-nega yeraqraq o adamdam ba-beged o va-or o va-sheti o va-erev o ve-khol keli or nega tzara'at hu ve-hor'ah et ha-kohen.
verses.study_note: The diagnostic color criterion for garment tzara'at is greenish (yeraqraq) or reddish (adamdam) — distinct from the white/white-reddish criteria for skin tzara'at. The reduplication of both color words (yeraq-raq, adam-dam) creates intensive color adjectives — deeply greenish, thoroughly reddish. These colors correspond to what we would recognize as mold or mildew growth on fabric or leather. The condition is classified as 'nega tzara'at' — using the same plague-terminology as skin conditions — and must be shown to the priest. The same word (nega, stroke) applied to both skin and fabric defilements unifies the category under a single theological principle: defilement is defilement, wherever it manifests.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall look upon the plague, and shut up it that hath the plague seven days:
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַנֶּגַע וְהִסְגִּיר אֶת הַנֶּגַע שִׁבְעַת יָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen et ha-nega ve-hisgir et ha-nega shiv'at yamim.
verses.study_note: The garment examination procedure mirrors the skin examination procedure: after the priest sees the affected item, it is 'shut up' (ve-hisgir — the same quarantine verb used for persons in vv.4,5,11,21,26,31) for seven days. The use of the same verb for quarantining a person and quarantining a garment is significant — it applies the same diagnostic framework and the same pastoral patience to objects as to persons. The garment receives the same seven-day discernment period that an ambiguous skin case receives. The procedural parallelism reinforces the unity of the purity system across different categories of potential defilement.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall look on the plague on the seventh day: if the plague be spread in the garment, either in the warp, or in the woof, or in a skin, or in any work that is made of skin; the plague is a fretting leprosy; it is unclean.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה אֶת הַנֶּגַע בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי כִּי פָשָׂה הַנֶּגַע בַּבֶּגֶד אוֹ בַשְּׁתִי אוֹ בָעֵרֶב אוֹ בָעוֹר לְכֹל אֲשֶׁר יֵעָשֶׂה הָעוֹר לִמְלָאכָה צָרַעַת מַמְאֶרֶת הִוא טְמֵאָה הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah et ha-nega bayyom hash-shevi'i ki fasah ha-nega ba-beged o va-sheti o va-erev o va-or le-khol asher ye'aseh ha-or li-melakhah tzara'at mam'eret hi teme'ah hi.
verses.study_note: After the seven-day quarantine, if the affected area has spread in the garment, warp, woof, or leather, the diagnosis is 'fretting leprosy' (tzara'at mam'eret). The word mam'eret (H3992) comes from a root meaning malignant, corroding, destructive — this is not merely discoloration but an aggressive, spreading defilement that is consuming the material. The verdict 'it is unclean' (teme'ah hi, using the feminine form of tame for the garment) marks the application of the clean/unclean framework to inanimate objects. Things, not only persons, can be tamei. This extension of purity categories to objects anticipates the NT's application of holiness and defilement to all areas of life.

verses.kjv_text: He shall therefore burn that garment, whether warp or woof, in woollen or in linen, or any thing of skin, wherein the plague is: for it is a fretting leprosy; it shall be burnt in the fire.
verses.original_text: וְשָׂרַף אֶת הַבֶּגֶד אוֹ אֶת הַשְּׁתִי אוֹ אֶת הָעֵרֶב בַּצֶּמֶר אוֹ בַפִּשְׁתִּים אוֹ אֶת כָּל כְּלִי הָעוֹר אֲשֶׁר יִהְיֶה בוֹ הַנֶּגַע כִּי צָרַעַת מַמְאֶרֶת הִוא בָּאֵשׁ תִּשָּׂרֵף׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-saraf et ha-beged o et ha-sheti o et ha-erev ba-tzemer o va-pishtim o et kol keli ha-or asher yihyeh vo ha-nega ki tzara'at mam'eret hi ba-esh tissaref.
verses.study_note: The 'fretting leprosy' garment must be burned — a more severe disposal than the washing prescribed for recoverable items. 'It shall be burnt in the fire' (ba-esh tissaref) — total destruction by fire. The same disposal method is used for sin offering remnants (Lev 6:30; 9:11) and for objects contaminated by serious defilement. Fire is the ultimate purifier and the ultimate destroyer; what cannot be washed clean must be consumed. The finality of burning — nothing remains, nothing is recovered — reflects the irremediable nature of mam'eret (fretting/malignant) tzara'at. Some defilements are beyond laundering; they require complete destruction.

verses.kjv_text: And if the priest shall look, and, behold, the plague be not spread in the garment, either in the warp, or in the woof, or in any thing of skin;
verses.original_text: וְאִם יִרְאֶה הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה לֹא פָשָׂה הַנֶּגַע בַּבֶּגֶד אוֹ בַשְּׁתִי אוֹ בָעֵרֶב אוֹ בְכָל כְּלִי עוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im yir'eh ha-kohen ve-hinneh lo fasah ha-nega ba-beged o va-sheti o va-erev o ve-khol keli or.
verses.study_note: The non-spreading case opens the recovery procedure for garments. As with skin tzara'at, the diagnostic pivot is the spreading criterion (fasah): if the mark has not spread after seven days, the garment may be recoverable. The verse functions as the opening of the conditional that continues in verse 54 — the non-spreading condition triggers the washing procedure rather than the burning verdict. The parallel with the human skin examination is exact: stability opens the path toward cleanness; spreading closes it.

verses.kjv_text: Then the priest shall command that they wash the thing wherein the plague is, and he shall shut it up seven days more:
verses.original_text: וְצִוָּה הַכֹּהֵן וְכִבְּסוּ אֵת אֲשֶׁר בּוֹ הַנֶּגַע וְהִסְגִּירוֹ שִׁבְעַת יָמִים שֵׁנִית׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-tzivvah ha-kohen ve-kibbesu et asher bo ha-nega ve-hisgiro shiv'at yamim shenit.
verses.study_note: The non-spreading garment is washed (kibbesu — the laundering verb) and shut up for a second seven-day quarantine period. This mirrors the double quarantine procedure of the ambiguous skin cases (vv.5-6). The washing introduces the possibility of cleanness without yet granting it — the wash may remove the surface coloration but the underlying condition must be re-examined. The priest commands (ve-tzivvah) the washing — priestly authority governs even the domestic action of laundering a garment under examination. Nothing in the purification process is self-administered without priestly oversight.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall look on the plague, after that it is washed: and, behold, if the plague have not changed his colour, and the plague be not spread; it is unclean; thou shalt burn it in the fire; it is fret inward, whether it be bare within or without.
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן אַחֲרֵי הֻכַּבֵּס אֶת הַנֶּגַע וְהִנֵּה לֹא הָפַךְ הַנֶּגַע אֶת עֵינוֹ וְהַנֶּגַע לֹא פָשָׂה טָמֵא הוּא בָּאֵשׁ תִּשְׂרְפֶנּוּ פְּחֶתֶת הִוא בְּקָרַחְתּוֹ אוֹ בְגַבַּחְתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah ha-kohen acharei hukkabbes et ha-nega ve-hinneh lo hafakh ha-nega et eino ve-ha-nega lo fasah tame hu ba-esh tishrepennu pechettet hi be-qarachto o ve-gabbachto.
verses.study_note: After washing, if the plague has 'not changed his colour' (lo hafakh et eino — literally 'has not turned its eye/appearance') and has not spread, it is still unclean and must be burned. This case is subtle: the garment was washed (potential positive) but the plague persisted unchanged (negative indicator). The plague's unchanged appearance after washing indicates it is deeply embedded in the material — it is 'fret inward' (pechettet, H6356) — a term indicating a corrosive, eating-inward condition. The distinction between 'bare within' and 'bare without' (be-qarachto o ve-gabbachto — using the baldness terms from vv.40-41 for the bald spot in the fabric) describes whether the discoloration is on the interior or exterior of the material.

verses.kjv_text: And if the priest look, and, behold, the plague be somewhat dark after the washing of it; then he shall rend it out of the garment, or out of the skin, or out of the warp, or out of the woof:
