verses.original_text: וְאִם רָאָה הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה כֵּהָה הַנֶּגַע אַחֲרֵי הֻכַּבֵּס אֹתוֹ וְקָרַע אֹתוֹ מִן הַבֶּגֶד אוֹ מִן הָעוֹר אוֹ מִן הַשְּׁתִי אוֹ מִן הָעֵרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im ra'ah ha-kohen ve-hinneh kehah ha-nega acharei hukkabbes oto ve-qara oto min ha-beged o min ha-or o min ha-sheti o min ha-erev.
verses.study_note: The positive recovery case: if after washing the plague has 'become dark' (kehah — faded, as in the skin case of v.6), the priest may tear out (ve-qara — to tear, to rend) the affected portion from the rest of the garment. The tearing out of the affected section parallels the surgical removal principle: excise the defiled part to preserve the whole. The word qara (to tear) is also the word used for tearing garments in mourning (v.45) — here the tearing is remedial, not lamenting. This is the only case in the garment section where partial recovery is possible: the diseased area is cut away and the remaining material can potentially be declared clean.

verses.kjv_text: And if it appear still in the garment, either in the warp, or in the woof, or in any thing of skin; it is a spreading plague: thou shalt burn that wherein the plague is with fire.
verses.original_text: וְאִם תֵּרָאֶה עוֹד בַּבֶּגֶד אוֹ בַשְּׁתִי אוֹ בָעֵרֶב אוֹ בְכָל כְּלִי עוֹר פֹּרַחַת הִוא בָּאֵשׁ תִּשְׂרֹף אֶת אֲשֶׁר בּוֹ הַנֶּגַע׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im tera'eh od ba-beged o va-sheti o va-erev o ve-khol keli or porachat hi ba-esh tisrof et asher bo ha-nega.
verses.study_note: If after the tear-out procedure the plague reappears — 'it appear still' (tera'eh od) — in any part of the garment, it is classified as a 'spreading plague' (porachat hi, from parach — 'blooming/spreading'). Total destruction follows: 'thou shalt burn that wherein the plague is with fire' (ba-esh tisrof et asher bo ha-nega). The plague's re-emergence after partial excision demonstrates that it is systemic, not localized — the entire affected object must be destroyed. This escalation from partial excision to total burning reflects the principle that defilement that proves irremediable must be completely eliminated to protect the community.

verses.kjv_text: And the garment, either warp, or woof, or whatsoever thing of skin it be, which thou shalt wash, if the plague be departed from them, then it shall be washed the second time, and shall be clean.
verses.original_text: וְהַבֶּגֶד אוֹ הַשְּׁתִי אוֹ הָעֵרֶב אוֹ כָל כְּלִי הָעוֹר אֲשֶׁר תְּכַבֵּס וְסָר מֵהֶם הַנֶּגַע וְכֻבַּס שֵׁנִית וְטָהֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ha-beged o ha-sheti o ha-erev o kol keli ha-or asher tekabbes ve-sar mehem ha-nega ve-kubas shenit ve-taher.
verses.study_note: The clean verdict for the washed garment: if washing causes the plague to 'depart' (ve-sar — to turn aside, to leave), the garment is washed a second time and declared clean. The double washing for the now-clean garment mirrors the double quarantine for ambiguous skin cases — thoroughness before the clean verdict is granted. The verb sar (departed, turned away) is significant: the same word used for Israel's turning away from the LORD (Num 14:43) and for the turning away of sin and judgment. When the plague departs, cleanness is restored through the prescribed washing process. The garment's clean verdict (ve-taher) restores it to use within the covenant community.

verses.kjv_text: This is the law of the plague of leprosy in a garment of woollen or linen, either in the warp, or woof, or any thing of skins, to pronounce it clean, or to pronounce it unclean.
verses.original_text: זֹאת תּוֹרַת נֶגַע הַצָּרַעַת בְּבֶגֶד הַצֶּמֶר אוֹ הַפִּשְׁתִּים אוֹ הַשְּׁתִי אוֹ הָעֵרֶב אוֹ כָּל כְּלִי עוֹר לְטַהֲרוֹ אוֹ לְטַמְּאוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Zot torat nega ha-tzara'at be-veged ha-tzemer o ha-pishtim o ha-sheti o ha-erev o kol keli or le-taharo o le-tamme'o.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with the standard Levitical colophon: 'This is the law' (zot torat) of the garment tzara'at section — and by extension of the entire chapter. The phrase zot torat appears at the conclusion of major legal units in Leviticus (6:9; 6:14; 6:25; 7:1; etc.), functioning as a legal summary statement. The dual purpose of the law is stated: 'to pronounce it clean, or to pronounce it unclean' (le-taharo o le-tamme'o) — the law exists to enable both verdicts. This binary structure of the entire chapter — clean/unclean — is the Levitical theological grammar, and chapter 13 is its most sustained expression. The priest's function as diagnostician serves both restoration (pronouncing clean) and protection (pronouncing unclean).
verses.doctrinal_note: The closing formula — 'to pronounce it clean, or to pronounce it unclean' (le-taharo o le-tamme'o) — summarizes the entire priestly diagnostic vocation of Leviticus 13. The priest's authority to pronounce is the Levitical anticipation of the ultimate divine verdict over all things. In Christ, this dual function reaches its fulfillment: He is simultaneously the one who pronounces clean by His atoning work and the one who will pronounce the final judgment over all that is defiled and irremediable. The justification of the sinner (the ultimate 'tahor' verdict, Rom 8:1 — 'no condemnation') and the condemnation of the unrepentant (the ultimate 'tame' verdict, Rev 20:15) are both accomplished by the Great High Priest whose diagnostic authority is perfect and whose cleansing power is complete. Leviticus 13's elaborate case-law ultimately exists to press the question: who can stand the examination? And to prepare the way for the answer: only those who are covered by the blood of the one who took their uncleanness upon Himself (Heb 9:14; 1 John 1:7).

meta.overview:
Leviticus 13 is the most extensive chapter in the book, comprising 59 verses of diagnostic law governing tzara'at — the condition translated "leprosy" in the KJV but encompassing a range of skin conditions, mold-like growths, and discolorations that rendered a person or object ritually unclean. The chapter is organized around the priest's diagnostic function: Aaron and his sons are appointed not merely as sacrificial officiants but as medical-spiritual inspectors whose examination determines whether an Israelite may remain within the covenant community or must be excluded from it.

The Hebrew word tzara'at (H6883) does not correspond precisely to Hansen's disease (modern leprosy) but to a broader category of skin anomalies that the priestly code treats as symbolically significant. What is theologically crucial is not the medical taxonomy but the theological principle: certain conditions make a person tamei (ritually impure, unclean) and require separation from the tabernacle and the congregation. The skin — the body's outermost boundary — becomes the visible surface on which the drama of cleanness and uncleanness is enacted. Visible corruption spreading outward from the body is the most vivid physical metaphor for sin's defilement permeating a human life.

The diagnostic cases are organized with remarkable legal precision: fresh eruptions (vv.1-8), chronic infections (vv.9-17), boil-related conditions (vv.18-23), burn-related conditions (vv.24-28), head and beard conditions (vv.29-37), white spots (vv.38-39), baldness-related conditions (vv.40-44), and the behavioral requirements for the declared unclean person (vv.45-46), followed by tzara'at in garments (vv.47-59). The repetitive case-law structure ("if the plague be... the priest shall look... and pronounce him clean/unclean") reflects the thoroughness and equity of the divine legal system — no case is too obscure to receive divine guidance.

Christologically, Leviticus 13 provides the essential background for understanding Jesus' ministry to lepers. When Jesus healed lepers and commanded them to "show yourselves to the priests" (Luke 17:14), He was directly invoking the Levitical system. When He touched the leper and declared "be thou clean" (Matt 8:3), He both fulfilled and superseded the priestly diagnostic role — He does not merely pronounce cleanness but creates it. The leper's isolation "without the camp" (v.46) prefigures the one who "suffered without the gate" (Heb 13:12) bearing the full weight of human uncleanness. The priest in Leviticus 13 diagnoses and excludes; the Great High Priest takes the exclusion upon Himself and restores the unclean to the community of God.
meta.theme: Priestly Diagnosis of Tzara'at: Uncleanness, Isolation, and the Boundaries of the Holy Community
meta.keyWordMeaning: Leprosy/skin-disease — from an uncertain root, possibly related to striking or smiting. Tzara'at is the comprehensive term for the category of skin conditions, fabric molds, and house-plagues governed by Leviticus 13-14. It is not merely a medical diagnosis but a ritual-theological category: the one afflicted is tamei (unclean) and excluded from the holy community until pronounced tahor (clean) by a priest. The NT fulfillment is explicit: Jesus heals lepers by touch (Matt 8:3), pronounces them clean, and sends them to the priests — inverting the Levitical order by creating cleanness rather than merely certifying it.
outline: Diagnosis of Fresh Skin Eruptions (vv.1-8): Initial examination, seven-day quarantine, and re-examination procedure
Diagnosis of Chronic and Spreading Conditions (vv.9-17): Old tzara'at versus spreading conditions and clean white spots
Tzara'at Arising from Boils (vv.18-23): Diagnostic procedure for post-boil eruptions
Tzara'at Arising from Burns (vv.24-28): Diagnosis of eruptions following burn wounds
Tzara'at of the Head and Beard: Scall Conditions (vv.29-37): Special procedures for scalp and facial hair areas
Bright Spots and Baldness Conditions (vv.38-44): White spots, bald-head and bald-forehead diagnoses
The Declared Unclean Person: Behavior and Isolation (vv.45-46): The leper's required conduct — torn clothes, covered lip, crying unclean
Tzara'at in Garments (vv.47-59): Diagnostic procedure for mildew-like conditions in wool, linen, and leather

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayedabber YHWH el Moshe lemor.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with the standard Mosaic address formula, but notably without Aaron — unlike chapter 13 which was addressed jointly to Moses and Aaron (Lev 13:1), chapter 14's restoration law is given to Moses alone. The diagnostic laws of expulsion (ch.13) required the priestly expertise of Aaron; the restoration laws of re-entry (ch.14) are revealed to Moses as mediator-lawgiver. This distinction reflects the different functions: priests execute the law, but the law itself originates with God through Moses. The divine origin of the restoration ritual is thus established before any detail is given — the path back into the community is not a human invention but a divinely ordained provision.

verses.kjv_text: This shall be the law of the leper in the day of his cleansing: He shall be brought unto the priest:
verses.original_text: זֹאת תִּהְיֶה תּוֹרַת הַמְּצֹרָע בְּיוֹם טָהֳרָתוֹ וְהוּבָא אֶל הַכֹּהֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Zot tihyeh torat ha-metzora be-yom toharato ve-huva el ha-kohen.
verses.study_note: The chapter's opening formula is remarkably precise: 'this shall be the law of the leper in the day of his cleansing' (torat ha-metzora be-yom toharato). The word metzora (the leper, the one afflicted with tzara'at) is brought to the priest — the same passive 'he shall be brought' (ve-huva) as in 13:2, indicating communal initiative in the restoration as in the diagnosis. But the crucial direction is established in verse 3: the priest goes out of the camp to him. The law of the leper's cleansing (torat ha-metzora) is the formal heading for the entire chapter, parallel to the closing colophon of 13:59. 'The day of his cleansing' presupposes that the healing has occurred — God has acted before the priest acts. The priest certifies and ritualizes a healing that God has already accomplished.
verses.doctrinal_note: The structure of verse 2 — the priest goes to the leper, not the leper to the priest — is one of the most profound directional statements in Levitical theology. The holy one goes to the defiled one; the clean approaches the unclean. This reverses every human instinct of religious performance and anticipates the incarnation: 'While we were yet sinners, Christ died for us' (Rom 5:8). The priest does not wait for the leper to cleanse himself sufficiently to approach the sanctuary. He goes outside the camp — the place of exclusion, shame, and judgment — to initiate the restoration. Jesus enacted this Levitical pattern perfectly: He touched lepers (Matt 8:3), He went to tax collectors (Luke 19:5), He sought the lost sheep (Luke 15:4). The Great High Priest's ministry always moves outward toward the excluded.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall go forth out of the camp; and the priest shall look, and, behold, if the plague of leprosy be healed in the leper;
verses.original_text: וְיָצָא הַכֹּהֵן אֶל מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן וְהִנֵּה נִרְפָּא נֶגַע הַצָּרַעַת מִן הַצָּרוּעַ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-yatza ha-kohen el mi-chutz la-machaneh ve-ra'ah ha-kohen ve-hinneh nirpa nega ha-tzara'at min ha-tzaru'a.
verses.study_note: The decisive directional movement: 'the priest shall go forth out of the camp' (ve-yatza ha-kohen el mi-chutz la-machaneh). The priest exits the holy precincts of the camp to go to the leper who dwells outside in isolation (Lev 13:46). The priestly examination confirms the healing: 'the plague of leprosy be healed' (nirpa nega ha-tzara'at) — the niphal of rapa indicates that the healing has been accomplished, presumably by divine act. The priest does not heal the leper; he examines and certifies a healing already accomplished. This sequence — God heals, priest certifies — is the Levitical pattern of grace preceding ceremony. The priest's examination outside the camp is the only occasion in the entire Torah where the priest performs his official function outside the camp boundary.

verses.kjv_text: Then shall the priest command to take for him that is to be cleansed two birds alive and clean, and cedar wood, and scarlet, and hyssop:
verses.original_text: וְצִוָּה הַכֹּהֵן וְלָקַח לַמִּטַּהֵר שְׁתֵּי צִפֳּרִים חַיּוֹת טְהֹרוֹת וְעֵץ אֶרֶז וּשְׁנִי תוֹלַעַת וְאֵזֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-tzivvah ha-kohen ve-laqach la-mittaher shtei tzipporim chayyot tehorot ve-etz erez u-sheni tola'at ve-ezov.
verses.study_note: The priest commands the gathering of four elements for the purification ceremony: (1) two living clean birds (shtei tzipporim chayyot tehorot) — small birds (possibly sparrows or similar), specified as living and ritually clean; (2) cedar wood (etz erez) — from the mighty, durable cedar, associated with incorruptibility and royalty; (3) scarlet thread (sheni tola'at — literally 'the worm of crimson,' a dye extracted from the coccus ilicis worm, producing the color of blood and sacrifice); (4) hyssop (ezov) — the small aromatic plant used for sprinkling blood and water in purification rites (Exod 12:22; Ps 51:7; Num 19:18). These four elements, taken together, form a ceremony of extraordinary symbolic density whose clearest interpretation is typological.
verses.doctrinal_note: The four-element combination — two birds, cedar, scarlet, hyssop — is one of the most christologically rich symbol-clusters in the Old Testament. The cedar (incorruptibility, royal dignity) and the hyssop (smallness, humility) together span the full range of Christ's person: the one who is simultaneously the eternal Son (cedar) and the humble servant who stooped to death (hyssop). Paul captures this tension in Philippians 2:6-8. The scarlet thread (sheni tola'at) recalls Rahab's scarlet cord (Josh 2:18,21) — the blood-colored token of covenant protection, and ultimately the blood of Christ as the seal of the new covenant (Matt 26:28). Hyssop specifically connects to Passover (Exod 12:22), David's prayer for cleansing (Ps 51:7), and the moment at the cross when Jesus was offered vinegar on a hyssop branch (John 19:29). The two birds will enact in the following verses the death and resurrection of Christ with remarkable precision.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall command that one of the birds be killed in an earthen vessel over running water:
verses.original_text: וְצִוָּה הַכֹּהֵן וְשָׁחַט אֶת הַצִּפֹּר הָאֶחָת אֶל כְּלִי חֶרֶשׂ עַל מַיִם חַיִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-tzivvah ha-kohen ve-shachat et ha-tzippor ha-echat el keli cheres al mayyim chayyim.
verses.study_note: The first bird is killed (shachat — the same ritual slaughter verb used for sacrificial animals) in an earthen vessel (keli cheres) over running water (mayyim chayyim — living water, flowing water). The earthen vessel is a clay pot — the most common, humble container, made from the ground (adamah), suggesting the creaturely nature of the instrument. The 'living water' (mayyim chayyim) is not still water but flowing, spring-fed water — the same term used for the water of the red heifer purification (Num 19:17) and connected to the life-giving water of the Spirit (John 4:10-11; 7:38). The bird's blood drips into the vessel of living water, mingling blood and life-water — a potent image of the atonement's union of death and life.

verses.kjv_text: As for the living bird, he shall take it, and the cedar wood, and the scarlet, and the hyssop, and shall dip them and the living bird in the blood of the bird that was killed over the running water:
verses.original_text: אֶת הַצִּפֹּר הַחַיָּה יִקַּח אֹתָהּ וְאֶת עֵץ הָאֶרֶז וְאֶת שְׁנִי הַתּוֹלַעַת וְאֶת הָאֵזֹב וְטָבַל אוֹתָם וְאֵת הַצִּפֹּר הַחַיָּה בְּדַם הַצִּפֹּר הַשְּׁחוּטָה עַל הַמַּיִם הַחַיִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Et ha-tzippor ha-chayyah yiqqach otah ve-et etz ha-erez ve-et sheni ha-tola'at ve-et ha-ezov ve-taval otam ve-et ha-tzippor ha-chayyah be-dam ha-tzippor ha-shechutah al ha-mayyim ha-chayyim.
verses.study_note: The living bird, cedar wood, scarlet thread, and hyssop are all dipped (taval — to dip, to immerse) in the blood of the slaughtered bird mixed with the living water. The living bird is thus covered in the blood of the dead bird. This is the ceremony's central act: the living bird carries the blood of the dead bird — it is blood-covered yet alive. The four elements together — cedar, hyssop, scarlet, bird — are dipped as a bundle into the blood-water mixture. Nothing remains unstained; all are covered in the blood of the one that died. The living bird is now the blood-carrier, about to be released.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall sprinkle upon him that is to be cleansed from the leprosy seven times, and shall pronounce him clean, and shall let the living bird loose into the open field.
verses.original_text: וְהִזָּה עַל הַמִּטַּהֵר מִן הַצָּרַעַת שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים וְטִהֲרוֹ וְשִׁלַּח אֶת הַצִּפֹּר הַחַיָּה עַל פְּנֵי הַשָּׂדֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-hizzah al ha-mittaher min ha-tzara'at sheva pe'amim ve-tiharo ve-shillach et ha-tzippor ha-chayyah al penei ha-sadeh.
verses.study_note: The blood-dipped hyssop bundle is used to sprinkle (hizzah — to sprinkle, to scatter) the person being cleansed seven times (sheva pe'amim). Seven is the number of covenant completion: the sevenfold sprinkling saturates the ceremony with the fullness of covenantal restoration. After the sprinkling, two actions occur simultaneously: the priest 'shall pronounce him clean' (ve-tiharo — the decisive verbal declaration restoring covenant status) and 'shall let the living bird loose' (ve-shillach et ha-tzippor ha-chayyah — to send away, to release). The bird is released 'over the open field' (al penei ha-sadeh) — into the unlimited open expanse, carrying the blood of the dead bird away from the leper into freedom. This is the most vivid acted parable in all of Leviticus.
verses.doctrinal_note: The release of the blood-covered living bird into the open field is the enacted anticipation of Christ's resurrection. The dead bird (v.5) = the substitutionary death of Christ; the living bird dipped in the dead bird's blood (v.6) = the resurrection bearing the marks of the cross; the release into the open field = the ascension of the risen Christ into the unlimited expanse of heaven. The sevenfold sprinkling that precedes the release = the complete and perfect application of Christ's blood to the sinner. The simultaneous declaration 'he is clean' and release of the bird makes a theological statement: the pronouncement of the sinner's cleanness and the reality of the resurrection-atonement are inseparable acts. John's Gospel frames this with precision: 'I lay down my life, that I might take it again' (John 10:17) — the same one who died is the same one who lives, and his living carries the marks and effectiveness of his dying. Compare also the scapegoat of Leviticus 16:21-22 — another bird/animal carrying sin away — but here the carrier is not the sin but the blood of atonement, released into freedom.

verses.kjv_text: And he that is to be cleansed shall wash his clothes, and shave off all his hair, and wash himself in water, that he may be clean: and after that he shall come into the camp, and shall tarry abroad out of his tent seven days.
verses.original_text: וְכִבֶּס הַמִּטַּהֵר אֶת בְּגָדָיו וְגִלַּח אֶת כָּל שְׂעָרוֹ וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם וְטָהֵר וְאַחַר יָבוֹא אֶל הַמַּחֲנֶה וְיָשַׁב מִחוּץ לְאָהֳלוֹ שִׁבְעַת יָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-kibbes ha-mittaher et begadav ve-gillach et kol se'aro ve-rachatz ba-mayyim ve-taher ve-achar yavo el ha-machaneh ve-yashav mi-chutz le-oholo shiv'at yamim.
verses.study_note: The healed leper's active participation in the cleansing process now begins: he washes his garments (kibbes), shaves all his hair (gillach et kol se'aro), and bathes in water (rachatz ba-mayyim). The total shaving — all hair, not just the affected area — represents a complete stripping away of the old defiled identity, a radical new beginning. After this he may re-enter the camp, but he must remain outside his tent (mi-chutz le-oholo) for seven more days. The re-entry to the camp but not to the tent is a graduated restoration — he is no longer outside the community but not yet at full domestic life. The seven-day intermediate period mirrors the seven-day quarantine periods of chapter 13 — time structured in covenant cycles.

verses.kjv_text: But it shall be on the seventh day, that he shall shave all his hair off his head and his beard and his eyebrows, even all his hair he shall shave off: and he shall wash his clothes, also he shall wash his flesh in water, and he shall be clean.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה בַיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי יְגַלַּח אֶת כָּל שְׂעָרוֹ אֶת רֹאשׁוֹ וְאֶת זְקָנוֹ וְאֵת גַּבֹּת עֵינָיו וְאֶת כָּל שְׂעָרוֹ יְגַלֵּחַ וְכִבֶּס אֶת בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ אֶת בְּשָׂרוֹ בַּמַּיִם וְטָהֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-hayah bayyom hash-shevi'i yegallach et kol se'aro et rosho ve-et zeqano ve-et gabbot einav ve-et kol se'aro yegallach ve-kibbes et begadav ve-rachatz et besaro ba-mayyim ve-taher.
verses.study_note: On the seventh day, a second and more comprehensive shaving is performed: head, beard, and eyebrows — every hair on the body is removed. The specificity of 'head and beard and eyebrows' (rosho, zeqano, gabbot einav) ensures that the shaving is literally total — not a strand of the old unclean existence remains. The eyebrow shaving is the most extreme marker of this complete stripping — it removes even the most inconspicuous body hair. This is followed by another washing of garments and bathing of flesh. The double cleansing (day one: wash, shave, bathe; day seven: shave more thoroughly, wash, bathe) reflects the progressive, multi-stage character of covenantal restoration. After the seventh-day procedures, the person is tahor — clean — and ready for the eighth-day ceremonies at the tabernacle.

verses.kjv_text: And on the eighth day he shall take two he lambs without blemish, and one ewe lamb of the first year without blemish, and three tenth deals of fine flour for a meat offering, mingled with oil, and one log of oil.
verses.original_text: וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי יִקַּח שְׁנֵי כְבָשִׂים תְּמִימִם וְכַבְשָׂה אַחַת בַּת שְׁנָתָהּ תְּמִימָה וּשְׁלֹשָׁה עֶשְׂרֹנִים סֹלֶת מִנְחָה בְּלוּלָה בַשֶּׁמֶן וְלֹג אֶחָד שָׁמֶן׃
verses.transliteration: U-vayyom hash-shemini yiqqach shenei khvasim temimim ve-khavsa achat bat shanata temimah u-sheloshah esronim solet minchah belulah va-shemen ve-log echad shamen.
verses.study_note: The eighth day (bayyom hash-shemini) — the day of new beginning — brings the restored leper to the sanctuary for the most comprehensive set of personal offerings in the entire Levitical system. The required offerings are: two male lambs without blemish (one for the guilt offering, one for the burnt offering); one female lamb of the first year without blemish (for the sin offering); three tenths of an ephah of fine flour for the grain offering mixed with oil; and one log of oil (approximately one-third of a pint). The costliness of this offering set — four animals and significant grain and oil — reflects the gravity of the restoration being accomplished. The eighth day's new-creation resonance is fully intentional: the restored leper enters a new existence on the day that transcends the week.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest that maketh him clean shall present the man that is to be made clean, and those things, before the LORD, at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation:
verses.original_text: וְהֶעֱמִיד הַכֹּהֵן הַמְטַהֵר אֵת הָאִישׁ הַמִּטַּהֵר וְאֹתָם לִפְנֵי יְהוָה פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-he'emid ha-kohen ha-metaher et ha-ish ha-mittaher ve-otam lifnei YHWH petach ohel mo'ed.
verses.study_note: The priest who performs the cleansing (ha-kohen ha-metaher — the cleansing-priest) presents (he'emid — causes to stand) the person being cleansed and all his offerings before the LORD at the entrance to the tabernacle (petach ohel mo'ed). The phrase 'at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation' is the precise point of the boundary between the outside world and the holy precincts — the threshold of the sacred. The restored leper now stands where previously he could not stand: at the very entrance of the divine dwelling. The journey from 'outside the camp' (Lev 13:46) to 'the door of the tabernacle' is the entire trajectory of Levitical restoration theology — from maximum exclusion to the threshold of maximum access.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall take one he lamb, and offer him for a trespass offering, and the log of oil, and wave them for a wave offering before the LORD:
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַכֶּבֶשׂ הָאֶחָד וְהִקְרִיב אֹתוֹ לְאָשָׁם וְאֶת לֹג הַשָּׁמֶן וְהֵנִיף אֹתָם תְּנוּפָה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-laqach ha-kohen et ha-keves ha-echad ve-hiqriv oto le-asham ve-et log ha-shamen ve-henif otam tenufah lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: The first offering of the eighth-day ceremony is the guilt offering (asham) — one male lamb — together with the log of oil, both waved as a wave offering (tenufah) before the LORD. The asham (guilt/trespass offering) is typically prescribed for cases of misappropriation of holy things or violation of specific covenant obligations. Its use here for the restored leper is unusual and theologically significant: the leper's exclusion from the sanctuary and community, though not due to personal sin, has nevertheless left him in a state that requires formal restitution and re-consecration. The wave offering (tenufah, presented to God and 'given back' to the priest) precedes the blood ritual, presenting the animal to the divine sovereign before its blood is applied.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall slay the lamb in the place where he shall kill the sin offering and the burnt offering, in the holy place: for as the sin offering is the priest’s, so is the trespass offering: it is most holy:
verses.original_text: וְשָׁחַט אֶת הַכֶּבֶשׂ בִּמְקוֹם אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁחַט אֶת הַחַטָּאת וְאֶת הָעֹלָה בִּמְקוֹם הַקֹּדֶשׁ כִּי כַחַטָּאת הָאָשָׁם הוּא לַכֹּהֵן קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-shachat et ha-keves bi-mqom asher yishchat et ha-chatta'at ve-et ha-olah bi-mqom ha-qodesh ki ka-chatta'at ha-asham hu la-kohen qodesh qadashim hu.
verses.study_note: The guilt offering lamb is slaughtered in the holy place (bi-mqom ha-qodesh) — the same location as the sin and burnt offerings, the designated space on the north side of the altar (Lev 1:11). The statement 'for as the sin offering is the priest's, so is the trespass offering' establishes the priestly portion of the guilt offering's meat (Lev 7:6-7). The classification 'most holy' (qodesh qadashim) — the highest holiness category in the Levitical system — is applied to the guilt offering, elevating it to the same status as the most restricted offerings. The restored leper's guilt offering is as holy as anything offered on the altar — the ceremony of restoration is no lesser occasion than any other priestly act.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall take some of the blood of the trespass offering, and the priest shall put it upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot:
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח הַכֹּהֵן מִדַּם הָאָשָׁם וְנָתַן הַכֹּהֵן עַל תְּנוּךְ אֹזֶן הַמִּטַּהֵר הַיְמָנִית וְעַל בֹּהֶן יָדוֹ הַיְמָנִית וְעַל בֹּהֶן רַגְלוֹ הַיְמָנִית׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-laqach ha-kohen mi-dam ha-asham ve-natan ha-kohen al tenukh ozen ha-mittaher ha-yemanit ve-al bohen yado ha-yemanit ve-al bohen raglo ha-yemanit.
verses.study_note: The blood of the guilt offering is applied to three precise points on the restored leper's body: the lobe of the right ear (tenukh ozen ha-yemanit), the thumb of the right hand (bohen yado ha-yemanit), and the great toe of the right foot (bohen raglo ha-yemanit). This triad of application is identical to the priestly ordination ceremony of Leviticus 8:23-24, where Aaron and his sons received the same blood application. The restored leper is being consecrated with the same consecration as the priesthood — his re-entry into the community is nothing less than a new ordination to covenant life. The right ear, right thumb, and right great toe represent the whole person: hearing (obedience to God's word), working (service with the hands), and walking (the direction of one's life).
verses.doctrinal_note: The application of blood to ear, thumb, and toe is one of the most profound typological acts in all of Leviticus, and it runs in two directions simultaneously. Backward: it recapitulates the priestly ordination of Leviticus 8, declaring that the restored leper is being re-ordained to covenant life with the full dignity of a priest. Forward: it anticipates the new covenant reality that all believers are a 'royal priesthood' (1 Pet 2:9), consecrated by Christ's blood to hear, work, and walk in His service. The blood on the ear consecrates the hearing to the Word of God ('faith cometh by hearing,' Rom 10:17); the blood on the thumb consecrates the hands to holy service ('present your members as instruments of righteousness,' Rom 6:13); the blood on the great toe consecrates the walk to the path of holiness ('walk worthy of the calling,' Eph 4:1). The entire person — from the faculty of reception to the means of action to the direction of movement — is claimed by the blood of atonement.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall take some of the log of oil, and pour it into the palm of his own left hand:
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח הַכֹּהֵן מִלֹּג הַשָּׁמֶן וְיָצַק עַל כַּף הַכֹּהֵן הַשְּׂמָאלִית׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-laqach ha-kohen mi-log ha-shamen ve-yatzaq al kaf ha-kohen ha-sema'lit.
verses.study_note: After the blood application, the oil ceremony begins. The priest pours (yatzaq — to pour out) some of the log of oil into the palm of his own left hand (kaf ha-kohen ha-sema'lit). The use of the left hand as the vessel for the oil is unique to this ceremony — the right hand is the hand of strength and blessing in Hebrew thought, but here the left serves as the vessel while the right performs the application. This deliberate reversal may signal that the oil's power does not reside in the hand's natural strength but in what the oil itself represents. The priest's own palm becomes the sacred vessel for the anointing oil — a reminder that priestly ministry is embodied, personal, and handled with the priest's own flesh.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall dip his right finger in the oil that is in his left hand, and shall sprinkle of the oil with his finger seven times before the LORD:
verses.original_text: וְטָבַל הַכֹּהֵן אֶת אֶצְבָּעוֹ הַיְמָנִית מִן הַשֶּׁמֶן אֲשֶׁר עַל כַּפּוֹ הַשְּׂמָאלִית וְהִזָּה מִן הַשֶּׁמֶן בְּאֶצְבָּעוֹ שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-taval ha-kohen et etzba'o ha-yemanit min ha-shemen asher al kappo ha-sema'lit ve-hizzah min ha-shemen be-etzba'o sheva pe'amim lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: The priest dips his right finger (etzba'o ha-yemanit) into the oil in his left palm and sprinkles (hizzah) oil seven times before the LORD. The sevenfold sprinkling of oil before the LORD mirrors the sevenfold blood sprinkling of verse 7 — both blood and oil receive this complete, covenantal application before the LORD. The oil is first directed toward God (seven times before the LORD) before being applied to the person — the anointing is first and fundamentally a divine act before it is a human benefit. Oil in the Old Testament consistently represents the Spirit of God (Isa 61:1; 1 Sam 16:13; Zech 4:2-6), and the sevenfold sprinkling before the LORD anticipates the Spirit's fullness poured out in the new covenant.

verses.kjv_text: And of the rest of the oil that is in his hand shall the priest put upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot, upon the blood of the trespass offering:
verses.original_text: וּמִיֶּתֶר הַשֶּׁמֶן אֲשֶׁר עַל כַּפּוֹ יִתֵּן הַכֹּהֵן עַל תְּנוּךְ אֹזֶן הַמִּטַּהֵר הַיְמָנִית וְעַל בֹּהֶן יָדוֹ הַיְמָנִית וְעַל בֹּהֶן רַגְלוֹ הַיְמָנִית עַל דַּם הָאָשָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: U-mi-yeter ha-shemen asher al kappo yitten ha-kohen al tenukh ozen ha-mittaher ha-yemanit ve-al bohen yado ha-yemanit ve-al bohen raglo ha-yemanit al dam ha-asham.
verses.study_note: The remaining oil from the priest's left palm is applied to the same three points that received the blood: right ear lobe, right thumb, right great toe — but now 'upon the blood of the trespass offering' (al dam ha-asham). Oil is applied over blood, not in place of it. This layering of oil upon blood is theologically precise: the Spirit is given on the basis of the atonement, not independently of it. The blood-then-oil sequence — identical to the priestly ordination sequence of Lev 8:23-30 — establishes the theological order: atonement first (blood), then consecration/gifting (oil). The Spirit's anointing rests upon the foundation of Christ's blood; there is no Spirit-anointing apart from the cross.

verses.kjv_text: And the remnant of the oil that is in the priest’s hand he shall pour upon the head of him that is to be cleansed: and the priest shall make an atonement for him before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהַנּוֹתָר בַּשֶּׁמֶן אֲשֶׁר עַל כַּף הַכֹּהֵן יִתֵּן עַל רֹאשׁ הַמִּטַּהֵר וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ha-notar ba-shemen asher al kaf ha-kohen yitten al rosh ha-mittaher ve-khipper alav ha-kohen lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: The ceremony's climax: whatever oil remains in the priest's palm is poured upon the head (al rosh) of the person being cleansed. The pouring of oil on the head is the anointing act par excellence — the same gesture used for anointing kings (1 Sam 10:1; 16:13) and the high priest (Lev 8:12; 21:10). The restored leper receives a head anointing comparable to royal and priestly consecration. This is followed by the atonement declaration: 'the priest shall make atonement for him before the LORD' (ve-khipper alav ha-kohen lifnei YHWH). The kipper (atonement) here covers the entire ceremony — the blood application, the oil, the head anointing — all constitute one comprehensive act of priestly atonement effecting the person's complete restoration before God.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall offer the sin offering, and make an atonement for him that is to be cleansed from his uncleanness; and afterward he shall kill the burnt offering:
verses.original_text: וְעָשָׂה הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַחַטָּאת וְכִפֶּר עַל הַמִּטַּהֵר מִטֻּמְאָתוֹ וְאַחַר יִשְׁחַט אֶת הָעֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-asah ha-kohen et ha-chatta'at ve-khipper al ha-mittaher mi-tum'ato ve-achar yishchat et ha-olah.
verses.study_note: Following the guilt offering and oil ceremony, the priest now offers the sin offering (chatta'at) with its accompanying atonement kipper, followed by the burnt offering (olah). The sequence of offerings — asham (guilt), then chatta'at (sin), then olah (burnt) — moves from specific restitution to general sin-purification to total consecration. Each offering addresses a different dimension of the person's need: the guilt offering addresses the specific debt of the leper's exclusion; the sin offering addresses the general defilement of the person; the burnt offering expresses total self-consecration to God. The threefold offering structure ensures that the restoration is not merely partial but comprehensive.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall offer the burnt offering and the meat offering upon the altar: and the priest shall make an atonement for him, and he shall be clean.
verses.original_text: וְהֶעֱלָה הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הָעֹלָה וְאֶת הַמִּנְחָה הַמִּזְבֵּחָה וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן וְטָהֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-he'elah ha-kohen et ha-olah ve-et ha-minchah ha-mizbeachah ve-khipper alav ha-kohen ve-taher.
verses.study_note: The complete sequence concludes: the burnt offering (olah) and grain offering (minchah) are offered on the altar, the final kipper declaration is made, and the outcome is stated with one word: 've-taher' — and he shall be clean. This single verb is the destination of the entire journey from diagnosis (Lev 13) through external ceremony (Lev 14:1-9) through intermediate purification (14:8-9) through the eighth-day sacrificial complex (14:10-20): tahor — clean. The simplicity of the conclusion after the elaborate procedure emphasizes that the complex ceremony produces a simple, total result. All of the blood, all of the oil, all of the offerings, the wave ceremony, the head anointing — all of it results in one thing: the person is clean.

verses.kjv_text: And if he be poor, and cannot get so much; then he shall take one lamb for a trespass offering to be waved, to make an atonement for him, and one tenth deal of fine flour mingled with oil for a meat offering, and a log of oil;
verses.original_text: וְאִם דַּל הוּא וְאֵין יָדוֹ מַשֶּׂגֶת וְלָקַח כֶּבֶשׂ אֶחָד אָשָׁם לִתְנוּפָה לְכַפֵּר עָלָיו וְעִשָּׂרוֹן סֹלֶת אֶחָד בָּלוּל בַּשֶּׁמֶן לְמִנְחָה וְלֹג שָׁמֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im dal hu ve-ein yado masseget ve-laqach keves echad asham li-tenufah le-khapper alav ve-issaron solet echad balul ba-shemen le-minchah ve-log shamen.
verses.study_note: The provision for the poor (dal — the thin, poor, weak one) reflects one of Leviticus's recurring pastoral concerns: that economic limitation should not exclude anyone from covenant access. 'And if he be poor, and cannot get so much' (ve-im dal hu ve-ein yado masseget — literally 'his hand does not reach') — the idiom of economic insufficiency that appears throughout the Torah. The essential structure of the ceremony is maintained: one male lamb for the guilt offering (asham) with the wave offering and the log of oil are kept constant. The sin and burnt offerings are reduced from full lambs to two doves or pigeons. The grain offering is reduced from three-tenths to one-tenth of an ephah. The core offering (asham + oil) is non-negotiable; the supplementary offerings are scaled.
verses.doctrinal_note: The provision for the poor leper is one of the most theologically significant pastoral provisions in the entire Torah. It establishes the principle that access to God's cleansing is not gatekept by economic resources. The same atonement is available to the wealthy (full offering set, vv.10-20) and to the poor (reduced offering set, vv.21-32), because the efficacy of the cleansing rests not in the quantity of the offering but in the divine appointment of the ceremony. This principle reaches its fullest expression in Christ's once-for-all sacrifice: 'not with corruptible things, as silver and gold,' but 'with the precious blood of Christ' (1 Pet 1:18-19). The free gift of grace (Eph 2:8-9) ensures that no economic barrier prevents access to God's cleansing. The poor leper's reduced offering still achieves tahor — complete cleanness. The measure of God's grace is not proportional to the measure of human contribution.

verses.kjv_text: And two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, such as he is able to get; and the one shall be a sin offering, and the other a burnt offering.
verses.original_text: וּשְׁתֵּי תֹרִים אוֹ שְׁנֵי בְּנֵי יוֹנָה אֲשֶׁר תַּשִּׂיג יָדוֹ וְהָיָה אֶחָד חַטָּאת וְהָאֶחָד עֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: U-shtei torim o shenei benei yonah asher taissig yado ve-hayah echad chatta'at ve-ha-echad olah.
verses.study_note: The poor leper's reduced offerings substitute two doves or two young pigeons (turtledoves, torim, or young pigeons, benei yonah) for the sin and burnt offering lambs. These are the same bird substitutions used for the reduced offerings of other poor-provision laws in Leviticus (Lev 5:7; 12:8). Notably, this is the same offering brought by Mary and Joseph for the purification offering at Jesus' presentation in the temple (Luke 2:24) — identifying Jesus' parents as among the poor of Israel. The phrase 'such as he is able to get' (asher taissig yado — which his hand is able to attain) frames the entire provision as meeting the person where they are. God's gracious provision matches human capacity, not the other way around.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall bring them on the eighth day for his cleansing unto the priest, unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהֵבִיא אֹתָם בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי לְטָהֳרָתוֹ אֶל הַכֹּהֵן אֶל פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-hevi otam bayyom hash-shemini le-toharato el ha-kohen el petach ohel mo'ed lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: The poor person's ceremony takes place on the same eighth day (bayyom hash-shemini) and at the same location (petach ohel mo'ed — the door of the tabernacle) as the full offering. The equality of place and time signals the equality of access: poor and wealthy leper arrive at the same door, before the same LORD, on the same eighth day. The temporal and spatial equality compensates for the material inequality of the offerings. God does not have a separate, lesser threshold for the poor — they come before the same divine presence on the same sacred day. The eighth day's new-creation significance applies equally to all who are restored, regardless of economic standing.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall take the lamb of the trespass offering, and the log of oil, and the priest shall wave them for a wave offering before the LORD:
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח הַכֹּהֵן אֶת כֶּבֶשׂ הָאָשָׁם וְאֶת לֹג הַשָּׁמֶן וְהֵנִיף אֹתָם הַכֹּהֵן תְּנוּפָה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-laqach ha-kohen et keves ha-asham ve-et log ha-shamen ve-henif otam ha-kohen tenufah lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: The poor person's ceremony begins identically to the wealthy person's: the guilt offering lamb and the log of oil are waved before the LORD. The guilt offering (asham) with its oil is the non-negotiable core of the ceremony for both wealthy and poor. The two elements that cannot be reduced — the asham lamb and the log of oil — represent the essential atonement-and-Spirit structure that no economic provision can substitute. What God requires absolutely (atonement and consecration) is maintained; what God allows to be scaled (the supplementary sin and burnt offerings) is reduced for those of limited means. The irreducible core reveals what is theologically essential about the restoration ceremony.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall kill the lamb of the trespass offering, and the priest shall take some of the blood of the trespass offering, and put it upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot:
verses.original_text: וְשָׁחַט אֶת כֶּבֶשׂ הָאָשָׁם וְלָקַח הַכֹּהֵן מִדַּם הָאָשָׁם וְנָתַן עַל תְּנוּךְ אֹזֶן הַמִּטַּהֵר הַיְמָנִית וְעַל בֹּהֶן יָדוֹ הַיְמָנִית וְעַל בֹּהֶן רַגְלוֹ הַיְמָנִית׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-shachat et keves ha-asham ve-laqach ha-kohen mi-dam ha-asham ve-natan al tenukh ozen ha-mittaher ha-yemanit ve-al bohen yado ha-yemanit ve-al bohen raglo ha-yemanit.
verses.study_note: The blood application to right ear, right thumb, and right great toe is performed identically for the poor leper as for the wealthy — the same three points, the same right-side emphasis. This repetition (vv.14 and 25 use nearly identical language) reinforces the equality of the blood application regardless of economic status. The poor leper's ear, thumb, and toe receive the exact same blood consecration as the wealthy leper. The blood of the guilt offering claims the hearing, working, and walking of the poor with the same thoroughness as the rich. There is no tiered consecration in God's covenantal restoration.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall pour of the oil into the palm of his own left hand:
verses.original_text: וּמִן הַשֶּׁמֶן יִצֹק הַכֹּהֵן עַל כַּף הַכֹּהֵן הַשְּׂמָאלִית׃
verses.transliteration: U-min ha-shemen yitzok ha-kohen al kaf ha-kohen ha-sema'lit.
verses.study_note: The oil ceremony for the poor leper begins identically with the priest pouring oil into his left palm — the same procedure as verse 15. The abbreviated oil ceremony (vv.26-29) mirrors the full ceremony of vv.15-18 precisely. The visual and procedural identity of the two ceremonies reinforces the theological equality of their outcomes. The same oil poured into the same left palm, applied by the same right finger, sprinkled seven times before the same LORD — the rich and poor leper receive the same anointing from the same God through the same priestly ministry.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall sprinkle with his right finger some of the oil that is in his left hand seven times before the LORD:
verses.original_text: וְהִזָּה הַכֹּהֵן בְּאֶצְבָּעוֹ הַיְמָנִית מִן הַשֶּׁמֶן אֲשֶׁר עַל כַּפּוֹ הַשְּׂמָאלִית שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-hizzah ha-kohen be-etzba'o ha-yemanit min ha-shemen asher al kappo ha-sema'lit sheva pe'amim lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: The sevenfold oil sprinkling before the LORD is performed identically for the poor leper — same finger, same direction, same number. The seven-times-before-the-LORD application of oil is the non-negotiable element of the ceremony for both wealthy and poor. Seven is the number of covenant completeness: the poor leper receives the same complete oil-consecration before God as the wealthy. This repeated identical application emphasizes that the Spirit's anointing (typified by the oil) is given in its fullness to all who come through the priestly ceremony, regardless of the size of their accompanying offerings.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall put of the oil that is in his hand upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot, upon the place of the blood of the trespass offering:
verses.original_text: וְנָתַן הַכֹּהֵן מִן הַשֶּׁמֶן אֲשֶׁר עַל כַּפּוֹ עַל תְּנוּךְ אֹזֶן הַמִּטַּהֵר הַיְמָנִית וְעַל בֹּהֶן יָדוֹ הַיְמָנִית וְעַל בֹּהֶן רַגְלוֹ הַיְמָנִית עַל מְקוֹם דַּם הָאָשָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-natan ha-kohen min ha-shemen asher al kappo al tenukh ozen ha-mittaher ha-yemanit ve-al bohen yado ha-yemanit ve-al bohen raglo ha-yemanit al meqom dam ha-asham.
verses.study_note: Oil is applied to the same three points — right ear, right thumb, right great toe — this time described as 'upon the place of the blood of the trespass offering' (al meqom dam ha-asham). The phrase meqom dam (the place of the blood) emphasizes the site-specificity of the application: oil goes precisely where blood has already been applied. This microscopic detail teaches that the Spirit's work (oil) follows the exact footprint of the atonement (blood) — not one fraction more or less. The anointing of the Spirit covers precisely what the blood has covered, neither exceeding it nor falling short of it. The Spirit does not operate in a domain separate from the cross but applies and confirms what Christ's blood accomplished.

verses.kjv_text: And the rest of the oil that is in the priest’s hand he shall put upon the head of him that is to be cleansed, to make an atonement for him before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהַנּוֹתָר מִן הַשֶּׁמֶן אֲשֶׁר עַל כַּף הַכֹּהֵן יִתֵּן עַל רֹאשׁ הַמִּטַּהֵר לְכַפֵּר עָלָיו לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ha-notar min ha-shemen asher al kaf ha-kohen yitten al rosh ha-mittaher le-khapper alav lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: The remaining oil is poured on the head of the poor leper — the same head anointing as verse 18. The head anointing with the atonement declaration (le-khapper alav lifnei YHWH) is the climax of the oil ceremony for both wealthy and poor. The poor leper receives the same head anointing — the same royal/priestly gesture of consecration — as the wealthy. Both receive the oil on the head before the LORD; both receive the same kipper declaration. The atonement accomplished before God is identical regardless of economic standing. The reduced offering ceremony for the poor is not a lesser atonement but the same atonement at lesser cost.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall offer the one of the turtledoves, or of the young pigeons, such as he can get;
verses.original_text: וְעָשָׂה אֶת הָאֶחָד מִן הַתֹּרִים אוֹ מִן בְּנֵי הַיּוֹנָה מֵאֲשֶׁר תַּשִּׂיג יָדוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-asah et ha-echad min ha-torim o min benei ha-yonah me-asher taissig yado.
verses.study_note: The poor person's sin offering — one turtledove or young pigeon 'such as he can get' (me-asher taissig yado — as far as his hand reaches). The phrase appears again, framing the entire poor provision with the same idiom of economic limitation. The law works with what the poor person can actually access, not with an ideal standard that excludes them. 'Such as he can get' is a pastoral acknowledgment of real economic circumstances — the law meets people in their actual situations. The single bird substitutes for the full female lamb sin offering without diminishing the theological function of the ceremony.

verses.kjv_text: Even such as he is able to get, the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering, with the meat offering: and the priest shall make an atonement for him that is to be cleansed before the LORD.
verses.original_text: אֵת אֲשֶׁר תַּשִּׂיג יָדוֹ אֶת הָאֶחָד חַטָּאת וְאֶת הָאֶחָד עֹלָה עַל הַמִּנְחָה וְכִפֶּר הַכֹּהֵן עַל הַמִּטַּהֵר לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Et asher taissig yado et ha-echad chatta'at ve-et ha-echad olah al ha-minchah ve-khipper ha-kohen al ha-mittaher lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: The poor ceremony concludes with the same kipper declaration as the full ceremony: 'the priest shall make atonement for him before the LORD.' The phrase 'what his hand is able to get' (asher taissig yado) appears for the third time in the poor provision, forming an inclusio around vv.21-31 that frames the entire reduced ceremony under the principle of grace working within human limitation. The atonement declaration is not diminished: kipper is kipper, regardless of the size of the accompanying offering. The priest makes the same atonement before the same LORD for the poor leper as for the wealthy. The efficacy of priestly mediation does not fluctuate with offering size.

verses.kjv_text: This is the law of him in whom is the plague of leprosy, whose hand is not able to get that which pertaineth to his cleansing.
verses.original_text: זֹאת תּוֹרַת אֲשֶׁר בּוֹ נֶגַע צָרָעַת אֲשֶׁר לֹא תַשִּׂיג יָדוֹ בְּטָהֳרָתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Zot torat asher bo nega tzara'at asher lo taissig yado be-toharato.
verses.study_note: The colophon for the poor provision section: 'This is the law for the one who has the plague of leprosy, whose hand is not sufficient for his cleansing.' The phrase 'whose hand is not sufficient' (asher lo taissig yado) is the closing definition of the entire reduced ceremony. The law is defined by what it is for: not for the ceremonially adequate but for the economically insufficient. This summary statement is itself a pastoral declaration — there is a specific, named provision for those whose hand cannot reach the standard. No one is excluded from cleansing on the basis of poverty. The law anticipates the gospel's declaration that salvation is for those who have nothing to bring (Luke 6:20; Rev 22:17).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל אַהֲרֹן לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayedabber YHWH el Moshe ve-el Aharon lemor.
verses.study_note: The house tzara'at section (vv.33-53) is addressed to both Moses and Aaron — as in chapter 13 — because it requires both prophetic legislation and priestly implementation. The joint address signals a new and distinct sub-section within the chapter, addressing a different category of tzara'at: not skin or garment but dwelling places. The extension of the tzara'at laws to houses reflects the comprehensive scope of the defilement category: what affects persons also affects their environments. The house tzara'at laws anticipate Israel's settlement of Canaan (v.34 specifies 'when ye be come into the land of Canaan') — this legislation is future-oriented, given before Israel entered the Promised Land.

verses.kjv_text: When ye be come into the land of Canaan, which I give to you for a possession, and I put the plague of leprosy in a house of the land of your possession;
verses.original_text: כִּי תָבֹאוּ אֶל אֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי נֹתֵן לָכֶם לַאֲחֻזָּה וְנָתַתִּי נֶגַע צָרַעַת בְּבֵית אֶרֶץ אֲחֻזַּתְכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ki tavo'u el eretz Kena'an asher ani noten lakhem la-achuzzah ve-natatti nega tzara'at be-veit eretz achuzzatekhem.
verses.study_note: The house tzara'at legislation is explicitly projected into the future: 'when ye be come into the land of Canaan.' The laws given in the wilderness apply to a settled life in a specific land — the gift-land ('which I give to you for a possession,' asher ani noten lakhem la-achuzzah). The phrase 'and I put the plague of leprosy in a house' (ve-natatti nega tzara'at be-veit) is remarkable: the LORD Himself is the agent of the house plague. This is not accidental mold but divinely permitted defilement within Israel's possession — the Promised Land itself is not immune from tzara'at. The divine sovereignty over the plague ('I put') prevents purely naturalistic interpretations of house tzara'at and frames it within the larger theology of divine testing and purification of Israel's dwelling.
verses.doctrinal_note: The statement 'I put the plague of leprosy in a house' (ve-natatti nega tzara'at be-veit) is one of the most theologically honest statements in the Torah about divine sovereign action. God explicitly takes agency for the defilement entering the houses of His own covenant people in their own promised land. This is consistent with the broader prophetic-theological framework: divine discipline can inhabit even the gifts of God. The Promised Land itself is not a guaranteed zone of immunity from defilement; it must be continually maintained in holiness. The house tzara'at passage foreshadows the temple's theology: a building can be defiled (the glory departing, Ezek 10) or holy (the glory dwelling, Exod 40:34). The NT application is explicit in 1 Cor 3:16-17 — the believer and the church community are God's temple; defilement within the body of Christ requires the same priestly examination, quarantine, and if necessary, demolition (church discipline, Matt 18:17) as the defiled Canaanite house.

verses.kjv_text: And he that owneth the house shall come and tell the priest, saying, It seemeth to me there is as it were a plague in the house:
verses.original_text: וּבָא אֲשֶׁר לוֹ הַבַּיִת וְהִגִּיד לַכֹּהֵן לֵאמֹר כְּנֶגַע נִרְאָה לִי בַּבָּיִת׃
verses.transliteration: U-va asher lo ha-bayit ve-higgid la-kohen lemor ke-nega nir'ah li va-bayit.
verses.study_note: The homeowner (asher lo ha-bayit — the one to whom the house belongs) comes to report to the priest. The careful phrasing 'it seemeth to me there is as it were a plague' (ke-nega nir'ah li — like a plague appears to me) reflects appropriate epistemic humility: the homeowner does not diagnose but reports a visual impression for priestly evaluation. This humility is appropriate — the definitive diagnosis belongs to the priest, not the layperson. The homeowner's responsibility is to report and submit to priestly examination; the priest's responsibility is to investigate and render a verdict. This division of responsibility between lay report and priestly judgment is the community model for addressing potential defilement within the body.

verses.kjv_text: Then the priest shall command that they empty the house, before the priest go into it to see the plague, that all that is in the house be not made unclean: and afterward the priest shall go in to see the house:
verses.original_text: וְצִוָּה הַכֹּהֵן וּפִנּוּ אֶת הַבַּיִת בְּטֶרֶם יָבֹא הַכֹּהֵן לִרְאוֹת אֶת הַנֶּגַע וְלֹא יִטְמָא כָּל אֲשֶׁר בַּבַּיִת וְאַחַר כֵּן יָבֹא הַכֹּהֵן לִרְאוֹת אֶת הַבָּיִת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-tzivvah ha-kohen u-finnu et ha-bayit be-terem yavo ha-kohen lir'ot et ha-nega ve-lo yittama kol asher ba-bayit ve-achar ken yavo ha-kohen lir'ot et ha-bayit.
verses.study_note: The priest commands that the house be emptied (u-finnu — to clear out) before he enters to examine it. The reason is protective: 'that all that is in the house be not made unclean.' If the priest enters and declares the house defiled (tame), everything inside becomes defiled as well. By emptying the house first, the potentially contaminating verdict affects only the building, not its contents. This provision protects the property of the homeowner — a pastoral and economic consideration written into the law. The priest's examination comes only after the house is cleared, demonstrating priestly care for the community's welfare alongside the enforcement of purity standards.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall look on the plague, and, behold, if the plague be in the walls of the house with hollow strakes, greenish or reddish, which in sight are lower than the wall;
verses.original_text: וְרָאָה אֶת הַנֶּגַע וְהִנֵּה הַנֶּגַע בְּקִירֹת הַבַּיִת שְׁקַעֲרוּרֹת יְרַקְרַקֹּת אוֹ אֲדַמְדַּמֹּת וּמַרְאֵיהֶן שָׁפָל מִן הַקִּיר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ra'ah et ha-nega ve-hinneh ha-nega be-qirot ha-bayit sheqa'aru'rot yeraqraqqot o adamdammot u-mar'eihen shafal min ha-qir.
verses.study_note: The priest's examination of the house walls identifies the specific diagnostic presentation: 'hollow strakes' (sheqa'aru'rot — sunken, hollowed-out areas) that are 'greenish or reddish' (yeraqraqqot o adamdammot) and appear 'lower than the wall' (shafal min ha-qir — sunken below the surface). The color criteria (greenish/reddish) mirror the garment tzara'at of Lev 13:49, and the sunken appearance parallels the 'deeper than the skin' criterion of human tzara'at (Lev 13:3). The diagnostic consistency across persons, garments, and buildings demonstrates the unified theological framework: the same principles of defilement-detection apply to all potential harbors of tzara'at. The priestly diagnostic eye sees the same indicators regardless of the material examined.

verses.kjv_text: Then the priest shall go out of the house to the door of the house, and shut up the house seven days:
verses.original_text: וְיָצָא הַכֹּהֵן מִן הַבַּיִת אֶל פֶּתַח הַבָּיִת וְהִסְגִּיר אֶת הַבַּיִת שִׁבְעַת יָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-yatza ha-kohen min ha-bayit el petach ha-bayit ve-hisgir et ha-bayit shiv'at yamim.
verses.study_note: The priest exits to the door of the house (el petach ha-bayit) and 'shuts up' (hisgir) the house for seven days. The quarantine verb hisgir — used for shutting up a person with ambiguous tzara'at (Lev 13:4,5,11,21,26,31) — is now applied to a house. The house receives the same seven-day discernment quarantine as an ambiguous skin case. The door (petach) serves as the diagnostic boundary: the priest stands at the threshold, having seen the interior, and closes access from the outside. The house's quarantine period is not merely sanitary but covenantal — the same divinely appointed cycle of seven days that governs all Levitical discernment processes.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall come again the seventh day, and shall look: and, behold, if the plague be spread in the walls of the house;
verses.original_text: וְשָׁב הַכֹּהֵן בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי וְרָאָה וְהִנֵּה פָּשָׂה הַנֶּגַע בְּקִירֹת הַבָּיִת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-shav ha-kohen bayyom hash-shevi'i ve-ra'ah ve-hinneh fasah ha-nega be-qirot ha-bayit.
verses.study_note: On the seventh day the priest returns (ve-shav — literally 'returns,' the same shuv that carries repentance resonances) and examines the walls. If the plague has spread (fasah — the diagnostic spreading verb used throughout chapters 13-14), the next step is remedial: scraping and replacing the affected stones (vv.40-42). The spreading criterion is as decisive for houses as for persons and garments — an actively spreading plague requires intervention. The priest's return on the seventh day and his focused examination (ve-ra'ah — he looked) mirrors the pattern of every previous quarantine re-examination in the Levitical laws.

verses.kjv_text: Then the priest shall command that they take away the stones in which the plague is, and they shall cast them into an unclean place without the city:
verses.original_text: וְצִוָּה הַכֹּהֵן וְחִלְּצוּ אֶת הָאֲבָנִים אֲשֶׁר בָּהֵן הַנֶּגַע וְהִשְׁלִיכוּ אֹתָם אֶל מִחוּץ לָעִיר אֶל מָקוֹם טָמֵא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-tzivvah ha-kohen ve-chiltzu et ha-avanim asher bahen ha-nega ve-hishlichu otam el mi-chutz la-ir el maqom tame.
verses.study_note: The remedial procedure for the spreading house plague: the affected stones are removed (chaltzu — to pull out, to extract) and discarded 'outside the city in an unclean place' (el mi-chutz la-ir el maqom tame). The defiled stones go to the designated 'unclean place' — the spatial analog to the leper's dwelling 'outside the camp.' The affected material is surgically removed and placed where unclean things belong. This extraction procedure mirrors the surgical tearing-out of the affected portion of a garment (Lev 13:56) — partial excision in hopes of preserving the whole. The city has its 'outside' just as the camp does, and defiled material must exit to the appropriate location.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall cause the house to be scraped within round about, and they shall pour out the dust that they scrape off without the city into an unclean place:
verses.original_text: וְאֶת הַבַּיִת יַקְצִעַ מִבַּיִת סָבִיב וְשָׁפְכוּ אֶת הֶעָפָר אֲשֶׁר הִקְצוּ אֶל מִחוּץ לָעִיר אֶל מָקוֹם טָמֵא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-et ha-bayit yaqtzi'a mi-bayit saviv ve-shafechu et he-afar asher hiqtzu el mi-chutz la-ir el maqom tame.
verses.study_note: After removing the defiled stones, the interior walls are scraped (yaqtzi'a — to scrape, shave) all around (saviv — the same 'round about' thoroughness as the sprinkling of blood on the altar). The scrapings (dust, afar) are also taken outside the city to the unclean place. The thorough interior scraping removes all potentially affected plaster and organic material from the wall surfaces — a comprehensive decontamination of the house's interior. The scraped dust joins the defiled stones in the unclean place. The two-step remediation (remove stones + scrape plaster) is a thorough assault on the house tzara'at at both the structural (stones) and surface (plaster) levels.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall take other stones, and put them in the place of those stones; and he shall take other morter, and shall plaister the house.
verses.original_text: וְלָקְחוּ אֲבָנִים אֲחֵרוֹת וְהֵבִיאוּ אֶל תַּחַת הָאֲבָנִים וְעָפָר אַחֵר יִקַּח וְטָח אֶת הַבָּיִת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-laqechu avanim acherot ve-hevi'u el tachat ha-avanim ve-afar acher yiqqach ve-tach et ha-bayit.
verses.study_note: New stones (avanim acherot — other, different stones) replace the removed defiled ones, and new plaster (afar acher — different/new dust/mortar) replaces the scraped plaster. The word acher (other, different) appears twice, emphasizing the complete replacement of the defiled material with fresh, undefiled material. This is the building's equivalent of the leper's total shaving and new washing — a complete replacement of the affected structure. The house is rebuilt from within — the same walls, but with new stones and new plaster in place of the defiled components. Restoration is possible because the structure (the walls themselves) remains; only the defiled components are replaced.

verses.kjv_text: And if the plague come again, and break out in the house, after that he hath taken away the stones, and after he hath scraped the house, and after it is plaistered;
verses.original_text: וְאִם יָשׁוּב הַנֶּגַע וּפָרַח בַּבַּיִת אַחֲרֵי חִלֵּץ אֶת הָאֲבָנִים וְאַחֲרֵי הִקְצוֹת אֶת הַבַּיִת וְאַחֲרֵי הִטּוֹחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im yashuv ha-nega u-farach ba-bayit acharei chilletz et ha-avanim ve-acharei hiqtzot et ha-bayit ve-acharei hittoch.
verses.study_note: The chapter addresses the recurrence case: if after the stone-removal, scraping, and replastering, the plague returns (yashuv — returns, comes back) and breaks out (farach — blooms, sprouts, the same parach used for tzara'at in persons and garments), the situation is now terminal for the house. Three successive remediation steps are listed — removing stones, scraping, replastering — and if the plague returns after all three, there is no further treatment. The verb 'returns' (yashuv) carries the full weight of recurrence and persistence: the house's defilement is systemic, not localized. Like the skin tzara'at that reappears after cleansing (Lev 13:7; 13:35), recurrent house tzara'at indicates irredeemable defilement.

verses.kjv_text: Then the priest shall come and look, and, behold, if the plague be spread in the house, it is a fretting leprosy in the house: it is unclean.
verses.original_text: וּבָא הַכֹּהֵן וְרָאָה וְהִנֵּה פָּשָׂה הַנֶּגַע בַּבָּיִת צָרַעַת מַמְאֶרֶת הִוא בַּבַּיִת טָמֵא הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: U-va ha-kohen ve-ra'ah ve-hinneh fasah ha-nega ba-bayit tzara'at mam'eret hi ba-bayit tame hu.
verses.study_note: The priest re-examines the remediated house and finds the plague has spread — the definitive verdict follows: 'it is a fretting leprosy in the house' (tzara'at mam'eret hi ba-bayit). The same term mam'eret (malignant, corroding, fretting) applied to irredeemable garment tzara'at (Lev 13:51-52) is now applied to the house. 'It is unclean' (tame hu) — the house as a whole bears the unclean verdict, just as the person bears it. The structural parallel between the diagnosis formulas for persons (Lev 13:44), garments (Lev 13:51), and houses demonstrates the comprehensive and consistent logic of the defilement theology. What is mam'eret (malignant) in all three categories meets the same fate: destruction by fire (garments, Lev 13:52) or demolition (houses, Lev 14:45).

verses.kjv_text: And he shall break down the house, the stones of it, and the timber thereof, and all the morter of the house; and he shall carry them forth out of the city into an unclean place.
verses.original_text: וְנָתַץ אֶת הַבַּיִת אֶת אֲבָנָיו וְאֶת עֵצָיו וְאֵת כָּל עֲפַר הַבָּיִת וְהוֹצִיא אֶל מִחוּץ לָעִיר אֶל מָקוֹם טָמֵא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-nattatz et ha-bayit et avanahv ve-et etzav ve-et kol afar ha-bayit ve-hotzi el mi-chutz la-ir el maqom tame.
verses.study_note: Total demolition: 'he shall break down the house' (ve-nattatz et ha-bayit) — the verb nattatz means to demolish, tear down, overthrow. Stones, timber, and all the mortar/dust — every component — are taken outside the city to the unclean place. The thoroughness of the demolition ('its stones, and the timber thereof, and all the mortar') mirrors the thoroughness of the earlier remediation attempts (stones removed, interior scraped, new plaster applied). When partial remediation fails, total demolition follows. The same principle applies throughout the Levitical purity system: when the defiled thing cannot be made clean, it must be destroyed. The house that cannot be cleansed is torn down completely; nothing is salvaged for use within the clean city.

verses.kjv_text: Moreover he that goeth into the house all the while that it is shut up shall be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְהַבָּא אֶל הַבַּיִת כָּל יְמֵי הִסְגִּירוֹ יִטְמָא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ha-ba el ha-bayit kol yemei hisgiro yittama ad ha-arev.
verses.study_note: During the quarantine period, anyone who enters the house becomes unclean until evening (tame ad ha-erev). The house's quarantine status is contagious to those who enter it — the defilement transfers to occupants. This provision protects community members from casual exposure during the diagnostic period and discourages unauthorized entry into the sealed house. The 'until evening' (ad ha-erev) period is the standard short-duration impurity for minor contacts with defiled things or persons throughout Leviticus. The quarantine's contagion principle reinforces the seriousness of the house's uncertain status and the wisdom of the clearing procedure of verse 36.

verses.kjv_text: And he that lieth in the house shall wash his clothes; and he that eateth in the house shall wash his clothes.
verses.original_text: וְהַשֹּׁכֵב בַּבַּיִת יְכַבֵּס אֶת בְּגָדָיו וְהָאֹכֵל בַּבַּיִת יְכַבֵּס אֶת בְּגָדָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ha-shokhev ba-bayit yekhabbes et begadav ve-ha-okhel ba-bayit yekhabbes et begadav.
verses.study_note: Two specific activities within the quarantined house that increase the degree of contact — sleeping (shokhev) and eating (okhel) — require the additional purification of washing garments. These represent more prolonged contact with the potentially defiled house than a brief entry: sleeping implies extended full-body contact with the house environment; eating implies sustained residence and consumption within it. Those who merely entered become unclean until evening (v.46); those who slept or ate must additionally wash their garments. The graduated impurity response to different levels of exposure reflects the proportionality of the Levitical purity system.

verses.kjv_text: And if the priest shall come in, and look upon it, and, behold, the plague hath not spread in the house, after the house was plaistered: then the priest shall pronounce the house clean, because the plague is healed.
verses.original_text: וְאִם בֹּא יָבֹא הַכֹּהֵן וְרָאָה וְהִנֵּה לֹא פָשָׂה הַנֶּגַע בַּבַּיִת אַחֲרֵי הִטּוֹחַ אֶת הַבָּיִת וְטִהַר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַבַּיִת כִּי נִרְפָּא הַנֶּגַע׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im bo yavo ha-kohen ve-ra'ah ve-hinneh lo fasah ha-nega ba-bayit acharei hittoch et ha-bayit ve-tihar ha-kohen et ha-bayit ki nirpa ha-nega.
verses.study_note: The clean verdict for the remediated house: if after replastering the plague has not spread (lo fasah), the priest pronounces the house clean (ve-tihar ha-kohen et ha-bayit). The reason given: 'because the plague is healed' (ki nirpa ha-nega — niphal of rapa, the healing has been accomplished). The same niphal 'has been healed' used for the leper in verse 3 is now used for the house — the house receives the same healing declaration as the person. The parallel is complete: priest examines, plague identified, remediation attempted, re-examination confirms healing, clean verdict issued. Person, garment, and house are all subject to the same diagnostic and restorative process.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall take to cleanse the house two birds, and cedar wood, and scarlet, and hyssop:
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח לְחַטֵּא אֶת הַבַּיִת שְׁתֵּי צִפֳּרִים וְעֵץ אֶרֶז וּשְׁנִי תוֹלַעַת וְאֵזֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-laqach le-chatte' et ha-bayit shtei tzipporim ve-etz erez u-sheni tola'at ve-ezov.
verses.study_note: The cleansed house now requires its own two-bird ceremony — identical to the leper's cleansing ceremony of verses 4-7. The same four elements (two birds, cedar wood, scarlet thread, hyssop) are prescribed for both the cleansed leper and the cleansed house. This structural parallel is one of the most striking features of Leviticus 14: the same blood-and-release ceremony that restores a person to the covenant community also restores a house to the covenant community's use. Person and dwelling are treated with the same priestly ceremony — both are brought back through the same enacted parable of death and life, the same blood-covered living bird released into the open field.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall kill the one of the birds in an earthen vessel over running water:
verses.original_text: וְשָׁחַט אֶת הַצִּפֹּר הָאֶחָת אֶל כְּלִי חֶרֶשׂ עַל מַיִם חַיִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-shachat et ha-tzippor ha-echat el keli cheres al mayyim chayyim.
verses.study_note: The first bird is killed over living water in an earthen vessel — identical to verse 5. The repetition of the exact procedure for house cleansing (vv.50-53) mirrors the leper's ceremony (vv.5-7) with only the substitution of 'house' for 'person' in the application. This deliberate repetition demonstrates the unifying principle of the entire chapter: the same death-and-resurrection ceremony cleanses both persons and places. The ceremony's meaning is not exhausted by its application to human beings; the same typological drama of substitutionary death and liberating life applies to every sphere that defilement has entered.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall take the cedar wood, and the hyssop, and the scarlet, and the living bird, and dip them in the blood of the slain bird, and in the running water, and sprinkle the house seven times:
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח אֶת עֵץ הָאֶרֶז וְאֶת הָאֵזֹב וְאֵת שְׁנִי הַתּוֹלַעַת וְאֵת הַצִּפֹּר הַחַיָּה וְטָבַל אֹתָם בְּדַם הַצִּפֹּר הַשְּׁחוּטָה וּבַמַּיִם הַחַיִּים וְהִזָּה אֶל הַבַּיִת שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-laqach et etz ha-erez ve-et ha-ezov ve-et sheni ha-tola'at ve-et ha-tzippor ha-chayyah ve-taval otam be-dam ha-tzippor ha-shechutah u-va-mayyim ha-chayyim ve-hizzah el ha-bayit sheva pe'amim.
verses.study_note: The blood-and-water dipping of cedar, hyssop, scarlet, and the living bird mirrors verses 6-7 precisely. The sevenfold sprinkling is now directed toward the house (el ha-bayit) rather than the person (al ha-mittaher). The seven sprinklings of blood-water on the house are the house's equivalent of the leper's seven-fold sprinkling — the complete, covenantal application of the atonement to every part of the dwelling. The ceremony's direction from person to house demonstrates that the atonement's reach is not limited to human beings. As Hebrews 9:21-22 notes, Moses sprinkled both the tabernacle and all the vessels with blood — all the materials of the covenant community require the blood of purification.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall cleanse the house with the blood of the bird, and with the running water, and with the living bird, and with the cedar wood, and with the hyssop, and with the scarlet:
verses.original_text: וְחִטֵּא אֶת הַבַּיִת בְּדַם הַצִּפֹּר וּבַמַּיִם הַחַיִּים וּבַצִּפֹּר הַחַיָּה וּבְעֵץ הָאֶרֶז וּבָאֵזֹב וּבִשְׁנִי הַתּוֹלַעַת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-chitte' et ha-bayit be-dam ha-tzippor u-va-mayyim ha-chayyim u-va-tzippor ha-chayyah u-ve-etz ha-erez u-va-ezov u-vi-sheni ha-tola'at.
verses.study_note: The summary of the house cleansing elements: blood of the bird, living water, living bird, cedar wood, hyssop, and scarlet. All six elements are explicitly listed in the cleansing formula (ve-chitte' et ha-bayit — he cleanses the house using all of them together). The comprehensive listing functions as a legal checklist — every element must be present for the ceremony to be valid. The verb chitte' (to de-sin, to purify from sin) in the piel here applied to the house is the same intensive purification verb used for the sin offering's effect on persons. The house is 'de-sinned' — made sin-clean — by the blood, water, and all the symbolic materials together.

verses.kjv_text: But he shall let go the living bird out of the city into the open fields, and make an atonement for the house: and it shall be clean.
verses.original_text: וְשִׁלַּח אֶת הַצִּפֹּר הַחַיָּה אֶל מִחוּץ לָעִיר אֶל פְּנֵי הַשָּׂדֶה וְכִפֶּר עַל הַבַּיִת וְטָהֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-shillach et ha-tzippor ha-chayyah el mi-chutz la-ir el penei ha-sadeh ve-khipper al ha-bayit ve-taher.
verses.study_note: The living bird is released 'outside the city into the open fields' (el mi-chutz la-ir el penei ha-sadeh) — the house version substitutes 'outside the city' for the leper ceremony's 'over the open field' (v.7). The blood-covered bird carries the house's atonement away into the unlimited open space. The atonement declaration follows: ve-khipper al ha-bayit (he makes atonement for the house) — kipper is applied to the building. The final state: ve-taher — 'it shall be clean.' The house achieves the same tahor outcome as the person (v.20). The house's journey from defiled to examined to remediated to ceremonially cleansed to declared tahor mirrors exactly the person's journey from Leviticus 13 to 14:20.

verses.kjv_text: This is the law for all manner of plague of leprosy, and scall,
verses.original_text: זֹאת הַתּוֹרָה לְכָל נֶגַע הַצָּרַעַת וְלַנֶּתֶק׃
verses.transliteration: Zot ha-torah le-khol nega ha-tzara'at ve-la-neteq.
verses.study_note: The closing colophon begins (vv.54-57), providing a comprehensive summary of the entire body of legislation in chapters 13-14. 'This is the law' (zot ha-torah) — the standard Levitical colophon formula (cf. 13:59; 7:1,37; 6:9,14,25). The summary covers 'all manner of plague of leprosy' (le-khol nega ha-tzara'at — for every type of tzara'at) and the 'scall' (neteq — the scalp/beard condition of Lev 13:30-37). The colophon's comprehensive framing signals that what follows (vv.54-57) covers all the legislation of the preceding two chapters, not merely the house tzara'at section. The two-chapter unit (Lev 13-14) is treated as a single legislative block concluded by one summary formula.

verses.kjv_text: And for the leprosy of a garment, and of a house,
verses.original_text: וְלַצָּרַעַת הַבֶּגֶד וְלַבָּיִת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-la-tzara'at ha-beged ve-la-bayit.
verses.study_note: The colophon continues its comprehensive listing, here covering garment tzara'at (Lev 13:47-59) and house tzara'at (Lev 14:33-53).
