These two categories — garment and house — represent the extension of the tzara'at legislation beyond the human body to all material environments. The listing of garment and house alongside skin conditions in the closing summary demonstrates the theological unity of the entire two-chapter unit: the same defilement category (tzara'at) operates across persons, garments, and buildings, and the same priestly diagnostic and restorative authority governs all three domains. Nothing in the covenant community's material world is outside the scope of priestly oversight.

verses.kjv_text: And for a rising, and for a scab, and for a bright spot:
verses.original_text: וְלַשְּׂאֵת וְלַסַּפַּחַת וְלַבֶּהָרֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-la-se'et ve-la-sappachat ve-la-baheret.
verses.study_note: The colophon returns to the three original surface presentations of chapter 13:2 — the rising (se'et), the scab (sappachat), and the bright spot (baheret) — the three visual categories that open the entire body of legislation. By closing with these three terms, the colophon creates an inclusio with the legislation's opening: from the first three presentations to the last summary word, the law has addressed every manifestation of every category. The ring structure (opening terms → entire body of law → closing terms) is a literary device signaling completeness. What began with three observable skin conditions has been comprehensively addressed through two full chapters of priestly diagnostic and restorative legislation.

verses.kjv_text: To teach when it is unclean, and when it is clean: this is the law of leprosy.
verses.original_text: לְהוֹרֹת בְּיוֹם הַטָּמֵא וּבְיוֹם הַטָּהֹר זֹאת תּוֹרַת הַצָּרָעַת׃
verses.transliteration: Le-horot be-yom ha-tame u-ve-yom ha-tahor zot torat ha-tzara'at.
verses.study_note: The final verse states the dual purpose of the entire legislation: 'to teach when it is unclean, and when it is clean' (le-horot be-yom ha-tame u-ve-yom ha-tahor). The infinitive le-horot (to teach, to direct) is the root of the word Torah itself — the law is fundamentally instructional, designed to teach Israel the distinction between tame and tahor. The final summary formula 'this is the law of leprosy' (zot torat ha-tzara'at) closes both chapter 14 and the entire two-chapter legislative unit, gathering all of Leviticus 13-14 under one heading. The entire elaborately detailed body of law — 116 verses — has served one ultimate instructional purpose: to teach Israel when something is clean and when it is unclean.
verses.doctrinal_note: The closing statement — 'to teach when it is unclean, and when it is clean' — is the Levitical statement of the law's fundamental purpose as moral and spiritual instructor. Paul describes the law's instructional function in Galatians 3:24 — the law is a 'schoolmaster' (paidagogos) to bring us to Christ. The entire elaborate system of tzara'at diagnosis, quarantine, and restoration exists to teach two things: (1) the reality and seriousness of human defilement before a holy God, and (2) the divine provision of priestly mediation that enables restoration. Both lessons drive the reader to Christ: the defilement of sin requires the Great High Priest who diagnoses rightly (John 2:25), provides atonement personally (Heb 9:12), applies the blood to our ears, hands, and feet (1 Pet 1:2; Heb 10:22), anoints with the Spirit (Acts 10:38), and pronounces the final clean verdict — 'no condemnation' (Rom 8:1). Leviticus 13-14 is ultimately the extended answer to the question: can the unclean be made clean? And the answer is: yes, through the priest who goes outside the camp to find the excluded one.

meta.overview:
Leviticus 14 is the ceremonial counterpart to chapter 13. Where chapter 13 diagnosed uncleanness and expelled the leper from the community, chapter 14 prescribes the elaborate multi-stage ritual by which a healed leper is ceremonially cleansed and progressively restored to full covenant standing. The chapter divides into three major sections: the two-bird ceremony and initial purification outside the camp (vv.1-9); the eighth-day sacrificial offerings in the tabernacle court for full re-entry (vv.10-32, including a special provision for the poor at vv.21-32); and the diagnostic and purification laws for tzara'at in houses (vv.33-53), concluding with the chapter's colophon (vv.54-57).

The opening ritual is among the most visually and theologically arresting in all of Scripture. The priest goes outside the camp to the healed leper — Christ goes to the sinner, not vice versa — and performs a ceremony involving two living birds, cedar wood, scarlet thread, and hyssop. One bird is killed over living water; the other is dipped in the blood and released alive. The living bird carries the blood of the dead bird into the open field — a vivid enacted parable of substitutionary atonement and resurrection. The hyssop (associated with Passover blood-application, Exod 12:22, and with cleansing, Ps 51:7), cedar wood (incorruptibility), and scarlet thread (blood, sacrifice, and the scarlet cord of Rahab, Josh 2:18) together form a cluster of typological symbols whose NT fulfillment centers on Christ's death and resurrection.

The eighth-day ceremony (vv.10-20) brings the restored person into the tabernacle court for a complete set of offerings: sin offering, burnt offering, grain offering, and the unique guilt offering (asham) with its blood applied to the right ear, right thumb, and right great toe — the same triad as the priestly ordination ceremony of Leviticus 8. This application of blood and then oil to these extremities consecrates the restored person's hearing, working, and walking — the whole person is claimed for God's service through the priest's ministry. The oil poured on the head (v.18) mirrors the anointing of Aaron (Lev 8:12) — the healed leper receives an anointing that restores full covenant standing and prefigures the Spirit's anointing in the new covenant.

The house tzara'at section (vv.33-53) extends the principles of chapters 13-14 from persons and garments to dwelling places, using identical diagnostic and purification procedures. The house, like the body, can harbor defilement that spreads; the priest examines, quarantines, orders demolition where necessary, and declares clean where possible. Christologically, the entire chapter answers the leper's cry of chapter 13: there is a way back. The elaborate, costly, priest-mediated restoration points to the one Great High Priest whose cleansing is both complete and permanent (Heb 9:12-14).
meta.theme: The Priest-Mediated Restoration of the Unclean: Cleansing, Atonement, and Re-entry into the Holy Community
meta.keyWordMeaning: Cleansing/purification — from taher, to be clean, to purify. The goal of every ritual in Leviticus 14. The noun toharah appears in the opening formula 'this shall be the law of the leper in the day of his cleansing' (v.2) and governs the entire chapter. It denotes not merely physical washing but the comprehensive restoration of covenant standing — the removal of defilement and the re-establishment of the person's place within the holy community gathered around the divine presence. In the NT, this cleansing is accomplished once-for-all by Christ (Heb 9:14; 1 John 1:7) — the blood that does not need repeating.
outline: The Priest Goes Outside the Camp (vv.1-3): The fundamental direction of priestly grace — going out to the excluded
The Two-Bird Ceremony: Death, Blood, and Release (vv.4-7): Cedar wood, hyssop, scarlet thread, living water, and the enacted parable of death and resurrection
Initial Purification and Seven-Day Transition (vv.8-9): Washing, shaving, re-entry to the camp but not yet the tent
The Eighth-Day Sacrificial Restoration (vv.10-20): Guilt offering blood on ear/thumb/toe, oil anointing, sin and burnt offerings
Provision for the Poor Leper (vv.21-32): Scaled-down offerings maintaining the essential structure for those of limited means
Tzara'at in Houses: Diagnosis and Procedure (vv.33-47): The diagnostic examination, quarantine, and re-examination of house plagues
Cleansing the Affected House (vv.48-53): The two-bird ceremony applied to the house — parallel with the leper's initial cleansing
The Closing Colophon (vv.54-57): Summary formula covering all categories of the tzara'at legislation

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses and to Aaron, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל אַהֲרֹן לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh ve'el-Aharon lemor.
verses.study_note: The dual address to both Moses and Aaron is significant and relatively rare in Leviticus — it occurs at key junctures where both the civil-covenant mediator (Moses) and the cultic-priestly mediator (Aaron) are jointly responsible for the legislation. Compare Leviticus 11:1 (clean/unclean animals) and 13:1 (skin diseases). The bodily discharge laws require both dimensions of Israelite leadership because they touch both communal holiness (Moses's domain) and sanctuary access (Aaron's domain). The chapter's content — the most physically intimate legislation in the Torah — is governed by the same solemn formula of divine address that opens laws about the tabernacle and the Day of Atonement. Nothing in human bodily experience falls outside the reach of God's covenantal ordering.

verses.kjv_text: Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, When any man hath a running issue out of his flesh, because of his issue he is unclean.
verses.original_text: דַּבְּרוּ אֶל בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַאֲמַרְתֶּם אֲלֵהֶם אִישׁ אִישׁ כִּי יִהְיֶה זָב מִבְּשָׂרוֹ זוֹבוֹ טָמֵא הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Dabberu el-benei Yisra'el va'amartem alehem ish ish ki yihyeh zav mibesaro zovo tame hu.
verses.study_note: The chapter's first category is introduced: the male zov (running discharge, pathological genital emission). "Any man" (ish ish — the doubled form is emphatic and inclusive: every man, any man at all) who has a "running issue out of his flesh" (zav mibesaro) is declared ritually impure. The word zov comes from zuv (to flow, gush), describing an abnormal, persistent discharge — distinguished from the normal emission of v.16 by its chronic, ongoing character. Ancient medicine has identified this with gonorrhea or other urogenital infections producing persistent discharge. The declaration "tame hu" (he is unclean) is categorical and immediate: the condition produces impurity regardless of how it was contracted. The phrase "out of his flesh" (mibesaro) — basar (flesh) is the standard term for the body, including specifically the genitals (Lev 6:10; Ezek 16:26). The bodily source of the discharge is the seat of the impurity.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Levitical category of bodily discharge impurity reflects the theological principle that the human body — fallen in Adam (Gen 3; Rom 5:12) — is subject to disorder, malfunction, and the intrusion of death even into the processes of life and generation. The abnormal discharge is not the result of a specific sin but is a consequence of the general condition of fallen creaturely existence. This connects to Paul's description of the body as subject to "corruption" (1 Cor 15:42-44) and to the creation's "bondage of corruption" awaiting redemption (Rom 8:21). The purification laws anticipate the ultimate bodily redemption — the resurrection of the body — when all such disorders will be permanently healed (Rev 21:4; 1 Cor 15:53-54).

verses.kjv_text: And this shall be his uncleanness in his issue: whether his flesh run with his issue, or his flesh be stopped from his issue, it is his uncleanness.
verses.original_text: וְזֹאת תִּהְיֶה טֻמְאָתוֹ בְּזוֹבוֹ רָר בְּשָׂרוֹ אֶת זוֹבוֹ אוֹ הֶחְתִּים בְּשָׂרוֹ מִזּוֹבוֹ טֻמְאָתוֹ הִיא׃
verses.transliteration: Vezot tihyeh tum'ato bezovo rar besaro et-zovo o hechtim besaro mizzovo tum'ato hi.
verses.study_note: A technical clarification: the man's impurity applies whether the discharge is "running" (rar besaro et-zovo — his flesh flows with his discharge) or "stopped up" (hechtim besaro mizzovo — his flesh is blocked/clogged from its discharge). This addresses the full clinical range of the condition: both the active flowing state and the intermittent or temporarily blocked state still constitute the zov impurity. The word rar (to flow, trickle, ooze) describes a continuous or repetitive seeping. The word hechtim (to stop up, seal, block — from chatam, to seal/close) describes a temporary obstruction that does not resolve the condition. The legal principle established is important: it is the underlying condition (the zov state) that defines the impurity, not merely the moment of active discharge. This prevents a man from claiming he is clean during a symptom-free interval of a chronic condition.

verses.kjv_text: Every bed, whereon he lieth that hath the issue, is unclean: and every thing, whereon he sitteth, shall be unclean.
verses.original_text: כָּל הַמִּשְׁכָּב אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁכַּב עָלָיו הַזָּב יִטְמָא וְכָל הַכְּלִי אֲשֶׁר יֵשֵׁב עָלָיו יִטְמָא׃
verses.transliteration: Kol hammishkav asher yishkav alav hazzav yitamma vekol hakli asher yeshev alav yitamma.
verses.study_note: The impurity of the zav (person with discharge) extends to objects he contacts — specifically his bed (mishkav — lying place) and any object upon which he sits (keli — vessel, utensil, object). This principle of transmitted impurity through contact is a foundational element of the Levitical system. The zav does not contaminate by intention but by the objective state of his body; the objects he uses most intimately — his bed and his seat — become secondary sources of impurity. This secondary contamination could then affect anyone who touched these objects (vv.5-6), creating a chain of transmitted impurity that required management. The practical social significance was considerable: a person with chronic discharge needed to be carefully managed within the community to prevent inadvertent contamination of others, particularly those approaching the sanctuary.

verses.kjv_text: And whosoever toucheth his bed shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִגַּע בְּמִשְׁכָּבוֹ יְכַבֵּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם וְטָמֵא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ish asher yigga bemishkavo yekhabbes begadav verachatz bammayyim vetame ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: Anyone who touches the bed of the zav contracts secondary impurity — milder than the primary impurity of the zav himself but still requiring action. The prescribed response is twofold: wash one's clothes (yekhabbes begadav) and bathe in water (rachatz bammayyim), after which one is unclean only until evening. The time-bound nature of the impurity (until evening — ad ha'arev) is important: it is not permanent, and it resolves at sundown without requiring sacrifice. The evening boundary (erev — evening, sunset) as the transition point between ritual states appears throughout Leviticus and Numbers. The Israelite day began at sunset (Gen 1: "evening and morning"), so sundown marked the beginning of a new day and, ritually, the transition to cleanness. This pattern of contamination-washing-waiting-cleansing established a daily rhythm of attentiveness to holiness in ordinary communal life.

verses.kjv_text: And he that sitteth on any thing whereon he sat that hath the issue shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְהַיֹּשֵׁב עַל הַכְּלִי אֲשֶׁר יֵשֵׁב עָלָיו הַזָּב יְכַבֵּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם וְטָמֵא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayoshev al-hakeli asher yeshev alav hazzav yekhabbes begadav verachatz bammayyim vetame ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: The same purification requirement — laundering clothes, bathing in water, impure until evening — applies to whoever sits on any object upon which the zav has sat. The parallel structure of vv.5-6 establishes a careful symmetry: the bed (lying, rest, v.5) and the seat (sitting, activity, v.6) are the two primary contact objects, and both transmit identical secondary impurity with identical remedies. The repetition of the formula is characteristic of Levitical legal style: comprehensive enumeration through parallelism rather than compressed general statement. Every specific case is named and addressed individually, establishing that no corner of daily life is outside the scope of covenant holiness management.

verses.kjv_text: And he that toucheth the flesh of him that hath the issue shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְהַנֹּגֵעַ בִּבְשַׂר הַזָּב יְכַבֵּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם וְטָמֵא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vehannogea bivshar hazzav yekhabbes begadav verachatz bammayyim vetame ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: Direct bodily contact with the zav — touching his flesh (bivshar hazzav) — produces the same secondary impurity as touching his bed or seat. The three cases now covered (bed, seat, body) represent an escalating directness of contact: first the objects he uses in sleep, then the objects he uses in activity, then his actual body. All produce identical secondary impurity with identical remedies. The phrase "touching the flesh" (nogea bivshar) is particularly significant in light of Mark 5:25-34: the woman with the chronic discharge reached out and touched the garment of Jesus — an act that in the Levitical system should have transmitted her impurity to Him. Instead, healing power flowed from Him to her. Jesus reverses the direction of holiness-transfer entirely: instead of the unclean contaminating the clean, the holy cleanses the unclean.

verses.kjv_text: And if he that hath the issue spit upon him that is clean; then he shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְכִי יָרֹק הַזָּב בַּטָּהוֹר וְכִבֶּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם וְטָמֵא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi yarok hazzav battahor vekhibbes begadav verachatz bammayyim vetame ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: Spitting (rok — to spit) is added to the list of contaminating contacts. This is not merely about hygiene but about the theology of the body's fluids: saliva, like blood and semen, is a bodily fluid that in this context carries the zov impurity. The ancient world widely understood saliva as a medium connected to the person's inner life and vitality (cf. Job 30:10; Isa 50:6 — spitting as contemptuous attack). In the Levitical system, any bodily discharge from the zav transmits his impurity. The remedy remains unchanged: washing clothes and bathing in water, unclean until evening. Notably, in the New Testament, Jesus spits to heal: in Mark 7:33 (deaf-mute) and John 9:6 (blind man) He uses His own saliva as the instrument of miraculous healing — a deliberate reversal of the contamination principle.

verses.kjv_text: And what saddle soever he rideth upon that hath the issue shall be unclean.
verses.original_text: וְכָל הַמֶּרְכָּב אֲשֶׁר יִרְכַּב עָלָיו הַזָּב יִטְמָא׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol hammerkhav asher yirkhav alav hazzav yitamma.
verses.study_note: The saddle (merkav — riding equipment, from rakhav, to ride) is added to the list of contaminated objects. Like the bed and seat, the saddle is an object bearing the sustained bodily contact and weight of the zav. The principle extends from domestic furniture (bed, seat) to outdoor transport — covering the full range of objects that support the human body in its daily activities. The Levitical legislation is comprehensively systematic: the zav's impurity follows him into every sphere of life, indoors and outdoors, at rest and in motion. This comprehensive scope ensures that Israel's social fabric — which was also the covenant community fabric — was permeated by the consciousness of holiness at every point.

verses.kjv_text: And whosoever toucheth any thing that was under him shall be unclean until the even: and he that beareth any of those things shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְכָל הַנֹּגֵעַ בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר יִהְיֶה תַחְתָּיו יִטְמָא עַד הָעָרֶב וְהַנֹּשֵׂא אוֹתָם יְכַבֵּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם וְטָמֵא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol hannogea bekhol asher yihyeh tachtav yitamma ad ha'arev vehannose otam yekhabbes begadav verachatz bammayyim vetame ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: Two new sub-cases are introduced: (1) whoever touches anything that was under the zav (the contaminated objects of vv.4-9) becomes impure until evening; (2) whoever carries (nose — bears, lifts) those contaminated objects must additionally launder their clothes and bathe, not just wait until evening. The distinction is instructive: mere touching of a contaminated object produces minor impurity (wait until evening); carrying or bearing the object produces slightly more significant impurity (washing required as well). The principle that handling and bearing contaminated objects more thoroughly involves the person is consistent with the graduated system throughout Leviticus 11-15. "Bearing" (nasa) the contaminated object is a more sustained, more complete form of contact than momentary touching.

verses.kjv_text: And whomsoever he toucheth that hath the issue, and hath not rinsed his hands in water, he shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר יִגַּע בּוֹ הַזָּב וְיָדָיו לֹא שָׁטַף בַּמָּיִם וְכִבֶּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם וְטָמֵא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol asher yigga bo hazzav veyadav lo shataf bammayyim vekhibbes begadav verachatz bammayyim vetame ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: A nuanced legal case: whoever the zav touches — provided the zav has not rinsed his hands in water first — becomes impure. The conditional clause "and hath not rinsed his hands" (veyadav lo shataf bammayyim) implies that if the zav had rinsed his hands first, the contact might not produce impurity in the person touched. This is a remarkable provision: it places a degree of responsibility and agency on the zav himself to manage his impurity through a simple preparatory act (hand-rinsing) before touching others. The Levitical system is not simply a binary clean/unclean toggle but includes graduated precautions. The hand-rinsing practice connects to the extensive handwashing traditions of Second Temple Judaism (Mark 7:3-5) and to the Christian practice in 1 Timothy 2:8 ("lifting up holy hands").

verses.kjv_text: And the vessel of earth, that he toucheth which hath the issue, shall be broken: and every vessel of wood shall be rinsed in water.
verses.original_text: וּכְלִי חֶרֶשׂ אֲשֶׁר יִגַּע בּוֹ הַזָּב יִשָּׁבֵר וְכָל כְּלִי עֵץ יִשָּׁטֵף בַּמָּיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Ukeli cheres asher yigga bo hazzav yishshaver vekhol keli etz yishshatef bammayyim.
verses.study_note: Two categories of vessels receive different treatment based on their material: earthenware (keli cheres — clay vessel) must be broken and destroyed; wooden vessels (keli etz) need only be rinsed in water. This distinction appears elsewhere in Leviticus (6:28; 11:32-35) and reflects the practical reality that porous clay absorbs impurity into its substance and cannot be fully cleansed, while smooth wood can be washed clean. The breaking of contaminated earthenware is the most drastic purification measure in this chapter — it acknowledges that some contamination cannot be reversed but must be destroyed. Theologically, the breakable earthen vessel resonates with Paul's image in 2 Corinthians 4:7: "we have this treasure in earthen vessels" — the human body as fragile clay that holds divine glory, subject to breakage and death, awaiting resurrection.

verses.kjv_text: And when he that hath an issue is cleansed of his issue; then he shall number to himself seven days for his cleansing, and wash his clothes, and bathe his flesh in running water, and shall be clean.
verses.original_text: וְכִי יִטְהַר הַזָּב מִזּוֹבוֹ וְסָפַר לוֹ שִׁבְעַת יָמִים לְטָהֳרָתוֹ וְכִבֶּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ בְּשָׂרוֹ בְּמַיִם חַיִּים וְטָהֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi yithar hazzav mizzovo vesafar lo shivat yamim letaharato vekhibbes begadav verachatz besaro bemmayyim chayyim vetaher.
verses.study_note: The cleansing of the zav begins when the discharge ceases. He then counts seven days for his cleansing (shivat yamim letaharato) — the seven-day count is a watch period confirming the discharge has genuinely stopped. On the seventh day he washes his clothes and bathes his flesh in "running water" (mayyim chayyim — living water). The phrase mayyim chayyim is the chapter's most theologically rich term: literally "living waters," waters that flow, spring water, a running stream as opposed to still cistern water. The use of living/flowing water for the most significant purifications in Leviticus (cf. Lev 14:5-6,50-51; Num 19:17) establishes a water-theology that flows directly into Jesus's self-identification in John 4:10-14 and 7:37-38 as the source of "living water." The life-giving, purifying, flowing water of Leviticus becomes in Christ the spring of water welling up to eternal life.
verses.doctrinal_note: The requirement of "living water" (mayyim chayyim) for the most significant purification in the Levitical system is one of the Old Testament's most profound Christological anticipations. John 4:10-14 records Jesus's offer of "living water" (hydor zon — the exact Greek equivalent of mayyim chayyim) to the Samaritan woman. John 7:37-38 extends this: "If any man thirst, let him come unto me and drink. He that believeth on me... out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water." Jesus is the mayyim chayyim — the living, flowing, purifying water — that the Levitical system pointed toward. Zechariah 13:1 speaks of "a fountain opened to the house of David for sin and for uncleanness" (tum'ah — the very word of Leviticus 15). Ezekiel 47 describes the river of life flowing from the temple. Revelation 22:1 consummates it: "a pure river of water of life, clear as crystal, proceeding out of the throne of God and of the Lamb."

verses.kjv_text: And on the eighth day he shall take to him two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, and come before the LORD unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, and give them unto the priest:
verses.original_text: וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי יִקַּח לוֹ שְׁתֵּי תֹרִים אוֹ שְׁנֵי בְנֵי יוֹנָה וּבָא לִפְנֵי יְהוָה אֶל פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וּנְתָנָם אֶל הַכֹּהֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Uvayom hashemini yikkach lo shetei torim o shenei venei yonah uva lifnei YHWH el petach ohel mo'ed unatanam el hakkohen.
verses.study_note: After the seven-day counting period and bathing, on the eighth day the cleansed zav must bring two birds to the tabernacle. The eighth day again signals new beginning — the threshold of full restoration after the seven-day completion cycle (compare circumcision on the eighth day, Lev 12:3; the priests' ordination completed on the eighth day, Lev 9:1; the leper's cleansing on the eighth day, Lev 14:10). The birds — two turtledoves or two young pigeons — are the standard poor-man's offering (Lev 12:8), making this sacrifice accessible to all Israelites regardless of economic means. The man must come "before the LORD" (lifnei YHWH) — approaching the threshold of divine presence — signaling that the completion of purification is not merely a private health matter but a covenant-relational event: full restoration of access to God's presence.
verses.doctrinal_note: The eighth-day sacrifice after the seven-day counting period embeds the theology of new creation within the purification process. Seven represents completion of the created order (Gen 2:2-3); eight represents the threshold beyond it — new beginning, new creation, new covenant access. Christ's resurrection on the first day of the week is simultaneously the eighth day: beyond and after the completed seven-day cycle of the old creation, inaugurating the new. First Peter 3:20-21 makes the typological connection explicit in the context of baptism: as Noah's family was saved "through water" and emerged into a new beginning, so baptism now saves "by the resurrection of Jesus Christ." Every eighth-day purification in Leviticus points to the ultimate eighth-day event — resurrection — that renders all such purifications fulfilled.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall offer them, the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering; and the priest shall make an atonement for him before the LORD for his issue.
verses.original_text: וְעָשָׂה אֹתָם הַכֹּהֵן אֶחָד חַטָּאת וְאֶחָד עֹלָה וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן לִפְנֵי יְהוָה מִזּוֹבוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asah otam hakkohen echad chatta't ve'echad olah vekhipper alav hakkohen lifnei YHWH mizzovo.
verses.study_note: The priest offers the two birds: one as a sin offering (chatta't), one as a burnt offering (olah). This pairing — sin offering and burnt offering — mirrors the purification sacrifice for childbirth (Lev 12:6-7) and the leper's cleansing (Lev 14:19-20), establishing it as the standard two-part atonement for restored access to God's presence. The sin offering addresses the ritual defilement and its social-spiritual consequences; the burnt offering expresses total renewed dedication and consecration. The priest makes atonement (yekapper — kaphar) "before the LORD for his issue" (lifnei YHWH mizzovo) — the atonement is specifically applied to the condition of the discharge, not to moral sin per se. The zov was not sinful but it was a condition requiring covenantal remedy, and that remedy is priestly atonement.
verses.doctrinal_note: The requirement of priestly atonement for the cleansed zav — despite the fact that the discharge was not a moral sin — reveals the profound Levitical theology of the human condition before a holy God. Even the involuntary physical disorders of fallen humanity require priestly mediation and sacrificial atonement before full access to God's presence is restored. This is a powerful statement of total human need: not only our deliberate sins but our very bodily state as fallen creatures requires atonement. Hebrews 9:13-14 applies this directly: "if the blood of bulls and of goats, and the ashes of an heifer sprinkling the unclean, sanctifieth to the purifying of the flesh: how much more shall the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God, purge your conscience from dead works to serve the living God?"

verses.kjv_text: And if any man’s seed of copulation go out from him, then he shall wash all his flesh in water, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ כִּי תֵצֵא מִמֶּנּוּ שִׁכְבַת זָרַע וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם אֶת כָּל בְּשָׂרוֹ וְטָמֵא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ish ki tetze mimmennu shikhvat zara verachatz bammayyim et-kol besaro vetame ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: The second male category: the normal seminal emission (shikhvat zera — lying of seed, seminal discharge). This produces a milder and simpler form of impurity than the zov: the man need only wash his entire body in water and is unclean until evening — no counting of days, no sacrifice required. The phrase "seed of copulation" (shikhvat zera) is the standard euphemism for semen throughout Leviticus (15:16-18,32; 19:20; 22:4) and Numbers (5:13). The normalcy of this impurity and the simplicity of its remedy (water + sunset) confirms that Leviticus does not view sexuality as inherently sinful. Rather, the substances most intimately connected with the transmission of life — semen and blood — exist within the orbit of divine holiness and require simple ritual management. No moral judgment is implied: the man is not guilty; he is temporarily in a ritual state requiring simple cleansing.

verses.kjv_text: And every garment, and every skin, whereon is the seed of copulation, shall be washed with water, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְכָל בֶּגֶד וְכָל עוֹר אֲשֶׁר יִהְיֶה עָלָיו שִׁכְבַת זָרַע וְכֻבַּס בַּמַּיִם וְטָמֵא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol beged vekhol or asher yihyeh alav shikhvat zara vekhubas bammayyim vetame ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: The contaminated objects in the case of normal emission are simpler than those of the zov: any garment (beged) or skin/leather (or) that has seminal fluid on it must be washed in water and is impure until evening. The material category "skin/leather" (or) refers to leather garments and articles, which like wood can be washed clean (unlike clay, which must be broken). The simplicity and brevity of vv.16-17 relative to vv.2-15 underscores the proportionality of the Levitical system: abnormal, chronic conditions require more extensive processes; normal, occasional conditions require minimal response. The system is calibrated to the degree and duration of the condition.

verses.kjv_text: The woman also with whom man shall lie with seed of copulation, they shall both bathe themselves in water, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְאִשָּׁה אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁכַּב אִישׁ אֹתָהּ שִׁכְבַת זָרַע וְרָחֲצוּ בַמַּיִם וְטָמְאוּ עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ishah asher yishkav ish otah shikhvat zara verachatzu bammayyim vetame'u ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: Both the man and woman who have sexual intercourse (yishkav ish otah — a man lies with her) must bathe in water and are impure until evening. The equal application of the purification requirement to both partners is theologically significant: neither partner is more defiled than the other; both are equally subject to the same simple, temporary impurity and the same simple remedy. This equality before the purity law is consistent with the Genesis 1:27 framework of equal creation in the image of God and the covenantal complementarity of Genesis 2:24. The verse also confirms, implicitly, that marital sexual intercourse within the covenant is entirely legitimate — the impurity is minimal (until evening only, no sacrifice) and is simply a bodily fact requiring the same treatment as many other normal bodily conditions in Leviticus.
verses.doctrinal_note: The equal purification requirement for both man and woman after marital intercourse is a subtle but powerful affirmation of the dignity and equality of both sexes before the covenant law. Neither partner is viewed as more defiled, more guilty, or more responsible than the other. This stands in contrast to ancient Near Eastern cultures where women were typically viewed as more ritually dangerous in sexual contexts. Hebrews 13:4 provides the New Covenant summary: "Marriage is honourable in all, and the bed undefiled: but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge." The word translated "undefiled" (amiantos) means unpolluted, unstained — precisely the opposite of the Levitical tum'ah, and the New Testament's declaration that the purification requirements of Leviticus 15:18 are fulfilled and transcended in Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And if a woman have an issue, and her issue in her flesh be blood, she shall be put apart seven days: and whosoever toucheth her shall be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְאִשָּׁה כִּי תִהְיֶה זָבָה דָּם יִהְיֶה זֹבָהּ בִּבְשָׂרָהּ שִׁבְעַת יָמִים תִּהְיֶה בְנִדָּתָהּ וְכָל הַנֹּגֵעַ בָּהּ יִטְמָא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ishah ki tihyeh zavah dam yihyeh zovah bivsarah shivat yamim tihyeh veniddatah vekhol hannogea bah yitamma ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: The chapter now pivots to the female parallel: the niddah, the monthly menstrual discharge. "If a woman have an issue, and her issue in her flesh be blood" — the defining characteristic is blood (dam), the fluid Leviticus consistently treats as the seat of life belonging to God (Lev 17:11). During the seven days of her niddah (separation/menstruation) she is in a state of ritual impurity. Anyone who touches her becomes impure until evening — the lightest secondary contamination in the chapter. The word niddah (from nadad, to be separated, removed) is the standard term for menstrual separation throughout the Torah, now applied to the period during which the woman is set apart from full community participation. The niddah period is the most regularly recurring ritual state in Levitical law — affecting every adult Israelite woman monthly. The Levitical system thus built a monthly rhythm of impurity and cleansing into the communal life of the covenant people.
verses.doctrinal_note: The niddah legislation, like the other bodily discharge laws, is best understood theologically through the lens of blood-theology (Lev 17:11) rather than social suppression of women. Blood is the medium of life, atonement, and covenant — it belongs to God. The monthly discharge of blood places the woman within the ritual sphere of life-blood, requiring the same managed process of separation and restoration that governs all contact with the substance God has reserved as the means of atonement. The Mishnah's tractate Niddah and centuries of Jewish practice attest to the central communal and spiritual significance of this law in the life of Jewish women. The New Testament's silence on niddah, together with Christ's healing of the woman with the twelve-year flow (Mark 5:25-34), signals the fulfillment of the entire impurity system in His person — He who is the ultimate blood-atonement renders the management of blood-impurity both honored in its original purpose and transcended in its ultimate fulfillment.

verses.kjv_text: And every thing that she lieth upon in her separation shall be unclean: every thing also that she sitteth upon shall be unclean.
verses.original_text: וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר תִּשְׁכַּב עָלָיו בְּנִדָּתָהּ יִטְמָא וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר תֵּשֵׁב עָלָיו יִטְמָא׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol asher tishkav alav beniddatah yitamma vekhol asher teshev alav yitamma.
verses.study_note: The contamination pattern for the niddah exactly mirrors that of the male zav: her bed and her sitting-objects become secondary sources of impurity. The deliberate structural parallelism is theologically intentional — male and female are subject to identical categories of impurity from bodily discharge, treated symmetrically throughout the chapter. The repetition of yitamma (shall be unclean) for both objects mirrors vv.4-6 precisely. This structural echo across the chapter's male and female sections reinforces the anthropological equality embedded in the Levitical system: the same theology of the body and its fluids applies to both sexes, governed by the same principles and subject to the same remedies.

verses.kjv_text: And whosoever toucheth her bed shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְכָל הַנֹּגֵעַ בְּמִשְׁכָּבָהּ יְכַבֵּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם וְטָמֵא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol hannogea bemishkavah yekhabbes begadav verachatz bammayyim vetame ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: Whoever touches the niddah's bed must wash clothes and bathe in water — exactly the same remedy as for touching the male zav's bed (v.5). The formula is word-for-word identical: the structural parallelism reaches down to the precise wording of the purification requirements. This careful literary mirroring is the Levitical legislator's way of establishing the principle of equal treatment under the purity law. The secondary contamination of a woman's menstrual bed is treated identically to the secondary contamination of a man's discharge-bed. The remedy is equally accessible to all: washing clothes, bathing in water, waiting for sundown.

verses.kjv_text: And whosoever toucheth any thing that she sat upon shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְכָל הַנֹּגֵעַ בְּכָל כְּלִי אֲשֶׁר תֵּשֵׁב עָלָיו יְכַבֵּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם וְטָמֵא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol hannogea bekhol keli asher teshev alav yekhabbes begadav verachatz bammayyim vetame ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: The sitting-object parallel to v.6: whoever touches any object upon which the niddah has sat must launder clothes and bathe, becoming impure until evening. The three-part secondary contamination chain — bed, seat, direct touch — applies in exactly parallel fashion to the niddah as to the male zav (vv.5-7). The Levitical legal structure operates by establishing a male case and then immediately providing the female parallel, ensuring the comprehensive scope of the purity regulations and their even-handed application to all Israelites.

verses.kjv_text: And if it be on her bed, or on any thing whereon she sitteth, when he toucheth it, he shall be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְאִם עַל הַמִּשְׁכָּב הוּא אוֹ עַל הַכְּלִי אֲשֶׁר הִיא יֹשֶׁבֶת עָלָיו בְּנָגְעוֹ בוֹ יִטְמָא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im al-hammishkav hu o al-hakeli asher hi yoshevet alav benag'o vo yitamma ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: A refinement: even if the person merely touches the contaminated object that is already on her bed — or on the object she sits on — without directly touching the niddah herself, the result is impurity until evening. This verse addresses the edge case of indirect contact: touching a contaminated object that is already resting on another contaminated object. The impurity-result is minor (until evening only, no washing required here) — the lightest possible contamination. The Levitical system's characteristic thoroughness ensures that every plausible scenario of contact is addressed, creating a comprehensive framework that makes its underlying theological principles unavoidable in daily life.

verses.kjv_text: And if any man lie with her at all, and her flowers be upon him, he shall be unclean seven days; and all the bed whereon he lieth shall be unclean.
verses.original_text: וְאִם שָׁכֹב יִשְׁכַּב אִישׁ אֹתָהּ וּתְהִי נִדָּתָהּ עָלָיו וְטָמֵא שִׁבְעַת יָמִים וְכָל הַמִּשְׁכָּב אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁ�כַּב עָלָיו יִטְמָא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im shakhov yishkav ish otah utehi niddatah alav vetame shivat yamim vekhol hammishkav asher yishkav alav yitamma.
verses.study_note: If a man has sexual intercourse with a woman during her niddah, he contracts her full seven-day impurity — "her niddah shall be upon him." The infinitive absolute (shakhov yishkav — "lying he shall lie") emphasizes the deliberateness and fullness of the act. His impurity is not the minor "until evening" of other secondary contacts but the full seven days of the niddah herself — he has absorbed the primary impurity entirely. His bed also becomes contaminated. Leviticus 18:19 and 20:18 treat intercourse during the niddah as a prohibited act; here the focus is the ritual consequence rather than the prohibition. The "flowers" in the KJV ("her flowers be upon him") is a somewhat archaic translation of niddatah (her separation/impurity) — the underlying Hebrew is simply "her niddah shall be upon him."
verses.doctrinal_note: The seven-day transmission of the woman's full impurity to the man who lies with her during the niddah is the harshest secondary impurity case in the chapter. It illustrates a principle that runs throughout Leviticus: proximity to impurity through intimate contact transmits impurity most completely. This principle has its positive counterpart in the transmission of holiness through the most intimate contact of all — union with Christ. Romans 6:3-5 describes baptism as being "planted together in the likeness of his death" and resurrection: the believer's union with Christ is so complete that His death and resurrection are transmitted as the believer's own righteousness. Second Peter 1:4 speaks of believers becoming "partakers of the divine nature." What the niddah's impurity transmitted negatively, Christ's holiness transmits positively — and permanently.

verses.kjv_text: And if a woman have an issue of her blood many days out of the time of her separation, or if it run beyond the time of her separation; all the days of the issue of her uncleanness shall be as the days of her separation: she shall be unclean.
verses.original_text: וְאִשָּׁה כִּי יָזוּב זוֹב דָּמָהּ יָמִים רַבִּים בְּלֹא עֶת נִדָּתָהּ אוֹ כִי תָזוּב עַל נִדָּתָהּ כָּל יְמֵי זוֹב טֻמְאָתָהּ כִּימֵי נִדָּתָהּ תִּהְיֶה טְמֵאָה הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ishah ki yazuv zov damah yamim rabbim belo et niddatah o ki tazuv al niddatah kol yemei zov tum'atah kimei niddatah tihyeh teme'ah hi.
verses.study_note: The fourth and final category: the female zov, the abnormal chronic blood discharge outside or beyond the normal niddah period. "Many days" (yamim rabbim) and "out of the time of her separation" (belo et niddatah — not at the time of her niddah) distinguish this from normal menstruation. This condition — persistent, non-cyclic uterine bleeding — is precisely the condition of the woman in Mark 5:25-34 who had "an issue of blood twelve years" and is described with the vocabulary of Leviticus 15 (the Greek rhusei haimatos, flow of blood, renders precisely yamim rabbim). For every day of her abnormal discharge, she is in the full niddah-state of impurity. The twelve-year sufferer of Mark 5 had therefore been ritually impure — excluded from full covenant community participation and sanctuary access — for twelve continuous years when she reached out to touch Jesus.
verses.doctrinal_note: Mark 5:25-34 is the definitive New Testament commentary on Leviticus 15:25-30. The woman with the twelve-year issue of blood represents the complete Levitical zov sufferer: ceremonially unclean for over a decade, isolated from full community life, excluded from the sanctuary, having spent all her resources on physicians without relief. Her touch of Jesus's garment should, in the Levitical system, have transmitted her impurity to Him. Instead, power (dunamis) went out from Jesus and she was "made whole" (sesostai — saved, healed, made complete). Jesus does not become unclean; she becomes clean. Christ is the one person in whom the direction of holiness-transmission is permanently reversed: He does not absorb impurity from contact with the unclean but transmits cleanness to them. This is the Christological fulfillment of the entire Levitical purity system — not its abolition but its completion in the one who is perfectly holy and whose holiness is inexhaustible.

verses.kjv_text: Every bed whereon she lieth all the days of her issue shall be unto her as the bed of her separation: and whatsoever she sitteth upon shall be unclean, as the uncleanness of her separation.
verses.original_text: כָּל הַמִּשְׁכָּב אֲשֶׁר תִּשְׁכַּב עָלָיו כָּל יְמֵי זוֹבָהּ כְּמִשְׁכַּב נִדָּתָהּ יִהְיֶה לָּהּ וְכָל הַכְּלִי אֲשֶׁר תֵּשֵׁב עָלָיו טָמֵא יִהְיֶה כְּטֻמְאַת נִדָּתָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Kol hammishkav asher tishkav alav kol yemei zovah kemishkav niddatah yihyeh lah vekhol hakeli asher teshev alav tame yihyeh ketum'at niddatah.
verses.study_note: The contamination rules for the female zov exactly mirror those for the niddah (vv.20-23): her bed and her sitting-objects are impure throughout all the days of her discharge. The phrase "as the bed of her separation" (kemishkav niddatah) and "as the uncleanness of her separation" (ketum'at niddatah) use the niddah as the standard of comparison — the female zov is essentially an extended, chronic niddah. For the woman of Mark 5 with twelve years of zov, this meant twelve years in which every bed she slept in and every chair she sat on was a source of secondary impurity to anyone who contacted it. The comprehensive social isolation this entailed — while not a legal exclusion from all community life — made her existence a sustained experience of marginalisation within the covenant community.

verses.kjv_text: And whosoever toucheth those things shall be unclean, and shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even.
verses.original_text: וְכָל הַנֹּגֵעַ בָּם יִטְמָא וְכִבֶּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם וְטָמֵא עַד הָעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol hannogea bam yitamma vekhibbes begadav verachatz bammayyim vetame ad ha'arev.
verses.study_note: Whoever touches the contaminated objects of the female zov must launder clothes and bathe, remaining impure until evening. This remedy mirrors exactly the secondary contamination requirements for touching the male zav's contaminated objects (vv.5-7). The complete symmetry between male and female cases is here at its most compact: a single verse covers the female zov's secondary contamination with the identical formula used for the male zav. The Levitical legislation achieves its systematic completeness through this careful parallel construction, ensuring that the reader understands male and female purity law as a unified, coherent, symmetrically structured system.

verses.kjv_text: But if she be cleansed of her issue, then she shall number to herself seven days, and after that she shall be clean.
verses.original_text: וְאִם טָהֲרָה מִזּוֹבָהּ וְסָפְרָה לָּהּ שִׁבְעַת יָמִים וְאַחַר תִּטְהָר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im taharah mizzovah vesafrah lah shivat yamim ve'achar tithar.
verses.study_note: When the discharge ceases, the female zov counts seven days for herself — exactly paralleling the male zav's seven-day count (v.13). The phrase "she shall number to herself" (vesafrah lah) — the reflexive lah (to herself, for herself) emphasizes the personal, inward character of the counting. This is not a passive external process supervised by a priest but a personal, self-directed spiritual discipline: the woman counts her own days, attending to her own bodily state before God, taking personal responsibility for her own ritual restoration. The "and after that she shall be clean" (ve'achar tithar) is the clean simplicity of the resolution — after the seven-day watch period, before any sacrifice, she is already on the path to taharah. The counting itself is an act of faith and attentiveness.

verses.kjv_text: And on the eighth day she shall take unto her two turtles, or two young pigeons, and bring them unto the priest, to the door of the tabernacle of the congregation.
verses.original_text: וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי תִּקַּח לָהּ שְׁתֵּי תֹרִים אוֹ שְׁנֵי בְּנֵי יוֹנָה וְהֵבִיאָה אוֹתָם אֶל הַכֹּהֵן אֶל פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Uvayom hashemini tikkach lah shetei torim o shenei benei yonah vehevi'ah otam el-hakkohen el petach ohel mo'ed.
verses.study_note: On the eighth day the female zov brings the same two birds as the male zav (v.14) — the complete structural parallel is maintained to the final detail. She takes the birds for herself (tikkach lah — she takes to herself, the reflexive lah again), comes to the priest at the tabernacle door. The same eighth-day new-beginning theology applies: she crosses the threshold of full restoration on the day that symbolizes new creation. The woman with the twelve-year issue of blood who was healed by touching Jesus's garment (Mark 5:25-34) experienced her eighth-day in that moment of healing — instant, complete restoration of the access to God's presence that twelve years of continuous zov had denied her.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall offer the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering; and the priest shall make an atonement for her before the LORD for the issue of her uncleanness.
verses.original_text: וְעָשָׂה הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הָאֶחָד חַטָּאת וְאֶת הָאֶחָד עֹלָה וְכִפֶּר עָלֶיהָ הַכֹּהֵן לִפְנֵי יְהוָה מִזּוֹב טֻמְאָתָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asah hakkohen et-ha'echad chatta't ve'et-ha'echad olah vekhipper aleha hakkohen lifnei YHWH mizzov tum'atah.
verses.study_note: The priest offers the sin offering and burnt offering for the female zov — the exact parallel of v.15 for the male zav. The atonement (kipper) is made "before the LORD for the issue of her uncleanness" (lifnei YHWH mizzov tum'atah) — the specific nature of the atonement is defined by the specific nature of the condition. The symmetry is now complete: four categories (male zov / male emission / female niddah / female zov) have been addressed with perfect structural balance. The four categories represent the full range of reproductive and bodily discharge conditions across both sexes — the complete human biological range brought under covenant holiness management. The priest's atonement closes each section with the same movement: human impurity addressed by priestly mediation before God's presence.

verses.kjv_text: Thus shall ye separate the children of Israel from their uncleanness; that they die not in their uncleanness, when they defile my tabernacle that is among them.
verses.original_text: וְהִזַּרְתֶּם אֶת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מִטֻּמְאָתָם וְלֹא יָמֻתוּ בְּטֻמְאָתָם בְּטַמְּאָם אֶת מִשְׁכָּנִי אֲשֶׁר בְּתוֹכָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehizzartem et-benei Yisra'el mittum'atam velo yamutu vetum'atam betamme'am et-mishkani asher betokham.
verses.study_note: The theological purpose of the entire chapter is stated in this summary verse: "separate the children of Israel from their uncleanness that they die not." The verb hizzartem (from nazar, to separate, set apart, dedicate — the root of nazir, Nazirite) is commanding: cause Israel to be separated from their impurity. The rationale is starkly stated: unmanaged tum'ah in the midst of the camp where God's tabernacle (mishkani — My dwelling, from shakan, to dwell — the root of Shekinah) dwells is potentially lethal. The holiness of God's dwelling presence in the midst of the camp means that uncleanness brought into that presence courts death (cf. Lev 10:1-2; Num 4:15,20). The management of ritual impurity through the processes of Leviticus 15 was not mere hygiene but covenant life-protection: maintaining the conditions under which a holy God could dwell among a people whose bodily processes generated ongoing impurity.
verses.doctrinal_note: Verse 31 identifies the ultimate purpose of all the purity legislation: separation from tum'ah in order to live in the presence of God's dwelling (mishkan). This is the central tension of the entire Old Testament: a holy God has chosen to dwell "in the midst" of a fallen people whose bodies and lives generate ongoing impurity. The elaborate purification system is the divinely ordained mechanism for managing this tension. In Christ, this tension is resolved permanently. Hebrews 10:19-22 declares that "we have boldness to enter into the holiest by the blood of Jesus... having our hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience and our bodies washed with pure water." The Levitical system's entire purpose — separation from tum'ah for access to God's presence — is achieved once and for all in the blood of Christ. Revelation 21:3 is the final fulfillment: "Behold, the tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell with them" — the mishkan theology of Leviticus 15:31 reaches its consummate expression in the new creation.

verses.kjv_text: This is the law of him that hath an issue, and of him whose seed goeth from him, and is defiled therewith;
verses.original_text: זֹאת תּוֹרַת הַזָּב וַאֲשֶׁר תֵּצֵא מִמֶּנּוּ שִׁכְבַת זָרַע לְטָמְאָה בָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Zo't torat hazzav va'asher tetze mimmennu shikhvat zara letam'ah vah.
verses.study_note: The summary formula "this is the law of" (zo't torat) closes the chapter's content with a formal legal recapitulation. The chapter covers two male categories: the zav (abnormal discharge, vv.2-15) and the one from whom seminal emission comes (shikhvat zera, vv.16-18). The summary is structured in exactly the order the chapter presented its content: first the zav (abnormal), then the emission (normal). This recapitulation formula, which appears throughout Leviticus at the close of major pericopes, gives the legal material its character as a concluded, sealed body of divine instruction.

verses.kjv_text: And of her that is sick of her flowers, and of him that hath an issue, of the man, and of the woman, and of him that lieth with her that is unclean.
verses.original_text: וְהַדָּוָה בְּנִדָּתָהּ וְהַזָּב אֶת זוֹבוֹ לַזָּכָר וְלַנְּקֵבָה וּלְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁכַּב עִם טְמֵאָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehaddavah beniddatah vehazzav et-zovo lazzakhar velannekevah ule'ish asher yishkav im teme'ah.
verses.study_note: The closing verse of the summary covers the two female categories: the davah (the menstruant in her niddah — the word davah from davah, to be faint/weak, carries the sense of the physical discomfort of menstruation) and the female zov (vv.25-30). The summary then generalizes: "the male and the female" — the entire range of both sexes is covered. The final clause — "him that lieth with her that is unclean" (v.24) — completes the summary by including the case of secondary full-impurity transmission through intercourse with a niddah. The chapter's summary thus covers all four primary categories and their key secondary cases in a single sweeping final verse, sealing Leviticus 15 as a coherent and comprehensive body of law governing bodily discharge impurity for the entire covenant community.
verses.doctrinal_note: The closing summary of Leviticus 15 encompasses the entire covenant community — male and female, normal and abnormal, primary and secondary impurity — within a single body of law. The theological achievement of the chapter is the demonstration that every human being, in the normal course of embodied existence, regularly moves between states of ritual impurity and ritual cleanness. No Israelite was permanently exempt from tum'ah; every Israelite regularly required the processes of washing, waiting, and at times sacrifice, to maintain their standing within the covenant community. This was by design: the Levitical purity system embedded a perpetual consciousness of human creaturely impurity and divine holiness into the daily rhythms of life. The New Testament fulfillment is not the abolition of this consciousness but its transformation: in Christ, the cleansing is permanent (Heb 10:14), the access unrestricted (Heb 4:16), and the indwelling presence of the Holy Spirit makes the believer's body itself the temple of God (1 Cor 6:19-20) — the very mishkan that Leviticus 15:31 sought to protect from defilement.

meta.overview: Leviticus 15 addresses one of the most intimate dimensions of the Levitical holiness system: ritual impurity arising from bodily discharges. The chapter covers four categories in a deliberate symmetrical structure — two male conditions (abnormal discharge, vv.1-15; normal emission, vv.16-17) and two female conditions (normal menstruation, vv.19-24; abnormal discharge, vv.25-30) — framed by an introduction (vv.1-2) and a theological summary (vv.31-33). The chapter's subject matter is profoundly physical: seminal emissions, menstrual blood, and pathological genital discharges. Yet it is framed throughout with the language of divine address to Moses and Aaron, holiness, and sanctuary access. This is not ancient hygiene law or cultural taboo but a theological system that treats the human body — with all its biological processes — as existing within the sphere of God's covenant holiness. The central principle is that bodily fluids associated with the transmission and boundaries of life (blood, semen) make a person ritually impure, requiring a process of washing, waiting, and in the case of abnormal discharges, sacrifice, before full reintegration into covenant community and sanctuary access. The theology of tum'ah (ritual impurity) in this chapter is not a judgment on sexuality or biological function — both marriage and procreation are divinely ordained (Gen 1:28; 2:24) — but a recognition that the bodily processes most intimately connected with life, death, and generativity belong within the orbit of God's holiness and require the consecrating structures of covenant to navigate rightly. Leviticus 15 finds its most dramatic New Testament fulfillment in Mark 5:25-34: the woman with the issue of blood (a chronic zov discharge of the type described in vv.25-30) who had been ritually impure for twelve years touched the garment of Jesus. In the Levitical system, such contact would have made Jesus ritually impure. Instead, power went out from Him and she was healed — the direction of holiness was reversed. Christ does not become unclean by contact with the unclean; He makes the unclean clean. This is the definitive Christological statement on Leviticus 15 and the entire purity system: Jesus is not merely a participant in the system but its fulfillment and transcendence.
meta.theme: The Holiness of the Body — Discharge, Impurity, and the Path of Cleansing
meta.keyWordMeaning: Discharge/flow/issue — the central term of the chapter, from zuv (H2100), to flow, gush, run. Used for both the abnormal male discharge (vv.2-15) and the abnormal female discharge (vv.25-30). The same root describes the Promised Land flowing (zavat) with milk and honey (Exod 3:8), suggesting that zuv/flow is a morally neutral term describing movement and abundance — it is the context (holy/unholy, normal/abnormal, controlled/uncontrolled) that determines ritual significance. In Levitical theology, uncontrolled bodily flow requires the ordering structures of covenant purification.
outline: Introduction: God Speaks to Moses and Aaron (vv.1-2a)
The Male Abnormal Discharge — Zov (vv.2b-15): Contamination, Washing, and Sacrifice
The Male Normal Emission (vv.16-18): Uncleanness Until Evening
The Female Normal Discharge — Niddah (vv.19-24): Menstrual Impurity and Contact
The Female Abnormal Discharge — Zov (vv.25-30): Extended Impurity and Sacrifice
Theological Summary: Protecting the Sanctuary and the Camp (vv.31-33)

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses after the death of the two sons of Aaron, when they offered before the LORD, and died;
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה אַחֲרֵי מוֹת שְׁנֵי בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן בְּקָרְבָתָם לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וַיָּמֻתוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh acharei mot shenei venei Aharon beqorvatam lifnei YHWH vayyamutu.
verses.study_note: The opening temporal marker 'after the death of the two sons of Aaron' anchors the Day of Atonement legislation in a specific, devastating historical event: the deaths of Nadab and Abihu in Leviticus 10:1-2, who offered 'strange fire' before the LORD and were consumed. The phrase beqorvatam lifnei YHWH (in their drawing near before the LORD) uses the verb qarav — to draw near, the same root as qorban (offering/sacrifice). Their unauthorized approach cost them their lives and now frames the entire Yom Kippur chapter as a response: access to God is possible, but only on his terms. The chapter's severe restrictions on priestly entry are mercy, not cruelty — they protect the priest from the same fate.
verses.doctrinal_note: The death of Nadab and Abihu as the backdrop for Yom Kippur establishes the foundational principle that a holy God cannot be approached casually or presumptuously. The chapter answers the question their deaths raised: how, then, can anyone draw near to God at all? The answer is the Day of Atonement — access is possible, but only through prescribed substitution, blood, and the covering of sin. This principle finds its ultimate resolution in Hebrews 10:19-22: believers may now enter the Most Holy Place with boldness (parresia — confidence, free speech) through the blood of Jesus, by the new and living way he has consecrated through the veil, that is, his flesh.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Speak unto Aaron thy brother, that he come not at all times into the holy place within the vail before the mercy seat, which is upon the ark; that he die not: for I will appear in the cloud upon the mercy seat.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה דַּבֵּר אֶל־אַהֲרֹן אָחִיךָ וְאַל יָבֹא בְכָל עֵת אֶל־הַקֹּדֶשׁ מִבֵּית לַפָּרֹכֶת אֶל פְּנֵי הַכַּפֹּרֶת אֲשֶׁר עַל הָאָרֹן וְלֹא יָמוּת כִּי בֶּעָנָן אֵרָאֶה עַל־הַכַּפֹּרֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh dabber el-Aharon achikha ve'al yavo vekhol et el-haqqodesh mibbeit lapparokhet el penei hakkapporet asher al ha'aron velo yamut ki be'anan ere'eh al-hakkapporet.
verses.study_note: The divine restriction is absolute: Aaron must not come 'at all times' (bekhol et — at any time he wishes) into the holy place behind the veil. The phrase 'the holy place within the vail' refers to the Qodesh HaQodashim — the Most Holy Place, the inner sanctum containing the ark. The kapporet (mercy seat) is explicitly named — the golden lid of the ark where the cherubim face each other and where God promises to meet Israel. The reason given is extraordinary: 'for I will appear in the cloud upon the mercy seat' — God's glory-presence (shekinah) dwells there, and unauthorized intrusion into that divine space means death. The restriction is protection, not exclusion.
verses.doctrinal_note: The kapporet (mercy seat, H3727) is one of the most theologically loaded objects in all of Scripture. Its name derives from kipper (to atone/cover): it is literally the 'atonement cover' or 'place of propitiation.' The LXX translates it as hilasterion, the exact word Paul uses in Romans 3:25 for Christ himself: 'whom God hath set forth to be a propitiation [hilasterion] through faith in his blood.' Christ is both the High Priest who enters the Most Holy Place and the kapporet upon which the blood is applied — he is simultaneously the officiant and the place of atonement. The cloud (anan) in which God appears at the mercy seat (v.2) is the same divine glory-cloud (shekinah) that filled the tabernacle in Exodus 40:34-35, connecting the Yom Kippur ritual to the entire theology of the divine presence.

verses.kjv_text: Thus shall Aaron come into the holy place: with a young bullock for a sin offering, and a ram for a burnt offering.
verses.original_text: בְּזֹאת יָבֹא אַהֲרֹן אֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ בְּפַר בֶּן בָּקָר לְחַטָּאת וְאַיִל לְעֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Bezot yavo Aharon el haqqodesh bepar ben baqar lechattot ve'ayil le'olah.
verses.study_note: The Hebrew bezot ('with this / in this way') is emphatic — entry is only possible in this prescribed manner, no other. Two animals are required for Aaron's personal preparation: a young bull (par ben baqar) as a sin offering (chattat) for himself and his household, and a ram for a burnt offering (olah). The bull is specifically for the priest's own sin — Aaron cannot minister atonement for others without first dealing with his own impurity. This self-atonement requirement is the foundational contrast with Christ, who 'needed not daily... to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins' (Hebrews 7:27), because he was 'holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners.'
verses.doctrinal_note: The requirement that Aaron first offer a sin offering for himself before atoning for Israel establishes the inherent limitation of the Levitical priesthood: a sinful priest offering atonement for a sinful people is an imperfect solution requiring annual repetition. Hebrews 7:26-28 makes this contrast explicit: the law appoints as high priests men who have weakness, 'but the word of the oath, which was since the law, maketh the Son, who is consecrated for evermore.' Christ's once-for-all self-offering (Hebrews 9:14) accomplishes what Aaron's annual bull could not — a permanent, complete, sufficient atonement that never needs repeating.

verses.kjv_text: He shall put on the holy linen coat, and he shall have the linen breeches upon his flesh, and shall be girded with a linen girdle, and with the linen mitre shall he be attired: these are holy garments; therefore shall he wash his flesh in water, and so put them on.
verses.original_text: כְּתֹנֶת בַּד קֹדֶשׁ יִלְבָּשׁ וּמִכְנְסֵי בַד יִהְיוּ עַל בְּשָׂרוֹ וּבְאַבְנֵט בַּד יַחְגֹּר וּבְמִצְנֶפֶת בַּד יִצְנֹף בִּגְדֵי קֹדֶשׁ הֵם וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם אֶת בְּשָׂרוֹ וּלְבֵשָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Ketonet bad qodesh yilbash umikhnsei bad yihyu al besaro uve'avnet bad yachgor uvemitznefet bad yitznof bigdei qodesh hem verachatz bamayim et besaro ulevesham.
verses.study_note: On Yom Kippur, Aaron does not wear his magnificent golden high priestly vestments (described in Exodus 28) but instead dresses in four plain white linen garments: a linen tunic (ketonet bad), linen undergarments (mikhnsei bad), a linen sash (avnet bad), and a linen turban (mitznefet bad). The word bad (linen) appears four times, underscoring the simplicity and purity of the Yom Kippur attire. The contrast with the gold, blue, purple, and scarlet of the normal vestments is intentional — this is a day of humility and atonement, not priestly splendor. Bathing before donning these garments emphasizes the bodily purity required for the holiest day.
verses.doctrinal_note: The white linen garments of Yom Kippur carry rich typological significance. Linen (bad/shesh) in Leviticus is consistently associated with purity and priestly service. Revelation 19:8 identifies fine linen as the 'righteousness of saints' and the garment of the bride of Christ. The heavenly armies ride in 'fine linen, white and clean' (Rev 19:14). Christ's burial shroud was fine linen (John 19:40), and the resurrection angels appear in white. Aaron's white linen on Yom Kippur anticipates the righteousness-clothing that God provides for those he forgives — the exchange of filthy garments for clean ones, as dramatically enacted in Zechariah 3:3-5.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall take of the congregation of the children of Israel two kids of the goats for a sin offering, and one ram for a burnt offering.
verses.original_text: וּמֵאֵת עֲדַת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל יִקַּח שְׁנֵי שְׂעִירֵי עִזִּים לְחַטָּאת וְאַיִל אֶחָד לְעֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ume'et adat benei Yisra'el yiqqach shenei se'irei izzim lechattot ve'ayil echad le'olah.
verses.study_note: The two goats (shenei se'irei izzim) taken from the congregation are the centerpiece of the Yom Kippur ritual. They are taken 'from the congregation of the children of Israel' — this is a communal, national act, not merely a priestly one. The entire covenant people provides the means of their own atonement. The two goats function as a single sin offering (note the singular 'chattat' covering both) — they represent two aspects of one atonement transaction. Together they form a complete picture of what it means to deal with sin: propitiation (blood) and expiation (removal). A single animal cannot adequately represent both dimensions.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron shall offer his bullock of the sin offering, which is for himself, and make an atonement for himself, and for his house.
verses.original_text: וְהִקְרִיב אַהֲרֹן אֶת פַּר הַחַטָּאת אֲשֶׁר לוֹ וְכִפֶּר בַּעֲדוֹ וּבְעַד בֵּיתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiqriv Aharon et par hachattat asher lo vekipper ba'ado uvead beito.
verses.study_note: The key verb appears for the first time in the chapter: vekipper — 'and he shall make atonement.' This is the verb kippur, the root of Yom Kippur. The Piel stem (intensive active) indicates a strong, deliberate act of atonement. Aaron presents his own bull and makes atonement first for himself (ba'ado — for him) and his household (beito). The sequence is deliberate: the priest cannot stand as mediator between Israel and God without himself being atoned for. This is the Levitical order — personal cleansing precedes representative ministry. The phrase 'his house' likely refers to Aaron's priestly household — the entire Levitical priesthood, not merely his biological family.
verses.doctrinal_note: The verb kipper (H3722) is the theological nucleus of Leviticus 16 and of the entire Levitical sacrificial system. It occurs fourteen times in this chapter alone. Its basic meaning involves covering, wiping, or propitiating — removing the barrier of sin between the offerer and God. When kipper is applied to the mercy seat (kapporet), the picture is complete: the blood covers the ark's lid, symbolically covering the law within it, so that instead of the law condemning Israel, the mercy seat speaks propitiation. Paul's declaration that 'God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself, not imputing their trespasses unto them' (2 Corinthians 5:19) is the New Testament proclamation of what kipper accomplished in shadow at Yom Kippur.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall take the two goats, and present them before the LORD at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation.
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח אֶת שְׁנֵי הַשְּׂעִירִם וְהֶעֱמִיד אֹתָם לִפְנֵי יְהוָה פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Velaqach et shenei hasse'irim vehe'emid otam lifnei YHWH petach ohel mo'ed.
verses.study_note: Both goats are brought and made to stand (he'emid — caused to stand upright, presented) before the LORD at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting. They stand together before God before their distinct fates are determined by lot. This joint presentation before the LORD is significant: both goats belong to God before either is assigned its role. The lot that follows (v.8) is not a human choice but a divine determination — God decides which goat dies and which bears the sins away. The location 'at the door of the tabernacle' is the public threshold between the ordinary and the sacred, where all major sacrificial transactions begin.
verses.doctrinal_note: The two goats standing together before the LORD at the tabernacle entrance form one of the most profound typological images in the Old Testament. Early church fathers (Barnabas, Justin Martyr, Tertullian) and later interpreters consistently saw in the two goats a dual picture of Christ's atoning work: the slain goat as propitiation (Christ crucified, satisfying divine justice with blood) and the scapegoat as expiation (Christ bearing our sins away, so that they are remembered no more — Hebrews 10:17). Together they express the two inseparable dimensions of atonement: wrath satisfied and guilt removed. No single image or act could fully capture what the cross accomplished.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron shall cast lots upon the two goats; one lot for the LORD, and the other lot for the scapegoat.
verses.original_text: וְנָתַן אַהֲרֹן עַל שְׁנֵי הַשְּׂעִירִם גּוֹרָלוֹת גּוֹרָל אֶחָד לַיהוָה וְגוֹרָל אֶחָד לַעֲזָאזֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Venatan Aharon al shenei hasse'irim goralot goral echad laYHWH vegoral echad la'Aza'zel.
verses.study_note: The lot-casting (goralot) is the divinely ordained mechanism by which the two goats receive their assignments. The goral (lot) in Israel was a sacred device for discerning God's will — used at the division of the land (Numbers 26:55), the choice of Saul as king (1 Samuel 10:20-21), and the assignment of priestly courses (1 Chronicles 24:5). The first lot is designated 'for the LORD' (laYHWH) — this goat will be slaughtered as a blood sacrifice. The second is 'for Azazel' (la'Aza'zel). The identity of Azazel is debated: it may mean 'the goat that departs' (ez-azel), 'complete removal,' or a reference to a wilderness demon/desolation. Most importantly, the Azazel goat is sent away from the covenant community.
verses.doctrinal_note: The word Azazel (H5799) appears only four times in the Hebrew Bible, all in Leviticus 16. Three major interpretations exist: (1) a geographical designation — a remote, desolate place; (2) a descriptive term for the goat itself — 'complete removal' or 'the goat that departs'; (3) a proper name for a wilderness demon or fallen angel (as in 1 Enoch 8:1; 10:4-8). The Mishnah (Yoma 6) describes the goat being pushed off a rocky cliff in the wilderness. What is certain theologically is the contrast: one goat goes to YHWH (the holy sanctuary), one goes to Azazel (the desolate wilderness). The covenant God and the chaos of the wilderness are placed in symbolic opposition, and Israel's sins are sent to the latter, away from the former.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron shall bring the goat upon which the LORD’s lot fell, and offer him for a sin offering.
verses.original_text: וְהִקְרִיב אַהֲרֹן אֶת הַשָּׂעִיר אֲשֶׁר עָלָה עָלָיו הַגּוֹרָל לַיהוָה וְעָשָׂהוּ חַטָּאת׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiqriv Aharon et hassa'ir asher alah alav haggoral laYHWH ve'asahu chattat.
verses.study_note: The first goat — the one upon which the LORD's lot fell — is brought as a sin offering (chattat). The phrase 'upon which the lot came up' (asher alah alav haggoral) uses alah (to ascend/come up), perhaps echoing the ascent of the smoke of the olah to God. This goat dies. Its blood will be the instrument of atonement in the Most Holy Place. The word chattat (sin offering) here covers the sins of the entire nation — a weight of communal guilt that no single family's animal could bear. This goat represents the propitiation dimension of Yom Kippur atonement.

verses.kjv_text: But the goat, on which the lot fell to be the scapegoat, shall be presented alive before the LORD, to make an atonement with him, and to let him go for a scapegoat into the wilderness.
verses.original_text: וְהַשָּׂעִיר אֲשֶׁר עָלָה עָלָיו הַגּוֹרָל לַעֲזָאזֵל יָעֳמַד חַי לִפְנֵי יְהוָה לְכַפֵּר עָלָיו לְשַׁלַּח אֹתוֹ לַעֲזָאזֵל הַמִּדְבָּרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehassa'ir asher alah alav haggoral la'Aza'zel yo'amad chai lifnei YHWH lekhapper alav leshallach oto la'Aza'zel hammidbbarah.
verses.study_note: The Azazel goat is presented alive (chai) before the LORD — it does not immediately die but stands in the divine presence carrying the sins that will be confessed over it. The phrase lekhapper alav (to make atonement upon/over it) is remarkable: atonement is made by means of the live goat, not by killing it. This goat's atoning function is different from the slaughtered goat — it makes atonement through its living removal of sin rather than through its death. The destination is hammidbbarah — into the wilderness (the definite article + directional he: to the wilderness place). The wilderness is the anti-sanctuary, the opposite of covenant life.
verses.doctrinal_note: The living Azazel goat that carries Israel's sins into the wilderness (hammidbarah) enacts the theological dimension of expiation — the removal and elimination of sin rather than its propitiation. Psalm 103:12 expresses this same truth: 'As far as the east is from the west, so far hath he removed our transgressions from us.' Micah 7:19 similarly: 'thou wilt cast all their sins into the depths of the sea.' The NT fulfillment is declared in Hebrews 10:17: 'their sins and iniquities will I remember no more.' Christ fulfills both goats: he is the slain sacrifice whose blood propitiates (the LORD's goat) and simultaneously the one who 'bore our sins in his own body' and removed them utterly (the Azazel goat — 1 Peter 2:24).

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron shall bring the bullock of the sin offering, which is for himself, and shall make an atonement for himself, and for his house, and shall kill the bullock of the sin offering which is for himself:
verses.original_text: וְהִקְרִיב אַהֲרֹן אֶת פַּר הַחַטָּאת אֲשֶׁר לוֹ וְכִפֶּר בַּעֲדוֹ וּבְעַד בֵּיתוֹ וְשָׁחַט אֶת פַּר הַחַטָּאת אֲשֶׁר לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiqriv Aharon et par hachattat asher lo vekipper ba'ado uvead beito veshachat et par hachattat asher lo.
verses.study_note: The procedure now begins in earnest: Aaron brings his bull (described in v.3) and performs its slaughter (shachat — the act of ritual slaughter). The repetition from verse 6 is deliberate — the text underscores that Aaron must atone for himself before ministering for others. The verb shachat (to slaughter) describes the killing of the animal at the altar, the moment when the substitute dies in the offerer's place. The bull's blood will be used in the next steps for the priestly atonement ceremony. No one enters the Most Holy Place without blood.
