verses.kjv_text: And he shall take a censer full of burning coals of fire from off the altar before the LORD, and his hands full of sweet incense beaten small, and bring it within the vail:
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח מְלֹא הַמַּחְתָּה גַּחֲלֵי אֵשׁ מֵעַל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה וּמְלֹא חָפְנָיו קְטֹרֶת סַמִּים דַּקָּה וְהֵבִיא מִבֵּית לַפָּרֹכֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Velaqach melo hamachtah gachalei esh me'al hammizbeach millifnei YHWH umelo chafanav qetoret sammim daqqah vehevi mibbeit lapparokhet.
verses.study_note: Before the blood enters the Most Holy Place, Aaron takes a firepan (machtah) full of burning coals from the altar and both his hands full (melo chafanav — a double-handful) of finely ground incense (qetoret sammim daqqah). He brings these within the veil (mibbeit lapparokhet) — into the Most Holy Place itself. The incense cloud that he makes (v.13) will cover the mercy seat so that he does not see the full glory of God and die. The incense precedes the blood in the ritual sequence: it is a mediating layer between human fragility and divine holiness. This is one of the most dramatic moments in all of Israel's worship — a single man entering the presence of the infinite God.
verses.doctrinal_note: The incense cloud (qetoret) that Aaron creates in the Most Holy Place to cover the mercy seat connects to a rich biblical theology of intercession. Incense is consistently the symbol of prayer and intercession in Scripture: Psalm 141:2, 'Let my prayer be set forth before thee as incense'; Revelation 5:8 and 8:3-4 describe golden bowls of incense as the prayers of the saints. The high priest entering the Most Holy Place behind a cloud of intercessory incense anticipates Christ's priestly intercession described in Hebrews 7:25: 'he ever liveth to make intercession for them.' The incense also connects to the golden altar of incense in the tabernacle (Exod 30:1-10), which in Revelation 8:3 is directly before the throne of God.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall put the incense upon the fire before the LORD, that the cloud of the incense may cover the mercy seat that is upon the testimony, that he die not:
verses.original_text: וְנָתַן אֶת הַקְּטֹרֶת עַל הָאֵשׁ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וְכִסָּה עֲנַן הַקְּטֹרֶת אֶת הַכַּפֹּרֶת אֲשֶׁר עַל הָעֵדֻת וְלֹא יָמוּת׃
verses.transliteration: Venatan et haqqetoret al ha'esh lifnei YHWH vekhissah anan haqqetoret et hakkapporet asher al ha'edut velo yamut.
verses.study_note: The incense is placed on the fire inside the veil, creating a cloud (anan) that covers (khissah — to cover, conceal) the mercy seat. This cloud-cover is the precondition for the priest's survival — 'that he die not.' The same verb khissah (to cover) is used for the protective function of the incense cloud as for the covering of sin in the atonement itself. The mercy seat rests 'upon the testimony' (al ha'edut) — upon the ark which contains the two stone tablets of the law (the 'testimony,' edut). The incense cloud between Aaron and the kapporet reflects the profound truth that access to God, even for the High Priest, requires a mediating covering.
verses.doctrinal_note: The incense cloud covering the mercy seat on Yom Kippur reflects the principle that no human being can survive direct exposure to the full divine glory. Even the greatest High Priest needs a mediating layer between himself and God. This stands in stark contrast to the Christian's standing in Christ: Hebrews 4:16 declares that believers may now 'come boldly unto the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy.' The incense-cloud mediation of Leviticus 16 has given way to the direct access purchased by Christ's blood. The cloud of incense was temporary mercy; Christ's intercession is permanent and complete.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall take of the blood of the bullock, and sprinkle it with his finger upon the mercy seat eastward; and before the mercy seat shall he sprinkle of the blood with his finger seven times.
verses.original_text: וְלָקַח מִדַּם הַפָּר וְהִזָּה בְאֶצְבָּעוֹ עַל פְּנֵי הַכַּפֹּרֶת קֵדְמָה וְלִפְנֵי הַכַּפֹּרֶת יַזֶּה שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים מִן הַדָּם בְּאֶצְבָּעוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Velaqach middam happar vehizzah be'etzba'o al penei hakkapporet qedmah velifnei hakkapporet yazzeh sheva pe'amim min haddam be'etzba'o.
verses.study_note: The climactic blood ritual: Aaron takes the bull's blood and sprinkles it with his finger (be'etzba'o — the instrument of precise, deliberate application) on the face of the mercy seat, eastward (the direction toward the entrance, toward the people) and then before the mercy seat seven times. The number seven (sheva pe'amim) is the number of completeness and divine perfection — the blood application is total and complete. The blood applied to the kapporet directly transforms the lid of the ark from a throne of judgment into a seat of mercy — the law below can no longer condemn because blood above has satisfied its demands.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sprinkling of blood on the mercy seat seven times is the central act of the entire Yom Kippur ritual and the most direct typological pointer to Christ's atonement in all of Scripture. Hebrews 9:12 declares that Christ entered 'the greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands... neither by the blood of goats and calves, but by his own blood... having obtained eternal redemption.' The blood that Aaron annually sprinkled on the kapporet has been replaced by the once-for-all presentation of Christ's blood before the Father. The sevenfold sprinkling speaks completeness: Christ's atonement is exhaustive, lacking nothing, covering every sin of every believer for all time.

verses.kjv_text: Then shall he kill the goat of the sin offering, that is for the people, and bring his blood within the vail, and do with that blood as he did with the blood of the bullock, and sprinkle it upon the mercy seat, and before the mercy seat:
verses.original_text: וְשָׁחַט אֶת שְׂעִיר הַחַטָּאת אֲשֶׁר לָעָם וְהֵבִיא אֶת דָּמוֹ אֶל מִבֵּית לַפָּרֹכֶת וְעָשָׂה אֶת דָּמוֹ כַּאֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה לְדַם הַפָּר וְהִזָּה אֹתוֹ עַל הַכַּפֹּרֶת וְלִפְנֵי הַכַּפֹּרֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Veshachat et se'ir hachattat asher la'am vehevi et damo el mibbeit lapparokhet ve'asah et damo ka'asher asah ledam happar vehizzah oto al hakkapporet velifnei hakkapporet.
verses.study_note: Having atoned for himself and his priestly house, Aaron now slaughters the goat that is 'for the people' (la'am) and brings its blood through the veil into the Most Holy Place for the second blood application. The procedure mirrors the bull's blood application precisely — on and before the mercy seat. This second entry into the Most Holy Place is for the whole covenant community. The parallel structure (bull for the priest, goat for the people) declares that the Yom Kippur atonement is comprehensive: it covers every stratum of Israel's sinfulness, from priest to layperson.
verses.doctrinal_note: The blood of the people's goat applied in the Most Holy Place is the moment of communal atonement — Israel's corporate guilt addressed before God. This communal atonement anticipates what theologians call the 'definite atonement' of Christ: the blood applied for a specific people (2 Corinthians 5:21: 'he hath made him to be sin for us'). The phrase 'as he did with the blood of the bull' signals that the same divine standard of atonement applies to both priest and people — there is one way of atonement for all. Christ's atonement similarly makes 'no difference between Jew and Greek' (Romans 10:12): one sacrifice, one blood, one atonement for all who are in covenant with God through faith.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall make an atonement for the holy place, because of the uncleanness of the children of Israel, and because of their transgressions in all their sins: and so shall he do for the tabernacle of the congregation, that remaineth among them in the midst of their uncleanness.
verses.original_text: וְכִפֶּר עַל הַקֹּדֶשׁ מִטֻּמְאֹת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וּמִפִּשְׁעֵיהֶם לְכָל חַטֹּאתָם וְכֵן יַעֲשֶׂה לְאֹהֶל מוֹעֵד הַשֹׁכֵן אִתָּם בְּתוֹךְ טֻמְאֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vekipper al haqqodesh mitumot benei Yisra'el umippishe'ihem lekhol chattotam vekhen ya'aseh le'ohel mo'ed hashokhen ittam betokh tuma'otam.
verses.study_note: This verse reveals the remarkable theological logic of Yom Kippur: the sanctuary itself needs atonement. The sins of Israel, even unintentional and unknown ones, accumulate as a kind of ritual contamination on the sanctuary where God dwells. Three sin categories are named: tumot (uncleannesses, ritual impurities), pisheihem (transgressions — deliberate rebellions), and chattotam (sins — all their sins comprehensively). The holy place is defiled because it dwells 'in the midst' of an impure people. God's holy dwelling among a sinful nation creates a continual pollution problem that Yom Kippur annually addresses.
verses.doctrinal_note: The concept that Israel's sins defile the sanctuary where God dwells carries profound theological weight. God's willingness to dwell among sinful people (hashokhen ittam — the one dwelling with them) is extraordinary grace — but it creates the problem that his holiness is perpetually threatened by their impurity. This is the tension that all of Leviticus manages: how can a holy God dwell among an unholy people? The final answer to this question comes in Revelation 21:3-4: God's tabernacle will be with humanity in the new creation, where 'there shall be no more curse' and the barrier of sin has been permanently removed through Christ's atonement.

verses.kjv_text: And there shall be no man in the tabernacle of the congregation when he goeth in to make an atonement in the holy place, until he come out, and have made an atonement for himself, and for his household, and for all the congregation of Israel.
verses.original_text: וְכָל אָדָם לֹא יִהְיֶה בְּאֹהֶל מוֹעֵד בְּבֹאוֹ לְכַפֵּר בַּקֹּדֶשׁ עַד צֵאתוֹ וְכִפֶּר בַּעֲדוֹ וּבְעַד בֵּיתוֹ וּבְעַד כָּל קְהַל יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol adam lo yihyeh be'ohel mo'ed bevo'o lekhapper baqqodesh ad tzeto vekipper ba'ado uvead beito uvead kol qehal Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: The absolute solitude requirement: no human being (kol adam — literally 'all of Adam') may be in the tabernacle when Aaron enters the Most Holy Place. The High Priest must enter alone. This unique solitude on Yom Kippur contrasts with all other sanctuary ministry, which involved multiple priests, Levites, and witnesses. The aloneness communicates that the act of atonement is entirely the work of the appointed mediator — no human assistance, no witnesses, no support. The complete comprehensiveness of the atonement then described (for himself, his house, and all the congregation) matches the totality of the solitude: one man does it all.
verses.doctrinal_note: The complete solitude of the High Priest in the Most Holy Place on Yom Kippur is one of the most powerful typological images in the Torah. Christ's atoning work at Calvary was similarly accomplished in ultimate aloneness: 'I have trodden the winepress alone; and of the people there was none with me' (Isaiah 63:3). The cry 'My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me' (Psalm 22:1; Matthew 27:46) represents the ultimate aloneness — the mediator bearing the full weight of the world's sin in complete isolation. As Aaron could receive no help in the Most Holy Place, Christ received none at the cross. The mediator acts alone so that the atonement he obtains can cover all.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall go out unto the altar that is before the LORD, and make an atonement for it; and shall take of the blood of the bullock, and of the blood of the goat, and put it upon the horns of the altar round about.
verses.original_text: וְיָצָא אֶל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ אֲשֶׁר לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו וְלָקַח מִדַּם הַפָּר וּמִדַּם הַשָּׂעִיר וְנָתַן עַל קַרְנוֹת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Veyatza el hammizbeach asher lifnei YHWH vekipper alav velaqach middam happar umiddam hassa'ir venatan al qarnot hammizbeach saviv.
verses.study_note: After completing the inner atonement (Most Holy Place), Aaron moves outward to the altar of burnt offering (the bronze altar in the court). The blood used here is a mixture: both the bull's blood and the goat's blood. This mixture is applied to the four horns (qarnot) of the altar — the protruding corners where sacrificial blood was regularly applied (Lev 4:7,18,25,30). The altar, like the sanctuary, has been defiled by the sins of those who offered upon it, and requires Yom Kippur purification. The atonement moves outward from the Most Holy Place → the sanctuary → the altar, sanctifying every component of Israel's worship infrastructure.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall sprinkle of the blood upon it with his finger seven times, and cleanse it, and hallow it from the uncleanness of the children of Israel.
verses.original_text: וְהִזָּה עָלָיו מִן הַדָּם בְּאֶצְבָּעוֹ שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים וְטִהֲרוֹ וְקִדְּשׁוֹ מִטֻּמְאֹת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vehizzah alav min haddam be'etzba'o sheva pe'amim vetiharo veqiddeshoh mittumo'ot benei Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: The sevenfold blood sprinkling is applied to the outer altar as well — mirroring the sevenfold application at the mercy seat (v.14). Two verbs describe the result: vetiharo (and he shall cleanse it — taher, ritual purification) and veqiddeshoh (and he shall sanctify/hallow it — qadash, to make holy). The double action of purification and sanctification shows that atonement accomplishes two things: it removes impurity (negative) and it confers holiness (positive). The altar is both cleansed of defilement and positively consecrated for continued service. This double transaction mirrors the NT's twin concepts of justification (forgiveness) and sanctification (positive new standing).

verses.kjv_text: And when he hath made an end of reconciling the holy place, and the tabernacle of the congregation, and the altar, he shall bring the live goat:
verses.original_text: וְכִלָּה מִכַּפֵּר אֶת הַקֹּדֶשׁ וְאֶת אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְאֶת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וְהִקְרִיב אֶת הַשָּׂעִיר הֶחָי׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhillah mekhapper et haqqodesh ve'et ohel mo'ed ve'et hammizbeach vehiqriv et hassa'ir hechai.
verses.study_note: The transition marker: vekhillah (and he finished/completed) — the blood atonement work is done. The Most Holy Place, the tent of meeting, and the altar have all been atoned. Now Aaron turns to the live goat (hassa'ir hechai). The word hechai (the living one) emphasizes the contrast with what has just happened — blood has been shed, animals have died. Now a living animal steps forward to play its unique, complementary role. The completeness (killah — from kalah, to complete, finish) of the blood work precedes the scapegoat ritual: expiation follows propitiation in the divine order.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sequence of Yom Kippur is theologically instructive: propitiation (blood on the mercy seat) always precedes expiation (scapegoat sending). Sin must first be covered before it can be carried away. This order is preserved in the NT: Christ's blood must first satisfy divine justice (Romans 3:25-26) before the forgiveness that results can be proclaimed (Hebrews 10:17-18). There is no cheap expiation — no carrying away of guilt — without the costly propitiation of blood. The order cannot be reversed. The scapegoat ritual would be meaningless without the prior blood atonement in the Most Holy Place.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron shall lay both his hands upon the head of the live goat, and confess over him all the iniquities of the children of Israel, and all their transgressions in all their sins, putting them upon the head of the goat, and shall send him away by the hand of a fit man into the wilderness:
verses.original_text: וְסָמַךְ אַהֲרֹן אֶת שְׁתֵּי יָדָו עַל רֹאשׁ הַשָּׂעִיר הַחַי וְהִתְוַדָּה עָלָיו אֶת כָּל עֲוֹנֹת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְאֶת כָּל פִּשְׁעֵיהֶם לְכָל חַטֹּאתָם וְנָתַן אֹתָם עַל רֹאשׁ הַשָּׂעִיר וְשִׁלַּח בְּיַד אִישׁ עִתִּי הַמִּדְבָּרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamakh Aharon et shetei yadav al rosh hassa'ir hechai vehitvaddah alav et kol avonot benei Yisra'el ve'et kol pishe'ihem lekhol chattotam venatan otam al rosh hassa'ir veshillach beyad ish itti hammidbbarah.
verses.study_note: This is the most dramatic visible act of the entire Yom Kippur ceremony. Aaron lays both hands (shetei yadav — both, not one) on the head of the live goat and confesses (hitvaddah — Hithpael reflexive of yadah, to acknowledge, confess) all the iniquities (avonot), transgressions (pesha'im), and sins (chattot) of Israel over it. The tripartite sin vocabulary is comprehensive: avon (iniquity, moral guilt and its consequence), pesha (rebellion, willful transgression), chattat (sin, failure, offense) — every category of human wrongdoing before God. The verb samakh (to lean heavily, press firmly) with both hands indicates the deliberate, total transfer of sin-guilt from Israel to the goat.
verses.doctrinal_note: The hand-laying confession over the scapegoat is one of the most powerful pictures of imputation in the entire Old Testament. Aaron's hands physically transferring Israel's sin to the goat's head enacts in visible, embodied form the theological principle of substitutionary atonement: guilt is transferred from the guilty party to the innocent substitute. Isaiah 53:6 uses identical language: 'the LORD hath laid on him the iniquity of us all' (hifgiya bo et avon kullanu). The hiph'il of paga (to cause to fall upon, to lay upon) is the causative form of the burden-transfer. Paul's summary in 2 Corinthians 5:21 is the NT crystallization: 'he hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin; that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.' The scapegoat is the most visible Old Testament image of this great exchange.

verses.kjv_text: And the goat shall bear upon him all their iniquities unto a land not inhabited: and he shall let go the goat in the wilderness.
verses.original_text: וְנָשָׂא הַשָּׂעִיר עָלָיו אֶת כָּל עֲוֹנֹתָם אֶל אֶרֶץ גְּזֵרָה וְשִׁלַּח אֶת הַשָּׂעִיר בַּמִּדְבָּר׃
verses.transliteration: Venasa hassa'ir alav et kol avonotam el eretz gezerah veshillach et hassa'ir bammidbbar.
verses.study_note: The scapegoat 'bears' (nasa — to lift up, carry, bear) the iniquities of Israel to 'a land not inhabited' (eretz gezerah — a land of cutting off, a desolate, cut-off place). The verb nasa (to bear/carry) is the exact word used in Isaiah 53:4,11,12 for the Suffering Servant bearing the sins of the people: 'Surely he hath borne (nasa) our griefs... he shall bear their iniquities... he bore the sin of many.' The sin is not merely symbolically associated with the goat — it is carried (nasa) to a place of permanent separation. The gezerah (cut-off land) represents the ultimate removal of covenant guilt from the community.
verses.doctrinal_note: The verb nasa (to bear, carry) used here for the scapegoat's carrying of Israel's sin is one of the most theologically significant verbs in the Old Testament's atonement vocabulary. Isaiah uses it three times of the Suffering Servant (53:4,11,12), and the author of Hebrews explicitly connects the scapegoat sending with Christ: 'so Christ was once offered to bear (anaphero — LXX nasa) the sins of many' (Hebrews 9:28). The scapegoat is sent to eretz gezerah (the cut-off land) — total geographical separation expressing total forensic separation. Hebrews 13:11-13 makes this typological connection explicit: 'the bodies of those beasts... are burned without the camp. Wherefore Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate.' Christ went outside, into the 'cut-off' place, carrying our sins with him.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron shall come into the tabernacle of the congregation, and shall put off the linen garments, which he put on when he went into the holy place, and shall leave them there:
verses.original_text: וּבָא אַהֲרֹן אֶל אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וּפָשַׁט אֶת בִּגְדֵי הַבָּד אֲשֶׁר לָבַשׁ בְּבֹאוֹ אֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ וְהִנִּיחָם שָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Uva Aharon el ohel mo'ed ufashat et bigdei habad asher lavash bevo'o el haqqodesh vehiniicham sham.
verses.study_note: Having completed the scapegoat ceremony, Aaron returns to the tabernacle and removes (pashat — to strip off) the white linen garments. The phrase 'leave them there' (vehiniicham sham) is unusual — the linen garments are left inside the tabernacle, not carried out. Later instructions (v.28) indicate they will be burned. The linen garments, having been worn in the Most Holy Place and throughout the Yom Kippur ceremony, are now set aside. The ritual transition from the Yom Kippur role back to regular priestly ministry requires a change of garments — Aaron will bathe again (v.24) and put on his regular priestly vestments before completing the remaining burnt offerings.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall wash his flesh with water in the holy place, and put on his garments, and come forth, and offer his burnt offering, and the burnt offering of the people, and make an atonement for himself, and for the people.
verses.original_text: וְרָחַץ אֶת בְּשָׂרוֹ בַמַּיִם בְּמָקוֹם קָדוֹשׁ וְלָבַשׁ אֶת בְּגָדָיו וְיָצָא וְעָשָׂה אֶת עֹלָתוֹ וְאֶת עֹלַת הָעָם וְכִפֶּר בַּעֲדוֹ וּבְעַד הָעָם׃
verses.transliteration: Verachatz et besaro vamayim bemaqom qadosh velavash et begadav veyatza ve'asah et olatoh ve'et olat ha'am vekipper ba'ado uvad ha'am.
verses.study_note: A second full immersion marks the transition: Aaron washes his body with water in a holy place and then dons his regular priestly garments (the ornate blue, purple, and scarlet vestments of Exodus 28). He emerges from the tabernacle to offer the burnt offerings — his own olah and the people's olah. The burnt offerings (olot) that conclude Yom Kippur express the complete consecration and dedication that follows atonement: once sin is dealt with, the proper response is total self-offering to God. The cycle is complete: preparation → atonement → renewal.

verses.kjv_text: And the fat of the sin offering shall he burn upon the altar.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת חֵלֶב הַחַטָּאת יַקְטִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et chelev hachattat yaqtir hammizbeachah.
verses.study_note: The fat portions (chelev) of the sin offering are burned on the altar — consistent with the general Levitical rule that the fat belongs to the LORD (Lev 3:16-17; 7:23-25). Even in the unique Yom Kippur ceremony, the normal rules governing what is offered to God are maintained. The fat of the Yom Kippur sin offering (the bull and goat) follows the same consecration pattern as all other offerings: the fatty portions ascending to God in fire. This brief verse maintains continuity between the extraordinary Yom Kippur ritual and the ordinary sacrificial principles governing all Israel's worship.

verses.kjv_text: And he that let go the goat for the scapegoat shall wash his clothes, and bathe his flesh in water, and afterward come into the camp.
verses.original_text: וְהַמְשַׁלֵּחַ אֶת הַשָּׂעִיר לַעֲזָאזֵל יְכַבֵּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ אֶת בְּשָׂרוֹ בַּמַּיִם וְאַחֲרֵי כֵן יָבוֹא אֶל הַמַּחֲנֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehameshallei'ach et hassa'ir la'Aza'zel yekhabbes begadav verachatz et besaro bamayim ve'acharei khen yavo el hammachaneh.
verses.study_note: The designated man who led the scapegoat (hameshallei'ach — the one who sent it away) is defiled by the act and must launder his garments and bathe before returning to the camp. The man became ritually impure by handling the sin-laden goat. His defilement is not a moral failing but a ritual consequence of contact with the sin-bearer. This requirement establishes that even handling the instrument of Israel's expiation is not without cost — the scapegoat ritual's power comes with a price for those who execute it. The careful purification required before re-entering the camp maintains the holiness of the covenant community.

verses.kjv_text: And the bullock for the sin offering, and the goat for the sin offering, whose blood was brought in to make atonement in the holy place, shall one carry forth without the camp; and they shall burn in the fire their skins, and their flesh, and their dung.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת פַּר הַחַטָּאת וְאֵת שְׂעִיר הַחַטָּאת אֲשֶׁר הוּבָא אֶת דָּמָם לְכַפֵּר בַּקֹּדֶשׁ יוֹצִיא אֶל מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה וְשָׂרְפוּ בָאֵשׁ אֶת עֹרֹתָם וְאֶת בְּשָׂרָם וְאֶת פִּרְשָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et par hachattat ve'et se'ir hachattat asher huva et damam lekhapper baqqodesh yotzi el michuz lamachaneh vesarefu va'esh et orotam ve'et besaram ve'et pirsham.
verses.study_note: The carcasses of the bull and the goat — the animals whose blood had been brought into the Most Holy Place — must be taken completely outside the camp and burned entirely. The Yom Kippur sin offering animals are not eaten as in regular sin offerings (Lev 6:26,30). Their complete incineration 'outside the camp' (el michuz lamachaneh) signals that they have been wholly consumed in the work of atonement: nothing is retained, nothing is consumed for human benefit, the entire animal is devoted to the work of expiation. The three elements burned — skins (orot), flesh (basar), and dung (pirsham) — represent the complete animal.
verses.doctrinal_note: The burning of the Yom Kippur sin offerings 'outside the camp' is explicitly applied to Christ in Hebrews 13:11-12: 'For the bodies of those beasts, whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin, are burned without the camp. Wherefore Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate.' Christ's crucifixion at Golgotha, outside the walls of Jerusalem, fulfills this typological requirement precisely. He is the sin offering whose blood has been brought before God, whose body was 'burned' (consumed, offered) completely outside the city — outside the covenant community, bearing its ultimate rejection in order to accomplish its ultimate atonement.

verses.kjv_text: And he that burneth them shall wash his clothes, and bathe his flesh in water, and afterward he shall come into the camp.
verses.original_text: וְהַשֹּׂרֵף אֹתָם יְכַבֵּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ אֶת בְּשָׂרוֹ בַּמַּיִם וְאַחֲרֵי כֵן יָבוֹא אֶל הַמַּחֲנֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehassoref otam yekhabbes begadav verachatz et besaro bamayim ve'acharei khen yavo el hammachaneh.
verses.study_note: The person who burns the Yom Kippur sin offering carcasses outside the camp likewise becomes defiled and must launder and bathe before returning to the covenant community. This mirrors the requirement for the scapegoat handler (v.26). The pattern is consistent: any person who directly handles the sin-bearing animals — whether the live scapegoat or the slaughtered sin offering — incurs defilement. Direct contact with the externalization of Israel's sin, even in the divinely ordained context of atonement, carries a ritual cost. This underscores the serious, weighty nature of sin-bearing: it is not clean or neutral work.

verses.kjv_text: And this shall be a statute for ever unto you: that in the seventh month, on the tenth day of the month, ye shall afflict your souls, and do no work at all, whether it be one of your own country, or a stranger that sojourneth among you:
verses.original_text: וְהָיְתָה לָכֶם לְחֻקַּת עוֹלָם בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִי בֶּעָשׂוֹר לַחֹדֶשׁ תְּעַנּוּ אֶת נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם וְכָל מְלָאכָה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ הָאֶזְרָח וְהַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּתוֹכְכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayetah lakhem lechuqqat olam bachoded hashevii be'asor lachoddesh te'annu et nafshotekhem vekhol melakhah lo ta'asu ha'ezrach vehagger hagggar betokheikhem.
verses.study_note: The ceremonial legislation transitions to the eternal statute (chuqqat olam — a permanent decree). Three elements characterize Yom Kippur as a civil-religious observance: it falls in the seventh month (Tishri — the holiest month) on the tenth day; all Israel must 'afflict their souls' (te'annu et nafshotekhem — the Hebrew idiom for fasting and self-denial); and all work is strictly prohibited (kol melakhah lo ta'asu — the same Sabbath-rest formula). The inclusion of 'the stranger that sojourneth among you' extends the observance even to non-Israelites living within the covenant community — Yom Kippur is not merely ethnic but covenantal.
verses.doctrinal_note: The designation of Yom Kippur as a chuqqat olam (eternal, permanent statute) raises the question of its relationship to the new covenant. Hebrews 9:25-26 argues that Christ's atonement is the eschatological fulfillment that gives Yom Kippur its meaning: 'nor yet that he should offer himself often, as the high priest entereth into the holy place every year... but now once in the end of the world hath he appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself.' The annual repetition of Yom Kippur was inherently a confession of incompleteness (Hebrews 10:3-4). Christ's once-for-all sacrifice is the eternal Yom Kippur — the one atonement to which all annual observances pointed and in which they are fulfilled.

verses.kjv_text: For on that day shall the priest make an atonement for you, to cleanse you, that ye may be clean from all your sins before the LORD.
verses.original_text: כִּי בַיּוֹם הַזֶּה יְכַפֵּר עֲלֵיכֶם לְטַהֵר אֶתְכֶם מִכֹּל חַטֹּאתֵיכֶם לִפְנֵי יְהוָה תִּטְהָרוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki vayom hazzeh yekhappe'r aleikhem letaher etkhem mikkol chattoteikhem lifnei YHWH titharu.
verses.study_note: This verse provides the definitive theological purpose statement of Yom Kippur: atonement (yekhappe'r) leading to cleansing (letaher) resulting in being clean (titharu) 'from all your sins before the LORD.' The phrase mikkol chattoteikhem (from all your sins) is comprehensive — no sin is excluded from Yom Kippur's scope. The climactic prepositional phrase lifnei YHWH (before the LORD) identifies the legal forum: Israel is clean in the divine court, before the divine Judge. This is forensic justification — a verdict of 'clean' pronounced by God himself. The entire Yom Kippur apparatus exists for this one purpose: that Israel might stand clean before the holy God.
verses.doctrinal_note: The declaration 'ye shall be clean from all your sins before the LORD' (titharu lifnei YHWH) is the Old Testament's clearest anticipation of justification — the forensic declaration of cleanness before God. The NT equivalent is Romans 5:1: 'Therefore being justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ.' The peace with God that Paul describes is the realized, permanent form of what titharu lifnei YHWH could only annually and provisionally describe. Yom Kippur produced a temporary forensic cleanness; Christ's atonement produces an eternal one — 'by one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified' (Hebrews 10:14).

verses.kjv_text: It shall be a sabbath of rest unto you, and ye shall afflict your souls, by a statute for ever.
verses.original_text: שַׁבַּת שַׁבָּתוֹן הִיא לָכֶם וְעִנִּיתֶם אֶת נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם חֻקַּת עוֹלָם׃
verses.transliteration: Shabbat shabbaton hi lakhem ve'inniitem et nafshotekhem chuqqat olam.
verses.study_note: Yom Kippur is designated a Shabbat Shabbaton — a Sabbath of Sabbaths, a Sabbath of complete rest. The double Sabbath formula (shabbat + shabbaton) is the most emphatic possible designation: it exceeds even the weekly Sabbath in its absolute rest requirement. The same formula applies to the Sabbath Year (Lev 25:4) and the weekly Sabbath itself in Leviticus 23:3. On Yom Kippur, all work ceases completely in conjunction with the soul-affliction of fasting. The combination of rest and affliction creates a day of total dependence on God — Israel can neither work nor eat, relying entirely on divine grace. The formula chuqqat olam (eternal statute) echoes verse 29, framing the Shabbat Shabbaton requirement as permanent.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest, whom he shall anoint, and whom he shall consecrate to minister in the priest’s office in his father’s stead, shall make the atonement, and shall put on the linen clothes, even the holy garments:
verses.original_text: וְכִפֶּר הַכֹּהֵן אֲשֶׁר יִמְשַׁח אֹתוֹ וַאֲשֶׁר יְמַלֵּא אֶת יָדוֹ לְכַהֵן תַּחַת אָבִיו וְלָבַשׁ אֶת בִּגְדֵי הַבָּד בִּגְדֵי הַקֹּדֶשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Vekipper hakkohen asher yimshach oto va'asher yemalle et yado lekhahein tachat aviv velavash et bigdei habad bigdei haqqodesh.
verses.study_note: The legislation extends beyond Aaron to his successors: the priest who is anointed (yimshach — Qal imperfect from mashach, to anoint) and consecrated (yemalle et yado — literally 'whose hand is filled,' the idiom for priestly ordination) in his father's place. The Yom Kippur law is thus dynastic and institutional, not personal to Aaron alone. Each successive High Priest performs the same ceremony in the same white linen garments. This perpetuation clause answers a practical question: what happens when Aaron dies? The atonement institution outlives its first officiant. The chuqqat olam (v.29,31) applies to every generation of high priests.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall make an atonement for the holy sanctuary, and he shall make an atonement for the tabernacle of the congregation, and for the altar, and he shall make an atonement for the priests, and for all the people of the congregation.
verses.original_text: וְכִפֶּר אֶת מִקְדַּשׁ הַקֹּדֶשׁ וְאֶת אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְאֶת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ יְכַפֵּר וְעַל הַכֹּהֲנִים וְעַל כָּל עַם הַקָּהָל יְכַפֵּר׃
verses.transliteration: Vekipper et miqdash haqqodesh ve'et ohel mo'ed ve'et hammizbeach yekhappe'r ve'al hakkohenim ve'al kol am haqqahal yekhappe'r.
verses.study_note: The comprehensive scope of Yom Kippur atonement is summarized in a fivefold enumeration: the holy sanctuary (miqdash haqqodesh — the Most Holy Place), the tent of meeting (ohel mo'ed — the outer sanctuary), the altar (hammizbeach — the bronze altar), the priests (hakkohenim — the entire priestly order), and all the people of the assembly (kol am haqqahal). The verb kipper appears three times in this verse, punctuating each major category of atonement. Nothing is exempt, no one is excluded: the entire worship infrastructure and the entire covenant community are brought under the Yom Kippur atonement.

verses.kjv_text: And this shall be an everlasting statute unto you, to make an atonement for the children of Israel for all their sins once a year. And he did as the LORD commanded Moses.
verses.original_text: וְהָיְתָה זֹּאת לָכֶם לְחֻקַּת עוֹלָם לְכַפֵּר עַל בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מִכָּל חַטֹּאתָם אַחַת בַּשָּׁנָה וַיַּעַשׂ כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayetah zot lakhem lechuqqat olam lekhapper al benei Yisra'el mikkol chattotam achat bashshanah vayyaas ka'asher tzivvah YHWH et Mosheh.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with the eternal statute formula and its two defining qualifications: mikkol chattotam (from all their sins — comprehensive scope) and achat bashshanah (once a year — deliberate frequency). The annual single occurrence of Yom Kippur is both the beauty and the limitation of the Levitical system. Its once-a-year-ness declares that atonement is not daily or piecemeal but a decisive annual event. Yet its repetition year after year declares incompleteness — a perfect, final atonement would not need repeating. The closing phrase 'And he did as the LORD commanded Moses' (vayyaas ka'asher tzivvah YHWH et Mosheh) is the obedience formula that appears throughout Exodus and Leviticus, confirming that the entire Yom Kippur ceremony was immediately enacted as commanded.
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase 'once a year' (achat bashshanah) is the most theologically loaded phrase in Leviticus 16. Hebrews uses it as the key contrast with Christ's once-for-all atonement: 'But into the second went the high priest alone once every year, not without blood' (Hebrews 9:7). The contrast is made explicit in Hebrews 10:1-4: the annual repetition 'can never... make the comers thereunto perfect' and 'can never take away sins.' The annual Yom Kippur was a shadow (skia) pointing to the substance (soma) which is Christ (Colossians 2:17). Christ's atonement is 'once for all' (ephapax — Hebrews 9:12; 10:10) — the eschatological Yom Kippur that permanently fulfills what the annual ceremony could only provisionally represent. The entire Levitical system was a divinely designed pedagogy, teaching Israel what kind of atonement was needed, until the One who could provide it finally came.

meta.overview:
Leviticus 16 is the theological summit of the entire book of Leviticus — and arguably of the entire Old Testament sacrificial system. It prescribes the one annual ceremony in which the High Priest alone enters the Most Holy Place (the Holy of Holies), makes atonement for his own sins, and then for the sins of the entire nation of Israel. This is Yom Kippur — the Day of Atonement — the holiest day in the Hebrew calendar and the culmination of the Levitical theology of access to a holy God.

The chapter opens with a divine warning occasioned by the death of Nadab and Abihu (chapter 10): Aaron cannot enter the Most Holy Place whenever he wishes, but only on the prescribed day and in the prescribed manner, lest he die. The elaborate ritual that follows unfolds in careful sequence: Aaron bathes, dresses in plain white linen (not his ornate high priestly garments), offers a bull for his own sin, and then casts lots over two goats. One goat is slaughtered as a sin offering for the people, and its blood is brought behind the veil to be sprinkled on and before the kapporet (the mercy seat, the atonement cover) of the ark. The other goat — the scapegoat (Azazel goat) — has the sins of Israel confessed over it and is sent into the wilderness by the hand of a designated man, carrying the nation's iniquities away to a desolate place.

The theological architecture of Yom Kippur is breathtaking. Two goats together accomplish what no single animal could: one illustrates propitiation — the satisfying of divine wrath through blood — and the other illustrates expiation — the removal and carrying away of guilt. Both dimensions are necessary for full atonement. The blood on the mercy seat transforms the lid of the ark from a throne of judgment (where the law below would condemn) into a place of meeting — kapporet derives from kipper (to cover, to atone), and the LORD promises to meet Israel there.

The New Testament reads Leviticus 16 as the most direct typological pointer to Christ's atoning work. The book of Hebrews (chapters 9–10) uses the Day of Atonement as its primary framework: Christ is the true High Priest who has entered the true Most Holy Place — heaven itself — not with the blood of bulls and goats but with his own blood, having obtained eternal redemption. Paul identifies Christ as the hilasterion (the mercy seat / propitiation) in Romans 3:25, using the precise LXX term for the kapporet. The tearing of the temple veil at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:51) is the definitive sign that the barrier between God and humanity — which Leviticus 16 could only temporarily and annually address — has been permanently removed. Yom Kippur is fulfilled, not abolished.
meta.theme: The Day of Atonement: Access to the Holy God Through Blood and Substitution
meta.keyWordMeaning: Atonement — from the root kipper (H3722), to cover, to wipe clean, to make propitiation. Kippur is the noun form and gives its name to Yom Kippur (the Day of Atonement). The word carries the dual idea of covering sin (so it no longer stands before God as accusation) and appeasing divine wrath through substitutionary sacrifice. The LXX renders it with hilasmos/hilasterion (propitiation), which Paul uses in Romans 3:25 for Christ's atoning work. Kippur is the theological center of the entire Levitical system — every sacrifice points toward and derives its efficacy from the atonement principle.
outline: The Warning: Access to the Holy Place Is Not Presumptuous (vv.1-2): The death of Nadab and Abihu as the backdrop
Aaron's Preparation: Bathing, White Linen, and the Offerings (vv.3-5): The high priest's personal preparation
The Sin Offering for Aaron: The Bull and the First Incense (vv.6-10): Atonement for the priest himself
The Two Goats: Lot-Casting and the Scapegoat Designated (vv.7-10): The dual goat typology
Blood on the Mercy Seat: Propitiation in the Most Holy Place (vv.11-17): The climactic atonement ritual
The Altar of Burnt Offering Purified (vv.18-19): Extending atonement to the sanctuary furniture
The Scapegoat: Confession and Sending Away (vv.20-22): Expiation — the removal of Israel's sins
Aaron's Re-robing and Final Offerings (vv.23-25): Transition from atonement to regular service
The Scapegoat Handler Purified (vv.26-28): Defilement and cleansing of those who handled sin
The Eternal Statute: Yom Kippur Legislated Forever (vv.29-34): The annual Day of Atonement commanded for all generations

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayedabber YHWH el Moshe lemor.
verses.study_note: The standard Mosaic address formula opens the chapter. Unlike the joint Moses-Aaron address of chapters 13-14, chapter 17 is addressed to Moses alone as lawgiver-mediator. The legislation that follows, however, is explicitly directed to 'Aaron, and his sons, and all the children of Israel' (v.2) — Moses receives the law in order to transmit it to the entire covenant community without exception. The divine origin of what follows is thus firmly established before any content is given: the blood theology of this chapter is not Mosaic tradition but divine revelation, carrying the full weight of YHWH's legislative authority.

verses.kjv_text: Speak unto Aaron, and unto his sons, and unto all the children of Israel, and say unto them; This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded, saying,
verses.original_text: דַּבֵּר אֶל אַהֲרֹן וְאֶל בָּנָיו וְאֶל כָּל בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵיהֶם זֶה הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Dabber el Aharon ve-el banav ve-el kol benei Yisra'el ve-amarta alehem zeh ha-davar asher tzivvah YHWH lemor.
verses.study_note: The three-fold address — Aaron, his sons, all the children of Israel — is the most comprehensive possible audience for the legislation. Priests and laity alike are bound by what follows. The phrase 'this is the thing which the LORD hath commanded' (zeh ha-davar asher tzivvah YHWH) is a solemn legislative formula, distinct from the more common 'speak to the children of Israel.' It presents the following law not merely as instruction but as divine command (tzivvah — from tzavah, to command with authority). The inclusion of both Aaron and all Israel signals that the blood laws to follow are not merely cultic-priestly regulations but covenant obligations binding on the entire people of God.

verses.kjv_text: What man soever there be of the house of Israel, that killeth an ox, or lamb, or goat, in the camp, or that killeth it out of the camp,
verses.original_text: אִישׁ אִישׁ מִבֵּית יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁחַט שׁוֹר אוֹ כֶשֶׂב אוֹ עֵז בַּמַּחֲנֶה אוֹ אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁחַט מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Ish ish mi-beit Yisra'el asher yishchat shor o kesev o ez ba-machaneh o asher yishchat mi-chutz la-machaneh.
verses.study_note: The emphatic 'whatsoever man' (ish ish — literally 'man, man,' a distributive expression of universality) indicates that no individual is exempt from the law. Three domesticated animals are specified: ox (shor), lamb (kesev), and goat (ez) — the primary animals of Israelite agriculture and the very animals permitted for altar sacrifice. The scope covers slaughter both 'in the camp' (ba-machaneh) and 'out of the camp' (mi-chutz la-machaneh) — every location is covered. This comprehensive geographic scope prevents any loophole by which slaughter outside the camp might escape the law's requirements. The three animals specified are precisely the animals that chapters 1-7 have devoted extensive legislation to governing as sacrificial offerings, establishing that all slaughter of these animals falls within the sacred-sacrificial domain.

verses.kjv_text: And bringeth it not unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, to offer an offering unto the LORD before the tabernacle of the LORD; blood shall be imputed unto that man; he hath shed blood; and that man shall be cut off from among his people:
verses.original_text: וְאֶל פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד לֹא הֱבִיאוֹ לְהַקְרִיב קָרְבָּן לַיהוָה לִפְנֵי מִשְׁכַּן יְהוָה דָּם יֵחָשֵׁב לָאִישׁ הַהוּא דָּם שָׁפָךְ וְנִכְרַת הָאִישׁ הַהוּא מִקֶּרֶב עַמּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-el petach ohel mo'ed lo hevi'o le-haqriv qorban la-YHWH lifnei mishkan YHWH dam yechashev la-ish ha-hu dam shafakh ve-nikhrat ha-ish ha-hu mi-qerev ammo.
verses.study_note: The penalty for unauthorized slaughter is severe: 'blood shall be imputed unto that man' (dam yechashev la-ish ha-hu — blood shall be reckoned to him) and he 'hath shed blood' (dam shafakh). The language of bloodguilt applied to a person who kills a livestock animal without bringing it to the tabernacle is startling. It equates unauthorized slaughter with homicide in terms of its covenant violation — the same 'shed blood' (dam shafakh) vocabulary used for murder (Gen 9:6; Num 35:33). The rationale is that blood belongs to God, and to shed it outside the divinely appointed context is to treat it as a common thing — a form of profanation equivalent to taking life outside divine authorization. The penalty is 'cutting off' (nikhrat — the karet penalty, the most severe in the Levitical system, implying divine excision from the covenant community, potentially including premature death).
verses.doctrinal_note: The equation of unauthorized animal slaughter with bloodguilt — 'he hath shed blood' — establishes the foundational principle that blood is not merely biological fluid but carries covenantal and theological weight as the bearer of life. This is the premise that makes verse 11 possible: blood can function as atonement because it carries life. The unauthorized shedding of blood — whether human (murder) or animal (unsanctioned slaughter) — is a violation of divine sovereignty over life itself. God is the author of life; blood is its vehicle; the altar is its divinely appointed destination. The New Testament develops this principle to its furthest implication: the blood of Christ is the ultimate fulfillment of every altar-blood since Abel (Heb 12:24). Where unauthorized blood shed outside the appointed place brought karet (cutting off), the blood of Christ shed at the appointed place and time ('in the fullness of time,' Gal 4:4) achieves the opposite: not cutting off but adoption into the covenant family (Rom 8:15).

verses.kjv_text: To the end that the children of Israel may bring their sacrifices, which they offer in the open field, even that they may bring them unto the LORD, unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, unto the priest, and offer them for peace offerings unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: לְמַעַן אֲשֶׁר יָבִיאוּ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת זִבְחֵיהֶם אֲשֶׁר הֵם זֹבְחִים עַל פְּנֵי הַשָּׂדֶה וֶהֱבִיאֻם לַיהוָה אֶל פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד אֶל הַכֹּהֵן וְזָבְחוּ זִבְחֵי שְׁלָמִים לַיהוָה אֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Le-ma'an asher yavi'u venei Yisra'el et zivcheihem asher hem zovechim al penei ha-sadeh ve-hevi'um la-YHWH el petach ohel mo'ed el ha-kohen ve-zavechu zivchei shelamim la-YHWH otam.
verses.study_note: The purpose clause (le-ma'an — in order that) explains the legislation of verses 3-4: the law exists 'to the end that' Israel's existing sacrifices offered 'in the open field' (al penei ha-sadeh) may be redirected to the tabernacle. The phrase 'in the open field' suggests a pre-Mosaic or popular practice of field sacrifice — animal slaughter accompanied by some form of religious act in non-designated locations. The law channels these offerings through the priestly system at the tabernacle and redefines them as shelamim (peace/fellowship offerings) — the offering type most associated with communal participation and shared meals. The reform is not prohibition of sacrifice but centralization and proper ordering of it.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall sprinkle the blood upon the altar of the LORD at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, and burn the fat for a sweet savour unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְזָרַק הַכֹּהֵן אֶת הַדָּם עַל מִזְבַּח יְהוָה פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְהִקְטִיר הַחֵלֶב לְרֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-zaraq ha-kohen et ha-dam al mizbach YHWH petach ohel mo'ed ve-hiqtir ha-chelev le-reach nicho'ach la-YHWH.
verses.study_note: The properly presented animal has its blood sprinkled (zaraq — the general verb for altar blood-sprinkling) on the LORD's altar at the tabernacle entrance, and its fat (chelev — the premium, reserved fat portions) burned as a sweet savour (reach nicho'ach). The two priestly acts — blood on the altar and fat burned — are the essential liturgical acts that transform an animal slaughter into a divine offering. The blood belongs to the altar; the fat belongs to the fire. These two acts are the minimum necessary for the slaughter to be properly sanctified. Without them, the animal's blood is 'shed' (dam shafakh, v.4) — merely killed. With them, the blood 'covers' (kipper) and the fat 'ascends' to God as a pleasing fragrance.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall no more offer their sacrifices unto devils, after whom they have gone a whoring. This shall be a statute for ever unto them throughout their generations.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא יִזְבְּחוּ עוֹד אֶת זִבְחֵיהֶם לַשְּׂעִירִם אֲשֶׁר הֵם זֹנִים אַחֲרֵיהֶם חֻקַּת עוֹלָם תִּהְיֶה זֹּאת לָהֶם לְדֹרֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-lo yizbechu od et zivcheihem la-se'irim asher hem zonim achareihem chuqqat olam tihyeh zot lahem le-dorotam.
verses.study_note: The true motivation behind the centralization law is now revealed: Israel has been 'going a whoring' (zonim — the covenant-unfaithfulness idiom for spiritual adultery) after the se'irim — translated 'devils' in the KJV but literally 'he-goats' or 'hairy ones,' referring to goat-demons or wilderness spirits to whom unauthorized sacrifices were being offered. The se'irim (from sa'ir, a hairy goat, also used for Azazel's goat in Lev 16:8-10) are the demonic entities associated with wilderness places and pagan high-place worship. The command is absolute: 'they shall no more offer' (lo yizbechu od) — this practice is permanently prohibited. The formula 'a statute for ever' (chuqqat olam) places this prohibition among the permanent, unrepeatable covenant ordinances (cf. Lev 16:29,31,34). The spiritual adultery (zonim) metaphor is the strongest possible condemnation: worshiping se'irim is not merely unauthorized religion but covenant-breaking betrayal equivalent to adultery.
verses.doctrinal_note: The revelation of se'irim-worship as Israel's wilderness practice illuminates the entire structure of the chapter: the centralization law (vv.3-6) is not merely administrative but is a direct combat measure against demonic worship. Paul makes this connection explicit in 1 Corinthians 10:20 — 'the things which the Gentiles sacrifice, they sacrifice to devils, and not to God.' The demonic reality behind idolatrous sacrifice means that unauthorized slaughter and the consumption of blood in pagan contexts is participation in the demonic, not merely cultural preference. This is the background for the Jerusalem Council's decision to instruct Gentile believers to 'abstain from pollutions of idols' and from blood (Acts 15:20,29) — connecting Leviticus 17's two main concerns (unauthorized sacrifice + blood prohibition) to the church's practical ethics. The 'statute for ever' (chuqqat olam) for Israel has its NT analog in the permanent separation of the church from demonic worship of any kind (2 Cor 6:14-16).

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt say unto them, Whatsoever man there be of the house of Israel, or of the strangers which sojourn among you, that offereth a burnt offering or sacrifice,
verses.original_text: וַאֲלֵהֶם תֹּאמַר אִישׁ אִישׁ מִבֵּית יִשְׂרָאֵל וּמִן הַגֵּר אֲשֶׁר יָגוּר בְּתוֹכָם אֲשֶׁר יַעֲלֶה עֹלָה אוֹ זָבַח׃
verses.transliteration: Va-alehem tomar ish ish mi-beit Yisra'el u-min ha-ger asher yagur be-tokham asher ya'aleh olah o zavach.
verses.study_note: The centralization law is now extended beyond native Israelites to include the ger (resident alien, stranger who sojourns). The repeated ish ish (every man) applies universally, and now explicitly includes the ger — the non-Israelite living within the covenant community. This extension is significant: the ger is obligated to the same worship-centralization requirements as the native Israelite. Two offering types are specified in this application: the olah (burnt offering — complete ascent to God) and the zavach (sacrifice — the broader term for any slaughter with a cultic dimension). The inclusion of the ger reflects the Torah's consistent principle that those who dwell within Israel share Israel's covenant obligations in matters of worship and blood (cf. Lev 17:10,12,13; Num 15:15-16).

verses.kjv_text: And bringeth it not unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, to offer it unto the LORD; even that man shall be cut off from among his people.
verses.original_text: וְאֶל פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד לֹא יְבִיאֶנּוּ לַעֲשׂוֹת אֹתוֹ לַיהוָה וְנִכְרַת הָאִישׁ הַהוּא מֵעַמָּיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-el petach ohel mo'ed lo yevi'ennu la-asot oto la-YHWH ve-nikhrat ha-ish ha-hu me-ammav.
verses.study_note: The karet penalty applies equally to the ger as to the native Israelite for offering sacrifices outside the tabernacle. The equal application of the severest covenant penalty to both Israelite and resident alien underscores that the blood laws are not ethnic markers but universal covenant principles grounded in the nature of blood itself. The phrase 'from among his people' (me-ammav) applied to the ger is notable — the ger who sojourns within Israel has joined 'his people' in a sense: the covenant community is the people he may be cut off from. Inclusion in the covenant community brings both blessing and obligation.

verses.kjv_text: And whatsoever man there be of the house of Israel, or of the strangers that sojourn among you, that eateth any manner of blood; I will even set my face against that soul that eateth blood, and will cut him off from among his people.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אִישׁ מִבֵּית יִשְׂרָאֵל וּמִן הַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּתוֹכָם אֲשֶׁר יֹאכַל כָּל דָּם וְנָתַתִּי פָנַי בַּנֶּפֶשׁ הָאֹכֶלֶת אֶת הַדָּם וְהִכְרַתִּי אֹתָהּ מִקֶּרֶב עַמָּהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish ish mi-beit Yisra'el u-min ha-ger ha-gar be-tokham asher yokhal kol dam ve-natatti fanai ba-nefesh ha-okheleth et ha-dam ve-hikhrati otah mi-qerev ammah.
verses.study_note: The blood-prohibition section opens with the most personal and direct of all the covenant penalties: not merely karet declared by the community but 'I will even set my face against that soul' (ve-natatti fanai ba-nefesh — literally 'I will give my face against the soul'). The phrase 'set my face against' (natan panim be-) is the idiom of divine personal opposition — the LORD Himself turns his countenance in active hostility against the one who eats blood. This is stronger than the earlier karet formula: it is not merely covenant excision but divine enmity. The use of nefesh (soul) for the eater of blood creates a deliberate contrast with verse 11 where nefesh is used for the life carried in the blood — the one who consumes blood (nefesh eating) violates the principle that blood covers the nefesh (soul/life) as atonement.

verses.kjv_text: For the life of the flesh is in the blood: and I have given it to you upon the altar to make an atonement for your souls: for it is the blood that maketh an atonement for the soul.
verses.original_text: כִּי נֶפֶשׁ הַבָּשָׂר בַּדָּם הִוא וַאֲנִי נְתַתִּיו לָכֶם עַל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ לְכַפֵּר עַל נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם כִּי הַדָּם הוּא בַּנֶּפֶשׁ יְכַפֵּר׃
verses.transliteration: Ki nefesh ha-basar ba-dam hi va-ani netativ lakhem al ha-mizbe'ach le-khapper al nafshoteikhem ki ha-dam hu ba-nefesh yekhappe'r.
verses.study_note: The theological apex of Leviticus and one of the most concentrated doctrinal statements in the entire Old Testament. The verse contains three sequential theological propositions: (1) 'the life of the flesh is in the blood' (nefesh ha-basar ba-dam hi) — the ontological principle that life (nefesh) is carried by and equivalent to blood (dam). The blood is not merely associated with life but is its bearer and representative; (2) 'I have given it to you upon the altar to make an atonement for your souls' (va-ani netativ lakhem al ha-mizbe'ach le-khapper al nafshoteikhem) — the soteriological principle that God Himself is the giver (va-ani — 'and I') of the blood as the atoning instrument. The initiative, provision, and appointment of atonement is entirely divine; (3) 'it is the blood that maketh an atonement for the soul' (ki ha-dam hu ba-nefesh yekhappe'r) — the explanatory principle: blood atones for the soul because blood carries the soul/life, and thus life covers life in the exchange. The blood of the animal carries its life, and that life is offered to God as a covering (kipper) for the human life that has incurred death-debt through sin.
verses.doctrinal_note: Leviticus 17:11 is the doctrinal foundation of the entire Levitical sacrificial system and, by direct extension, the doctrinal foundation of the New Testament atonement theology. Every major NT atonement text draws on its three propositions. (1) 'The life of the flesh is in the blood' — Paul's argument in Romans 5:9 ('justified by his blood') and 1 Corinthians 11:25 ('this cup is the new testament in my blood') rest on the blood = life equation. The blood of Christ is life given for life, not merely fluid shed. (2) 'I have given it to you' — the divine initiative is absolute. God does not accept a sacrifice humans devise; He prescribes the one He accepts. This becomes in the NT 'God commendeth his love toward us' (Rom 5:8) and 'God so loved the world that he gave' (John 3:16). The at-one-ment is entirely from above, never from below. (3) 'The blood... maketh atonement for the soul' — kipper (to cover, to atone) by means of blood-for-soul. Hebrews 9:22 quotes the principle explicitly: 'without shedding of blood is no remission.' Peter confirms the Levitical theology in 1 Pet 1:18-19 — not redeemed with 'corruptible things, as silver and gold... but with the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish.' John's declaration 'the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from all sin' (1 John 1:7) is the new-covenant fulfillment of ki ha-dam hu ba-nefesh yekhappe'r. The principle of Lev 17:11 is not abolished in the NT — it is fulfilled and perfected: what every drop of altar-blood pointed to, the blood of the Son of God accomplishes once and for all (Heb 9:12; 10:10).

verses.kjv_text: Therefore I said unto the children of Israel, No soul of you shall eat blood, neither shall any stranger that sojourneth among you eat blood.
verses.original_text: עַל כֵּן אָמַרְתִּי לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל כָּל נֶפֶשׁ מִכֶּם לֹא תֹאכַל דָּם וְהַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּתוֹכֶם לֹא יֹאכַל דָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Al ken amarti li-vnei Yisra'el kol nefesh mikkem lo tokhal dam ve-ha-ger ha-gar be-tokhem lo yokhal dam.
verses.study_note: The blood prohibition is now restated following the theological rationale of verse 11, making the connection explicit: 'Therefore' (al ken — on account of this) I said... No soul (kol nefesh) shall eat blood. The word nefesh used for 'soul' in 'no soul of you shall eat' creates a deliberate echo of the nefesh-in-the-blood theology of verse 11 — the very soul (nefesh) that the blood covers on the altar must not eat the blood that covers it. The prohibition applies equally to native Israelite and resident alien — the same two-fold inclusion as verse 10. The theological rationale (v.11) makes the prohibition universal: blood's sanctity as the divinely appointed atonement instrument is not a culturally relative practice but a principle grounded in the nature of life itself.

verses.kjv_text: And whatsoever man there be of the children of Israel, or of the strangers that sojourn among you, which hunteth and catcheth any beast or fowl that may be eaten; he shall even pour out the blood thereof, and cover it with dust.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אִישׁ מִבְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וּמִן הַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּתוֹכָם אֲשֶׁר יָצוּד צֵיד חַיָּה אוֹ עוֹף אֲשֶׁר יֵאָכֵל וְשָׁפַךְ אֶת דָּמוֹ וְכִסָּהוּ בֶּעָפָר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish ish mi-benei Yisra'el u-min ha-ger ha-gar be-tokham asher yatzud tzeyd chayyah o of asher ye'akhel ve-shafakh et damo ve-kissahu be-afar.
verses.study_note: The blood law is extended to hunting. Wild animals (chayyah — beasts, wild animals) and birds (of) that are caught by hunting (tzeyd — to hunt, trap) require special blood treatment because they cannot be brought to the tabernacle for priestly blood-sprinkling as domestic animals can. The prescription is: pour out (shafakh) the blood and cover it (kissahu) with dust (afar — earth, dust). The covering with earth mirrors the law of Genesis 4:10 — blood 'cries out' from the ground (Cain's fratricide); here, blood is covered so it does not cry out. The hunter returns the blood to the ground from which all flesh came, acknowledging that the life (nefesh) in the blood belongs to the Creator who gave it.

verses.kjv_text: For it is the life of all flesh; the blood of it is for the life thereof: therefore I said unto the children of Israel, Ye shall eat the blood of no manner of flesh: for the life of all flesh is the blood thereof: whosoever eateth it shall be cut off.
verses.original_text: כִּי נֶפֶשׁ כָּל בָּשָׂר דָּמוֹ בְנַפְשׁוֹ הוּא וָאֹמַר לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל דַּם כָּל בָּשָׂר לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ כִּי נֶפֶשׁ כָּל בָּשָׂר דָּמוֹ הִוא כָּל אֹכְלָיו יִכָּרֵת׃
verses.transliteration: Ki nefesh kol basar damo ve-nafsho hu va-omar li-vnei Yisra'el dam kol basar lo tokhelu ki nefesh kol basar damo hi kol okhlav yikaret.
verses.study_note: The theological rationale of verse 11 is restated and applied universally — now not merely to the three domesticated animals of verse 3 but to 'all flesh' (kol basar). The principle is stated twice for emphasis: 'the blood of it is for the life thereof' (damo ve-nafsho hu — 'its blood, it is its life') and 'the life of all flesh is the blood thereof' (ki nefesh kol basar damo hi). The double statement creates an envelope of theological grounding around the prohibition: the law is not cultural but ontological — rooted in what blood fundamentally is. The karet penalty is stated without the earlier 'I will set my face' intensification (v.10), suggesting this verse is more declarative than the personal divine opposition of v.10, but the penalty is identical. 'All flesh' (kol basar) extends the prohibition beyond the three domestic species to every category of animal blood.
verses.doctrinal_note: The universal scope of verse 14 — 'the blood of all flesh' — gives the blood-prohibition its permanent theological character. Genesis 9:4, spoken to Noah before the law, before Israel, before the tabernacle, already prohibits consuming blood: 'But flesh with the life thereof, which is the blood thereof, shall ye not eat.' The Noahic prohibition establishes that the blood law is not Israelite-specific but reflects a creational principle about the relationship between life and blood that predates the Sinai covenant. The Jerusalem Council's instruction to Gentile believers to abstain from blood (Acts 15:29) reflects this pre-Mosaic, universal scope of the blood principle. The NT does not abrogate the theological principle of verse 11 — it fulfills it. The Lord's Supper does not involve literal blood-drinking but the communal proclamation and participation in Christ's blood-atonement (1 Cor 10:16; 11:26) — the 'cup' is the new-covenant blood of Christ, received by faith, not by consumption.

verses.kjv_text: And every soul that eateth that which died of itself, or that which was torn with beasts, whether it be one of your own country, or a stranger, he shall both wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even: then shall he be clean.
verses.original_text: וְכָל נֶפֶשׁ אֲשֶׁר תֹּאכַל נְבֵלָה וּטְרֵפָה בָּאֶזְרָח וּבַגֵּר וְכִבֶּס בְּגָדָיו וְרָחַץ בַּמַּיִם וְטָמֵא עַד הָעֶרֶב וְטָהֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-khol nefesh asher tokhal nevelah u-terefah ba-ezrach u-va-ger ve-kibbes begadav ve-rachatz ba-mayyim ve-tame ad ha-erev ve-taher.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with the nevelah (naturally dead animal) and terefah (animal torn by a predator) cases — animals that have died without proper slaughter and blood-draining. These are not deliberately chosen violations of the blood law but inadvertent or emergency consumption situations. The purification requirement is relatively minor: washing garments (kibbes begadav), bathing in water (rachatz ba-mayyim), and uncleanness until evening (tame ad ha-erev) followed by automatic cleanness (ve-taher). The graduated penalty — simple one-day impurity rather than karet — reflects the lesser severity of the violation (no deliberate consumption of blood from a properly slaughtered animal, but consumption of an animal whose blood may not have been fully drained). The law applies equally to the native (ezrach) and the resident alien (ger).

verses.kjv_text: But if he wash them not, nor bathe his flesh; then he shall bear his iniquity.
verses.original_text: וְאִם לֹא יְכַבֵּס וּבְשָׂרוֹ לֹא יִרְחָץ וְנָשָׂא עֲוֹנוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im lo yekhabbes u-vesaro lo yirchatz ve-nasa avono.
verses.study_note: The chapter's final verse addresses non-compliance with the minor purification requirements of verse 15: if the person who ate nevelah or terefah fails to wash his garments and bathe his flesh, 'he shall bear his iniquity' (ve-nasa avono). The phrase nasa avon (to bear iniquity) is the standard formula for carrying the consequence of one's own sin — it is not the karet of permanent covenant excision but the ongoing weight of unforgiven guilt resting on the person. The graduated response is instructive: deliberate blood consumption = karet (divine cutting off); inadvertent consumption of nevelah/terefah with proper purification = clean by evening; inadvertent consumption without seeking purification = nasa avon (bearing one's guilt). The law's graduated penalties reflect proportionate justice — the same principle that governs all Levitical purity legislation.

meta.overview:
Leviticus 17 stands at the structural hinge of the book, transitioning from the ceremonial legislation of chapters 1-16 toward the holiness code of chapters 18-27. Its sixteen verses are among the most theologically significant in the entire Torah, establishing two foundational principles that govern all subsequent Israelite religious and ethical life: the centralization of all animal slaughter at the tabernacle (vv.1-9), and the absolute prohibition on consuming blood grounded in the most explicit statement of atonement theology in the Old Testament (vv.10-16).

The chapter opens with a sweeping law requiring that every Israelite who slaughters an ox, lamb, or goat must bring it to the door of the tabernacle as an offering to the LORD (vv.3-4). The purpose is dual: to end the practice of offering sacrifices to the wilderness goat-demons (se'irim) at unauthorized locations (v.7), and to ensure that all animal slaughter is oriented toward God rather than treated as a purely secular act. The law applies to all Israelites including resident aliens (gerim), establishing covenant community standards that transcend ethnic identity.

The heart of the chapter and arguably the theological apex of the entire book of Leviticus is verse 11: 'For the life of the flesh is in the blood: and I have given it to you upon the altar to make an atonement for your souls: for it is the blood that maketh an atonement for the soul.' This single verse provides the divine rationale for the entire sacrificial system and the prohibition on blood consumption. The blood's sanctity derives from the principle of life-equivalence (dam = nephesh, blood = life) and the divine appointment of blood as the instrument of atonement. God Himself 'gives' the blood for this purpose — the sacrificial system is not a human invention but a divine provision. The blood on the altar is life given for life, soul covering soul.

The chapter concludes with provisions governing the consumption of animals that die naturally (nevelah — v.15) or are killed by wild beasts (terefah — v.16). These boundary cases extend the chapter's concern for blood to include even uninstructed encounters with animal carcasses, establishing graduated purification requirements for unintentional contact with these categories.

Christologically, Leviticus 17 provides the most explicit Old Testament foundation for the New Testament's atonement theology. The writer of Hebrews draws directly on verse 11 when he states 'without the shedding of blood there is no remission' (Heb 9:22). Paul's doctrine of justification by the blood of Christ (Rom 3:25; 5:9) and John's declaration that 'the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from all sin' (1 John 1:7) are theological extensions of the principle established in Leviticus 17:11. The chapter's absolute prohibition on blood consumption — the blood belongs to God, given for atonement — becomes in the NT the ground for understanding the Lord's Supper, where Christ offers His blood as the new-covenant fulfillment of everything the altar blood typified.
meta.theme: The Divine Ownership of Blood: Centralized Worship, Atonement Theology, and the Life-Principle of Sacrifice
meta.keyWordMeaning: Blood — the most theologically significant word in Leviticus 17, occurring 13 times in 16 verses. Dam (H1818) denotes the literal blood of living creatures, but in Leviticus 17 it is elevated to a theological category: blood = life (nephesh), and God has appointed this blood-life as the instrument of atonement on the altar. The chapter's organizing principle is that blood belongs to God — it cannot be consumed (vv.10-14), it cannot be shed outside the appointed place (vv.3-7), and it is the divinely given instrument by which the life of the animal covers the life-debt of the worshiper (v.11). The NT fulfillment is the blood of Christ, which accomplishes what all the altar blood typified: 'the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from all sin' (1 John 1:7).
outline: The Scope of the Legislation (vv.1-2): Divine address to Moses for all Israel including priests and laity
Centralized Slaughter at the Tabernacle (vv.3-7): All oxen, lambs, and goats must be brought to the tabernacle door — ending unauthorized sacrifice and worship of se'irim (goat-demons)
The Perpetual Statute for All Residents (vv.8-9): Extension of the centralization law to resident aliens; penalty of cutting off
The Prohibition on Consuming Blood (vv.10-12): The absolute ban on blood consumption with the divine rationale stated
The Theological Rationale of Verse 11: Life is in the blood; blood is given by God for atonement on the altar
Extension to Hunting and Wild Animals (vv.13-14): Blood of hunted animals must be poured out and covered with earth
Nevelah and Terefah: Contact with Naturally Dead or Torn Animals (vv.15-16): Graduated impurity for inadvertent contact

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: The standard divine address formula opens one of the Bible's most ethically significant chapters. The attribution of the chapter's content directly to YHWH — the covenant God who revealed Himself to Moses at the burning bush — is theologically essential. What follows is not Mosaic social custom, Israelite tribal convention, or ancient Near Eastern religious taboo: it is divine revelation given to the covenant mediator. The sexual ethics of Leviticus 18 derive their authority entirely from their source in the character and command of God. This framing preempts the modern objection that biblical sexual ethics are merely culturally conditioned: the text presents them as transcendent divine revelation, as binding on Israel because of who gave them, not merely because of the culture that received them.

verses.kjv_text: Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, I am the LORD your God.
verses.original_text: דַּבֵּר אֶל בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵהֶם אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Dabber el-benei Yisra'el ve'amarta alehem ani YHWH Eloheikhem.
verses.study_note: The chapter's foundational statement: "I am the LORD your God" (ani YHWH Eloheikhem). This formula — the same that opens the Ten Commandments (Exod 20:2) — grounds every subsequent prohibition in covenant identity rather than legal obligation alone. The sexual ethics that follow are not arbitrary divine decrees but expressions of the character of the One who redeemed Israel from Egypt. "I am the LORD" (ani YHWH) asserts the self-existent, covenant-keeping God's personal authority. "Your God" (Eloheikhem) — the possessive marks the relationship: these are God's people, and the laws governing their intimate lives are given by their Covenant Lord. The formula appears five times in the chapter's preamble and conclusion (vv.2,4,5,6,30), forming a theological bracket around all the specific prohibitions.
verses.doctrinal_note: The covenant formula "I am the LORD your God" (ani YHWH Eloheikhem) as the foundation of sexual ethics is one of the most important structural features of Leviticus 18. It establishes that biblical sexual morality is not primarily deontological (rule-based) nor consequentialist (outcome-based) but covenantal — rooted in a personal relationship with the God who redeemed His people. Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 6:18-20 follows exactly this logic: "your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you... ye are not your own; for ye are bought with a price: therefore glorify God in your body." The New Covenant parallel to "I am the LORD your God" who brought you out of Egypt is "ye are bought with a price" — redemption as the ground of sexual holiness.

verses.kjv_text: After the doings of the land of Egypt, wherein ye dwelt, shall ye not do: and after the doings of the land of Canaan, whither I bring you, shall ye not do: neither shall ye walk in their ordinances.
verses.original_text: כְּמַעֲשֵׂה אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם אֲשֶׁר יְשַׁבְתֶּם בָּהּ לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ וּכְמַעֲשֵׂה אֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי מֵבִיא אֶתְכֶם שָׁמָּה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ וּבְחֻקֹּתֵיהֶם לֹא תֵלֵכוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Kema'asei eretz Mitzrayim asher yeshavtem bah lo ta'asu ukema'asei eretz Kena'an asher ani mevi etkhem shammah lo ta'asu uvechukkotehem lo telechu.
verses.study_note: The ethical standard is defined negatively by two counter-examples: Egypt (where Israel was) and Canaan (where Israel is going). This double prohibition frames Israel's sexual ethic as intentional distinctiveness — a counter-cultural holiness maintained between two poles of pagan practice. The Egyptian context is historically attested: ancient Egypt had no incest prohibitions between siblings (the royal house regularly practiced sibling marriage), recognized various forms of sexual practice outside the Mosaic prohibitions, and included sexual acts in certain cultic settings. The Canaanite context is confirmed by archaeology and the prophetic literature: Canaanite fertility religion incorporated sexual rites, temple prostitution (qedeshah), and the child-sacrifice practices associated with Molech (v.21). "Their ordinances" (chukkotehem — their statutes, their prescribed customs) are explicitly rejected: Israel is to be governed by God's chukkot (v.4), not the nations' chukkot. The ethical framework is explicitly anti-syncretistic.
verses.doctrinal_note: The double prohibition of Egypt and Canaan in v.3 establishes a principle with permanent theological application: the covenant people's sexual ethics are never to be defined by the surrounding culture, whether the culture they have left (Egypt = the world of origin) or the culture they are entering (Canaan = the world of destination). This principle is directly applied by Paul in Romans 12:2: "be not conformed to this world" — the Greek suschematizo (to be shaped according to the same pattern) is the precise parallel to "walk in their ordinances." The New Covenant community, like Israel, is called to a sexual ethic that is distinctive from both its past (pre-conversion life) and its present cultural context (Ephesians 4:17-24; 1 Thessalonians 4:3-5).

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall do my judgments, and keep mine ordinances, to walk therein: I am the LORD your God.
verses.original_text: אֶת מִשְׁפָּטַי תַּעֲשׂוּ וְאֶת חֻקֹּתַי תִּשְׁמְרוּ לָלֶכֶת בָּהֶם אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Et mishpatai ta'asu ve'et chukotai tishmeru lalekheth bahem ani YHWH Eloheikhem.
verses.study_note: The positive command corresponds exactly to the negative prohibitions of v.3: do My judgments (mishpatim), keep My ordinances (chukkot), walk in them. The pairing of mishpatim (judgments — legal rulings with rational grounding) and chukkot (ordinances — prescribed statutes, some of which resist simple rational explanation) covers the full range of Levitical legislation. The sexual laws of vv.6-23 fall into both categories: some have clear rational grounding (incest taboos, adultery), others are presented as divine decree whose full rationale is God's holiness and covenant distinctiveness. "To walk therein" (lalekheth bahem) — the walking metaphor recurs from v.3: Israel is to walk in God's ordinances rather than the nations' ordinances. Sexual ethics are a pattern of life, a way of walking, not merely a checklist of prohibitions. The chapter closes with the repeated covenant formula, sealing the positive command with divine identity.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall therefore keep my statutes, and my judgments: which if a man do, he shall live in them: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת חֻקֹּתַי וְאֶת מִשְׁפָּטַי אֲשֶׁר יַעֲשֶׂה אֹתָם הָאָדָם וָחַי בָּהֶם אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ushmartem et-chukotai ve'et mishpatai asher ya'aseh otam ha'adam vachai bahem ani YHWH.
verses.study_note: The most significant verse in the chapter beyond its immediate context — indeed one of the most quoted Torah texts in the New Testament. "Which if a man do, he shall live in them" (asher ya'aseh otam ha'adam vachai bahem) — the principle of life through obedience. The verb chai (to live, be alive) in connection with Torah-keeping represents the covenantal promise: life is found in the path of God's commandments. This is cited in Nehemiah 9:29, Ezekiel 20:11,13,21, and is the foundation of the Mosaic covenant's life-promise. The phrase ha'adam (the human, the person — the universal term for humanity, from adam, mankind) extends the principle beyond Israel: these are human laws, not merely ethnic regulations, because they flow from the Creator of all humanity.
verses.doctrinal_note: Leviticus 18:5 ("which if a man do, he shall live in them") is one of the most theologically contested texts in the New Testament.
