Paul cites it twice: in Galatians 3:12 ("the law is not of faith: but the man that doeth them shall live in them") and Romans 10:5 ("Moses describeth the righteousness which is of the law, that the man which doeth those things shall live by them"). Paul uses v.5 to define "law-righteousness" in contrast to "faith-righteousness" (Rom 10:6; Gal 3:11 — "the just shall live by faith"). The contrast is not between a good law and a bad principle, but between what law requires (perfect doing) and what faith receives (Christ's perfect doing imputed). Luke 10:28 records Jesus using the same principle ("this do, and thou shalt live") in response to the lawyer's question about eternal life — pointing to the standard the law sets and the impossibility of meeting it fully without Christ (Rom 8:3-4; Gal 3:21-22).

verses.kjv_text: None of you shall approach to any that is near of kin to him, to uncover their nakedness: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: אִישׁ אִישׁ אֶל כָּל שְׁאֵר בְּשָׂרוֹ לֹא תִקְרְבוּ לְגַלּוֹת עֶרְוָה אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ish ish el-kol she'er besaro lo tikkevu legalot ervah ani YHWH.
verses.study_note: The general principle of the incest prohibitions is stated before the specific cases: "None of you shall approach any near of kin to uncover nakedness." The word she'er (near of kin, blood relative, from sha'ar — flesh, kinship) denotes the close bodily connection of biological relatedness. The idiom "to uncover nakedness" (legalot ervah — to lay bare, expose, reveal the nakedness) is the chapter's repeated euphemism for sexual intercourse, drawing on the profound vulnerability and intimacy of bodily exposure. The ish ish (every man, any man) construction broadens the prohibition universally. "I am the LORD" closes the verse as it closes every unit in this chapter — the repeated covenant formula is not mere refrain but a theological anchor: each prohibition is grounded not in social utility but in divine character. Approaches that produce forbidden intimacy within family structures violate the created order of bodily kinship that God established.
verses.doctrinal_note: The incest prohibitions of Leviticus 18 reflect a creational principle embedded in the structure of human society: the family is the fundamental unit of human community, and its internal bonds require protection from sexual confusion. The incest taboo exists in some form in virtually every human culture — anthropologists have noted its near-universal presence — which suggests it reflects a deeply embedded creational reality. Paul's horror at the Corinthian incest case (1 Cor 5:1-5) demonstrates the New Testament's continuity with this principle: the Corinthian case involved a man with his stepmother (precisely the case of Lev 18:8), and Paul's judgment — "deliver such an one unto Satan" — reflects the severity with which the apostle treats the violation of these creational structures even in the New Covenant community.

verses.kjv_text: The nakedness of thy father, or the nakedness of thy mother, shalt thou not uncover: she is thy mother; thou shalt not uncover her nakedness.
verses.original_text: עֶרְוַת אָבִיךָ וְעֶרְוַת אִמְּךָ לֹא תְגַלֵּה אִמְּךָ הִוא לֹא תְגַלֶּה עֶרְוָתָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ervat avikha ve'ervat immekha lo tegalleh immekha hi lo tegalleh ervatah.
verses.study_note: The first specific prohibition is the most fundamental: sexual relations with one's mother are absolutely forbidden. The verse uses a double formulation — "the nakedness of your father, and the nakedness of your mother" — because the mother's nakedness is also the father's nakedness (they are one flesh, Gen 2:24). This is not two separate prohibitions but one: to violate the mother is to violate the father's marriage as well. The additional rationale "she is your mother" (immekha hi) is unique in this chapter — most prohibitions give no rationale beyond "I am the LORD." Here the identity-statement is given: the mother-son relationship itself is the ground of prohibition. The natural role-relationship between mother and child is incompatible with the sexual relationship. The most famous biblical violation of this prohibition is Reuben's sin with Bilhah (Gen 35:22), which permanently cost him his firstborn preeminence (Gen 49:3-4).

verses.kjv_text: The nakedness of thy father’s wife shalt thou not uncover: it is thy father’s nakedness.
verses.original_text: עֶרְוַת אֵשֶׁת אָבִיךָ לֹא תְגַלֵּה עֶרְוַת אָבִיךָ הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ervat eshet avikha lo tegalleh ervat avikha hi.
verses.study_note: The stepmother prohibition extends the mother-prohibition: the father's wife (eshet avikh — any wife of the father, not necessarily the man's biological mother) is protected as "the father's nakedness." The rationale mirrors v.7: to violate the stepmother is to violate the father's most intimate bond. This prohibition is directly relevant to the New Testament: 1 Corinthians 5:1 describes a man in the Corinthian church who has his "father's wife" (gynaika tou patros) — the exact category of Leviticus 18:8. Paul's response — calling for immediate excommunication and apostolic judgment — demonstrates the continuing normative force of this Levitical prohibition in the New Covenant community. The parallel case in the patriarchal narratives is Reuben's sin with Bilhah (Gen 35:22).

verses.kjv_text: The nakedness of thy sister, the daughter of thy father, or daughter of thy mother, whether she be born at home, or born abroad, even their nakedness thou shalt not uncover.
verses.original_text: עֶרְוַת אֲחוֹתְךָ בַת אָבִיךָ אוֹ בַת אִמֶּךָ מוֹלֶדֶת בַּיִת אוֹ מוֹלֶדֶת חוּץ לֹא תְגַלֶּה עֶרְוָתָן׃
verses.transliteration: Ervat achotekha bat avikha o vat immekha moledet bayit o moledet chutz lo tegalleh ervatam.
verses.study_note: The sister prohibition is comprehensive: it covers full sisters (daughter of the same father and mother), half-sisters through the father's line (bat avikha), and half-sisters through the mother's line (bat immekha). "Born at home or born abroad" (moledet bayit o moledet chutz) — whether born within the household (legitimate wife's child) or outside it (concubine's child) — removes any social distinction that might otherwise qualify the prohibition. The comprehensiveness is deliberate: the kinship bond, not social legitimacy, defines the boundary. This prohibition stands in dramatic contrast to the patriarchal period, when Abraham married his half-sister Sarah (Gen 20:12). The Sinai revelation brought a higher, more specific definition of the incest boundary, superseding earlier practice.

verses.kjv_text: The nakedness of thy son’s daughter, or of thy daughter’s daughter, even their nakedness thou shalt not uncover: for theirs is thine own nakedness.
verses.original_text: עֶרְוַת בַּת בִּנְךָ אוֹ בַת בִּתְּךָ לֹא תְגַלֶּה עֶרְוָתָן כִּי עֶרְוָתְךָ הֵנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Ervat bat-binkha o vat-bittekha lo tegalleh ervatam ki ervatekha hennah.
verses.study_note: Granddaughters through both son and daughter are protected. The rationale is given: "for theirs is thine own nakedness" (ki ervatekha hennah) — the grandfather's nakedness extends to his granddaughters because they are his own flesh. The extension of the nakedness-identification downward through two generations reflects the Hebrew understanding of corporate bodily identity: a man's descendants carry his flesh forward through time. The granddaughter prohibition confirms that the incest boundary is not merely about immediate household proximity but about genealogical flesh-connection across generations.

verses.kjv_text: The nakedness of thy father’s wife’s daughter, begotten of thy father, she is thy sister, thou shalt not uncover her nakedness.
verses.original_text: עֶרְוַת בַּת אֵשֶׁת אָבִיךָ מוֹלֶדֶת אָבִיךָ אֲחוֹתְךָ הִוא לֹא תְגַלֶּה עֶרְוָתָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ervat bat eshet avikha moledet avikha achotekha hi lo tegalleh ervatah.
verses.study_note: The half-sister prohibition via the paternal line: the daughter of the father's wife, born of the father — a paternal half-sister. The verse adds the identity-statement "she is your sister" (achotekha hi) — like v.7's "she is your mother," the relational identity itself grounds the prohibition. This addresses a specific scenario: a man might argue that a half-sister born of a different mother is not really "his sister" and thus not covered by the v.9 prohibition. The verse closes that argument: if she is born of your father, she is your sister regardless of the maternal line.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy father’s sister: she is thy father’s near kinswoman.
verses.original_text: עֶרְוַת אֲחוֹת אָבִיךָ לֹא תְגַלֵּה שְׁאֵר אָבִיךָ הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ervat achot avikha lo tegalleh she'er avikha hi.
verses.study_note: The paternal aunt prohibition: the father's sister is she'er avikh (near kin of your father) — her flesh is bound up with the father's. The rationale is kinship-flesh identification: the aunt carries the same genealogical flesh-connection as the father himself. The prohibition reflects the principle that collateral kinship lines carry the same protective significance as direct ascendant-descendant lines. This prohibition was violated in the case of Amram, father of Moses, who married his aunt Jochebed (Exod 6:20) — another case where the Sinai legislation brought a heightened standard beyond what had been practiced in the patriarchal and early Mosaic period.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy mother’s sister: for she is thy mother’s near kinswoman.
verses.original_text: עֶרְוַת אֲחוֹת אִמְּךָ לֹא תְגַלֵּה כִּי שְׁאֵר אִמְּךָ הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ervat achot immekha lo tegalleh ki she'er immekha hi.
verses.study_note: The maternal aunt prohibition parallels the paternal aunt of v.12: the mother's sister is she'er immekh (near kin of your mother). The bilateral symmetry of vv.12-13 — covering both paternal and maternal aunts — ensures comprehensive protection of collateral kinship lines. The kinship structure is consistently bilateral in the Levitical incest laws: both the father's side and the mother's side are covered with equal specificity, reflecting an egalitarian understanding of both parents' kinship lines as sources of equally binding family obligation.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy father’s brother, thou shalt not approach to his wife: she is thine aunt.
verses.original_text: עֶרְוַת אֲחִי אָבִיךָ לֹא תְגַלֵּה אֶל אִשְׁתּוֹ לֹא תִקְרָב דֹּדָתְךָ הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ervat achi avikha lo tegalleh el-ishto lo tikrav dodatekha hi.
verses.study_note: The uncle's wife prohibition: do not approach (tikrav — same root as korban, offering, drawing near) the wife of your father's brother. The uncle's nakedness is protected through his wife — just as the father's nakedness is protected through the mother (v.7-8). The term dodah (aunt — specifically the uncle's wife, as distinct from the father's or mother's own sister in vv.12-13) designates the relational bond created through the uncle's marriage. The prohibition demonstrates that marriage creates genuine kinship bonds that are as binding on the nephew as if the uncle's wife were a blood relative. Affinal (marriage-created) kinship is treated as equivalent to consanguinal (blood) kinship throughout the Levitical incest code.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy daughter in law: she is thy son’s wife; thou shalt not uncover her nakedness.
verses.original_text: עֶרְוַת כַּלָּתְךָ לֹא תְגַלֵּה אֵשֶׁת בִּנְךָ הִוא לֹא תְגַלֶּה עֶרְוָתָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ervat kallatekha lo tegalleh eshet binkha hi lo tegalleh ervatah.
verses.study_note: The daughter-in-law prohibition is among the most emphatic in the chapter — stated twice: "do not uncover her nakedness... she is your son's wife... do not uncover her nakedness." The double statement mirrors the double statement of v.7 for the mother. The rationale "she is your son's wife" (eshet binkha hi) grounds the prohibition in the son's marital bond: the daughter-in-law is protected as the son's flesh (Gen 2:24 — "they shall be one flesh"). To violate her is to violate the son's most intimate bond. The most dramatic biblical violation of this prohibition is Judah's intercourse with his daughter-in-law Tamar (Gen 38:15-18), whom he did not recognize. The story's moral complexity — Judah's culpability despite his ignorance — is addressed by the Levitical law's unambiguous prohibition.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy brother’s wife: it is thy brother’s nakedness.
verses.original_text: עֶרְוַת אֵשֶׁת אָחִיךָ לֹא תְגַלֵּה עֶרְוַת אָחִיךָ הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ervat eshet achikha lo tegalleh ervat achikha hi.
verses.study_note: The sister-in-law prohibition: the brother's wife is the brother's nakedness — she is identified with the brother's intimate bond. This verse must be read alongside Deuteronomy 25:5-10 (levirate marriage), which commands a surviving brother to marry his deceased childless brother's wife to raise up an heir. This appears contradictory with Leviticus 18:16, but the Deuteronomic law applies only to the specific case of the deceased, childless brother — it is a redemptive exception, not a general license. Jesus references Deuteronomy 25 in the Sadducees' resurrection question (Matt 22:24-28), showing both laws were recognized and their relationship understood. The prohibition also applies to the case of a living brother — as in the case of Herod Antipas, whose marriage to Herodias (his living brother Philip's wife) John the Baptist condemned (Mark 6:17-18), citing this very prohibition.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of a woman and her daughter, neither shalt thou take her son’s daughter, or her daughter’s daughter, to uncover her nakedness; for they are her near kinswomen: it is wickedness.
verses.original_text: עֶרְוַת אִשָּׁה וּבִתָּהּ לֹא תְגַלֵּה אֶת בַּת בְּנָהּ וְאֶת בַּת בִּתָּהּ לֹא תִקַּח לְגַלּוֹת עֶרְוָתָהּ שְׁאֵרָה הֵנָּה זִמָּה הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ervat ishah uvittah lo tegalleh et-bat-benah ve'et bat-bittah lo tikkach legalot ervatah she'erah hennah zimmah hi.
verses.study_note: The prohibition of taking a woman and her daughter (or granddaughter) simultaneously — sequential or concurrent marriage or sexual relations across mother-daughter or grandmother-granddaughter lines. This prohibition has a different character from the preceding ones: it addresses the integrity of the mother-daughter relationship and the family unit. A man who has had sexual relations with a woman cannot then take her daughter, and vice versa. The word zimmah (wickedness, lewdness, depravity) appears for the first time in this chapter here — a particularly strong moral judgment. Zimmah (H2154 — from zamam, to plan, scheme, devise) describes deliberate, premeditated wickedness — sexual depravity that is willed and planned, not merely stumbled into. The prohibition covers not only concurrent relations but the family disruption created by sequential relations across generations within the same kinship line.
verses.doctrinal_note: The appearance of zimmah (wickedness, depravity) as a moral characterization in v.17 is the first explicit moral evaluation in the specific prohibitions section (vv.6-23). The term is used throughout the prophetic literature for sexual wickedness of the grossest kind: Ezekiel 16:27,43,58 uses zimmah repeatedly for Jerusalem's spiritual adultery; Ezekiel 23:21,27 for the sins of Samaria and Jerusalem; Hosea 6:9 for priestly violence. The occurrence of zimmah here connects this specific sexual sin to the prophetic category of gross wickedness — not merely ritual impurity but moral corruption of the deepest kind. In the New Testament, the comprehensive category of porneia (sexual immorality — 1 Cor 6:18; Gal 5:19; Eph 5:3) covers the range of sins described in Leviticus 18, with the same moral seriousness that zimmah conveys.

verses.kjv_text: Neither shalt thou take a wife to her sister, to vex her, to uncover her nakedness, beside the other in her life time.
verses.original_text: וְאִשָּׁה אֶל אֲחֹתָהּ לֹא תִקָּח לִצְרֹר לְגַלּוֹת עֶרְוָתָהּ עָלֶיהָ בְּחַיֶּיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ishah el-achotah lo tikkach litzror legalot ervatah aleha bechayyeha.
verses.study_note: This prohibition is distinctive: it forbids taking a wife's sister as a concurrent wife (beside the other in her lifetime). The word litzror (to vex, afflict, be a rival — from tzarar, to press, distress, rival) is particularly illuminating: it acknowledges the pain that such a situation would cause the first wife. The word tzarah (rival wife, from the same root) is used in 1 Samuel 1:6 for Peninnah's relationship to Hannah. This prohibition stands in tension with the patriarchal precedent: Jacob married both Leah and Rachel (Gen 29-30), who were sisters. The Sinai legislation prohibits this arrangement going forward, protecting the first wife from the rival-sister dynamic. The limiting clause "in her lifetime" (bechayyeha) implies that after the first wife's death, the prohibition may not apply to marrying the deceased wife's sister — a point that generated debate in later Jewish law.

verses.kjv_text: Also thou shalt not approach unto a woman to uncover her nakedness, as long as she is put apart for her uncleanness.
verses.original_text: וְאֶל אִשָּׁה בְּנִדַּת טֻמְאָתָהּ לֹא תִקְרַב לְגַלּוֹת עֶרְוָתָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'el ishah beniddat tum'atah lo tikrav legalot ervatah.
verses.study_note: The menstrual intercourse prohibition connects back to Leviticus 15:19-24 and 15:24. Sexual intercourse during the niddah (menstrual separation) is prohibited — and whereas Leviticus 15 described the ritual consequences (seven-day impurity), Leviticus 20:18 gives the covenant consequence (being cut off). The appearance of this prohibition alongside the incest and other sexual prohibitions in Leviticus 18 elevates it above a purely ritual matter into the category of an ethical violation. The prohibition reflects the theology of blood — the menstrual discharge belongs to the sphere of God's holy blood-management (Lev 17:11), and the combining of sexual approach with this sacred-boundary time violates both the woman's cycle and God's ordering of bodily life. Ezekiel lists this violation among the sins of Jerusalem and its priests (Ezek 18:6; 22:10).

verses.kjv_text: Moreover thou shalt not lie carnally with thy neighbour’s wife, to defile thyself with her.
verses.original_text: וְאֶל אֵשֶׁת עֲמִיתְךָ לֹא תִתֵּן שְׁכָבְתְּךָ לְזָרַע לְטָמְאָה בָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'el eshet amitekha lo titten shekhavtekha lezara letam'ah bah.
verses.study_note: The adultery prohibition is stated with unusual directness: "you shall not give your lying (shekhavtekha lezara — your seed-lying, your emission of seed) to your neighbour's wife to defile yourself by her." The word amitekha (your neighbour, your fellow-citizen, from am, people) identifies the violated party as a full member of the covenant community — not a stranger or enemy but a fellow Israelite. The defilement (letam'ah — ritual-moral defilement) applies to the man himself: adultery is self-defiling, not merely an offence against the wronged spouse. The prohibition assumes and protects the covenant of marriage: the husband's exclusive sexual claim on his wife is a covenant right grounded in their one-flesh union (Gen 2:24). Adultery is the covenant violation that destroys two households simultaneously — the adulterer's and the neighbour's.
verses.doctrinal_note: The adultery prohibition of Leviticus 18:20 is one of the Ten Commandments' prohibitions (Exod 20:14; Deut 5:18) stated in the sexual ethics context of the Holiness Code. Jesus intensifies the prohibition in the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:27-28): "whosoever looketh on a woman to lust after her hath committed adultery with her already in his heart." The Levitical command is behavioral; Christ's teaching reaches to the interior. The New Testament grounds marital fidelity in the covenant of creation (Matt 19:4-6; Eph 5:31-32) and elevates it as the image of Christ's relationship with the church (Eph 5:25-32) — the highest possible ground for sexual ethics. Hebrews 13:4 declares "the bed undefiled" for the married, but "whoremongers and adulterers God will judge."

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt not let any of thy seed pass through the fire to Molech, neither shalt thou profane the name of thy God: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: וּמִזַּרְעֲךָ לֹא תִתֵּן לְהַעֲבִיר לַמֹּלֶךְ וְלֹא תְחַלֵּל אֶת שֵׁם אֱלֹהֶיךָ אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Umizzar'akha lo titten leha'avir laMolekh velo techallel et shem Eloheikha ani YHWH.
verses.study_note: The Molech prohibition stands in an unexpected position — inserted among sexual prohibitions rather than in a separate idolatry section. This placement is theologically deliberate: child sacrifice to Molech is understood as the ultimate violation of the generative order that the sexual prohibitions protect. Children are the fruit of the sexual union; to offer them to Molech is to pervert the procreative purpose of sexuality entirely. Molech (or Moloch) was a Canaanite deity worshiped with the sacrifice of children — passing them "through the fire" (leha'avir ba'esh is the full phrase; here abbreviated). "You shall not profane the name of your God" (velo techallel et shem Eloheikha) — the Molech prohibition is a violation of the divine Name: child sacrifice dishonors the God who is the giver of life and the protector of children. The prohibition appears again in Leviticus 20:2-5 with the death penalty attached.
verses.doctrinal_note: The placement of the Molech prohibition within the sexual ethics context of Leviticus 18 establishes a profound theological connection: the protection of human sexuality and the protection of human life (especially children) belong to the same covenantal structure. The fruit of sexual union — children — must not be destroyed in the service of other gods. This connection between sexual ethics and the sanctity of life resonates across the canon: Psalm 127:3 declares children "an heritage of the LORD: and the fruit of the womb is his reward." In the New Covenant, both the protection of marriage (Heb 13:4) and the protection of children (Mark 10:13-16; Matt 18:1-6) are explicitly grounded in Christ's own authority and teaching. The modern parallel to Molech sacrifice — abortion offered to the gods of convenience, career, or sexual freedom — is a question the church has consistently engaged by returning to the theological framework of Leviticus 18 and 20.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not lie with mankind, as with womankind: it is abomination.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת זָכָר לֹא תִשְׁכַּב מִשְׁכְּבֵי אִשָּׁה תּוֹעֵבָה הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et zakhar lo tishkav mishkevei ishah to'evah hi.
verses.study_note: The prohibition of male homosexual intercourse is stated with stark brevity and the chapter's strongest moral characterization: to'evah (abomination). The grammatical structure is precise: "with a male you shall not lie lyings-of-a-woman" — mishkevei ishah (the lyings/beds of a woman) is the plural of mishkav (bed, lying), used in a specialized sense for sexual intercourse from the woman's perspective (cf. Num 31:17,35). The prohibition addresses the act, not merely orientation or intention. The word to'evah (abomination, H8441 — from ta'av, to abhor, loathe) is the strongest evaluative term in the Levitical legal vocabulary — stronger than zimmah (v.17) and used for the most fundamentally disordered acts. To'evah appears in Leviticus 18 only here (v.22) and in v.26-27,29,30 in the summary, where it covers all the chapter's prohibited acts. However, within the specific prohibitions, only male homosexual intercourse receives this designation individually.
verses.doctrinal_note: Leviticus 18:22 is one of the most discussed texts in contemporary ethics and theology. The prohibition is unambiguous in its linguistic scope: "with a male you shall not lie lyings-of-a-woman" — the act of male anal intercourse is prohibited. The word to'evah (abomination) is the strongest moral characterization in Levitical law. In the New Testament, Romans 1:26-27 provides the most extensive theological commentary on same-sex sexual relations, describing both female and male homosexual intercourse as "against nature" (para phusin — contrary to the natural design) and as a consequence of the suppression of the knowledge of God (vv.21-24). First Corinthians 6:9-10 lists arsenokoitai (men who lie with males — a compound of arsen, male, and koite, bed/lying, precisely reflecting the LXX of Lev 18:22 and 20:13) among those who will not inherit the kingdom of God. First Timothy 1:10 similarly includes arsenokoitai in a vice list. The consistent canonical testimony — Leviticus 18:22; 20:13; Romans 1:26-27; 1 Corinthians 6:9-10; 1 Timothy 1:10 — forms an unbroken chain of prohibition across both Testaments, grounded in the creational design of male-female complementarity (Gen 1:27-28; 2:22-24; Matt 19:4-6).

verses.kjv_text: Neither shalt thou lie with any beast to defile thyself therewith: neither shall any woman stand before a beast to lie down thereto: it is confusion.
verses.original_text: וּבְכָל בְּהֵמָה לֹא תִתֵּן שְׁכָבְתְּךָ לְטָמְאָה בָהּ וְאִשָּׁה לֹא תַעֲמֹד לִפְנֵי בְהֵמָה לְרִבְעָהּ תֶּבֶל הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Uvkhol behemah lo titten shekhavtekha letam'ah bah ve'ishah lo ta'amod lifnei behemah leribbah tevel hu.
verses.study_note: Bestiality is prohibited for both men and women. The prohibition is one of the most comprehensive in the chapter, explicitly addressing both male and female cases (unlike most prohibitions in vv.6-22 which address the male perspective). The characterization is tevel (H8397) — confusion, mixing, perversion, from balal (to mix, confuse, as in Babel). Tevel designates the crossing of categorical boundaries in the created order: the human-animal boundary is a creational distinction that sexual union between species violates. This is distinct from to'evah (which addresses an act contrary to human relational design) — tevel specifically designates category-confusion: the mixing of that which God has separated. The explicit prohibition of female bestiality alongside male bestiality reflects the chapter's consistent concern to address both sexes completely.
verses.doctrinal_note: The prohibition of bestiality under the category of tevel (confusion/mixing) reflects the creational theology of Genesis 1, where God's creative work was precisely a work of separation and distinction — light from darkness, waters from dry land, species according to their kinds. Bestiality violates the human-animal boundary established in creation and confirmed in Genesis 2:18-20, where no suitable helper for Adam was found among the animals — the negative declaration that preceded the creation of woman. The Song of Solomon 4-5 celebrates human-to-human love as the only appropriate context for sexual expression. Second Peter 2:12 uses the language of "brute beasts" in describing the moral degradation of false teachers, and Jude 7 — in a context addressing sexual sin — refers to "going after strange flesh." The Christological foundation of all sexual ethics is the marriage covenant as an image of Christ and the church (Eph 5:31-32) — an image that requires the full humanity of both parties.

verses.kjv_text: Defile not ye yourselves in any of these things: for in all these the nations are defiled which I cast out before you:
verses.original_text: אַל תִּטַּמְּאוּ בְּכָל אֵלֶּה כִּי בְכָל אֵלֶּה נִטְמְאוּ הַגּוֹיִם אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי מְשַׁלֵּחַ מִפְּנֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Al tittamme'u bekhol elleh ki vekhol elleh nitme'u haggoyim asher ani meshalleach mipeneikhem.
verses.study_note: The theological conclusion begins: "Do not defile yourselves in any of these things." The word tame (defile, become impure — the same root as the ritual purity system of Lev 11-15) is now applied to these sexual violations. The sexual sins of vv.6-23 are not merely ethical violations but a form of defilement — they corrupt the person, the community, and ultimately the land. The nations being cast out (haggoyim asher ani meshalleach — the nations whom I am sending away) before Israel are named as the precedent: they committed exactly these acts and were therefore expelled from the land. The Canaanite dispossession is presented not as ethnic cleansing but as moral consequence — they defiled the land by these acts, and the land expelled them (v.25). The same fate awaits Israel if they follow suit.
verses.doctrinal_note: Verse 24 introduces one of the most significant ethical principles in the Hebrew Bible: sexual sin has consequences that extend beyond the individual to the community, the covenant, and the land itself. The Canaanites lost their land not because of ethnicity but because of their moral practices — specifically the sexual and cultic violations listed in vv.6-23. Paul applies this territorial-moral principle in a New Covenant context in 1 Corinthians 5:6-7: "a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump. Purge out therefore the old leaven." Sexual immorality in the covenant community has a corporate corrupting effect — it is never merely private. This is why Paul commands excommunication for the Corinthian case (1 Cor 5:1-5) rather than mere personal rebuke: the community's holiness is implicated. First Corinthians 6:18-19 provides the foundation: the body is the temple of the Holy Spirit — sexual sin against the body is therefore also sin against God's holy dwelling.

verses.kjv_text: And the land is defiled: therefore I do visit the iniquity thereof upon it, and the land itself vomiteth out her inhabitants.
verses.original_text: וַתִּטְמָא הָאָרֶץ וָאֶפְקֹד עֲוֹנָהּ עָלֶיהָ וַתָּקִא הָאָרֶץ אֶת יֹשְׁבֶיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vatitma ha'aretz va'efkod avonah aleha vattaki ha'aretz et yoshveha.
verses.study_note: One of the most remarkable theological statements in the Torah: "the land vomiteth out her inhabitants" (vattaki ha'aretz et yoshveha). The verb ki (to vomit, disgorge) is visceral, anthropomorphic, and deliberate. The land is presented as a responsive entity that reacts to the moral state of its inhabitants — it cannot hold down (metaphorically, stomach) the weight of their sexual violations and disgorges them as a sick stomach vomits. "I do visit the iniquity thereof upon it" (va'efkod avonah aleha) — the divine visitation (paqad, to visit, attend to, call to account) of the land's iniquity precedes and produces the vomiting. The Canaanite dispossession is described as a divinely engineered consequence of accumulated moral defilement — the land responding to the visitation of God's moral order upon it. This ecological-moral theology is deeply consistent: the creation is not morally neutral; it exists in covenantal relationship with its Creator and responds to human moral behaviour.
verses.doctrinal_note: The theology of the land "vomiting out" its inhabitants is one of Scripture's most profound statements about the relationship between human moral behaviour and creation. This concept runs from the Flood (Gen 6-9 — the earth cleansed by water because of moral corruption) through the Canaanite dispossession (Lev 18:25) through the Babylonian exile (Lam 4:22; Jer 5:7-9) to the New Testament (Romans 8:20-22 — the whole creation groaneth, subjected to futility because of human sin). The land is not inert geography but a creation that responds to covenant-keeping or covenant-breaking by its inhabitants. Isaiah 24:5-6 generalises: "the earth also is defiled under the inhabitants thereof; because they have transgressed the laws, changed the ordinance, broken the everlasting covenant. Therefore hath the curse devoured the earth." The New Creation (Rev 21:1) is the ultimate resolution: "the first earth was passed away" and a creation emerges that no longer groans under the weight of human sin.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall therefore keep my statutes and my judgments, and shall not commit any of these abominations; neither any of your own nation, nor any stranger that sojourneth among you:
verses.original_text: וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אַתֶּם אֶת חֻקֹּתַי וְאֶת מִשְׁפָּטַי וְלֹא תַעֲשׂוּ מִכֹּל הַתּוֹעֵבֹת הָאֵלֶּה הָאֶזְרָח וְהַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּתוֹכְכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ushmartem attem et-chukotai ve'et mishpatai velo ta'asu mikkol hatto'evot ha'elleh ha'ezrach vehagger haggur betokheikhem.
verses.study_note: The statutes and judgments are now to be kept by both the native Israelite (ha'ezrach — the native-born, the citizen, the indigenous person) and the ger (the resident alien, the sojourner, the non-Israelite living among them). The inclusion of the sojourner is theologically significant: these prohibitions are not merely tribal customs but universal moral standards that apply to all people living within the covenant community's space. The alien who sojourns among Israel is subject to the same sexual prohibitions as the native Israelite — because these prohibitions flow from the created order that governs all humanity, not merely from the Sinai covenant that governs Israel specifically. This universalist dimension anticipates Paul's argument in Romans 1:18-32 that the sexual ethics of Leviticus 18 are knowable through natural revelation and are therefore violations for which all humanity is accountable.

verses.kjv_text: (For all these abominations have the men of the land done, which were before you, and the land is defiled;)
verses.original_text: כִּי אֶת כָּל הַתּוֹעֵבֹת הָאֵל עָשׂוּ אַנְשֵׁי הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר לִפְנֵיכֶם וַתִּטְמָא הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki et-kol hatto'evot ha'el asu anshei ha'aretz asher lifneikhem vatitma ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The historical basis for the warning is stated directly: the former inhabitants of the land — the Canaanites — committed all these abominations (to'evot). This is not hypothetical future risk but documented historical pattern: these practices were established in the land before Israel arrived. The land's defilement is presented as accomplished fact (vatitma — it became defiled) as a result. The historical context provides both urgency and sobriety: Israel is not being warned against purely theoretical temptations but against the specific practices of people who preceded them in the land and whom they will observe or encounter as remnants. Genesis 15:16 had already indicated that the Amorites' iniquity was not yet complete — the Sinai revelation to Israel comes at the point of the cup's fullness.

verses.kjv_text: That the land spue not you out also, when ye defile it, as it spued out the nations that were before you.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא תָקִיא הָאָרֶץ אֶתְכֶם בְּטַמַּאֲכֶם אֹתָהּ כַּאֲשֶׁר קָאָה אֶת הַגּוֹי אֲשֶׁר לִפְנֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Velo taki ha'aretz etkhem betamme'akhem otah ka'asher ka'ah et-haggoy asher lifneikhem.
verses.study_note: The conditional warning to Israel is stated with the same visceral vomiting metaphor (ki/taki — to vomit) used in v.25. The logic is explicit and symmetrical: just as the land vomited out the Canaanites for these acts, it will vomit out Israel if they do the same. The condition "when ye defile it" (betamme'akhem otah) makes clear that Israel is not immunized by covenant privilege — covenant membership amplifies moral responsibility rather than diminishing it. This is precisely the prophetic argument: Amos 3:2 states it with full clarity: "You only have I known of all the families of the earth: therefore I will punish you for all your iniquities." The land-vomiting was eventually fulfilled in the Babylonian exile (722 BC for the northern kingdom, 586 BC for Judah) — Israel was indeed expelled from the land for precisely the violations described in Leviticus 18 (Ezek 20:7-26).

verses.kjv_text: For whosoever shall commit any of these abominations, even the souls that commit them shall be cut off from among their people.
verses.original_text: כִּי כָּל אֲשֶׁר יַעֲשֶׂה מִכֹּל הַתּוֹעֵבוֹת הָאֵלֶּה וְנִכְרְתוּ הַנְּפָשׁוֹת הָעֹשֹׂת מִקֶּרֶב עַמָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ki kol asher ya'aseh mikkol hatto'evot ha'elleh venikhretu hannefahsot ha'osot mikkerev ammam.
verses.study_note: The covenant consequence for those who commit these acts is stated: "the souls that commit them shall be cut off from among their people" (venikhretu hannefahshot ha'osot mikkerev ammam). The karet (cutting off) penalty is the most frequent covenant sanction in Leviticus for serious violations — used for the most egregious breaches of covenant obligation. The meaning of karet is debated: it may include death at divine hands (premature death without descendants), excommunication from the covenant community, or eschatological exclusion. The phrase "from among their people" (mikkerev ammam) indicates expulsion from the covenant community at minimum, and potentially something more final. The use of nephahshot (souls, living persons) as the subject is notable — the whole person bears responsibility for these acts, not merely their body.
verses.doctrinal_note: The karet (cutting off) penalty in Leviticus 18:29 for sexual abominations connects directly to the New Testament's treatment of sexual sin as grounds for exclusion from the Kingdom of God. First Corinthians 6:9-10 lists sexual sinners (pornoi, adulterers, effeminate, arsenokoitai) among those who "shall not inherit the kingdom of God." Galatians 5:19-21 includes the "works of the flesh" — porneia, uncleanness, lasciviousness — among those who "shall not inherit the kingdom of God." The karet of Leviticus 18:29 and the kingdom-exclusion of Paul's letters are not equivalent in mechanism (the former is covenant community exclusion, the latter eschatological) but are theologically continuous: persistent, unrepented sexual violation of the boundaries established in Leviticus 18 places a person outside the covenant community of God's people. First John 1:9 provides the remedy: "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins, and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" — the karet-sentence is not the final word for the repentant.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore shall ye keep mine ordinance, that ye commit not any one of these abominable customs, which were committed before you, and that ye defile not yourselves therein: I am the LORD your God.
verses.original_text: וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת מִשְׁמַרְתִּי לְבִלְתִּי עֲשׂוֹת מֵחֻקּוֹת הַתּוֹעֵבֹת אֲשֶׁר נַעֲשׂוּ לִפְנֵיכֶם וְלֹא תִטַּמְּאוּ בָּהֶם אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ushmartem et-mishmarti levilti asot mechukkot hatto'evot asher na'asu lifneikhem velo tittamme'u bahem ani YHWH Eloheikhem.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with a double emphasis: "keep my ordinance" (mishmeret — my guard, my watch, my charge — from shamar, to keep, guard, watch) and the final iteration of the covenant formula "I am the LORD your God" (ani YHWH Eloheikhem). The word mishmeret is stronger than the individual commandments: it is the standing charge, the ongoing watchfulness, the posture of covenant attentiveness. "That ye commit not any one of these abominable customs" — the word chukkot here is used for the Canaanites' sexual customs (as in v.3), inverting the positive chukkot of God's ordinances: Israel is to walk in God's chukkot (v.4), not in the Canaanites' to'evah-chukkot. The chapter is perfectly bracketed: it opens with "I am the LORD your God" (v.2) and closes with "I am the LORD your God" (v.30). The covenant identity that grounds every prohibition also constitutes the final call to obedience.
verses.doctrinal_note: The closing verse's return to "I am the LORD your God" (ani YHWH Eloheikhem) completes the theological arc of the chapter. The sexual ethics of Leviticus 18 are not grounded in natural law arguments, social utility, psychological research, or cultural consensus — though these may support the biblical conclusions — but in the covenant identity of the God who redeemed His people. This is the New Testament's grounding as well: "your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you, which ye have of God, and ye are not your own; for ye are bought with a price: therefore glorify God in your body, and in your spirit, which are God's" (1 Cor 6:19-20). The identity formula "I am the LORD your God who brought you out of Egypt" finds its New Covenant parallel in "ye are bought with a price." Redemption — both the Exodus and the cross — is the ultimate ground of the covenant community's sexual holiness.

meta.overview: Leviticus 18 is one of the most comprehensive and foundational treatments of sexual ethics in the entire Bible. Positioned at the center of the Holiness Code (Lev 17-26), the chapter opens with the covenant formula "I am the LORD your God" (vv.2,4,5,6) — grounding every prohibition in the character and authority of the God who redeemed Israel from Egypt. The chapter is structured in three parts: a theological preamble (vv.1-5) establishing the framework of covenant distinctiveness from Egypt and Canaan; an extensive list of forbidden sexual unions (vv.6-23); and a theological conclusion (vv.24-30) warning that the land itself will "vomit out" those who practice these things — as it vomited out the Canaanites. The forbidden unions cover incest in its many forms (vv.6-18), menstrual intercourse (v.19), adultery (v.20), child sacrifice to Molech (v.21 — which belongs here as a violation of the generative order), male homosexual intercourse (v.22), and bestiality (v.23). The preamble's theological logic is remarkable: Israel is not to do as Egypt does (where they were) nor as Canaan does (where they are going). The ethical standard is not cultural consensus or natural impulse but the revealed will of the covenant God. Verse 5 — "which if a man do, he shall live in them" — is one of the most quoted texts in the New Testament, cited by Paul in Romans 10:5 and Galatians 3:12 to define the principle of law-righteousness in contrast to faith-righteousness. The chapter's conclusion introduces a theology of land-morality unique in the ancient world: the land of Canaan is not morally neutral territory but a space that responds to human moral and spiritual behaviour. Sexual sin is not merely personal; it defiles the community, the covenant, and the land itself. This ecological-moral theology anticipates the prophetic tradition (Jer 3:1-3; Hos 4:1-3) and ultimately the New Testament's vision of creation groaning under the weight of human sin (Rom 8:20-22). Leviticus 18 is not merely a list of prohibitions but a theological statement about the nature of human sexuality as created by God, its proper boundaries within the covenant community, and the consequences — personal, communal, and cosmic — of transgressing those boundaries.
meta.theme: Covenant Sexuality — Distinctiveness, Boundaries, and the Holiness of Human Relationships
meta.keyWordMeaning: Nakedness/shame/indecency — the key term of the chapter, appearing over twenty times. From arah (H6168), to be bare, to expose, to lay uncovered. Ervah denotes both the physical nakedness of the genitals and the shame/vulnerability associated with their unlawful exposure. "To uncover the nakedness" (galah ervah — H1540/H6172) is the chapter's repeated idiom for sexual intercourse, emphasizing both the physical act and the relational exposure and vulnerability it entails. The term carries the connotation that human sexuality involves the deepest personal exposure — and therefore demands the deepest covenantal protection.
outline: Theological Preamble: I Am the LORD — Not Like Egypt, Not Like Canaan (vv.1-5)
Incest Prohibited: Near Kin Defined (v.6)
Specific Incest Prohibitions: Mother, Stepmother, Sister (vv.7-9)
Granddaughter, Half-Sister, Aunt, Uncle's Wife, Sister-in-Law (vv.10-16)
Multiple Sister Marriages and Other Near-Kin (vv.17-18)
Additional Prohibitions: Menstrual Intercourse, Adultery, Molech, Homosexual Act, Bestiality (vv.19-23)
Theological Conclusion: The Land Vomits — Defilement, Cutting Off, and Covenant Obligation (vv.24-30)

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHWH el Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: The standard prophetic introduction formula — vaydabber YHWH el Mosheh lemor — signals that what follows is direct divine speech. In Leviticus this formula appears over thirty times, emphasizing that the entire Holiness Code is the direct word of God to Moses. The simplicity of this opening belies the weight of what follows: the most comprehensive ethical code in all of Torah. The divine speaker is YHWH — the covenant name revealed at the burning bush (Exodus 3:14) and sealed at Sinai — which links the ethical commands to the character of the covenant God, not merely to abstract moral principle.

verses.kjv_text: Speak unto all the congregation of the children of Israel, and say unto them, Ye shall be holy: for I the LORD your God am holy.
verses.original_text: דַּבֵּר אֶל כָּל עֲדַת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵהֶם קְדֹשִׁים תִּהְיוּ כִּי קָדוֹשׁ אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Dabber el kol adat benei Yisra'el ve'amarta alehem qedoshim tihyu ki qadosh ani YHWH Eloheikhem.
verses.study_note: The foundational command of the entire Holiness Code is addressed not merely to Aaron or the priests but to 'all the congregation of the children of Israel' (kol adat benei Yisra'el) — the entire covenant community without exception. The imperative qedoshim tihyu (you shall be holy — plural) is grounded in a causal clause: ki qadosh ani YHWH Eloheikhem (because holy am I, the LORD your God). The word order in Hebrew is emphatic — 'holy' comes first in both the command and its grounding. The ethical demand derives from and mirrors the divine character: Israel is to be holy because their God is holy. This is the principle of imitatio Dei — the imitation of God — which is the deepest foundation of biblical ethics.
verses.doctrinal_note: The command 'Be ye holy: for I the LORD your God am holy' is cited verbatim in 1 Peter 1:15-16, applying Israel's Holiness Code directly to the NT church: 'But as he which hath called you is holy, so be ye holy in all manner of conversation; because it is written, Be ye holy; for I am holy.' Peter's direct quotation establishes that the holiness vocation of the old covenant people is not abolished but extended to the new covenant community. The theological principle — imitatio Dei, conformity to God's character — remains the foundation of Christian ethics. Ephesians 5:1 expresses the same principle: 'Be ye therefore followers [imitators] of God, as dear children.' Holiness is not primarily rule-keeping but character-conformity: becoming like the holy God through covenant relationship.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall fear every man his mother, and his father, and keep my sabbaths: I am the LORD your God.
verses.original_text: אִישׁ אִמּוֹ וְאָבִיו תִּירָאוּ וְאֶת שַׁבְּתֹתַי תִּשְׁמֹרוּ אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ish immo ve'aviv tira'u ve'et shabbetotai tishmoru ani YHWH Eloheikhem.
verses.study_note: Two foundational commands appear in striking sequence: honoring parents and keeping the Sabbath. Notably, the mother is named first (immo ve'aviv — his mother and his father), reversing the more common father-first order (Exod 20:12). This inversion likely emphasizes the mother's equal authority in the household or reflects a context where the mother was more practically present. The verb is yare (to fear/revere) — not merely 'honor' (kabed of Exodus 20:12) but a stronger reverential respect. The Sabbath command follows immediately: the two institutions of family and sacred time are the bedrock of covenant community. The verse closes with the divine-name seal: Ani YHWH Eloheikhem.
verses.doctrinal_note: The pairing of parental reverence and Sabbath observance in a single verse reveals the biblical theology of authority: both parents and Sabbath are divinely appointed structures that require submission. The family represents God's authority mediated through human institutions; the Sabbath represents God's authority over time itself. Ephesians 6:1-3 cites the fifth commandment for its NT application, and Colossians 2:16-17 addresses the Sabbath as a shadow fulfilled in Christ. Together these two commands form the hinge between the first and second tables of the Decalogue: the parent-authority command closes the first table (duties to God and God-appointed authorities), and the neighbor-love commands of this chapter develop the second.

verses.kjv_text: Turn ye not unto idols, nor make to yourselves molten gods: I am the LORD your God.
verses.original_text: אַל תִּפְנוּ אֶל הָאֱלִילִים וֵאלֹהֵי מַסֵּכָה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ לָכֶם אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Al tifnu el ha'elilim ve'elohei massekah lo ta'asu lakhem ani YHWH Eloheikhem.
verses.study_note: Two forms of idolatry are prohibited: turning toward elilim (idols, nothings — a contemptuous diminutive) and making elohei massekah (gods of cast metal — poured, molten images). The word elilim (H457) is a wordplay on El (God) — the elilim are 'not-gods,' hollow imitations of divine reality. The Holiness Code's first ethical application of 'be holy' is: do not worship what is not God. Idolatry is the fundamental betrayal of the covenant because it replaces the living God with a created substitute. The divine-name refrain 'I am the LORD your God' at the close grounds the prohibition: you already have a God — you need no other.

verses.kjv_text: And if ye offer a sacrifice of peace offerings unto the LORD, ye shall offer it at your own will.
verses.original_text: וְכִי תִזְבְּחוּ זֶבַח שְׁלָמִים לַיהוָה לִרְצֹנְכֶם תִּזְבָּחֻהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vechi tizbechu zebach shelamim laYHWH lirtzonkhem tizzbachu hu.
verses.study_note: The peace offering (shelamim) section of Leviticus 19 (vv.5-8) inserts cultic regulations within the ethical code, illustrating that worship and ethics are not separate categories for Israel. The shelamim (from shalom — peace, wholeness, completion) is a communal meal shared between the offerer, the priests, and God himself. The phrase lirtzonkhem (at your own will / according to your pleasure) indicates the shelamim is a voluntary, willing offering — not obligatory like the sin offering. The willingness of the worshiper is the condition for its acceptance: forced or reluctant worship is no worship at all.

verses.kjv_text: It shall be eaten the same day ye offer it, and on the morrow: and if ought remain until the third day, it shall be burnt in the fire.
verses.original_text: בְּיוֹם זִבְחֲכֶם יֵאָכֵל וּמִמָּחֳרָת וְהַנּוֹתָר עַד יוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי בָּאֵשׁ יִשָּׂרֵף׃
verses.transliteration: Beyom zivchakhem ye'akhel umimmacharat vehannatar ad yom hashelishi ba'esh yissaref.
verses.study_note: The peace offering must be eaten on the day of offering or the following day — any meat remaining on the third day must be burned. This two-day limit reflects both practical (food preservation in a hot climate) and theological concerns. The sacred meal has a time boundary that forces complete consumption within the family's circle of sharing. Leftover sacred meat that lingers beyond its appointed time becomes piggul (v.7 — offensive, abominable), losing its status as holy food. The time constraint communicates that the peace with God celebrated in the shelamim is a present, engaged reality requiring full participation — not something to be consumed partially and set aside.

verses.kjv_text: And if it be eaten at all on the third day, it is abominable; it shall not be accepted.
verses.original_text: וְאִם הֵאָכֹל יֵאָכֵל בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי פִּגּוּל הוּא לֹא יֵרָצֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im he'akhol ye'akhel bayyom hashelishi piggul hu lo yeratzeh.
verses.study_note: The absolute emphatic construction he'akhol ye'akhel (infinitive absolute + finite verb — literally 'if eating it shall be eaten') underscores the seriousness of consuming the peace offering on the third day. Such meat becomes piggul — a unique technical term appearing only in Leviticus (7:18; 19:7; Isaiah 65:4) for sacrificial meat that has become ritually abominable. The word piggul derives from a root meaning to stink, to putrefy. The declaration lo yeratzeh (it shall not be accepted/favored) uses the same root as ratzon (will, favor) from v.5 — the willing offering has become an unacceptable abomination through delay and mishandling. What was holy becomes corrupt by being held past its time.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore every one that eateth it shall bear his iniquity, because he hath profaned the hallowed thing of the LORD: and that soul shall be cut off from among his people.
verses.original_text: וְאֹכְלָיו עֲוֹנוֹ יִשָּׂא כִּי אֶת קֹדֶשׁ יְהוָה חִלֵּל וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מֵעַמֶּיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'okhlov avono yissa ki et qodesh YHWH hillel venikhretah hannefesh hahi me'ammeihah.
verses.study_note: Eating the piggul-meat carries a severe consequence: the person 'bears his iniquity' (avono yissa — nasa, to carry the guilt and its consequence) because he has profaned (hillel — Piel of chalal, to pierce, to desecrate) the holy thing of the LORD. The Piel hillel is intensive: to thoroughly desecrate, to treat as common what God has set apart. The punishment is karet — 'cutting off' from the covenant people — the most severe divine punishment short of death, typically understood as divine excision from covenant blessing and life. The eating of profaned sacred food treats the sacred as common, a fundamental inversion of the holy/profane distinction that structures all of Leviticus.

verses.kjv_text: And when ye reap the harvest of your land, thou shalt not wholly reap the corners of thy field, neither shalt thou gather the gleanings of thy harvest.
verses.original_text: וּבְקֻצְרְכֶם אֶת קְצִיר אַרְצְכֶם לֹא תְכַלֶּה פְּאַת שָׂדְךָ לִקְצֹר וְלֶקֶט קְצִירְךָ לֹא תְלַקֵּט׃
verses.transliteration: Uvequtzrekhem et qetzir artzekhem lo tekhalleh pe'at sadekha liqtzor veleket qetzirkha lo telaqqet.
verses.study_note: The gleaning laws (vv.9-10) are among the most socially transformative commands in all of Torah. Two prohibitions govern the harvest: (1) do not completely reap the pe'at (corners/edges) of the field; (2) do not gather the leket (gleanings — what falls during reaping). The word pe'ah (corner, edge) is plural in its technical usage and refers to the unmowed margins of a field deliberately left standing. The leket are the stalks and grains that fall from the reapers' hands or sickles. Both are legally the property of the poor. The landowner has no right to them. Holiness in agriculture means leaving space in one's productivity for others' survival.
verses.doctrinal_note: The gleaning laws of Leviticus 19:9-10 create what modern ethicists would call a structural provision for poverty: not mere charity but a legal entitlement of the poor built into the agricultural system. Ruth's gleaning in the fields of Boaz (Ruth 2) is the most famous narrative illustration of this law in action. The NT's application of gleaning-logic appears in Paul's principle: 'he that had gathered much had nothing over; and he that had gathered little had no lack' (2 Corinthians 8:15, quoting Exodus 16:18). The Jubilee principle and the gleaning law together form a biblical economic ethic of sufficiency rather than accumulation: enough for all, not excess for few. Jesus's disciples plucking grain on the Sabbath (Matthew 12:1) were exercising the gleaning right — the rabbis recognized this as legal for travelers passing through fields.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt not glean thy vineyard, neither shalt thou gather every grape of thy vineyard; thou shalt leave them for the poor and stranger: I am the LORD your God.
verses.original_text: וְכַרְמְךָ לֹא תְעוֹלֵל וּפֶרֶט כַּרְמְךָ לֹא תְלַקֵּט לֶעָנִי וְלַגֵּר תַּעֲזֹב אֹתָם אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vekharmekha lo te'olel uferet kharmekha lo telaqqet le'ani velaggeir ta'azov otam ani YHWH Eloheikhem.
verses.study_note: The gleaning law extends from grain fields to vineyards. Two specific vineyard prohibitions mirror the field law: do not glean (lo te'olel — do not strip the smaller/secondary clusters) and do not gather the individual fallen grapes (peret — a scattered grape, from parar, to break). The beneficiaries are identified explicitly: the ani (poor, one bowed down, economically vulnerable) and the ger (the resident alien, the foreigner living among Israel). The ger — the stranger — has equal gleaning rights with the native poor. The law then closes with the divine-name formula: Ani YHWH Eloheikhem — 'I am the LORD your God.' The creator and covenant God is the silent third party in every harvest: the poor's legal advocate who has already assigned them their share.
verses.doctrinal_note: The inclusion of the ger (resident alien) as a legal recipient of gleaning rights alongside the native poor is a remarkable extension of covenant provision to non-Israelites. The ger appears repeatedly in Leviticus 19 (vv.10,33,34) as a protected class within Israel's ethical community. This extension anticipates the NT's universalization of covenant blessing to the Gentiles. Paul's quotation of 'I will call them my people, which were not my people' (Romans 9:25, from Hosea 2:23) and the incorporation of Gentiles as 'fellow citizens' and 'fellow heirs' (Ephesians 2:19; 3:6) are the eschatological fulfillment of what the gleaning law enacted in economic microcosm: the stranger is included in the covenant community's provision by divine law.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall not steal, neither deal falsely, neither lie one to another.
verses.original_text: לֹא תִגְנֹבוּ וְלֹא תְכַחֲשׁוּ וְלֹא תְשַׁקְּרוּ אִישׁ בַּעֲמִיתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Lo tignovu velo tekhachashu velo teshaqeru ish be'amito.
verses.study_note: Three prohibitions in rapid succession form a triad of covenant-speech integrity: lo tignovu (you shall not steal — the eighth commandment echo), lo tekhachashu (you shall not deal falsely / deceive — Piel of kachash, to deny, to lie by concealment), and lo teshaqeru (you shall not lie — Piel of shaqar, to deal falsely, to deceive actively). The movement is instructive: from external action (stealing property) to speech act (active deception) to internal disposition made external (lying). The phrase ish be'amito (a man with his neighbor) makes the relational context explicit: these prohibitions govern the horizontal covenant relationships between Israelites. Covenant community is constituted by truth-telling; its destruction begins with deception.
verses.doctrinal_note: The triad of theft-deception-lying has its deepest NT treatment in Ephesians 4:25-28: 'Wherefore putting away lying, speak every man truth with his neighbour: for we are members one of another... let him that stole steal no more.' Paul's grounding of honest speech in the fact that 'we are members one of another' (mele alleloon esmen) is precisely the logic of Leviticus 19: the covenant community is an organic unity in which deception tears the social fabric. Jesus declared the devil 'a liar, and the father of it' (John 8:44), making lying an alignment with demonic character — the opposite of the holy God whose word is truth.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall not swear by my name falsely, neither shalt thou profane the name of thy God: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא תִשָּׁבְעוּ בִשְׁמִי לַשָּׁקֶר וְחִלַּלְתָּ אֶת שֵׁם אֱלֹהֶיךָ אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Velo tishave'u vishmi lashshaqer vechillalta et shem Elohekha ani YHWH.
verses.study_note: False swearing by the divine name profanes (chillalta — Piel of chalal, the same verb of desecration used in v.8) the name of God. This is the third commandment of the Decalogue (Exodus 20:7: 'Thou shalt not take the name of the LORD thy God in vain') applied in relational context. The shevu'at shav (vain/false oath) uses God's name to guarantee a lie — it treats the holy name as a mere speech tool rather than as the identity of the living God. The result is chillul Hashem (desecration of the Name) — the antithesis of kiddush Hashem (sanctification of the Name). Every false oath by God's name reduces the divine name to the level of the lie sworn by it.
verses.doctrinal_note: The NT addresses oath-taking directly in Matthew 5:33-37, where Jesus says: 'Ye have heard that it hath been said by them of old time, Thou shalt not forswear thyself... But I say unto you, Swear not at all... but let your communication be, Yea, yea; Nay, nay.' Jesus radicalizes the prohibition: the goal is such habitual truthfulness that oaths become unnecessary. When every word is reliable, the oath-system becomes redundant. James 5:12 repeats this principle directly. The deeper issue is integrity of character: the person whose yes always means yes and whose no always means no has internalized the holiness of divine speech itself — for God's word is absolutely reliable (Numbers 23:19).

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not defraud thy neighbour, neither rob him: the wages of him that is hired shall not abide with thee all night until the morning.
verses.original_text: לֹא תַעֲשֹׁק אֶת רֵעֲךָ וְלֹא תִגְזֹל לֹא תָלִין פְּעֻלַּת שָׂכִיר אִתְּךָ עַד בֹּקֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Lo ta'ashoq et re'akha velo tigzol lo talin pe'ullat sakhir ittekha ad boqer.
verses.study_note: Two economic crimes are listed: ashaq (defraud, oppress — withholding what is owed) and gazal (rob, seize by violence). Then a specific application: the hired worker's wages (pe'ullat sakhir) must not be held overnight. In the ancient world, day laborers depended on their daily pay (peulah — wages, work done) for that evening's food. Withholding it was not a mere accounting delay but a life-threatening act of oppression. The command lo talin (shall not stay overnight) personifies the wages as a vulnerable guest in the employer's household — even the pay must not be kept overnight against its owner's need. Deuteronomy 24:14-15 expands this: 'lest he cry against thee unto the LORD, and it be sin unto thee.'
verses.doctrinal_note: The prohibition on withholding wages becomes in the NT an explicit eschatological warning. James 5:4 declares: 'Behold, the hire of the labourers who have reaped down your fields, which is of you kept back by fraud, crieth: and the cries of them which have reaped are entered into the ears of the Lord of sabaoth.' The wages of oppressed workers 'cry out' — using the same theological vocabulary as Abel's blood crying from the ground (Genesis 4:10). Economic injustice is not a secular matter but a covenant crime that reaches the divine court. Paul echoes the principle in Colossians 4:1: 'Masters, give unto your servants that which is just and equal; knowing that ye also have a Master in heaven.'

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not curse the deaf, nor put a stumblingblock before the blind, but shalt fear thy God: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: לֹא תְקַלֵּל חֵרֵשׁ וְלִפְנֵי עִוֵּר לֹא תִתֵּן מִכְשֹׁל וְיָרֵאתָ מֵאֱלֹהֶיךָ אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Lo teqallel cheresh velifnei iver lo titten mikhshol veyareta me'Elohekha ani YHWH.
verses.study_note: The holiness of God requires protection of the most vulnerable, specifically those who cannot defend themselves against exploitation. Two cases: cursing the deaf person (who cannot hear the curse and therefore cannot respond or defend himself) and placing a stumbling block (mikhshol) before the blind (who cannot see the obstacle). Both cases share a common structure: the victim cannot know about or protect against the harm. This is precisely why God's knowledge must be invoked as the grounding: 'you shall fear your God' (veyareta me'Elohekha). When the victim cannot see or hear, God sees and hears everything. The unseen God is the advocate of those whose vulnerability makes them invisible to the powerful. The mikhshol (stumbling block) became a rich metaphorical term in later Scripture for any spiritual or moral obstacle to another person.
verses.doctrinal_note: The command not to place a stumbling block before the blind (mikhshol lifnei iver) gave rise to a major NT ethical principle. Paul uses the stumbling block metaphor extensively in Romans 14:13-21 (proskomma, skandalon) for actions that lead weaker believers into sin: 'that no man put a stumblingblock or an occasion to fall in his brother's way.' Jesus uses skandalon in Matthew 18:6-7 with devastating severity: 'But whoso shall offend one of these little ones which believe in me, it were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck.' The Levitical protection of the physically blind becomes in the NT a protection of the spiritually vulnerable — and the severity of the warning intensifies, because spiritual blindness is a graver condition than physical.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall do no unrighteousness in judgment: thou shalt not respect the person of the poor, nor honor the person of the mighty: but in righteousness shalt thou judge thy neighbour.
verses.original_text: לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ עָוֶל בַּמִּשְׁפָּט לֹא תִשָּׂא פְנֵי דָל וְלֹא תֶהְדַּר פְּנֵי גָדוֹל בְּצֶדֶק תִּשְׁפֹּט עֲמִיתֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Lo ta'asu avel bammishpat lo tissa penei dal velo tehdar penei gadol betzedek tishpot amitekha.
verses.study_note: The judicial ethics of the Holiness Code: no avel (perversity, injustice) in the mishpat (judgment, legal proceedings). Two specific prohibitions bracket the command: do not lift the face (nasa panim — to show partiality, literally to raise someone's face before the court) of the dal (the poor, the weak) and do not honor (hadar — to show deference) the face (panim) of the gadol (the great, the powerful). Justice is blind in both directions — neither poverty nor power creates a presumption of rightness or wrongness in the court. The positive command betzedek tishpot (in righteousness you shall judge) sets the standard: tzedek (righteousness, justice, right-doing) is the goal.
verses.doctrinal_note: The prohibition on partiality in judgment (nasa panim — respect of persons) becomes a foundational principle in NT ethics. James 2:1-9 applies it directly: 'My brethren, have not the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory, with respect of persons' — giving better treatment to the wealthy while despising the poor. He calls this a violation of 'the royal law' (v.8 — from Leviticus 19:18). Acts 10:34 declares: 'God is no respecter of persons' (ouk estin prosopoleptes ho Theos) — using the exact LXX vocabulary of nasa panim. The divine impartiality that grounds Leviticus 19:15 is revealed to be the character of Christ himself, extended to all nations without distinction.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not go up and down as a talebearer among thy people: neither shalt thou stand against the blood of thy neighbour: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: לֹא תֵלֵךְ רָכִיל בְּעַמֶּיךָ לֹא תַעֲמֹד עַל דַּם רֵעֶךָ אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Lo telekh rakhil be'amekha lo ta'amod al dam re'ekha ani YHWH.
verses.study_note: Two speech-ethics commands: no rakil (talebearing — the rakil is a peddler or merchant, here used metaphorically for one who trades in gossip and damaging information) and no standing against the blood (dam) of one's neighbor. The rakhil metaphor is vivid: the gossip moves through the community like a merchant peddling dangerous wares. The second prohibition, lo ta'amod al dam re'ekha (do not stand against the blood of your neighbor), is interpreted variously as prohibiting false testimony that leads to capital punishment, standing idly by while someone is in danger of death, or plotting against someone's life. The am (blood) is the life itself: the neighbor's very survival is what the gossip or false testimony threatens.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not hate thy brother in thine heart: thou shalt in any wise rebuke thy neighbour, and not suffer sin upon him.
verses.original_text: לֹא תִשְׂנָא אֶת אָחִיךָ בִּלְבָבֶךָ הוֹכֵחַ תּוֹכִיחַ אֶת עֲמִיתֶךָ וְלֹא תִשָּׂא עָלָיו חֵטְא׃
verses.transliteration: Lo tisna et achikha bilvavekha hokheach tokhi'ach et amitekha velo tissa alav chet.
verses.study_note: The Holiness Code reaches the internal life: do not hate your brother in your heart (bilvavekha — literally 'in your heart'), establishing that covenant ethics governs not merely external action but inward disposition. Hatred concealed in the heart while maintaining outward cordiality is itself a violation of holiness. The positive command follows: hokheach tokhi'ach (infinitive absolute + finite verb — you shall certainly rebuke) your neighbor. Direct, honest reproof (tokhechah) is commanded as the alternative to silent hatred. The purpose clause velo tissa alav chet (and you shall not carry sin on his account) is remarkable: failing to reprove a sinning neighbor makes the silent observer complicit in the sin.
verses.doctrinal_note: The command to rebuke a neighbor honestly rather than harboring hidden hatred has profound NT resonance. Matthew 18:15-17 preserves Jesus's teaching on fraternal correction — the step-by-step process of reproof between brothers that begins privately and escalates only when necessary. The NT principle is continuous with Leviticus 19:17: the alternative to rebuke is not gracious silence but complicity. Galatians 6:1-2 adds the restorative dimension: 'Brethren, if a man be overtaken in a fault, ye which are spiritual, restore such an one in the spirit of meekness.' The Greek katartizo (to restore) is a surgical/nautical term for setting a broken bone or a dislocated joint — reproof done in the spirit of restoration. Concealed hatred corrodes the community; honest love speaks the hard word that restores.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not avenge, nor bear any grudge against the children of thy people, but thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: לֹא תִקֹּם וְלֹא תִטֹּר אֶת בְּנֵי עַמֶּךָ וְאָהַבְתָּ לְרֵעֲךָ כָּמוֹךָ אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Lo tiqqom velo tittor et benei ammekha ve'ahavta lere'akha kamokha ani YHWH.
verses.study_note: The ethical summit of Leviticus 19 and one of the most famous verses in all of Scripture. Two negative commands precede the positive: lo tiqqom (you shall not take vengeance — naqqam, to avenge, to demand satisfaction by retaliation) and lo tittor (you shall not bear a grudge — natar, to keep, to hold onto, to watch over in resentment). The grudge is resentment stored like a guarded treasure. Then the positive command: ve'ahavta lere'akha kamokha (and you shall love your neighbor as yourself) — the agape-demand. Ahav (to love, to have deep covenant affection and loyal commitment) directed to the re'a (neighbor, associate) kamokha (as yourself — using yourself as the measure and standard of love). Ani YHWH — God himself seals the command as its author and ground.
verses.doctrinal_note: Leviticus 19:18 is the most quoted verse of the Hebrew Bible in the New Testament. Jesus identifies it as the second great commandment alongside Deuteronomy 6:4-5: 'Thou shalt love the Lord thy God' (Matthew 22:36-40). He declares that 'on these two commandments hang all the law and the prophets.' Paul calls love 'the fulfilling of the law' (Romans 13:8-10), summarizing all the commandments in this one: 'Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself.' Galatians 5:14 repeats: 'all the law is fulfilled in one word, even in this; Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself.' James 2:8 designates it 'the royal law' (nomon basilikon) — the king's law, supreme over all other laws. The command is not an NT invention but the Torah's own disclosure of its ethical core. Christ fulfills it not by abolishing it but by embodying it completely and empowering it through the Spirit (Romans 5:5).

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall keep my statutes. Thou shalt not let thy cattle gender with a diverse kind: thou shalt not sow thy field with mingled seed: neither shall a garment mingled of linen and woollen come upon thee.
verses.original_text: אֶת חֻקֹּתַי תִּשְׁמֹרוּ בְּהֶמְתְּךָ לֹא תַרְבִּיעַ כִּלְאַיִם שָׂדְךָ לֹא תִזְרַע כִּלְאַיִם וּבֶגֶד כִּלְאַיִם שַׁעַטְנֵז לֹא יַעֲלֶה עָלֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Et chuqqotai tishmoru behemtekha lo tarbia kilayim sadekha lo tizra kilayim ubeged kilayim sha'atnez lo ya'aleh alekha.
verses.study_note: Three kilayim (mixed-kind) prohibitions: no cross-breeding of livestock (lo tarbia kilayim — do not cause diverse-kind to lie together), no sowing a field with mixed seed, and no wearing sha'atnez — a garment of mixed linen and wool. The word kilayim (dual of kil, restraint, boundary) means 'two-kinds' — the mixing of categories God has created as distinct. The sha'atnez command (Deuteronomy 22:11 also uses the unique word sha'atnez, possibly of Egyptian or Semitic origin meaning 'woven' or 'combed') is the most distinctive: priestly garments actually were made of combined linen and wool (Exodus 39:29), suggesting that what was reserved for the sanctuary was prohibited for ordinary wear — the mixing belongs to the holy domain alone.

verses.kjv_text: And whosoever lieth carnally with a woman, that is a bondmaid, betrothed to an husband, and not at all redeemed, nor freedom given her; she shall be scourged; they shall not be put to death, because she was not free.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ כִּי יִשְׁכַּב אֶת אִשָּׁה שִׁכְבַת זֶרַע וְהִוא שִׁפְחָה נֶחֱרֶפֶת לְאִישׁ וְהָפְדֵּה לֹא נִפְדָּתָה אוֹ חָפְשָׁה לֹא נִתַּן לָהּ בִּקֹּרֶת תִּהְיֶה לֹא יוּמְתוּ כִּי לֹא חֻפָּשָׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ish ki yishkav et ishah shikhvat zera vehi shiphchah nechirfet le'ish vehafddeh lo nifdata o chafshhah lo nattan lah biqqoret tihyeh lo yumatu ki lo chuffashshah.
verses.study_note: This difficult law addresses a case where a man lies with a female slave (shiphchah) who is betrothed (necherrefet — designated, assigned) to another man but has not yet been redeemed from slavery or given her freedom. The standard penalty for adultery (death) is mitigated because the woman is not free — her legal status as a slave means the full adultery law does not apply in the same way. The biqqoret (investigation/punishment — a unique word, possibly meaning scourging or inquiry) applies. The case illustrates the Torah's careful gradation of legal responsibility: full moral agency and full legal penalty presuppose freedom. The law protects the enslaved woman from the full weight of a law structured for free persons.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall bring his trespass offering unto the LORD, unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, even a ram for a trespass offering.
verses.original_text: וְהֵבִיא אֶת אֲשָׁמוֹ לַיהוָה אֶל פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד אֵיל אָשָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehevi et ashamo laYHWH el petach ohel mo'ed ayil asham.
verses.study_note: The man who sinned in this case must bring an asham (guilt/trespass offering) — a ram (ayil) offered at the door of the tent of meeting. The asham is the offering prescribed for violations involving property, trust, or defilement of what belongs to another (Leviticus 5:14–6:7). The enslaved woman belongs, in the legal sense, to another man; lying with her constitutes a violation of his rights. The asham's prescription here demonstrates that God's holiness requires not merely human punishment but divine atonement: the sin against the human order is simultaneously a sin against God requiring sacrificial resolution. The man's act, even with its mitigated penalty, still requires atonement.

verses.kjv_text: And the priest shall make an atonement for him with the ram of the trespass offering before the LORD for his sin which he hath done: and the sin which he hath done shall be forgiven him.
verses.original_text: וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן בְּאֵיל הָאָשָׁם לִפְנֵי יְהוָה עַל חַטָּאתוֹ אֲשֶׁר חָטָא וְנִסְלַח לוֹ מֵחַטָּאתוֹ אֲשֶׁר חָטָא׃
verses.transliteration: Vekipper alav hakkohen be'eyil ha'asham lifnei YHWH al chattato asher chata venislach lo mechattato asher chata.
verses.study_note: The verse's conclusion is the theological payoff of the entire ritual sequence: venislach lo (and it shall be forgiven him) — Niphal of salach (to forgive, to pardon — a verb used exclusively of divine forgiveness in the Hebrew Bible). The priest makes atonement (kipper) with the asham ram before the LORD, and God forgives. The forensic declaration of divine pardon (salach) is the goal toward which all the Levitical sacrificial machinery moves. No human authority can pronounce this forgiveness — only God can salach. The kipper-salach sequence (atonement → forgiveness) is the Old Testament's compressed statement of what the NT unpacks as justification: substitutionary sacrifice before God resulting in the divine verdict of pardon.

verses.kjv_text: And when ye shall come into the land, and shall have planted all manner of trees for food, then ye shall count the fruit thereof as uncircumcised: three years shall it be as uncircumcised unto you: it shall not be eaten of.
verses.original_text: וְכִי תָבֹאוּ אֶל הָאָרֶץ וּנְטַעְתֶּם כָּל עֵץ מַאֲכָל וַעֲרַלְתֶּם עָרְלָתוֹ אֶת פִּרְיוֹ שָׁלֹשׁ שָׁנִים יִהְיֶה לָכֶם עֲרֵלִים לֹא יֵאָכֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vechi tavo'u el ha'aretz unetatem kol etz ma'akhal va'araltem orlato et piryo shalosh shanim yihyeh lakhem arelim lo ye'akhel.
verses.study_note: The firstfruit tree law introduces the concept of orla — the 'foreskin' of the fruit tree. For the first three years after planting, the fruit is considered arelim (uncircumcised) and must not be eaten. The metaphor is striking: orla (foreskin) is the mark of the uncircumcised, the one not yet incorporated into the covenant. Applying this to fruit trees: the young tree's early fruit is 'uncircumcised' — not yet consecrated, not yet fit for use. This prohibition has both practical rationale (early fruit is of poor quality and heavy cropping of young trees damages long-term productivity) and theological meaning (the land's produce requires a period of consecration before it can be used).
verses.doctrinal_note: The application of circumcision language (orla) to fruit trees is one of the Old Testament's most striking metaphorical extensions of the covenant sign. Orla (foreskin) becomes in Hebrew a broader metaphor for whatever is unconsecrated, unripe, not yet incorporated into the covenant order. Deuteronomy 10:16 commands: 'circumcise therefore the foreskin of your heart' — the metaphor extends from the body to the inner life. Jeremiah 4:4 repeats this. Paul's teaching on circumcision of the heart (Romans 2:28-29; Colossians 2:11) stands in continuity with this metaphorical trajectory: the physical sign points to the spiritual reality of consecration, and Christ's work accomplishes the true 'circumcision' of the heart (Colossians 2:11 — 'the circumcision made without hands').

verses.kjv_text: But in the fourth year all the fruit thereof shall be holy to praise the LORD withal.
verses.original_text: וּבַשָּׁנָה הָרְבִיעִת יִהְיֶה כָּל פִּרְיוֹ קֹדֶשׁ הִלּוּלִים לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Uvashannah harevi'it yihyeh kol piryo qodesh hillulim laYHWH.
verses.study_note: In the fourth year, the entire crop (kol piryo — all its fruit) becomes qodesh hillulim laYHWH — holy for praise/jubilation to the LORD. The word hillulim (from hillel — to praise, to glorify, to celebrate) appears only here and in Judges 9:27 in the Hebrew Bible. It is the plural of jubilant praise: the fourth year's fruit is entirely devoted to God as a great celebration.
