The fruit of the first full harvest cycle belongs wholly to God in an act of joyful dedication. Only in the fifth year (v.25) may the owner eat freely. The three-year restraint, followed by a year of total consecration, followed by free use — this is the pattern of consecration before consumption that structures Israel's relationship to the land's gifts.

verses.kjv_text: And in the fifth year shall ye eat of the fruit thereof, that it may yield unto you the increase thereof: I am the LORD your God.
verses.original_text: וּבַשָּׁנָה הַחֲמִישִׁת תֹּאכְלוּ אֶת פִּרְיוֹ לְהוֹסִיף לָכֶם תְּבוּאָתוֹ אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Uvashannah hachamishit tokhlu et piryo lehosif lakhem teuvatoh ani YHWH Eloheikhem.
verses.study_note: Only in the fifth year may the owner freely eat (tokhlu — you shall eat) the fruit. The purpose clause lehosif lakhem tevuatoh (to increase/add its yield to you) carries an implicit promise: the patient consecration of the first three years and the total dedication of the fourth year result in God blessing the increase of the fifth. The verse closes with the divine-name formula Ani YHWH Eloheikhem — the covenant God who makes promises is the one whose blessing transforms patient obedience into abundant harvest. The principle is the same as the Sabbath year: restraint and consecration, when practiced in faith, unleash divine blessing rather than withholding human benefit.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall not eat any thing with the blood: neither shall ye use enchantment, nor observe times.
verses.original_text: לֹא תֹאכְלוּ עַל הַדָּם לֹא תְנַחֲשׁוּ וְלֹא תְעוֹנֵנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Lo tokhlu al haddam lo tenachashu velo te'onenu.
verses.study_note: Three prohibitions on the boundary between food, magic, and divination: (1) lo tokhlu al haddam — you shall not eat 'over' or 'with' the blood. The preposition al (over, upon) has been variously interpreted: eating meat with blood still in it (cf. Leviticus 17:14; 1 Samuel 14:32-33), or perhaps eating while blood rites were being performed. (2) lo tenachashu — you shall not practice nachash (divination by omens, serpent-augury; the verb is cognate with nachash, serpent). (3) lo te'onenu — you shall not practice me'onen (cloud-reading, time-observation, astrology — possibly from anan, cloud, or from ayin, eye). Both divination practices were common in Canaan and Mesopotamia, and both make the practitioner dependent on forces other than YHWH for knowledge of the future.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall not round the corners of your heads, neither shalt thou mar the corners of thy beard.
verses.original_text: לֹא תַקִּפוּ פְּאַת רֹאשְׁכֶם וְלֹא תַשְׁחִית אֵת פְּאַת זְקָנֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Lo taqqifu pe'at rosheikhem velo tashchit et pe'at zeqanekha.
verses.study_note: Two prohibitions on hair and beard grooming, each involving the pe'ah (corners, edges) — the same word used for the corners of the field in v.9. Do not round (naqqif — to go around, to cut in a circle) the corners of the head and do not destroy (tashchit — Hifil from shachat, to destroy, corrupt, ruin) the corners of the beard. These practices were associated with specific pagan mourning rites (cf. Jeremiah 9:26; 25:23) or religious observances of neighboring nations. The prohibition maintains the Israelite's distinct bodily appearance as a marker of covenant identity. The prohibition preserves the natural 'corners' — Israel must not erase its boundaries, even on the body.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall not make any cuttings in your flesh for the dead, nor print any marks upon you: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְשֶׂרֶט לָנֶפֶשׁ לֹא תִתְּנוּ בִּבְשַׂרְכֶם וּכְתֹבֶת קַעֲקַע לֹא תִתְּנוּ בָּכֶם אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Veseret lanefesh lo titnnu bivsarkhem ukhtovvet qa'aqa' lo titnnu bakhem ani YHWH.
verses.study_note: Two bodily practices prohibited: (1) seret (cutting, incision) lanefesh (for the dead/soul) — self-laceration as a mourning rite for the dead, practiced in Canaan (cf. 1 Kings 18:28 — Baal prophets cutting themselves) and forbidden in Deuteronomy 14:1; (2) ketovvet qa'aqa' — a tattoo mark (ketovvet from katav, to write; qa'aqa' is an onomatopoeic word for a stabbing/pricking sound). Tattoos as permanent marks were associated with cultic devotion to particular deities or with the marking of slaves. The prohibition maintains the body as YHWH's creation rather than a canvas for pagan religious devotion. Both practices are forms of marking the body in ways that assert allegiance or mourning practices incompatible with Israel's covenant identity.

verses.kjv_text: Do not prostitute thy daughter, to cause her to be a whore; lest the land fall to whoredom, and the land become full of wickedness.
verses.original_text: אַל תְּחַלֵּל אֶת בִּתְּךָ לְהַזְנוֹתָהּ וְלֹא תִזְנֶה הָאָרֶץ וּמָלְאָה הָאָרֶץ זִמָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Al techallel et bittekha lehaznotah velo tizhneh ha'aretz umale'ah ha'aretz zimmah.
verses.study_note: The father is prohibited from prostituting (lehaznot — Hifil of zanah, to prostitute, to cause to fornicate) his daughter. The Hifil causative form makes him the active agent: forcing or selling his daughter into prostitution. The verb chillel (al techallel — do not profane) is the same desecration verb of vv.8 and 12: the daughter's body is holy and her prostitution is a profanation of her God-given dignity. The social consequence is described in sweeping ecological terms: the land itself commits harlotry (tizhneh ha'aretz) and becomes full of zimmah (lewdness, sexual depravity). Sexual sin does not remain a private act — it defiles the land as a community, echoing Leviticus 18:24-28 where Canaan's sexual abominations vomited out its inhabitants.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall keep my sabbaths, and reverence my sanctuary: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: אֶת שַׁבְּתֹתַי תִּשְׁמֹרוּ וּמִקְדָּשִׁי תִּירָאוּ אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Et shabbetotai tishmoru ummiqdashi tira'u ani YHWH.
verses.study_note: A pairing that appeared earlier in the chapter (v.3 had parents + Sabbath; here Sabbath + sanctuary) — the Sabbath and the miqdash (sanctuary) are the twin pillars of Israel's sanctified time and space. Sabbath guards holy time; the sanctuary guards holy space. The verb for sanctuary is yare (to revere, to fear) — the same word used for parental reverence in v.3. Sacred space demands the same quality of reverential awe as sacred relationships. The repetition of this Sabbath command within the Holiness Code (vv.3, 30; Leviticus 26:2) signals its foundational importance: covenant holiness is expressed in the regular, ordered rhythm of sacred time (Sabbath) and sacred space (sanctuary).

verses.kjv_text: Regard not them that have familiar spirits, neither seek after wizards, to be defiled by them: I am the LORD your God.
verses.original_text: אַל תִּפְנוּ אֶל הָאֹבֹת וְאֶל הַיִּדְּעֹנִים אַל תְּבַקְּשׁוּ לְטָמְאָה בָהֶם אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Al tifnu el ha'ovot ve'el hayidde'onim al tevaqeshu letamme'ah vahem ani YHWH Eloheikhem.
verses.study_note: Two categories of spiritist practitioners are forbidden: the ov (pl. ovot — a medium, one who consulted the spirits of the dead; possibly from av, father, suggesting ancestor-spirit consultation) and the yidde'oni (pl. yidde'onim — a wizard or familiar spirit, from yada, to know — one who claims secret knowledge through spirit contact). Turning to (tifnu el — the same verb as turning to idols in v.4) these practitioners constitutes a turning away from YHWH. The purpose clause letamme'ah vahem (to be defiled by them) identifies the consequence: spiritism defiles the practitioner, the same way idolatry defiles. The divine-name formula Ani YHWH Eloheikhem seals the prohibition: Israel already has the living God who speaks — why seek the dead?
verses.doctrinal_note: The prohibition on consulting the ov and yidde'oni reaches one of its most dramatic narrative expressions in 1 Samuel 28, where Saul — cut off from divine guidance because of his disobedience — consults the witch of Endor and calls up Samuel's spirit. The act seals his doom: 1 Chronicles 10:13-14 explicitly identifies spiritism as a reason for his death ('Saul died for his transgression... and also for asking counsel of one that had a familiar spirit, to enquire of it'). The NT equivalent prohibition is explicit in Acts 19:18-20 (converts burning their occult books) and Galatians 5:19-21 (witchcraft/sorcery as a work of the flesh). Revelation 21:8 lists sorcerers among those excluded from the new Jerusalem. God's guidance through his Word makes necromancy not merely dangerous but theologically absurd: 'to the law and to the testimony: if they speak not according to this word, it is because there is no light in them' (Isaiah 8:19-20).

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt rise up before the hoary head, and honour the face of the old man, and fear thy God: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: מִפְּנֵי שֵׂיבָה תָּקוּם וְהָדַרְתָּ פְּנֵי זָקֵן וְיָרֵאתָ מֵאֱלֹהֶיךָ אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Mippnei sevah taqum vehhadarta penei zaqen veyareta me'Elohekha ani YHWH.
verses.study_note: Honor for the elderly is commanded in two physical acts: taqum (you shall rise up — standing to one's feet) when the white-haired (sevah — grey/white head) elderly person approaches, and hadar (to honor, to show deference, to treat with dignity) the face of the zaqen (elder, old person). The term hadar (honor, splendor) is the same word used for the magnificence of God (Psalm 29:4) and the beauty of holiness (1 Chronicles 16:29): to honor the elderly is to reflect divine honor back to God's image-bearers. The grounding in divine fear (veyareta me'Elohekha) again links social ethics to theological conviction: the reason for elder-honor is God's own character as the Ancient of Days who is himself worthy of supreme honor.
verses.doctrinal_note: The command to rise before the elderly stands in theological continuity with the NT's teaching on honor within the covenant community. 1 Timothy 5:1-2 extends the principle: 'Rebuke not an elder, but intreat him as a father; and the elder women as mothers.' The treatment of the elderly as repositories of wisdom, dignity, and covenant memory is grounded in the biblical theology of the image of God: the older person's face is the face of a fellow image-bearer who has lived longer in God's service. 1 Peter 5:5 applies the elder-honor principle ecclesially: 'Likewise, ye younger, submit yourselves unto the elder.' The ultimate grounding is Proverbs 16:31: 'The hoary head is a crown of glory; it is found in the way of righteousness' — longevity in righteousness is itself a form of God's gift and deserves the community's recognition.

verses.kjv_text: And if a stranger sojourn with thee in your land, ye shall not vex him.
verses.original_text: וְכִי יָגוּר אִתְּךָ גֵּר בְּאַרְצְכֶם לֹא תוֹנוּ אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vechi yagur ittekha ger be'artzekhem lo tonu oto.
verses.study_note: The treatment of the ger (resident alien, sojourner) receives direct legislative attention. The ger is one who gurs — who sojourns, who lives as a foreigner in a land not his own, with no land rights, no tribal protection, and no covenant ancestry. Lo tonu oto (you shall not wrong/oppress him) uses the verb yanah (to oppress, to treat unjustly), a word applied throughout the prophets to social exploitation of the vulnerable (Ezekiel 18:7; 45:8). The ger's vulnerability is precisely his foreignness: he has no kin-group to advocate for him, no legal standing as a landowner, and is entirely dependent on the goodwill of the host community. The Torah makes that goodwill a divine command rather than a social nicety.

verses.kjv_text: But the stranger that dwelleth with you shall be unto you as one born among you, and thou shalt love him as thyself; for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt: I am the LORD your God.
verses.original_text: כְּאֶזְרָח מִכֶּם יִהְיֶה לָכֶם הַגֵּר הַגָּר אִתְּכֶם וְאָהַבְתָּ לוֹ כָמוֹךָ כִּי גֵרִים הֱיִיתֶם בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ke'ezrach mikkhem yihyeh lakhem haggeir haggar ittkhem ve'ahavta lo kamokha ki gerim heyitem be'eretz Mitzrayim ani YHWH Eloheikhem.
verses.study_note: The most radical extension of v.18's love command: the ger is to be treated ke'ezrach (like a native born, like a full citizen) and loved ve'ahavta lo kamokha (the identical phrasing as v.18 — you shall love him as yourself). The love command of v.18 was addressed to fellow Israelites (benei ammekha). Here it is extended to the non-Israelite resident: the circle of neighbor-love is explicitly expanded beyond ethnic and covenant boundaries. The grounding is theological-historical: ki gerim heyitem be'eretz Mitzrayim (because you were strangers in the land of Egypt). Israel's own experience of vulnerability and oppression as gers in Egypt is the empathic foundation for their treatment of foreigners in their midst. The Exodus-memory creates an ethics of solidarity.
verses.doctrinal_note: The extension of ve'ahavta kamokha (love as yourself) to the ger in v.34 is one of the most significant ethical developments in the entire Torah. When Jesus identified Leviticus 19:18 as the second great commandment, his subsequent parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25-37) answered 'Who is my neighbor?' in a way that directly extends the ger-principle: the neighbor is whoever is vulnerable before you, regardless of ethnicity or covenant standing. Paul's declaration that 'there is neither Jew nor Greek' in Christ (Galatians 3:28) is the eschatological culmination of Leviticus 19:34: the barrier between ezrach (native) and ger (stranger) is dissolved in the new humanity created in Christ. The church as 'a holy nation' (1 Peter 2:9) composed of formerly alienated people — 'who in time past were not a people, but are now the people of God' (1 Peter 2:10) — is Leviticus 19:34 fulfilled.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall do no unrighteousness in judgment, in meteyard, in weight, or in measure.
verses.original_text: לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ עָוֶל בַּמִּשְׁפָּט בַּמִּדָּה בַמִּשְׁקָל וּבַמְּשׂוּרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Lo ta'asu avel bammishpat bammiddah vamishqal uvammesurah.
verses.study_note: The justice command of v.15 (no avel in the mishpat) is extended from the courtroom to the marketplace. Three measurement terms are listed: middah (length measure — the measuring rod), mishqal (weight — the scales), and mesurah (liquid measure — the container for grain or fluid). All three represent the standard commercial instruments of ancient trade. Dishonest weights and measures (using different scales to buy and sell) were among the most common economic frauds in the ancient Near East, denounced repeatedly by the prophets (Amos 8:5; Micah 6:11; Proverbs 11:1; 20:23). The avel (injustice, perversity) that corrupts the law court equally corrupts the market: God's justice extends into commercial transactions.
verses.doctrinal_note: Honest weights and measures as a component of holiness reveal that the Holiness Code conceives of ethics as comprehensive: no domain of human interaction is outside God's moral governance. Commerce is as much a covenant matter as worship. The NT equivalent principle appears in 2 Corinthians 8:21: 'Providing for honest things, not only in the sight of the Lord, but also in the sight of men.' The Reformation doctrine of the 'calling' (vocation) developed this principle: all honest work done faithfully is done before God. Luther declared that a cobbler glorifies God by making honest shoes, not by sewing crosses on them. The Holiness Code grounds this conviction: 'I am the LORD your God' applies equally to the sanctuary and the marketplace.

verses.kjv_text: Just balances, just weights, a just ephah, and a just hin, shall ye have: I am the LORD your God, which brought you out of the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: מֹאזְנֵי צֶדֶק אַבְנֵי צֶדֶק אֵיפַת צֶדֶק וְהִין צֶדֶק יִהְיֶה לָכֶם אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתִי אֶתְכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Mo'oznei tzedeq avnei tzedeq eifat tzedeq vehin tzedeq yihyeh lakhem ani YHWH Eloheikhem asher hotzeti etkhem me'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The fourfold repetition of tzedeq (just, righteous) — just balances (mo'oznei tzedeq), just weights (avnei tzedeq), just ephah (eifat tzedeq — the dry grain measure), just hin (hin tzedeq — the liquid measure) — creates a rhythmic emphasis on commercial integrity. The ephah was approximately 22 liters (a standard dry measure) and the hin approximately 3.7 liters (for liquids and oil). The closing formula reaches its most expanded form: Ani YHWH Eloheikhem asher hotzeti etkhem me'eretz Mitzrayim (I am the LORD your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt). The Exodus-redemption is the ultimate ground of commercial ethics: God who redeemed Israel at enormous cost demands that they not defraud one another at trivial profit.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore shall ye observe all my statutes, and all my judgments, and do them: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת כָּל חֻקֹּתַי וְאֶת כָּל מִשְׁפָּטַי וַעֲשִׂיתֶם אֹתָם אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ushemartem et kol chuqqotai ve'et kol mishpatai va'asitem otam ani YHWH.
verses.study_note: The closing formula of Leviticus 19 brings the chapter to its conclusion with the full scope of the obligation stated: 'all my statutes' (kol chuqqotai — the fixed, divinely decreed laws) and 'all my judgments' (kol mishpatai — the case-law decisions reflecting divine justice). The two words together cover the full range of Torah legislation: chuqqim are the laws whose reasons may not be fully apparent (kilayim, sha'atnez, orla) and mishpatim are the case-law judgments whose equity is evident to reason (gleaning, honest weights, love of neighbor). Both categories in their totality are to be kept (shemartem — guarded, observed) and done (va'asitem — performed, enacted). The final seal: Ani YHWH — I am the LORD. The entire chapter rests on this foundation: the character and identity of the covenant God who commands holiness because he is holy.
verses.doctrinal_note: The closing formula Ani YHWH (I am the LORD) with which Leviticus 19 ends provides the theological architecture of the entire chapter. The divine name appears approximately sixteen times across thirty-seven verses — sealing every cluster of commands with the same declaration of divine identity and authority. This structure encodes the central principle of biblical ethics: morality is grounded not in social contract, not in human reason alone, not in consequentialist calculation, but in the character of the holy God who has redeemed his people and calls them to reflect his image. The NT's supreme statement of this ethic is 1 John 4:19: 'We love, because he first loved us' — the indicative of divine redemption generates the imperative of human response. Leviticus 19 and 1 John share the same grammar of grace: what God is and what God has done is the ground of what his people are called to be and do.

meta.overview:
Leviticus 19 is the ethical summit of the Holiness Code (chapters 17–26) and one of the most important chapters in all of the Torah. It opens with the foundational command of the entire Code — 'Be ye holy: for I the LORD your God am holy' (v.2) — and then unfolds the practical, social, and moral implications of that holiness across every domain of Israelite life: family relations, worship, economics, agriculture, justice, speech, sexuality, commercial practice, and the treatment of foreigners. The chapter is simultaneously the most comprehensive ethical code in the Pentateuch and the most explicitly theological, grounding each commandment in the repeated refrain 'I am the LORD' (Ani YHWH) or 'I am the LORD your God' (Ani YHWH Eloheikhem), which appears approximately sixteen times in thirty-seven verses. Every ethical demand flows from and returns to the character of the divine name.

The chapter echoes and expands the Decalogue: parents (v.3) and Sabbath (v.3) recall the fifth and fourth commandments; idolatry prohibitions (v.4) echo the first and second; theft, lying, and false oaths (vv.11-12) recall the eighth, ninth, and third; the neighbor-love commandment (v.18) provides the positive ethical foundation for the second table of the law. But Leviticus 19 does not merely repeat Sinai — it extends covenant ethics into daily economic, agricultural, judicial, and social life in ways that make holiness a comprehensive way of living rather than merely a cultic category.

The chapter's most quoted verse is v.18: 'thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself' — which Jesus identified as the second great commandment (Matthew 22:39), which Paul declared the fulfillment of the entire law (Romans 13:9; Galatians 5:14), and which James called 'the royal law' (James 2:8). What is often overlooked is that v.34 extends this same love command to the resident alien (ger): 'thou shalt love him as thyself.' Neighborly love is not ethnically bounded — it encompasses the stranger, the vulnerable, and the foreign.

Structurally, Leviticus 19 has a remarkable literary coherence: commands are grouped by theme, the divine-name refrain punctuates each section as a theological seal, and the chapter moves from the innermost circle (family, worship) outward to society (economics, justice), to agriculture (gleaning, firstfruits), to the body (tattoos, hair), to commerce (weights and measures), and finally back to the covenant formula (v.37). The chapter embodies the theological conviction that holiness is not primarily a cultic or ritual category but a comprehensive orientation of the entire self — social, economic, bodily, judicial, and relational — toward the character of the holy God.
meta.theme: Imitatio Dei: Holiness Expressed in Every Dimension of Ethical Life
meta.keyWordMeaning: Holy — the foundational attribute of God and the defining vocation of Israel. From a root meaning to be set apart, distinct, consecrated. Qadosh describes God's absolute otherness from all creaturely categories and simultaneously Israel's calling to reflect that character in the world. In Leviticus 19, qadosh is not primarily a cultic term but an ethical one: to be holy is to be just, honest, compassionate, sexually pure, and faithful to the covenant God. The NT directly quotes Leviticus 19:2 in 1 Peter 1:15-16, extending Israel's holiness vocation to the church: 'as he which hath called you is holy, so be ye holy in all manner of conversation.'
outline: The Foundation: Be Holy as I Am Holy (vv.1-2): The imitatio Dei principle
Decalogue Echoes: Parents, Sabbath, Idols (vv.3-4): The first and second tables in miniature
Peace Offerings: Proper Worship and Eating (vv.5-8): The timing of sacred meals
Gleaning Laws: Holiness in Agriculture for the Poor (vv.9-10): Leave the edges and the fallen
Prohibitions on Theft, Deception, and False Oaths (vv.11-12): Covenant integrity in speech and property
Justice for the Vulnerable: Wages, Handicapped, and Courts (vv.13-16): Protection of the powerless
Love Your Neighbor: The Royal Law (vv.17-18): The summit of the second table
Laws Against Mixing: Cattle, Seed, and Garments (v.19): The holiness of categorical boundaries
Sexual Ethics and Restitution (vv.20-22): Justice and atonement in sexual sin
Firstfruits of Trees (vv.23-25): Patient consecration of the land's produce
Body Holiness: Blood, Divination, and Mourning Rites (vv.26-28): Separation from pagan practices
Sabbath, Sanctuary, and Spiritism (vv.29-31): Holiness of time, place, and spiritual allegiance
Respect for the Elderly and Love for the Stranger (vv.32-34): Covenant dignity for all persons
Honest Weights and the Closing Formula (vv.35-37): Commercial integrity and the Exodus foundation

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayedabber YHWH el Moshe lemor.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with the standard divine-speech formula addressed to Moses alone, establishing the legislative authority of what follows. Unlike Leviticus 18 which opens with direct address to the children of Israel (Lev 18:2), chapter 20's laws are first addressed to Moses as the lawgiver before being transmitted to Israel (v.2 specifies 'thou shalt say to the children of Israel'). This two-stage transmission — God to Moses, Moses to Israel — is the pattern of the most weighty covenant legislation. The chapter that follows contains some of the most severe penalties in the entire Torah, and their divine origin is thus firmly established in the opening formula.

verses.kjv_text: Again, thou shalt say to the children of Israel, Whosoever he be of the children of Israel, or of the strangers that sojourn in Israel, that giveth any of his seed unto Molech; he shall surely be put to death: the people of the land shall stone him with stones.
verses.original_text: וְאֶל בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל תֹּאמַר אִישׁ אִישׁ מִבְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וּמִן הַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל אֲשֶׁר יִתֵּן מִזַּרְעוֹ לַמֹּלֶךְ מוֹת יוּמָת עַם הָאָרֶץ יִרְגְּמֻהוּ בָאָבֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-el benei Yisra'el tomar ish ish mi-benei Yisra'el u-min ha-ger ha-gar be-Yisra'el asher yitten mi-zar'o la-Molech mot yumat am ha-aretz yirgemehu va-aven.
verses.study_note: The first and most severe capital offense addressed is the offering of children to Molech (la-Molech — to the Molech). The universal ish ish (man, man) formula encompasses both native Israelites and resident aliens — the most horrifying covenant violation does not discriminate by ethnicity. 'Giveth any of his seed' (yitten mi-zar'o — gives of his offspring) is the Levitical idiom for child sacrifice, the passing of a child through fire to the Canaanite deity Molech. The death-penalty formula mot yumat (dying he shall die) is stated with the emphatic infinitive absolute — the strongest possible affirmation of the capital verdict. The execution method is stoning by 'the people of the land' (am ha-aretz) — the entire community participates, signifying corporate responsibility for enforcing covenantal purity. Stoning is the communal death penalty, symbolizing that all Israel rejects the offense.

verses.kjv_text: And I will set my face against that man, and will cut him off from among his people; because he hath given of his seed unto Molech, to defile my sanctuary, and to profane my holy name.
verses.original_text: וַאֲנִי אֶתֵּן אֶת פָּנַי בָּאִישׁ הַהוּא וְהִכְרַתִּי אֹתוֹ מִקֶּרֶב עַמּוֹ כִּי מִזַּרְעוֹ נָתַן לַמֹּלֶךְ לְמַעַן טַמֵּא אֶת מִקְדָּשִׁי וּלְחַלֵּל אֶת שֵׁם קָדְשִׁי׃
verses.transliteration: Va-ani etten et panai ba-ish ha-hu ve-hikhrati oto mi-qerev ammo ki mi-zar'o natan la-Molech le-ma'an tamme' et miqdashi u-le-challel et shem qodshi.
verses.study_note: God personally guarantees the penalty even where the community fails to execute it: 'I will set my face against that man' (va-ani etten et panai — the emphatic first-person 'I myself' + the face-setting formula of divine opposition, cf. Lev 17:10) and 'I will cut him off' (ve-hikhrati). The dual rationale for the penalty is theologically precise: Molech-worship defiles 'my sanctuary' (et miqdashi) and profanes 'my holy name' (et shem qodshi). The offense has two dimensions — spatial (the sanctuary is defiled) and personal (the divine Name is profaned). The sanctuary is God's dwelling-point in Israel's midst; the Name is His self-disclosure and covenant identity. Both are violated when Israel gives what belongs to God (its children, its worship) to a competing deity. The phrase 'profane my holy name' (challel et shem qodshi) becomes a recurring prophetic condemnation of Israel's covenant failure (Ezek 20:39; 36:20-23).
verses.doctrinal_note: The two-fold rationale — sanctuary defiled and Name profaned — establishes the theological framework for understanding why Molech worship is the capital offense above all others. At stake is not merely the child's life (though that too is an absolute violation of Gen 9:6) but the integrity of God's dwelling-place and God's Name among the nations. The Name (shem) of God is the revelation of His character and covenant identity — to profane it is to misrepresent God to the watching world. Ezekiel develops this theme most fully: Israel's covenant failures profaned the Name among the nations (Ezek 36:20), and the new-covenant restoration is explicitly 'for my holy name's sake' (Ezek 36:22). The NT fulfillment is Christ's mission to hallow the Name (Matt 6:9 — 'hallowed be thy name') and to be himself the Name above every name (Phil 2:9-10) — the one who reverses the profanation of every Molech-worshiper by bearing their covenant curse (Gal 3:13) and restoring the Name's honor through perfect obedience.

verses.kjv_text: And if the people of the land do any ways hide their eyes from the man, when he giveth of his seed unto Molech, and kill him not:
verses.original_text: וְאִם הַעְלֵם יַעְלִימוּ עַם הָאָרֶץ אֶת עֵינֵיהֶם מִן הָאִישׁ הַהוּא בְּתִתּוֹ מִזַּרְעוֹ לַמֹּלֶךְ לְבִלְתִּי הָמִית אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-im ha'alem ya'alim am ha-aretz et eineihem min ha-ish ha-hu be-tito mi-zar'o la-Molech le-vilti hamit oto.
verses.study_note: The law anticipates communal failure to execute the capital sentence — a failure of justice by willful blindness. The emphatic infinitive absolute construction 'hide their eyes' (ha'alem ya'alim — hiding they shall hide) mirrors the emphatic death-penalty formula mot yumat, creating a parallel: just as the offender 'dying shall die,' the community that willfully 'hiding hides its eyes' will face its own divine consequence. 'Hide their eyes' (ha'alem et eineihem) is the idiom for deliberate inaction in the face of known wrongdoing — not mere ignorance but the choice to look away. The law treats communal complicity in capital offenses with the same theological seriousness as the offense itself. A community that refuses to maintain its own covenantal purity becomes complicit in its defilement.

verses.kjv_text: Then I will set my face against that man, and against his family, and will cut him off, and all that go a whoring after him, to commit whoredom with Molech, from among their people.
verses.original_text: וְשַׂמְתִּי אֲנִי אֶת פָּנַי בָּאִישׁ הַהוּא וּבְמִשְׁפַּחְתּוֹ וְהִכְרַתִּי אֹתוֹ וְאֵת כָּל הַזֹּנִים אַחֲרָיו לִזְנוֹת אַחֲרֵי הַמֹּלֶךְ מִקֶּרֶב עַמָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-samti ani et panai ba-ish ha-hu u-ve-mishpachto ve-hikhrati oto ve-et kol ha-zonim acharav lizanot acharei ha-Molech mi-qerev ammam.
verses.study_note: When the community refuses to act, God Himself will execute judgment — not only against the individual offender but against his whole family (u-ve-mishpachto). The expansion of divine judgment to the family reflects the covenant's corporate dimension: a household that tolerates or participates in Molech worship is collectively implicated. The 'all that go a whoring after him' (kol ha-zonim acharav) employs the same covenant-adultery vocabulary (zanah) as Lev 17:7 — spiritual idolatry is covenantal prostitution, and those who follow the spiritual adulterer share his judgment. The phrase 'from among their people' (mi-qerev ammam) — now plural — signals that whole family groups are excised from the covenant community when justice fails at the community level.

verses.kjv_text: And the soul that turneth after such as have familiar spirits, and after wizards, to go a whoring after them, I will even set my face against that soul, and will cut him off from among his people.
verses.original_text: וְהַנֶּפֶשׁ אֲשֶׁר תִּפְנֶה אֶל הָאֹבֹת וְאֶל הַיִּדְּעֹנִים לִזְנוֹת אַחֲרֵיהֶם וְנָתַתִּי אֶת פָּנַי בַּנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא וְהִכְרַתִּי אֹתָהּ מִקֶּרֶב עַמָּהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ha-nefesh asher tifneh el ha-ovot ve-el ha-yidde'onim lizanot achareihem ve-natatti et panai ba-nefesh ha-hi ve-hikhrati otah mi-qerev ammah.
verses.study_note: Consulting familiar spirits (ovot) and wizards (yidde'onim — 'knowing ones') is condemned with the same divine-opposition formula as Molech worship: 'I will set my face against that soul.' The two categories of illicit supernatural consultation — the ov (possibly a spirit medium or the ghost of the dead consulted through a medium) and the yidde'oni (a wizard or one who claims to 'know' the future through occult means) — are condemned as spiritual prostitution (lizanot achareihem — going whoring after them). The turning of the soul (nefesh) toward these entities rather than toward the LORD is the spiritual equivalent of a spouse turning to a paramour. The chapter's opening concern (Molech) and this concern (familiar spirits) bracket the holiness imperative of vv.7-8, which directly follows.

verses.kjv_text: Sanctify yourselves therefore, and be ye holy: for I am the LORD your God.
verses.original_text: וְהִתְקַדִּשְׁתֶּם וִהְיִיתֶם קְדֹשִׁים כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-hitqaddishtem vi-hyitem qedoshim ki ani YHWH Eloheikhem.
verses.study_note: The holiness imperative stands at the chapter's structural center, transitioning from the prohibitions of vv.1-6 to the penalties of vv.9-21. 'Sanctify yourselves' (ve-hitqaddishtem — the hitpael reflexive of qadash, to be holy, to consecrate) is the active human side of the holiness relationship: Israel is called to actively pursue, choose, and maintain holiness. 'Be ye holy' (vi-hyitem qedoshim) is the resulting state. The ground for both imperatives is the divine identity: 'for I am the LORD your God' (ki ani YHWH Eloheikhem) — the same covenant-name declaration that opened the Ten Commandments (Exod 20:2) and the Holiness Code (Lev 19:2). The LORD's identity as Israel's God is both the ground and the goal of Israel's holiness — they are to be holy because He is holy, and because He is their God.
verses.doctrinal_note: Verse 7 is the positive theological heart of a chapter dominated by capital penalties. Before and after the catalog of death sentences, the LORD calls Israel not merely to fear punishment but to actively pursue holiness (hitqaddishtem — reflexive, self-consecrating action) and to be holy (qedoshim — the state of set-apart-ness that mirrors God's own character). The holiness call of Leviticus 20:7 is directly cited in the New Testament: 1 Peter 1:15-16 quotes the parallel text 'Be ye holy, for I am holy' (Lev 11:44-45; 19:2; 20:7) and applies it to the behavior of believers in Christ. The NT does not abrogate the holiness imperative but extends it: the ground of holiness for the church is not merely 'I am YHWH your God' but 'Christ, as a son over his own house; whose house are we' (Heb 3:6) and 'you are the temple of the Holy Spirit' (1 Cor 6:19). The reflexive hitqaddishtem anticipates what the NT calls the 'putting on' of the new man (Eph 4:24; Col 3:10) — holiness is not merely passive reception but active orientation of the self toward the holy God.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall keep my statutes, and do them: I am the LORD which sanctify you.
verses.original_text: וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת חֻקֹּתַי וַעֲשִׂיתֶם אֹתָם אֲנִי יְהוָה מְקַדִּשְׁכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: U-shemartem et chuqqotai va-asitem otam ani YHWH meqaddishkhem.
verses.study_note: The corollary to the holiness imperative: keeping the statutes (shemartem et chuqqotai) and doing them (va-asitem otam) is the behavioral expression of holiness. The parallelism of 'keep' and 'do' is thoroughgoing — guarding the law and enacting it are two dimensions of a single obedience. The verse's climax is the most significant reversal in the chapter: 'I am the LORD which sanctify you' (ani YHWH meqaddishkhem — the participial form, 'I am YHWH, the One who sanctifies you'). Where verse 7 called Israel to 'sanctify yourselves' (reflexive), verse 8 declares that God Himself is the sanctifying agent — ani YHWH meqaddishkhem. The human act of self-consecration (v.7) and the divine act of sanctification (v.8) are not in tension but in covenant partnership: Israel pursues holiness precisely because God is the one who accomplishes it. This is the OT pattern of divine-human synergy in sanctification that Paul develops in Phil 2:12-13 — 'work out your own salvation... for it is God which worketh in you.'

verses.kjv_text: For every one that curseth his father or his mother shall be surely put to death: he hath cursed his father or his mother; his blood shall be upon him.
verses.original_text: כִּי אִישׁ אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יְקַלֵּל אֶת אָבִיו וְאֶת אִמּוֹ מוֹת יוּמָת אָבִיו וְאִמּוֹ קִלֵּל דָּמוֹ בוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki ish ish asher yeqallel et aviv ve-et immo mot yumat aviv ve-immo killel damo vo.
verses.study_note: The capital penalty section begins with the fifth commandment's most extreme violation: cursing (qallel — to treat as slight, to curse, to revile) one's father and mother. The mot yumat formula is applied — parental cursing is a capital offense. The phrase 'his blood shall be upon him' (damo vo) is the standard formula of criminal self-liability: the offender bears his own blood-guilt, and the community executing the sentence is innocent of his blood. Parental cursing is capital not merely because it violates familial respect but because parents as the image of God's generative authority — to curse them is to assault the covenantal order of authority through which God governs human society. The fifth commandment stands between the God-directed commandments (1-4) and the human-directed ones (6-10), suggesting that parental honor is the bridge between divine and human authority.

verses.kjv_text: And the man that committeth adultery with another man’s wife, even he that committeth adultery with his neighbour’s wife, the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִנְאַף אֶת אֵשֶׁת אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִנְאַף אֶת אֵשֶׁת רֵעֵהוּ מוֹת יוּמַת הַנֹּאֵף וְהַנֹּאָפֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish asher yina'af et eshet ish asher yina'af et eshet re'ehu mot yumat ha-no'ef ve-ha-no'afet.
verses.study_note: Adultery (na'af — to commit adultery) with another man's wife is a capital offense carrying the mot yumat sentence for both parties — the adulterer (ha-no'ef) and the adulteress (ha-no'afet). The double specification — 'another man's wife' and 'his neighbour's wife' — emphasizes that adultery is always a three-way violation: against the marriage covenant, against the offended spouse, and against the community covenant structure. Both parties receive the same penalty — the law does not treat the woman as merely the victim of the man. The equal application of the death penalty to both adulterer and adulteress reflects an equality before the law that was distinctive in the ancient world. The prohibition is rooted in the seventh commandment (Exod 20:14) and its Deuteronomic parallel (Deut 5:18; 22:22).
verses.doctrinal_note: The capital penalty for adultery in Leviticus 20:10 provides the judicial background for the NT's most dramatic atonement narrative: John 8:1-11, the woman caught in adultery. The scribes and Pharisees who bring the woman to Jesus are invoking this very text. Jesus' response — 'He that is without sin among you, let him first cast a stone' — does not abrogate the Levitical penalty but exposes the hypocrisy of the accusers and redirects the judicial question. When all the accusers depart, Jesus performs a priest-like function: He does not condemn ('Neither do I condemn thee') and He calls to holiness ('go, and sin no more'). The legal question Jesus navigates is this: the woman is guilty under Lev 20:10; the accusers are themselves guilty under the broader law (Rom 3:23); only a sinless judge can execute the sentence. Jesus is that judge (John 5:22) — but He comes first not to judge but to save (John 3:17; 12:47). The woman's release from the death sentence is a parable of justification: the one who deserves mot yumat is released by the judge who will himself bear the death-sentence on her behalf (2 Cor 5:21).

verses.kjv_text: And the man that lieth with his father’s wife hath uncovered his father’s nakedness: both of them shall surely be put to death; their blood shall be upon them.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁכַּב אֶת אֵשֶׁת אָבִיו גִּלָּה עֶרְוַת אָבִיו מוֹת יוּמְתוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם דְּמֵיהֶם בָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish asher yishkav et eshet aviv gillah ervat aviv mot yumetu sheneihem demeihem bam.
verses.study_note: Intercourse with one's father's wife (eshet aviv — not necessarily the man's mother, but his father's wife, i.e., a stepmother) is given the capital penalty: both parties (sheneihem — the two of them) shall die, and 'their blood shall be upon them' (demeihem bam — plural blood-liability formula). The interpretive phrase 'hath uncovered his father's nakedness' (gillah ervat aviv) is the Levitical idiom for incest: the 'nakedness' (ervah) of a person is coextensive with those most intimately related to them, so violating the father's wife is treated as violating the father himself. This case is the most flagrant: Reuben's incest with Bilhah (Gen 35:22) and Absalom's public violation of David's concubines (2 Sam 16:21-22) are historical instances of this offense, both treated as assertions of power against the father.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man lie with his daughter in law, both of them shall surely be put to death: they have wrought confusion; their blood shall be upon them.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁכַּב אֶת כַּלָּתוֹ מוֹת יוּמְתוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם תֶּבֶל עָשׂוּ דְּמֵיהֶם בָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish asher yishkav et kallato mot yumetu sheneihem tevel asu demeihem bam.
verses.study_note: Intercourse with one's daughter-in-law (kallah — daughter-in-law, bride) carries the same capital sentence for both parties. The interpretive term added here is 'tevel' — translated 'confusion' in the KJV — the unique Hebrew word (H8397) for a specific category of sexual perversion involving a violation of proper relational categories. Tevel (from balal, to mix, to confuse) implies a catastrophic confusion of social and covenantal boundaries — the mixing of what must not be mixed. The father-son relationship and the marriage covenant are simultaneously violated when a man takes his son's wife. Judah's near-incest with Tamar (Gen 38:15-26) approaches this category and demonstrates how the violation scrambles the generational order of the household.

verses.kjv_text: If a man also lie with mankind, as he lieth with a woman, both of them have committed an abomination: they shall surely be put to death; their blood shall be upon them.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁכַּב אֶת זָכָר מִשְׁכְּבֵי אִשָּׁה תּוֹעֵבָה עָשׂוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם מוֹת יוּמְתוּ דְּמֵיהֶם בָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish asher yishkav et zakhar mishkevei ishah to'evah asu sheneihem mot yumetu demeihem bam.
verses.study_note: Male homosexual intercourse is classified as to'evah (abomination) — the strongest condemnation category in the Levitical vocabulary (cf. Lev 18:22). The phrase 'as he lieth with a woman' (mishkevei ishah — the lyings of a woman) specifies that the sexual act performed is the same act that belongs within the male-female covenant of marriage, but performed with a male partner — a confusion of the creational sexual order. Both parties receive the same capital sentence with equal blood-liability. The to'evah classification connects this prohibition directly to the Canaanite practices that defiled the land (Lev 18:26-30; 20:23) and serves as the explicit background for Paul's treatment in Romans 1:26-27, where homosexual practice is described as a 'natural' consequence of the suppression of the knowledge of the Creator.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man take a wife and her mother, it is wickedness: they shall be burnt with fire, both he and they; that there be no wickedness among you.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִקַּח אֶת אִשָּׁה וְאֶת אִמָּהּ זִמָּה הִוא בָּאֵשׁ יִשְׂרְפוּ אֹתוֹ וְאֶתְהֶן לְמַעַן אֲשֶׁר לֹא תִהְיֶה זִמָּה בְּתוֹכְכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish asher yiqqach et ishah ve-et immah zimmah hi ba-esh yisrefu oto ve-ethen le-ma'an asher lo tihyeh zimmah be-tokhekhem.
verses.study_note: Taking both a woman and her mother is classified as zimmah — a particular category of moral depravity, translated 'wickedness' but implying a planned, calculated evil (from zamam, to devise, to plan). The penalty here shifts from stoning to burning (ba-esh yisrefu — they shall be burned with fire) — the most severe execution method in the Torah, typically reserved for the most extreme covenant violations (cf. Josh 7:25 for Achan's family). The stated purpose of the penalty is communal prevention: 'that there be no wickedness among you' (le-ma'an asher lo tihyeh zimmah be-tokhekhem). The law explicitly functions as a deterrent and protective mechanism for the covenant community's moral integrity.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man lie with a beast, he shall surely be put to death: and ye shall slay the beast.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִתֵּן שְׁכָבְתּוֹ בִּבְהֵמָה מוֹת יוּמָת וְאֶת הַבְּהֵמָה תַּהֲרֹגוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish asher yitten shekhavto bi-vhemah mot yumat ve-et ha-behemah taharogu.
verses.study_note: Bestiality (intercourse with an animal, behemah) carries the capital penalty for the man and the command to kill the animal as well. The animal's killing is not punitive (the animal has no moral agency) but ritual-purgative: the animal that has been involved in tevel (the confusion of categories) must be destroyed to purge the defilement from the community. The same logic applies in verse 16 — the animal is killed alongside the human perpetrator. The double killing eliminates all physical traces of the act from the covenant community. This passage is the background for the Mosaic law's explicit statement that such practices were among the reasons Canaan's inhabitants were expelled from the land (v.23).

verses.kjv_text: And if a woman approach unto any beast, and lie down thereto, thou shalt kill the woman, and the beast: they shall surely be put to death; their blood shall be upon them.
verses.original_text: וְאִשָּׁה אֲשֶׁר תִּקְרַב אֶל כָּל בְּהֵמָה לְרִבְעָה אֹתָהּ וְהָרַגְתָּ אֶת הָאִשָּׁה וְאֶת הַבְּהֵמָה מוֹת יוּמָתוּ דְּמֵיהֶם בָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ishah asher tiqrav el kol behemah le-rive'ah otah ve-haragta et ha-ishah ve-et ha-behemah mot yumattu demeihem bam.
verses.study_note: The female equivalent of verse 15 prescribes identical penalties. 'To lie down thereto' (le-rive'ah otah — to be mounted by it, the hiphil of raba, to crouch, to lie down for breeding) specifically describes a woman presenting herself for intercourse with an animal. The same capital penalty and the same blood-liability formula (demeihem bam) apply. The equal application of capital penalties to both male and female perpetrators of bestiality demonstrates the law's gender-neutral application of its most severe sanctions — both men and women are fully moral agents before the law, equally capable of and responsible for the most serious violations.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man shall take his sister, his father’s daughter, or his mother’s daughter, and see her nakedness, and she see his nakedness; it is a wicked thing; and they shall be cut off in the sight of their people: he hath uncovered his sister’s nakedness; he shall bear his iniquity.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִקַּח אֶת אֲחֹתוֹ בַּת אָבִיו אוֹ בַת אִמּוֹ וְרָאָה אֶת עֶרְוָתָהּ וְהִיא תִרְאֶה אֶת עֶרְוָתוֹ חֶסֶד הוּא וְנִכְרְתוּ לְעֵינֵי בְּנֵי עַמָּם עֶרְוַת אֲחֹתוֹ גִּלָּה עֲוֹנוֹ יִשָּׂא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish asher yiqqach et achoto bat aviv o vat immo ve-ra'ah et ervatah ve-hi tir'eh et ervato chesed hu ve-nikhredu le-einei benei ammam ervat achoto gillah avono yissa.
verses.study_note: The penalty section transitions from capital offenses to covenant-excision offenses with the case of sibling incest. The striking word used for this violation is chesed — the very word for covenant love and kindness that describes God's steadfast love (hesed) throughout the OT. Here it may mean 'disgrace' (a semantic range of chesed in some contexts) or more likely 'a thing to be ashamed of' (the ironic use of a covenant-love word for its antithesis). The penalty is karet — both parties are 'cut off in the sight of their people' (le-einei benei ammam) — a public excision with the shame of communal witnessing. 'He shall bear his iniquity' (avono yissa) is the additional formula of self-carried guilt.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man shall lie with a woman having her sickness, and shall uncover her nakedness; he hath discovered her fountain, and she hath uncovered the fountain of her blood: and both of them shall be cut off from among their people.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁכַּב אֶת אִשָּׁה דָּוָה וְגִלָּה אֶת עֶרְוָתָהּ אֶת מְקֹרָהּ הֶעֱרָה וְהִיא גִּלְּתָה אֶת מְקוֹר דָּמֶיהָ וְנִכְרְתוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם מִקֶּרֶב עַמָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish asher yishkav et ishah davah ve-gillah et ervatah et meqorah he'erah ve-hi gilletah et meqor dameiha ve-nikhredu sheneihem mi-qerev ammam.
verses.study_note: Intercourse with a woman during menstruation (davah — sick, the menstruating woman) receives the karet penalty for both parties. The elaborate language here — 'he hath discovered her fountain' (et meqorah he'erah) and 'she hath uncovered the fountain of her blood' (et meqor dameiha) — emphasizes the blood-source dimension of the violation. In the context of Leviticus 17's theology of blood, intentional intercourse during the menstrual period (when the woman's blood is the center of the act) represents a deliberate disregard for the ritual significance of blood. The karet penalty (versus the lesser seven-day impurity for inadvertent contact, Lev 15:24) distinguishes intentional, repeated violation from accidental contact.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy mother’s sister, nor of thy father’s sister: for he uncovereth his near kin: they shall bear their iniquity.
verses.original_text: וְעֶרְוַת אֲחוֹת אִמְּךָ וַאֲחוֹת אָבִיךָ לֹא תְגַלֵּה כִּי אֶת שְׁאֵרוֹ הֶעֱרָה עֲוֹנָם יִשָּׂאוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ervat achot immekha va-achot avikha lo tegalleh ki et she'ero he'erah avonam yissau.
verses.study_note: Intercourse with either a maternal or paternal aunt is prohibited, with the lesser penalty of 'they shall bear their iniquity' (avonam yissau) rather than karet or death. The rationale given is 'for he uncovereth his near kin' (ki et she'ero he'erah — literally 'his flesh, his near kin'). She'er (H7607) denotes the close relative, the one of near flesh — a biological/kinship term that grounds the prohibition in the creational bonds of family. The lesser penalty (nasa avon without explicit karet) may reflect the greater genealogical distance compared to sibling incest (v.17), but the violation remains a serious covenant offense.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man shall lie with his uncle’s wife, he hath uncovered his uncle’s nakedness: they shall bear their sin; they shall die childless.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁכַּב אֶת דֹּדָתוֹ עֶרְוַת דֹּדוֹ גִּלָּה חֶטְאָם יִשָּׂאוּ עֲרִירִים יָמֻתוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish asher yishkav et dodato ervat dodo gillah cheta'am yissau aririm yamutu.
verses.study_note: Intercourse with one's uncle's wife (dodah — aunt by marriage) carries a distinctive penalty: 'they shall die childless' (aririm yamutu). Aririm (H6185 — from the root arar, to curse, or from the word for stripping bare) means childless, stripped of progeny. Death without children was among the most severe social and covenantal consequences in the ancient world — it meant the extinction of one's name from Israel. The unique childlessness penalty here may reflect the nature of the violation: a sexual act that scrambles the generational structure of the family is punished by being denied the generation that would perpetuate the family. The covenant community's continuation through offspring is disrupted by the very act.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man shall take his brother’s wife, it is an unclean thing: he hath uncovered his brother’s nakedness; they shall be childless.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִקַּח אֶת אֵשֶׁת אָחִיו נִדָּה הִוא עֶרְוַת אָחִיו גִּלָּה עֲרִירִים יִהְיוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish asher yiqqach et eshet achiv niddah hi ervat achiv gillah aririm yihyu.
verses.study_note: Taking one's brother's wife is classified as niddah (H5079 — impurity, the term used for the menstrual separation period in Lev 15) — here applied metaphorically to a social-sexual violation: the relationship is 'impure' as an ongoing state, not merely during a temporary period. The penalty is childlessness (aririm yihyu — they shall be childless) rather than death. This prohibition is in apparent tension with the levirate marriage law (Deut 25:5-10), which requires a man to marry his deceased brother's widow — illustrating that Hebrew law carefully calibrated different scenarios (living brother vs. deceased brother) within a coherent framework. John the Baptist's condemnation of Herod Antipas for marrying his brother Philip's wife (Matt 14:3-4; Mark 6:18) invokes this very prohibition.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall therefore keep all my statutes, and all my judgments, and do them: that the land, whither I bring you to dwell therein, spue you not out.
verses.original_text: וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת כָּל חֻקֹּתַי וְאֶת כָּל מִשְׁפָּטַי וַעֲשִׂיתֶם אֹתָם וְלֹא תָקִיא אֶתְכֶם הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי מֵבִיא אֶתְכֶם שָׁמָּה לָשֶׁבֶת בָּהּ׃
verses.transliteration: U-shemartem et kol chuqqotai ve-et kol mishpatai va-asitem otam ve-lo taqiya etkhem ha-aretz asher ani mevi etkhem shammah lashevet bah.
verses.study_note: The transition to the holiness rationale section: Israel must keep all the statutes and judgments 'that the land... spue you not out' (ve-lo taqiya etkhem ha-aretz — and the land shall not vomit you out). The verb taqiya (to vomit, to spew out, H6958) is strikingly visceral — the land is personified as a stomach that has been force-fed defilement and will expel the defiling inhabitants. This vomiting metaphor appears in Leviticus 18:25 and 28 as well — a consistent and deliberate image for the land's moral response to persistent covenant violation. The land has a sanctity of its own; it is not neutral territory but holy space given by a holy God for a holy people. It will not indefinitely contain moral defilement.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall not walk in the manners of the nation, which I cast out before you: for they committed all these things, and therefore I abhorred them.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא תֵלְכוּ בְּחֻקֹּת הַגּוֹי אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי מְשַׁלֵּחַ מִפְּנֵיכֶם כִּי אֶת כָּל אֵלֶּה עָשׂוּ וָאָקֻץ בָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-lo telechu be-chuqqot ha-goi asher ani meshalleach mi-peneikem ki et kol elleh asu va-aqutz bam.
verses.study_note: The Canaanite expulsion is now given its explicit moral rationale: they committed all these things (et kol elleh asu — all the sexual and religious violations catalogued in chapters 18 and 20) and 'therefore I abhorred them' (va-aqutz bam — and I loathed them, from qut, to feel disgust, to abhor). The Canaanite dispossession is not ethnic cleansing but moral judgment — the same acts that would bring Israel under divine judgment (v.22) have already brought the Canaanites under it. This establishes that the moral standards of Leviticus 20 are not arbitrary Israelite ethnic markers but universal moral principles that God applies equally to all nations. The dispossession of Canaan is the historical precedent and warning for Israel's own future expulsion if it repeats the same patterns.
verses.doctrinal_note: The theological statement 'I abhorred them' (va-aqutz bam) applied to the Canaanite nations provides one of the most explicit instances in the Torah of divine moral judgment on non-Israelite peoples based on universal moral standards. This passage is crucial for two NT theological developments. First, Paul's argument in Romans 1:18-32 that the Gentile world is under divine judgment for moral violations: the 'wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness' — not merely against Israel's covenant violations but against all human suppression of moral truth. Second, the holiness of the land as a theologically significant category pointing forward to the holy land of the new creation (Rev 21:27 — 'there shall in no wise enter into it any thing that defileth'). The new Jerusalem, like the old Promised Land, cannot contain persistent defilement — but the mechanism of exclusion in the new creation is not expulsion but the atoning work of Christ that permanently cleanses those who are His.

verses.kjv_text: But I have said unto you, Ye shall inherit their land, and I will give it unto you to possess it, a land that floweth with milk and honey: I am the LORD your God, which have separated you from other people.
verses.original_text: וָאֹמַר לָכֶם אַתֶּם תִּירְשׁוּ אֶת אַדְמָתָם וַאֲנִי אֶתְּנֶנָּה לָכֶם לָרֶשֶׁת אֹתָהּ אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבַשׁ אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם אֲשֶׁר הִבְדַּלְתִּי אֶתְכֶם מִן הָעַמִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Va-omar lakhem attem tirshu et admatam va-ani ettenennah lakhem lareshet otah eretz zavat chalav u-devash ani YHWH Eloheikhem asher hivdalti etkhem min ha-ammim.
verses.study_note: The promise of the land (eretz zavat chalav u-devash — the land flowing with milk and honey, the classic covenant land formula, Exod 3:8) is set within the context of divine separation: 'I am the LORD your God, which have separated you from other people' (asher hivdalti etkhem min ha-ammim). The verb hivdalti (from badal, to separate, to distinguish) is the key term: Israel's election is defined as a separation from the nations. This same badal verb is used for God's creative separations in Genesis 1 (separating light from darkness, waters above from waters below) — Israel's election participates in the same creative-separating act. The promise of the land and the law of separation are inseparable: the land is given to the separated people, and the separated people must maintain their separation to remain in the land.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall therefore put difference between clean beasts and unclean, and between unclean fowls and clean: and ye shall not make your souls abominable by beast, or by fowl, or by any manner of living thing that creepeth on the ground, which I have separated from you as unclean.
verses.original_text: וְהִבְדַּלְתֶּם בֵּין הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהֹרָה לַטְּמֵאָה וּבֵין הָעוֹף הַטָּמֵא לַטָּהֹר וְלֹא תְשַׁקְּצוּ אֶת נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם בַּבְּהֵמָה וּבָעוֹף וּבְכֹל אֲשֶׁר תִּרְמֹשׂ הָאֲדָמָה אֲשֶׁר הִבְדַּלְתִּי לָכֶם לְטַמֵּא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-hivdaltem bein ha-behemah ha-tehorah la-teme'ah u-vein ha-of ha-tame la-tahor ve-lo teshaqetzu et nafshoteikhem ba-behemah u-va-of u-ve-khol asher tirmos ha-adamah asher hivdalti lakhem le-tamme.
verses.study_note: The same badal (separation) vocabulary used for Israel's election (v.24) is now applied to the dietary distinctions of Leviticus 11. Israel is to put difference (ve-hivdaltem — to make distinctions, to separate actively) between clean and unclean animals, just as God has separated (asher hivdalti — the same verb) them from the nations. The dietary laws are the daily enacted embodiment of the election-separation principle. 'Make your souls abominable' (teshaqetzu et nafshoteikhem — from shaqatz, to make abominable/disgusting) is the strong language for the defilement of the inner person through consumption of the wrong things. The dietary distinctions are thus not merely hygienic but ontological — they shape the inner being (nefesh) of the person who maintains or violates them.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall be holy unto me: for I the LORD am holy, and have severed you from other people, that ye should be mine.
verses.original_text: וִהְיִיתֶם לִי קְדֹשִׁים כִּי קָדוֹשׁ אֲנִי יְהוָה וָאַבְדִּל אֶתְכֶם מִן הָעַמִּים לִהְיוֹת לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vi-hyitem li qedoshim ki qadosh ani YHWH va-avdil etkhem min ha-ammim lihyot li.
verses.study_note: The climactic holiness formula of the chapter and one of the most theologically compressed statements in all of Leviticus: 'And ye shall be holy unto me' (vi-hyitem li qedoshim — be holy to me) + 'for I the LORD am holy' (ki qadosh ani YHWH) + 'and have separated you from other people' (va-avdil etkhem min ha-ammim) + 'that ye should be mine' (lihyot li). Four propositions in one verse: (1) the imperative of holiness directed toward God personally ('to me'); (2) the ground of holiness — God's own holy character; (3) the mechanism of holiness — separation from the nations; (4) the goal of holiness — belonging to YHWH (lihyot li — 'to be to me,' the possessive formula of covenant ownership). 'That ye should be mine' is the most intimate possible statement of covenant belonging — Israel is God's possession (segullah, Exod 19:5), His own (li).
verses.doctrinal_note: Leviticus 20:26 is the fullest expression of the election-holiness theology that undergirds the entire Levitical legislation. The four-part structure — imperative, ground, mechanism, goal — maps directly onto NT ecclesiology. (1) The imperative ('be ye holy unto me') is cited directly in 1 Peter 1:15-16, applied to the church: 'as he which hath called you is holy, so be ye holy in all manner of conversation.' (2) The ground ('for I the LORD am holy') is unchanged in the NT — the church's holiness is grounded in the same divine holy character, now fully revealed in Christ who is 'holy, harmless, undefiled' (Heb 7:26). (3) The mechanism of separation ('I have separated you from other peoples') finds its NT analog in the church's election: 'chosen... out of the world' (John 15:19) and 'come out from among them and be ye separate' (2 Cor 6:17). (4) The goal ('that ye should be mine') is the covenant possession formula fulfilled in the NT as 'a people for his own possession' (Titus 2:14, echoing Exod 19:5; Deut 7:6) and 'ye are not your own, ye are bought with a price' (1 Cor 6:19-20). Every theological brick of the Levitical holiness structure is present and perfected in the new covenant community in Christ.

verses.kjv_text: A man also or woman that hath a familiar spirit, or that is a wizard, shall surely be put to death: they shall stone them with stones: their blood shall be upon them.
verses.original_text: וְאִישׁ אוֹ אִשָּׁה כִּי יִהְיֶה בָהֶם אוֹב אוֹ יִדְּעֹנִי מוֹת יוּמָתוּ בָּאֶבֶן יִרְגְּמוּ אֹתָם דְּמֵיהֶם בָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve-ish o ishah ki yihyeh vahem ov o yidde'oni mot yumatu ba-aven yirgemu otam demeihem bam.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with a return to the familiar spirits and wizards of verse 6, now with an upgraded penalty: where verse 6 prescribed karet (divine cutting off), verse 27 prescribes capital execution by stoning — the mot yumat formula applied to the person 'in whom is' (ki yihyeh vahem — who has within them) a familiar spirit (ov) or a wizard-spirit (yidde'oni). The phrase 'hath a familiar spirit' (yihyeh vahem ov) indicates not merely consulting such spirits but harboring them — being a practitioner of spirit-mediumship, not merely a one-time inquirer. The chapter thus forms a ring composition: Molech worship and demonic consultation open (vv.1-6) and close (v.27) the chapter, enclosing the holiness imperatives (vv.7-8) and the penalty catalogue (vv.9-21) within the framework of the most serious idolatrous violations. The stoning penalty for harboring familiar spirits has its most dramatic OT example in Saul's reign: he expelled the mediums (1 Sam 28:3) early in his reign but then consulted the witch of Endor himself — an act that marked the final seal of divine rejection upon his kingship (1 Chr 10:13-14).

meta.overview:
Leviticus 20 is the judicial companion to Leviticus 18. Where chapter 18 catalogued the forbidden sexual and religious practices that define the boundary between Israel and the nations, chapter 20 prescribes the penalties for those violations, placing the community's covenantal fidelity on the most serious legal footing in the ancient world. The chapter is not a catalogue of arbitrary punishments but a sustained theological argument: the severity of the penalties reflects the magnitude of what is at stake — the holiness of God, the integrity of the covenant community, the purity of the land, and the very possibility of divine presence among a people.

The chapter opens with the capital crime of Molech worship (vv.1-5) — the practice of offering children to the Canaanite deity Molech, perhaps the most extreme expression of syncretistic covenant-breaking. The penalty is death by the community (v.2) and, where the community fails to act, divine karet (v.3) and the destruction of the offender's family (v.5). The section on familiar spirits and wizards (vv.6-8) frames the entire chapter with the theological hinge of verses 7-8 — the holiness imperative: 'Sanctify yourselves therefore, and be ye holy: for I am the LORD your God... I am the LORD which sanctify you.' This double divine-name declaration is the theological heart around which all the penalties revolve.

The capital penalty section (vv.9-21) prescribes death for cursing parents (v.9), adultery (v.10), various forms of incest (vv.11-12,14,17,19-20), homosexual practice (v.13), bestiality (vv.15-16), and other sexual violations, with lesser penalties (karet, childlessness, bearing iniquity) for less severe offenses. The progression is from capital offenses to covenant-level offenses to individual-consequence offenses, reflecting a graduated sense of the gravity of each violation.

The closing section (vv.22-26) provides the theological rationale for the entire chapter: the land itself has moral and spiritual integrity. Canaan 'vomited out' its former inhabitants for these very practices (v.23); Israel will be equally expelled if it repeats them (v.22). The land is not merely real estate but a holy space that rejects persistent defilement. The chapter concludes with the election-holiness formula: 'I have separated you from other people, that ye should be mine' (v.26) — the positive ground for all the prohibitions. The penalties are not punitive sadism but the enforcement mechanism of a holy community that exists to display the character of a holy God.
meta.theme: Covenant Penalties and the Holiness Imperative: Divine Justice as the Enforcement of Holiness
meta.keyWordMeaning: He shall surely be put to death — the emphatic death-penalty formula of Leviticus 20, occurring 9 times in 27 verses. The construction uses the infinitive absolute (mot — dying) followed by the hophal imperfect (yumat — he shall be put to death), creating the Hebrew superlative for absolute certainty: 'dying he shall die.' This formula does not represent arbitrary legislation but the divine assessment that certain covenant violations carry a death-claim upon the offender — violations so serious that they threaten the entire covenant community's existence before a holy God. The NT fulfillment is not the abolition of the principle but its transformation: Christ becomes the one upon whom the mot yumat falls on behalf of all covenant violators (Isa 53:8; Gal 3:13), so that the justified sinner, who deserved the death-sentence, receives instead the life of resurrection.
outline: Molech Worship: Capital Crime and Divine Karet (vv.1-5): Death penalty for child sacrifice; divine judgment where community fails to act
Familiar Spirits and the Holiness Imperative (vv.6-8): Karet for consulting mediums; the theological center — sanctify yourselves, for I am YHWH who sanctifies you
Cursing Parents (v.9): Death penalty for dishonoring father and mother
Capital Sexual Offenses: Adultery, Incest, and Homosexual Practice (vv.10-16): Mot yumat penalties for the most serious sexual covenant violations
Lesser Sexual Penalties: Karet and Bearing Iniquity (vv.17-21): Graduated penalties for less severe sexual violations
The Land Vomits Its Inhabitants: Holiness as Prerequisite for Dwelling (vv.22-24): The moral geography of the Promised Land
The Election-Holiness Formula and Separation from the Nations (vv.25-26): Israel's separation as the positive ground for the prohibitions
Mediums and Wizards: Death Penalty Closing the Chapter (v.27): Capital punishment for those who harbor familiar spirits

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Speak unto the priests the sons of Aaron, and say unto them, There shall none be defiled for the dead among his people:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה אֱמֹר אֶל הַכֹּהֲנִים בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵהֶם לְנֶפֶשׁ לֹא יִטַּמָּא בְּעַמָּיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh emor el-hakkohanim benei Aharon ve'amarta alehem lenefesh lo yitamma be'ammav.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with a distinctive formula: "And the LORD said unto Moses, Speak unto the priests the sons of Aaron." Unlike the standard Levitical formula "the LORD spoke to Moses saying, Speak to the children of Israel," this address is targeted specifically at the priestly class. The precision of "the sons of Aaron" (benei Aharon) underscores the hereditary nature of Israelite priesthood — one was a priest by birth into the Aaronic line, not by choice or achievement. The first prohibition addresses contact with the dead: "there shall none be defiled for the dead among his people" (lenefesh lo yitamma be'ammav). The word nefesh here means a dead person, a corpse (the soul that has departed) — one of the few uses of nefesh for the dead body rather than the living soul. Contact with a human corpse was the most serious source of ritual impurity in the Levitical system (Num 19), conveying seven days of impurity and requiring the elaborate red heifer purification. For the ordinary priest, this defilement is not absolutely prohibited — vv.2-3 will specify the permitted exceptions — but the default rule is separation from the dead.
verses.doctrinal_note: The priestly prohibition on corpse-defilement in Leviticus 21 establishes the typological framework that Hebrews 7:26 applies to Christ: "For such an high priest became us, who is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners, and made higher than the heavens." The Greek achrantos (undefiled) in Hebrews 7:26 is the precise New Testament counterpart to lo yitamma (shall not be defiled) in Leviticus 21:1. Yet Christ, in His incarnation, touched the dead and raised them (Mark 5:41; Luke 7:14; John 11:43) without becoming defiled — the direction of holiness-transfer reversed completely. He is not defiled by contact with death; He conquers it. Hebrews 2:14-15 explains: "that through death he might destroy him that had the power of death" — the High Priest entered the realm of death itself and emerged victorious, fulfilling and transcending every purification boundary the Levitical system had established.

verses.kjv_text: But for his kin, that is near unto him, that is, for his mother, and for his father, and for his son, and for his daughter, and for his brother,
verses.original_text: כִּי אִם לִשְׁאֵרוֹ הַקָּרֹב אֵלָיו לְאִמּוֹ וּלְאָבִיו וְלִבְנוֹ וּלְבִתּוֹ וּלְאָחִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ki im lishe'ero hakkarov elav le'immo ule'aviv ulevno ulevitto ule'achiv.
verses.study_note: The exceptions to the corpse-defilement prohibition are carefully enumerated: the priest may defile himself for the death of the closest biological relatives — mother, father, son, daughter, brother. The phrase she'ero hakkarov elav (his near kin close to him) establishes the principle of proximity: the permissible defilement is limited to those bound by the most immediate blood-kinship. The list is deliberately specific. Mother and father (both parents) are included without distinction. Sons and daughters (both children) are included. The brother is included (v.2), and in v.3 the unmarried sister will be added. What is notably absent from the permitted list, compared to ordinary Israelite mourning customs, is significant: cousins, aunts, uncles, and more distant relatives are excluded. The priest's grief, though real, must be bounded by his priestly calling. His capacity to weep and mourn is not removed — it is structured.

verses.kjv_text: And for his sister a virgin, that is nigh unto him, which hath had no husband; for her may he be defiled.
verses.original_text: וְלַאֲחֹתוֹ הַבְּתוּלָה הַקְּרוֹבָה אֵלָיו אֲשֶׁר לֹא הָיְתָה לְאִישׁ לָהּ יִטַּמָּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vela'achotoh habbetulah hakkerovah elav asher lo hayetah le'ish lah yitamma.
verses.study_note: The unmarried sister is included among those for whom the priest may defile himself, but with a significant qualifier: she must be a virgin who has not been married (asher lo hayetah le'ish — who has not belonged to a man). The reasoning is that an unmarried sister remains in her family household and under the practical care of her male relatives, including her priestly brother. Once she is married, she has passed under her husband's household and is no longer the priest's immediate domestic responsibility in the same way. The specification "nigh unto him" (hakkerovah elav — the one near to him) reinforces the proximity principle from v.2: it is nearness of present relationship, not merely the kinship category, that determines the permission. A married sister who has her own household is no longer the same kind of near kin as an unmarried sister sharing the family home.

verses.kjv_text: But he shall not defile himself, being a chief man among his people, to profane himself.
verses.original_text: לֹא יִטַּמָּא בַּעַל בְּעַמָּיו לְחַלְּלוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Lo yitamma ba'al be'ammav lechalelo.
verses.study_note: This verse is one of the most difficult in the chapter, with debated interpretation. The phrase ba'al be'ammav is the crux: ba'al can mean "husband," "owner," "chief," or "master." Interpretations include: (1) He shall not defile himself as a husband/head of household among his people (i.e., for a wife — a wife is notably absent from the permitted list of vv.2-3); (2) He shall not defile himself as a leader among his people (i.e., his priestly status prohibits unnecessary defilement beyond the permitted exceptions). The most contextually coherent reading connects this verse to the missing wife from vv.2-3: the priest is not permitted to defile himself for his wife's death the way ordinary Israelites might mourn. The wife, though the most intimate of all human bonds, does not appear in the permitted list. This is consistent with the high priest's even stricter prohibition in v.11. "To profane himself" (lechalelo — from chalal, to pierce, defile, profane) connects to the chapter's governing concern: priestly self-defilement in unpermitted ways is self-profanation, a corruption of the sacred role.

verses.kjv_text: They shall not make baldness upon their head, neither shall they shave off the corner of their beard, nor make any cuttings in their flesh.
verses.original_text: לֹא יִקְרְחוּ קָרְחָה בְּרֹאשָׁם וּפְאַת זְקָנָם לֹא יְגַלֵּחוּ וּבִבְשָׂרָם לֹא יִשְׂרְטוּ שָׂרָטֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Lo yiqrechu karechah berosham upe'at zekhanam lo yegallechu uvevsaram lo yisretu saratet.
verses.study_note: Three mourning practices are prohibited for priests: (1) baldness on the head (qorchah — shaving or tearing hair as a sign of grief); (2) shaving the corners of the beard (pe'at zekhanam — the edges/corners of their beard); (3) cuttings in the flesh (saretet — incisions, gashes cut into the body as mourning rites). All three were common ancient Near Eastern mourning practices — archaeological and literary evidence confirms that hair-removal and self-laceration were practiced in Egypt, Canaan, and Mesopotamia as expressions of intense grief. Leviticus 19:27-28 already prohibited these for all Israelites; their prohibition for priests in Leviticus 21 is a heightened restatement of this principle with particular force for the priestly class. The theological rationale is clear: these practices were associated with pagan mourning rites and the cult of the dead, and they were expressions of despair that denied the hope and holiness that Israel's covenant with the living God provided.

verses.kjv_text: They shall be holy unto their God, and not profane the name of their God: for the offerings of the LORD made by fire, and the bread of their God, they do offer: therefore they shall be holy.
verses.original_text: קְדֹשִׁים יִהְיוּ לֵאלֹהֵיהֶם וְלֹא יְחַלְּלוּ שֵׁם אֱלֹהֵיהֶם כִּי אֶת אִשֵּׁי יְהוָה לֶחֶם אֱלֹהֵיהֶם הֵם מַקְרִיבִם וְהָיוּ קֹדֶשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Qedoshim yihyu le'Elohehem velo yechalelu shem Elohehem ki et-ishei YHWH lechem Elohehem hem maqrivim vehavu qodesh.
verses.study_note: The theological summary of the priests' heightened holiness requirements is given in this pivotal verse. Three statements are made: (1) "They shall be holy unto their God" (qedoshim yihyu — plural: they shall be holy ones); (2) "They shall not profane the name of their God" (velo yechalelu shem Elohehem — the same chalal root as profanation throughout the chapter); (3) The rationale: "for the offerings of the LORD made by fire, and the bread of their God, they offer" (ki et-ishei YHWH lechem Elohehem hem maqrivim). The phrase lechem Elohim (bread/food of God) is a remarkable anthropomorphism that recurs throughout Leviticus 21-22 (vv.6,8,17,21,22; 22:25) — God's offerings are described as His food, His bread, reflecting the ancient Near Eastern understanding of sacrificial meals as divine nourishment. The offering of God's bread is the priests' defining function, and it requires that those who offer it be qodesh (holy, set apart, consecrated).
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase lechem Elohim (bread of God, food of God) in Leviticus 21:6,8 is one of the most theologically rich expressions in the Levitical system. It describes the offerings as God's food — His bread — in a way that is deliberately anthropomorphic, positioning the altar as the divine table. This reaches its ultimate fulfillment in the New Testament's Eucharistic theology. John 6:35 records Jesus declaring: "I am the bread of life" (ego eimi ho artos tes zoes) — the ultimate lechem Elohim (bread of God) is not the animal offerings on the altar but the Son of God Himself, given for the life of the world (John 6:51). First Corinthians 10:16-17 develops this further: "The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the communion of the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, is it not the communion of the body of Christ?" The priestly offering of God's bread in Leviticus 21 anticipates the moment when God Himself provides the bread — and is the bread — for the world's salvation.

verses.kjv_text: They shall not take a wife that is a whore, or profane; neither shall they take a woman put away from her husband: for he is holy unto his God.
verses.original_text: אִשָּׁה זֹנָה וַחֲלָלָה לֹא יִקָּחוּ וְאִשָּׁה גְּרוּשָׁה מֵאִישָׁהּ לֹא יִקָּחוּ כִּי קָדֹשׁ הוּא לֵאלֹהָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ishah zonah vachalal lo yikkahu ve'ishah gerushah me'ishah lo yikkahu ki qadosh hu le'Elohav.
verses.study_note: The ordinary priests are prohibited from marrying three categories of women: (1) a whore (zonah — from zanah, to commit fornication/harlotry; broadly, a sexually immoral woman or cultic prostitute); (2) a profane/defiled woman (chalalah — from chalal, to profane; a woman who has been desecrated, often specifically one who has had relations with a man forbidden to her); (3) a divorced woman (gerushah — from garash, to drive out, expel; a woman sent away from her husband).
